[G10] MUSICAL INSTRUMENTS -- G10 covers musical instruments and related apparatus, including the design, construction and operation of acoustic, electronic and electro-acoustic devices such as string, wind, percussion and keyboard instruments, as well as accessories that modify or support their sound production.
[G10B] ORGANS, HARMONIUMS OR SIMILAR WIND MUSICAL INSTRUMENTS WITH ASSOCIATED BLOWING APPARATUS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General design of organs, harmoniums, or similar wind musical instruments with associated blowing apparatus. Details or accessories thereof.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Accordions or concertinas; Keyboards therefor -> G10D11/00 - Automatic wind instruments -> G10F1/12  **Glossary:** - Organ: In this subclass, the term 'organ' refers to a musical instrument played from at least one keyboard, and possibly a pedal board, which produces sound by the movement of air in pipes, the air or 'wind' being supplied by blowing apparatus which may comprise a source of wind and a reservoir, for example an electric blower and bellows.
[G10B 1/00] General design of organs, harmoniums or similar wind musical instruments with associated blowing apparatus -- It covers overall design features of pipe organs, harmoniums and similar wind musical instruments that include a system for producing air flow such as bellows or electric blowers. The class addresses the structural layout, wind-supply arrangement and integration with the key action and related components.
[G10B 1/02] of organs, i.e. pipe organs -- G10B 1/02 concerns the general design of organs, specifically pipe organs, covering the configuration of pipes, wind-supply system and associated blowing apparatus in wind-type musical instruments.
[G10B 1/04] with electric action -- The subclass G10B 1/04 concerns organs, harmoniums and similar wind instruments whose key or stop action is performed electrically instead of purely mechanically. It covers designs in which electric circuitry or motors activate the valves that control the blowing apparatus, providing electronic control of pitch and dynamics.
[G10B 1/06] with pneumatic action -- G10B 1/06 covers the general design of organs, harmoniums and related wind instruments that employ pneumatic action to operate the blowing apparatus and control the airflow to the pipes or reeds. It pertains to the mechanical and air-handling features that use pressurized air to actuate the instrument's keys or stops.
[G10B 1/08] of harmoniums, i.e. reed organs -- G10B 1/08 covers features specific to the design of reed organs, commonly known as harmoniums. It includes structural and functional aspects of the instrument's reed and air-supply system within the broader category of organs and similar wind instruments.
[G10B 3/00] Details or accessories -- G10B 3/00 pertains to the details or accessories of organs, harmoniums and similar wind musical instruments that incorporate a blowing apparatus, such as mounting brackets, couplers, bellows improvements, reed adjustments and other components that modify or support the instrument's performance.
[G10B 3/02] Blowers -- G10B 3/02 covers the details and accessories of organs, harmoniums and similar wind musical instruments that involve a blowing apparatus. It includes components such as bellows, air-supply mechanisms, regulators, valves and other parts that generate, control or modify the airflow needed to produce sound.
[G10B 3/04] Reservoirs -- Reservoirs are air-storage components used in organs, harmoniums and similar wind instruments with a blowing apparatus to provide a steady supply of pressurised air to the reeds or pipes. They include wind-chests, bellows, air tanks and other devices that accumulate and regulate airflow for consistent sound production.
[G10B 3/06] Valves -- G10B 3/06 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to valves used as details or accessories in organs, harmoniums, and similar wind musical instruments with associated blowing apparatus. This classification covers the specific mechanisms that control air flow within these instruments.
[G10B 3/08] Pipes, e.g. open pipes, reed pipes -- G10B 3/08 covers the pipes used in organs, harmoniums and similar wind instruments, including open pipes and reed pipes, as well as any details or accessories related to these pipe types. It falls under the physics subclass for musical instruments.
[G10B 3/10] Actions, e.g. key actions, couplers or stops -- G10B 3/10 relates to the mechanical and pneumatic components that transmit the player's input to the sound-producing elements of pipe organs, harmoniums and similar wind instruments, including key actions, couplers and stop mechanisms. The classification covers the design, construction and accessories of these action systems used to control airflow and pipe selection.
[G10B 3/12] Keys or keyboards -- The G10B 3/12 subgroup specifies keys, keyboards and their associated mechanisms for organs, harmoniums and similar wind instruments, covering the design, construction and arrangement of key levers, pallets and control assemblies that operate the instrument's blowing apparatus. It includes any accessory that forms part of the keyboard system used to produce pitches.
[G10B 3/14] Pedals or pedal boards -- The G10B 3/14 subclass covers pedals and pedal boards used as accessories for organs, harmoniums and related wind instruments. It includes devices that transmit foot motion to operate keys, stops or other functions, enabling hands-free control of the instrument's sound production.
[G10B 3/16] Swell chambers -- The CPC subclass G10B 3/16 covers swell chambers, which are components that modify the volume or expressive dynamics of organs, harmoniums and related wind instruments by enclosing part of the wind supply and allowing pressure changes. These chambers are considered accessories that influence the instrument's sound production rather than the primary blowing or reed mechanisms.
[G10B 3/18] Tremolo-producing devices -- G10B 3/18 pertains to accessories for organs, harmoniums and similar wind instruments that generate a tremolo effect, such as mechanisms that modulate airflow or sound to produce rapid variations in volume or pitch.
[G10B 3/20] Transposing devices -- The G10B 3/20 subgroup concerns devices that shift the pitch produced by organs, harmoniums or similar wind instruments with associated blowing apparatus so the instrument can be played in a different musical key without retuning. It includes mechanical, electronic or hybrid transposing mechanisms that alter the relationship between the keys or manuals and the sounding pipes.
[G10B 3/22] Details of electric action systems for organs, e.g. contacts therein -- G10B 3/22 relates to the details and accessories of electric-action systems in organs, such as the contacts and other components that operate the instrument's sound-producing mechanisms. It is classified under physics → musical instruments → organs, harmoniums or similar wind instruments with associated blowing apparatus.
[G10B 3/24] Cases -- This CPC subclass covers protective cases for organs, harmoniums and similar wind instruments, including carrying and storage containers designed to shield the instrument from damage, dust or moisture. It falls under the "details or accessories" group for these instruments.
[G10C] PIANOS, HARPSICHORDS, SPINETS OR SIMILAR STRINGED MUSICAL INSTRUMENTS WITH ONE OR MORE KEYBOARDS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General design of pianos, harpsichords, spinets or similar stringed musical instruments with one or more keyboards.  Details and accessories thereof.  Combinations of said musical instruments with other musical instruments, e.g. with bells or xylophones.  Methods or tools specially adapted for the manufacture or maintenance thereof.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Automatic musical instruments -> G10F - Automatic pianos with or without keyboards -> G10F1/02 , G10F1/04 - Combination instruments whose one part is an automatic piano -> G10F1/22 - Details or accessories of automatic pianos -> G10F5/00
[G10C 1/00] General design of pianos, harpsichords, spinets or similar stringed musical instruments with one or more keyboards -- G10C 1/00 covers the overall structural and mechanical design of pianos, harpsichords, spinets and related stringed keyboard instruments, addressing aspects such as frame construction, string arrangement, soundboard configuration, action mechanisms, and the integration of one or more keyboards. It includes innovations that affect the instrument's sound production, durability and playing characteristics.
[G10C 1/02] of upright pianos -- This CPC subgroup G10C 1/02 pertains to upright pianos and relates to the overall construction and layout of such instruments, including the arrangement of strings, soundboard, action, and case that distinguish them from grand or other keyboard types. It falls under the broader class for stringed musical instruments with keyboards.
[G10C 1/04] of grand pianos -- G10C 1/04 concerns the general design of grand pianos, a subcategory of piano-type keyboard stringed instruments. It includes patentable features such as the case shape, string layout, action mechanism and soundboard configuration that distinguish grand pianos from upright or other keyboard instruments.
[G10C 1/06] of harpsichords spinets or similar stringed musical instruments -- G10C 1/06 relates to the overall design of harpsichords, spinets and comparable stringed keyboard instruments. It covers structural and mechanical features such as the case, soundboard, bridge, stringing and action mechanisms that affect sound production and playing characteristics.
[G10C 3/00] Details or accessories -- This subclass covers components, fittings and optional parts that are attached to or used with keyboard stringed instruments such as pianos, harpsichords and spinets, including pedals, action mechanisms, keyboards, dampers and other accessories.
[G10C 3/02] Cases -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fall boards; Dust Covers; Lids, Legs.
[G10C 3/04] Frames -- G10C 3/04 deals with the frames that provide the structural support for pianos, harpsichords, spinets and similar keyed string instruments. It includes the metal or wooden chassis that carries the soundboard, string tension and the action mechanism, as well as related reinforcement components.
[G10C 3/06] Resonating means, e.g. soundboards or resonant strings -- G10C 3/06 covers the resonating components of keyboard stringed instruments such as pianos, harpsichords and spinets, focusing on elements that amplify or modify the sound produced, including soundboards, resonant strings, and similar structures designed to enhance acoustic output. These features are considered accessories that influence the instrument's tonal qualities rather than its primary playing mechanism.
[G10C 3/07] Strings -- The CPC subclass G10C 3/07 relates to the strings of pianos, harpsichords, spinets and similar keyboard stringed instruments. It covers the design, material, winding, mounting, tuning mechanisms and any accessory or replacement components specific to those strings. This classification is used for patent documents concerning improvements or innovations in string construction, adjustment or maintenance for such keyboard instruments.
[G10C 3/08] Arrangements thereof -- G10C 3/08 classifies arrangements of details or accessories for pianos, harpsichords, spinets and similar stringed keyboard instruments. It covers the configuration and mounting of components such as the action, soundboard, case, pedals or auxiliary devices.
[G10C 3/10] Tuning pins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pin Blocks.
[G10C 3/103] the axis of the pins being parallel to the strings -- This subclass covers keyboard string instruments where the driving pins, such as those in the action that strike the strings, are arranged with their longitudinal axis parallel to the strings themselves. It deals with design details or accessories that implement or affect this pin-string orientation.
[G10C 3/106] the axis of the pins being perpendicular to the strings -- The classification G10C 3/106 covers stringed keyboard instruments such as pianos, harpsichords and spinets where the pins that hold the strings are oriented so their axes are perpendicular to the string length, a design that influences string tension, vibration and sound production. It addresses the geometric relationship of the pin axis to the strings as a technical feature.
[G10C 3/12] Keyboards -- This CPC subclass G10C 3/12 relates to keyboards that form part of pianos, harpsichords, spinets or similar stringed keyboard instruments. It covers the design, construction, mounting and functional details of the keys and key-assemblies, including key levers, hammers, action mechanisms and any accessories directly attached to the keyboard. The classification is used for patents concerning improvements or modifications of these keyboard components.
[G10C 3/125] Materials or treatment of materials for the manufacturing of keys -- This CPC entry (G10C 3/125) deals with the selection, composition, and surface treatment of the keys used in pianos, harpsichords, spinets and similar keyboard stringed instruments. It falls under the physics class for musical instruments and addresses material choices and processing methods that affect key performance, durability and feel.
[G10C 3/14] for playing by the feet -- G10C 3/14 refers to accessories that enable a piano, harpsichord, spinet or similar keyboard instrument to be played using the feet, such as foot-operated levers, pedals or other mechanisms that translate foot motion into key actuation.
[G10C 3/16] Actions -- This subclass deals with the mechanical or electro-mechanical components that translate a player's finger pressure on a keyboard into motion of hammers or other devices that set the strings vibrating. It includes levers, springs, escapements, repetition mechanisms and other action elements for pianos, harpsichords, spinets and similar keyed stringed instruments.
[G10C 3/161] specially adapted for upright pianos -- G10C 3/161 covers inventions that are specially adapted for upright pianos, including modifications, mechanisms or accessories that enhance the instrument's function, ergonomics or sound production. It falls under details or accessories for pianos, harpsichords, spinets and similar keyboard stringed instruments within the physics-musical instruments section of the CPC.
[G10C 3/163] the action being mounted in a plane below the keyboard -- This subclass covers the arrangement of a keyboard action in which the hammer or plectrum mechanism is mounted in a plane below the keys, typical of horizontally oriented piano actions and similar stringed keyboard instruments.
[G10C 3/165] for plucking the strings -- The CPC subclass G10C 3/165 covers devices or mechanisms that pluck the strings of pianos, harpsichords, spinets or similar keyboard stringed instruments. It includes any attachment, actuator or accessory designed to strike or pull the strings to produce sound, rather than using the conventional hammer or reed action. This classification is used for inventions that replace or augment traditional string-excitation methods.
[G10C 3/166] for damping the strings -- The CPC entry G10C 3/166 covers devices or accessories that damp the strings of pianos, harpsichords, spinets or similar keyboard stringed instruments. It pertains to mechanisms that stop or control string vibration, such as dampers, stops or related control systems, used to achieve silence or dynamic effect during or after playing.
[G10C 3/168] with hanging jacks, i.e. jacks connected to hammer-butts or hammer-shanks -- G10C 3/168 covers accessories for pianos, harpsichords, spinets and similar keyboard stringed instruments that use hanging jacks, meaning the jacks are attached to the hammer-butts or hammer-shanks within the action mechanism.
[G10C 3/18] Hammers -- G10C 3/18 covers the hammers that strike the strings in pianos, harpsichords, spinets and similar keyboard stringed instruments. It includes hammer heads, shanks, felt or other striking surfaces and related mounting or adjustment mechanisms used as part of the instrument's action.
[G10C 3/20] involving the use of hydraulic, pneumatic or electromagnetic means -- This subclass concerns accessories or details for pianos, harpsichords, spinets and similar keyed stringed instruments that employ hydraulic, pneumatic or electromagnetic actuation, covering mechanisms that use fluid pressure, air pressure or magnetic forces to move keys, hammers or other functional parts.
[G10C 3/22] specially adapted for grand pianos -- This CPC subclass covers accessories, devices or modifications that are specially adapted for grand pianos, including components designed to fit the shape, string layout or action of a grand piano such as specialized pedals, hammers, mounting systems or other tailored fittings.
[G10C 3/23] with hammers mounted above the strings, striking downwards -- This CPC entry covers accessories or details for pianos, harpsichords, spinets and similar keyed string instruments in which the hammers are positioned above the strings and strike downward. It is part of the physics-musical instruments hierarchy, specifically dealing with the hammer-string arrangement of these keyboard instruments.
[G10C 3/24] Repetition [tremolo] mechanisms -- G10C 3/24 covers mechanisms that produce repeated or tremolo notes on pianos, harpsichords, spinets and similar keyed string instruments, such as repeat actions, tremolo devices, and related auxiliary parts that enable rapid note re-articulation.
[G10C 3/26] Pedals or pedal mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All pedals.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Keyboards or keys for playing by the feet -> G10C3/14
[G10C 3/28] Transposing devices -- G10C 3/28 covers transposing mechanisms for pianos, harpsichords, spinets and similar keyed stringed instruments, enabling the player to change the pitch relationship between the keyboard and the sounding strings. Such devices include levers, shift rails or other accessories that allow the instrument to be played in different keys without retuning.
[G10C 3/30] Couplers, e.g. for playing octaves -- G10C 3/30 covers mechanical or electronic devices that connect two or more keys so that when one is pressed the linked keys also sound, typically used to produce octave intervals in pianos, harpsichords, spinet type keyboards and similar instruments.
[G10C 5/00] Combinations with other musical instruments, e.g. with bells or xylophones -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combinations with electronic musical instruments.
[G10C 5/10] Switching musical instruments to a keyboard, e.g. switching a piano mechanism or an electrophonic instrument to a keyboard -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Switching the piano into a silent mode.
[G10C 9/00] Methods, tools or materials specially adapted for the manufacture or maintenance of musical instruments covered by this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Also materials.
[G10D] STRINGED MUSICAL INSTRUMENTS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General design of stringed musical instruments, e.g. bowed or rubbed string instruments, plucked or strummed string instruments or zithers.  Details of, or accessories for, stringed musical instruments, e.g. slide-bars.  General design of wind-actuated musical instruments, e.g. air reed wind instruments, beating-reed wind instruments, lip-reed wind instruments or free-reed wind instruments.  Details of, or accessories for, wind musical instruments.  Accordions, concertinas or the like; Keyboards therefor.  General design of percussion musical instruments.  Details of, or accessories for, percussion musical instruments.  Combinations of different musical instruments.  Aeolian harps or singing-flame musical instruments.  Musical instruments not otherwise provided for.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Organs, harmoniums or like musical instruments with associated blowing apparatus -> G10B - Pianos, harpsichords, spinets or similar stringed musical instruments with one or more keyboards -> G10C - Automatic musical instruments -> G10F - Automatic percussion musical instruments -> G10F1/08 - Automatic wind musical instruments -> G10F1/12 - Automatic stringed musical instruments other than pianofortes -> G10F1/16 - Electrophonic musical instruments in which tones are generated by electromechanical means -> G10H - Whistles -> G10K5/00  **Glossary:** - Air-reed wind instrument: In this subclass, the term "air-reed wind instrument" refers to a wind musical instrument which is of the type wherein an air current is directed against a ramp edge. - Wind (musical) instrument: A musical instrument in which sound is produced by the vibration of air, typically by the player blowing into the instrument. - Zither: In this subclass, the term "zither" refers to a stringed musical instrument with a resonator and without a neck, whose strings are stretched parallel to a surface of the resonator, and played manually.
[G10D 1/00] General design of stringed musical instruments -- This class covers inventions relating to the overall structural and ergonomic aspects of stringed musical instruments such as guitars, violins and harps, including body shape, neck construction and mounting of strings. It addresses features that affect playability, sound production and instrument stability, without focusing on specific components like strings or accessories.
[G10D 1/02] Bowed or rubbed string instruments, e.g. violins or hurdy-gurdies -- G10D 1/02 classifies the design features of stringed instruments that are sounded by bowing or rubbing, such as violins, violas and hurdy-gurdies. It covers the arrangement and construction of components like the resonant body, neck, bridge, fingerboard and bowing mechanisms that affect tone production and playability.
[G10D 1/04] Plucked or strummed string instruments, e.g. harps or lyres -- G10D 1/04 classifies inventions concerning the overall design of plucked or strummed stringed instruments such as harps, lyres and similar devices. It addresses the configuration of the body, resonator, string arrangement and playing mechanism but not individual string or tuning details.
[G10D 1/05] with fret boards or fingerboards -- G10D 1/05 covers designs of stringed musical instruments that incorporate a fret board or fingerboard. It falls under the physics subclass for musical instruments, specifically general design of stringed instruments, and is used by the EPO and USPTO in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[G10D 1/06] Mandolins -- G10D 1/06 covers mandolins, including their overall shape, sound-board, neck, bridge and related structural features. The entry classifies designs specific to the mandolin family within the broader category of stringed musical instruments.
[G10D 1/08] Guitars -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cut-away guitars; folding instruments.
[G10D 1/085] Mechanical design of electric guitars -- G10D 1/085 pertains to the mechanical design aspects specific to electric guitars, encompassing structural features and components that define their physical construction and functionality. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with the general design of stringed musical instruments under physics.
[G10D 1/10] Banjos -- G10D 1/10 pertains to the overall design of banjos, covering aspects such as body shape, resonator, neck, bridge and string arrangement. It falls within the CPC hierarchy for stringed musical instruments and addresses structural features specific to this instrument.
[G10D 11/00] Accordions, concertinas or the like -- G10D 11/00 classifies free-reed aerophones such as accordions, concertinas and similar instruments that use a bellows to force air through vibrating reeds, often operated by a keyboard or buttons. The entry covers their construction, sound-production mechanisms and related accessories, placing them within the stringed musical-instrument subclass of physics.
[G10D 11/02] Actions -- G10D 11/02 covers the mechanical actions of accordions, concertinas and similar free-reed instruments, detailing the keys, buttons, levers, bellows-control devices and other actuation mechanisms that the player manipulates to produce sound.
[G10D 1/12] Zithers, e.g. autoharps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dulcimers.
[G10D 1/14] Struck string instruments, e.g. dulcimers -- The CPC subclass G10D 1/14 covers the design and construction of struck string instruments such as dulcimers, where strings are set into vibration by hammers or mallets. It is situated under the physics hierarchy for musical instruments, specifically the general design of stringed musical instruments.
[G10D 13/00] Percussion musical instruments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drumkits.  Drums anchors.  Drun-risers.  Drum-keys.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Struck string instruments -> G10D1/14
[G10D 13/01] General design of percussion musical instruments -- G10D 13/01 classifies patents concerning the overall design of percussion musical instruments, including their shape, structure, mounting and acoustic optimisation. It addresses how the instrument's body, resonators and playing surfaces are arranged and constructed to achieve desired sound and ergonomic performance. The subclass is situated in the physics branch under musical instruments, specifically within stringed and percussion categories.
[G10D 13/02] Drums -- In the CPC hierarchy under physics → musical instruments → percussion, G10D 13/02 designates drums, a class of percussion devices that generate sound when a membrane stretched over a resonant shell is struck with hands, sticks or beaters, providing rhythm and timbre in virtually all musical styles.
[G10D 13/03] Practice drumkits or pads -- The CPC code G10D 13/03 covers devices used for practicing on drum kits or pads, including electronic and acoustic practice sets, drum pads, and related components designed to simulate percussion performance for training purposes. It addresses their construction, mounting, and acoustic or electronic features that enable realistic drumming practice.
[G10D 13/04] Timpani -- The CPC code G10D 13/04 designates timpani, the kettle-drum family of tuned percussion instruments that use a membrane stretched over a resonant shell and are tuned by adjusting the drumhead tension. It covers their construction, tuning devices and playing techniques.
[G10D 13/06] Castanets, cymbals, triangles, tambourines without drumheads or other single-toned percussion musical instruments -- G10D 13/06 covers single-toned percussion instruments such as castanets, cymbals, triangles and tambourines that lack drumheads. These items are placed under the hierarchy of physics, musical instruments, stringed musical instruments and percussion musical instruments in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[G10D 13/063] Cymbals -- The CPC entry G10D 13/063 designates cymbals, metal percussion discs that are struck together or with a beater to produce a crashing sound used for rhythmic accents in music. They are classified under physics and the hierarchical path of musical instruments, specifically as percussion instruments related to stringed instruments.
[G10D 13/065] Hi-hats -- G10D 13/065 classifies hi-hats, a percussion instrument made of two cymbals mounted on a stand and played with a foot-operated pedal, commonly used in drum kits to produce crisp, syncopated accents.
[G10D 13/08] Multi-toned musical instruments with sonorous bars, blocks, forks, gongs, plates, rods or teeth -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements of musical Bells; Steel Drums.
[G10D 13/09] with keyboards -- G10D 13/09 covers stringed or percussion musical instruments that incorporate a keyboard mechanism. It falls under the physics subclass for musical instruments, specifically the stringed and percussion categories. The code is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify patents for keyboards integrated with such instruments.
[G10D 13/10] Details of, or accessories for, percussion musical instruments -- G10D 13/10 covers the design, construction, and ancillary components of percussion musical instruments, including items such as drumheads, mounting hardware, muffling devices, tuning mechanisms, and other accessories that affect the instrument's performance or usability.
[G10D 13/11] Pedals -- G10D 13/11 covers foot-operated pedals that form part of a musical instrument's playing or control mechanism, such as sustain, damper or tone-changing pedals on pianos, foot-triggered hammers for percussion, and similar devices that are activated by the player's feet to affect sound production or instrument response.
[G10D 13/12] Drumsticks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bass-Drum Beaters; Gloves with hardened Tips.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Pedals for beaters -> G10D13/11
[G10D 13/14] Mutes or dampers -- G10D 13/14 pertains to devices that mute or dampen the sound of stringed and percussion musical instruments, such as mutes fitted to strings, bridges or soundboards and dampers that restrict vibration. These accessories are designed to control volume, sustain or timbre by limiting the instrument's resonant motion.
[G10D 13/16] Tuning devices -- G10D 13/16 classifies tuning devices for musical instruments, covering mechanical, electronic or acoustic means that adjust pitch, such as tuning pegs, fine-adjustment mechanisms, digital tuners and related accessories used with stringed or percussion instruments.
[G10D 13/18] Snares -- The CPC subclass G10D 13/18 covers snares, which are metal wires, cords or other resonant elements fitted to a drum head to create a characteristic buzzing or rattling sound when the drum is struck. This element falls within the hierarchy of physics, musical instruments, stringed musical instruments and percussion musical instruments.
[G10D 13/20] Drumheads -- G10D 13/20 designates drumheads, the stretched membranes or skins that cover the resonant opening of percussion drums and create sound when struck. This subclass falls within physics-musical instruments-stringed musical instruments-percussion musical instruments, covering the materials, construction and attachment methods of drumhead components.
[G10D 13/22] Shells -- G10D 13/22 classifies shells that function as resonating or sound-producing bodies in musical instruments, particularly those used with percussion or stringed instruments. It covers the design, material and construction of hollow or semi-hollow components that amplify or modify vibration, such as drum shells, resonator bowls or tubular sound-boxes.
[G10D 13/24] Material for manufacturing percussion musical instruments -- G10D 13/24 covers the selection and formulation of materials used to construct percussion musical instruments, including drum shells, heads, rods, mallets and resonators, and encompasses woods, metals, polymers, composites and other substances chosen for acoustic, durability or aesthetic properties.
[G10D 13/25] for changing the sound or tone  mutes or dampers  G10D13/12 -- G10D 13/25 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers devices designed to alter the sound or tone of percussion instruments, specifically focusing on mutes or dampers. These mechanisms are used to modify the acoustic properties by reducing volume or changing timbre.
[G10D 13/26] Mechanical details of electronic drums -- G10D 13/26 covers the mechanical design of electronic drums, including the construction of striking pads, trigger mechanisms, mounting systems and other hardware that translate a player's impact into electronic signals. It falls under physics → musical instruments → stringed musical instruments → percussion musical instruments.
[G10D 13/28] Mountings or supports for individual drums  supports for carrying musical instrument, while playing  G10G5/005 -- This entry G10D 13/28 falls under physics → musical instruments → stringed musical instruments → percussion musical instruments and pertains to mountings or supports for individual drums and devices that enable a player to carry the instrument while playing.
[G10D 15/00] Combinations of different musical instruments -- This group covers inventions that integrate two or more distinct musical instruments into a single device, for example a hybrid instrument that combines a stringed element with a wind or percussion component. It applies to designs where the combined functionality creates new playing techniques or sound possibilities, and it falls under physics, musical instruments, and stringed musical instruments.
[G10D 17/00] Aeolian harps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Kazoos.
[G10D 3/00] Details of, or accessories for, stringed musical instruments, e.g. slide-bars -- **Definition:** This place covers:  String cleaners.
[G10D 3/01] Endpins or accessories therefor -- G10D 3/01 relates to endpins and other accessories for stringed musical instruments, covering components such as slide-bars or supports that aid playing or holding instruments like violins, violas, cellos and similar.
[G10D 3/02] Resonating means, horns or diaphragms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sound Posts.
[G10D 3/04] Bridges -- G10D 3/04 refers to bridges for stringed musical instruments, the components that support the strings and transmit their vibrations to the instrument's body. This subclass also covers related accessories such as slide-bars that modify string length or tension.
[G10D 3/046] Mutes -- G10D 3/046 pertains to mutes for stringed musical instruments, covering devices that dampen, soften or alter the sound produced by instruments such as violins, guitars, or cellos. It includes various designs, from simple rubber or wooden plugs to adjustable mechanisms, and falls under the broader categories of physics, musical instruments and stringed instrument accessories.
[G10D 3/053] Capos, i.e. capo tastos -- G10D 3/053 covers caps, also known as capo tastos, which are devices attached to the neck of a stringed instrument to raise the pitch of the strings. It includes designs, mechanisms and accessories that clamp the strings at a chosen fret. The class falls under physics, musical instruments, stringed musical instruments and accessories.
[G10D 3/06] Necks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fretboards; Frets.
[G10D 3/08] Fingerboards in the form of keyboards -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Chord Selectors.
[G10D 3/09] for zithers -- G10D 3/09 is a Cooperative Patent Classification entry used by the EPO and USPTO for inventions relating to accessories of stringed musical instruments, specifically zithers. It includes items such as detail parts, slide-bars and other supplementary components designed for zither performance or construction.
[G10D 3/095] Details of removable or collapsible necks, e.g. suitable for transport or storage -- This class covers designs and components that allow the neck of a stringed instrument to be removed or folded for convenient transport or compact storage, including the mechanisms, joints and fittings that enable such detachable or collapsible configurations. It also includes accessories that secure or support the neck in its removable or collapsible form.
[G10D 3/10] Strings -- The G10D 3/10 subclass covers components and accessories that relate specifically to the strings of stringed musical instruments, including the strings themselves, devices for adjusting tension, slide-bars, and mounting or replacement mechanisms. It addresses the design, material composition and functional features of these elements, encompassing any detail that affects the performance or maintenance of stringed instruments.
[G10D 3/12] Anchoring devices for strings, e.g. tail pieces or hitchpins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Nuts.
[G10D 3/13] Tail pieces -- G10D 3/13 classifies tail pieces, the hardware fitted to the rear end of a stringed musical instrument to anchor and tension the strings, commonly found on violins, guitars and similar instruments. It resides in the CPC subgroup for details or accessories of stringed instruments, under the broader categories of physics, musical instruments and stringed musical instruments.
[G10D 3/14] Tuning devices, e.g. pegs, pins, friction discs or worm gears -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Heads.
[G10D 3/147] Devices for altering the string tension during playing -- G10D 3/147 of the CPC, under the physics-musical-instrument hierarchy, covers devices that change the tension of a string while it is being played, such as mechanical levers, motor-driven tuners or slide-bars that allow the performer to raise or lower pitch in real time.
[G10D 3/153] Tremolo devices -- The G10D 3/153 entry covers mechanisms that create a periodic variation in pitch or volume on stringed musical instruments, such as tremolo bars, vibrato tails or motor-driven systems that oscillate the strings. These devices are accessories designed to alter string vibration for expressive effect, including hand-operated levers and electronic actuators.
[G10D 3/16] Bows -- G10D 3/16 covers bows and related accessories for stringed musical instruments, including the design, construction, and modification of bow sticks, hair, frog, and mechanisms such as slide-bars that affect tension and articulation. The entry addresses both traditional violin-type bows and specialized variants used on guitars, violins, and other plucked or bowed instruments.
[G10D 3/166] Guides for bows -- G10D 3/166 covers guides that are attached to or used with the bows of stringed instruments to direct or stabilize bow movement, such as rail or channel devices that aid consistent playing technique. It is an accessory for stringed musical instruments, falling under details of, or accessories for, stringed musical instruments.
[G10D 3/173] Plectra or similar accessories for playing -- G10D 3/173 covers plectra and comparable devices used to strike or pluck strings on musical instruments such as guitars, mandolins and other stringed instruments, as well as related accessories that facilitate playing, for example slide-bars or mounting hardware.
[G10D 3/18] Chin-rests, hand-rests, shoulder rests or guards being removable from, or integral with the instrument -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Thumb-rests.
[G10D 3/20] Winding tools separate from the musical instruments, e.g. tuning keys -- G10D 3/20 covers winding tools that are not integral to stringed musical instruments, such as tuning keys used to adjust string tension. These accessories are employed to wind or tighten strings but are separate from the instrument itself.
[G10D 3/22] Material for manufacturing stringed musical instruments -- G10D 3/22 classifies the raw and processed materials employed in constructing stringed musical instruments, including woods, metals, composites and related components such as fingerboards, bridges, bows and slide-bars. It is part of the CPC group G10D, situated under Physics → Musical Instruments → Stringed Musical Instruments and is used by the EPO and USPTO for patent indexing.
[G10D 7/00] General design of wind musical instruments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Didgeridoos.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Accordions or concertinas -> G10D11/00 - Whistles -> G10K5/00
[G10D 7/02] of the type wherein an air current is directed against a ramp edge -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pan pipes.
[G10D 7/026] with air currents blown into an opening arranged on the cylindrical surface of the tube, e.g. transverse flutes, piccolos or fifes -- This CPC subclass G10D 7/026 covers wind instruments in which the airstream is directed into an opening cut into the cylindrical surface of the tube, as seen in transverse flutes, piccolos and fifes. It relates to the general design of such side-blown wind instruments.
[G10D 7/03] with a windway for leading the air to the labium, e.g. recorders -- The CPC subclass G10D 7/03 covers wind musical instruments that incorporate a windway to direct the airstream onto a labium, as in recorders. It focuses on the design of the airflow channel and its interaction with the labium for sound production.
[G10D 7/04] with Helmholtz resonators, e.g. ocarinas -- The CPC entry G10D 7/04 falls under the group for wind musical instruments and covers designs that incorporate Helmholtz resonators, such as ocarinas, to shape the instrument's tonal characteristics. It specifies features of the resonating cavity that enhance sound production and control pitch within the broader field of musical instrument physics.
[G10D 7/06] Beating-reed wind instruments, e.g. single or double reed wind instruments -- G10D 7/06 pertains to wind instruments that generate sound by beating a reed, encompassing both single-reed and double-reed types such as clarinets, oboes and saxophones. The subclass addresses the design, reed configuration and acoustic principles specific to these beating-reed wind instruments.
[G10D 7/063] Oboes -- G10D 7/063 addresses the technical design of oboes, covering features such as bore shape, tone-hole arrangement, reed and mouthpiece construction, key mechanisms and the materials used to influence acoustic performance. It falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification for wind-instrument engineering.
[G10D 7/066] Clarinets -- G10D 7/066 concerns the overall design of clarinets, covering features such as bore geometry, material selection, tone-hole placement, keywork mechanisms and acoustic optimisation. It applies to both traditional and modern clarinet forms, addressing structural elements that affect sound production and player ergonomics. This subclass groups inventions that improve the instrument's performance, durability or manufacturability.
[G10D 7/08] Saxophones -- G10D 7/08 classifies saxophones, a family of single-reed wind instruments that blend woodwind and brass characteristics. The subclass addresses the overall construction, key mechanisms, bore geometry and acoustic design features that set saxophones apart from other reed or brass instruments.
[G10D 7/10] Lip-reed wind instruments, i.e. using the vibration of the musician's lips, e.g. cornets, trumpets, trombones or French horns -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Alpine Horns.
[G10D 7/12] Free-reed wind instruments -- Class G10D 7/12 relates to free-reed wind instruments, including devices like harmonicas, accordions and concertinas, where sound is produced by air flowing past a vibrating reed that is not directly struck. The subclass covers the overall construction, reed mounting, airflow channels and mechanisms for playing these instruments.
[G10D 7/14] Mouth-organs -- The G10D 7/14 subclass covers mouth-organ components of wind musical instruments, such as mouthpieces, embouchure devices and related shaping elements that control airflow into the instrument. It is used for patents describing design, construction or modification of these parts to affect sound production or player comfort.
[G10D 7/15] with movable mouthpiece -- G10D 7/15 covers wind-instrument designs that incorporate a mouthpiece which can be detached, repositioned or otherwise moved relative to the rest of the instrument, enabling adjustments to playing feel, tuning or facilitating compact storage and transport. This subclass falls under the broader category of musical-instrument physics dealing with general design features of wind instruments.
[G10D 7/16] with keyboards -- G10D 7/16 covers wind-type musical instruments that are played via a keyboard, including devices such as pipe organs, harmoniums and accordions, where the keyboard operates valves, reeds or air streams to produce notes. The classification addresses the overall mechanical and ergonomic design of the keyboard-controlled wind-instrument system.
[G10D 9/00] Details of, or accessories for, wind musical instruments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Instrument cleaners.  Lip protectors.  Thumb-rests.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Accordions or concertinas -> G10D11/00
[G10D 9/01] Tuning devices -- G10D 9/01 covers devices that adjust the pitch of stringed or related wind instruments, including pegs, machine heads, fine-tuning mechanisms and other accessories that alter string tension to achieve accurate tuning. It pertains to the design, construction and mounting of such tuning devices.
[G10D 9/02] Mouthpieces -- G10D 9/02 designates mouthpieces, the detachable components that shape the airstream and influence tone and articulation in wind musical instruments. This subclass falls within the CPC hierarchy of Physics → Musical Instruments → Stringed Musical Instruments → Details of, or accessories for, wind musical instruments, and covers design, construction, and variations of mouthpiece forms for diverse wind instruments.
[G10D 9/025] Movable mouthpieces -- Movable mouthpieces are detachable or adjustable components for wind instruments that can be swapped or repositioned to alter tonal or ergonomic properties. The classification includes mechanisms that allow the mouthpiece to be lifted, slid, or otherwise moved while maintaining proper acoustical alignment.
[G10D 9/03] Cupped mouthpieces -- The CPC subclass G10D 9/03 covers the design and features of cupped mouthpieces used in wind musical instruments, detailing their shape, material and acoustic characteristics. It pertains to accessories that affect the way air is directed into the instrument's bore, influencing tone production and playability.
[G10D 9/035] Reeds -- G10D 9/035 classifies reeds, the thin vibrating blades or fipple components that generate sound in woodwind and free-reed instruments, and the accessories that support their installation and adjustment. The code falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification for details of, or accessories for, wind musical instruments within the broader physics and musical instrument domains.
[G10D 9/04] Valves -- G10D 9/04 covers valves used in wind musical instruments, including piston, rotary and double types that change the effective length of the instrument's tubing to produce different pitches. The subclass includes the design, construction, mounting and related accessories of these pitch-changing devices.
[G10D 9/047] for wood wind instruments -- This CPC subclass G10D 9/047 covers inventions relating to accessories, fittings, or detailed components specifically designed for woodwind instruments, such as clarinets, flutes, oboes and saxophones, including mechanisms for tone production, key work, mouthpieces, reeds, or other ancillary parts that improve performance or handling.
[G10D 9/053] with continuous change of tonal pitch -- The G10D 9/053 entry covers accessories or design features for wind instruments that permit a continuous change of tonal pitch, such as sliding mechanisms, variable-length tubing, valve systems or electronic controls that enable glissandi or micro-tonal adjustments during performance.
[G10D 9/06] Mutes -- G10D 9/06 pertains to mutes, devices attached to wind or brass instruments to alter their sound by partially or fully blocking the sound outlet. The classification covers various mute types such as cup, harmon and barrel mutes, and related accessories used to change timbre, volume or pitch.
[G10D 9/08] Material for manufacturing wind musical instruments -- G10D 9/08 covers the materials employed in the construction of wind musical instruments, including metals, wood, plastics, composites and other substances that affect acoustic properties, durability and manufacturing processes. The code is used to classify patents on material selection, treatment and innovative compositions for flutes, clarinets, brass and similar wind instruments.
[G10D 9/10] Resonating bodies, e.g. tubes -- G10D 9/10 covers resonating bodies such as tubes used in musical instruments, including designs that shape or modify the acoustic properties of wind and stringed instruments. The class includes components that act as sound chambers or ducts to enhance resonance.
[G10D 9/11] Bells -- The code G10D 9/11 covers bells and similar resonators used as sound-producing elements in musical contexts, detailing their construction, mounting and associated accessories. It falls within the class for details of or accessories for wind musical instruments, addressing design, materials and attachment methods.
[G10D 99/00] Musical instruments not otherwise provided for -- G10D 99/00 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers musical instruments that do not fit into more specific categories, encompassing unique or less common types of stringed instruments. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office to organize patents for review and search.
[G10F] AUTOMATIC MUSICAL INSTRUMENTS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Automatic musical instruments, e.g., pianofortes, musical boxes, carillons and barrel organs.  Independent players for keyboard instruments.  Details or accessories thereof.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Non-musical aspects of musical toy instruments -> A63H5/00 - Arrangements for the associated working of recording or reproducing apparatus with automatic musical instruments -> G11B31/02
[G10F 1/00] Automatic musical instruments -- G10F 1/00 is the CPC subclass for automatic musical instruments, covering devices that generate sound without direct human playing such as player pianos, mechanical drums, and other self-actuated or programmed music-producing machines, using pneumatic, electronic, or mechanical means. It sits under Physics → Musical Instruments → Automatic Musical Instruments.
[G10F 1/02] Pianofortes with keyboard -- G10F 1/02 covers pianofortes with a keyboard that are designed to play automatically, including self-acting or electro-mechanical pianos where the keyboard mechanism triggers the strings without manual performance. This subclass falls under automatic musical instruments within the physics category of musical instruments.
[G10F 1/04] Pianofortes which have no keyboard -- G10F 1/04 covers piano-type instruments that generate sound without a conventional keyboard, including self-playing or player pianos and other automatically actuated pianofortes. The classification applies to mechanisms that replace manual key operation with mechanical, pneumatic, electronic or other automatic actuation systems. It is situated under physics → musical instruments → automatic musical instruments.
[G10F 1/06] Musical boxes with plucked teeth, blades, or the like -- G10F 1/06 covers automatic musical instruments that generate sound by plucking a series of tuned metal teeth, blades or similar resonators, as found in traditional musical boxes. The devices usually employ a rotating cylinder or disc with pins or projections that engage the teeth to produce a melodic pattern. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of Physics → Musical Instruments → Automatic Musical Instruments.
[G10F 1/08] Percussion musical instruments -- G10F 1/08 covers automatic percussion musical instruments, i.e., devices that produce percussive sounds without manual striking, such as self-playing drums, xylophones, or cymbal rigs that operate by mechanical, pneumatic, or electronic actuation. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of physics → musical instruments → automatic musical instruments.
[G10F 1/10] Carillons -- G10F 1/10 covers carillons, which are sets of tuned bells mounted in a tower and operated automatically to produce melodic sequences, typically driven by a mechanical, pneumatic or electronic control system. They fall under the automatic musical instruments subclass of musical instruments within the physics category.
[G10F 1/12] Wind-actuated instruments -- G10F 1/12 covers musical instruments that generate sound by means of a wind-driven mechanism, such as pipe organs, reed organs and other self-playing devices that use air pressure or flow to vibrate pipes, reeds or other sound-producing elements without manual performance.
[G10F 1/14] Barrel-organs -- G10F 1/14 designates barrel-organs, a type of automatic musical instrument that uses a rotating pinned barrel or drum to trigger pipes and produce pre-programmed tunes. The classification covers the construction, operation and variations of these mechanically driven organs, often employed in street-performance and fair settings.
[G10F 1/16] Stringed musical instruments other than pianofortes -- G10F 1/16 covers automatically played stringed instruments that are non-pianoforte, such as mechanical violins or harps. It falls under the automatic musical instruments subclass of the physics field, describing devices that produce music from strings without human intervention.
[G10F 1/18] to be played by a bow -- G10F 1/18 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to automatic musical instruments that are specifically designed or adapted to be played by a bow, encompassing mechanisms and devices that automate the bowing action typically performed by a musician.
[G10F 1/20] to be plucked -- The G10F 1/20 subclass pertains to automatic musical instruments that generate sound by plucking strings. It is situated within the physics hierarchy under musical instruments and automatic musical instruments.
[G10F 1/22] Combinations of two or more instruments -- The subclass G10F 1/22 covers inventions that join two or more automatic musical instruments into a combined system, allowing them to be played together or coordinated by a common control. Examples include a keyboard that incorporates a built-in drum module or a multi-instrument synthesizer that synchronizes strings and percussion.
[G10F 3/00] Independent players for keyboard instruments -- The code covers devices that generate sound automatically for keyboard instruments, such as self-actuated player pianos, electromechanical hammer-striking mechanisms, and dedicated electronic sound generators that operate without manual key depression.
[G10F 5/00] Details or accessories -- G10F 5/00 covers the design and construction of auxiliary components and specific details for automatic musical instruments, such as mechanisms that drive or control sound production, mounting brackets, protective housings, and other non-primary parts that support the instrument's operation.
[G10F 5/02] Actions -- G10F 5/02 relates to the mechanisms that cause or control the movement of sound-producing elements in automatic musical instruments, such as hammers, reeds, or digital actuators. It covers the design and operation of striking, plucking, blowing or other actions that initiate sound generation.
[G10F 5/04] Tune barrels, sheets, rollers, spools, or the like -- The code G10F 5/04 designates mechanical or electro-mechanical components that hold encoded musical information for self-playing instruments. It includes rotating barrels, perforated paper sheets, punched rollers, spools and similar media that are read to drive the instrument's performance.
[G10F 5/06] Driving or setting of tune barrels, discs, or the like -- The subclass G10F 5/06 covers the mechanisms that drive or position tune-producing components such as barrels, discs, cylinders or similar elements in automatic musical instruments. It includes devices that rotate, advance, or otherwise set these components to select or change the programmed melody.
[G10G] REPRESENTATION OF MUSIC -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Musical aids, e.g., means for the representation of music, chord or note indicators for keyboards or fingerboards, means for transposing or transcribing music.  Recording music in notation form, e.g., recording the mechanical operation of a musical instrument.  Support means for musical instruments.  Other auxiliary devices or accessories, e.g., separate holders for resin or strings, conductor's batons and tuning forks or like devices.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Music stands -> A47B19/00 - Non-musical aspects of musical toy instruments -> A63H5/00 - Metronomes -> G04F5/02 - Teaching music -> G09B15/00
[G10G 1/00] Means for the representation of music -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electronic displays of sheet music.
[G10G 1/02] Chord or note indicators, fixed or adjustable, for keyboard of fingerboards -- The CPC subclass G10G 1/02 covers devices that indicate chords or individual notes on keyboards mounted on fingerboards, such as those of stringed instruments, where the indicators may be fixed or adjustable. These indicators provide visual pitch information to assist performers in playing the correct notes.
[G10G 1/04] Transposing -- The G10G 1/04 subgroup covers means for transposing music, i.e., converting a piece from one key to another. It includes devices, software or methods that alter pitch relationships in written or reproduced music, facilitating performance in different vocal or instrumental ranges.
[G10G 3/00] Recording music in notation form, e.g. recording the mechanical operation of a musical instrument -- G10G 3/00 covers technologies that capture the operation of a musical instrument and automatically generate musical notation. This includes mechanical or electronic systems that transcribe key-strokes, finger movements or sound vibrations into written scores, enabling real-time recording of performance data in notation form.
[G10G 3/02] using mechanical means only -- G10G 3/02 covers techniques for capturing a musical performance in notation using only mechanical devices, such as tracing the physical movements of strings, keys or hammers to generate written music without electronic or digital assistance. It applies to systems that directly translate the mechanical action of an instrument into a visual record of the notes played.
[G10G 3/04] using electrical means -- G10G 3/04 covers methods that employ electrical technologies to capture the performance of a musical instrument in a notated form, such as transforming mechanical actions into an electronic representation that can be stored or displayed as musical notation.
[G10G 5/00] Supports for musical instruments -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cellopins -> G10D3/01 - Mountings for individual drums -> G10D13/28
[G10G 5/005] while playing, e.g. cord, strap or harness  rests or guards as part of the instruments,  see  the particular instruments, e.g.  G10D3/18 -- G10G 5/005 relates to supports for musical instruments that are used while playing, including cords, straps, harnesses, rests or guards that form part of the instrument's structure. The classification covers devices that secure or protect the instrument during performance, as defined in the broader category of representation of music.
[G10G 7/00] Other auxiliary devices or accessories, e.g. conductors' batons or separate holders for resin or strings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Music stands fixed to the musical instrument  Covers for musical instruments  Conductors' batons, majorettes' batons  Means for transporting musical instruments  Humidifiers used within musical instruments  Electronic tuning meters
[G10G 7/005] Carrying cases for musical instruments  interior fittings for luggage  A45C13/02 -- This CPC subclass covers designs and features of carrying cases specifically for musical instruments, including internal fixtures and accessories that protect or support the instrument inside luggage. It also includes interior fittings for travel containers used to transport instruments such as case compartments, padding or holders.
[G10G 7/02] Tuning forks or like devices -- G10G 7/02 covers tuning forks and similar tone-generating devices used as auxiliary accessories for musical instruments. These items produce a precise pitch for tuning, pitch reference, or instructional purposes, often made of metal or other resonant materials.
[G10H] ELECTROPHONIC MUSICAL INSTRUMENTS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Glossary:** - Musical instrument: "tool", "device" , "process" or "protocol" for performing some musical task involving electrophonic signals, e.g. musical parameters - Music: A n art form whose medium is sound and silence. Its common elements are pitch (which governs melody and harmony), rhythm (and its associated concepts tempo, meter, and articulation), dynamics, and the sonic qualities of timbre and texture.Music (including singing, the vocalized form of music) is distinguished from speech by its particular and deliberate emphasis on the above common elements, especially rhythm and melody.By contrast, speech is distinguished from music by its particular and deliberate emphasis on conveying meaning: Speech is the vocalized form of human communication - Musical: Generally employed in a restrictive limiting sense with respect to speech, general audio and sound, i.e. implying an intentional and artistic main focus at least by the performer on at least one of harmony, melody, rhythm, timbre, or expressivity. Even though one person's music may be noise to another, music is a performing art, and musical character is defined by the mere artistic intent of the performer - Musical content: Set of musical parameters. - Musical parameters: Constituent element of "musical content" as defined above. Musical parameters include e.g. pitch, rhythm, timbre, texture, expressivity or dynamics. - MIDI: MIDI stands for Musical Instrument Digital Interface and refers to a note oriented music file and transmission format. Many variations and improvements of this note oriented format exist. The use of the acronym MIDI should be broadly interpreted as also referring to any note oriented format for transmission or recording. - Performance: has three meanings in this field : -in a first meaning, it is an event in which a performer or group of (typically human) performers behave in a particular way (e.g. in their manner of singing or performing music) for another group of people. -in a second meaning it refers to a metric quantifying how well an entity (human, device, or process) deals with a specific aspect of a specific (not necessarily musical, e.g. mflops for a DSP processor in a synthesiser) task- in a third, narrower meaning, it is meant as the strict intersection of the above two meanings, i.e. quantifying the closeness of a performer's performance to a predetermined musical or singing reference i - Karaoke: (translation from the Japanese: "empty orchestra"; synonyms: KTV, Noraebang) Karaoke refers to singing into a microphone by amateur performers along e.g. recorded music or a music video, often with a lyrics display or with performance evaluation - Instrument karaoke: Playing a predetermined melody on a musical instrument or a musical instrument interface, often with score following along with recorded or synthesised accompaniment, often with means for evaluating or scoring the quality of the performance - Rhythm: Regular recurrence or pattern in time, associated concepts: meter, tempo, articulation and beat: - Expressivity: Musical properties which cannot be properly described by notions of harmony, rhythm, pitch, timbre or texture, and which are linked to a particular manner of execution of a musical piece, e.g. indications of mood, e.g. "dolce", or to corresponding note execution parameters such as vibrato or legato, some of which can be coded in communications protocols such as MIDI e.g. expressivity controller. - Polyphony: Ability of a synthesiser to simultaneously generate a limited number of unrelated melodic lines, Polyphony is conventionally quantified as the number of available "voices": a sound-generating device with six voices may be described as being, for example, six-voice polyphonicEach melodic line or simultaneous note requires one resource entity (for example a block of electronic hardware or a time-slot in a Digital Signal Processor program) capable of generating a single tone, and this is what is known as one "voice" - Voice: Has several important meanings in this field :- Resource entity (hardware, time slot) needed to generate a single tone or a single melodic line, in the context of polyphony. The term is generic, and is not meant to imply that the line should necessarily be vocal in character, instead referring to instrumentation or simply to register.This field-specific meaning of "voice" is relevant for G10H1/18 selecting circuits; it is further defined in the definition of "polyphony" and in that of "part" - Sounds generated by vocal chords (e.g. human vocal folds) or synthetic versions thereof, e.g.: - as the medium of speech to communicate meaning; - for artistic musical purposes, e.g. with greater emphasis on melody or rhythm, as in singing, chorus, descant; or - for instrument control purposes (e.g. G10H5/005 voice controlled instruments) - Speech: Definite vocal sounds that form words to express thoughts and ideas - Part: In addition to the usual meaning, a piece of a whole, a part has three more preci se meanings in a musical sense:- A part is a strand or melody of music played by an individual instrument or voice (or group of identical instruments or voices) within a larger work. In the context of polyphonic composition the term voice may be used instead of part to denote a single melodic line or textural layer. This field-specific meaning is very commonly used in connection with MIDI - A part also refers to the separate printed or manuscript copies of the music for each individual instrument in an ensemble or orchestra, as distinct from the score, which holds the music for all the instruments.- A part in great Highland Bagpipe music is a musical strain or sentence. Usually each part consists of four phrases, either one or two bars long. Several sentences combine to produce a paragraph or complete work or tune. - Audio signal: An audio signal is a representation of sound, usually electrical, in analog, digital or coded form, without restriction as to the category of sound being represented, e.g. speech, music, noise, The category of sound being represented, e.g. speech, music or noise, is primarily defined with respect to the features of the audio signal and with respect to the main intent of the source or performer. This category, as defined in this glossary, is very relevant for proper classification
[G10H 1/00] Details of electrophonic musical instruments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of electrophonic musical instruments, electrophonic musical tools, electrophonic musical data or electrophonic musical processing.
[G10H 1/0008] Associated control or indicating means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Producing, processing or displaying musical information, status information or musical parameters, e.g. for information of the user or as control parameters, e.g. for controlling electrophonic musical instruments, indexing or retrieving musical data from musical databases.  Musical analysis of audio or music signals; extraction of musical parameters.  User interfaces for musicians, such as specialised displays.  Control of electrophonic musical instruments: This group is appropriate for classifying control details which are not otherwise provided for in all other groups in G10H1/00 .  Music databases relying on musical parameters which are the result of musical analysis, relate to composing or synthesis, e.g. wavetables or sound banks, include note oriented data, or are otherwise specifically meant for use by a device classified in electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 1/0016] Means for indicating which keys, frets or strings are to be actuated, e.g. using lights or leds -- G10H 1/0016 covers systems that visually signal the performer which keys, frets or strings on an electrophonic instrument should be activated, typically by employing light sources such as LEDs embedded in the instrument's playing surface. The indication can be dynamic, changing in response to control signals or musical input to guide real-time performance.
[G10H 1/0025] Automatic or semi-automatic music composition, e.g. producing random music, applying rules from music theory or modifying a musical piece  automatically producing a series of tones  G10H1/26 -- G10H 1/0025 covers automatic or semi-automatic music composition, including systems that generate random music, apply music-theory rules, or modify an existing piece to produce a series of tones. It falls under the subclass of electrophonic musical instruments within the physics hierarchy.
[G10H 1/0033] Recording/reproducing or transmission of music for electrophonic musical instruments -- G10H 1/0033 pertains to the recording, reproduction and transmission of musical content specifically for use with electrophonic musical instruments, covering methods and devices that store, play back or send music signals to such electronic instruments.
[G10H 1/0041] in coded form -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Musical content recording, reproducing or storage or corresponding data formats or data structures, in coded form e.g. PCM, MP3, ADPCM; also corresponding metadata contents in cases the metadata includes musical parameters (transmission of musical contents G10H1/0058 , wireless transmission G10H1/0083 ).  Musical data structures used for recording, e.g. in musical libraries such as wavetables or song fragments indexed with musical parameters such as tempo, chord, genre, for remix composing applications.
[G10H 1/005] on magnetic tape -- G10H 1/005 covers musical instruments that use magnetic tape to generate, record, or modify sounds. The classification includes tape-based playback mechanisms, tape loops, and tape-driven synthesis employed in electrophonic instruments, addressing the technical features of tape transport, heads and signal processing.
[G10H 1/0058] Transmission between separate instruments or between individual components of a musical system  G10H1/0083  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Modes of transmission or transmission protocols, e.g. MIDI to or from an electrophonic musical instrument.  Any transmission, also when it is not music per se, even if it only represents control data or transmission of network information for electrophonic musical instruments: e.g. latency data transmission for music jamming over the internet (see also G10H2240/175 ("transmission jams")), G10H2240/281 transmission protocols specially used for musical instruments.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Transmission between separate instruments or between individual components of a musical system using wireless transmission, e.g. radio, light, infrared -> G10H1/0083
[G10H 1/0066] using a MIDI interface -- A MIDI interface is employed to transmit musical performance data between an electrophonic instrument and external devices, enabling digital control, sound generation, and synchronization with other equipment. This subclass covers systems and methods that use MIDI signals for input, output, or interconnection of such instruments. It includes hardware and software arrangements that facilitate MIDI-based communication and control of electronic musical devices.
[G10H 1/0075] with translation or conversion means for unvailable commands, e.g. special tone colors -- G10H 1/0075 covers electrophonic musical instruments that include translation or conversion mechanisms to handle unavailable or non-standard commands, producing special tone colors or timbral variations. It addresses devices that map atypical control inputs into distinct sound-shaping functions.
[G10H 1/0083] using wireless transmission, e.g. radio, light, infrared -- G10H 1/0083 pertains to electrophonic musical instruments that utilize wireless transmission methods such as radio, light, or infrared for signal transfer between components. This classification covers the technical aspects of wireless communication within these instruments to facilitate performance and connectivity without physical wiring.
[G10H 1/0091] Means for obtaining special acoustic effects  combined with modulation  G10H1/043 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Musical effects not otherwise provided for, e.g. DJ scratch effects.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Means for controlling the tone frequencies, e.g. attack, decay; Means for producing special musical effects, e.g. vibrato, glissando -> G10H1/02 - Means for controlling the tone frequencies by additional continuous modulation -> G10H1/043 - Circuits for establishing the harmonic content of tones, by combining tones, for obtaining chorus, celeste or ensemble effects -> G10H1/10 - Means for processing the signal picked up from the strings, for distorting the signal, e.g. to simulate tube amplifiers -> G10H3/187 - Aspects of games using an electronically generated display having two or more dimensions, e.g. 3D sound effects in virtual videogame spaces -> A63F13/00 - Arrangements for producing a reverberation or echo sound -> G10K15/08
[G10H 1/02] Means for controlling the tone frequencies, e.g. attack or decay -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The time dependent control of:  The control of tone colour modulation of musical signal (e.g. spectral contents, timbre variation, filtering).  **Glossary:** - ADSR: denotes a form of envelope used for synthesizing a tone which is split into four time segments: Attack, Decay, Sustain, Release. An ADSR envelope is defined by an attack time, decay time, sustain level and release time - Attack time: is the time taken for initial run-up of level from nil to peak, beginning when the key is first pressed - Decay time: is the time taken for the subsequent run down from the attack level to the designated sustain level - Sustain level: is the level during the main sequence of the sound's duration, until the key is released - Release time: is the time taken for the level to decay from the sustain level to zero after the key is released
[G10H 1/04] by additional modulation -- G10H 1/04 covers electrophonic musical instruments that incorporate additional electronic modulation of the generated sound, such as pitch-shifting, tremolo, vibrato, or other waveform-altering effects applied after the primary tone generation. It includes circuits or controls that modify timbre, dynamics, or frequency in real time.
[G10H 1/043] Continuous modulation -- Continuous modulation refers to methods for varying one or more sound parameters of an electrophonic musical instrument in a smooth, uninterrupted manner, such as continuously changing pitch, amplitude, filter settings or timbre via control voltages, envelopes or digital signals, enabling expressive real-time shaping of tones.
[G10H 1/045] by electromechanical means -- The G10H 1/045 subclass covers electrophonic musical instruments that generate or modify sound by electromechanical means, where mechanical motion is transformed into electrical signals or electrical energy drives mechanical sound-producing elements. Examples include electric guitars with pickups, amplifiers with electromechanical transducers, and keyboards employing motor-driven tone generators.
[G10H 1/047] by acousto-mechanical means, e.g. rotating speakers or sound deflectors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Continuous modulation by acousto-mechanical means.  Electronic or computer simulations of the effect of such acousto-mechanical means, e.g. Leslie effect.
[G10H 1/053] during execution only -- G10H 1/053 relates to electrophonic musical instruments and covers aspects that are relevant only while the device is being played, such as real-time control of sound generation, performance-time adjustments, and other functions that occur during execution, without addressing construction, storage or post-play features.
[G10H 1/0535] by switches incorporating a mechanical vibrator, the envelope of the mechanical vibration being used as modulating signal -- G10H 1/0535 classifies electrophonic musical instruments that employ switches containing a mechanical vibrator, where the vibration's envelope is used as the modulating signal to control sound generation. This falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments, describing a method of converting mechanical vibration characteristics into electronic modulation.
[G10H 1/055] by switches with variable impedance elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electric or mechanical switches or analogue control elements with variable impedance for controlling electrophonic musical instruments or computer music interfaces.  Analogue variable impedance elements, e.g. strain gauge, potentiometer, variable inductor, as used in electrophonic musical instruments, regardless of its control effects.  Indexing Codes G10H2220/275 (input key switch) and G10H2220/561 (transducer resistor) represent additional aspects which should be considered for finer classification.
[G10H 1/0551] using variable capacitors -- G10H 1/0551 covers electrophonic musical instruments that incorporate variable capacitors to adjust electrical parameters such as frequency, resonance or tonal characteristics. The classification applies to devices where the capacitor's capacitance is changed during operation to control pitch, timbre or signal processing.
[G10H 1/0553] using optical or light-responsive means -- The G10H 1/0553 subclass covers electrophonic musical instruments that employ optical or light-responsive means, such as photo-electric sensors, light-activated switches, or illumination-controlled sound modulation. It addresses how light-sensitive components are used to generate, modify, or control musical output.
[G10H 1/0555] using magnetic or electromagnetic means -- G10H 1/0555 pertains to electrophonic musical instruments that use magnetic or electromagnetic techniques, including the generation, modulation, or amplification of sound through magnetic fields, electromagnetic pickups, or transducer drive mechanisms, and is a sub-category of detailed aspects of such instruments within the physics-musical-instrument hierarchy.
[G10H 1/0556] using piezoelectric means -- The G10H 1/0556 entry classifies electrophonic musical instruments that employ piezoelectric means to generate or modify sound, such as piezo-generated vibrations used for tone production, pickup, or acoustic enhancement in electronic keyboards, guitars and similar devices.
[G10H 1/0558] using variable resistors -- G10H 1/0558 classifies inventions that use variable resistors, such as potentiometers or digital equivalents, to alter electrical parameters that affect sound generation, modulation, or control in electrophonic musical instruments. The entry covers circuits where resistance adjustment shapes tone, volume, pitch or other acoustic characteristics within electronic or electro-acoustic devices.
[G10H 1/057] by envelope-forming circuits -- G10H 1/057 classifies electrophonic musical instruments that employ envelope-forming circuits to shape the amplitude envelope of the generated sound, such as synthesizers or electronic effects that control attack, decay, sustain, and release parameters of audio signals. This entry falls under the physics-based hierarchy for musical instrument technology.
[G10H 1/0575] using a data store from which the envelope is synthesized  tones synthesized from a data store  G10H7/00 -- G10H 1/0575 covers electrophonic musical instruments that generate tones by retrieving envelope data from a stored data set, using that information to shape the sound's amplitude over time. The classification applies to devices where the envelope of a synthesized tone is derived directly from predefined digital or analog data.
