[G10H 1/06] Circuits for establishing the harmonic content of tones -- This subclass covers electronic circuits that control or generate the harmonic makeup of musical tones, such as filters, resonators, additive or subtractive synthesis networks, and related tone-shaping circuitry used in electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 1/08] by combining tones -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Circuits for establishing the harmonic content of tones during execution -> G10H1/14 - Circuits for establishing the harmonic content of tones by non-linear elements -> G10H1/16 - Chord -> G10H1/38
[G10H 1/10] for obtaining chorus, celeste or ensemble effects -- G10H 1/10 covers technologies that add chorus, celeste or ensemble effects to electrophonic musical instruments such as electronic keyboards or synthesizers. The classification includes methods and apparatus that create multiple slightly-detuned voices or simulated instrument ensembles to enrich timbre.
[G10H 1/12] by filtering complex waveforms -- G10H 1/12 pertains to electrophonic musical instruments that manipulate complex audio signals by means of filtering. It includes techniques and circuitry that shape waveforms-such as equalization, spectral shaping or other signal-processing filters-to alter timbre or tone before the sound is reproduced. This subclass falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 1/125] using a digital filter -- G10H 1/125 relates to the use of digital filters in electrophonic musical instruments, describing how digital signal-processing techniques are applied to modify or shape audio output. It covers filtering for tone control, noise reduction, and sound-effect generation in electronic keyboards, synthesizers and similar devices.
[G10H 1/14] during execution -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Voice controlled instruments -> G10H5/005
[G10H 1/16] by non-linear elements -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Circuits for establising the harmonic content of tones during execution -> G10H1/14
[G10H 1/18] Selecting circuits -- G10H 1/18 covers selecting circuits used in electrophonic musical instruments, such as electronic switches, multiplexers or routing networks that choose between different sound sources, timbres or signal paths. The classification addresses circuit designs that enable the performer or controller to select specific audio outputs or effects within an instrument's electronic architecture.
[G10H 1/181] Suppression of switching-noise -- G10H 1/181 relates to methods and apparatus for reducing the transient noise that occurs when electronic musical-instrument circuits switch, such as clicks or pops heard during note changes. It includes filtering, smoothing, timing or other techniques that suppress switching-noise in synthesizers, digital keyboards and similar electrophonic instruments.
[G10H 1/182] Key multiplexing  G10H1/185  takes precedence -- G10H 1/182 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to key multiplexing in electrophonic musical instruments, a technique for efficiently managing multiple keys or switches. This entry is subordinate to broader categories on musical instruments and electrophonic instrument details, with G10H 1/185 taking precedence when applicable.
[G10H 1/183] Channel-assigning means for polyphonic instruments -- G10H 1/183 relates to channel-assigning means for polyphonic electronic instruments, defining mechanisms that direct individual notes or voices to separate output channels or speakers, thus allowing independent routing of multiple sound streams within a single instrument.
[G10H 1/185] associated with key multiplexing -- G10H 1/185 covers electrophonic musical instruments that employ key multiplexing, a technique in which the states of many keys are detected through a reduced set of electronic connections, enabling compact and cost-effective keyboard scanning.
[G10H 1/186] Microprocessor-controlled keyboard and assigning means -- The entry G10H 1/186 defines microprocessor-controlled keyboards that incorporate electronic means for assigning pitches or other sound parameters to each key press. It covers instruments where a microprocessor scans the keys, allocates voices and manages control functions for sound generation.
[G10H 1/187] using multiplexed channel processors  G10H1/186  takes precedence -- This subclass covers electrophonic musical instruments that employ multiplexed channel processors to handle multiple audio or control signals simultaneously, enabling efficient routing and processing of sound streams in a single device. It refines G10H 1/186, which takes precedence for broader multiplexing arrangements.
[G10H 1/188] with means to assign more than one channel to any single key -- G10H 1/188 covers electrophonic musical instruments that provide a way to route a single key's signal to more than one audio channel, such as stereo or multitrack outputs. This feature allows each key to generate multiple channel assignments, enabling richer sound layering or spatial effects in electronic keyboards and similar devices.
[G10H 1/20] for transposition -- G10H 1/20 covers features of electrophonic musical instruments that allow the musical pitch to be transposed, such as electronic keyboards or digital sound generators with pitch-shift functions, transposition controls, or circuitry that changes the output frequency to a different key.
[G10H 1/22] for suppressing tones -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Selecting which notes or voices to suppress from polyphonic music, e.g. to alleviate the effects of insufficient hardware capabilities or to save processing power.  Also covers deliberately simplifying polyphony or melody, suppressing notes for correcting errors in music signal transmission (e.g. frozen notes due to a missing note-off command).
[G10H 1/24] for selecting plural preset register stops -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details specifically dealing with relevant aspects of selection of different tone colours or instrument voices, e.g. piano, violin, trumpet.
[G10H 1/26] for automatically producing a series of tones -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Automatically producing a predetermined and unchangeable sequence of musical tones upon initial triggering, specifically dealing with musical parameters.  Circuits for musical cards or the like, algorithmically producing a pre-programmed, unchangeable melody, e.g. from a coded sequence of tones in a ROM.
[G10H 1/28] to produce arpeggios -- The G10H 1/28 subclass refers to features of electrophonic musical instruments that automatically generate arpeggiated sequences of notes. It includes electronic keyboards, guitar synthesizers, and similar devices that produce arpeggios as part of their performance functionality.
[G10H 1/30] to reiteratively sound two tones -- G10H 1/30 classifies features of electrophonic musical instruments that generate two tones in a repeated or alternating pattern, such as devices that cycle between two pitches or produce simultaneous dual tones in a rhythmic sequence. This subclass falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments, covering mechanisms and control methods for iteratively sounding two tones.
[G10H 1/32] Constructional details -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanical details of electrophonic musical instruments, where such mechanical details are not otherwise provided for.  This includes for example:
[G10H 1/34] Switch arrangements, e.g. keyboards or mechanical switches specially adapted for electrophonic musical instruments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional details at keyboard level or key level, mechanisms linked to individual keys or keyboards.  Key-like user input controls for electrophonic musical instruments, e.g. pedals, touchscreen active zones, not only including mechanical switches with contacts, but also switches in a generalised sense, e.g. light barriers, even with continuously varying output.
[G10H 1/342] for guitar-like instruments with or without strings and with a neck on which switches or string-fret contacts are used to detect the notes being played  electric guitars in which the tones are generated by the vibration of strings  G10H3/18 -- This CPC subclass relates to electric guitars that have a neck equipped with switches or string-fret contacts which detect the notes being played, and where the tones are produced by the vibration of the strings.
[G10H 1/344] Structural association with individual keys  electrically operated wind-actuated organs  G10B3/22 -- G10H 1/344 pertains to electrically operated, wind-driven organs where each individual key is structurally connected to a specific pipe or sound-producing element, allowing a key press to directly control the airflow to that particular pipe.
[G10H 1/346] Keys with an arrangement for simulating the feeling of a piano key, e.g. using counterweights, springs, cams -- G10H 1/346 pertains to electrophonic musical instruments and defines key mechanisms that mimic the tactile feel of a piano key by employing counterweights, springs, cams or similar devices to provide realistic resistance and motion.
[G10H 1/348] Switches actuated by parts of the body other than fingers -- The G10H 1/348 subclass covers switches in electrophonic musical instruments that are triggered by body parts other than the fingers, such as the palm, wrist, forearm, foot, head, or mouth. It includes designs where these alternative actuators control sound generation or instrument functions without manual finger contact.
[G10H 1/36] Accompaniment arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Accompaniment systems, e.g. karaoke.  G10H1/361 also includes instrument karaoke, in which the performer does not sing to recorded music but is expected to play a specific melody on an instrument in synchrony with recorded music.
[G10H 1/361] Recording/reproducing of accompaniment for use with an external source, e.g. karaoke systems -- G10H 1/361 relates to the recording and reproduction of accompaniment tracks that are meant to be combined with an external audio source, such as karaoke systems. It falls under electrophonic musical instruments dealing with backing-music generation for live performance.
[G10H 1/363] using optical disks, e.g. CD, CD-ROM, to store accompaniment information in digital form -- G10H 1/363 covers electrophonic musical instruments that store accompaniment data on optical discs such as CDs or CD-ROMs. The entry pertains to methods of recording, retrieving or playing digital backing tracks from these media to accompany live performance.
[G10H 1/365] the accompaniment information being stored on a host computer and transmitted to a reproducing terminal by means of a network, e.g. public telephone lines -- The entry covers systems where background or accompaniment data for an electrophonic musical instrument is kept on a central host computer and delivered to a playback device via a communications network such as telephone lines, enabling the terminal to reproduce the accompaniment in sync with the performer.
[G10H 1/366] with means for modifying or correcting the external signal, e.g. pitch correction, reverberation, changing a singer's voice -- G10H 1/366 pertains to electrophonic musical instruments equipped with features that alter or refine an external audio input, such as pitch correction, adding reverberation effects, or transforming vocal characteristics.
[G10H 1/368] displaying animated or moving pictures synchronized with the music or audio part -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Features specific to synchronisation of musical parameters to moving images, musical accompaniment of slide shows, background music dependence on videogame environment or videogame character actions.  Features specific to karaoke synchronized with animated pictures (karaoke lyrics G10H1/0008 , G10H2220/011 display lyrics).  Musical games where user actions on musical parameters are expected to be synchronized to music and video, e.g. rhythmic hopscotch type games such as Dance Revolution.  Generation of artistic images related to music parameters (informative musical displays G10H1/0008 ).
[G10H 1/38] Chord -- The G10H 1/38 entry pertains to chords as they are produced, processed or displayed by electrophonic musical instruments, covering methods for generating simultaneous notes, chord-related signal handling and user interfaces that allow selection or manipulation of chord structures.
[G10H 1/383] Chord detection and/or recognition, e.g. for correction, or automatic bass generation -- G10H 1/383 covers inventions that detect or recognise musical chords, such as algorithms that identify chord symbols or pitch classes to enable automatic correction of performances or generation of accompanying bass lines. It applies to both hardware and software implementations in electrophonic instruments.
[G10H 1/386] One-finger or one-key chord systems -- G10H 1/386 relates to chord-generation mechanisms that let a player produce full chords by depressing a single finger or key. It includes electronic or digital instruments that use preset chord-selection circuits, software, or memory-based systems to trigger multiple notes from one input, simplifying chord playing on keyboards, synthesizers and similar electrophonic devices.
[G10H 1/40] Rhythm -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Analysis of rhythmic information such as tempo, timing, e.g. of onsets, beat.  Processing of rhythmic information for processing music, such as selecting music from a database, music composition.  Generation of rhythmic information for use in electrophonic musical instruments: e.g. timing control, timing processing, timing classification, timing synchronisation, timing encoding of musical data, synthesis of rhythmic information.  Display of rhythmic information in music such as tempo, timing, beat, onsets.
[G10H 1/42] comprising tone forming circuits -- This subclass covers electrophonic musical instruments that use tone-forming circuits to generate sound, such as synthesizers, oscillators and other electronic tone generators. It is placed within the physics hierarchy under musical instruments and electrophonic devices.
[G10H 1/44] Tuning means -- G10H 1/44 covers the means for tuning electrophonic musical instruments, i.e., any device or method that adjusts the pitch of an electronically generated sound. This includes variable-frequency oscillators, pitch-control knobs, digital tuning algorithms, and other mechanisms that allow a user to set or modify musical pitch.
[G10H 1/46] Volume control -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Volume control specifically provided in electrophonic musical instruments: e.g. MIDI volume control, MIDI velocity controller, volume control for electric guitars, for musical keyboards.
[G10H 2210/00] Aspects or methods of musical processing having intrinsic musical character, i.e. involving musical theory or musical parameters or relying on musical knowledge, as applied in electrophonic musical tools or instruments -- This CPC subgroup covers techniques for processing music that embed musical theory, parameters or knowledge-such as pitch, harmony, rhythm or timbre-into the operation of electronic musical instruments and tools. It includes methods that apply intrinsically musical concepts in synthesis, transformation, analysis or other signal-processing functions of electrophonic devices.
[G10H 2210/005] Musical accompaniment, i.e. complete instrumental rhythm synthesis added to a performed melody, e.g. as output by drum machines -- This CPC subclass deals with the creation of a full instrumental rhythm track that accompanies a performed melody, such as the output of drum machines. It covers methods and aspects of musical processing with intrinsic musical character, applied to electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/011] Fill-in added to normal accompaniment pattern -- G10H 2210/011 covers the addition of fill-in material to a standard accompaniment pattern in electrophonic musical instruments. It pertains to methods that modify or embellish a pre-existing accompaniment by inserting rhythmic, melodic or harmonic elements, relying on musical theory and parameters to enhance the musical output.
[G10H 2210/015] Accompaniment break, i.e. interrupting then restarting -- The CPC entry G10H 2210/015 falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments and concerns methods of musical processing that rely on musical theory. It specifically covers the technique of an accompaniment break, where an accompanying part is temporarily halted and then resumed during performance with electrophonic instruments.
[G10H 2210/021] Background music, e.g. for video sequences or elevator music -- This subclass deals with providing background music, such as a soundtrack for video clips or ambient elevator music, using electronic musical instruments or devices. It includes methods of generating, selecting, or controlling such music in electrophonic contexts.
[G10H 2210/026] for games, e.g. videogames -- This CPC subclass (G10H 2210/026) covers methods of musical processing that possess an intrinsic musical character-such as theory, parameters or musical knowledge-when applied in electrophonic musical instruments for games, including video games. It is situated under Physics → Musical Instruments → Electrophonic Musical Instruments.
[G10H 2210/031] Musical analysis, i.e. isolation, extraction or identification of musical elements or musical parameters from a raw acoustic signal or from an encoded audio signal -- The G10H 2210/031 subclass covers techniques that isolate, extract or identify musical elements or parameters-such as pitch, rhythm, timbre or harmonic content-from raw acoustic signals or encoded audio files. These methods are applied to electrophonic musical instruments and tools, linking physics-based signal processing with musical theory.
[G10H 2210/036] of musical genre, i.e. analysing the style of musical pieces, usually for selection, filtering or classification -- G10H 2210/036 pertains to methods and aspects in electrophonic musical instruments that analyze the musical genre or style of pieces, primarily for purposes such as selection, filtering, or classification. This involves applying musical theory and knowledge to process music electronically.
[G10H 2210/041] based on mfcc [mel -frequency spectral coefficients] -- G10H 2210/041 covers techniques that use mel-frequency cepstral coefficients for analyzing, synthesizing or transforming musical signals in electro-phonic instruments. It relates to musical-theory-based processing such as timbre classification, pitch detection or sound synthesis.
[G10H 2210/046] for differentiation between music and non-music signals, based on the identification of musical parameters, e.g. based on tempo detection -- The code G10H 2210/046 designates techniques for separating music from non-music audio by extracting musical parameters, for example tempo detection. It applies musical-theory based analysis to electrophonic musical tools and instruments, fitting within the physics-musical-instrument hierarchy of the CPC.
[G10H 2210/051] for extraction or detection of onsets of musical sounds or notes, i.e. note attack timings -- The entry G10H 2210/051 covers techniques for extracting or detecting the onset of musical sounds or notes, specifically the timing of note attacks, in electrophonic musical instruments. It relates to processing methods that rely on musical theory or parameters.
[G10H 2210/056] for extraction or identification of individual instrumental parts, e.g. melody, chords, bass -- This CPC subclass G10H 2210/056 relates to methods for extracting or identifying individual instrumental parts such as melody, chords or bass from electronic musical signals. The classification falls under physics > musical instruments > electrophonic musical instruments and concerns processing that uses musical theory or parameters.
[G10H 2210/061] for extraction of musical phrases, isolation of musically relevant segments, e.g. musical thumbnail generation, or for temporal structure analysis of a musical piece, e.g. determination of the movement sequence of a musical work -- This subclass covers techniques for extracting musical phrases or isolating musically relevant segments from an electrophonic instrument's output, such as generating musical thumbnails, and for analyzing the temporal structure of a piece, for example determining the sequence of movements in a work.
[G10H 2210/066] for pitch analysis as part of wider processing for musical purposes, e.g. transcription, musical performance evaluation -- The subclass G10H 2210/066 covers methods that extract and analyse pitch as part of broader musical-signal processing, such as automatic transcription or evaluation of musical performance, using musical theory or parameters in electrophonic instruments.
[G10H 2210/071] for rhythm pattern analysis or rhythm style recognition -- A method for analysing rhythm patterns or recognising rhythm styles applied to electrophonic musical instruments, covering techniques that use musical theory, parameters or knowledge to process rhythmic data within electronic or digital music tools. This falls under the physics branch for musical instruments, specifically electro-phonic devices.
[G10H 2210/076] for extraction of timing, tempo -- G10H 2210/076 covers techniques used in electronic musical instruments to analyse and retrieve timing or tempo information from a musical signal. It relates to processing methods that rely on musical theory or parameters to determine the rhythmic structure of a performance.
[G10H 2210/081] for automatic key or tonality recognition, e.g. using musical rules or a knowledge base -- This subclass pertains to methods and systems that automatically identify the musical key or tonality of a piece, typically using rule-based algorithms or a knowledge base, within electro-phonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/086] for transcription of raw audio or music data to a displayed or printed staff representation or to displayable MIDI-like note-oriented data, e.g. in pianoroll format -- This classification covers methods that convert raw audio or music signals into a visual staff notation, printed sheet music, or into displayable note-oriented data such as MIDI or pianoroll formats. It relates to musical processing that relies on musical theory and parameters within electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/091] for performance evaluation, i.e. judging, grading or scoring the musical qualities or faithfulness of a performance, e.g. with respect to pitch, tempo or other timings of a reference performance -- The code G10H 2210/091 covers methods and systems for evaluating the quality of a musical performance, such as judging, grading or scoring its pitch, tempo, timing and other musical parameters against a reference. It applies to electrophonic musical instruments and tools, linking musical theory with performance assessment.
[G10H 2210/095] Inter-note articulation aspects, e.g. legato or staccato -- This CPC subclass under G10H 2210/095 deals with aspects of inter-note articulation such as legato and staccato in electrophonic musical instruments, covering methods that modify note transitions using musical theory. It falls within physics, musical instruments, and electrophonic musical instruments, focusing on intrinsic musical processing.
[G10H 2210/101] Music Composition or musical creation -- G10H 2210/101 covers methods and aspects of music composition or musical creation that involve musical theory, parameters or knowledge, as applied to electrophonic musical instruments. It falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments and addresses intrinsically musical processing.
[G10H 2210/105] Composing aid, e.g. for supporting creation, edition or modification of a piece of music -- G10H 2210/105 classifies composing aids that assist in the creation, editing or modification of a musical piece by applying musical theory, parameters or knowledge within electrophonic instruments or software. It resides in the physics-musical instruments-electrophonic musical instruments branch of the CPC.
[G10H 2210/111] Automatic composing, i.e. using predefined musical rules -- The subclass G10H 2210/111 relates to methods that automatically generate music by applying predefined compositional rules within electrophonic musical instruments or software, using musical theory, parameters or knowledge to create musical output. It falls under the broader categories of physics, musical instruments and electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/115] using a random process to generate a musical note, phrase, sequence or structure -- This subclass deals with methods that employ stochastic or random techniques to create musical notes, phrases, sequences or structures in electrophonic instruments. It covers generation of musical material using probability, random number generators or other nondeterministic processes as part of musical processing.
[G10H 2210/121] using a knowledge base -- This subclass covers methods in electrophonic musical instruments that employ a knowledge base-such as a database of musical rules, styles or parameters-to influence or control sound generation, processing or performance, relying on musical theory or parameters. It includes systems that retrieve, analyse or apply stored musical information to shape the musical output.
[G10H 2210/125] Medley, i.e. linking parts of different musical pieces in one single piece, e.g. sound collage, DJ mix -- G10H 2210/125 covers the creation of medleys, where sections of distinct musical works are combined into a single continuous piece, as in sound collages or DJ mixes. It falls under the CPC group for electrophonic musical instruments, addressing processing methods that rely on musical theory and parameters.
[G10H 2210/131] Morphing, i.e. transformation of a musical piece into a new different one, e.g. remix -- The CPC subclass G10H 2210/131 covers methods and aspects of musical processing that involve intrinsic musical content, specifically the morphing of a musical piece into a different version such as a remix, using transformation techniques in electophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/136] Morphing interpolation, i.e. interpolating in pitch, harmony or time, tempo or rhythm, between two different musical pieces, e.g. to produce a new musical work -- G10H 2210/136 pertains to morphing interpolation in electrophonic musical instruments, where pitch, harmony, time, tempo or rhythm are interpolated between two distinct musical pieces to generate a new work. It falls under aspects of musical processing that rely on musical theory and parameters.
[G10H 2210/141] Riff, i.e. improvisation, e.g. repeated motif or phrase, automatically added to a piece, e.g. in real time -- The CPC class G10H 2210/141 concerns methods that automatically generate or insert riffs-improvised repeated motifs or phrases-in electrophonic musical instruments, typically in real-time performance.
[G10H 2210/145] Composing rules, e.g. harmonic or musical rules, for use in automatic composition -- This subclass covers methods that define musical or harmonic rule sets for use in computer-based or electronic composition systems, enabling automatic generation of music according to specified theory or stylistic parameters. It belongs to the CPC group for electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/151] using templates, i.e. incomplete musical sections, as a basis for composing -- This class relates to methods that employ pre-made template fragments such as incomplete melodic or harmonic passages to generate new music on electronic or electrophonic instruments. It covers compositional techniques that rely on musical theory and parameters, using these templates as starting material.
[G10H 2210/155] Musical effects -- G10H 2210/155 covers musical effects, i.e., methods and processes that manipulate sound parameters such as pitch, timbre, dynamics or spatialization based on musical theory and knowledge when used in electrophonic musical instruments. It falls under the physics-musical instruments-electrophonic instruments branch of the CPC.
[G10H 2210/161] Note sequence effects, i.e. sensing, altering, controlling, processing or synthesising a note trigger selection or sequence, e.g. by altering trigger timing, triggered note values, adding improvisation or ornaments or also rapid repetition of the same note onset -- This class covers techniques for sensing, altering, controlling, processing or synthesising note-trigger sequences in electrophonic musical instruments, including changes to timing, note values, ornaments, improvisation or rapid repetition of a single note onset, all based on musical-theory parameters.
[G10H 2210/165] Humanizing effects, i.e. causing a performance to sound less machine-like, e.g. by slightly randomising pitch or tempo -- This CPC subgroup covers techniques that make synthesized or computer-generated performances sound more natural by introducing subtle variations in pitch, timing or other musical parameters. Methods such as randomising tempo or applying micro-tuning to reduce a mechanistic feel are included. It falls under electrophonic musical instruments in the physics hierarchy.
[G10H 2210/171] Ad-lib effects, i.e. adding a musical phrase or improvisation automatically or on player's request, e.g. one-finger triggering of a note sequence -- G10H 2210/171 covers automatic or player-initiated ad-lib effects in electrophonic musical instruments, where a short musical phrase or improvisation is generated by the instrument. This includes one-finger triggering of preset note sequences or other methods that add spontaneous musical material in response to a player's request.
[G10H 2210/175] Fillnote, i.e. adding isolated notes or passing notes to the melody -- The entry G10H 2210/175 covers methods for adding isolated or passing notes-known as fillnotes-to a melody, a musical-processing technique that relies on theory and musical parameters. It applies to electrophonic musical instruments, encompassing any processing that inserts these decorative notes within a melodic line.
[G10H 2210/181] Gracenote, i.e. adding a different and very short ornamental note at the beginning or at the end of a melody note, e.g. appoggiatura, acciaccatura, sparsh-swar -- The G10H 2210/181 entry concerns techniques for inserting a very brief ornamental note-commonly called a gracenote, such as an appoggiatura, acciaccatura or sparsh-swar-at the beginning or end of a melody note in electrophonic musical instruments, using musical-theoretic parameters to modify pitch or timing.
[G10H 2210/185] Arpeggio, i.e. notes played or sung in rapid sequence, one after the other, rather than ringing out simultaneously, e.g. as a chord -- This CPC subclass covers arpeggios, the rapid sequential playing or singing of individual notes of a chord rather than sounding them together, as a musical-processing technique applied to electrophonic musical instruments. It falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/191] Tremolo, tremulando, trill or mordent effects, i.e. repeatedly alternating stepwise in pitch between two note pitches or chords, without any portamento between the two notes -- G10H 2210/191 classifies methods for producing tremolo, tremulando, trill or mordent effects in electrophonic musical instruments, where the pitch repeatedly alternates stepwise between two notes or chords without a gliding transition. It falls under the physics-based category for musical instruments, covering musical-theory-driven processing applied to electronic sound generation.
[G10H 2210/195] Modulation effects, i.e. smooth non-discontinuous variations over a time interval, e.g. within a note, melody or musical transition, of any sound parameter, e.g. amplitude, pitch, spectral response or playback speed -- The G10H 2210/195 class covers modulation effects applied to electrophonic musical instruments, defined as smooth, continuous variations over time of any sound parameter such as amplitude, pitch, spectral content or playback speed. These effects occur within a note, melodic line or musical transition and rely on musical theory or knowledge to shape the evolving sound.
[G10H 2210/201] Vibrato, i.e. rapid, repetitive and smooth variation of amplitude, pitch or timbre within a note or chord -- G10H 2210/201 covers vibrato-rapid, repetitive, smooth modulation of amplitude, pitch or timbre within a note or chord-when implemented in electrophonic musical instruments. It falls under the physics-musical instruments hierarchy, specifically the subclass for musical-theory-based processing in electronic sound tools.
[G10H 2210/205] Amplitude vibrato, i.e. repetitive smooth loudness variation without pitch change or rapid repetition of the same note, bisbigliando, amplitude tremolo or tremulants -- This classification covers techniques that modulate a sound's loudness in a smooth, repetitive way without changing pitch, such as amplitude vibrato, bisbigliando, amplitude tremolo and tremulants, when used in electrophonic musical instruments. It is indexed under CPC code G10H 2210/205 within physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/211] Pitch vibrato, i.e. repetitive and smooth variation in pitch, e.g. as obtainable with a whammy bar or tremolo arm on a guitar -- This CPC subclass (G10H 2210/211) concerns pitch vibrato, a repetitive smooth variation in pitch that can be produced on electrophonic instruments such as a guitar using a whammy bar or tremolo arm. It falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments and addresses musical-theoretic processing of pitch.
[G10H 2210/215] Rotating vibrato, i.e. simulating rotating speakers, e.g. Leslie effect -- This code covers techniques for producing a rotating vibrato effect, such as the Leslie speaker simulation, applied to electrophonic musical instruments. It relates to musical processing methods that involve musical theory or parameters and is classified under physics > musical instruments > electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/221] Glissando, i.e. pitch smoothly sliding from one note to another, e.g. gliss, glide, slide, bend, smear or sweep -- It covers methods and aspects of musical processing that involve a continuous pitch transition, known as glissando, in electronic or electro-acoustic instruments. It includes techniques such as slides, bends, sweeps, and smears used to vary pitch smoothly between notes.
[G10H 2210/225] Portamento, i.e. smooth continuously variable pitch-bend, without emphasis of each chromatic pitch during the pitch change, which only stops at the end of the pitch shift, as obtained, e.g. by a MIDI pitch wheel or trombone -- The entry concerns portamento, a smooth continuously variable pitch-bend that does not emphasize individual chromatic pitches during the shift and stops only at the final pitch. It applies to electrophonic musical instruments and tools such as MIDI pitch wheels or trombones, classifying the method of musical processing within the physics-musical instruments hierarchy.
[G10H 2210/231] Wah-wah spectral modulation, i.e. tone color spectral glide obtained by sweeping the peak of a bandpass filter up or down in frequency, e.g. according to the position of a pedal, by automatic modulation or by voice formant detection -- This subclass covers techniques for creating a wah-wah effect by continuously shifting the center frequency of a band-pass filter, producing a glide in timbre. The modulation may be controlled manually with a pedal, generated automatically, or triggered by detecting vocal formants. Used in electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/235] Flanging or phasing effects, i.e. creating time and frequency dependent constructive and destructive interferences, obtained, e.g. by using swept comb filters or a feedback loop around all-pass filters with gradually changing non-linear phase response or delays -- This subclass covers techniques that produce flanging or phasing effects in electrophonic musical instruments, generated by time- and frequency-varying constructive and destructive interference. The effects are realised with swept-comb filters, feedback loops around all-pass filters, or variable-delay networks that create a gradually changing non-linear phase response.
[G10H 2210/241] Scratch effects, i.e. emulating playback velocity or pitch manipulation effects normally obtained by a disc-jockey manually rotating a LP record forward and backward -- G10H 2210/241 covers scratch effects, which emulate the forward-or-backward movement of a vinyl record to alter playback velocity or pitch, as used in electrophonic musical instruments such as DJ controllers. The code falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments and pertains to musical-theory-based processing methods.
[G10H 2210/245] Ensemble, i.e. adding one or more voices, also instrumental voices -- G10H 2210/245 covers methods and apparatus for creating an ensemble effect in electrophonic musical instruments, that is, adding one or more additional voices, including instrumental voices, to a sound source. It applies to processing techniques that rely on musical theory or parameters to generate harmonized or layered output.
[G10H 2210/251] Chorus, i.e. automatic generation of two or more extra voices added to the melody, e.g. by a chorus effect processor or multiple voice harmonizer, to produce a chorus or unison effect, wherein individual sounds from multiple sources with roughly the same timbre converge and are perceived as one -- The CPC subclass G10H 2210/251 pertains to methods that automatically generate two or more extra voices to accompany a melody, as implemented in chorus-effect processors or multi-voice harmonizers, creating a chorus or unison effect where sounds of similar timbre converge and are perceived as a single source.
[G10H 2210/255] Unison, i.e. two or more voices or instruments sounding substantially the same pitch, e.g. at the same time -- The CPC subclass G10H 2210/255 covers unison, meaning two or more voices or instruments sound at essentially the same pitch at the same time. It is located under physics, musical instruments and electrophonic musical instruments, addressing aspects of musical processing that involve intrinsic musical character such as theory, parameters or knowledge applied to electrophonic tools.
[G10H 2210/261] Duet, i.e. automatic generation of a second voice, descant or counter melody, e.g. of a second harmonically interdependent voice by a single voice harmonizer or automatic composition algorithm, e.g. for fugue, canon or round composition, which may be substantially independent in contour and rhythm -- The code covers methods that automatically generate a second musical line such as a descant, counter-melody or other interdependent voice using a single-voice harmonizer or composition algorithm, applicable to forms like fugue, canon or round. The generated voice can have largely independent contour and rhythm while remaining harmonically linked to the original.
[G10H 2210/265] Acoustic effect simulation, i.e. volume, spatial, resonance or reverberation effects added to a musical sound, usually by appropriate filtering or delays -- G10H 2210/265 pertains to the simulation of acoustic effects in electrophonic musical instruments, such as volume, spatial, resonance, or reverberation enhancements, typically achieved through filtering or delay techniques. This classification covers methods that add these effects to musical sounds, relying on intrinsic musical knowledge and parameters.
[G10H 2210/271] Sympathetic resonance, i.e. adding harmonics simulating sympathetic resonance from other strings -- G10H 2210/271 pertains to the simulation of sympathetic resonance in electrophonic musical instruments, where additional harmonics are generated to mimic the natural resonances produced by other vibrating strings, enhancing the instrument's tonal richness and complexity.
[G10H 2210/275] Helmholtz resonance effect, i.e. using, exciting or emulating air resonance in a cavity -- The G10H 2210/275 entry covers the use or simulation of Helmholtz resonance-air vibration in a cavity-in electrophonic musical instruments, including techniques that excite, produce, or emulate this acoustic effect. It applies to methods of musical processing where the resonance is an intrinsic musical parameter, relying on musical theory or knowledge for sound generation or manipulation.
[G10H 2210/281] Reverberation or echo -- G10H 2210/281 relates to methods and apparatus that produce or manipulate reverberation or echo effects in electrophonic musical instruments, applying musical-theoretic processing of sound. It is classified under physics, musical instruments and electrophonic musical instruments, addressing intrinsic musical processing based on musical parameters or knowledge.
[G10H 2210/285] Electromechanical effectors therefor, i.e. using springs or similar electromechanical audio delay units -- The CPC subclass G10H 2210/285 covers electromechanical effectors used in electrophonic musical instruments, such as springs and similar audio delay mechanisms that create time-based effects. These components process musical signals by introducing delay or modulation through physical motion, linking musical theory parameters with electromechanical action.
[G10H 2210/291] Reverberator using both direct, i.e. dry, and indirect, i.e. wet, signals or waveforms, indirect signals having sustained one or more virtual reflections -- This CPC subclass covers reverberation devices that combine the original dry audio signal with one or more processed wet signals that simulate sustained virtual reflections. It applies to electrophonic musical instruments and other audio-processing tools where both direct and indirect waveforms are used to produce a reverberant effect.
[G10H 2210/295] Spatial effects, musical uses of multiple audio channels, e.g. stereo -- G10H 2210/295 covers spatial audio effects and the musical use of multiple audio channels such as stereo in electrophonic musical instruments. It falls within the hierarchy Physics - Musical Instruments - Electrophonic Musical Instruments and addresses methods that involve musical theory, parameters or knowledge applied to these tools.
[G10H 2210/301] Soundscape or sound field simulation, reproduction or control for musical purposes, e.g. surround or 3D sound -- G10H 2210/301 covers techniques for creating, reproducing or managing virtual soundscapes and sound fields for musical applications, including surround-sound and three-dimensional audio systems. It relates to the musical processing of acoustic environments using electrophonic instruments, employing musical theory or parameters to control the perceived spatial audio.
[G10H 2210/305] Source positioning in a soundscape, e.g. instrument positioning on a virtual soundstage, stereo panning or related delay or reverberation changes -- This class relates to methods for locating audio sources within a virtual soundscape, including positioning instruments on a virtual stage, applying stereo panning, and adjusting delay or reverberation to create spatial effects.
[G10H 2210/311] Distortion, i.e. desired non-linear audio processing to change the tone colour, e.g. by adding harmonics or deliberately distorting the amplitude of an audio waveform -- G10H 2210/311 refers to the intentional use of non-linear audio processing to alter the tone colour of sound, such as adding harmonic content or deliberately distorting the amplitude of a waveform. It applies to electrophonic musical instruments and tools where musical theory or parameters guide the distortion effect.
[G10H 2210/315] Dynamic effects for musical purposes, i.e. musical sound effects controlled by the amplitude of the time domain audio envelope, e.g. loudness-dependent tone colour or musically desired dynamic range compression or expansion -- The CPC subclass G10H 2210/315 defines dynamic musical effects that are driven by the audio-signal envelope, such as tone-colour changes or dynamic range compression and expansion that vary with loudness. It applies to electrophonic instruments and processing methods that use musical theory or parameters to shape sound dynamics.
[G10H 2210/321] Missing fundamental, i.e. creating the psychoacoustic impression of a missing fundamental tone through synthesis of higher harmonics, e.g. to play bass notes pitched below the frequency range of reproducing speakers -- G10H 2210/321 covers techniques for producing the missing-fundamental illusion, where the perception of a low pitch is created by synthesising its higher harmonics instead of reproducing the tone itself, allowing bass notes below a speaker's range to be heard. It falls under the CPC class for electrophonic musical instruments and musical-processing methods that rely on musical theory and parameters.
[G10H 2210/325] Musical pitch modification -- The G10H 2210/325 entry covers techniques for altering the pitch of notes produced by electrophonic musical instruments, encompassing methods that adjust tuning, bend notes, shift frequencies or apply other pitch-modifying effects. It pertains to musical-theory-based processing where the musical character of the sound is intentionally changed within electronic or electro-acoustic instruments.
[G10H 2210/331] Note pitch correction, i.e. modifying a note pitch or replacing it by the closest one in a given scale -- G10H 2210/331 covers techniques for correcting the pitch of a musical note in electronic instruments or processing systems, such as shifting a note to the nearest pitch in a predetermined scale. The classification applies to methods that adjust or replace pitch based on musical theory within electrophonic musical tools.
[G10H 2210/335] Chord correction, i.e. modifying one or several notes within a chord, e.g. to correct wrong fingering or to improve harmony -- The CPC subclass G10H 2210/335 falls under Physics → Musical Instruments → Electrophonic Musical Instruments and covers methods of musical processing that require musical knowledge, specifically techniques for chord correction that alter one or more notes in a chord to fix fingering errors or enhance harmonic quality.
[G10H 2210/341] Rhythm pattern selection, synthesis or composition -- The G10H 2210/341 class covers techniques for selecting, synthesising or composing rhythmic patterns in electronic musical instruments, involving musical theory, parameters or knowledge. It relates to electrophonic tools where the processing has an intrinsic musical character.
[G10H 2210/346] Pattern variations, break or fill-in -- The classification covers methods for generating or modifying musical patterns such as variations, breaks or fill-ins within electronic musical instruments. It relates to musical-theory-based processing applied to electrophonic tools.
[G10H 2210/351] Inserting a drum roll, e.g. as pattern break -- G10H 2210/351 covers the method of inserting a drum-roll pattern, such as a break, into the musical output of an electrophonic instrument. It relates to musical-theory-based processing that manipulates rhythm or timbre within electronic or digital drum-synthesizer systems, allowing a predefined roll to be triggered or inserted during performance.
[G10H 2210/356] Random process used to build a rhythm pattern -- This class covers techniques that employ stochastic or random algorithms to generate rhythmic sequences for electronic musical instruments, focusing on musical structure rather than pure signal processing. It includes methods using probability distributions, random number generators, or algorithmic composition to create rhythm patterns with intrinsic musical character.
[G10H 2210/361] Selection among a set of pre-established rhythm patterns -- The CPC subclass G10H 2210/361 covers methods for choosing among a set of pre-established rhythm patterns in electrophonic musical instruments, involving musical theory or parameters. It resides under Physics → Musical Instruments → Electrophonic Musical Instruments.
[G10H 2210/366] Random process affecting a selection among a set of pre-established patterns -- This subclass covers techniques in electronic musical instruments where a stochastic or random mechanism determines which of a predefined musical pattern is selected for playback or further processing. It applies to systems that use probability to vary rhythm, melody, or timbre by choosing among pre-set patterns, supporting generative or adaptive performance functions.
[G10H 2210/371] Rhythm syncopation, i.e. timing offset of rhythmic stresses or accents, e.g. note extended from weak to strong beat or started before strong beat -- This code covers methods for creating syncopated rhythms in electrophonic musical instruments, where the timing of stresses or accents is deliberately offset from the regular beat. It includes techniques such as extending notes from weak to strong beats or initiating sounds slightly before a strong beat to create a perceived displacement of rhythm.
[G10H 2210/375] Tempo or beat alterations -- G10H 2210/375 covers techniques for changing tempo or beat in electronic musical instruments, including methods that modify timing, rhythm or pulse as part of musical signal processing. It falls under physics, musical instruments and electrophonic musical instruments and relates to musical theory-based processing.
[G10H 2210/381] Manual tempo setting or adjustment -- G10H 2210/381 relates to manual tempo setting or adjustment in electrophonic musical instruments, covering hand-operated controls that let a performer select or modify the speed of musical output. It belongs to the physics category for musical instruments, focusing on musical-theory-based processing within electronic tools.
[G10H 2210/385] Speed change, i.e. variations from preestablished tempo, tempo change, e.g. faster or slower, accelerando or ritardando, without change in pitch -- This CPC subclass covers techniques for changing the tempo of an electrophonic musical instrument without altering pitch, such as accelerando or ritardando. It falls under the physics hierarchy for musical instruments and electrophonic musical instruments and relates to musical-theory-based processing.
[G10H 2210/391] Automatic tempo adjustment, correction or control -- G10H 2210/391 covers techniques for automatically adjusting, correcting or controlling the tempo of electrophonic musical instruments or related processing tools, using musical theory or parameters to maintain rhythmic accuracy. It applies to systems that detect tempo deviations and apply real-time modifications in electronic performance or playback.
[G10H 2210/395] Special musical scales, i.e. other than the 12-interval equally tempered scale -- This CPC subclass concerns special musical scales other than the standard twelve-interval equal-tempered system, including microtonal, just-intonation, and other alternative tuning structures, as applied to electophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/401] Microtonal scale -- G10H 2210/401 concerns methods and aspects of musical processing that employ microtonal scales in electrophonic musical instruments, focusing on generation, control or alteration of pitches outside the standard twelve-tone equal temperament. It applies musical theory and parameter adjustments within electronic or electroacoustic tools to achieve finer pitch resolutions.
[G10H 2210/405] Honkytonk scale, for producing, e.g. a honky-tonk piano effect, i.e. with deliberately detuned notes within each octave -- The G10H 2210/405 entry covers a honky-tonk scale used to create a deliberately detuned piano sound where notes within each octave are slightly out of tune, producing the characteristic honky-tonk effect. It pertains to musical processing methods applied to electrophonic instruments.
[G10H 2210/411] Railsback scale, i.e. stretched scale for piano tuning with bass keys having lower pitches and treble keys having higher pitches than foreseen by the equally tempered scale -- G10H 2210/411 covers the Railsback scale, a stretched piano tuning where bass keys are tuned lower and treble keys higher than in equal temperament, reflecting intrinsic musical processing in electrophonic instruments.
[G10H 2210/415] Equally tempered scale, i.e. note tuning scale in which every pair of adjacent notes has an identical frequency ratio equal to 2 to the power 1/n if the scale has n notes per octave -- This CPC subclass defines the equally tempered scale, a tuning system where each successive note has an identical frequency ratio equal to the nth root of two, applied in electrophonic musical instruments and related musical-processing methods.
[G10H 2210/421] 10 equal intervals per octave -- The G10H 2210/421 class concerns the division of the musical octave into ten equal steps and the associated tuning or pitch-spacing methods used in electrophonic musical instruments. It addresses theoretical and practical aspects of applying a ten-equal-interval scale in music processing, including synthesis, sound generation and related musical-parameter control.
[G10H 2210/425] 19 equal intervals per octave, offering better major thirds, far better minor thirds and overall far greater consonance than normal 12-semitone equal temperament, at the cost of a flatter fifth -- The G10H 2210/425 subclass addresses tuning schemes that divide the octave into 19 equal steps, a temperament that yields markedly better-sounding major thirds, far improved minor thirds and overall greater consonance than the conventional 12-semitone equal temperament, while producing a slightly flatter perfect fifth.
[G10H 2210/431] Quarter tone scale, i.e. 24 equal intervals per octave, e.g. for Arabic music -- The CPC subclass G10H 2210/431 covers quarter-tone scales, which divide the octave into 24 equal steps. It applies to electrophonic musical instruments and processing methods that use this microtonal system, such as in Arabic music, and relates to musical theory and parameters within the physics of musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/435] Huygens scale, i.e. 31 equal intervals per octave, provides near-just major thirds, and provides decent matches for harmonics up to at least 13, despite a slightly less accurate fifth than the standard 12 interval equally tempered scale -- The subclass G10H 2210/435 concerns the application of the Huygens 31-tone equal-tempered scale in electrophonic musical instruments, describing a tuning system that divides the octave into 31 equal intervals. It highlights the scale's near-just major thirds and good harmonic matches up to the 13th harmonic, while noting its slightly less accurate perfect fifth compared with the conventional 12-tone equal temperament.
[G10H 2210/441] Janko scale, i.e. 41 equal intervals per octave, e.g. as used in the "tonal plexus" keyboard with 211 keys per octave arranged in 12 staggered columns, i.e. in 41 regions of 5 keys each plus 6 duplicate enharmonic keys -- G10H 2210/441 relates to musical processing methods that use the Janko scale, a tuning system dividing the octave into 41 equal steps. It covers applications of this 41-EDO scale in electrophonic instruments, for example keyboards such as the "tonal plexus" that feature 211 keys per octave arranged in twelve staggered columns, forming 41 groups of five keys with six duplicate enharmonic keys.
[G10H 2210/445] 45 equal intervals per octave -- The CPC subclass G10H 2210/445 covers methods and aspects of musical processing that employ a division of the octave into 45 equal intervals, used in electrophonic musical instruments or tools. It falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments and relates to musical-theory parameters.
[G10H 2210/451] Holder scale or Holdrian comma, i.e. 53 equal intervals per octave, with 31 intervals equal to an almost just perfect fifth -- This entry concerns the holder scale, also known as the Holdrian comma, a tuning system that splits the octave into 53 equal intervals, with 31 of those intervals forming an almost just perfect fifth. It relates to musical theory and processing methods applied in electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/455] 70 equal intervals per octave -- The G10H 2210/455 entry covers methods and aspects of musical processing that involve the division of an octave into 70 equal intervals, applied to electophonic musical instruments. It pertains to theoretical or parametric treatments requiring musical knowledge, such as tuning, synthesis, or control techniques that use this specific microtonal scale.
[G10H 2210/461] Jankovski scale or twelfth tone scale, i.e. octave divided in 72 equal intervals, e.g. moria in Byzantine music theory -- The G10H 2210/461 classification covers methods of musical processing that rely on musical theory within electrophonic instruments, specifically the Jankovski or twelfth-tone scale. This scale divides an octave into 72 equal intervals and is used in Byzantine music theory.
[G10H 2210/465] 84 equal intervals per octave -- G10H 2210/465 covers musical processing methods that divide the octave into 84 equal intervals, a microtonal tuning system applied to electrophonic instruments. It pertains to the theoretical and parameter-based aspects of such scales within the field of electronic musical instrument design.
[G10H 2210/471] Natural or just intonation scales, i.e. based on harmonics consonance such that most adjacent pitches are related by harmonically pure ratios of small integers -- The G10H 2210/471 subclass covers natural or just intonation tuning systems used in electrophonic musical instruments, where intervals between adjacent notes are defined by simple integer ratios that produce harmonic consonance. It pertains to musical-theory-based processing methods that rely on these intrinsically musical scales.
[G10H 2210/476] Zarlino scales, e.g. octave subdivision based on the pitch ratios 9/8 + 10/9 + 16/15 + 9/8 + 10/9 + 9/8 + 16/15 -- This CPC entry G10H 2210/476 covers methods of musical processing that employ Zarlino scales, such as octave subdivisions based on the pitch ratios 9/8, 10/9 and 16/15, when used in electrophonic musical instruments. It belongs to the hierarchy Physics - Musical Instruments - Electrophonic Musical Instruments.
[G10H 2210/481] Pythagorean scale, i.e. in which the frequency relationships of all intervals should be based on the perfect fifth, with ratio 3:2 -- G10H 2210/481 is a CPC subclass for electrophonic musical instruments that deals with musical-theoretic processing methods, specifically the Pythagorean scale in which all interval frequencies are derived from the perfect fifth using a 3:2 ratio. This classification covers the implementation of that tuning system in electronic music tools.
[G10H 2210/486] Werckmeister scales, i.e. family of scales with 12 mostly rational intervals, e.g. for organs -- This CPC subclass covers methods and aspects of musical processing that involve the use of Werckmeister tuning systems, a family of temperaments with twelve intervals defined by rational ratios, commonly applied to organ keyboards and other electrophonic musical instruments. It falls under physics, musical instruments and electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/491] Meantone scales, i.e. in which all non-octave intervals are generated from a stack of tempered perfect fifths -- The entry covers musical processing techniques that use meantone tuning, where all intervals except the octave are derived from a series of tempered perfect fifths. It applies to electrophonic musical instruments and tools that rely on musical theory or parameters, facilitating the creation of scales with specific harmonic characteristics.
[G10H 2210/496] Redfield scales, i.e. 12 intervals per octave, based on note ratios equal to (2**p)*(3**q)*(5**r) with p, q, r positive or negative integers -- G10H 2210/496 covers methods for generating or processing musical material that uses Redfield scales, a twelve-tone division of the octave in which each step is defined by frequency ratios of the form 2^p·3^q·5^r with integer exponents. The classification falls under physics, musical instruments, electrophonic musical instruments and pertains to musical-theoretic processing in electronic tools.
[G10H 2210/501] Altered natural scale, i.e. 12 unequal intervals not foreseen in the above -- G10H 2210/501 pertains to electrophonic musical instruments and methods that employ altered natural scales, specifically twelve unequal intervals that are not anticipated in conventional scale definitions. It covers musical-processing techniques relying on music theory or parameters to create or manipulate such non-standard scalar structures within electronic or electronic-assisted instruments.
[G10H 2210/506] Danielou 53 interval scale, with note ratios equal to (2**p)(3**q)(5**r), with p, q, r positive or negative integers -- The entry G10H 2210/506 covers musical processing methods that use a Danielou 53-tone interval scale built from frequency ratios expressed as powers of 2, 3 and 5, with the exponents allowed to be positive or negative integers. It pertains to electrophonic musical instruments where such mathematically defined scales are applied in composition, synthesis or analysis, relying on music theory and the intrinsic musical character of the scale.
[G10H 2210/511] Arabic scales, i.e. either double harmonic scale or major locrian scale -- G10H 2210/511 covers methods and aspects of musical processing that employ Arabic scales, such as the double harmonic or major locrian scale, in electrophonic musical instruments. The entry applies musical theory and parameters to electronic tools, fitting within the physics hierarchy under musical instruments and electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/515] Balinese scales, e.g. for gamelan, with instruments played in pairs and tuned slightly apart to produce interference beating ideally at a consistent speed for all pairs of notes in all registers -- The entry covers Balinese gamelan scales in which paired instruments are intentionally detuned to generate interference beating, with the beat rate kept constant for all notes across registers. It applies musical-theoretic processing to electrophonic instruments.
[G10H 2210/521] Polynesian scales -- The G10H 2210/521 class covers the use of Polynesian scales in electrophonic musical instruments, focusing on musical-theoretic aspects such as pitch organization, intervals and melodic patterns specific to Polynesian traditions. It applies these scale concepts to the processing, synthesis or performance functions of electronic music tools.
[G10H 2210/525] Diatonic scales, e.g. aeolian, ionian or major, dorian, locrian, lydian, mixolydian, phrygian, i.e. seven note, octave-repeating musical scales comprising five whole steps and two half steps for each octave, in which the two half steps are separated from each other by either two or three whole steps -- This CPC subclass covers the definition and use of diatonic seven-note scales such as major, minor and the church modes, characterized by a pattern of five whole steps and two half steps per octave with the half steps separated by either two or three whole steps. It applies to electrophonic musical instruments and processing methods that rely on musical theory.
[G10H 2210/531] Bluenote scale, i.e. 7-tone scale of 2+1+2+1+3+1+2 semitones -- G10H 2210/531 refers to the bluenote scale in electrophonic musical instruments, a 7-tone scale with a specific semitone pattern that is often used in blues and jazz music. This classification covers aspects of musical processing that involve musical theory or parameters unique to this scale.
[G10H 2210/535] Hexatonal or hexatonic scales, i.e. six pitches or notes per octave, e.g. whole tone scale, augmented scale, Prometheus scale, blues scale -- G10H 2210/535 covers the use of six-note (hexatonal or hexatonic) scales-such as whole-tone, augmented, Prometheus and blues scales-in electrophonic musical instruments and processing methods that rely on musical theory or parameters. This falls under physics-related musical instrument classification.
[G10H 2210/541] Pentatonal or pentatonic scale, i.e. five pitches or notes per octave, e.g. basic Chinese musical scale, black piano keys, Javanese gamelan slendro scale or Japanese shakuhachi flute -- G10H 2210/541 covers methods and aspects of musical processing that involve the pentatonic scale - five pitches per octave, such as Chinese, Javanese slendro or Japanese shakuhachi scales, when applied to electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/545] Yona Nuki, i.e. a family of pentatonic scales without fourth or seventh, e.g. Hirajoshi, Iwato, Kumoi, Sino-indian [Raga Amritavarsini] used, e.g. for japanese traditional music, koto or shamisen tunings -- This CPC entry G10H 2210/545 covers aspects or methods of musical processing with intrinsic musical character, such as applying pentatonic scales lacking fourth or seventh - including Hirajoshi, Iwato, Kumoi and Indian Raga Amritavarsini - to electrophonic musical instruments and tunings for Japanese traditional instruments like koto or shamisen.
[G10H 2210/551] Okinawa pentatonic scale, i.e. Okinawan min'yo, e.g. including the half-steps omitted in the min'yo pentatonic scale used in the main japanese islands -- G10H 2210/551 pertains to the encoding, synthesis or digital manipulation of the Okinawan pentatonic (min'yo) scale, including the characteristic half-step intervals omitted in the standard Japanese pentatonic scale, for use in electronic musical instruments and processing tools.
[G10H 2210/555] Tonality processing, involving the key in which a musical piece or melody is played -- G10H 2210/555 covers techniques for processing musical tonality in electrophonic instruments, such as detecting, analyzing or altering the key of a melody or piece. The classification applies to methods that rely on musical theory or parameters to manage key-related functions within electronic music tools.
[G10H 2210/561] Changing the tonality within a musical piece -- The entry covers techniques for shifting the tonal center or key of a musical piece while using electro-phonic instruments, focusing on methods that rely on musical theory and parameters such as scales, harmonies and pitch relationships. It applies to software or hardware that processes musical signals to alter tonality during performance or playback.
[G10H 2210/565] Manual designation or selection of a tonality -- G10H 2210/565 concerns the manual setting or choice of a musical key or tonal centre in electronic or electrophonic instruments. It relates to methods of musical processing that rely on musical theory and parameters, applied within electrophonic musical tools. The classification falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2210/571] Chords -- This CPC subclass covers chords as musical constructs used in electronic or electro-acoustic instruments, focusing on the processing and generation of multiple simultaneous pitches according to musical theory. It includes methods for creating, analyzing, modifying or rendering chord structures within electrophonic musical tools.
[G10H 2210/576] Chord progression -- G10H 2210/576 refers to chord progression, covering the arrangement and sequencing of chords in electrophonic musical instruments. It includes methods that apply musical theory or parameters to generate, modify or analyze chord sequences within electronic music tools.
[G10H 2210/581] Chord inversion -- G10H 2210/581 deals with chord inversion in electrophonic musical instruments, covering methods that change the order of notes within a chord. It addresses musical-theoretic processing applied to electronic or electric tools used for music creation.
[G10H 2210/586] Natural chords, i.e. adjustment of individual note pitches in order to generate just intonation chords -- The code G10H 2210/586 covers methods for producing natural chords by adjusting individual note pitches to achieve just intonation, i.e. tuning intervals according to simple frequency ratios rather than equal temperament. It pertains to musical-theoretic processing within electrophonic instruments.
[G10H 2210/591] Chord with a suspended note, e.g. 2nd or 4th -- G10H 2210/591 concerns the processing of musical information in electronic musical instruments where a chord includes a suspended note, such as a second or fourth interval. It addresses methods that rely on musical theory or parameters for generating or manipulating these suspended-note chords within electrophonic tools.
[G10H 2210/596] Chord augmented -- The CPC subclass G10H 2210/596 relates to chord augmentation techniques used in electphonic musical instruments, covering methods that modify or expand chords by adding notes or altering harmonic structures. It addresses processing steps that rely on musical theory and parameters, focusing on how such augmented chords are generated, rendered, or controlled within electronic or digitally assisted musical tools.
[G10H 2210/601] Chord diminished -- The code G10H 2210/601 refers to the treatment of diminished chords in electro-phonic musical instruments, covering methods and aspects of musical processing that involve this specific harmonic structure. It falls under physics → musical instruments → electro-phonic musical instruments and relates to musical theory applied in electronic tools.
[G10H 2210/606] Chord ninth, i.e. including ninth or above, e.g. 11th or 13th -- The classification G10H 2210/606 refers to chord structures that include a ninth degree or higher, such as eleventh or thirteenth chords, as used in the processing or synthesis of sounds in electrophonic musical instruments. It falls under the physics-musical instruments hierarchy, specifically aspects of musical processing that rely on musical theory.
[G10H 2210/611] Chord ninth or above, to which is added a tension note -- The entry covers methods and devices for generating or processing chords that extend beyond the octave, specifically ninth or higher intervals, with an added tension note, as applied in electrophonic musical instruments and related signal-processing systems.
[G10H 2210/616] Chord seventh, major or minor -- This subclass covers techniques for producing, analyzing, or modifying seventh chords - both major and minor - in electronic or electro-acoustic musical instruments and related signal-processing systems. It includes methods for encoding the harmonic structure, synthesizing the chord sound, or detecting it in musical data.
[G10H 2210/621] Chord seventh dominant -- G10H 2210/621 covers methods and aspects of musical processing that deal with the dominant seventh chord in electrophonic musical instruments. It includes analysis, generation, transformation or control of that chord within electronic or digital music systems, reflecting its theoretical and perceptual characteristics.
[G10H 2210/626] Chord sixth -- The CPC subclass G10H 2210/626 concerns the use of a chord sixth, an interval created by adding a sixth degree to a chord, within electrophonic musical instruments. It relates to methods of musical processing that rely on musical theory or parameters when using electronic or electro-acoustic tools.
[G10H 2220/00] Input/output interfacing specifically adapted for electrophonic musical tools or instruments -- The G10H 2220/00 subclass covers interfaces that enable input-output connections specially designed for electronic musical instruments, such as MIDI, audio, or control signal links to computers, amplifiers, or other devices. It includes hardware and circuitry that translate musical performance data into electronic form or vice versa.
[G10H 2220/005] Non-interactive screen display of musical or status data -- This CPC subgroup covers arrangements that present musical information or instrument status on a screen without requiring user interaction, such as visual read-outs of notes, tuning, battery level or performance parameters for electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2220/011] Lyrics displays, e.g. for karaoke applications -- G10H 2220/011 refers to devices that present song lyrics to users, such as screens or projectors used in karaoke systems, and are integrated with electrophonic musical instruments to synchronize text display with audio playback.
[G10H 2220/015] Musical staff, tablature or score displays, e.g. for score reading during a performance -- The entry covers devices that provide visual presentation of musical notation-staff lines, tablature or full scores-on a display to assist a performer during play, especially when linked to electronic musical instruments. It relates to input-output interfacing for electrophonic tools.
[G10H 2220/021] Indicator, i.e. non-screen output user interfacing, e.g. visual or tactile instrument status or guidance information using lights, LEDs or seven segments displays -- G10H 2220/021 covers indicators that provide non-screen feedback for electrophonic musical instruments, such as visual or tactile status cues conveyed by LEDs, lights or seven-segment displays. The provision is intended to guide the user without requiring a graphical screen, delivering instrument information through simple visual or tactile signals.
[G10H 2220/026] associated with a key or other user input device, e.g. key indicator lights -- The class G10H 2220/026 covers interfacing arrangements for electrophonic musical instruments that connect a key or other user input device to the instrument, such as key indicator lights that show which keys are active. It is nested under physics, musical instruments and electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2220/031] Blinking or flashing indicator lights -- Patent classification G10H 2220/031 covers the use of blinking or flashing indicator lights as part of input or output interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments. It includes devices that signal status, mode changes, or user interaction through visual light cues integrated into the instrument's electronics.
[G10H 2220/036] Chord indicators, e.g. displaying note fingering when several notes are to be played simultaneously as a chord -- The code covers systems that show musicians which keys or finger positions to use when playing multiple notes at once on electronic instruments. These chord indicators can be visual displays, lights or screens that present the required fingering for each note of a chord, helping performers execute complex chords accurately.
[G10H 2220/041] Remote key fingering indicator, i.e. fingering shown on a display separate from the instrument itself or substantially disjoint from the keys -- A remote key fingering indicator is an input/output interface for electrophonic musical instruments that displays the fingering on a separate screen rather than on the instrument's own keys. This lets musicians view finger positions without the display being integrated into the instrument.
[G10H 2220/046] Drumpad indicator, e.g. drumbeat strike indicator light on a drumpad or rim -- The G10H 2220/046 classification covers visual signalling devices incorporated into electronic drum pads or rims that illuminate to indicate a drumbeat strike, such as indicator lights that flash when a pad is hit. This input/output interfacing aids performers in timing, practice and live feedback.
[G10H 2220/051] Fret indicator, e.g. for playing guidance on a string instrument or string instrument emulator -- This classification covers devices that provide visual or tactile cues indicating fret positions on stringed instruments or their electronic emulators, used to guide the player in finger placement. It includes integrated or attachable indicators such as LEDs, displays or tactile markers that assist performance.
[G10H 2220/056] Hand or finger indicator, e.g. for indicating which hand or which specific finger should be used -- A hand or finger indicator is a device integrated into electrophonic musical instruments to show the performer which hand or specific finger should be used for a particular note or action. It aids accurate playing by providing visual or tactile cues directly on the instrument's interface.
[G10H 2220/061] LED, i.e. using a light-emitting diode as indicator -- This CPC subclass covers the use of light-emitting diodes as visual indicators within the input or output circuitry of electronic musical instruments, such as status lights or performance feedback. It includes LEDs integrated into control panels, displays, or signal pathways to signal operating conditions.
[G10H 2220/066] Colour, i.e. indications with two or more different colours -- G10H 2220/066 designates input-output interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments that use colour indications involving two or more different hues, enabling visual feedback or status signals in devices such as controllers, displays or light-based output modules.
[G10H 2220/071] Pedal indicator, e.g. guitar pedal status lights -- G10H 2220/071 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to features in electrophonic musical instruments that provide visual feedback through pedals, such as status lights on guitar pedals, indicating settings or active effects.
[G10H 2220/076] String indicator, e.g. on a stringed musical instrument for indicating which string is to be played, plucked or bowed -- A string indicator is a device attached to a stringed musical instrument that signals the specific string to be plucked or bowed, helping performers select the correct note. It falls under CPC G10H 2220/076, covering input/output interfacing for electrophonic musical tools.
[G10H 2220/081] Beat indicator, e.g. marks or flashing LEDs to indicate tempo or beat positions -- A beat indicator for electrophonic musical instruments provides visual cues such as marks or flashing LEDs that display the tempo or current beat position, helping performers stay in time.
[G10H 2220/086] Beats per minute [BPM] indicator, i.e. displaying a tempo value, e.g. in words or as numerical value in beats per minute -- A beats-per-minute (BPM) indicator is an interface for electrophonic musical instruments that displays the current tempo, either as a numeric value or written out in words, enabling performers to monitor and adjust speed during play.
[G10H 2220/091] Graphical user interface [GUI] specifically adapted for electrophonic musical instruments, e.g. interactive musical displays, musical instrument icons or menus -- This CPC subclass G10H 2220/091 concerns graphical user interfaces that are specially designed for electrophonic musical instruments, enabling interaction through visual displays, instrument icons, and menus. It includes interactive screens and menu systems that control or modify sound generation in electronic keyboards, synthesizers, digital wind controllers and similar devices.
[G10H 2220/096] using a touch screen -- G10H 2220/096 pertains to using a touch screen as an input-output interface for electrophonic musical instruments, enabling touch-based control or display functions on devices such as digital keyboards or synthesizers. It is classified under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2220/101] for graphical creation, edition or control of musical data or parameters -- The entry G10H 2220/101 falls under physics - musical instruments - electrophonic musical instruments and relates to input/output interfacing adapted for such tools.
