It specifically concerns systems used for the graphical creation, editing or control of musical data or parameters.
[G10H 2220/106] using icons, e.g. selecting, moving or linking icons, on-screen symbols, screen regions or segments representing musical elements or parameters -- This CPC entry G10H 2220/106 covers graphical user-interface techniques in which on-screen icons such as symbols, regions or segments are selected, moved or linked to control musical elements or parameters of electrophonic instruments. It pertains to input/output interfacing adapted for electronic musical tools.
[G10H 2220/111] for graphical orchestra or soundstage control, e.g. on-screen selection or positioning of instruments in a virtual orchestra, using movable or selectable musical instrument icons -- G10H 2220/111 pertains to the graphical interface elements used for controlling virtual orchestras or sound stages in electrophonic musical instruments, allowing users to select or position instrument icons on-screen.
[G10H 2220/116] for graphical editing of sound parameters or waveforms, e.g. by graphical interactive control of timbre, partials or envelope -- This subclass covers systems that let users edit sound parameters or waveforms of electrophonic instruments through graphical interfaces, such as interactive controls for timbre, partials or envelope shaping. It pertains to input/output interfacing adapted for electronic musical tools.
[G10H 2220/121] for graphical editing of a musical score, staff or tablature -- The subclass G10H 2220/121 covers functions that allow graphical editing of musical notation - scores, staves or tablature - directly in the interface of electronic musical instruments. It pertains to input and output features specially adapted for electrophonic tools.
[G10H 2220/126] for graphical editing of individual notes, parts or phrases represented as variable length segments on a 2D or 3D representation, e.g. graphical edition of musical collage, remix files or pianoroll representations of MIDI-like files -- The CPC entry G10H 2220/126 covers software interfaces that let users graphically edit individual notes, parts or phrases displayed as variable-length segments on two- or three-dimensional screens, such as pianoroll or collage views of MIDI-like files for remixing or composition, and is classified under input/output interfacing for electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2220/131] for abstract geometric visualisation of music, e.g. for interactive editing of musical parameters linked to abstract geometric figures -- This CPC subclass covers interfaces that provide abstract geometric visualisation of music, enabling interactive editing of musical parameters through linked geometric figures, specifically adapted for electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2220/135] Musical aspects of games or videogames -- This CPC subclass covers the musical components of electronic games and video games, such as sound generation, synchronization with gameplay, and interactive audio features. It addresses how these games produce, manipulate and integrate music through electrophonic instrument interfaces.
[G10H 2220/141] Games on or about music, i.e. based on musical knowledge, e.g. musical multimedia quizzes -- G10H 2220/141 covers software or systems that provide musical games or quizzes based on musical knowledge, often delivered as multimedia quizzes and interfacing with electronic musical instruments.
[G10H 2220/145] Multiplayer musical games, e.g. karaoke-like multiplayer videogames -- The G10H 2220/145 subclass covers interfaces and methods for multiplayer musical games that use electrophonic instruments, such as karaoke-style video game systems allowing several players to interact simultaneously. It addresses input/output arrangements tailored to collaborative electronic music entertainment.
[G10H 2220/151] Musical difficulty level setting or selection -- This subclass covers features that let a player choose or adjust the difficulty level of an electrophonic musical instrument, such as settings that simplify note patterns or response speed. It applies to input/output interfaces that adapt the instrument's behavior to match a selected skill level.
[G10H 2220/155] User input interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments -- User input interfaces specifically designed for electrophonic musical instruments, such as keyboards, synthesizers, digital controllers and similar devices, that translate performer actions-pressing, sliding, gesturing or other inputs-into electronic signals for controlling sound generation or instrument parameters. This subclass covers the hardware and methods for such interfacing.
[G10H 2220/161] with 2D or x/y surface coordinates sensing -- G10H 2220/161 pertains to input/output interfaces in electrophonic musical instruments that are specifically adapted for sensing two-dimensional or x/y surface coordinates, enabling precise interaction and control over sound generation or manipulation.
[G10H 2220/165] for string input, i.e. special characteristics in string composition or use for sensing purposes, e.g. causing the string to become its own sensor -- The subclass G10H 2220/165 concerns input and output interfacing for electrophonic musical instruments where the strings themselves function as sensors or signal sources. It includes special string compositions, configurations or treatments that enable a string to generate, modulate or detect electrical signals, effectively turning the vibrating string into an integrated sensing element for the instrument.
[G10H 2220/171] using electrified strings, e.g. strings carrying coded or AC signals for transducing, sustain, fret length or fingering detection -- The entry covers technologies that utilize strings carrying coded or alternating-current signals on electrophonic musical instruments to perform functions such as sound transduction, sustain control, and detection of fret length or fingering. Such methods enable precise electronic monitoring and manipulation of string behavior for performance and analysis.
[G10H 2220/175] using nonmagnetic string materials, e.g. nylon -- The code G10H 2220/175 covers the use of non-magnetic strings such as nylon in electrophonic musical instruments, focusing on input or output interfaces that rely on these materials. It applies to designs where the string material helps avoid magnetic interference, improving signal fidelity in electronic or hybrid instruments.
[G10H 2220/181] by nonresonant wave interaction, i.e. string sensing using wavelengths unrelated to string resonant wavelengths, e.g. ultrasonic waves, microwave or light waves, propagated along a musical instrument string to measure its fret length, e.g. for MIDI transcription -- G10H 2220/181 pertains to the use of nonresonant wave interaction for sensing strings on electrophonic musical instruments, employing wavelengths unrelated to the string's natural resonances, such as ultrasonic, microwave, or light waves, to determine fret length for applications like MIDI transcription.
[G10H 2220/185] Stick input, e.g. drumsticks with position or contact sensors -- The classification covers interfaces that use sticks-most commonly drumsticks fitted with position, motion or contact sensors-to control electrophonic musical instruments. These devices turn the physical gestures of a stick into electronic signals for sound generation or processing.
[G10H 2220/191] Plectrum or pick sensing, e.g. for detection of string striking or plucking -- G10H 2220/191 relates to pick or plectrum sensing systems that detect the striking or plucking of strings and generate electronic signals for timing, dynamics or other control functions in electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2220/195] Particle energy or molecular configuration used as musical control data -- G10H 2220/195 pertains to the use of particle energy or molecular configuration as a method for generating musical control data in electrophonic musical instruments, integrating unique physical phenomena into the input mechanisms for sound creation or manipulation.
[G10H 2220/201] for movement interpretation, i.e. capturing and recognizing a gesture or a specific kind of movement, e.g. to control a musical instrument -- The entry covers interfacing technology for electrophonic musical instruments that captures and recognises user gestures or specific movements to generate control signals. It includes sensors, algorithms and software that translate motion into musical parameters, enabling gesture-based performance of electronic instruments.
[G10H 2220/206] Conductor baton movement detection used to adjust rhythm, tempo or expressivity of, e.g. the playback of musical pieces -- This CPC subclass covers systems that detect a conductor's baton movement and use the data to modify timing, tempo or expressive parameters of music playback in electrophonic instruments. It falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments, focusing on input/output interfacing adapted for such tools.
[G10H 2220/211] for microphones, i.e. control of musical parameters either directly from microphone signals or by physically associated peripherals, e.g. karaoke control switches or rhythm sensing accelerometer within the microphone casing -- This CPC subclass covers input and output interfaces specially adapted for electrophonic musical instruments, concentrating on microphone-based control of musical parameters. It includes systems that act directly on microphone signals or incorporate associated peripherals such as karaoke control switches or rhythm-sensing accelerometers built into the microphone housing.
[G10H 2220/215] using a magnetic strip on a card or sheet -- This subclass concerns methods and devices that employ a magnetic stripe on a card or sheet to convey data or control signals to electrophonic musical instruments. It includes reading or writing magnetic tracks to trigger functions, select presets, or exchange performance information between the instrument and external equipment.
[G10H 2220/221] Keyboards, i.e. configuration of several keys or key-like input devices relative to one another -- G10H 2220/221 classifies the spatial arrangement of multiple keys or key-like input devices on electophonic musical instruments, specifying how the keys are configured relative to one another. It pertains to input/output interfacing for such tools within the physics-musical instruments hierarchy.
[G10H 2220/226] Whole-tone keyboards, i.e. having as many keys on the upper row as on the lower row -- The G10H 2220/226 subclass covers whole-tone keyboards, which have an equal number of keys in the upper and lower rows, providing a uniform step size across the scale. These devices are part of the input/output interfacing for electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2220/231] Alphanumeric, used for musical purposes or with additional musical features, e.g. typewriter or pc-type keyboard reconfigured such that letters or symbols are assigned to musical notes -- The code G10H 2220/231 relates to alphanumeric input devices that are adapted for musical use, such as typewriter-style or PC keyboards where letters or symbols are assigned to specific notes or musical functions. It covers input-output interfacing for electrophonic instruments that employ a reconfigured alphanumeric layout to control pitch, articulation or other musical parameters.
[G10H 2220/236] representing an active musical staff or tablature, i.e. with key-like position sensing at the expected note positions on the staff -- The CPC class G10H 2220/236 covers input/output interfacing for electrophonic musical instruments that visually represent a staff or tablature and incorporate key-like position sensing at the expected note locations, allowing the instrument to detect which note is being played.
[G10H 2220/241] on touchscreens, i.e. keys, frets, strings, tablature or staff displayed on a touchscreen display for note input purposes -- This classification G10H 2220/241 covers input/output interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments that use a touchscreen to display keys, frets, strings, tablature or staff for note entry. It lies within the physics hierarchy under musical instruments, electrophonic musical instruments, and specifies touchscreen-based note-input methods.
[G10H 2220/246] with reduced number of keys per octave, some notes missing -- The G10H 2220/246 entry classifies input-output interfacing for electrophonic musical instruments that employ a reduced-key keyboard, omitting certain notes within each octave. It includes designs where the number of keys per octave is intentionally limited, affecting the instrument's pitch layout. This falls under the CPC hierarchy for physics, musical instruments, and electrophonic devices.
[G10H 2220/251] arranged as 2D or 3D arrays -- This classification covers designs where the input or output interfaces for electronic musical instruments are organized as two-dimensional or three-dimensional arrays, enabling spatial mapping of controls or sensors. It applies to electrophonic devices that require arrayed I/O configurations.
[G10H 2220/256] foldable or rollable, e.g. for transport -- G10H 2220/256 refers to input-output interfacing for electrophonic musical instruments that is foldable or rollable, allowing the device to be compacted for easy transport or storage. This subclass falls under the physics-based hierarchy for musical instruments and their electronic interfaces.
[G10H 2220/261] Numeric keypad used for musical purposes, e.g. musical input via a telephone or calculator-like keyboard -- The entry covers numeric keypads that are adapted as musical input devices, enabling users to trigger notes or control sound parameters through telephone-style or calculator-style keyboards. It pertains to interfacing methods for electrophonic musical instruments that employ numeric keypads for performance or control.
[G10H 2220/265] Key design details -- The code G10H 2220/265 addresses the key design details of input/output interfacing for electrophonic musical instruments, describing the arrangement, actuation mechanisms, and feedback features of keys in devices such as keyboards and synthesizers. It is nested under physics, musical instruments, electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2220/271] Velocity sensing for individual keys, e.g. by placing sensors at different points along the kinematic path for individual key velocity estimation by delay measurement between adjacent sensor signals -- The G10H 2220/271 entry covers methods for sensing the velocity of each key on an electrophonic musical instrument by installing multiple sensors along the key's motion path and deriving the speed from the time delay between adjacent sensor signals. This enables precise individual key velocity estimation for electronic keyboards and similar devices.
[G10H 2220/275] Switching mechanism or sensor details of individual keys, e.g. details of key contacts, hall effect or piezoelectric sensors used for key position or movement sensing purposes -- This subclass covers the design and characteristics of switching mechanisms or sensors attached to individual keys of electrophonic musical instruments. It includes key contacts, Hall-effect devices, piezoelectric elements and other components that detect key position or motion for electronic control or sound generation. The focus is on the technical details of these key-level sensing elements.
[G10H 2220/281] with two contacts, switches or sensor triggering levels along the key kinematic path -- The entry covers input-output interfacing for electrophonic musical instruments that uses two contacts, switches or sensors positioned to trigger different levels along the key's kinematic path. It applies to mechanisms that detect key position during travel and generate variable electrical signals, such as in electronic keyboards and similar devices.
[G10H 2220/285] with three contacts, switches or sensor triggering levels along the key kinematic path -- Covers electrophonic musical instrument key mechanisms that incorporate three separate contacts, switches or sensors arranged along the key's travel path to provide multiple triggering levels such as initial press, intermediate motion and full depression, enabling nuanced control of sound parameters.
[G10H 2220/291] with four or more contacts, switches or sensor triggering levels along the key kinematic path -- The CPC subclass G10H 2220/291 concerns electrophonic musical instruments whose key mechanisms incorporate four or more contacts, switches or sensors arranged along the key's kinematic path to trigger different levels of operation, providing enhanced input-output interfacing for nuanced control of the instrument.
[G10H 2220/295] Switch matrix, e.g. contact array common to several keys, the actuated keys being identified by the rows and columns in contact -- This CPC entry covers switch matrices used in electrophonic musical instruments where a common contact array is shared among multiple keys, with each key identified by its row and column coordinates. It pertains to input/output interfacing for electronic keyboards or similar devices.
[G10H 2220/301] Fret-like switch array arrangements for guitar necks -- This classification covers arrangements in which a guitar neck incorporates a series of switches positioned like frets to form a switch array for electronic control of signals. The design enables performers to activate or modify sound functions by pressing the fret-like switches, integrating input interfacing directly into the instrument.
[G10H 2220/305] using a light beam to detect key, pedal or note actuation -- It covers input-output interfacing for electrophonic musical instruments that use a light beam to sense key, pedal or note actuation, converting the mechanical motion into electrical signals for sound generation or control.
[G10H 2220/311] with controlled tactile or haptic feedback effect -- This subclass defines interfacing arrangements for electrophonic musical devices that deliver a deliberate tactile or haptic sensation to the performer, such as vibration or force feedback linked to musical parameters. It includes mechanisms that control these feedback effects through the instrument's input or output circuitry.
[G10H 2220/315] for joystick-like proportional control of musical input -- G10H 2220/315 pertains to the use of joystick-like devices for providing proportional control inputs in electrophonic musical instruments, enabling nuanced and dynamic performance interactions.
[G10H 2220/321] Garment sensors, i.e. musical control means with trigger surfaces or joint angle sensors, worn as a garment by the player, e.g. bracelet, intelligent clothing -- G10H 2220/321 covers wearable sensor systems that serve as musical control interfaces, such as bracelets or intelligent clothing that detect trigger surfaces or joint-angle movements to modulate electrophonic instruments. These garment-based sensors enable the player to generate or alter sound through body gestures and movements.
[G10H 2220/326] Control glove or other hand or palm-attached control device -- G10H 2220/326 pertains to control gloves or other hand- or palm-attached devices that serve as input/output interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments. These devices allow performers to manipulate sound parameters through gestures, finger movements, or other hand actions.
[G10H 2220/331] Ring or other finger-attached control device -- G10H 2220/331 pertains to finger-worn devices such as rings or similar accessories that function as control elements for electrophonic musical instruments, enabling performers to trigger, adjust or modulate sounds through gestures, pressure or other finger movements.
[G10H 2220/336] Control shoe or boot, i.e. sensor-equipped lower part of lower limb, e.g. shoe, toe ring, sock, ankle bracelet or leg control attachment -- The entry covers sensor-filled footwear or leg accessories that act as control devices for electronic musical instruments, enabling gestures or movements of the lower limb to generate or modify sound signals. It includes shoes, socks, toe rings, ankle bracelets or similar attachments that communicate with the instrument's electronic circuitry.
[G10H 2220/341] Floor sensors, e.g. platform or groundsheet with sensors to detect foot position, balance or pressure, steps, stepping rhythm, dancing movements or jumping -- The code G10H 2220/341 designates floor-mounted sensors, such as platforms or ground sheets, that detect a performer's foot position, balance, pressure, stepping rhythm, dancing movements or jumps and convey that information to electrophonic musical instruments. It is part of the physics-musical-instrument hierarchy for input/output interfacing.
[G10H 2220/346] Hopscotch sensing mats, i.e. including several step sensing zones, e.g. for detection of rhythmic dancing in time to background music according to stepping indications -- G10H 2220/346 covers hopscotch-style sensing mats that contain multiple step-detecting zones and are used to recognise rhythmic foot movements in sync with background music, enabling interactive dance or game applications.
[G10H 2220/351] Environmental parameters, e.g. temperature, ambient light, atmospheric pressure, humidity, used as input for musical purposes -- This classification covers interfaces for electro-phonic musical instruments that use environmental parameters such as temperature, ambient light, atmospheric pressure or humidity as input signals for musical purposes, allowing these conditions to control or modify sound generation or processing.
[G10H 2220/355] Geolocation input, i.e. control of musical parameters based on location or geographic position, e.g. provided by GPS, Wi-Fi® network location databases or mobile phone base station position databases -- G10H 2220/355 covers input and output interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments that use geolocation data to influence sound or performance parameters. It includes systems that obtain position from GPS, Wi-Fi networks, mobile-phone base stations or similar location services.
[G10H 2220/361] Mouth control in general, i.e. breath, mouth, teeth, tongue or lip-controlled input devices or sensors detecting, e.g. lip position, lip vibration, air pressure, air velocity, air flow or air jet angle -- The G10H 2220/361 entry covers input and output interfaces that use the performer's mouth or breath to control electrophonic musical instruments, such as sensors detecting lip position, lip vibration, air pressure, airflow or jet angle. It includes devices where breath, teeth, tongue or lips act as the primary input mechanism for generating or modifying sound. This classification falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2220/365] Bow control in general, i.e. sensors or transducers on a bow -- This classification covers sensors or transducers on a bow used to control electrophonic musical instruments, enabling detection of bow movements, pressure, or position for electronic sound generation or processing. It falls under input/output interfacing for electrophonic musical tools.
[G10H 2220/371] Vital parameter control, i.e. musical instrument control based on body signals, e.g. brainwaves, pulsation, temperature or perspiration -- G10H 2220/371 covers interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments that adjust performance parameters in response to the player's physiological signals such as brainwaves, heartbeat, skin temperature or sweat. The classification applies to systems that translate these vital signs into musical control functions.
[G10H 2220/376] using brain waves, e.g. EEG -- This subclass pertains to methods and devices that use brain-wave signals, such as EEG, to control or interact with electrophonic musical instruments, providing a direct neural interface for sound generation or manipulation.
[G10H 2220/381] using glottal signals from an electroglottograph [EGG] or from a neck-worn glottis pick-up device -- This subclass covers techniques that employ glottal activity measured by an electroglottograph or a neck-mounted glottis pickup as a control or input signal for electrophonic musical instruments, enabling the performer to trigger, modulate or shape electronic sounds directly with vocal fold vibrations.
[G10H 2220/386] using genetic information [DNA] or unique characterizing features of individuals, e.g. fingerprints, iris, facial or vocal features -- The G10H 2220/386 subclass covers interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments that employ personal genetic or biometric data such as DNA, fingerprints, iris patterns, facial characteristics or vocal traits. These features can be used to identify users, customize sound parameters, or control performance functions based on unique individual signatures.
[G10H 2220/391] Angle sensing for musical purposes, using data from a gyroscope, gyrometer or other angular velocity or angular movement sensing device -- The code G10H 2220/391 designates technology that detects angular motion - such as from a gyroscope or gyrometer - and uses that data to control or influence electrophonic musical instruments. It belongs to the physics branch of musical instrument classifications, specifically to input/output interfacing for electronic music devices.
[G10H 2220/395] Acceleration sensing or accelerometer use, e.g. 3D movement computation by integration of accelerometer data, angle sensing with respect to the vertical, i.e. gravity sensing -- The code G10H 2220/395 refers to the use of accelerometers or other motion sensors in electrophonic musical instruments to compute three-dimensional movement or to sense the instrument's angle relative to gravity, enabling gesture-controlled or expressive performance features.
[G10H 2220/401] 3D sensing, i.e. three-dimensional (x, y, z) position or movement sensing -- The G10H 2220/401 subclass covers technologies that provide three-dimensional position or motion detection for electrophonic musical instruments, enabling the instrument to sense x, y, z coordinates of a performer's gestures or the instrument's components. This includes sensors, algorithms and interfaces that translate spatial movement into musical control signals.
[G10H 2220/405] Beam sensing or control, i.e. input interfaces involving substantially immaterial beams, radiation, or fields of any nature, used, e.g. as a switch as in a light barrier, or as a control device, e.g. using the theremin electric field sensing principle -- G10H 2220/405 pertains to input interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments that utilize immaterial beams or fields, such as light barriers or electric fields, for switching or controlling functions, exemplified by the theremin's use of electric field sensing.
[G10H 2220/411] Light beams -- This class covers technologies that use light beams as sensors or transmitters for controlling or communicating with electronic musical devices, such as optical keyboards, laser-based gesture control, or photo-detector interfaces that translate light signals into musical data.
[G10H 2220/415] Infrared beams -- It covers use of infrared light beams as a means of transmitting or receiving control, performance or data signals between an electrophonic musical instrument and external devices. Infrared beams may be used for gestures, proximity detection or wireless linking of keyboards, controllers, or sound modules.
[G10H 2220/421] Laser beams -- G10H 2220/421 covers laser-based input or output devices that connect to electrophonic musical instruments, such as optical scanners, modulators or display systems that translate musical data via laser beams. It is used for interfacing tasks like gesture control, optical encoding of performance parameters, or laser-driven sound generation within the broader field of physics and musical instrument technology.
[G10H 2220/425] Radio control, i.e. input or control device involving a radio frequency signal -- This CPC subclass covers input or control devices that use radio-frequency signals to operate or interface electrophonic musical instruments, such as wireless controllers, remote switches, and radio-based modulation of electronic sound generators. It falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2220/431] Use of microwaves -- G10H 2220/431 pertains to the use of microwave technology in the input or output interfaces of electrophonic musical instruments, covering sensors, transmitters or receivers that employ microwave frequencies to convey musical data or control signals.
[G10H 2220/435] Ultrasound, i.e. input or control device involving inaudible pressure waves, e.g. focused as a beam -- The CPC subclass G10H 2220/435 concerns input or control devices for electrophonic musical instruments that use ultrasound - inaudible pressure waves, often focused as a beam - to influence sound generation or instrument operation.
[G10H 2220/441] Image sensing, i.e. capturing images or optical patterns for musical purposes or musical control purposes -- G10H 2220/441 covers technologies that capture visual information such as images or optical patterns to be used in controlling or generating musical output in electrophonic instruments. It includes sensors and imaging systems that translate visual signals into musical parameters for interfacing with electronic musical tools.
[G10H 2220/445] Bar codes or similar machine readable optical code patterns, e.g. two dimensional mesh pattern, for musical input or control purposes -- The G10H 2220/445 entry concerns the use of bar codes or comparable machine-readable optical patterns, including two-dimensional meshes, as input or control signals for electrophonic musical instruments. It addresses interfacing methods that translate such codes into musical performance data.
[G10H 2220/451] Scanner input, e.g. scanning a paper document such as a musical score for automated conversion into a musical file format -- G10H 2220/451 covers technology for scanning paper documents such as printed musical scores and automatically converting the visual information into a digital music file. It relates to input interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments, enabling the capture of sheet music into an electronic format for playback or editing.
[G10H 2220/455] Camera input, e.g. analyzing pictures from a video camera and using the analysis results as control data -- This classification covers interfaces that connect video cameras to electrophonic musical instruments, where the camera captures images and the image analysis is used to generate control signals for the instrument.
[G10H 2220/461] Transducers, i.e. details, positioning or use of assemblies to detect and convert mechanical vibrations or mechanical strains into an electrical signal, e.g. audio, trigger or control signal -- G10H 2220/461 relates to transducers for electrophonic musical instruments, describing the design, positioning or use of assemblies that detect mechanical vibrations or strains and convert them into electrical signals such as audio, trigger or control signals.
[G10H 2220/465] Bridge-positioned, i.e. assembled to or attached with the bridge of a stringed musical instrument -- This CPC subclass covers bridge-mounted input or output interfaces specially adapted for electrophonic stringed musical instruments, where the device is attached to the instrument's bridge to handle signal conversion or transmission. It includes circuitry, connectors or transducers designed specifically for that position.
[G10H 2220/471] at bottom, i.e. transducer positioned at the bottom of the bridge, between the bridge and the body of the instrument -- This subclass pertains to electrophonic musical instruments that employ a transducer positioned at the bottom of the bridge, between the bridge and the instrument body, to convert string vibrations into electrical signals for input-output interfacing.
[G10H 2220/475] on the side, i.e. picking up vibrations from a side of the bridge -- It covers a pickup arrangement that senses the vibration of an electrophonic instrument by mounting a transducer on the side of the bridge rather than on its top surface. This side-mounted sensor converts the mechanical motion into an electrical signal for further processing in the instrument.
[G10H 2220/481] on top, i.e. transducer positioned between the strings and the bridge structure itself -- The code G10H 2220/481 covers a transducer mounted on top of an electrophonic string instrument, positioned directly between the strings and the bridge structure to sense or modify vibration. This sub-category falls under input/output interfacing for electrophonic musical instruments within the broader physics-musical instruments hierarchy.
[G10H 2220/485] One transducer per string, e.g. 6 transducers for a 6 string guitar -- G10H 2220/485 defines configurations in which each string of an electrophonic string instrument is equipped with its own transducer, for example a six-string guitar fitted with six separate pickups. This allows individual electrical signals to be generated and processed per string and is classified under input/output interfacing for electrophonic musical tools.
[G10H 2220/491] Two or more transducers per string, e.g. 8 transducers on a 4-string violin bridge -- This CPC subclass covers arrangements where each string of an electrophonic musical instrument, such as a violin, is equipped with multiple transducers, for example eight transducers distributed across a four-string bridge, enabling enhanced signal capture or processing.
[G10H 2220/495] Single bridge transducer, common to all strings -- The CPC class G10H 2220/495 covers a single bridge transducer that is shared by all strings of an electrophonic musical instrument. It pertains to the interfacing of input/output devices specifically designed for such instruments, allowing a common pickup or driver mounted on the bridge to capture vibrations from multiple strings.
[G10H 2220/501] Two or more bridge transducers, at least one transducer common to several strings -- G10H 2220/501 pertains to electrophonic musical instruments and defines arrangements where two or more bridge transducers are employed, with at least one transducer shared among several strings, enabling combined signal pickup or actuation across the strings.
[G10H 2220/505] Dual coil electrodynamic string transducer, e.g. for humbucking, to cancel out parasitic magnetic fields -- A dual-coil electrodynamic string transducer uses two coils arranged to generate opposite magnetic fields, producing a humbucking effect that cancels parasitic magnetic interference. It is specifically designed for electrophonic musical instruments, improving signal clarity by reducing unwanted noise from environmental magnetic sources.
[G10H 2220/511] Stacked, i.e. one coil on top of the other -- G10H 2220/511 relates to interfacing arrangements for electrophonic musical instruments where the signal or drive coils are stacked, meaning one coil is placed directly on top of another. This vertical stacking improves magnetic coupling and can reduce noise or size in devices such as pickups, tone generators, or effect units.
[G10H 2220/515] Staggered, i.e. two coils side by side -- The G10H 2220/515 entry covers staggered arrangements in which two coils are positioned side by side for input/output interfacing of electrophonic musical instruments. This configuration is used to improve signal capture, balance, or noise suppression in electronic musical devices.
[G10H 2220/521] Hall effect transducers or similar magnetic field sensing semiconductor devices, e.g. for string vibration sensing or key movement sensing -- This CPC subclass G10H 2220/521 covers Hall-effect and similar magnetic-field sensing semiconductor devices used as input or output interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments, such as sensors that detect string vibration or key movement.
[G10H 2220/525] Piezoelectric transducers for vibration sensing or vibration excitation in the audio range -- G10H 2220/525 classifies piezoelectric transducers that detect or generate vibrations in the audio range for use as input or output interfaces with electrophonic musical instruments. This covers devices that sense or excite sound-frequency vibrations to control or receive signals from such instruments.
[G10H 2220/531] made of piezoelectric film -- G10H 2220/531 refers to input/output interfaces in electrophonic musical instruments that utilize piezoelectric film as a material for sensing or generating sound, enhancing the instrument's responsiveness and tonal quality.
[G10H 2220/535] Piezoelectric polymer transducers, e.g. made of stretched and poled polyvinylidene difluoride [PVDF] sheets in which the molecular chains of vinylidene fluoride CH -- This subclass covers piezoelectric polymer transducers such as stretched and poled PVDF sheets used as input or output interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments, converting mechanical vibrations to electrical signals or vice versa with polymer piezo materials.
[G10H 2220/541] using piezoceramics, e.g. lead titanate [PbTiO -- The entry covers input or output interfaces for electrophonic musical instruments that incorporate piezoceramic components such as lead titanate (PbTiO₃). It addresses devices that convert electrical signals to mechanical motion or vice-versa using the piezoelectric effect to control or sense instrument performance.
[G10H 2220/545] Barium titanate piezoceramics [BaTiO -- The CPC entry G10H 2220/545 covers the application of barium-titanate piezoceramic components in input and output interfaces that are specially adapted for electrophonic musical instruments. It falls under the hierarchy of physics, musical instruments, electrophonic musical instruments, and interfacing, and addresses how the piezoelectric properties of BaTiO₃ are used to convert electrical signals to acoustic output or vice-versa in such devices.
[G10H 2220/551] using LZT or PZT [lead-zirconate-titanate] piezoceramics [Pb[ZrxTi1-x]O3, 0=x=1] -- The CPC entry G10H 2220/551 falls under Physics → Musical Instruments → Electrophonic Musical Instruments and concerns input/output interfacing specially adapted for electrophonic tools that employ lead-zirconate-titanate (LZT or PZT) piezoceramic elements (Pb[ZrₓTi₁₋ₓ]O₃, 0 ≤ x ≤ 1). It covers the use of these piezoceramics in signal conversion, transduction, or control connections for such instruments.
[G10H 2220/555] Bimorph transducers, i.e. piezoelectric bending multilayer structures with one or more piezoelectric layers, e.g. piezo on metal, serial bimorph or parallel bimorph -- The G10H 2220/555 entry covers piezoelectric bimorph transducers-multilayer structures that bend when driven, comprising one or more piezo layers bonded to a metal or other substrate, in serial or parallel configurations. These bending elements are used for input-output interfacing of electrophonic musical instruments, enabling signal conversion or actuation within the instrument's electronic system.
[G10H 2220/561] Piezoresistive transducers, i.e. exhibiting vibration, pressure, force or movement -dependent resistance, e.g. strain gauges, carbon-doped elastomers or polymers for piezoresistive drumpads, carbon microphones -- G10H 2220/561 covers piezoresistive transducers that change resistance in response to vibration, pressure, force or motion, including strain-gauge elements, carbon-doped elastomers and polymers used in electronic drum pads and carbon microphones, and is applied to input/output interfacing for electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2220/565] Shielding, electromagnetic or magnetic, e.g. for transducers, i.e. for controlling, orienting or suppressing magnetic fields or for preventing unintentional generation, propagation and reception of electromagnetic energy in electrophonic musical instruments, their vicinity or their interconnections -- This CPC subclass covers methods and structures that provide electromagnetic or magnetic shielding for transducers and other components of electrophonic musical instruments, aimed at controlling, orienting or suppressing magnetic fields and preventing unintended generation, propagation or reception of electromagnetic energy in the instruments, their surroundings or interconnections.
[G10H 2230/00] General physical, ergonomic or hardware implementation of electrophonic musical tools or instruments, e.g. shape or architecture -- The G10H 2230/00 subclass covers the overall physical and ergonomic design of electrophonic musical instruments, such as the shape, housing, layout, and structural features that affect how the device is held, played, or integrated into an environment. It includes innovations in casing, ergonomics, and mechanical architecture that improve user comfort, durability, or acoustic interaction without addressing the electronic circuitry itself. This subclass is part of the broader group on electrophonic musical instruments within the physics section.
[G10H 2230/005] Device type or category -- This subclass groups electrophonic musical instruments by their overall device type or category, such as keyboard, drum, or wind-style designs, emphasizing the physical and ergonomic architecture rather than the electronic circuitry.
[G10H 2230/011] Hybrid piano, e.g. combined acoustic and electronic piano with complete hammer mechanism as well as key-action sensors coupled to an electronic sound generator -- The entry covers hybrid pianos that integrate a full acoustic hammer mechanism with electronic key-action sensors linked to a digital sound generator, allowing simultaneous acoustic and synthesized output. It addresses the physical and ergonomic design of such electrophonic instruments.
[G10H 2230/015] PDA [personal digital assistant] or palmtop computing devices used for musical purposes, e.g. portable music players, tablet computers, e-readers or smart phones in which mobile telephony functions need not be used -- This subclass covers personal digital assistants, palmtop computers and other portable devices such as music players, tablets, e-readers or smartphones that are employed for musical functions when their mobile-telephony features are not required. [G10H 2230/035] Power management, i.e. specific power supply solutions for electrophonic musical instruments, e.g. auto power shut-off, energy saving designs, power conditioning, connector design, avoiding inconvenient wiring -- G10H 2230/035 covers power-management solutions for electrophonic musical instruments, including automatic shut-off, energy-saving designs, power conditioning, connector configurations and wiring optimisations that improve ergonomics and reliability of the instrument's hardware.

It includes methods such as suppressing less audible tones or reducing their complexity to keep performance stable.

[G10H 2230/051] Spint theremin, i.e. mimicking electrophonic musical instruments in which tones are controlled or triggered in a touch-free manner by interaction with beams, jets or fields, e.g. theremin, air guitar, water jet controlled musical instrument, i.e. hydrolauphone -- The entry covers the physical and ergonomic design of touch-free electrophonic instruments, where sound is generated or modulated by interacting with invisible beams, air or water jets, or electromagnetic fields. It focuses on shape, architecture and hardware aspects of the instrument.
[G10H 2230/215] Spint bagpipe, i.e. mimicking instruments with enclosed reeds fed from a constant reservoir -- The G10H 2230/215 entry covers spint bagpipes, mimicking instruments that use enclosed reeds supplied by a constant air reservoir, and relates to the general physical, ergonomic or hardware implementation of electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2230/221] Spint saxophone, i.e. mimicking conical bore musical instruments with single reed mouthpiece, e.g. saxophones, electrophonic emulation or interfacing aspects therefor -- This classification covers the physical design and ergonomic features of electronic devices that replicate or interface with conical-bore single-reed instruments such as saxophones, focusing on hardware aspects like shape, architecture, and mechanical interaction. It includes elements such as the instrument's casing, mouthpiece attachment and user-interface configuration for electronic emulation.

It classifies inventions that address the shape, architecture or hardware implementation of this electrophonic musical instrument.

It falls under the subclass for hardware implementation of electrophonic musical instruments within the CPC hierarchy.

[G10H 2240/215] Spread spectrum, i.e. transmission on a bandwidth considerably larger than the frequency content of the original information -- The G10H 2240/215 entry addresses spread-spectrum transmission methods applied to electrophonic musical instruments, where the signal is sent over a bandwidth far wider than the original information's frequency content. [G10H 2240/221] Time division multiplexing, with different channels in different time slots, the data in the time slots may be in digital or analog form -- The G10H 2240/221 entry defines time-division multiplexing techniques used in electrophonic musical instruments, where each channel occupies a separate time slot and the slots may carry either digital or analog signals. It pertains to data-organisation and communication methods specifically adapted for such electronic music tools.
[G10H 2240/225] Frequency division multiplexing -- Frequency division multiplexing in this class refers to techniques for separating multiple audio or control signals into distinct frequency bands so they can be transmitted or processed simultaneously through a single channel in electrophonic musical instruments. It addresses data organization and communication within such devices.
[G10H 2240/231] Quadrature modulation, e.g. QAM -- This CPC entry G10H 2240/231, classified under physics, musical instruments and electrophonic musical instruments, addresses quadrature modulation methods such as QAM for organizing or transmitting data within electronic musical tools. [G10H 2240/235] Pulse amplitude modulation, e.g. quantized or analog -- The CPC subclass G10H 2240/235 covers pulse amplitude modulation techniques, including both quantized and analog forms, as applied to the data handling or communication functions of electrophonic musical instruments. [G10H 2240/241] Telephone transmission, i.e. using twisted pair telephone lines or any type of telephone network -- This class covers the use of telephone networks, including twisted-pair lines, to transmit data related to the operation, control or synchronization of electrophonic musical instruments. [G10H 2240/251] Mobile telephone transmission, i.e. transmitting, accessing or controlling music data wirelessly via a wireless or mobile telephone receiver, analogue or digital, e.g. DECT, GSM, UMTS -- The CPC class G10H 2240/251 deals with the wireless transmission, access or control of music-related data via mobile or cordless telephone receivers, whether analog or digital, using standards such as DECT, GSM or UMTS, and is directed at data-organisation and communication functions for electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2240/255] Optical fibre transmission for electrophonic musical instrument purposes, e.g. hum mitigation -- G10H 2240/255 pertains to the application of optical-fibre transmission in electrophonic musical instruments, focusing on data-link or signal-transfer methods that reduce electrical noise such as hum, improve isolation, and enhance overall audio fidelity.
[G10H 2240/261] Satellite transmission for musical instrument purposes, e.g. processing for mitigation of satellite transmission delays -- This subclass concerns the use of satellite links with electrophonic musical instruments, specifically methods that process the signal to reduce or compensate for transmission delays. It includes techniques for managing latency, synchronising playback, and optimizing data flow between the instrument and remote locations via satellite.
[G10H 2240/265] CATV transmission, i.e. electrophonic musical instruments connected to community antennas or cable television networks -- This CPC subclass (G10H 2240/265) deals with data-organisation and communication methods that connect electrophonic musical instruments to community antenna or cable-television networks. It covers the transmission of instrument signals over CATV systems, enabling musical devices to use cable infrastructure for data exchange.

[G10H 2240/275] Musical interface to a personal computer PCI bus, "peripheral component interconnect bus" -- This subclass covers devices that connect electrophonic musical instruments to a personal computer through the PCI (peripheral component interconnect) bus, providing data exchange and control between the instrument and the computer. [G10H 2250/105] Comb filters -- Comb filters are signal-processing structures that create a series of regularly spaced peaks and troughs in a frequency spectrum by adding a delayed version of a signal to itself. [G10H 2250/135] Autocorrelation -- Autocorrelation is a mathematical technique that compares a signal with a delayed version of itself to reveal repeating patterns, periodicities or similarities. In the context of electrophonic musical instruments it is employed for pitch detection, timbre analysis, noise reduction and other signal-processing functions that support electronic music production and synthesis.

[G10H 2250/265] Blackman Harris window -- The G10H 2250/265 entry defines the Blackman Harris window, a tapering cosine function used in digital signal processing to minimise spectral leakage. It falls under physics and electrophonic musical instruments for algorithms and signal-processing methods that are adapted for or employed in electronic music synthesis and analysis.
[G10H 2250/271] Chebyshev window -- The Chebyshev window is a digital signal-processing window that yields a uniform ripple in the frequency-domain side lobes, enabling precise trade-offs between main-lobe width and side-lobe level. It is commonly employed in spectral analysis and algorithmic processing for electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2250/275] Gaussian window -- The entry covers the use of Gaussian window functions in digital signal processing for electrophonic musical instruments, providing time-frequency smoothing and reducing spectral leakage in algorithms such as Fourier transforms, filtering, and synthesis. It is classified under G10H 2250/275 in the CPC hierarchy for aspects of algorithms or signal processing methods adapted to electrophonic musical processing.
[G10H 2250/281] Hamming window -- G10H 2250/281 - Hamming window denotes a specific tapering function used in digital signal processing to minimise spectral leakage during Fourier analysis, a technique frequently applied in the processing of sounds generated by electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2250/285] Hann or Hanning window -- The Hann (or Hanning) window is a cosine-shaped taper applied to a finite data segment before performing a Fourier transform, reducing spectral leakage and smoothing edges. It is widely used in digital audio and other signal-processing applications for windowed analysis and synthesis. The classification places it under electrophonic musical instrument signal-processing methods.
[G10H 2250/291] Kaiser windows -- G10H 2250/291 - Kaiser windows are a family of adjustable window functions used in digital signal processing to control spectral leakage and shape filter responses, especially in the analysis and synthesis of electronic musical sounds. They are defined by a parameter that balances main-lobe width against side-lobe level, making them suitable for electrophonic instrument applications.

[G10H 2250/385] Train, i.e. sounds which are part of a railroad soundscape, e.g. steam engines, diesel, electric, train whistles, rail wheels, railway crossing -- G10H 2250/385 covers the capture, synthesis or processing of train-related sounds-such as steam, diesel or electric locomotives, whistles, rail wheels and crossing signals-for use in electrophonic musical instruments or related signal-processing methods. It pertains to non-musical sound sources that are specifically adapted to musical contexts.
[G10H 2250/391] Gensound footsteps, i.e. footsteps, kicks or tap-dancing sounds -- G10H 2250/391 refers to algorithmic or signal-processing techniques used to generate or modify footstep-type sounds such as footsteps, kicks or tap-dancing noises in electronic musical instruments, without any intrinsic musical meaning. The class is part of the physics-musical instruments hierarchy and deals with electrophonic sound synthesis rather than musical composition.
[G10H 2250/395] Gensound nature -- G10H 2250/395, titled Gensound nature, covers algorithms and signal-processing techniques that are employed in electrophonic musical instruments but lack intrinsic musical content. The classification addresses methods for generating, shaping or analyzing sound where the primary purpose is technical rather than musical, such as synthesis engines, sound-modeling or acoustic-to-digital conversion adapted for musical use.
[G10H 2250/401] Crowds, e.g. restaurant, waiting hall, demonstration or subway corridor at rush hour -- This CPC entry G10H 2250/401 treats algorithms and signal-processing methods that are not musical per se but are tailored for electrophonic musical applications involving crowd environments such as restaurants, waiting halls, demonstrations or rush-hour subway corridors. It addresses how to capture, model, filter or otherwise manage the acoustic characteristics of dense human assemblies in these settings for use in electronic music processing.
[G10H 2250/405] Fire, e.g. cracks and pops of burning wood -- The G10H 2250/405 entry covers fire-related sound phenomena such as cracks and pops of burning wood when they are employed in signal-processing algorithms or methods for electrophonic musical instruments, without possessing an intrinsic musical character. It falls under aspects of algorithms or signal processing adapted for or used in electrophonic musical processing within the broader physics-musical instruments hierarchy.
[G10H 2250/411] Water, e.g. seashore, waves, brook, waterfall, dripping faucet -- G10H 2250/411 covers algorithms and signal-processing techniques that generate or manipulate natural water sounds-such as seashore waves, brook flow, waterfall, or dripping faucet-within electrophonic musical instruments. The subclass is for non-musical sound sources that are adapted for use in electronic music processing.
[G10H 2250/415] Weather -- Covers algorithmic and signal-processing techniques that use weather data to control or generate sounds in electronic musical instruments, such as simulating wind, rain or temperature effects for synthesis or effects processing. The methods are not inherently musical but are adapted specifically for electrophonic music applications.
[G10H 2250/421] Rain -- G10H 2250/421 Rain relates to signal-processing algorithms or methods that create or emulate the sound of rain for use in electronic musical instruments. The techniques are not musical per se but are adapted for electrophonic music processing, such as rainfall synthesis, spatialization, or modulation effects.
[G10H 2250/425] Thunder -- Thunder is a signal-processing method for electrophonic musical instruments that generates thunder-like sound effects by shaping noise, applying filters and dynamic envelopes, and may be used in synthesis or electronic percussion. It is classified under G10H 2250/425 for algorithms adapted to musical processing without intrinsic musical character.
[G10H 2250/431] Natural aerodynamic noises, e.g. wind gust sounds, rustling leaves or beating sails -- G10H 2250/431 covers natural aerodynamic sounds such as wind gusts, rustling leaves or beating sails that are employed in electrophonic musical instruments or related signal-processing algorithms. The entry groups these non-musical acoustic phenomena when they are adapted for electronic music synthesis or analysis.
[G10H 2250/435] Gensound percussion, i.e. generating or synthesising the sound of a percussion instrument -- The CPC entry G10H 2250/435 covers methods and algorithms for electronically generating or synthesising percussion sounds, including signal-processing techniques used in electrophonic musical instruments that lack an intrinsic musical character but are specifically adapted for percussion sound production. It falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2250/441] Gensound string, i.e. generating the sound of a string instrument, controlling specific features of said sound -- This subclass covers techniques for digitally generating and shaping the sound of stringed instruments in electrophonic music devices, including algorithms and signal-processing methods that control parameters such as pitch, articulation, resonance and timbre. It relates to physics-based musical instruments and focuses on non-musical algorithmic processes specifically adapted for string sound synthesis.
[G10H 2250/445] Bowed string instrument sound generation, controlling specific features of said sound, e.g. use of fret or bow control parameters for violin effects synthesis -- G10H 2250/445 pertains to the digital synthesis and processing techniques specifically designed to emulate the unique sounds and playing characteristics of bowed string instruments like violins, focusing on aspects such as bowing and fretting to achieve realistic musical effects.
[G10H 2250/451] Plucked or struck string instrument sound synthesis, controlling specific features of said sound -- This CPC subclass covers methods and algorithms for synthesising the sound of plucked or struck string instruments, where the process allows direct control over particular acoustic parameters such as timbre, attack, decay or pitch, without involving musical content itself. It is used for electrophonic musical instrument applications.
[G10H 2250/455] Gensound singing voices, i.e. generation of human voices for musical applications, vocal singing sounds or intelligible words at a desired pitch or with desired vocal effects, e.g. by phoneme synthesis -- G10H 2250/455 concerns the creation of artificial singing voices for musical use, encompassing techniques that generate human-like vocal sounds, intelligible words or melodic pitch and vocal effects through phoneme synthesis and related signal-processing algorithms, without the sounds themselves being intrinsically musical.
[G10H 2250/461] Gensound wind instruments, i.e. generating or synthesising the sound of a wind instrument, controlling specific features of said sound -- The CPC entry G10H 2250/461 covers technologies that generate or synthesize the sound of wind instruments by electronic means, allowing control of specific acoustic features such as timbre, articulation or pitch. It falls under the physics-musical instruments hierarchy and pertains to algorithmic or signal-processing methods that are not musical in themselves but are applied to electrophonic musical processing.
[G10H 2250/465] Reed instrument sound synthesis, controlling specific features of said sound -- G10H 2250/465 pertains to techniques for synthesising reed-instrument sounds and for controlling specific acoustic features of those sounds within electophonic musical devices. It falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments and addresses algorithmic or signal-processing methods that lack intrinsic musical character but are specially adapted for musical processing.
[G10H 2250/471] General musical sound synthesis principles, i.e. sound category-independent synthesis methods -- G10H 2250/471 is a Cooperative Patent Classification entry that covers general musical sound synthesis principles, describing algorithmic and signal-processing methods that are sound-category independent but specifically adapted for use in electrophonic instruments. It is grouped under Physics, Musical Instruments, Electrophonic Musical Instruments and aspects of algorithms or signal processing methods without intrinsic musical character yet used in electronic music processing.
[G10H 2250/475] FM synthesis, i.e. altering the timbre of simple waveforms by frequency modulating them with frequencies also in the audio range, resulting in different-sounding tones exhibiting more complex waveforms -- Subclass G10H 2250/475 relates to FM synthesis, a technique that changes the timbre of basic waveforms by modulating them with frequencies in the audio range, producing complex tones with richer harmonic content. It is applied in electrophonic musical instruments and falls under the physics-musical-instruments hierarchy.
[G10H 2250/481] Formant synthesis, i.e. simulating the human speech production mechanism by exciting formant resonators, e.g. mimicking vocal tract filtering as in LPC synthesis vocoders, wherein musical instruments may be used as excitation signal to the time-varying filter estimated from a singer's speech -- G10H 2250/481 refers to formant synthesis techniques that simulate human speech production by exciting resonant formant filters, often using musical-instrument signals as the excitation source, as employed in LPC-based vocoders for electrophonic musical processing.
[G10H 2250/485] Formant correction therefor -- G10H 2250/485 covers methods and devices for correcting formants in electric or electronic musical instruments, including algorithmic processing that adjusts the spectral shape of a sound without adding musical content. It is used to improve timbre accuracy or compensate for changes caused by transposition, pitch shifting, or other signal modifications in electrophonic music applications.
[G10H 2250/491] Formant interpolation therefor -- G10H 2250/491 relates to formant interpolation methods employed in electrophonic musical instruments, describing algorithmic or signal-processing techniques that lack intrinsic musical character but are specifically adapted for musical sound synthesis or processing. It sits within the physics branch of the cooperative patent classification, under musical instruments and electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2250/495] Use of noise in formant synthesis -- This class covers methods that intentionally add or shape noise within formant synthesis techniques to generate or modify timbres in electronic musical instruments. It includes algorithmic approaches for controlling noise spectra and amplitude to affect vowel-like resonances used in synthetic speech or instrument sounds.
[G10H 2250/501] Formant frequency shifting, sliding formants -- The G10H 2250/501 entry covers algorithms that shift or glide formant frequencies in electronic musical signal processing, enabling dynamic timbre changes such as vowel-like modulation or vocal-style effects. It applies to electrophonic instruments where formant manipulation is used without any inherent musical content.
[G10H 2250/505] Parcor synthesis, i.e. music synthesis using partial autocorrelation techniques, e.g. in which the impulse response of the digital filter in a parcor speech synthesizer is used as a musical signal -- G10H 2250/505 covers parcor synthesis, a method of music generation that applies partial autocorrelation techniques by using the impulse response of a digital filter-originally from a speech synthesizer-as a musical signal. It falls under the physics-musical instruments hierarchy for electrophonic instruments, specifically algorithms or signal-processing methods adapted for musical use.
[G10H 2250/511] Physical modelling or real-time simulation of the acoustomechanical behaviour of acoustic musical instruments using, e.g. waveguides or looped delay lines -- G10H 2250/511 concerns techniques that physically model or simulate in real time the acoustomechanical behavior of acoustic musical instruments, often using waveguides or looped delay lines, to recreate their sound generation characteristics within electrophonic instruments.
[G10H 2250/515] Excitation circuits or excitation algorithms therefor -- The CPC subclass G10H 2250/515 concerns excitation circuits or the algorithms that generate excitation signals for electrophonic musical instruments. It covers electronic methods that initiate or sustain sound production, such as oscillator designs, driver circuits and digital excitation techniques, without implying any musical content. This entry sits under physics - musical instruments - electrophonic musical instruments and addresses signal-processing aspects used in instrument excitation.
[G10H 2250/521] Closed loop models therefor, e.g. with filter and delay line -- Closed-loop models therefor, e.g. with filter and delay line entries cover algorithmic signal-processing structures used in electronic musical instruments where the output is fed back through components such as filters or delay lines, creating feedback loops that shape timbre or sustain. The code applies to methods that are not intrinsically musical but are specifically adapted for electrophonic music processing.
[G10H 2250/525] Pluridimensional array-based models therefor -- The CPC subclass G10H 2250/525 covers pluridimensional array-based models used in algorithmic or signal-processing methods that are not intrinsically musical but are applied to electrophonic musical instruments. It sits under physics, musical instruments and electrophonic musical instruments and deals with mathematical structures for handling multi-dimensional audio data.
[G10H 2250/531] Room models, i.e. acoustic physical modelling of a room, e.g. concert hall -- The entry G10H 2250/531 covers acoustic physical modelling of rooms such as concert halls, used to simulate the sound field in electrophonic musical-instrument processing. It pertains to algorithmic or signal-processing methods that replicate room acoustics rather than to any intrinsic musical content, and falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments → non-musical algorithm aspects.
[G10H 2250/535] Waveguide or transmission line-based models -- G10H 2250/535 covers waveguide or transmission line-based models used in electrophonic musical instrument processing. It includes mathematical or algorithmic representations that simulate signal propagation, filtering, or resonant behavior in electronic sound-generation and processing systems, without intrinsic musical content.
[G10H 2250/541] Details of musical waveform synthesis, i.e. audio waveshape processing from individual wavetable samples, independently of their origin or of the sound they represent -- The CPC subclass G10H 2250/541 covers technical details of audio waveform synthesis, specifically processing individual wavetable samples to shape sound waves independent of their musical origin, and is applied in electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2250/545] Aliasing, i.e. preventing, eliminating or deliberately using aliasing noise, distortions or artifacts in sampled or synthesised waveforms, e.g. by band limiting, oversampling or undersampling, respectively -- G10H 2250/545 covers techniques for handling aliasing in sampled or synthesised audio signals. It includes methods to prevent, eliminate or deliberately exploit aliasing noise, distortions or artifacts by means such as band limiting, oversampling, undersampling or other related signal-processing approaches.
[G10H 2250/551] Waveform approximation, e.g. piecewise approximation of sinusoidal or complex waveforms -- G10H 2250/551 covers methods for approximating waveforms, such as piecewise representations of sinusoidal or complex signals, used in electronic musical instrument processing. It pertains to algorithmic or signal-processing techniques that lack intrinsic musical meaning but are specifically adapted for electrophonic music synthesis or analysis.
[G10H 2250/555] Piecewise linear waveform approximation -- This subclass covers methods that approximate audio waveforms with piecewise linear segments for use in electrophonic musical instrument signal processing. It includes algorithms that break a waveform into linear pieces to reduce computational load while preserving perceptual qualities.
[G10H 2250/561] Parabolic waveform approximation, e.g. using second order polynomials or parabolic responses -- G10H 2250/561 covers the use of parabolic waveform approximation-such as fitting a second-order polynomial or employing a parabolic filter response-to model or shape audio signals in electronic music processing.
[G10H 2250/565] Polynomial waveform approximation, i.e. using polynomials of third order or higher -- The G10H 2250/565 class covers the use of polynomial functions of third order or higher to approximate waveforms in signal-processing algorithms for electrophonic musical instruments. It pertains to mathematical methods that model or generate audio signals, without involving the musical content itself.
[G10H 2250/571] Waveform compression, adapted for music synthesisers, sound banks or wavetables -- G10H 2250/571 pertains to waveform compression techniques specifically tailored for use in music synthesizers, sound banks, or wavetables. These methods optimize storage and processing of audio samples while maintaining sound quality essential for musical applications.
[G10H 2250/575] Adaptive MDCT-based compression, e.g. using a hybrid subband-MDCT, as in ATRAC -- G10H 2250/575 covers adaptive compression techniques that employ modified discrete cosine transform (MDCT) blocks, often combined with subband filtering, as used in audio codecs such as ATRAC. The classification is applied to signal-processing algorithms designed for electronic musical instruments.
[G10H 2250/581] Codebook-based waveform compression -- The G10H 2250/581 class covers codebook-based waveform compression techniques that represent audio signals by matching segments to entries in a predefined codebook, thereby reducing data size for storage or transmission. It addresses algorithmic signal-processing methods used in electrophonic musical instruments without intrinsic musical content.
[G10H 2250/585] CELP [code excited linear prediction] -- Category G10H 2250/585 covers the use of code-excited linear prediction (CELP) techniques in signal processing for electrophonic musical instruments. It addresses algorithmic methods that, while not intrinsically musical, are adapted for audio synthesis, compression, or enhancement in electronic music equipment.
[G10H 2250/591] DPCM [delta pulse code modulation] -- G10H 2250/591 pertains to delta pulse code modulation, a differential form of PCM used in signal-processing algorithms for electrophonic musical instruments, addressing technical aspects of the method rather than musical content.
[G10H 2250/595] ADPCM [adaptive differential pulse code modulation] -- G10H 2250/595 classifies the application of adaptive differential pulse-code modulation (ADPCM) in electrophonic musical-instrument signal processing, where the algorithm compresses audio data by adaptively encoding sample differences rather than absolute values. It covers ADPCM use in electronic instruments without implying any inherent musical function of the method itself.
[G10H 2250/601] Compressed representations of spectral envelopes, e.g. LPC [linear predictive coding], LAR [log area ratios], LSP [line spectral pairs], reflection coefficients -- G10H 2250/601 covers compact ways of representing spectral envelopes in electrophonic musical instrument processing, including linear predictive coding, log-area ratios, line spectral pairs and reflection coefficients. The entry is for algorithmic or signal-processing methods adapted to, but not inherently musical, audio synthesis or analysis.
[G10H 2250/605] Dynamic range companding algorithms, e.g. "mu"-law, primarily used in the digital telephone systems of North America and Japan, or A-law as used in European digital telephone systems -- The entry concerns dynamic-range companding algorithms, notably μ-law and A-law, that compress and expand audio signals to fit the limited levels of digital telephone networks. These methods are classified under electrophonic musical instrument processing despite lacking intrinsic musical content, reflecting their adaptation for signal handling in North American, Japanese and European digital telephony systems.
[G10H 2250/611] Waveform decimation, i.e. integer division of the sampling rate for reducing the number of samples in a discrete-time signal, e.g. by low-pass anti-alias filtering followed by the actual downsampling -- This CPC subclass covers methods for reducing the sampling rate of a discrete-time signal by integer division, i.e. waveform decimation, typically achieved by applying a low-pass anti-alias filter followed by down-sampling. It applies to electrophonic musical instruments and related signal-processing algorithms without intrinsic musical content.
[G10H 2250/615] Waveform editing, i.e. setting or modifying parameters for waveform synthesis -- G10H 2250/615 covers waveform editing, the setting or modification of parameters used in waveform synthesis for electronic musical instruments. It is classified under physics, musical instruments, electrophonic musical instruments and relates to algorithmic or signal processing methods adapted for electrophonic music processing.
[G10H 2250/621] Waveform interpolation -- G10H 2250/621 relates to waveform interpolation techniques used in electronic musical instruments, describing algorithms that mathematically generate intermediate audio samples between known points to enhance sound quality, enable pitch shifting or time-stretching, and provide smoother signal reconstruction in electrophonic music processing.
[G10H 2250/625] Interwave interpolation, i.e. interpolating between two different waveforms, e.g. timbre or pitch or giving one waveform the shape of another while preserving its frequency or -- G10H 2250/625 covers interwave interpolation techniques that blend or transform one waveform into another, such as changing timbre or pitch while keeping the original frequency unchanged. It pertains to signal-processing algorithms used in electrophonic musical instruments, without intrinsic musical content.
[G10H 2250/631] Waveform resampling, i.e. sample rate conversion or sample depth conversion -- G10H 2250/631 covers techniques for converting digital audio waveforms from one sample rate or bit depth to another, including interpolation, decimation and related signal-processing operations used in electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 2250/635] Waveform resolution or sound quality selection, e.g. selection of high or low sampling rates, lossless, lossy or lossier compression algorithms -- G10H 2250/635 covers methods for selecting waveform resolution or sound quality in electrophonic musical instruments, such as choosing high or low sampling rates and applying lossless, lossy or progressively more lossy compression algorithms.
[G10H 2250/641] Waveform sampler, i.e. music samplers -- G10H 2250/641 relates to waveform samplers, or music samplers, that capture, store and reproduce audio signals for electronic music creation. It addresses algorithmic and signal-processing methods used in electrophonic musical instruments, focusing on the technical handling of sampled sound rather than the musical content itself.
[G10H 2250/645] Waveform scaling, i.e. amplitude value normalisation -- The CPC entry G10H 2250/645 covers methods for scaling waveforms by normalising their amplitude values in electrophonic musical-instrument processing. It includes algorithmic techniques that adjust signal levels without adding musical content, used to standardise signal strength for synthesis, sampling or effects.
[G10H 3/00] Instruments in which the tones are generated by electromechanical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Instruments in which a mechanically moving part is caused to move at the frequency of the generated note, and in which this movement is sensed by a movement sensor other than a microphone.  Details of movement transducers therefor, e.g. magnetic guitar pick-up;  Instrument-specific adaptations for contact microphones.  Audio signal processing specially adapted for further musical processing of signals from said transducers or for musical parameter extraction.  Percussion synthesis or drumpad triggers, even if the mechanically moving part is non-resonant, i.e. does not have a frequency of oscillation, see in particular G10H3/146 .
[G10H 3/02] using mechanical interrupters -- G10H 3/02 pertains to electrophonic musical instruments whose tones are produced by electromechanical means, specifically using mechanical interrupters to break or modulate an electric current and thereby generate sound. It is nested under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 3/03] using pick-up means for reading recorded waves, e.g. on rotating discs -- G10H 3/03 covers techniques that employ pick-up means to read recorded waveforms, such as those encoded on rotating discs, within electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 3/06] using photoelectric pick-up means -- This subclass concerns electrophonic musical instruments that generate tones by means of a photoelectric pickup, where light is modulated by a moving element (such as a string or reed) to produce an electrical signal for sound amplification. The method replaces traditional magnetic or piezoelectric transducers with optical sensing to capture the mechanical vibrations.
[G10H 3/08] using inductive pick-up means -- G10H 3/08 refers to electromechanical musical instruments that employ inductive pick-up means to convert string or other vibrating elements into electrical signals for amplification. The technology uses a magnet and coil to generate a voltage proportional to the mechanical motion, as found in electric guitars and similar instruments.
[G10H 3/09] using tapes or wires -- G10H 3/09 is a CPC sub-class for electrophonic musical instruments in which the tones are generated by electromechanical means, specifically employing tapes or wires to produce or shape the sound. It falls under the hierarchy Physics → Musical Instruments → Electrophonic Musical Instruments.
[G10H 3/10] using capacitive pick-up means -- The G10H 3/10 subclass covers electromechanical musical instruments that employ capacitive pick-up means to detect string or other vibrations and convert them into electric signals. It includes designs where a changing capacitance between a conductive element and the vibrating source is used as the primary method of sound pickup.
[G10H 3/12] using mechanical resonant generators, e.g. strings or percussive instruments, the tones of which are picked up by electromechanical transducers, the electrical signals being further manipulated or amplified and subsequently converted to sound by a loudspeaker or equivalent instrument -- G10H 3/12 covers electrophonic musical instruments that produce tones with mechanical resonators such as strings or percussive elements, capture the vibrations using electromechanical transducers, then manipulate or amplify the resulting electrical signals before reproducing the sound through a loudspeaker or equivalent device.
[G10H 3/125] Extracting or recognising the pitch or fundamental frequency of the picked up signal -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any pitch analysis for musical parameter extraction of an audio signal not specifically using a mechanical resonant generator.  This includes: note extraction, score transcription, performance evaluation e.g. of karaoke singing, pitch processing for query by humming.
