[G10H 3/14] using mechanically actuated vibrators with pick-up means -- The G10H 3/14 subclass covers electrophonic musical instruments that generate tones by mechanically driven vibrators whose vibrations are captured with a pick-up device. It includes devices such as string or reed actuators that are set in motion by mechanical means and then amplified electrically.
[G10H 3/143] characterised by the use of a piezoelectric or magneto-strictive transducer -- The G10H 3/143 subclass covers electro-phonic musical instruments that generate sound by means of a piezoelectric or magneto-strictive transducer, converting mechanical vibrations into electrical signals for sound production. This falls within the physics hierarchy under musical instruments and specifically electrophonic instruments.
[G10H 3/146] using a membrane, e.g. a drum; Pick-up means for vibrating surfaces, e.g. housing of an instrument -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electronic drums (see also Indexing Code G10H2230/275 ("spint drums"));  Vibration sensors sensing the vibrations of instrument bodies, also of guitars or other stringed instruments.
[G10H 3/16] using a reed -- G10H 3/16 relates to electrophonic musical instruments that generate tones by means of a vibrating reed, typically in electric organs or reed-based wind controllers. The code classifies devices where the reed is part of an electromechanical sound-generation system.
[G10H 3/18] using a string, e.g. electric guitar -- G10H 3/18 covers electromechanical musical instruments that produce tones with a string, such as electric guitars. It falls under physics, musical instruments, electophonic musical instruments, focusing on the use of a vibrating string to generate sound amplified electrically.
[G10H 3/181] Details of pick-up assemblies -- The classification G10H 3/181 covers the specific structural and functional aspects of pick-up assemblies used in electromechanical musical instruments, such as guitars or amplified strings. It includes the design, mounting, wiring, magnetic or piezoelectric elements, and any adjustment mechanisms that affect how vibrations are converted into electrical signals.
[G10H 3/182] using two or more pick-up means for each string -- G10H 3/182 covers electrophonic stringed instruments that employ two or more pickup devices on each string to sense its vibration, enhancing signal capture or tonal variety. It falls under the physics-related class for musical instruments that generate sound by electromechanical means.
[G10H 3/183] in which the position of the pick-up means is adjustable -- The CPC subclass G10H 3/183 covers electrophonic musical instruments whose pickup assembly can be moved or repositioned by the player. By allowing the pickup's location to be adjusted, the instrument can alter the point of sound capture, affecting tonal balance, volume, or timbre. This feature is found in devices that generate tones by electromechanical means, such as certain electric guitars or basses with adjustable bridge or body pickups.
[G10H 3/185] in which the tones are picked up through the bridge structure -- This CPC subclass covers electrophonic musical instruments whose sound is captured by a pickup attached to or integrated into the bridge of the instrument, converting bridge vibrations into electrical signals for amplification. Such designs are used in electric guitars and similar stringed instruments to convey the vibrations of the strings through the bridge.
[G10H 3/186] Means for processing the signal picked up from the strings  filtering  G10H1/12 -- In the Cooperative Patent Classification, G10H 3/186 covers means for processing the signal picked up from the strings, such as filtering, in electrophonic musical instruments where tones are generated by electromechanical means. This entry falls under the hierarchy Physics - Musical Instruments - Electrophonic Musical Instruments.
[G10H 3/187] for distorting the signal, e.g. to simulate tube amplifiers  changing the tone color by non-linear elements  G10H1/16 -- This CPC subgroup concerns electrophonic musical instruments in which the audio signal is deliberately altered to produce distortion or tube-amplifier emulation, typically by applying nonlinear circuit elements that change the tone colour, covering methods and circuits for signal-shaping within electronic instrument designs.
[G10H 3/188] for converting the signal to digital format  transmission using a MIDI interface  G10H1/0066 -- This CPC subclass covers equipment that transforms the output of electromechanical musical instruments into a digital representation and transmits it via a MIDI interface. It includes analog-to-digital converters and the necessary formatting circuitry for MIDI communication.
[G10H 3/20] using a tuning fork, rod or tube -- G10H 3/20 covers electrophonic musical instruments that produce sound by stimulating a tuning fork, rod or tube, where the vibration of the resonant element is converted into an electrical signal for amplification or processing. The classification applies to devices that use electromechanical transducers to capture the tone of these acoustic elements for further electronic treatment.
[G10H 3/22] using electromechanically actuated vibrators with pick-up means -- G10H 3/22 relates to electrophonic musical instruments in which sound is produced by electromechanically driven vibrators whose motion is captured by a pickup device. The entry covers the use of mechanical actuators powered electrically to generate tones that are then converted into electrical signals.
[G10H 3/24] incorporating feedback means, e.g. acoustic -- G10H 3/24 pertains to electrophonic musical instruments that generate tones by electromechanical means and incorporate feedback mechanisms, such as acoustic feedback, to modify or sustain the produced sound.
[G10H 3/26] using electric feedback -- This subclass covers electrophonic musical instruments that employ electric feedback to modify or sustain the sound produced. It includes methods where the amplified signal is re-introduced to the transducer or speaker to create self-oscillation or enhanced tonal effects.
[G10H 5/00] Instruments in which the tones are generated by means of electronic generators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Generation of musical tones by analogue electronic circuits.  Voice controlled instruments, even if the voice processing is performed by computer, and even if the output tone is synthesised from a data store.  Physical modelling of acoustic instruments, e.g. implemented by appropriate software.  Simulation of analogue circuits using digital means.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Instruments in which the tones are synthesised from a data store, e.g. computer organs -> G10H7/00
[G10H 5/002] Instruments using voltage controlled oscillators and amplifiers or voltage controlled oscillators and filters, e.g. Synthesisers -- G10H 5/002 classifies electrophonic musical instruments that generate tones using voltage-controlled oscillators combined with amplifiers or filters, a category that includes electronic synthesizers and similar devices.
[G10H 5/005] Voice controlled instruments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electrophonic musical instruments in which the output sound is controlled by processing the human voice or glottal signals of the performer in order to control parameters of the output audio signals, e.g. a trumpet sound, controlled by voice.  This is the correct classification for voice-controlled instruments even if the musical voice processing is performed by computer, and even if the output tone is synthesised from a data store under the control of the processed voice signals.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Recording/reproducing of accompaniment for use with an external source, e.g. karaoke systems: with means for modifying or correcting the external signal, e.g. pitch correction, reverberation, changing a singer's voice -> G10H1/366
[G10H 5/007] Real-time simulation of  G10B ,  G10C ,  G10D -type instruments using recursive or non-linear techniques, e.g. waveguide networks, recursive algorithms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Physical modelling of acoustic instruments implemented by digital or analogue means (e.g. using computer based simulation).
[G10H 5/02] using generation of basic tones -- This CPC subclass concerns electrophonic musical instruments that create basic tones by electronic generators such as oscillators or waveform synthesizers. It covers methods for producing the fundamental pitch directly from electronic circuitry, rather than from acoustic or mechanical sources.
[G10H 5/04] with semiconductor devices as active elements -- G10H 5/04 covers electrophonic musical instruments that employ semiconductor devices such as transistors, diodes or integrated circuits as the active elements for generating or shaping tones, rather than acoustic or purely electromechanical means.
[G10H 5/06] tones generated by frequency multiplication or division of a basic tone -- G10H 5/06 covers electophonic musical instruments that produce tones by multiplying or dividing the frequency of a basic tone, such as using octave doublers, harmonic generators, or subharmonic synthesizers. The method alters pitch by integer ratios rather than direct waveform generation.
[G10H 5/07] resulting in complex waveforms -- The CPC class G10H 5/07 covers electrophonic musical instruments that generate tones with electronic generators and use methods that produce complex waveforms, such as combining multiple sine waves or applying modulation to create richer timbres.
[G10H 5/08] tones generated by heterodyning -- G10H 5/08 covers electophonic musical instruments that create tones by heterodyning, i.e., mixing two electronic frequencies to produce new audible sounds. It applies to devices such as synthesizers and electronic organs where tone generation relies on frequency conversion rather than acoustic vibration of strings or reeds.
[G10H 5/10] using generation of non-sinusoidal basic tones, e.g. saw-tooth -- The CPC code G10H 5/10 covers electronic musical instruments that create basic tones with non-sinusoidal waveforms, such as saw-tooth or square waves. It pertains to the method of tone generation rather than the instrument's physical structure.
[G10H 5/12] using semiconductor devices as active elements -- G10H 5/12 covers electrophonic musical instruments whose sound-generation circuitry employs semiconductor devices, such as transistors or diodes, as the active elements that amplify, shape or produce the audio signal. This subclass falls under physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments.
[G10H 5/14] using electromechanical resonators, e.g. quartz crystals, as frequency determining element -- In electrophonic musical instruments, this subclass covers the use of electromechanical resonators, such as quartz crystals, as the frequency-determining element that controls the pitch of the generated tones. It pertains to devices where electronic generators produce sound and a resonator sets the oscillation frequency.
[G10H 5/16] using cathode ray tubes -- G10H 5/16 pertains to electrophonic musical instruments that employ cathode ray tubes to generate tones electronically. It is a subcategory of physics → musical instruments → electrophonic musical instruments, addressing the specific use of CRT technology in sound production.
[G10H 7/00] Instruments in which the tones are synthesised from a data store, e.g. computer organs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Computer architecture, computing hardware or waveform computation schemes specific to digital music synthesis.
[G10H 7/002] using a common processing for different operations or calculations, and a set of microinstructions, e.g. programs, to control the sequence thereof -- G10H 7/002 relates to electrophonic musical instruments that use a common processing unit to carry out various operations, with a set of microinstruction programs governing the calculation sequence for tone synthesis from stored data. This enables a single processor to execute functions such as waveform generation, filtering and control under a unified instruction set.
[G10H 7/004] with one or more auxiliary processor in addition to the main processing unit -- The CPC entry G10H 7/004 classifies electrophonic musical instruments-such as computer organs whose sounds are synthesized from stored data-that employ a main processing unit together with one or more auxiliary processors. The auxiliary processors support or enhance the primary unit's functions, enabling more complex sound generation, effects processing, or control tasks within the instrument. This code falls under Physics → Musical Instruments → Electrophonic Musical Instruments.
[G10H 7/006] using two or more algorithms of different types to generate tones, e.g. according to tone color or to processor workload -- This CPC subclass G10H 7/006 covers electrophonic musical instruments that generate tones using two or more different algorithms, for example one based on tone colour and another adjusted for processor workload. It applies to computer-organ style synthesizers that synthesize sounds from stored data.
[G10H 7/008] Means for controlling the transition from one tone waveform to another -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transition processing or controlling from one tone or music waveform to another, or from one music segment or music piece to another; means therefor.
[G10H 7/02] in which amplitudes at successive sample points of a tone waveform are stored in one or more memories -- This subclass of electrophonic musical instruments covers devices that generate tones by storing the amplitude values of successive sample points of a waveform in memory and reading them out to produce sound, typical of digital samplers, computer organs, and other sample-based synthesizers.
[G10H 7/04] in which amplitudes are read at varying rates, e.g. according to pitch -- G10H 7/04 covers electrophonic musical instruments that generate tones by reading stored amplitude data at different playback rates to produce varying pitches, as seen in computer-controlled organs and similar synthesizers.
[G10H 7/045] using an auxiliary register or set of registers, e.g. a shift-register, in which the amplitudes are transferred before being read -- This classification covers electrophonic musical instruments where an auxiliary register or series of registers such as shift-registers temporarily store amplitude data before it is accessed for sound generation, enabling buffering or processing of waveform levels. It applies to systems that transfer amplitudes between registers prior to reading them to shape or control the output tone.
[G10H 7/06] in which amplitudes are read at a fixed rate, the read-out address varying stepwise by a given value, e.g. according to pitch -- G10H 7/06 concerns electronic musical instruments that generate tones by reading amplitude data from a stored waveform at a constant sampling rate while incrementally stepping the read-out address to produce pitch changes, a technique typical of computer-organ synthesis.
[G10H 7/08] by calculating functions or polynomial approximations to evaluate amplitudes at successive sample points of a tone waveform -- The subclass G10H 7/08 covers electronic musical-instrument synthesis methods that generate tones by mathematically computing waveform amplitudes, using functions or polynomial approximations evaluated at successive sample points. This approach defines the sound through algorithmic calculations rather than stored samples, enabling real-time generation of tones in computer-controlled organs and similar devices.
[G10H 7/10] using coefficients or parameters stored in a memory, e.g. Fourier coefficients -- The subclass G10H 7/10 pertains to electrophonic musical instruments that generate tones by retrieving and using stored coefficients or parameters, such as Fourier coefficients, from a memory device. It covers instruments where sound synthesis relies on data-driven algorithms rather than live acoustic generation.
[G10H 7/105] using Fourier coefficients -- The subclass G10H 7/105 concerns the use of Fourier coefficients in electrophonic musical instruments that generate sounds from digital data, such as computer organs. It addresses how these coefficients are applied to synthesize tones.
[G10H 7/12] by means of a recursive algorithm using one or more sets of parameters stored in a memory and the calculated amplitudes of one or more preceding sample points -- G10H 7/12 covers electrophonic musical instruments that generate tones by applying a recursive algorithm, using one or more parameter sets stored in memory and the calculated amplitudes of preceding sample points. This method synthesises sounds from a data store, such as in computer-organ or digital synthesiser designs.
[G10K] SOUND-PRODUCING DEVICES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices that produce a sound by striking a resonating body, such as bells, chimes, gong.  Whistles, siren devices that produce a sound driven by rotating member, motor, or fluids, e.g., compressed gas.  Devices that produce a sound by vibrating a diaphragm or analogous element, such as foghorns, vehicle hooter, buzzer.  Methods or devices that transmit, conduct, or direct a sound wave.  Methods or devices for protection against, or damping of, acoustic waves.  Cones, diaphragms, or the like, for emitting or receiving sound.  Acoustics not otherwise provided for.  Arrangements for generating mechanical vibrations in fluids.  The production of sounds which may not be audible to human beings but which are audible to animals.  **Glossary:** - Acoustics; Sound: The terms acoustics and sound cover the technical fields dealing with mechanical vibrations at all infrasonic, sonic, and ultrasonic frequencies. However, generation or transmission of mechanical waves, in general, is covered by subclass B06B , subject to the exception specified in special rules of classification within this subclass mentioned above. - Musical instrument: Musical instrument can include a device that emits a single sound signal or, in other words, does not exclude a device that emits a single sound signal.
[G10K 1/00] Devices in which sound is produced by striking a resonating body, e.g. bells, chimes or gongs -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Multi-toned musical instruments -> G10D13/08 - Automatic carillons -> G10F1/10
[G10K 1/06] the resonating devices having the shape of a bell, plate, rod, or tube -- This CPC subclass (G10K 1/06) covers resonating devices that have the shape of a bell, plate, rod or tube and produce sound when the body is struck, such as bells, chimes or gongs. It is situated under physics → musical instruments → sound-producing devices.
[G10K 1/062] electrically operated -- The CPC entry G10K 1/062 covers sound-producing devices in which a resonating body is struck by an electrically powered actuator, such as a solenoid, motor or piezoelectric striker. It includes electric bells, chimes, gongs and similar instruments where the striking motion is generated by an electric drive rather than manual impact.
[G10K 1/063] the sounding member being a bell -- The G10K 1/063 subclass covers sound-producing devices whose resonating body is a bell, meaning the primary vibrating element that creates the audible tone is shaped and functions as a bell. This includes stand-alone bells, handheld or mounted bells, and similar devices where the bell itself is struck or otherwise excited to emit sound.
[G10K 1/064] Operating or striking mechanisms therefor -- G10K 1/064 covers the mechanisms that cause a resonating body of a struck musical instrument-such as a bell, chime or gong-to produce sound. It includes levers, hammers, motor-driven strikers and other devices that actuate, release or control the impact on the resonator, enabling automated or assisted playing.
[G10K 1/0645] provided with loudness adjustment -- G10K 1/0645 refers to struck resonating devices such as bells, chimes or gongs that incorporate a mechanism for adjusting their loudness, allowing the user to vary the sound volume produced by the instrument.
[G10K 1/065] for timed or repeated operation -- The subclass G10K 1/065 covers devices that generate sound by striking a resonating body such as bells, chimes or gongs, where the striking action is controlled by a timing or repetition mechanism. It includes mechanical or electronic systems that automatically trigger the percussion at preset intervals or in a cyclic fashion.
[G10K 1/066] the sounding member being a tube, plate or rod -- G10K 1/066 relates to struck sound-producing devices whose resonating element is a tube, plate or rod rather than a traditional bell or gong shape; it covers instruments in which a striker excites vibration of these members to generate audible tones.
[G10K 1/067] Operating or striking mechanisms therefor -- The CPC entry G10K 1/067 covers the various operating or striking mechanisms that cause a resonating body-such as a bell, chime or gong-to produce sound. It includes levers, hammers, motor-driven strikers and similar devices that initiate the impact required for the instrument to sound.
[G10K 1/068] hydraulically operated -- G10K 1/068 covers sound-producing devices whose resonating body is struck by a hydraulic actuator. It includes bells, chimes, gongs and similar instruments where hydraulic pressure drives the striker to generate sound.
[G10K 1/07] mechanically operated -- G10K 1/07 covers sound-producing devices in which a resonating body is struck by a mechanically driven element such as a hammer, cam, gear or motor, rather than by a human hand. The class includes automatic or self-acting striking mechanisms that generate musical tones from bells, chimes, gongs or similar resonators.
[G10K 1/071] Hand bells -- G10K 1/071 covers hand-bells, which are small, handheld devices that produce sound when the user strikes a resonating body, typically a metal or alloy cup, causing it to vibrate and emit a clear, sustained tone. These instruments are commonly used in musical performances and ceremonial contexts.
[G10K 1/072] Operating or striking mechanisms therefor -- The G10K 1/072 entry covers the mechanisms that actuate or strike sound-producing devices such as bells, chimes and gongs. It includes designs for hammers, levers, solenoids or other systems that initiate the vibration of the resonating body, focusing on the means of operating the instrument rather than the acoustics themselves.
[G10K 1/074] with rotary clappers or shells -- The subclass G10K 1/074 covers sound-producing devices such as bells, chimes or gongs that generate tones by means of rotary clappers or shells which strike a resonating body. It includes mechanisms where a rotating element carries one or more striking parts that hit the sound-radiating component to create audible vibrations.
[G10K 1/076] for timed or repeated operation -- G10K 1/076 designates sound-producing devices that operate by timed or repeated striking of a resonating body, such as bells, chimes or gongs driven by automatic or cyclic mechanisms.
[G10K 1/08] Details or accessories of general applicability -- The G10K 1/08 subclass covers general-purpose details and accessories for struck resonating-body sound devices such as bells, chimes and gongs. It includes fittings, mounting hardware, protective covers, tuning elements and other components that are not specific to a particular instrument but are usable across a range of such devices.
[G10K 1/10] Sounding members -- G10K 1/10 - Sounding members - covers the resonating components of struck acoustic instruments such as bells, chimes, gongs and tuned bars, including their shape, material and mounting that determine vibration and tone.
[G10K 11/00] Methods or devices for transmitting, conducting or directing sound in general -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or devices that transmit, conduct, or direct a sound wave.  Methods or devices for protection against, or damping of, acoustic waves.  Methods or devices for damping acoustic waves by electroacoustically regenerating the original acoustic waves in antiphase, and superimposing the two acoustic waves  Diffusers ( G10K11/20 )  Damping or steering of surface acoustic waves
[G10K 11/002] Devices for damping, suppressing, obstructing or conducting sound in acoustic devices  G10K1/06  -  G10K1/10  take precedence; for electro-mechanical transducers for communication  H04R3/002 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for damping, suppressing, obstructing or conducting sound in acoustic devices, e.g. for preventing cavitation in acoustic devices  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Resonating devices having the shape of a bell, plate, rod, or tube -> G10K1/06 - G10K1/10 - Electro-mechanical transducers for communication -> H04R3/002
[G10K 11/004] Mounting transducers, e.g. provided with mechanical moving or orienting device  mountings specially adapted to a particular sound-producing device,  see  the preceding groups  G10K1/00  -  G10K9/00 , e.g.  G10K1/26 ,  G10K1/28 ,  G10K9/22 ; arrangements of sonic watch equipment on submarines  B63G8/39 ; buoys  B63B22/00 -- This classification code pertains to the specific mounting and positioning of transducers, particularly those adapted for use with particular sound-producing devices. It includes considerations for mechanical movement or orientation and references related groups within the broader category of sound transmission methods and devices.
[G10K 11/006] Transducer mounting in underwater equipment, e.g. sonobuoys -- G10K 11/006 relates to the mounting of acoustic transducers in underwater equipment such as sonobuoys, covering the structures, fittings and methods used to secure and align the transducer with the surrounding medium. It addresses how the transducer is attached to ensure reliable sound transmission, acoustic coupling and protection against pressure and water ingress.
[G10K 11/008] Arrays of transducers  seismic streamers,  see   G01V1/20 -- G10K 11/008 covers the design and use of arrays of acoustic transducers incorporated in seismic streamer cables for underwater sound detection and transmission, typically in geophysical exploration. It deals with the arrangement and coupling of multiple transducers to capture seismic waves and convey the signals to surface processing equipment, referencing the broader classification G01V1/20.
[G10K 11/02] Mechanical acoustic impedances -- G10K 11/02 covers the concept and handling of mechanical acoustic impedances in sound-producing devices, focusing on how structures and materials resist or transmit acoustic energy. It includes methods for measuring, analyzing, and optimizing impedance to improve the efficiency of sound transmission, conduction or direction in musical instruments and related equipment.
[G10K 11/025] horns for impedance matching  see  provisionally also  G10K11/28 -- The G10K 11/025 subclass covers acoustic horns that are employed to match the impedance between a sound source and the surrounding medium, improving transmission efficiency. It includes designs of horn-shaped devices used in musical instrument amplification, loudspeakers and other sound-producing systems to control and direct acoustic energy.
[G10K 11/04] Acoustic filters -- G10K 11/04 refers to acoustic filters, devices that selectively attenuate or pass specific sound frequencies to shape, reduce or isolate noise in audio systems, hearing aids and measurement setups. They achieve this control through resonant cavities, membranes, porous materials or electronic circuits that influence how sound propagates.
[G10K 11/08] Non-electric sound-amplifying devices, e.g. non-electric megaphones -- G10K 11/08 is a CPC class for non-electric sound-amplifying devices, placed under physics → musical instruments → sound-producing devices → methods or devices for transmitting, conducting or directing sound. It includes items such as non-electric megaphones that boost acoustic volume without using electricity.
[G10K 11/16] Methods or devices for protecting against, or for damping, noise or other acoustic waves in general -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for manipulating acoustic surface waves -> G10K11/36
[G10K 11/161] in systems with fluid flow  G10K11/162  takes precedence; gas flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for machines or engines in general or for internal combustion engine  F01N , noise absorbers in pipes or pipe systems  F16L55/02 ; noise absorption in air conditioning and ventilation  F24F13/24 ; silencing exhaust or propulsion jets in aircraft  B64D33/06 -- G10K 11/161 covers methods and devices for directing or attenuating sound in fluid-flow systems, such as gas-flow silencers, exhaust apparatus for engines, and noise-absorbing components in pipes, ventilation, or aircraft jets. The class is subordinate to G10K 11/162 and intersects with related categories for engine exhaust noise control (F01N), pipe noise absorption (F16L55/02), HVAC sound reduction (F24F13/24) and aircraft jet silencing (B64D33/06).
[G10K 11/162] Selection of materials -- G10K 11/162 concerns the selection of materials used in sound-producing devices or methods for transmitting, conducting, or directing sound. It covers choices of substrates, membranes, diaphragms and other components based on acoustic, mechanical or durability properties.
[G10K 11/165] Particles in a matrix -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sound absorbers where the matrix is a gas and where the particles are in a fixed position
[G10K 11/168] Plural layers of different materials, e.g. sandwiches -- The G10K 11/168 entry relates to sound-transmission or directing devices that employ plural layers of different materials, often in a sandwich-type construction. It covers acoustic components where distinct layers are combined to modify, guide or enhance the propagation of sound.
[G10K 11/172] using resonance effects -- G10K 11/172 covers inventions that employ resonance phenomena to improve or control the transmission, conduction or direction of sound, typically within musical-instrument or other sound-producing devices. It includes methods or apparatuses that exploit natural or induced resonances to shape acoustic output, boost efficiency, or achieve specific tonal characteristics. This subclass falls under Physics → Musical Instruments → Sound-producing Devices → Methods or devices for transmitting, conducting or directing sound in general.
[G10K 11/175] using interference effects -- Sound/noise masking, classified in G10K11/1752 - G10K11/1754 , Acoustic noise cancellation, classified in G10K11/178
[G10K 11/1752] Masking -- The G10K 11/1752 "Masking" subgroup covers methods and devices that intentionally introduce sound to hide or reduce the perception of other noises, a technique used in hearing-aid design, acoustical treatment and audio-signal processing. It falls under sound-producing devices that transmit, conduct or direct sound, specifically addressing ways to mask unwanted acoustic signals.
[G10K 11/1754] Speech masking -- The G10K 11/1754 entry covers technologies that deliberately obscure or reduce the intelligibility of speech, typically by adding competing sounds or noise. It includes devices and methods designed for privacy, hearing protection, or audio testing that employ speech-masking techniques, falling under sound-transmission and direction concepts.
[G10K 11/178] by electro-acoustically regenerating the original acoustic waves in anti-phase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Acoustic active noise cancellation [ANC], i.e. issuing an acoustic wave field that destructively interferes with a pre-existing acoustic wave field, for the purposes of reducing or damping the pre-existing acoustic wave field.  The scheme has 4 main branches:  The analysis of input signals, output signals, or relationships between them, which are used by the invention in a special way to achieve its goal is classified in G10K11/1781 . For instance, if an application analyses a musical signal and turns off ANC if the music is very loud, G10K11/17827 should be assigned.  The handling of specific non-standard conditions, e.g. detection of instabilities or other malfunctions in the device, or detection of specific signals that must not be cancelled, e.g. speech, sirens or warnings, is classified in G10K11/1783 .  Inventions about specific details, e.g. specific details of the adaptive filter, hardware or software implementations for lowering power requirements or improving speed, geometric placement of loudspeakers and microphones or combinations with other acoustic elements, are classified in G10K11/1785 .  The overall ANC circuit structure is classified in G10K11/1787 , with the idea of having similar circuits together in one group. For instance, ANC in cars often involves a synthesizer using a reference signal based on engine parameters; as another example, systems in which music is to be played together with the ANC signal mostly have sub circuits that estimate and subtract the music signal from the error signal before the error signal is used to adapt the ANC signal.  **Glossary:** - plant: is interchangeably used to refer to both the primary path and the secondary path - primary path: is used to indicate the acoustic path from the noise source (rather than from the reference input) to the error microphone - feedforward: is used to indicate hybrid feedforward + feedback systems
[G10K 11/1781] characterised by the analysis of input or output signals, e.g. frequency range, modes, transfer functions -- The G10K 11/1781 entry relates to devices or methods that analyze acoustic input or output signals, focusing on characteristics such as frequency range, modal behavior and transfer functions, to aid in transmitting, conducting or directing sound. It sits under physics → musical instruments → sound-producing devices → methods or devices for transmitting, conducting or directing sound in general.
[G10K 11/17813] characterised by the analysis of the acoustic paths, e.g. estimating, calibrating or testing of transfer functions or cross-terms -- G10K 11/17813 relates to methods and devices that analyse acoustic paths, including the estimation, calibration or testing of transfer functions or cross-terms, to evaluate how sound propagates through a system.
[G10K 11/17815] between the reference signals and the error signals, i.e. primary path -- G10K 11/17815 concerns arrangements in which a primary acoustic path connects reference signals to error signals, defining how the sound-carrying medium or transmission line links these signals in systems such as active noise control, echo cancellation or feedback suppression. It specifies the physical or electronic conduit that carries the reference signal to the point where it is compared with the error signal for corrective processing.
[G10K 11/17817] between the output signals and the error signals, i.e. secondary path -- This sub-class deals with the secondary acoustic path that links the active output signals of a sound-producing device to the error-signal detector, covering methods and apparatus for modeling, measuring or compensating that path in feedback or active control systems. It includes techniques for characterising the delay, attenuation and distortion introduced between the speaker output and the microphone error signal used in adaptive filtering or noise-cancellation.
[G10K 11/17819] between the output signals and the reference signals, e.g. to prevent howling -- A technique placed between the output signal and its reference signal to suppress or eliminate acoustic feedback, such as howling, in sound-producing devices. It typically involves signal processing or control methods that detect and counteract feedback loops, improving stability of audio transmission or amplification systems.
[G10K 11/17821] characterised by the analysis of the input signals only -- This CPC subgroup concerns devices or methods that handle sound transmission or direction but perform only analysis of the incoming audio signals, without generating or modifying the sound itself. Typical applications include acoustic monitoring, diagnostic tools, and signal-processing systems that evaluate input characteristics for control or assessment purposes.
[G10K 11/17823] Reference signals, e.g. ambient acoustic environment -- G10K 11/17823 pertains to reference signals such as recordings of the ambient acoustic environment that are used in methods or devices for transmitting, conducting or directing sound. These signals serve as benchmarks or calibration inputs for acoustic processing, noise control, or sound reproduction systems.
[G10K 11/17825] Error signals -- In sound-producing devices, error signals refer to electronic or acoustic indications that a fault or abnormal condition has occurred, used to alert users or trigger corrective actions. They may be generated by monitoring circuits or sensors and can be audible alarms, visual indicators, or control signals that modify operation to prevent damage or maintain performance.
[G10K 11/17827] Desired external signals, e.g. pass-through audio such as music or speech -- G10K 11/17827 covers methods or devices that allow a musical instrument to receive and pass through external audio signals, such as music or speech, so the desired sound is reproduced alongside the instrument's own output. It pertains to sound-transmission and routing technology within musical-instrument systems.
[G10K 11/1783] handling or detecting of non-standard events or conditions, e.g. changing operating modes under specific operating conditions -- G10K 11/1783 covers methods and devices that detect or manage atypical events or conditions in sound-transmission systems, such as automatically switching operating modes when particular thresholds or faults are encountered. It includes monitoring, diagnostic and control functions that adapt the behavior of acoustic equipment to maintain performance under non-standard circumstances.
[G10K 11/17833] by using a self-diagnostic function or a malfunction prevention function, e.g. detecting abnormal output levels -- The code G10K 11/17833 falls under the CPC class for sound-producing devices and covers methods or devices that incorporate a self-diagnostic or malfunction-prevention function, such as detecting abnormal output levels, to ensure reliable transmission or direction of sound.
[G10K 11/17835] using detection of abnormal input signals -- The G10K 11/17835 subclass covers methods and devices that detect abnormal input signals within systems that transmit, conduct or direct sound. It focuses on sensing irregular or unintended audio signals in musical-instrument or audio-processing equipment to trigger control, protection or corrective actions.
[G10K 11/17837] by retaining part of the ambient acoustic environment, e.g. speech or alarm signals that the user needs to hear -- This subclass covers techniques that preserve selected ambient sounds such as speech or alarm signals while processing or transmitting audio, ensuring the user can still hear important cues. It applies to devices that mix or filter sound so that necessary environmental audio is retained alongside other audio output.
[G10K 11/1785] Methods, e.g. algorithms; Devices  G10K11/1781 ,  G10K11/1783  take precedence -- G10K 11/1785 covers specialised methods, such as algorithms, and accompanying devices for transmitting, conducting or directing sound, particularly in musical-instrument contexts. The entry refines earlier groups G10K 11/1781 and G10K 11/1783, taking precedence over them by defining more detailed technical solutions for sound-propagation control.
[G10K 11/17853] of the filter -- The entry G10K 11/17853 in the Cooperative Patent Classification (EPO/USPTO) pertains to methods or devices for transmitting, conducting, or directing sound, specifically focusing on aspects of filter design and implementation. This classification is part of a broader category that includes various sound-producing devices and musical instruments.
[G10K 11/17854] the filter being an adaptive filter -- G10K 11/17854 classifies adaptive filters used in sound-transmission, conduction or directing devices, covering systems that adjust filter characteristics in real time to optimise audio performance, such as noise reduction, echo cancellation or equalisation in musical-instrument and audio-equipment applications.
[G10K 11/17855] for improving speed or power requirements -- This subcategory includes methods or devices that increase the speed of, or lower the power needed for, transmitting, conducting or directing sound in musical-instrument related sound-producing equipment, such as amplifiers, speakers, acoustic waveguides or similar technologies.
[G10K 11/17857] Geometric disposition, e.g. placement of microphones -- The G10K 11/17857 subclass covers the geometric arrangement of sound transducers, such as the placement of microphones within a system, to optimise sound capture, spatial sampling or beamforming. It includes designs that specify relative locations, spacing, orientations and patterns that influence acoustic signal quality and directional characteristics.
[G10K 11/17861] using additional means for damping sound, e.g. using sound absorbing panels -- This classification covers methods or devices that employ extra means such as sound-absorbing panels to dampen acoustic energy in sound-producing apparatuses. It is nested under physics → musical instruments → sound-producing devices → methods or devices for transmitting, conducting or directing sound and is identified by code G10K 11/17861.
[G10K 11/1787] General system configurations -- G10K 11/1787 relates to the overall system layout of sound-transmission, conduction or directing devices, covering how components such as amplifiers, loudspeakers and other acoustic equipment are arranged and integrated into a complete system rather than detailing specific functional elements.
[G10K 11/17873] using a reference signal without an error signal, e.g. pure feedforward -- The entry covers sound-transmission or control techniques that use a reference signal directly, without an error-signal feedback loop, such as pure feedforward methods applied to acoustic devices. It pertains to methods or devices for guiding, conducting or directing sound, focusing on reference-based control rather than corrective feedback. This classification falls under the hierarchy of physics → musical instruments → sound-producing devices.
[G10K 11/17875] using an error signal without a reference signal, e.g. pure feedback -- G10K 11/17875 covers methods and devices that control or improve sound transmission by employing a feedback-only error signal, operating without an external reference signal. This pure feedback approach is used to adjust acoustic parameters such as gain, phase or direction in musical or other sound-producing systems.
[G10K 11/17879] using both a reference signal and an error signal -- The G10K 11/17879 subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to sound-producing device technologies that employ both a reference signal and an error signal to control, monitor or adjust the transmission, conduction or direction of audio. It covers methods or circuitry that compare the reference with the error to achieve desired acoustic performance.
[G10K 11/17881] the reference signal being an acoustic signal, e.g. recorded with a microphone -- G10K 11/17881 covers methods or devices in which the reference signal employed for alignment, calibration or control is an acoustic signal captured, for example, with a microphone. It falls within sound-producing devices and relates to transmitting, conducting or directing sound in general.
[G10K 11/17883] the reference signal being derived from a machine operating condition, e.g. engine RPM or vehicle speed -- This subclass covers systems where sound transmission or direction is controlled using a reference signal derived from a machine operating condition such as engine RPM or vehicle speed, enabling adaptive audio processing based on the equipment's state.
[G10K 11/17885] additionally using a desired external signal, e.g. pass-through audio such as music or speech -- When classifying in this group, classification is also made in the other appropriate groups under G10K11/1787 .
[G10K 11/18] Methods or devices for transmitting, conducting or directing sound -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mechanical acoustic impedances; Impedance matching, e.g. by horns; Acoustic resonators -> G10K11/02 - Devices for manipulating acoustic surface waves -> G10K11/36
[G10K 11/20] Reflecting arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Diffusers  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sound focusing or directing using reflection -> G10K11/28
[G10K 11/205] for underwater use -- G10K 11/205 covers methods or devices specifically adapted to transmit, conduct or direct sound in underwater environments, including underwater speakers, hydrophones, acoustic communication and sonar systems designed to function beneath the water surface.
[G10K 11/22] for conducting sound through hollow pipes, e.g. speaking tubes -- This subclass covers devices or methods that guide acoustic waves through hollow tubes, such as speaking tubes, for transmitting speech or sounds over a distance without electronic amplification. It applies to ducts, channels or pipes used in architectural or mechanical systems to conduct sound.
[G10K 11/24] for conducting sound through solid bodies, e.g. wires -- This CPC subclass covers technologies that guide acoustic energy through solid media such as rods, pipes or wires, enabling sound transmission without relying on air or fluid pathways. Applications include medical ultrasound waveguides, musical instrument components and acoustic sensing through structural elements.
[G10K 11/26] Sound-focusing or directing, e.g. scanning -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Diffraction
[G10K 11/28] using reflection, e.g. parabolic reflectors -- G10K 11/28 relates to sound-transmission or directing techniques that employ reflective surfaces, for example parabolic reflectors, to focus or steer acoustic energy. It is situated within the hierarchy of physics → musical instruments → sound-producing devices.
[G10K 11/30] using refraction, e.g. acoustic lenses -- This subgroup covers methods and devices that manipulate sound by refraction, such as acoustic lenses that focus, steer or reshape acoustic wave-fronts. It includes arrangements that use varying material properties or geometries to direct or concentrate sound energy for transmission, conduction or other acoustic functions.
[G10K 11/32] characterised by the shape of the source -- The G10K 11/32 subclass groups patents whose primary distinguishing feature is the shape of the sound-radiating source, such as a speaker cone, reed or other acoustic emitter, where the geometry is used to influence the direction, efficiency or quality of the generated sound.
[G10K 11/34] using electrical steering of transducer arrays, e.g. beam steering -- This subclass concerns methods and devices that direct acoustic energy by electrically steering arrays of transducers, such as beam-forming or beam-steering techniques. It applies to sound-producing equipment where the radiation pattern is changed without moving mechanical parts, using phase or amplitude control of individual array elements.
[G10K 11/341] Circuits therefor -- G10K 11/341 covers electrical circuits that form part of sound-producing devices, specifically those used to transmit, conduct or direct acoustic energy. The subclass includes amplifiers, filters, cross-overs and related circuit arrangements that manage audio signals in musical instruments and other sound-producing equipment.
[G10K 11/343] using frequency variation or different frequencies -- G10K 11/343 covers techniques that modulate or select multiple frequencies to influence how sound is generated or transmitted. This includes using frequency variation to control pitch, timbre or direction in musical or acoustic devices.
[G10K 11/345] using energy switching from one active element to another -- This CPC subgroup addresses methods and devices that transfer acoustic energy from one active element to another, such as switching the energy source or signal between transducers to control, amplify or redirect sound. It is used in systems that manage sound transmission or direction by dynamically reallocating energy among active components.
[G10K 11/346] using phase variation -- G10K 11/346 covers inventions that employ variations in the phase of sound waves to influence how acoustic energy is transmitted, conducted or directed. It includes methods and devices that adjust phase relationships for improved sound propagation, beam steering, noise reduction or acoustic imaging in musical-instrument and other sound-producing systems.
[G10K 11/348] using amplitude variation -- G10K 11/348 covers methods or devices that transmit, conduct or direct sound by varying the amplitude of the audio signal, such as amplitude modulation or volume-based encoding used in musical instruments, loudspeakers and other sound-producing systems. It includes techniques for controlling sound intensity to convey information or achieve desired acoustic effects.
[G10K 11/35] using mechanical steering of transducers -- G10K 11/35 covers techniques that steer acoustic transducers by mechanical means to control the direction or distribution of sound. It includes devices and methods where moving parts, such as pivots or linkages, adjust the orientation of speakers, horns or other sound-producing elements to direct acoustic energy without electronic beamforming.
[G10K 11/352] by moving the transducer -- G10K 11/352 relates to methods or devices that transmit, conduct or direct sound by moving the transducer, such as mechanisms that convert mechanical motion into acoustic output. It covers systems where the transducer's displacement is used to generate or control sound.
[G10K 11/355] Arcuate movement -- G10K 11/355 covers sound-transmission or directing devices that employ an arcuate or curved motion of components, such as waveguides, ducts, or moving parts that follow a circular path to shape or steer acoustic energy. The classification applies to mechanisms where the motion itself follows an arc rather than linear or rotary paths.
[G10K 11/357] by moving a reflector -- This subclass covers devices or methods that steer or modulate sound by physically moving a reflective surface, such as rotating or oscillating panels that redirect acoustic waves. It includes mechanisms where the reflector's position or orientation changes during operation to control sound propagation.
[G10K 11/36] Devices for manipulating acoustic surface waves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Damping, steering of Surface Acoustic Waves  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electro-acoustic amplifiers -> H03F13/00 - Networks comprising electro-acoustic elements -> H03H9/00
[G10K 1/26] Mountings -- Mountings are components that support, hold, or connect resonating bodies or their striking elements in instruments such as bells, chimes or gongs, allowing precise positioning and efficient vibration transmission. They include brackets, frames, supports and other attaching devices designed to secure the resonator within a larger assembly.
[G10K 1/28] Bells for towers or the like -- Bells intended for installation in towers, steeples, or similar structures, including clock or church bells, are classified under G10K 1/28. These devices produce sound by striking a resonating body and are part of the broader class of musical instruments and sound-producing devices.
[G10K 1/30] Details or accessories -- G10K 1/30 covers the various ancillary components, fittings and decorative elements that are attached to or used with struck resonating-body musical instruments such as bells, chimes and gongs. It includes mounts, straps, handles, protective housings and other accessories that modify, support or enhance the primary sound-producing device.
[G10K 13/00] Cones, diaphragms, or the like, for emitting or receiving sound in general -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Diaphragms for loudspeakers -> H04R7/00
[G10K 1/32] Sounding members -- G10K 1/32 covers sounding members, the individual resonating elements that generate sound when struck in devices such as bells, chimes, gongs, or similar instruments. These members include bars, plates, tubes, or bells that vibrate to produce audible tones and may be mounted singly or in arrays within larger sound-producing assemblies.
[G10K 1/34] Operating mechanisms -- This entry relates to the mechanical parts that cause a resonating body of a musical instrument to be struck, including hammers, levers, keyboards, actuators and other drive arrangements that initiate vibration. It covers devices such as bells, chimes and gongs where sound is produced by impact.
[G10K 1/341] for a still-standing bell -- G10K 1/341 covers sound-producing devices in which a resonating body is struck, specifically bells that remain fixed in an upright position while being sounded. The class includes stationary bells such as tower or clock bells that do not swing or move when hit.
[G10K 1/342] electrically operated -- G10K 1/342 covers sound-producing devices where a resonating body such as a bell, chime or gong is struck by a mechanism powered electrically, including electric hammers, solenoids or other actuators that initiate the strike.
[G10K 1/344] for an oscillating bell which is driven once per cycle -- This subclass covers bells that are set into oscillation and receive a single impulse for each vibration cycle, such as mechanisms that strike the bell once and allow it to ring freely. It relates to sound-producing devices where the resonating body is driven once per cycle.
[G10K 1/345] electrically operated -- G10K 1/345 pertains to devices where sound is generated by electronically activating a striking mechanism on a resonating body such as bells, chimes, or gongs. This classification covers the integration of electrical components to produce sound in these instruments.
[G10K 1/347] for an oscillating bell which is driven twice per cycle -- The G10K 1/347 entry covers oscillating bells that receive a driving input twice during each vibration cycle, improving control of tone and rhythm in sound-producing devices such as bells, chimes or gongs. It falls under musical instruments within the physics hierarchy.
[G10K 1/348] electrically operated -- G10K 1/348 covers sound-producing devices in which a resonating body such as a bell, chime or gong is struck by a mechanism that is powered electrically. The subclass includes electric striking assemblies, solenoid-driven hammers and other electromechanical actuators that generate musical or signalling tones without manual impact.
[G10K 1/36] Means for silencing or damping -- The G10K 1/36 entry covers any means that silence or dampen the sound produced by striking resonating bodies such as bells, chimes or gongs, including mutes, dampers, or other acoustic-absorbing devices. It lies within the CPC hierarchy under Physics → Musical Instruments → Sound-Producing Devices.
[G10K 1/38] Supports -- G10K 1/38 - Supports includes any component that holds, mounts, or suspends a struck resonating body such as a bell, chime or gong, for example frames, brackets, stands, sockets or mounting plates designed to position the instrument and facilitate vibration.

[G10K 15/046] using optical excitation, e.g. laser bundle -- G10K 15/046 covers sound-producing devices that generate acoustic waves by optical excitation, for example using a laser bundle to stimulate a material that radiates sound. The method is applied in musical instruments or acoustic measurement systems where light energy is converted into audible vibrations.
[G10K 15/06] using electric discharge -- The G10K 15/06 entry covers sound-producing devices that generate acoustic output by means of an electric discharge, such as spark-or plasma-based sources. It includes methods and apparatus for creating or modulating musical or audio signals using these discharge mechanisms. This subclass falls under the broader categories of physics, musical instruments, sound-producing devices, and acoustics not otherwise provided for.
[G10K 15/08] Arrangements for producing a reverberation or echo sound -- G10K 15/08 covers devices or configurations that create reverberation or echo effects, such as acoustic chambers, reflectors, or electronic processors designed to add ambience to musical sounds. It is classified under sound-producing devices within the acoustics category of musical instrument technology, addressing arrangements that intentionally generate delayed or reflected sound.
[G10K 15/10] using time-delay networks comprising electromechanical or electro-acoustic devices -- G10K 15/10 covers inventions that employ time-delay networks made from electromechanical or electro-acoustic components to shape, generate or modify sound in musical-instrument or acoustic devices. It includes arrangements of resonators, springs, delay lines or electronic circuits that introduce controlled phase or latency to affect timbre, pitch or spatial characteristics.
[G10K 15/12] using electronic time-delay networks -- The G10K 15/12 subclass covers sound-producing devices that employ electronic time-delay networks to modify audio signals. It includes designs where delayed signal paths are used for echo, reverberation, or phase-shift effects in musical instruments and other acoustic equipment.
[G10K 2200/00] Details of methods or devices for transmitting, conducting or directing sound in general -- This classification covers general technologies for moving sound, including any methods or apparatus that transmit, conduct, or steer acoustic energy from one place to another. It applies to a broad range of devices such as ducts, waveguides, speakers, and acoustic lenses, without focusing on a specific application or instrument. The entry is used to organize patents dealing with the fundamental handling of sound in a generic sense.
[G10K 2200/10] Beamforming, e.g. time reversal, phase conjugation or similar -- The CPC subclass G10K 2200/10 covers methods and devices that shape or steer acoustic energy, such as beamforming techniques that employ time reversal, phase conjugation or similar signal-processing approaches to direct sound. It falls within the hierarchy of physics → musical instruments → sound-producing devices, focusing on the transmission, conduction or direction of sound in general.
[G10K 2200/11] Underwater, e.g. transducers for generating acoustic waves underwater -- G10K 2200/11 covers devices that generate acoustic waves in water, such as underwater transducers used for sonar, communication or measurement. These methods involve converting electrical energy into sound pressure in a fluid medium and directing the waves for propagation or detection.
[G10K 2210/00] Details of active noise control [ANC] covered by -- The G10K 2210/00 entry pertains to active noise-control (ANC) features incorporated in sound-producing devices such as musical instruments. It covers the design, algorithms, sensors and actuators used to generate anti-noise signals that cancel unwanted acoustical emissions, as well as methods for integrating these ANC systems within the device's overall structure.
[G10K 2210/10] Applications -- This CPC subclass G10K 2210/10 covers applications of active noise control in sound-producing devices, particularly musical instruments. It includes methods, systems and components that generate anti-phase sound to cancel unwanted noise and improve acoustic performance, and is situated under physics, musical instruments and sound-producing devices.
[G10K 2210/101] One dimensional -- G10K 2210/101 - One dimensional falls under the CPC hierarchy Physics, Musical instruments, Sound-producing devices and concerns technical features of active noise control systems that operate in a single spatial dimension. It covers methods, apparatuses and arrangements for reducing unwanted sound by generating anti-phase acoustic signals along a one-dimensional path.
[G10K 2210/102] Two dimensional -- The CPC entry G10K 2210/102 "Two-dimensional" covers methods and systems for active noise control applied to sound-producing devices that operate in a planar or two-dimensional configuration, such as speaker arrays or flat-panel musical instruments. [G10K 2210/3013] Analogue, i.e. using analogue computers or circuits -- G10K 2210/3013 classifies analogue implementations of active noise control-systems that use analog computers or electronic circuits to generate anti-phase sound that cancels unwanted noise-in devices such as musical instruments and other sound-producing equipment.
[G10K 2210/3014] Adaptive noise equalizers [ANE], i.e. where part of the unwanted sound is retained -- G10K 2210/3014 covers adaptive noise equalizers used in musical-instrument sound-producing devices, where active noise control retains a portion of the undesired audio to shape the overall output. The technology adjusts equalization parameters in real time to balance noise reduction with desired acoustic characteristics.
[G10K 2210/3015] Averaging, e.g. exponential -- G10K 2210/3015 covers methods for averaging signals in active noise control systems, such as exponential or moving-average filters, applied to musical-instrument sound-producing devices. The entry addresses how these averaging techniques process acoustic or electrical signals to reduce unwanted noise and improve sound quality.
[G10K 2210/3016] Control strategies, e.g. energy minimization or intensity measurements -- The subclass G10K 2210/3016 concerns active-noise-control techniques applied to sound-producing devices, especially musical instruments, and details control strategies such as minimizing the energy required for cancellation or using acoustic-intensity measurements to drive the noise-reduction system.
[G10K 2210/3017] Copy, i.e. whereby an estimated transfer function in one functional block is copied to another block -- In active noise-cancellation systems the classification G10K 2210/3017 covers copying an estimated transfer function from one functional block to another, allowing the second block to use the same adaptive model for sound attenuation. This technique helps maintain consistent noise-reduction performance across multiple stages of the device.
[G10K 2210/3018] Correlators, e.g. convolvers or coherence calculators -- G10K 2210/3018 pertains to correlators such as convolvers or coherence calculators used in active noise control for sound-producing devices. It covers the calculation of correlation or coherence between acoustic signals to assess and adaptively cancel unwanted noise, typically within musical instrument or audio equipment applications.
[G10K 2210/3019] Cross-terms between multiple in's and out's -- G10K 2210/3019 addresses the analysis and mitigation of cross-terms that arise when several acoustic inputs and outputs interact in active noise-control systems for sound-producing devices such as musical instruments. The entry focuses on the signal-processing methods used to isolate and cancel undesired sound components in complex multi-channel configurations.
[G10K 2210/3021] Eigenfrequencies -- The entry G10K 2210/3021 covers the analysis and use of eigenfrequencies in active noise-control systems for musical-instrument sound-producing devices. It addresses how the natural resonant modes of a device are identified and exploited to reduce unwanted noise through adaptive feedback techniques.
[G10K 2210/3022] Error paths -- G10K 2210/3022 Error paths pertains to the identification and modeling of unintended signal routes, delays and feedback loops that degrade the performance of active noise control systems in sound-producing devices such as musical instruments. The classification addresses how these error paths influence ANC effectiveness and the methods used to mitigate them.
[G10K 2210/3023] Estimation of noise, e.g. on error signals -- G10K 2210/3023 pertains to active noise-control techniques for musical or other sound-producing devices, focusing on methods that estimate noise-often by analysing error signals-to improve cancellation performance. It falls under the physics hierarchy for musical instruments and sound-producing apparatus.
[G10K 2210/30231] Sources, e.g. identifying noisy processes or components -- G10K 2210/30231 covers the identification of noise-generating processes or components that serve as sources for active noise control systems. It includes methods and apparatus for locating, measuring, and characterising acoustic emissions in musical instruments and other sound-producing devices, facilitating the design of ANC solutions.
[G10K 2210/30232] Transfer functions, e.g. impulse response -- The G10K 2210/30232 subclass deals with the characterization and use of transfer functions, such as impulse responses, in active noise control systems for sound-producing devices, including musical instruments. It covers methods for modelling, measuring, or applying these functions to reduce unwanted noise in the acoustic output.
[G10K 2210/3024] Expert systems, e.g. artificial intelligence -- CPC entry G10K 2210/3024 (Expert systems, e.g. artificial intelligence) covers the use of AI-based expert systems to manage or improve active noise control in sound-producing devices such as musical instruments. It pertains to methods, algorithms or software that detect, analyze and suppress unwanted acoustic signals, enhancing performance or user experience of the instrument.
[G10K 2210/3025] Determination of spectrum characteristics, e.g. FFT -- G10K 2210/3025 covers techniques for extracting spectral information from acoustic signals, such as applying fast Fourier transform to determine frequency components. The methods are used to support active noise control in sound-producing devices, including musical instruments. It is part of the CPC hierarchy physics, musical instruments, sound-producing devices, details of active noise control.
[G10K 2210/3026] Feedback -- The CPC entry G10K 2210/3026 - Feedback - relates to the feedback aspects of active noise control in sound-producing devices, including musical instruments. It covers methods and arrangements that sense acoustic output and feed a corrective signal to reduce unwanted noise, enhancing sound quality.
[G10K 2210/3027] Feedforward -- The CPC subclass G10K 2210/3027 "Feedforward" relates to active noise-control (ANC) techniques applied to sound-producing devices, such as musical instruments, within the physics domain. It covers feedforward ANC arrangements that predict incoming noise and generate an anti-noise signal to cancel it before it reaches the listener.
[G10K 2210/3028] Filtering, e.g. Kalman filters or special analogue or digital filters -- G10K 2210/3028 pertains to filtering techniques such as Kalman, analogue or digital filters used in active noise control for sound-producing devices, including musical instruments. [G10K 2210/3031] Hardware, e.g. architecture -- G10K 2210/3031 refers to the hardware architecture employed in active noise-control (ANC) systems for sound-producing devices, covering the arrangement of microphones, speakers, amplifiers, digital signal-processing units and related circuitry that generate, detect and cancel unwanted acoustic energy.
[G10K 2210/3032] Harmonics or sub-harmonics -- This CPC subclass covers technologies that manipulate harmonic or sub-harmonic frequencies in active noise control systems for musical-instrument sound-producing devices, focusing on methods and devices that generate, detect, or cancel specific harmonic components to enhance noise reduction.
[G10K 2210/3033] Information contained in memory, e.g. stored signals or transfer functions -- G10K 2210/3033 covers the storage of data used in active noise control for sound-producing devices, including pre-recorded signals, filter coefficients or transfer functions kept in memory to generate anti-noise. The classification applies to electronic systems that retrieve these stored parameters to suppress unwanted sound.
[G10K 2210/3034] Integrators -- Integrators are circuit elements that mathematically sum or accumulate input signals over time, producing a continuous output proportional to the integral of the signal. In active noise control for musical and other sound-producing devices, they are used to generate the anti-noise waveform that cancels unwanted sound by processing reference and error signals. This function is classified under CPC G10K 2210/3034.
[G10K 2210/3035] Models, e.g. of the acoustic system -- The CPC subclass G10K 2210/3035 covers models of acoustic systems that are used to implement active noise control in sound-producing devices such as musical instruments. It includes mathematical, computational or simulation representations that describe how ANC mechanisms operate within these devices.
[G10K 2210/30351] Identification of the environment for applying appropriate model characteristics -- The G10K 2210/30351 entry covers techniques for detecting and characterising the acoustic environment so that an active-noise-control system can be matched with the most suitable modelling parameters. It relates to sound-producing devices such as musical instruments, focusing on how environmental identification informs the selection of ANC models.
[G10K 2210/3036] Modes, e.g. vibrational or spatial modes -- G10K 2210/3036 covers the definition and use of vibrational or spatial modes in active noise-control systems for sound-producing devices, such as musical instruments, addressing how these modal patterns are generated, detected or employed to cancel unwanted sound.

It pertains to musical instruments and other devices where real-time noise-cancellation optimization is required.
[G10K 2210/3041] Offline -- G10K 2210/3041 - Offline covers active noise control methods for sound-producing devices, particularly musical instruments, where the noise-cancelling processing is performed without real-time operation, using pre-recorded data or post-processing to suppress unwanted sounds.

It is a subset of CPC categories dealing with physics, musical instruments and sound-producing devices.

[G10K 2210/3048] Pretraining, e.g. to identify transfer functions -- The G10K 2210/3048 subclass concerns pretraining methods-such as model training or calibration-to identify transfer functions that enable active noise control in sound-producing devices, particularly musical instruments. [G10K 2210/3049] Random noise used, e.g. in model identification -- The CPC subclass G10K 2210/3049 pertains to active noise-control systems that employ random or stochastic acoustic signals, such as broadband noise, as inputs for identifying the dynamics of a device or environment. [G10K 2210/3217] Collocated sensor and cancelling actuator, e.g. "virtual earth" designs -- This CPC subclass defines arrangements where the noise-sensing element and the cancelling actuator are positioned together, often employing a virtual-earth configuration to enhance active noise control in sound-producing devices such as musical instruments. It covers ancillary control systems, algorithmic implementations, integration techniques and other features that do not fit within more specific ANC categories.

[G10K 2210/502] Ageing, e.g. of the control system -- G10K 2210/502 covers aspects of active noise control applied to musical-instrument sound-producing devices, specifically the ageing and degradation of the control system over time. [G10K 2210/504] Calibration -- G10K 2210/504 covers calibration techniques for active noise-control (ANC) systems integrated in sound-producing devices such as musical instruments. The entry specifies how the ANC performance is measured, adjusted and verified to achieve desired acoustic suppression, ensuring the device operates within defined noise-reduction parameters.
[G10K 2210/505] Echo cancellation, e.g. multipath-, ghost- or reverberation-cancellation -- G10K 2210/505 covers technologies for cancelling echoes in audio signals, including methods to suppress multipath, ghost, or reverberation effects, typically used in active-noise-control systems for musical instruments and other sound-producing devices.
[G10K 2210/506] Feedback, e.g. howling -- The G10K 2210/506 entry classifies technologies that manage feedback in active noise-control systems for sound-producing devices such as musical instruments, focusing on methods to prevent or suppress howling and related acoustic feedback problems.
[G10K 2210/507] Flow or turbulence -- G10K 2210/507 relates to the study and management of airflow or turbulence in active noise control systems for sound-producing devices. It covers methods that analyse how fluid movement influences acoustic output and how such flow characteristics can be harnessed or mitigated to reduce unwanted noise in musical instruments and other acoustic equipment.
[G10K 2210/508] Reviews on ANC in general, e.g. literature -- G10K 2210/508 is a CPC subclass for publications that review active-noise-control (ANC) technologies applied to sound-producing devices, especially musical instruments. It gathers general literature-type overviews of ANC methods, performance and implementation within the physics-to-musical-instrument hierarchy. This entry is used by the EPO and USPTO to index patent-related reviews of ANC concepts.
[G10K 2210/509] Hybrid, i.e. combining different technologies, e.g. passive and active -- This CPC entry G10K2210/509 falls under physics, musical instruments, sound-producing devices and covers hybrid active noise control systems that combine technologies such as passive and active methods. It addresses devices that integrate multiple noise-control techniques to improve acoustic performance.
[G10K 2210/51] Improving tonal quality, e.g. mimicking sports cars -- G10K 2210/51 covers technologies that improve the tonal quality of sound-producing devices, often by applying active noise control to shape the output, such as mimicking the distinctive sound of a sports car.
[G10K 2210/511] Narrow band, e.g. implementations for single frequency cancellation -- G10K 2210/511 pertains to active noise control systems that target a narrow frequency range, often designed to cancel a single tone. It covers methods and devices that generate anti-phase sound to suppress that specific frequency, typically used in musical instrument sound-producing devices.
[G10K 2210/512] Wide band, e.g. non-recurring signals -- This subclass concerns active noise-control systems that employ wide-band, non-recurring signals to cancel unwanted sound in musical-instrument or other sound-producing devices, detailing how broadband anti-noise is generated and applied to suppress noise across a broad frequency range.
[G10K 3/00] Rattles or like noise-producing devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Door-knockers
[G10K 5/00] Whistles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Infrasonic, sonic, and ultrasonic whistles
[G10K 5/02] Ultrasonic whistles -- Ultrasonic whistles are devices that generate sound at frequencies above the normal audible range, typically used for animal deterrence, distance measurement, cleaning or signaling where inaudible tones are required. They fall under sound-producing devices within musical instruments, classified in the physics section of the CPC.
[G10K 7/00] Sirens -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sirens where the sound is created by chopping a flow of air
[G10K 7/005] Ultrasonic sirens -- The G10K 7/005 class defines sirens that generate ultrasonic sound waves for signaling or deterrence purposes. These devices emit frequencies above the range of human hearing and are used in applications such as pest control, security alerts, and equipment monitoring, often employing electronic oscillators or piezoelectric transducers.
[G10K 7/02] in which the sound-producing member is rotated manually or by a motor -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sirens in which the sound-producing member is driven by a fluid -> G10K7/06
[G10K 7/04] by an electric motor -- G10K 7/04 classifies sirens whose sound-producing mechanism is driven by an electric motor, covering devices that use motor-generated rotation to create acoustic signals.
[G10K 7/06] in which the sound-producing member is driven by a fluid, e.g. by a compressed gas -- G10K 7/06 covers sirens whose acoustic element is powered by a fluid, typically compressed gas, causing a rotating or vibrating sound-producing member to emit a tone. This includes air-driven whistles, pneumatic sirens and similar devices that generate sound via fluid-driven motion.
[G10K 9/00] Devices in which sound is produced by vibrating a diaphragm or analogous element, e.g. fog horns, vehicle hooters or buzzers -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - The construction of, or circuits for, broadband-transducers, such as loudspeakers, microphones or like acoustic electromechanical transducers -> H04R
[G10K 9/02] driven by gas -- G10K 9/02 covers sound-producing devices whose diaphragm or equivalent is set in motion by a gas flow or pressure, such as compressed-air horns, fog-horns and other gas-driven acoustic emitters.
[G10K 9/04] by compressed gases, e.g. compressed air -- G10K 9/04 covers sound-producing devices that generate acoustic signals by releasing compressed gases such as air. Typical examples include fog horns, vehicle hooters, buzzers and similar apparatus that use a diaphragm or analogous element driven by the gas pressure.
[G10K 9/06] produced by detonation -- G10K 9/06 covers sound-producing devices where the acoustic energy is generated by a detonation, such as explosive charge-driven buzzers or horns. The classification applies to mechanisms that use the rapid expansion of gases from an explosion to drive a diaphragm or other radiating element.
[G10K 9/08] driven by water or other liquids -- G10K 9/08 classifies sound-producing devices, such as fog horns, vehicle hooters or buzzers, in which the sound is generated by a vibrating diaphragm and the energy source is water or other liquids.
[G10K 9/10] driven by mechanical means only -- G10K 9/10 covers sound-producing devices such as fog horns, vehicle hooters, or buzzers in which the diaphragm or analogous element is set into vibration solely by a mechanical force, without any electrical, pneumatic or hydraulic drive.
[G10K 9/12] electrically operated -- G10K 9/12 designates electrically operated sound-producing devices that generate acoustic output by vibrating a diaphragm or similar element, encompassing electric fog horns, vehicle hooters, buzzers and comparable electromechanical emitters.
[G10K 9/121] Flextensional transducers -- Flextensional transducers are acoustic devices that use a flexible, usually shell-shaped diaphragm to amplify the motion of a small piezoelectric element, generating powerful low-frequency sound waves. They are employed in marine sonar, underwater communication and industrial emitters where high acoustic output is required from a compact source.
[G10K 9/122] using piezoelectric driving means -- G10K 9/122 covers sound-producing devices that generate acoustic signals by vibrating a diaphragm or analogous element using a piezoelectric driver. The classification includes horns, hooters, buzzers and similar alerts where the piezoelectric element directly excites the diaphragm.
