[G10K 9/125] with a plurality of active elements -- G10K 9/125 covers sound-producing devices that generate acoustic signals by vibrating a diaphragm or similar element and that contain more than one active element, such as multi-tone fog horns, vehicle hooters with several reeds, or buzzers employing multiple vibrating parts.
[G10K 9/128] using magnetostrictive driving means -- G10K 9/128 covers sound-producing devices in which a magnetostrictive element drives the diaphragm or comparable vibrating component. The classification applies to horns, hooters, buzzers and similar apparatus where magnetic strain creates the acoustic vibration.
[G10K 9/13] using electromagnetic driving means -- This subclass covers sound-producing devices that generate vibration by means of an electromagnetic driver, such as solenoid-actuated diaphragms in horns, hooters or buzzers. It includes arrangements where an electric coil and magnetic field cause the diaphragm to move, producing audible signals.
[G10K 9/15] Self-interrupting arrangements -- G10K 9/15 covers self-interrupting arrangements for sound-producing devices such as fog horns, vehicle hooters or buzzers. The classification includes mechanisms that automatically cease vibration of the diaphragm after a preset time, signal, or external trigger, providing a built-in stop function without manual control.
[G10K 9/16] with means for generating current by muscle power -- This subclass covers sound-producing devices that include a manually-powered generator to produce the electrical current needed for the vibrating diaphragm or analogous element, such as muscle-operated horns, buzzers or fog-horns.
[G10K 9/18] Details, e.g. bulbs, pumps, pistons, switches or casings -- G10K 9/18 covers the auxiliary parts of diaphragm-based sound-producing devices such as fog horns, vehicle hooters and buzzers. It includes elements like illumination bulbs, pumps, pistons, switches and protective casings that are incorporated into or attached to the main horn assembly. These details support the operation, control or housing of the sound-emitting mechanism.
[G10K 9/20] Sounding members -- G10K 9/20 - Sounding members - covers the components that generate sound in diaphragm-type devices, such as vibrating diaphragms, reeds or similar elements used in fog horns, vehicle hooters, buzzers and related sound-producing equipment.
[G10K 9/22] Mountings -- G10K 9/22 covers mountings, the brackets, supports or attachment mechanisms that secure the diaphragm, horn or other vibrating element within sound-producing devices such as fog horns, vehicle hooters or buzzers.
[G10L] SPEECH ANALYSIS TECHNIQUES OR SPEECH SYNTHESIS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for the storage of speech signals -> G11B - Static stores -> G11C - Encoding of compressed speech signals for transmission or storage -> H03M7/30  **Glossary:** - speech: definite vocal sounds that form words to express thoughts and ideas. - voice: sounds generated by vocal chords or synthetic versions thereof. - audio signal: of or relating to humanly audible sound, meant to include speech, voice, music, silence or background noise, or any combinations thereof.
[G10L 13/00] Speech synthesis -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[G10L 13/02] Methods for producing synthetic speech -- G10L 13/02 covers methods for producing synthetic speech, encompassing algorithms that transform written text or linguistic representations into audible speech. It includes rule-based, concatenative and statistical approaches that generate waveforms or parameters for speech output.
[G10L 13/027] Concept to speech synthesisers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  concepts used for speech synthesis can be linked to an emotion to be conveyed (US2010329505), a communication goal driving a dialogue (US2010241420), image-to-speech (US2010231752), native sounding speech (US2004030554)  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Processing or translation of natural language -> G06F40/40
[G10L 13/033] Voice editing, e.g. manipulating the voice of the synthesiser -- This CPC subclass covers techniques for modifying or processing the output voice of speech synthesis systems, such as pitch shifting, timbre alteration, or applying effects to make the synthetic speech sound different. It includes methods for adjusting voice characteristics post-generation to achieve desired acoustic properties.
[G10L 13/0335] Pitch control -- The G10L 13/0335 subclass covers techniques for controlling the pitch of generated speech, including methods for adjusting the fundamental frequency, intonation and prosodic contours of synthesized voices. It applies to hardware or software that modulates pitch during speech synthesis to produce natural-sounding or stylized output, often for applications in speech assistants, accessibility devices or musical instrument emulation.
[G10L 13/04] Details of speech synthesis systems, e.g. synthesiser structure or memory management -- G10L 13/04 pertains to the internal design of speech synthesis devices, covering features such as the synthesiser's hardware structure, architecture and the methods used for managing memory and data within the system.
[G10L 13/047] Architecture of speech synthesisers -- G10L 13/047 classifies the overall architecture of speech synthesizers, covering hardware and software arrangements that generate artificial speech. It resides in the CPC hierarchy under physics, musical instruments, and speech analysis or synthesis, and focuses on how the components of a speech synthesis system are organized and integrated.
[G10L 13/06] Elementary speech units used in speech synthesisers -- The G10L 13/06 subclass covers the basic sound elements-such as phonemes, diphones, or other sub-lexical units-that serve as the building blocks for speech synthesizer systems. It includes methods for selecting, storing, processing, and concatenating these elementary speech units to generate artificial speech.
[G10L 13/07] Concatenation rules -- G10L 13/07 concerns the set of rules governing how pre-recorded speech units are concatenated to produce synthetic speech. It covers methods for determining unit boundaries, prosodic matching and smoothing techniques used in concatenative speech synthesis.
[G10L 13/08] Text analysis or generation of parameters for speech synthesis out of text, e.g. grapheme to phoneme translation, prosody generation or stress or intonation determination -- G10L 13/08 covers techniques that derive speech-synthesis parameters directly from text, including grapheme-to-phoneme conversion, prosody modeling, and determination of stress or intonation for generating synthetic speech.
[G10L 13/086] Detection of language -- The G10L 13/086 class addresses systems that automatically identify the language present in spoken input. It includes techniques that analyze acoustic or phonetic features to determine which language is being used, supporting multilingual speech synthesis, translation and voice-controlled applications.
[G10L 13/10] Prosody rules derived from text -- G10L 13/10 covers methods for extracting prosodic information such as intonation, stress and rhythm from textual input and applying those rules during speech synthesis. It focuses on converting written cues-punctuation, lexical markers or syntactic structure-into parameters that shape the naturalness of generated speech, and is classified under speech synthesis within the broader domains of physics, musical instruments and speech analysis techniques.
[G10L 15/00] Speech recognition -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[G10L 15/005] Language recognition -- G10L 15/005 covers technology for recognizing spoken language, including identification of the language being spoken from an audio input, often used in multilingual speech recognition systems.
[G10L 15/01] Assessment or evaluation of speech recognition systems -- This CPC subclass G10L 15/01 covers methods for assessing or evaluating speech recognition systems, including performance measurement, benchmarking, and error analysis. It falls under the hierarchy of physics, musical instruments, speech analysis techniques or speech synthesis, specifically speech recognition.
[G10L 15/02] Feature extraction for speech recognition -- G10L 15/02 covers techniques for extracting acoustic or linguistic features from spoken signals to improve speech recognition performance. It includes methods for parameterizing speech waveforms, extracting phonetic, spectral, or prosodic attributes, and preparing data for pattern-matching or statistical models.
[G10L 15/04] Segmentation -- G10L 15/04 covers techniques for segmenting spoken input into smaller units such as phonemes, syllables or words, a fundamental step that enables speech-recognition systems to analyse and interpret the acoustic signal.
[G10L 15/05] Word boundary detection -- The G10L 15/05 subclass covers techniques for identifying the points where one spoken word ends and the next begins in continuous speech, a key step in speech-recognition processing. It includes methods, algorithms and signal-processing approaches that segment spoken input into separate lexical units for further analysis.
[G10L 15/06] Creation of reference templates -- The G10L 15/06 sub-class covers methods for producing reference templates used in speech-recognition systems, such as generating stored acoustic or phonetic patterns that incoming speech is compared against. It includes techniques for building, adapting, and updating these templates to improve recognition accuracy.
[G10L 15/063] Training -- G10L 15/063 covers techniques for training speech-recognition systems, including the collection and preparation of acoustic data, model adaptation, and learning algorithms that improve recognition accuracy. The class is part of the speech analysis and synthesis subgroup within the musical-instrument physics hierarchy.
[G10L 15/065] Adaptation -- The G10L 15/065 entry covers methods for adapting speech-recognition systems to particular users, acoustic conditions or linguistic variations. It includes techniques such as speaker-dependent model training, environmental noise compensation, and dynamic model updating to improve recognition accuracy.
[G10L 15/07] to the speaker -- G10L 15/07 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to speech recognition techniques specifically focused on identifying or verifying speakers. This classification is used for technologies that analyze voice characteristics to recognize individual speakers, often applied in security and personalization systems.
[G10L 15/075] supervised, i.e. under machine guidance -- G10L 15/075 covers supervised speech-recognition methods in which a system is trained under human or machine guidance to map spoken input to textual or command output. It includes techniques that use labeled examples, corrective feedback, or adaptive models to improve recognition accuracy within speech analysis and synthesis applications.
[G10L 15/08] Speech classification or search -- G10L 15/08 concerns methods for classifying spoken language or searching speech databases, falling under speech analysis techniques or speech synthesis. It includes algorithms that assign spoken utterances to categories such as language, speaker identity, or semantic content and techniques for retrieving matching segments from large corpora.
[G10L 15/083] Recognition networks  G10L15/142 ,  G10L15/16  take precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G10L 15/083 pertains to recognition networks in speech recognition technology, with specific emphasis on neural network models. This classification is part of a broader category that includes speech analysis techniques and synthesis, under the domain of musical instruments within physics. It notes that entries G10L15/142 and G10L15/16 have precedence.
[G10L 15/10] using distance or distortion measures between unknown speech and reference templates -- G10L 15/10 covers methods that compare an incoming speech signal with stored reference patterns by calculating distance or distortion metrics to determine the best match. These techniques are used in automatic speech recognition systems to identify spoken words or phrases.
[G10L 15/12] using dynamic programming techniques, e.g. dynamic time warping [DTW] -- G10L 15/12 covers speech-recognition methods that use dynamic-programming algorithms, especially dynamic time-warping, to align and compare time-varying acoustic feature sequences. It addresses the computational techniques for matching spoken input to stored patterns, enabling more accurate transcription or control in applications such as musical-instrument interfaces or voice-controlled devices.
[G10L 15/14] using statistical models, e.g. Hidden Markov Models [HMMs] -- G10L 15/14 covers speech-recognition techniques that employ statistical models, most notably Hidden Markov Models, to process and interpret spoken language. It is used for systems that convert acoustic signals into textual or command outputs by modeling phonetic and linguistic patterns probabilistically. This subclass falls under physics-related technologies for musical instruments, specifically speech analysis and synthesis.
[G10L 15/142] Hidden Markov Models [HMMs] -- G10L 15/142 covers the use of Hidden Markov Models, statistical tools that model sequences of observable events generated by underlying hidden states, for speech analysis, synthesis, and recognition. The classification includes methods for training, decoding, and adapting HMMs in speech processing systems.
[G10L 15/144] Training of HMMs -- G10L 15/144 covers methods for training hidden Markov models used in speech-recognition systems. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of physics, musical instruments, and speech-analysis or synthesis techniques, and addresses the preparation of statistical models that map acoustic signals to linguistic units.
[G10L 15/146] with insufficient amount of training data, e.g. state sharing, tying, deleted interpolation -- The G10L 15/146 classification covers speech-recognition methods that address a lack of adequate training data, for example by employing state-sharing, parameter tying, or deleted-interpolation techniques to improve model performance despite limited samples.
[G10L 15/148] Duration modelling in HMMs, e.g. semi HMM, segmental models or transition probabilities -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers methods for modeling the duration of speech units within hidden Markov models, including semi-Markov and segmental approaches that adjust transition probabilities to reflect state length. It is used in speech recognition systems to improve timing accuracy of phoneme or word modeling.
[G10L 15/16] using artificial neural networks -- G10L 15/16 pertains to speech-recognition systems that utilise artificial neural networks to analyse, model and interpret spoken language. The classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification used by the EPO and USPTO and falls under the hierarchy physics-musical-instruments-speech analysis techniques or speech synthesis.
[G10L 15/18] using natural language modelling -- G10L 15/18 pertains to speech-recognition systems that employ natural-language modelling to enhance the conversion of spoken input into text. It includes techniques that use statistical or neural language models to predict word sequences, thereby improving accuracy and contextual understanding during transcription.
[G10L 15/1807] using prosody or stress -- The G10L 15/1807 subclass concerns speech-recognition methods that exploit prosodic cues such as intonation, rhythm, stress or emphasis to improve accuracy. It includes techniques that analyse pitch, duration or energy patterns to segment, identify or disambiguate spoken words. This approach leverages the natural stress patterns of language rather than relying solely on phonetic content.
[G10L 15/1815] Semantic context, e.g. disambiguation of the recognition hypotheses based on word meaning -- The G10L 15/1815 subclass covers methods that use semantic information to resolve ambiguities in speech-recognition results, such as selecting the most plausible hypothesis based on word meaning or context. It is part of the speech-analysis techniques branch of physics that addresses disambiguation through semantic context.
[G10L 15/1822] Parsing for meaning understanding -- G10L 15/1822 covers methods and systems that analyse recognised speech to extract semantic structures, such as parsing sentences to determine grammatical roles and meaning. It belongs to the CPC group on speech recognition under speech analysis and synthesis.
[G10L 15/183] using context dependencies, e.g. language models -- This subclass addresses speech-recognition techniques that exploit context dependencies, such as language models, to improve transcription accuracy. It includes methods that use statistical or neural language modelling to predict word sequences and resolve ambiguities in spoken input.
[G10L 15/187] Phonemic context, e.g. pronunciation rules, phonotactical constraints or phoneme n-grams -- This subclass covers methods that incorporate phonemic context - rules governing how phonemes combine, phonotactic constraints, or statistical n-gram models of phoneme sequences - into speech-recognition systems to enhance accuracy.
[G10L 15/19] Grammatical context, e.g. disambiguation of the recognition hypotheses based on word sequence rules -- This CPC entry G10L 15/19 covers methods that use grammatical context to disambiguate speech-recognition hypotheses, applying word sequence rules for improved accuracy. It falls under physics, musical instruments, speech analysis techniques or speech synthesis, and speech recognition.
[G10L 15/193] Formal grammars, e.g. finite state automata, context free grammars or word networks -- G10L 15/193 covers the use of formal grammatical models such as finite-state automata, context-free grammars and word-network representations for speech-recognition systems, detailing how these structures parse, generate or constrain linguistic sequences to improve recognition accuracy.
[G10L 15/197] Probabilistic grammars, e.g. word n-grams -- G10L 15/197 concerns the use of statistical language models such as word n-grams to represent probabilistic grammars for speech recognition. It covers the construction, adaptation and application of these models to predict word sequences and improve decoding accuracy.
[G10L 15/20] Speech recognition techniques specially adapted for robustness in adverse environments, e.g. in noise, of stress induced speech -- G10L 15/20 addresses speech-recognition methods that are specifically engineered to operate reliably in noisy or stress-induced acoustic environments, enhancing robustness against background interference. The subclass lies within the hierarchy of physics, musical instruments, speech analysis techniques or synthesis, and speech recognition.
[G10L 15/22] Procedures used during a speech recognition process, e.g. man-machine dialogue -- Procedures used during a speech-recognition process cover the control steps that manage interaction between a user and a system, such as turn-taking, error handling, and confirmation. These methods enable effective man-machine dialogue by coordinating input, processing, and feedback.
[G10L 15/222] Barge in, i.e. overridable guidance for interrupting prompts -- The code covers methods and systems that let a user interrupt or override a spoken prompt during a speech-recognition interaction, commonly known as "barge-in." It includes detecting the interruption and handling the new input while the original guidance is still being delivered.
[G10L 15/24] Speech recognition using non-acoustical features -- G10L 15/24 covers methods for speech recognition that rely on non-acoustical information such as linguistic context, speaker characteristics, visual lip movements or environmental cues rather than the sound signal itself. It is part of the CPC group for speech analysis and synthesis within the musical instruments physics hierarchy.
[G10L 15/25] using position of the lips, movement of the lips or face analysis -- This subclass covers speech-recognition methods that use visual information from a speaker's lip position, lip movements, or facial gestures to aid or perform recognition, often in lip-reading or audiovisual systems.
[G10L 15/26] Speech to text systems -- G10L 15/26 covers technologies that transform spoken language into written text, including algorithms, hardware and software that recognize and transcribe speech. It falls under speech analysis and synthesis methods used in devices such as dictation tools, voice assistants and automated transcription services.
[G10L 15/28] Constructional details of speech recognition systems -- G10L 15/28 concerns the structural and hardware aspects of speech-recognition systems, covering the arrangement of microphones, signal-processing units and computing modules that convert acoustic input into text or commands. It addresses design choices such as sensor placement, acoustic preprocessing, feature-extraction hardware and integration with user interfaces within the broader field of speech analysis and synthesis.
[G10L 15/285] Memory allocation or algorithm optimisation to reduce hardware requirements -- G10L 15/285 concerns techniques that allocate memory or streamline algorithms in speech-recognition systems to lower hardware requirements. It covers methods such as efficient data structures, compression and computational optimisations that reduce processor load, memory usage or power consumption.
[G10L 15/30] Distributed recognition, e.g. in client-server systems, for mobile phones or network applications -- G10L 15/30 refers to speech-recognition systems that distribute processing between a client device and a remote server, such as mobile phones or networked applications that transmit audio to a central processor for analysis. It covers client-server architectures and other distributed arrangements for recognizing spoken input.
[G10L 15/32] Multiple recognisers used in sequence or in parallel -- This classification covers systems that employ two or more speech recognisers operating either one after another or simultaneously to improve recognition performance. The approach may involve combining outputs or selecting the best result.
[G10L 15/34] Adaptation of a single recogniser for parallel processing, e.g. by use of multiple processors or cloud computing -- This subclass covers techniques that transform a speech recogniser intended for sequential operation so it can be executed on parallel hardware such as multiple processors, multicore systems, or cloud-based infrastructures, thereby distributing the computational load to increase speed, scalability and real-time performance.
[G10L 17/00] Speaker identification or verification techniques -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Glossary:** - speaker verification or authentication: refers to verifying that the user claimed identity is real, he is otherwise an impostor. Speaker recognition, or identification, aims at determining who the user is among a closed (finite number) set of users. He is otherwise unknown. - impostor: someone actively trying to counterfeit someone else's identity.
[G10L 17/02] Preprocessing operations, e.g. segment selection -- G10L 17/02 covers preprocessing operations applied to speech signals for speaker identification or verification, including selection, trimming, or segmentation of audio segments to enhance subsequent analysis. These techniques are part of the CPC hierarchy under physics, musical instruments, and speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 17/04] Training, enrolment or model building -- G10L 17/04 covers methods for training, enrolment or model building in speaker-identification and verification systems, including the collection of voice samples and the construction of acoustic models that represent individual speaker characteristics for use in speech-analysis applications.
[G10L 17/06] Decision making techniques -- G10L 17/06 relates to decision-making techniques employed in speaker identification or verification systems, covering methods for selecting, weighting, or fusing speaker models based on acoustic evidence. It falls under speech analysis techniques and speech synthesis within the broader categories of physics and musical instruments.
[G10L 17/08] Use of distortion metrics or a particular distance between probe pattern and reference templates -- G10L 17/08 covers methods for speaker identification or verification that evaluate a distortion metric or a defined distance between an unknown speech probe and stored reference voice templates. It includes techniques such as dynamic-time-warping, Euclidean or Mahalanobis distances used to assess similarity for authentication.
[G10L 17/10] Multimodal systems, i.e. based on the integration of multiple recognition engines or fusion of expert systems -- G10L 17/10 covers multimodal systems that combine several recognition engines or fuse expert systems for speaker identification or verification. It pertains to technologies integrating multiple speech analysis components to improve accuracy in identifying or authenticating speakers.
[G10L 17/12] Score normalisation -- G10L 17/12 concerns methods for adjusting the output scores of speaker-identification or verification systems so that they can be compared across different speakers, sessions or recording conditions. It includes techniques for scaling, calibrating or otherwise normalising likelihood or distance scores generated by speech-analysis algorithms. This falls under the broader categories of physics, musical instruments and speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 17/14] Use of phonemic categorisation or speech recognition prior to speaker recognition or verification -- G10L 17/14 covers methods that first apply phonemic categorisation or speech-recognition processing to an utterance and then use the resulting linguistic information to assist or improve speaker identification or verification. The approach exploits the recognised phoneme sequence as a preprocessing step before the speaker-recognition stage, enhancing accuracy or robustness of the final speaker-specific analysis.
[G10L 17/16] Hidden Markov models [HMM] -- G10L 17/16 denotes the use of hidden Markov models for speaker identification or verification within speech-analysis and synthesis technologies. The subclass falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification system, nested in the physics branch through musical instruments and speech-analysis techniques, and covers algorithms that model temporal speech patterns for biometric recognition.
[G10L 17/18] Artificial neural networks -- The G10L 17/18 entry covers the application of artificial neural-network techniques to speaker identification and verification within speech analysis or synthesis. It includes methods for training, modeling and classifying voice characteristics using neural architectures, such as deep or convolutional networks, to recognize or authenticate individual speakers.
[G10L 17/20] Pattern transformations or operations aimed at increasing system robustness, e.g. against channel noise or different working conditions -- G10L 17/20 covers methods that modify speech patterns or apply operations to make speaker-identification or verification systems more robust, for example by compensating for channel noise, variabilities in recording conditions, or changes in the speaker's environment. These techniques aim to preserve accuracy when the input signal is degraded or altered.
[G10L 17/22] Interactive procedures -- The G10L 17/22 entry covers interactive methods used to confirm or determine a speaker's identity, where the system engages the user during the verification process. It includes techniques that prompt the speaker for responses, adjust challenge material in real time, or require user-initiated actions to improve accuracy and security.
[G10L 17/24] the user being prompted to utter a password or a predefined phrase -- G10L 17/24 covers speech-based authentication methods in which the system prompts the user to utter a password or a predefined phrase for speaker identification or verification. It includes the generation of the prompt, selection of the phrase, and processing of the spoken response to confirm the claimed identity.
[G10L 17/26] Recognition of special voice characteristics, e.g. for use in lie detectors -- The code G10L 17/26 covers techniques that analyze and recognize distinctive vocal features such as stress, emotion, pitch changes or physiological cues, enabling systems to infer hidden states or verify identity, for example in lie-detector applications. It falls under speech analysis methods used to identify or authenticate speakers.
[G10L 19/00] Speech or audio signals analysis-synthesis techniques for redundancy reduction, e.g. in vocoders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Techniques for the reduction of data from audio sources, i.e. compression of audio. These techniques are applied to reduce the quantity of information to be stored or transmitted, but are independent of the end-application, medium or transmission channel, i.e. do only exploit the properties of the source signal itself or the final receiver exposed to this signal (the listener).  Mainly two types of sources can be distinguished :  "speech only" encompass signals produced by human speakers, and historically was to be understood as mono-channel, single speaker "telephone quality" speech having a narrow bandwidth limited to max. 4kHz. Encoding of speech only sources primarily aim at reducing the bit-rate while still providing fair intelligibility of the spoken content, but not always fidelity to the original.  "Audio signal" is broader and comprises speech as well as background information, e.g. music source having multiple channels. Encoding of audio deals primarily with transparent, i.e. "high fidelity" reproduction of the original signal.  The compression techniques can also be distinguished as being :  Lossy or Lossless, i.e. whether a perfect reconstruction of the source is possible, or only a perceptually acceptable approximation can be done.  The techniques classified in this subclass are based either on modelling the production of the signal (voice) or the perception of it (general audio).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Coding of signals within electronic musical instruments -> G10H  **Glossary:** - audio signal: is meant to include speech, music, silence or background signal, or any combinations thereof, unless explicitly specified
[G10L 19/0017] Lossless audio signal coding; Perfect reconstruction of coded audio signal by transmission of coding error  G10L19/24  takes precedence -- The G10L 19/0017 class addresses lossless audio signal coding methods that enable perfect reconstruction of the original signal by transmitting the coding error, often used in redundancy-reduction vocoders. It belongs to the speech-analysis-synthesis branch of physics and musical instruments. The broader subclass G10L 19/24 has priority over this specific entry.
[G10L 19/0018] Speech coding using phonetic or linguistical decoding of the source; Reconstruction using text-to-speech synthesis -- G10L 19/0018 covers methods that compress speech by decoding the source into phonetic or linguistic units and then reconstructing it with text-to-speech synthesis. It is used in vocoders and other speech coding systems to reduce redundancy while preserving intelligibility.
[G10L 19/002] Dynamic bit allocation -- Dynamic bit allocation comprises techniques that vary the number of bits assigned to different portions of a speech or audio signal during processing, adapting to signal characteristics to minimise redundancy and improve compression efficiency in vocoders and related speech-analysis or synthesis systems.
[G10L 19/005] Correction of errors induced by the transmission channel, if related to the coding algorithm -- G10L 19/005 concerns methods that correct errors introduced by the transmission channel using information tied to the speech-coding algorithm, such as redundancy or parity embedded in coded audio. It applies to vocoders and other speech or audio coding systems that integrate error-correction with the coding process.
[G10L 19/008] Multichannel audio signal coding or decoding using interchannel correlation to reduce redundancy, e.g. joint-stereo, intensity-coding or matrixing -- The G10L 19/008 entry covers methods for coding or decoding multichannel audio by exploiting inter-channel correlation to diminish redundancy, such as joint-stereo, intensity-coding, and matrixing techniques. It falls under speech-or-audio signal analysis-synthesis methods aimed at reducing data size while preserving sound quality.
[G10L 19/012] Comfort noise or silence coding -- G10L 19/012 relates to techniques that generate or encode comfort noise and silence during speech transmission, allowing a receiver to hear background sounds that mask abrupt gaps. These methods are used in vocoders and other speech synthesis systems to reduce perceived artifacts and improve the naturalness of reconstructed audio.
[G10L 19/018] Audio watermarking, i.e. embedding inaudible data in the audio signal -- G10L 19/018 covers techniques for embedding inaudible information into audio signals, known as audio watermarking. The methods modify the audio in a perceptually transparent way so that the hidden data can be detected or extracted later for purposes such as copyright protection, authentication, or tracking.
[G10L 19/02] using spectral analysis, e.g. transform vocoders or subband vocoders -- G10L 19/02 covers techniques that analyse speech or audio signals by spectral methods to reduce redundancy, typically employing transform-based or sub-band vocoders. It pertains to signal-processing approaches used in speech synthesis and analysis, falling under physics → musical instruments → speech analysis-synthesis.
[G10L 19/0204] using subband decomposition -- G10L 19/0204 refers to techniques that analyse or synthesise speech or audio signals by first decomposing them into sub-bands, a step often employed to reduce redundancy in vocoders and other speech coding methods. The entry is placed under physics, musical instruments and speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 19/0208] Subband vocoders -- G10L 19/0208 covers subband vocoders, which analyse speech or audio signals by splitting them into frequency subbands and processing each band separately to reduce redundancy and enable efficient synthesis. These techniques are used in speech coding, transmission and compression.
[G10L 19/0212] using orthogonal transformation -- G10L 19/0212 covers methods that apply orthogonal transformations such as Fourier, cosine or wavelet transforms to speech or audio signals for analysis or synthesis, mainly to reduce redundancy and improve coding efficiency in vocoders and similar devices.
[G10L 19/0216] using wavelet decomposition -- G10L 19/0216 relates to techniques that employ wavelet decomposition for the analysis and synthesis of speech or audio signals, targeting redundancy reduction such as in vocoder implementations. It falls under the broader categories of physics, musical instruments, and speech analysis or synthesis methods.
[G10L 19/022] Blocking, i.e. grouping of samples in time -- Blocking is the technique of grouping successive audio samples into time-aligned blocks for joint processing in speech or audio signal analysis and synthesis. By handling samples in blocks, redundancy can be reduced and operations such as encoding, filtering, or feature extraction become more efficient, a principle employed in vocoders and related speech-coding methods.
[G10L 19/025] Detection of transients or attacks for time/frequency resolution switching -- G10L 19/025 covers methods that identify sudden changes or attacks in speech or audio signals, such as transients, to dynamically switch between time-focused and frequency-focused analysis. By detecting these events, the system can adapt its resolution, improving efficiency in vocoders and other redundancy-reduction techniques.
[G10L 19/028] Noise substitution, i.e. substituting non-tonal spectral components by noisy source -- It covers methods for replacing non-tonal spectral components of an audio signal with a generated noise source to achieve redundancy reduction, typical in vocoders and speech synthesis. This falls under speech analysis-synthesis techniques for redundancy reduction.
[G10L 19/03] Spectral prediction for preventing pre-echo -- G10L 19/03 relates to spectral prediction methods employed to prevent pre-echo artifacts in speech or audio coding, especially in vocoders and other redundancy-reduction techniques. It is classified under speech and audio signal analysis-synthesis within the physics domain of musical instruments.
[G10L 19/032] Quantisation or dequantisation of spectral components -- This subclass concerns methods that quantise or de-quantise spectral components of speech or audio signals, typically within vocoder-type coding schemes that reduce redundancy. It includes techniques for compressing spectral coefficients and reconstructing them during synthesis, covering both the encoding and decoding stages of spectral data.
[G10L 19/035] Scalar quantisation -- G10L 19/035 concerns scalar quantisation techniques applied to speech or audio signal analysis-synthesis, especially for reducing redundancy in vocoder-type systems. It is situated in the CPC hierarchy under physics, musical instruments and speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 19/038] Vector quantisation, e.g. TwinVQ audio -- G10L 19/038 covers vector-quantisation techniques for speech and audio coding, including methods like TwinVQ that compress signals by reducing redundancy through quantising groups of spectral samples rather than individual samples. It falls under analysis-synthesis approaches for speech or audio signal processing within the broader physics-to-musical-instrument-to-speech-analysis hierarchy.
[G10L 19/04] using predictive techniques -- G10L 19/04 covers the use of predictive techniques in speech or audio signal processing to reduce redundancy, such as those employed in vocoders and other analysis-synthesis systems within the broader fields of physics, musical instruments, and speech analysis-synthesis.
[G10L 19/06] Determination or coding of the spectral characteristics, e.g. of the short-term prediction coefficients -- The CPC subclass G10L 19/06 covers methods for extracting or representing spectral features of speech or audio signals, such as determining short-term prediction coefficients used in vocoders and other redundancy-reduction techniques, and lies under physics, musical instruments, and speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 19/07] Line spectrum pair [LSP] vocoders -- Line-spectrum-pair (LSP) vocoders encode speech by representing the linear predictive filter with pairs of frequencies that define the spectral envelope, enabling efficient quantisation and synthesis. They are used to reduce redundancy in speech or audio signals and are a common technique in low-bit-rate coding. This classification covers methods and implementations of such LSP-based vocoders.
[G10L 19/08] Determination or coding of the excitation function -- The G10L 19/08 class covers techniques for extracting, modeling or digitally encoding the excitation component of speech or audio signals, such as estimating the glottal source or other periodic excitation signals. These methods are used in vocoders and other speech-coding or synthesis systems to reduce redundancy and improve efficiency.
[G10L 19/083] the excitation function being an excitation gain -- G10L 19/083 pertains to speech or audio-signal processing methods in which the excitation function is represented by an excitation gain, a technique commonly used in vocoders for redundancy reduction. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Physics → Musical Instruments → Speech Analysis Techniques or Speech Synthesis.
[G10L 19/087] using mixed excitation models, e.g. MELP, MBE, split band LPC or HVXC -- The entry covers speech and audio signal analysis-synthesis methods that employ mixed-excitation models such as MELP, MBE, split-band LPC or HVXC to reduce redundancy, typically used in vocoders and related communication systems.
[G10L 19/09] Long term prediction, i.e. removing periodical redundancies, e.g. by using adaptive codebook or pitch predictor -- The CPC class G10L 19/09 deals with long-term prediction methods used in speech or audio signal processing to eliminate periodic redundancies, typically employing adaptive codebooks or pitch predictors. It falls under Physics → Musical Instruments → Speech Analysis Techniques or Speech Synthesis.
[G10L 19/093] using sinusoidal excitation models -- G10L 19/093 concerns techniques that employ sinusoidal excitation models for speech or audio signal analysis and synthesis, using a set of sinusoidal components to represent the source excitation in vocoders and similar systems. The approach aims to reduce redundancy and improve efficiency or quality in speech processing applications.
[G10L 19/097] using prototype waveform decomposition or prototype waveform interpolative [PWI] coders -- G10L 19/097 covers techniques that compress speech or audio by representing the signal with a set of prototype waveforms and coding their decomposition or interpolation, a method used in vocoders to reduce redundancy. These prototype waveform coders analyze and synthesize the signal for efficient transmission or storage.
[G10L 19/10] the excitation function being a multipulse excitation -- G10L 19/10 covers techniques that use a multipulse excitation function in speech or audio signal processing, primarily for redundancy reduction such as in vocoder implementations. It falls under physics-related methods for musical instruments and speech analysis or synthesis, describing how multiple pulse waveforms replace a single excitation source to improve coding efficiency and signal quality.
[G10L 19/107] Sparse pulse excitation, e.g. by using algebraic codebook -- This classification covers methods that generate speech or audio signals using sparse pulse excitation, often implemented with algebraic codebooks to efficiently represent excitation in low-bit-rate vocoders. It falls under speech analysis-synthesis techniques for redundancy reduction within the physics sub-field of musical instruments.
[G10L 19/113] Regular pulse excitation -- G10L 19/113 relates to techniques that employ regular pulse excitation in the analysis or synthesis of speech and audio signals, such as vocoder methods that reduce redundancy by modeling the source signal as a periodic pulse train. It falls under the hierarchy of physics, musical instruments and speech analysis-synthesis techniques.
[G10L 19/12] the excitation function being a code excitation, e.g. in code excited linear prediction [CELP] vocoders -- G10L 19/12 relates to speech and audio signal processing methods that use a coded excitation source, such as code-excited linear prediction (CELP) vocoders, for synthesis. The entry covers techniques that reduce signal redundancy by generating the excitation function from a codebook during analysis-synthesis.
[G10L 19/125] Pitch excitation, e.g. pitch synchronous innovation CELP [PSI-CELP] -- G10L 19/125 covers speech-coding methods that generate the excitation signal by synchronising pitch periods, known as pitch synchronous innovation CELP. It deals with techniques for analysing or synthesising speech and audio signals to reduce redundancy, such as in vocoders and other speech compression systems.
[G10L 19/13] Residual excited linear prediction [RELP] -- The CPC subclass G10L 19/13 covers residual excited linear prediction (RELP), a technique for modelling and synthesising speech or audio signals that reduces redundancy. RELP estimates an excitation signal from the linear-prediction residual and is often employed in vocoder applications.
[G10L 19/135] Vector sum excited linear prediction [VSELP] -- G10L 19/135 covers vector sum excited linear prediction (VSELP), a speech-coding method that models speech as a linear prediction filter excited by a vector sum of codebook pulses. It is used to reduce redundancy in audio signals, typical of low-bit-rate vocoders.
[G10L 19/16] Vocoder architecture -- G10L 19/16, titled Vocoder architecture, falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification for speech analysis and synthesis within the physics of musical instruments. It covers designs of vocoders and related signal-processing structures that reduce redundancy in speech or audio signals. The subclass addresses architectural features and implementations used in speech coding and synthesis.
[G10L 19/167] Audio streaming, i.e. formatting and decoding of an encoded audio signal representation into a data stream for transmission or storage purposes -- The G10L 19/167 entry covers methods for formatting and decoding an encoded audio signal into a continuous data stream for transmission or storage, including streaming protocols, packetization and bit-rate adaptation used in speech and audio codecs. It falls under speech-analysis-or-synthesis techniques aimed at redundancy reduction.
[G10L 19/173] Transcoding, i.e. converting between two coded representations avoiding cascaded coding-decoding -- The CPC entry G10L 19/173 concerns transcoding, which converts a signal from one coded representation to another without first decoding it, a technique used to avoid cascaded coding-decoding steps. It applies to speech or audio signal analysis-synthesis methods that reduce redundancy, such as those employed in vocoders, and falls under the hierarchy of physics, musical instruments, and speech analysis-synthesis.
[G10L 19/18] Vocoders using multiple modes -- The G10L 19/18 class covers vocoder systems that employ two or more operating modes to analyze, compress, or synthesize speech or audio signals, often to reduce redundancy and improve quality. It lies within the speech-analysis-and-synthesis subgroup of physics for musical instruments.
[G10L 19/20] using sound class specific coding, hybrid encoders or object based coding -- G10L 19/20 relates to techniques that use sound-class-specific coding, hybrid encoders or object-based coding to analyse or synthesise speech or audio signals for redundancy reduction, such as in vocoder systems. It is situated within the hierarchy of physics, musical instruments, and speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 19/22] Mode decision, i.e. based on audio signal content versus external parameters -- The G10L 19/22 entry covers techniques that choose processing modes for speech or audio signals by evaluating the content of the signal itself rather than relying solely on externally supplied parameters. This decision-making is used in vocoders and other redundancy-reduction methods to optimise synthesis or analysis performance.
[G10L 19/24] Variable rate codecs, e.g. for generating different qualities using a scalable representation such as hierarchical encoding or layered encoding -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Coding of a signal with rate adaptation, e.g. adapted to voiced speech, unvoiced speech, transitions and noise/silence portions.  Coding of a signal with a core encoder providing a minimum level of quality, and extension layers to improve the quality but requiring a higher bitrate. It includes parameter based bandwidth extension (i.e. SBR) or channel extension.  This group is in opposition to G10L21/038 in which the bandwidth extension is artificial, i.e. based on the only narrowband encoded signal.
[G10L 19/26] Pre-filtering or post-filtering -- G10L 19/26 relates to methods that apply a filter before or after speech or audio-signal processing to reduce redundancy, such as pre-filtering or post-filtering stages used in vocoders and other speech analysis-synthesis systems.
[G10L 19/265] Pre-filtering, e.g. high frequency emphasis prior to encoding -- G10L 19/265 relates to pre-filtering techniques, such as high-frequency emphasis, applied to speech or audio signals before encoding to improve redundancy reduction in vocoders and similar speech-analysis or synthesis systems.
[G10L 2013/021] Overlap-add techniques -- The Overlap-add technique is a method for efficiently performing convolution or other linear operations on long digital signals by dividing the input into overlapping blocks, processing each block (often with a fast Fourier transform), and then adding the overlapping portions to reconstruct the output. It is widely used in audio, communications and image processing where real-time filtering of large data streams is required.
[G10L 2013/083] Special characters, e.g. punctuation marks -- G10L 2013/083 refers to devices, software or methods that enable the input, generation or processing of special-characters such as punctuation marks. It falls under the G10L group for data entry and handling, covering keyboards, virtual input panels and related control mechanisms.
[G10L 2013/105] Duration -- G10L 2013/105 - Duration refers to patent subject matter concerning the timing and length of speech elements in speech recognition or synthesis systems. It includes methods for measuring, adjusting, or controlling the duration of phonemes, words, or utterances to improve accuracy or naturalness.
[G10L 2015/022] Demisyllables, biphones or triphones being the recognition units -- The CPC entry G10L 2015/022 covers speech-processing technologies that use sub-syllabic units such as demisyllables, biphones or triphones as the basic elements for acoustic or language model recognition. It pertains to systems and methods that segment spoken input into these paired or triple phoneme groups for more accurate decoding in speech-recognition or synthesis applications.
[G10L 2015/025] Phonemes, fenemes or fenones being the recognition units -- This subclass covers speech processing technologies that use phonemes, fenemes or fenones as the fundamental units for recognition. It includes methods, devices and software that convert spoken language into text or commands by analyzing these acoustic units, such as voice command systems, automatic transcription and language-learning tools.
[G10L 2015/027] Syllables being the recognition units -- The CPC entry G10L 2015/027 covers speech-recognition systems that use syllables as the basic units for processing spoken input. It includes methods, devices and software that segment, identify and interpret syllables to convert speech into text or commands. This approach is applied in voice-controlled applications, transcription tools and interactive voice response systems.
[G10L 2015/0631] Creating reference templates; Clustering -- The CPC subclass G10L 2015/0631 covers methods and systems that generate reference templates and employ clustering techniques to group similar data, chiefly in speech-signal processing such as speaker recognition, speech synthesis, or acoustic model adaptation. It includes procedures for building template libraries, clustering acoustic features, and updating models based on the resulting clusters.
[G10L 2015/0633] using lexical or orthographic knowledge sources -- G10L 2015/0633 covers methods and systems that incorporate lexical or orthographic knowledge sources-such as dictionaries, word lists, or spelling rules-to enhance language processing tasks like speech recognition, text-to-speech, or machine translation, including disambiguation, error correction and pronunciation modeling.
[G10L 2015/0635] updating or merging of old and new templates; Mean values; Weighting -- The entry relates to methods for updating or merging existing and new templates by computing weighted mean values, enabling automatic adjustment of data models. It includes techniques that apply weighting factors during template integration to improve accuracy in data-processing applications.
[G10L 2015/0636] Threshold criteria for the updating -- This entry defines the numerical conditions that must be met before a speech-recognition or text-entry system updates its language or acoustic model, such as confidence-score limits, error-rate thresholds, or usage-frequency criteria that trigger model revision. It addresses adaptive updating mechanisms to maintain or improve performance in spoken-language applications.
[G10L 2015/0638] Interactive procedures -- G10L 2015/0638 Interactive procedures covers methods and apparatus for managing interactive communication sessions, such as voice-driven dialogs, menu navigation, and real-time user interaction in telephony and speech-enabled services.
[G10L 2015/081] Search algorithms, e.g. Baum-Welch or Viterbi -- The CPC entry G10L 2015/081 covers patent inventions that implement or improve statistical search methods for decoding or estimating hidden Markov models, such as the Baum-Welch training algorithm and the Viterbi path-finding algorithm, used in signal processing, speech recognition, and related data-analysis applications.
[G10L 2015/085] Methods for reducing search complexity, pruning -- G10L 2015/085 covers techniques that simplify search processes by eliminating unnecessary paths or candidates, such as pruning strategies in tree, graph or database searches. It includes methods that reduce computational load, improve efficiency, and limit the number of examined items during information retrieval, pattern matching or optimization procedures.
[G10L 2015/086] Recognition of spelled words -- G10L 2015/086 refers to technologies that recognize words which have been spelled out, converting a sequence of individually spoken or entered letters into the intended word for processing, display or further use.
[G10L 2015/088] Word spotting -- G10L 2015/088 covers techniques for locating specific words in continuous speech or audio streams without transcribing the entire content. It includes methods and systems for detecting, indexing and retrieving spoken material based on the presence of target words, useful in voice-controlled search, monitoring and assistive applications.
[G10L 2015/221] Announcement of recognition results -- The entry G10L 2015/221 covers systems, methods and devices for communicating the outcome of a recognition process, such as awards, certifications, competitions or rankings. It includes electronic or printed announcements, displays of scores or rankings, and any associated formatting or dissemination steps that convey the results to recipients or the public.
[G10L 2015/223] Execution procedure of a spoken command -- G10L 2015/223 covers techniques for carrying out a spoken command, including the detection, interpretation, and execution of voice instructions in electronic devices. It addresses the processing steps that transform spoken input into commands that trigger specific functions or actions.
[G10L 2015/225] Feedback of the input speech -- The CPC entry G10L 2015/225 relates to systems or methods that provide the user with an audible reproduction of the spoken input they have just entered, typically in speech-recognition or voice-controlled devices, enabling the user to verify or correct the captured speech.
[G10L 2015/226] using non-speech characteristics -- G10L 2015/226 covers technologies that employ non-speech cues-such as facial movements, lip patterns, gestures, physiological signals or other biometric data-to recognise, interpret or control speech-related functions, often supplementing or replacing conventional acoustic analysis.
[G10L 2015/227] of the speaker;  Human-factor methodology -- The G10L 2015/227 entry covers human-factor methodology applied to speakers, detailing approaches for evaluating, designing and optimizing speaker systems based on user ergonomics, perception and interaction. It includes techniques for assessing acoustic performance, comfort, control layout and overall usability to ensure the device meets human-centred design criteria.
[G10L 2015/228] of application context -- G10L 2015/228 covers techniques for obtaining, managing or using information that defines the context in which a software application operates, such as user environment, device state, location or operating conditions. It includes devices, programs or methods that adapt an application's behavior based on that contextual data.
[G10L 2019/0001] Codebooks -- G10L 2019/0001 - Codebooks - designates the use of predefined tables or algorithms that translate input data into coded forms for functions such as compression, encryption or error correction, and the hardware or software that implements those translations.
[G10L 2019/0002] Codebook adaptations -- G10L 2019/0002 covers inventions that modify or customize codebooks used in data processing applications such as encryption, compression, or signal coding. It includes methods for adapting, updating, or converting codebook entries to suit particular hardware, software environments or user requirements. The classification is applied to technical solutions that change the structure or use of codebooks.
[G10L 2019/0003] Backward prediction of gain -- The entry defines methods and apparatuses for determining gain settings by using backward-prediction algorithms that estimate forthcoming signal characteristics, enabling adaptive adjustment of amplification or attenuation in speech coding, audio enhancement, or hearing-aid applications to improve intelligibility and reduce distortion.
[G10L 2019/0004] Design or structure of the codebook -- G10L 2019/0004 covers the design and structural aspects of codebooks used in data processing, including the arrangement, format and indexing of tables that map input data to coded output for functions such as compression, encryption or error correction. It addresses how the codebook is organized, represented and accessed within software or hardware systems.
[G10L 2019/0005] Multi-stage vector quantisation -- G10L 2019/0005 covers multi-stage vector quantisation techniques used in data compression, where a signal is encoded by successive quantisation stages with progressively refined codebooks. The method improves compression efficiency and quality for audio, image or video data by applying hierarchical codebooks and residual coding. It falls within the CPC group for signal processing and data handling.
[G10L 2019/0006] Tree or treillis structures; Delayed decisions -- The G10L 2019/0006 entry covers arrangements that employ tree-or-trellis structures for processing or routing signals and that incorporate delayed decision techniques, such as postponing the selection of a path or output until additional information is available. These concepts are used in communication and signal-processing circuits to improve error correction, decoding or routing efficiency.
[G10L 2019/0007] Codebook element generation -- G10L 2019/0007, Codebook element generation, relates to methods for creating the individual entries of a codebook used in data compression, signal processing or vector quantisation. It includes techniques for selecting or adapting representative patterns, vectors or prototype signals that map input data to encoded forms for efficient storage or transmission.
[G10L 2019/0008] Algebraic codebooks -- G10L 2019/0008 covers algebraic codebooks, which are sets of code vectors created by algebraic constructions for use in signal processing, communications and data compression. It includes methods for generating, analysing and applying such codebooks in modulation, error-correction and source-coding schemes.
[G10L 2019/0009] Orthogonal codebooks -- G10L 2019/0009 covers orthogonal codebooks, which are predefined sets of mutually orthogonal sequences or matrices used in wireless communication systems to enable efficient channel estimation, beamforming and space-time coding, particularly in MIMO and massive-MIMO applications. The entry addresses the design, generation and application of such codebooks for improving signal separation and reducing interference.
[G10L 2019/001] Interpolation of codebook vectors -- G10L 2019/001 covers techniques for interpolating codebook vectors in digital signal processing, particularly in audio and speech coding where a set of representative vectors is refined to improve compression quality. The classification includes methods that generate intermediate vectors to enhance reconstruction accuracy or reduce bitrate.
[G10L 2019/0011] Long term prediction filters, i.e. pitch estimation -- The entry G10L 2019/0011 covers methods and apparatus for extracting the fundamental frequency of a signal by using long-term prediction filters. It includes pitch detection in speech coding, analysis, synthesis, and related signal-processing techniques that estimate periodicity from past samples.
[G10L 2019/0012] Smoothing of parameters of the decoder interpolation -- G10L 2019/0012 covers techniques for smoothing the parameters used in decoder interpolation, typically in audio or speech coding systems, where gradual adjustment of parameter values reduces audible artifacts during decoding. The entry details methods such as low-pass filtering, interpolation weighting, or predictive smoothing to improve output quality when transitioning between frames. This classification is applied to algorithms that manage parameter continuity in codec decoders.
[G10L 2019/0013] Codebook search algorithms -- G10L 2019/0013 covers inventions that implement algorithms for searching codebooks, including pattern-matching and lookup techniques used in data compression, cryptographic decoding, and error-correction contexts. It is part of the CPC group G10L, which addresses information retrieval, database and file-management methods.
[G10L 2019/0014] Selection criteria for distances -- G10L 2019/0014 covers the criteria used to select or define distances that a device measures, processes, or displays, such as ranges, thresholds, or intervals relevant to positioning, range-finding or distance-based functions. It includes methods for determining which distances are considered significant for the operation or output of the invention.
[G10L 2019/0015] Viterbi algorithms -- G10L 2019/0015 covers the use of the Viterbi algorithm, a dynamic-programming method for finding the most likely sequence of states in hidden Markov models, applied to speech, audio or signal processing tasks such as decoding, speech recognition, and error correction in digital communication systems.
[G10L 2019/0016] Codebook for LPC parameters -- The CPC entry G10L 2019/0016 relates to speech processing technologies, specifically to a codebook that stores quantised linear predictive coding (LPC) parameters for use in speech compression, synthesis or recognition. It covers methods for generating, organising and applying such LPC parameter codebooks within digital speech systems.
[G10L 2021/0135] Voice conversion or morphing -- G10L covers technology for processing and analyzing speech signals, including conversion and synthesis. The subclass 2021/0135 specifically addresses methods and systems that transform a speaker's voice characteristics to sound like another speaker, often using statistical or neural models to alter pitch, timbre, and other acoustic features while preserving linguistic content. This includes applications in entertainment, privacy protection, and assistive communication.
[G10L 2021/02082] the noise being echo, reverberation of the speech -- This CPC entry G10L 2021/02082 relates to speech processing methods that address echo and reverberation as noise, including detection, modelling and suppression techniques aimed at improving speech intelligibility in communication devices.
[G10L 2021/02085] Periodic noise -- G10L 2021/02085 falls under the CPC group for sound, speech, or music recording, storage, or reproduction. The subclass deals with methods or devices that generate, process, or reduce periodic noise such as hum, buzz, or vibration-induced tones in audio or acoustic systems. It is used for patents addressing noise cancellation, filtering, or modulation techniques targeting regularly repeating disturbances.
[G10L 2021/02087] the noise being separate speech, e.g. cocktail party -- The G10L 2021/02087 subclass concerns techniques for separating target speech from other concurrent speech signals, treating the interfering speech as noise. It covers methods and devices that isolate a speaker's voice in multi-speaker settings such as a cocktail-party environment to improve recognition or processing.
[G10L 2021/02161] Number of inputs available containing the signal or the noise to be suppressed -- The CPC entry concerns devices or methods that consider how many input channels carrying the desired signal and the unwanted noise are available for processing. It covers arrangements and techniques that use this count of inputs to implement or improve noise-suppression functions in audio, communication or measurement systems.
[G10L 2021/02163] Only one microphone -- G10L 2021/02163 covers inventions that use a single microphone for capturing speech or other audio signals in voice-controlled devices. The entry addresses techniques for improving signal quality, directionality or noise suppression when only one acoustic transducer is available. It is part of the CPC group for speech recognition and voice input.
[G10L 2021/02165] Two microphones, one receiving mainly the noise signal and the other one mainly the speech signal -- G10L 2021/02165 covers devices or methods that employ a pair of microphones where one is positioned or filtered to capture predominantly ambient noise and the other to capture primarily the speech signal, enabling the separation or suppression of noise from speech for clearer audio output.
[G10L 2021/02166] Microphone arrays; Beamforming -- The G10L 2021/02166 entry covers devices that use multiple microphones arranged in a configured array to capture sound, along with the signal-processing techniques that steer or focus the acoustic reception pattern, known as beamforming, to enhance desired audio sources and suppress noise.
[G10L 2021/02168] the estimation exclusively taking place during speech pauses -- This CPC subclass covers methods or devices that perform estimations such as speaker or acoustic parameter calculation only during the pauses between spoken segments. The approach uses silent intervals to process data without interfering with ongoing speech.
[G10L 2021/03643] Diver speech -- This CPC subclass covers methods and devices that enable spoken communication for underwater divers, including speech capture, processing, recognition or synthesis in a diving environment. It addresses the challenges of acoustic propagation in water and integrates with diving gear such as helmets or communication units.
[G10L 2021/03646] Stress or Lombard effect -- The G10L 2021/03646 subclass addresses techniques for detecting or simulating speaker stress and the Lombard effect, wherein speech parameters such as loudness, pitch, or articulation change in response to noise or physiological strain, and the corresponding adaptations in speech synthesis, coding, or recognition systems.
[G10L 2021/0575] Aids for the handicapped in speaking -- G10L 2021/0575 covers devices, systems or methods that assist individuals with speech impairments in producing or articulating spoken language. It includes voice synthesizers, speech-generating devices, augmentative and alternative communication tools, and related control interfaces designed for disabled users.
[G10L 2021/065] Aids for the handicapped in understanding -- G10L 2021/065 covers devices, software or methods that assist persons with disabilities in comprehending visual, textual or auditory information, such as speech-to-text converters, Braille output devices, captioning systems and other assistive technologies that facilitate understanding.
[G10L 2021/105] Synthesis of the lips movements from speech, e.g. for talking heads -- G10L 2021/105 relates to techniques that generate realistic lip motions from an audio signal, enabling talking-head or avatar animation synchronized with speech. It includes algorithms, models and devices that convert spoken language into visual articulation for applications such as virtual assistants, video dubbing and assistive communication.
[G10L 2025/783] based on threshold decision -- G10L 2025/783 covers control or monitoring functions for electrical power supplies that operate by comparing a measured parameter such as voltage, current or frequency with a preset limit and taking action when the threshold is crossed, for example activating, deactivating or adjusting a device based on that decision. It includes threshold-based logic implemented in circuits, software or control systems for power distribution and protection.
[G10L 2025/786] Adaptive threshold -- The CPC class G10L 2025/786 covers adaptive-threshold methods in speech-signal processing, where the detection or decision threshold is continuously adjusted based on the characteristics of the incoming audio, such as signal-to-noise ratio or speech activity. These techniques are used for tasks like voice-activity detection, noise suppression, and dynamic range control.
[G10L 2025/903] using a laryngograph -- G10L 2025/903 pertains to the use of a laryngograph to record and analyse vocal-cord vibrations or airflow during speech or breathing. It includes methods, devices and signal-processing techniques for medical diagnosis, voice training, or bio-feedback applications that rely on laryngographic measurements.
[G10L 2025/906] Pitch tracking -- The CPC sub-group G10L 2025/906 covers methods and apparatus for tracking the pitch of a sound signal, such as determining the fundamental frequency of musical tones, speech or other audio. It includes real-time pitch detection, analysis, and display for tuning instruments, voice training, or audio processing systems.
[G10L 2025/932] Decision in previous or following frames -- G10L 2025/932 covers methods, systems or devices that base a processing decision on information from a previous or a following video frame, such as using adjacent-frame data for compression, error correction, motion estimation, or object-tracking decisions. It addresses the use of temporal context to improve the accuracy or efficiency of video-signal handling.
[G10L 2025/935] Mixed voiced class; Transitions -- G10L 2025/935 covers inventions that analyse, generate or modify speech signals containing mixed-voiced sounds and the transitions between voiced and unvoiced segments. It includes techniques for detecting, classifying or synthesising these mixed-voiced classes in speech processing applications such as recognition, coding or synthesis.
[G10L 2025/937] Signal energy in various frequency bands -- G10L 2025/937 covers methods and apparatus for measuring or analyzing the energy of a signal across different frequency bands, including techniques for broadband, narrowband and multiband power assessment in telecommunications, broadcasting and EMC applications. It addresses the characterization of signal strength, spectral distribution and related performance parameters.
[G10L 21/00] Speech or voice signal processing techniques to produce another audible or non-audible signal, e.g. visual or tactile, in order to modify its quality or its intelligibility -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The subgroup deals with speech or voice modification applications, but receives also applications for speech or voice analysis techniques specially adapted to analyse or modify audio signals not necessarily including speech or voice but which are not music signals ( G10H ).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Speech or audio signal analysis-synthesis techniques for redundancy reduction, e.g. in vocoders; Coding or decoding of speech or audio signals, using source-filter models or psychoacoustic analysis -> G10L19/00  **Glossary:** - viseme: a visual representation of the mouth, lips, tongue and teeth corresponding to a phoneme.
[G10L 21/003] Changing voice quality, e.g. pitch or formants -- G10L 21/003 covers methods and devices that alter the quality of a spoken or sung voice, such as adjusting pitch, formant frequencies, or other acoustic characteristics to improve intelligibility or achieve a desired effect. It falls under speech analysis techniques or synthesis within the musical instruments branch of physics.
[G10L 21/007] characterised by the process used -- This subclass covers speech or voice signal processing methods that generate an alternative audible, visual or tactile output to alter the signal's quality or intelligibility. It is defined by the specific process employed rather than by the device or application.
[G10L 21/01] Correction of time axis -- G10L 21/01 covers methods that adjust the timing of speech or voice signals, such as stretching, compressing, or aligning the time axis to improve intelligibility, quality, or synchronization with other media. It falls under the CPC group for speech analysis and synthesis within the broader fields of physics and musical instruments.
[G10L 21/013] Adapting to target pitch -- G10L 21/013 covers methods that modify a spoken or sung signal by adjusting its pitch to match a desired target frequency. The techniques are used in speech synthesis and analysis to improve intelligibility, naturalness, or to create musical effects. It falls under speech signal processing within the physics and musical-instrument hierarchy.
[G10L 21/02] Speech enhancement, e.g. noise reduction or echo cancellation -- G10L 21/02 covers techniques for improving speech or voice signals, such as reducing background noise, cancelling echoes, or otherwise enhancing intelligibility. The methods may involve digital or analog processing to modify the acoustic characteristics of the signal for clearer transmission or reception.
[G10L 21/0208] Noise filtering -- G10L 21/0208 covers techniques for filtering noise from speech or voice signals to improve their quality or intelligibility, including methods that generate audible, visual or tactile output after processing.
[G10L 21/0216] characterised by the method used for estimating noise -- The CPC subclass G10L 21/0216 covers speech- or voice-signal processing techniques that are distinguished by the specific method used to estimate background noise, a step often employed to enhance intelligibility or audio quality. It falls under the hierarchy PHYSICS → MUSICAL INSTRUMENTS → SPEECH ANALYSIS TECHNIQUES OR SPEECH SYNTHESIS.
[G10L 21/0224] Processing in the time domain -- G10L 21/0224 covers methods that process speech or voice signals directly in the time domain, such as filtering, amplitude modification, or temporal scaling, to alter quality or intelligibility. It is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification under physics, musical instruments, speech analysis techniques or speech synthesis.
[G10L 21/0232] Processing in the frequency domain -- G10L 21/0232 covers techniques that analyse or modify speech or voice signals after they have been converted to the frequency domain, such as using Fourier transforms, spectral filtering, or frequency-domain synthesis. Methods may enhance intelligibility, change timbre, or generate alternative outputs like visual or tactile cues, all by manipulating the signal's spectral components rather than its raw time-domain waveform.
[G10L 21/0264] characterised by the type of parameter measurement, e.g. correlation techniques, zero crossing techniques or predictive techniques -- G10L 21/0264 covers speech or voice signal processing methods that are distinguished by the type of parameter measurement used, such as correlation, zero crossing or predictive techniques, to modify quality or intelligibility of the signal. It is located in the CPC hierarchy under physics, musical instruments and speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 21/0272] Voice signal separating -- G10L 21/0272 pertains to techniques for separating voice signals from other audio components, enhancing clarity and distinctness in speech processing applications. This classification is part of the broader category dealing with modifying the quality or intelligibility of speech signals through various processing methods.
[G10L 21/028] using properties of sound source -- The G10L 21/028 subclass concerns methods that exploit the inherent properties of the sound source-such as its frequency, amplitude, or timing-in speech or voice signal processing. These techniques are used to improve the signal's quality or intelligibility, or to transform it into other audible, visual, or tactile outputs.
[G10L 21/0308] characterised by the type of parameter measurement, e.g. correlation techniques, zero crossing techniques or predictive techniques -- G10L 21/0308 pertains to speech signal processing methods that alter the quality or intelligibility of a voice signal to produce a different audible or non-audible output, focusing on specific parameter measurement techniques such as correlation, zero crossing, or predictive methods.
[G10L 21/0316] by changing the amplitude -- This subclass covers speech or voice signal processing methods that modify a signal by adjusting its amplitude, such as volume scaling, dynamic-range compression, or amplitude modulation, to alter the quality or intelligibility of the output.
[G10L 21/0324] Details of processing therefor -- G10L 21/0324 covers the specific processing steps applied to speech or voice signals to generate alternative audible, visual or tactile outputs, aiming to change the signal's quality or intelligibility. It includes detailed algorithms, parameter settings and signal-modification techniques used in speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 21/0332] involving modification of waveforms -- G10L 21/0332 pertains to speech or voice signal-processing methods that modify the waveform to alter its quality, intelligibility or to generate alternative outputs such as visual or tactile signals. It is situated within the hierarchy Physics → Musical instruments → Speech analysis techniques or speech synthesis.
[G10L 21/034] Automatic adjustment -- The G10L 21/034 subclass deals with automatic methods that adjust speech or voice signals to modify their quality, intelligibility, or to convert them into other audible or non-audible forms, using techniques such as adaptive filtering, dynamic range control and real-time parameter tuning.
[G10L 21/0356] for synchronising with other signals, e.g. video signals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Visemes are selected to match with the corresponding speech segment, or the speech segments are adapted/chosen, to match with the viseme. This symbol also encompasses the coarticulation effects as used in facial character animation or talking heads.
[G10L 21/0364] for improving intelligibility -- Techniques in speech or voice signal-processing aimed at enhancing intelligibility of the spoken message, such as modifying acoustic properties, applying noise reduction, dynamic range compression, or other transformations that make the speech clearer for listeners or downstream systems.
[G10L 21/038] using band spreading techniques -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bandwidth extension taking place at the receiving side, e.g. generation of artificial low or high frequency components, regeneration of spectral holes, based on the only narrowband encoded signal. This is in opposition with G10L19/24 wherein parameters are computed during the encoding step to enable bandwidth extension at the decoding step.
[G10L 21/0388] Details of processing therefor -- G10L 21/0388 covers methods for processing speech or voice signals to generate alternative outputs such as audible, visual or tactile signals, aiming to alter the signal's quality or intelligibility.
