It falls under the CPC hierarchy of physics, musical instruments and speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 21/04] Time compression or expansion -- G10L 21/04 covers methods that compress or expand the time scale of speech or voice signals, altering the playback speed while preserving intelligibility or other qualities. These techniques are used to speed up or slow down audio for applications such as language learning, broadcasting, or accessibility.
[G10L 21/043] by changing speed -- This subclass covers methods that alter a speech or voice signal by changing its playback or speaking speed to affect intelligibility or perceived quality. Techniques include time-scale modification without pitch shift or with pitch correction, used in hearing aids, language learning, and audio effects.
[G10L 21/045] using thinning out or insertion of a waveform -- G10L 21/045 covers methods for modifying speech or voice signals by thinning out or inserting portions of the waveform. The techniques may be applied to improve intelligibility, change quality, or generate alternative outputs such as visual or tactile cues.
[G10L 21/047] characterised by the type of waveform to be thinned out or inserted -- G10L 21/047 covers speech-or voice-signal processing methods that modify quality or intelligibility by selectively thinning out or inserting waveforms of a specified type. It is classified under physics → musical instruments → speech analysis techniques or speech synthesis, focusing on techniques that produce alternative audible or non-audible signals such as visual or tactile outputs.
[G10L 21/049] characterised by the interconnection of waveforms -- The class covers methods that link or combine multiple speech-derived waveforms to generate new audible, visual or tactile signals, often to improve intelligibility, quality or to achieve synthesis. It includes techniques for interconnecting waveforms for modulation, transformation or hybrid signal generation.
[G10L 21/055] for synchronising with other signals, e.g. video signals -- G10L 21/055 covers speech and voice signal processing methods that synchronize the audio with other signals such as video to achieve timing alignment or coordinated presentation. The subclass is situated in the CPC hierarchy under physics, musical instruments, and speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 21/057] for improving intelligibility -- G10L 21/057 covers methods and devices for processing speech or voice signals to improve intelligibility. It includes techniques that transform the signal into audible, visual or tactile forms, altering its quality so the content is clearer and more easily understood.
[G10L 21/06] Transformation of speech into a non-audible representation, e.g. speech visualisation or speech processing for tactile aids -- G10L 21/06 covers methods that convert speech signals into non-audible forms such as visual displays or tactile feedback, enabling users to perceive speech through sight or touch. This includes speech visualization and processing for tactile aids to assist hearing-impaired individuals.
[G10L 21/10] Transforming into visible information -- G10L 21/10 pertains to methods and systems for converting speech or voice signals into visual information, aiming to enhance the understanding or quality of the original audio content. This includes techniques that transform auditory signals into graphical or textual representations.
[G10L 21/12] by displaying time domain information -- G10L 21/12 relates to speech or voice signal processing methods that present time-domain information-such as waveform displays-to users, enabling visual or tactile feedback that can be used to alter the signal's quality, intelligibility, or to create alternative audible or non-audible outputs. This classification falls under physics, musical instruments, and speech analysis or synthesis techniques.
[G10L 21/14] by displaying frequency domain information -- G10L 21/14 concerns techniques that present speech or voice signal information in the frequency domain, such as spectrograms or other visual displays, to assist in altering the signal's quality or intelligibility. By visualising spectral data, these methods support analysis, synthesis, or feedback processes in speech processing systems.
[G10L 21/16] Transforming into a non-visible representation -- G10L 21/16 pertains to methods and systems for converting speech or voice signals into a non-visible form, such as tactile or electrical signals, aimed at enhancing the quality or intelligibility of the original signal. This transformation is crucial for applications in assistive technologies and advanced communication systems.
[G10L 21/18] Details of the transformation process -- G10L 21/18 covers the detailed steps involved in transforming a speech or voice signal into another audible or non-audible output, such as visual or tactile representations, with the aim of altering its quality or intelligibility. It pertains to speech-signal processing techniques that modify how the original sound is perceived or perceived through alternative senses.
[G10L 25/00] Speech or voice analysis techniques not restricted to a single one of groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Muting semiconductor-based amplifiers when some special characteristics of a signal are sensed by a speech detector, e.g. sensing when no signal is present -> H03G3/34  **Glossary:** - audio signal: of or relating to humanly audible sound, e.g. it comprises any combination of background noise or silence, voice or speech, music
[G10L 25/03] characterised by the type of extracted parameters -- This subclass groups speech or voice analysis methods that are distinguished by the type of parameters extracted from the signal, such as spectral, temporal, cepstral or prosodic features, rather than by the specific processing technique used.
[G10L 25/06] the extracted parameters being correlation coefficients -- The entry covers techniques that derive correlation-coefficient parameters from speech or voice signals, using these statistical measures to characterize relationships between acoustic features for analysis, synthesis or other processing tasks. It applies to any speech-analysis method that employs such extracted coefficients rather than being limited to a single specific group.
[G10L 25/09] the extracted parameters being zero crossing rates -- It covers speech and voice analysis techniques that employ the zero-crossing rate-counting how often a signal changes sign-as an extracted parameter for tasks such as voice activity detection, speaker identification or speech quality assessment.
[G10L 25/12] the extracted parameters being prediction coefficients -- This subclass of G10L 25 concerns speech analysis techniques where the extracted parameters are prediction coefficients used to model the short-term spectral envelope of speech. It covers methods that compute linear predictive coding coefficients for feature extraction in speech synthesis or recognition.
[G10L 25/15] the extracted parameters being formant information -- The G10L 25/15 subclass deals with methods that extract formant parameters from speech or voice signals for analysis or synthesis. It covers techniques that measure resonant frequency characteristics without being limited to a single specific approach, supporting broader speech-processing applications.
[G10L 25/18] the extracted parameters being spectral information of each sub-band -- G10L 25/18 covers methods that extract spectral information from each sub-band of a speech signal, providing parameters such as band energies or frequency components for analysis, synthesis or recognition. It belongs to speech or voice analysis techniques not restricted to a single group.
[G10L 25/21] the extracted parameters being power information -- This subclass covers methods and devices that extract power-related parameters such as signal energy, average power or loudness from speech or voice signals, typically for evaluation, control or synthesis purposes. It applies to speech analysis techniques that are not confined to a single group.
[G10L 25/24] the extracted parameters being the cepstrum -- G10L 25/24 covers methods that extract cepstral parameters from speech or voice signals, using the cepstrum to represent spectral envelope information for analysis, identification, or synthesis. It is employed in speech processing systems where the cepstrum serves as a compact feature set for characterising spoken sounds.
[G10L 25/27] characterised by the analysis technique -- G10L 25/27 covers speech-or-voice analysis methods that are defined by the particular analysis technique employed, such as spectral, statistical or model-based approaches, without being limited to a single technique group. It groups together any voice-processing methods whose primary distinguishing feature is the way the signal is examined, rather than the specific application or acoustic parameter.
[G10L 25/30] using neural networks -- G10L 25/30 relates to applying neural-network methods in speech and voice analysis, including recognition, synthesis and processing of acoustic signals. It covers systems that use artificial neural networks to extract, model or generate speech characteristics, fitting within the broader categories of physics, musical instruments and speech analysis techniques.
[G10L 25/33] using fuzzy logic -- G10L 25/33 refers to methods that employ fuzzy-logic techniques for speech or voice analysis, encompassing any approach that is not limited to a single specific group of techniques. It falls under the hierarchy of physics, musical instruments, and speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 25/36] using chaos theory -- G10L 25/36 pertains to speech or voice analysis techniques that utilize chaos theory, falling under the broader category of methods not confined to a single group within the classification. This code is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European and United States patent offices for categorizing inventions related to physics, specifically musical instruments and speech technologies.
[G10L 25/39] using genetic algorithms -- G10L 25/39 covers the use of genetic algorithms in speech and voice analysis, including optimisation of feature extraction, model training and parameter tuning. It applies to any speech-processing method where evolutionary computation is employed, without limiting to a specific analysis group.
[G10L 25/45] characterised by the type of analysis window -- The subclass G10L 25/45 includes speech-or-voice analysis methods that are differentiated by the type of analysis window applied to the signal, such as its shape, length, overlap or position. It falls under speech analysis techniques within the physics-musical-instruments branch of the CPC.
[G10L 25/48] specially adapted for particular use -- G10L 25/48 covers speech or voice analysis methods that are specially adapted for a particular application, encompassing customized algorithms, devices, or procedures that are not confined to a single technique group. It includes tailored solutions for uses such as instrument interfacing, security monitoring, medical diagnostics, or other specific needs.
[G10L 25/51] for comparison or discrimination -- G10L 25/51 covers techniques that compare speech signals or discriminate among speakers, utterances, or phonetic elements, using features such as spectral, temporal, or statistical characteristics for purposes like speaker identification, verification, language or accent recognition, and word-or-phrase discrimination.
[G10L 25/54] for retrieval -- This CPC subgroup covers methods and systems for retrieving speech or voice data from stored collections, including searching, indexing and extracting spoken segments based on linguistic or acoustic criteria. It applies to any speech analysis technique not limited to a single specific method.
[G10L 25/57] for processing of video signals -- G10L 25/57 covers techniques for processing video signals that support speech or voice analysis, where the video data is used alongside audio to improve recognition, synthesis, or other analysis functions. The subclass falls under physics, musical instruments, and speech analysis techniques not limited to a single group. It includes hardware and software methods for integrating video streams with speech processing.
[G10L 25/60] for measuring the quality of voice signals -- Code G10L 25/60 covers techniques for evaluating the quality of voice signals, such as assessing clarity, intelligibility, or distortion, without being limited to a specific measurement method. It falls under the cooperative patent classification hierarchy of physics, musical instruments, and speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 25/63] for estimating an emotional state -- G10L 25/63 covers methods and systems that analyse speech or voice signals to estimate a speaker's emotional state, using various signal-processing or pattern-recognition techniques. The subclass includes algorithms that infer emotions from vocal cues without being limited to a single analysis group.
[G10L 25/66] for extracting parameters related to health condition -- G10L 25/66 covers speech-or voice-analysis techniques that extract parameters indicative of a speaker's health condition, such as markers for disease, stress, fatigue or other physiological states. It falls within the CPC hierarchy under physics, musical instruments and speech analysis or synthesis.
[G10L 25/69] for evaluating synthetic or decoded voice signals -- G10L 25/69 pertains to techniques for assessing the quality and characteristics of synthetic or decoded speech signals, such as objective and subjective measures used in speech synthesis and speech codec evaluation. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of physics, musical instruments, speech analysis techniques or speech synthesis, and addresses evaluation methods not limited to a single specific group.
[G10L 25/72] for transmitting results of analysis -- G10L 25/72 covers methods and systems for transmitting the results of speech or voice analysis, including sending processed data to display devices, storage media or remote users. It applies to any analysis technique not limited to a specific subgroup, facilitating communication of diagnostic or performance information derived from acoustic signals.
[G10L 25/75] for modelling vocal tract parameters -- G10L 25/75 includes methods and systems that model the physical characteristics of the vocal tract such as shape, length, cross-sectional area or impedance to predict or reproduce speech sounds. The classification covers computational or physical models used in speech analysis, synthesis or recognition without being limited to a particular modelling approach.
[G10L 25/78] Detection of presence or absence of voice signals -- G10L 25/78 covers methods and devices for detecting whether a voice signal is present or absent in a signal stream. It falls under speech or voice analysis techniques that are not limited to a single group and is applied in voice-activity detection for communications, audio recording, and related processing.
[G10L 25/81] for discriminating voice from music -- G10L 25/81 covers techniques for automatically separating or distinguishing spoken voice components from musical accompaniment in audio signals, enabling voice detection, removal or enhancement in mixed recordings. It includes algorithms, signal-processing methods and systems that identify phonetic content amidst music, supporting applications such as transcription, speaker tracking and audio indexing.
[G10L 25/84] for discriminating voice from noise -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for separating a speech signal from background noise, including voice activity detection, noise suppression, and techniques that improve the signal-to-noise ratio.
[G10L 25/87] Detection of discrete points within a voice signal -- G10L 25/87 relates to methods that locate individual points-such as pitch marks, zero-crossings or formant peaks-within a speech waveform. These detection techniques support analysis, coding and synthesis of voice by providing precise temporal or spectral markers.
[G10L 25/90] Pitch determination of speech signals -- The CPC subclass G10L 25/90 covers methods and devices for determining the pitch of speech signals, including algorithms that estimate the fundamental frequency of voiced speech. It belongs to the speech analysis techniques group and is used in applications such as speaker identification, speech synthesis, and language teaching.
[G10L 25/93] Discriminating between voiced and unvoiced parts of speech signals -- G10L 25/93 covers methods for distinguishing voiced from unvoiced portions of a speech signal, a fundamental step in speech and voice processing. These techniques analyse acoustic features such as periodicity, energy and spectral content to separate sounds produced with vocal-fold vibration from those without, supporting applications in speech recognition, synthesis and linguistic research.
[G10L 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- G10L 99/00 covers speech analysis, synthesis or related processing inventions that do not fall within any of the defined groups of the G10L subclass, such as unconventional algorithms, unique hardware configurations, or novel applications of speech technology.
[G11] INFORMATION STORAGE -- G11 deals with technologies for storing and retrieving information, covering recording media, storage devices, and related methods. It includes magnetic, optical, semiconductor and other physical forms used for data preservation and access.
[G11B] INFORMATION STORAGE BASED ON RELATIVE MOVEMENT BETWEEN RECORD CARRIER AND TRANSDUCER -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recording or playback of information by relative movement between a record track and a transducer, the transducer directly producing, or being directly actuated by, modulation in the track being recorded or played-back, and the extent of modulation corresponding to the signal being recorded or played-back;  Apparatus and machines for recording or playback, and parts thereof, such as heads;  Record carriers for use with such apparatus and machines;  Associated working of other apparatus with such apparatus and machines.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Recording measured values in a way that does not require playback through a transducer -> G01D9/00 - Recording or playback apparatus using mechanically marked tape, e.g. punched paper tape, or using unit records, e.g. punched or magnetically marked cards -> G06K - Transferring data from one type of record carrier to another type of record carrier -> G06K1/18 - Circuits for coupling output of reproducer to radio receiver -> H04B1/20 - Loudspeakers, microphones, gramophone pick-ups or like acoustic electromechanical transducers or circuits therefor -> H04R  **Glossary:** - Record carrier: means a body, such as a cylinder, disc, card, tape, or wire, capable of permanently holding information, which can be read-off by a sensing element movable relatively to the record carrier - Head: includes any means for converting sinusoidal or non-sinusoidal electric wave-forms into variations of the physical condition of at least the adjacent surface of the record carrier, or vice versa - Near-field interaction: means a very short distance interaction using scanning-probe techniques, e.g. quasi- contact or evanescent contact between head and record carrier
[G11B 11/00] Recording on or reproducing from the same record carrier wherein for these two operations the methods are covered by different main groups of groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Only the cases wherein the method of recording differs from the method of reproducing. The following recording methods (when associated to a different reproducing method) are covered:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Reading only or recording only using mechanical, magnetic, optical or other methods is covered respectively by groups -> G11B3/00 , G11B5/00 , G11B7/00 , G11B9/00 - Driving or moving of heads -> G11B3/02 , G11B5/48 , G11B7/08 , G11B21/02  **Glossary:** - Near-field interaction: Means a very short distance interaction using scanning-probe techniques, e.g. quasi- contact or evanescent contact between head and record carrier
[G11B 11/002] using recording by perturbation of the physical or electrical structure -- This subclass covers recording data on a medium by altering its physical or electrical structure rather than by adding separate marks, including methods such as magnetization, charge storage or phase-change modifications, where the same carrier is used for both recording and playback and the recording technique belongs to a different main group from the reproducing technique.
[G11B 11/005] with reproducing by using non-optical beam of radiation or particles, e.g. electrons, directly interacting with the memorised information  G11B11/007  takes precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code G11B 11/005 pertains to methods of recording on and reproducing from a record carrier using non-optical beams of radiation or particles, such as electrons, that directly interact with the stored information. This classification is specific to techniques where the recording and reproducing processes are distinct and covered by different main groups.
[G11B 11/007] with reproducing by means directly associated with the tip of a microscopic electrical probe as defined in  G11B9/14   details of heads  G11B9/1409 ; disposition or mounting of heads  G11B9/1418 -- This subclass concerns recording on or reproducing from the same record carrier when the two operations fall under different main groups, specifically reproducing by a microscopic-electrical probe tip as defined in G11B9/14, with reference to head details (G11B9/1409) and head disposition or mounting (G11B9/1418).
[G11B 11/03] using recording by deforming with non-mechanical means, e.g. laser, beam of particles -- G11B 11/03 covers information storage where recording and reproducing occur on the same carrier, but the recording step uses non-mechanical deformation methods such as lasers, particle beams, or other energy-based means to alter the medium.
[G11B 11/05] with reproducing by capacitive means -- The code covers information-storage methods in which the same carrier is both recorded on and later read, with the reading performed by detecting changes in capacitance caused by the recorded pattern. It applies to devices that use a capacitive transducer to reproduce data stored on a moving or static medium.
[G11B 11/06] with reproducing by mechanical sensing -- The CPC subgroup G11B 11/06 covers devices that record information on a single carrier while using a different main group for recording and reproducing, specifically where the playback is performed by mechanical sensing of the recorded pattern. This falls under physics → information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 11/08] using recording by electric charge or by variation of electric resistance or capacitance -- G11B 11/08 includes methods that store data on a single record carrier by altering its electric charge, resistance or capacitance, such as electrostatic, resistive or capacitive recording techniques. The same carrier can later be reproduced by different processes, which are classified elsewhere.
[G11B 11/10] using recording by magnetic means -- G11B 11/10 covers technologies that record on a magnetic medium and reproduce from the same carrier while the recording and playback methods belong to different primary groups. It deals with magnetic storage where information is written and read through relative motion between the record carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 11/105] using a beam of light or a magnetic field for recording and a beam of light for reproducing, e.g. light-induced thermomagnetic recording, Kerr effect reproducing -- G11B 11/105 covers methods that record information on a medium using a beam of light or a magnetic field and retrieve the data with a light beam, such as light-induced thermomagnetic recording or Kerr-effect playback.
[G11B 11/10502] characterised by the transducing operation to be executed -- This subclass concerns storage technologies that use relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer, where recording and reproducing occur on the same medium but are treated in separate main groups. The defining feature is the particular transducing operation-such as reading, writing, or erasing-that is executed, distinguishing the device or method by the nature of that operation.
[G11B 11/10504] Recording  for shaping of magnetic domains  G11B11/10528 , for compensation of shift  G11B11/1053 -- The G11B 11/10504 subgroup covers recording on a single magnetic medium where the processes for recording and reproducing are assigned to different main groups, specifically methods that shape magnetic domains (G11B 11/10528) and techniques that compensate for positional shift (G11B 11/1053). This classification applies to information storage systems that rely on the relative movement between the record carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 11/10506] by modulating only the light beam of the transducer -- This subclass covers optical recording or playback on a single medium in which the only control is applied to the light beam of the transducer, such as by varying its intensity, phase, polarization, or wavelength, while the beam itself is not moved relative to the carrier. The approach applies to methods where recording and reproducing are treated in separate main groups.
[G11B 11/10508] by modulating only the magnetic field at the transducer -- G11B 11/10508 relates to magnetic recording or reproducing on a single record carrier where the data is encoded solely by varying the magnetic field at the transducer, without any mechanical movement of the carrier or head. The subclass captures methods that use field modulation as the exclusive means of information storage or retrieval.
[G11B 11/1051] by modulating both the magnetic field and the light beam at the transducers -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 11/1051 pertains to methods for recording on or reproducing from a record carrier using techniques that involve modulating both the magnetic field and the light beam at the transducers, within the broader context of information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 11/10513] one of the light beam or the magnetic field being modulated by data and the other by a clock or frequency generator -- This CPC subclass covers recording or reproducing on a single medium where the data signal modulates either a light beam or a magnetic field while a separate clock or frequency generator modulates the other. It pertains to systems that use this dual-modulation arrangement to synchronize the data and timing functions during the same carrier's operation.
[G11B 11/10515] Reproducing  compensating pit shift  G11B11/1053 -- This subclass covers methods for reproducing data from a record carrier where the system compensates for a shift of the physical pits. It includes techniques that detect pit displacement caused by mechanical or thermal effects and adjust the read timing or positioning to restore correct data alignment during playback.
[G11B 11/10517] Overwriting or erasing  G11B11/10526  takes precedence -- G11B 11/10517 pertains to methods for overwriting or erasing data on a single record carrier when the recording and reproducing operations are classified under different main groups, with the procedure defined in G11B 11/10526 taking precedence.
[G11B 11/10519] Direct overwriting, i.e. performing erasing and recording using the same transducing means -- This CPC subclass G11B 11/10519 concerns methods in which the same transducing element both erases and records data on a single record carrier, allowing direct overwriting without separate erase and write mechanisms.
[G11B 11/10521] using a single light spot -- This subgroup deals with techniques that employ a solitary light beam as the transducer for both writing and reading on the same medium, where the write and read processes belong to different main groups. It includes devices such as optical disc systems that use a single laser spot for recording and playback.
[G11B 11/10523] Initialising -- G11B 11/10523 covers techniques for initializing a recording or playback system that uses a single record carrier, such as setting the initial position, calibrating the transducer, or preparing the medium before the first write or read operation.
[G11B 11/10526] Bulk initialisation or erasing, e.g. at least one whole information track with a single action -- The G11B 11/10526 subclass pertains to methods for bulk initialization or erasing of stored data, where an entire information track or comparable region of a record carrier is cleared or set to a defined state in a single operation. It is used for techniques that treat the whole track as one unit rather than addressing individual bits or sectors.
[G11B 11/10528] Shaping of magnetic domains, e.g. form, dimensions -- This subclass covers methods for shaping magnetic domains - adjusting their form, size or arrangement - on a record carrier used for both recording and playback. It belongs to the group dealing with information storage where recording and reproducing share the same medium, classified under G11B 11/10528.
[G11B 11/1053] to compensate for the magnetic domain drift or time shift -- The G11B 11/1053 subclass covers techniques for correcting magnetic domain drift or time-shift errors that arise during recording on, or playback from, the same magnetic medium. It includes methods that adjust timing, bias or signal processing to keep the recorded data accurately aligned despite variations in the medium's magnetic characteristics.
[G11B 11/10532] Heads -- Heads are the transducer components that make contact with a moving record carrier to write or read data, where recording and reproducing functions are treated in separate main groups. The code covers designs and arrangements of such heads in devices that store information through relative motion between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 11/10534] for recording by magnetising, demagnetising or transfer of magnetisation, by radiation, e.g. for thermomagnetic recording -- G11B 11/10534 covers methods of recording information on a magnetic medium by using radiation to magnetise, demagnetise or transfer magnetisation, such as thermomagnetic recording where heat from a light source induces magnetic changes.
[G11B 11/10536] using thermic beams, e.g. lasers -- This CPC subclass deals with recording on or reproducing from a single record carrier where the two operations fall under different main groups, and the methods use thermal beams such as lasers.
[G11B 11/10539] using electromagnetic beams, e.g. polarised light -- This subgroup covers methods that use electromagnetic beams, such as polarized light, to write or read information on a record carrier that moves relative to the transducer, with the same carrier being employed for both recording and reproducing, distinguished from other approaches that use different main groups.
[G11B 11/10541] for reproducing -- The G11B 11/10541 subclass covers methods for reproducing data from a record carrier when the recording operation is classified in a different main group, focusing on playback techniques that read information stored on the same medium through relative movement between the carrier and the transducer. This falls under information storage based on relative motion within the physics field.
[G11B 11/10543] using optical beam of radiation -- The G11B 11/10543 sub-group covers techniques that employ an optical beam of radiation to write or read data on a single record carrier that moves relative to the transducer, with the recording and reproducing functions defined in separate main groups.
[G11B 11/10545] interacting directly with the magnetisation on the record carrier -- This subclass covers methods that access the magnetic state of a recording medium by direct interaction with its magnetisation, such as magnetic heads or probes that read or write information without intermediate conversion. It applies when recording and reproducing are performed on the same carrier but are classified under different main groups.
[G11B 11/10547] interacting with the magnetisation of an intermediate transfer element, e.g. magnetic film, included in the head -- The CPC subclass G11B 11/10547 covers methods for interacting with the magnetisation of an intermediate transfer element, such as a magnetic film, that is incorporated into a recording or reproducing head. It pertains to devices where this magnetic film serves as a transfer medium between the record carrier and the transducer, enabling controlled magnetisation during recording or playback.
[G11B 11/1055] Disposition or mounting of transducers relative to record carriers -- G11B 11/1055 covers the way transducers are positioned or mounted in relation to a record carrier when both recording and playback occur on the same medium but are performed by different method groups. It falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 11/10552] Arrangements of transducers relative to each other, e.g. coupled heads, optical and magnetic head on the same base  for relative movement of transducers  G11B11/10573 -- G11B 11/10552 covers configurations where two or more transducers are positioned together so that they move relative to one another, such as coupled heads or an optical and a magnetic head mounted on the same carriage, enabling recording and playback on the same medium. The classification applies to arrangements that facilitate coordinated relative motion of the transducers.
[G11B 11/10554] the transducers being disposed on the same side of the carrier  flying heads  G11B11/1058 -- This CPC subclass covers recording or reproducing from a single record carrier when the transducers are positioned on the same side of the medium, such as flying heads in hard-disk or similar devices, addressing the arrangement of read/write elements on that side.
[G11B 11/10556] with provision for moving or switching or masking the transducers in or out of their operative position -- This subclass concerns information storage systems in which the transducer can be moved, switched or masked away from its active position during recording or playback on the same media. It addresses mechanisms that allow the transducer to be withdrawn or isolated while the record carrier remains stationary.
[G11B 11/10558] in view of the loading or unloading of the carrier -- This CPC subclass deals with methods where loading or unloading of a record carrier affects recording or playback on the same medium, with the two operations placed in different main groups. It is nested under physics - information storage - storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer and specifically addresses handling of the carrier during the process.
[G11B 11/1056] Switching or mechanically reversing the magnetic field generator -- The code G11B 11/1056 covers devices that change the direction of the magnetic field in a magnetic recording head by electrical switching or by moving parts that mechanically reverse the field generator. It applies to systems where recording and playback use the same medium but are implemented with different main groups of methods.
[G11B 11/10563] Access of indexed parts -- This subclass covers devices or methods that retrieve particular indexed segments of data stored on a single record carrier used for both recording and playback, where the indexing enables selective access. It applies to storage technologies that involve relative movement between the carrier and transducer, such as magnetic or optical disks, and where recording and reproducing are classified under different main groups.
[G11B 11/10565] Marks for track change, e.g. prepits, gray codes -- Marks applied to a recording medium to indicate track positions or changes, such as pre-pits or gray-code patterns used for servo control and navigation, are covered by this subclass. The entry pertains to methods that both record and reproduce from the same carrier while employing distinct main groups for the two operations.
[G11B 11/10567] Mechanically moving the transducers -- The G11B 11/10567 entry concerns techniques that physically shift the transducer relative to the recording medium in order to write or read data, such as moving read/write heads in magnetic or optical storage devices where the same carrier is used for both operations. This mechanical displacement of the transducer enables precise positioning for recording and playback.
[G11B 11/10569] Swing arm positioners -- The CPC entry G11B 11/10569 covers swing-arm positioners used to adjust the tone-arm in devices that record to or reproduce from the same disc or tape, where the positioning methods for recording and playback fall under different main groups. It belongs to the physics-based information-storage subclass that deals with relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer.
[G11B 11/10571] Sled type positioners -- The G11B 11/10571 entry covers sled-type positioners used in information storage devices where the record carrier and the transducer move relative to each other. It pertains to mechanisms that shift the transducer assembly linearly along the media for recording or reproducing on the same record carrier, with separate methods for each operation.
[G11B 11/10573] Control of relative positioning of the magnetic and optical transducers, e.g. to move simultaneously -- This CPC subgroup covers techniques for controlling the relative positioning of magnetic and optical transducers, such as moving them simultaneously during recording or playback on the same record carrier. It relates to devices or methods that coordinate the motion of both transducer types to achieve precise alignment.
[G11B 11/10576] with provision for moving the transducers for maintaining alignment or spacing relative to the carrier -- It covers devices or methods where the read/write transducer can be adjusted - moved or steered - to keep proper alignment or spacing with a moving record carrier during recording or playback, ensuring consistent contact or proximity. Typically applies to magnetic tape, optical disks, etc.
[G11B 11/10578] Servo format, e.g. prepits, guide tracks, pilot signals -- The subclass covers methods of adding servo information-such as pre-pits, guide tracks or pilot signals-directly onto a storage medium so that the same carrier can be used for both recording and reproducing while the head follows the encoded positioning cues.
[G11B 11/1058] Flying heads -- Flying heads refer to transducers that are suspended above a record carrier and move relative to it during recording or playback, allowing contactless operation on magnetic or optical media. The technology is used when the same medium is employed for both writing and reading but the processes are classified in different CPC groups.
[G11B 11/10582] Record carriers characterised by the selection of the material or by the structure or form -- The code covers physical record carriers whose material composition, structural design or shape defines their data-storage function, typically used when the carrier moves relative to a transducer for both recording and playback. It applies to media such as magnetic tapes, optical discs or other forms whose characteristics are selected to achieve the required performance.
[G11B 11/10584] characterised by the form, e.g. comprising mechanical protection elements -- This CPC subgroup covers information storage devices in which the record carrier and transducer move relative to each other and the recording and reproducing functions are assigned to different main groups. It specifically pertains to designs characterised by the form of mechanical protection elements, such as shields, housings or other structural features that safeguard the carrier.
[G11B 11/10586] characterised by the selection of the material -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 11/10586 pertains to information storage, specifically addressing the material selection for recording and reproducing processes that involve relative movement between a record carrier and transducer. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with physics and information storage technologies.
[G11B 11/10589] Details -- G11B 11/10589 concerns storage devices where the same medium is used for both recording and playback, but the recording technique is classified in a different main group from the reproduction technique. It covers arrangements such as magnetic, optical or other media where distinct transducer mechanisms or processes are employed for writing versus reading, reflecting the relative motion between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 11/10591] for improving write-in properties, e.g. Curie-point temperature -- G11B 11/10591 addresses methods and materials that enhance the write-in properties of a record carrier, such as controlling the Curie-point temperature of magnetic media to improve recording performance.
[G11B 11/10593] for improving read-out properties, e.g. polarisation of light -- The CPC subclass G11B 11/10593 falls under physics, specifically information storage based on relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer. It concerns methods where recording and reproducing are performed on the same carrier but are assigned to different main groups, and it addresses techniques that improve read-out properties such as polarisation of light.
[G11B 11/10595] Control of operating function -- G11B 11/10595 covers devices or methods that control the operating functions of a recording/reproducing system where recording and playback are performed on the same physical medium but the control mechanisms belong to different main groups. It addresses how the system initiates, adjusts, or terminates the recording or playback actions, ensuring proper coordination between the carrier and transducer.
[G11B 11/10597] Adaptations for transducing various formats on the same or different carriers -- The entry covers devices or methods that enable a single transducer to handle multiple recording or playback formats on the same or on different media, such as interchangeable heads, configurable circuitry or software that adapts to various signal types, allowing one carrier to be used for several format standards.
[G11B 11/11] using a beam, other than a beam of light for recording -- G11B 11/11 covers recording methods that employ a beam other than light-such as electron, ion or other particle beams-to write data on a record carrier, distinguishing the recording operation from reproducing methods that belong to separate main groups.
[G11B 11/115] other than a beam of light for reproducing -- The code G11B 11/115 covers techniques for reproducing information from a record carrier using methods other than a beam of light, where recording and reproducing are performed on the same medium but fall under different main groups. It applies to information-storage systems that rely on relative movement between the carrier and a transducer.
[G11B 11/12] using recording by optical means -- This subclass covers techniques in which information is inscribed on a record carrier by means of light, such as laser, LED or other optical energy sources, including optical writing to magnetic, phase-change or other media. It is part of the physics field of information storage where the carrier and transducer move relative to each other, distinguishing recording from reproducing operations.
[G11B 11/14] with reproducing by magnetic means -- G11B 11/14 covers devices that record on and reproduce from the same storage medium when the two operations are classified in separate main groups, specifically focusing on reproduction performed by magnetic means. This sub-group falls under information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer within the broader physics field.
[G11B 11/16] using recording by mechanical cutting, deforming or pressing -- G11B 11/16 covers techniques for recording information on a single medium by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing the surface, such as engraving, embossing or stamping, rather than by additive means. It applies when recording and reproducing from the same carrier are performed with methods belonging to different main groups.
[G11B 11/18] with reproducing by optical means -- G11B 11/18 covers technologies in which the same record carrier is used for both recording and playback, but the reproducing operation is performed by optical means such as laser or LED reading; it falls under information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer, exemplified by optical disc systems where data are written magnetically or chemically and retrieved optically.
[G11B 11/20] with reproducing by magnetic means -- This subclass concerns devices or methods that both record onto and reproduce from the same moving record carrier, where the playback operation is performed by magnetic means such as inductive heads or magnetic sensors. It falls under information storage technologies that rely on relative motion between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 11/22] with reproducing by capacitive means -- G11B 11/22 covers technologies that record on a single media but reproduce the stored information using capacitive pickup methods. It applies where the recording technique belongs to a different main group than the capacitive reproduction technique, such as magnetic recording combined with capacitive readout.
[G11B 11/24] using recording by near-field interactions -- G11B 11/24 covers methods that store data on a medium by exploiting near-field interactions between the transducer and the record carrier, such as inductive, capacitive, or magnetic coupling, allowing recording without direct contact or far-field radiation. This subclass applies when the same carrier is used for both recording and playback but the techniques belong to different main groups.
[G11B 11/26] using microscopic probe means -- The subclass G11B 11/26 covers methods of recording or reproducing data on a single medium where the operation is performed with a microscopic probe such as an atomic-force or scanning tunneling tip. It belongs to information storage based on relative motion between the carrier and transducer, and it distinguishes these operations from other main groups where recording and playback use separate techniques.
[G11B 13/00] Recording simultaneously or selectively by methods covered by different main groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group is limited to the combination of recording and reproducing on the same record carrier by more than one of the different method covered by groups G11B3/00 , G11B5/00 , G11B7/00 and G11B9/00  Recording simultaneously or selectively:  Using near-field interactions or transducing means and at least one other method or means for recording or reproducing  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Reading only or recording only using mechanical, magnetic, optical or other methods is covered respectively by groups -> G11B3/00 , G11B5/00 , G11B7/00 , G11B9/00 - Takes precedence -> G11B9/14 - Using recording by perturbation of the physical or electrical structure -> G11B11/002  **Glossary:** - Near-field interaction: Means a very short distance interaction using scanning-probe techniques, e.g. quasi- contact or evanescent contact between head and record carrier
[G11B 13/02] magnetically and by styli -- G11B 13/02 covers recording methods that use magnetic media and stylus contact to store data, either simultaneously or selectively, on a moving carrier such as a tape or disk. It falls under information storage based on the relative movement between the record carrier and the transducer, a sub-group of physics-related information-storage technologies.
[G11B 13/04] magnetically and optically -- G11B 13/04 pertains to data storage devices where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer and information is recorded or read using both magnetic and optical techniques, either at the same time or selectively, to exploit the advantages of each method.
[G11B 13/045] combined recording by magnetic and optic means -- G11B 13/045 covers technologies that record data on a medium using both magnetic and optical methods simultaneously or in a selectable mode. It applies to systems where a transducer and a light source act together to write information, often to increase capacity or reliability.
[G11B 13/06] optically and by styli -- G11B 13/06 covers technologies that record data on a moving medium by using both optical methods and stylus contact, either simultaneously or selectively, combining different main-group techniques within the information-storage field.
[G11B 13/08] using near-field interactions or transducing means and at least one other method or means for recording or reproducing -- G11B 13/08 covers storage or playback systems that combine a near-field interaction or a transducer with another recording or reproducing technique. It applies to devices where the data carrier moves relative to the read/write head and the near-field effect is used together with a different method.
[G11B 15/00] Driving, starting or stopping record carriers of filamentary or web form -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Recording/reproducing operations -> G11B5/00 , G11B7/00 , G11B9/00 , G11B11/00 - Magnetic heads -> G11B5/127 - Signal processing -> G11B20/00 - Record carriers, tape cartridges -> G11B23/00 - User interface aspects of drives -> G11B25/00 - Recording/reproducing apparatuses in combination with television sets -> G11B31/00 - Recording/reproducing apparatuses in combination with video cameras -> G11B31/006 , H04N23/00 - Vibration damping means -> G11B33/08
[G11B 15/005] Programmed access in sequence to indexed parts of tracks of operating tapes, by driving or guiding the tape  access by driving of both record carrier and head  G11B15/1816 ;  see  prov. also  G11B15/602 -- G11B 15/005 concerns programmed, sequential access to indexed sections of tape tracks by moving both the tape and the transducer head, allowing precise positioning of the record carrier and head for reading or writing.
[G11B 15/02] Control of operating function, e.g. switching from recording to reproducing -- The G11B 15/02 entry covers control mechanisms that manage the operating functions of filamentary or web-type record carriers, such as the switching, starting, stopping or mode change between recording and reproducing actions. It focuses on systems that direct these changes rather than the physical movement of the carrier itself.
[G11B 15/023] remotely controlled -- G11B 15/023 covers systems that drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers by means that can be controlled remotely. It includes remote-controlled motors, actuators or electronic circuits that manage the movement of paper, film or similar media in information-storage devices.
[G11B 15/026] by using processor, e.g. microcomputer -- This subclass deals with controlling the motion of filamentary or web record carriers in storage devices by using an electronic processor such as a microcomputer, enabling the starting, driving or stopping of the carrier.
[G11B 15/03] by using counters -- G11B 15/03 covers information-storage devices that control the motion of filamentary or web record carriers by using counters to monitor and regulate driving, starting or stopping of the carrier.
[G11B 15/04] Preventing, inhibiting, or warning against accidental erasing or double recording -- It pertains to devices or techniques that detect and stop accidental erasing or double recording of data on moving record carriers such as tapes, webs or filaments, providing warnings or inhibiting actions. The measures are applied in systems where the carrier motion relative to a transducer is used for information storage.
[G11B 15/05] by sensing features present on or derived from record carrier or container -- G11B 15/05 covers methods that sense features existing on or extracted from the record carrier or its container to control the motion of filamentary or web media. It includes detecting marks, patterns, or physical properties of the tape, paper, or drum to start, stop or adjust transport.
[G11B 15/06] by sensing auxiliary features on record carriers or containers, e.g. to stop machine near the end of a tape -- G11B 15/06 relates to detecting auxiliary features on moving record carriers or containers, such as markers that cause a machine to stop near the end of a tape. It is classified under physics, information storage, and mechanisms for driving, starting or stopping filamentary or web media.
[G11B 15/07] on containers -- The G11B 15/07 subclass covers devices and methods related to containers that hold or guide information carriers such as filamentary or web-type media, focusing on the mechanisms for driving, starting or stopping these carriers within the container. It addresses the design of housings, reels, spools and associated drive systems used in magnetic or optical storage formats.
[G11B 15/08] by photoelectric sensing -- G11B 15/08 covers the use of photoelectric sensors to drive, start or stop filamentary or web record carriers in information-storage systems. It includes techniques where light-based detection of carrier position controls the motion of the medium.
[G11B 15/087] by sensing recorded signals -- G11B 15/087 relates to systems that drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers by detecting the signals already recorded on the medium. The sensed playback signal is used to control the transport mechanism, ensuring proper spacing and speed during recording or playback.
[G11B 15/093] by sensing driving condition of record carrier, e.g. travel, tape tension -- G11B 15/093 covers devices that sense the driving condition of a filamentary or web record-carrier, such as its travel distance or tension, to control starting, stopping or speed of the carrier. The classification falls under information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 15/10] Manually-operated control -- G11B 15/10 covers manually-operated devices that start, stop or drive filamentary or web-form record carriers in information-storage systems, such as hand-cranked, lever- or pedal-actuated mechanisms that move tape, paper or similar media relative to a transducer.
[G11B 15/103] electrically operated -- G11B 15/103 designates electrically operated mechanisms that drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers in information-storage devices, where the carrier's movement relative to a transducer stores data. It is part of the physics field, under information storage based on relative motion between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 15/106] mechanically operated -- The G11B 15/106 subclass covers mechanically operated means for controlling the motion of filamentary or web-type record carriers, such as starting, stopping or driving the media in data storage devices. It pertains to physical information storage where the relative movement between the carrier and the transducer is achieved by mechanical actuation.
[G11B 15/12] Masking of heads; Selecting or switching of heads between operative and inoperative functions -- G11B 15/12 covers techniques for masking magnetic or other heads and for selecting or switching heads between active and inactive modes, used to control which head records or reads as the record carrier moves. It applies to systems where multiple heads are positioned over a filamentary or web record carrier and need to be engaged or disengaged during operation.
[G11B 15/125] conditioned by the operating function of the apparatus -- The G11B 15/125 class groups devices that drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers based on the operating function of the apparatus, such as the control logic that initiates motion in printers, tape drives or other data-recording machines. It falls under information storage by relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 15/14] Masking or switching periodically, e.g. of rotating heads -- The subclass covers periodic masking or switching techniques applied to moving transducers such as rotating magnetic heads, controlling when the head records, reads or is disconnected from the medium. It includes mechanisms that intermittently engage or disengage the head to manage data flow or protect the recording surface.
[G11B 15/16] by sensing presence, absence or position of record carrier or container -- G11B 15/16 covers mechanisms that drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers by detecting the carrier's presence, absence, or position, often using sensors that monitor the carrier or its container. This subclass falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 15/17] of container -- G11B 15/17 pertains to the driving, starting, or stopping mechanisms for information storage systems where the record carrier is in a filamentary or web form. This specific entry focuses on components or methods related to the container that houses such media.
[G11B 15/18] Driving -- G11B 15/18 covers devices that drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers used for information storage. It includes mechanisms such as rollers, capstans and motor arrangements that control the movement of magnetic tape, photographic film or similar media in recording or playback systems.
[G11B 15/1808] Driving of both record carrier and head  G11B15/467  takes precedence; mounting of head  G11B5/52 -- The G11B 15/1808 subclass covers systems that drive both the record-carrier (such as filamentary or web media) and the transducer head in a coordinated manner, typically for magnetic or optical recording. It supersedes the narrower G11B 15/467 subclass which deals only with driving the carrier, and relates to head-mounting arrangements defined in G11B 5/52.
[G11B 15/1816] Programmed access in sequence to indexed parts of operating tapes cooperating with rotating heads  see  provisional also  G11B15/005 -- The subclass G11B 15/1816 covers systems that read or write magnetic tape using rotating heads, where the tape is accessed in a programmed sequence to specific indexed sections. It deals with sequential control of tape sections for data retrieval or recording, often seen in video or data storage devices.
[G11B 15/1825] driving or moving the head in a direction which cuts across the direction of travel of the tape, e.g. for helicoïdal scanning -- The entry covers devices that shift the transducer head laterally across a moving magnetic or optical tape, moving it in a direction that cuts across the tape's travel path, as used in helicoidal or serpentine scanning systems. It relates to the mechanical actuation needed to position the head for parallel or angled data tracks.
[G11B 15/1833] with head driven in a plane, cyclically around an axis, e.g. on headwheel  construction of headwheel  G11B5/53 ,  G11B21/16 ; disposition of heads on headwheel  G11B5/531 ,  G11B21/02 -- This subclass covers recording heads that are moved in a single plane by rotating cyclically around an axis, typically mounted on a headwheel, and includes the design of the headwheel and the arrangement of multiple heads on it.
[G11B 15/1841] with provision for information tracking by moving the transducing part of the head relative to the headwheel, in the direction of the scanning movement, e.g. for skew or time base correction  in the direction which cuts across tracks, i.e. for track following  G11B3/38 ,  G11B5/588 ,  G11B7/085 ,  G11B21/08 ,  G11B21/10 ; by controlling headwheel rotation  G11B15/4733 , by guiding the tape  G11B15/602 -- G11B 15/1841 relates to magnetic or optical recording systems where the transducing element is moved relative to a headwheel to adjust its scanning position, enabling track-following, skew or time-base correction, and may involve control of headwheel rotation or tape guidance.
[G11B 15/185] using signals recorded in tracks disposed in parallel with the scanning direction -- The entry relates to information-storage devices in which data tracks run parallel to the scanning direction and the system uses signals recorded in those parallel tracks to control the driving, starting or stopping of filamentary or web-type record carriers. It falls under physics-based information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 15/1858] using auxiliary signals, i.e. pilot signals -- The subclass covers methods that employ auxiliary or pilot signals to control, synchronize, or monitor the movement of filamentary or web-type record carriers when they are started, driven, or stopped in information-storage systems. These signals are used to improve reliability or precision of the carrier's motion.
[G11B 15/1866] superimposed on the main signal track -- This subclass covers mechanisms that start, stop or regulate the motion of filamentary or web-type record carriers in which an additional signal is recorded on top of the primary signal track. It relates to driving systems that superimpose control or auxiliary data onto the main recording track of magnetic, optical or similar moving media.
[G11B 15/1875] adaptations for special effects or editing  signal processing or indexing therefor  G11B27/00 -- G11B 15/1875 covers apparatus that drives, starts or stops filamentary or web-type record carriers for special-effect or editing functions, including signal-processing and indexing operations. It includes mechanical or electronic adaptations that control carrier motion to create or modify recorded information.
[G11B 15/1883] for record carriers inside containers -- This subclass covers means for driving, starting or stopping record carriers such as filamentary tapes or webs when they are housed inside protective containers, cartridges or modules, including mechanisms that operate the carrier without removing it from the enclosure.
[G11B 15/1891] the record carrier being endless -- G11B 15/1891 deals with information storage devices that use an endless record carrier, such as a continuous loop of magnetic tape or web, where the medium has no defined start or stop point. The classification covers the driving, starting and stopping mechanisms for such filamentary or web forms, ensuring continuous movement relative to the transducer.
[G11B 15/20] Moving record carrier backwards or forwards by finite amounts, i.e. backspacing, forward spacing -- The G11B 15/20 entry covers devices that shift a filamentary or web-type record carrier a defined distance either backward or forward, enabling precise backspacing or forward spacing operations. It addresses the control of start, stop and incremental movement of such media.
[G11B 15/22] Stopping means -- A stopping means is a device or mechanism that halts the motion of a filamentary or web-type record carrier in information-storage equipment, such as printers, plotters, magnetic-tape recorders or similar systems, by applying a brake, clutch, or other actuation to arrest movement after a desired point.
[G11B 15/24] Drive disengaging means -- The G11B 15/24 subclass defines mechanisms for disengaging the drive that moves filamentary or web-type recording media. It covers devices that start, stop, or decouple the motor or similar actuator from the tape, film, or paper carrier in information-storage systems.
[G11B 15/26] Driving record carriers by members acting directly or indirectly thereon -- This CPC subclass G11B 15/26 covers mechanisms that move or stop record carriers such as filaments or webs by using members that act directly on the carrier or through intermediate components. It includes motors, actuators, and drive systems that start, drive, or halt the media in data-storage devices. The classification falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement.
[G11B 15/28] through rollers driving by frictional contact with the record carrier, e.g. capstan -- The entry G11B 15/28 relates to devices that drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers by using rollers that engage the carrier through frictional contact, such as capstans. It falls under the physics section for information storage based on relative movement between the carrier and the transducer, and covers mechanisms that control the motion of the media via roller-driven friction.
[G11B 15/285] through pneumatic means -- G11B 15/285 pertains to information-storage devices where a filamentary or web-type record carrier is driven, started or stopped by pneumatic means, using air pressure to control the movement of the media.
[G11B 15/29] through pinch-rollers -- The G11B 15/29 subclass deals with information-storage systems that employ pinch-rollers to drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers such as tapes, paper or film. It covers the mechanical arrangement where rollers grip the media to control its movement for recording or retrieval.
[G11B 15/295] with single capstan or drum simultaneously driving the record carrier at two separate points of an isolated part thereof, e.g. the capstan acting directly on the tape rollers -- This CPC subclass covers mechanisms where a single capstan or drum drives a filamentary or web record carrier at two separate points of an isolated part, for example a capstan that directly engages tape rollers to move the tape.
[G11B 15/30] through the means for supporting the record carrier, e.g. mandrel, turntable -- This subclass covers the supporting structures that hold the record medium while it is driven, started, or stopped, such as mandrels, turntables, or similar devices. The focus is on how these supports interact with the motion of filamentary or web-type record carriers in information storage systems.
[G11B 15/32] through the reels or cores on to which the record carrier is wound -- G11B 15/32 defines devices that drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers by moving the reels or cores on which the carrier is wound, enabling controlled motion of the recorded medium. It is classified under physics, information storage, and information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 15/34] through non-slip drive means, e.g. sprocket -- G11B 15/34 concerns driving, starting or stopping filamentary or web-type record carriers by means that prevent slip, such as sprockets engaging perforations or teeth on the media. It is part of the physics subclass for information storage based on relative motion between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 15/38] Driving record carriers by pneumatic means -- The subclass G11B 15/38 covers systems that drive or control filamentary or web-type record carriers-such as film, paper or magnetic tape-by using air pressure or vacuum, including mechanisms for starting, stopping or varying their speed. It falls within the information-storage field where the carrier moves relative to a transducer, and it specifically addresses pneumatic means of moving the media.
[G11B 15/385] directly, e.g. by rotating drum  guiding record carrier on rotating drum  G11B15/61 -- G11B 15/385 pertains to methods for driving, starting, or stopping filamentary or web form record carriers in information storage systems, specifically focusing on techniques that involve directly guiding the record carrier on a rotating drum. This includes mechanisms where the drum itself facilitates the movement and positioning of the recording medium during operation.
[G11B 15/40] Driving record carriers otherwise than by electric motor -- The class G11B 15/40 covers means of moving filamentary or web-type record carriers without using an electric motor, such as hand-crank, spring-wound, pneumatic, hydraulic or gravity-driven mechanisms. It relates to information-storage systems where the carrier's motion relative to the transducer is produced by non-electric power sources.
[G11B 15/42] manually -- The G11B 15/42 subclass covers manual methods for driving, starting or stopping filamentary or web-type record carriers in information-storage devices that rely on relative motion between the carrier and the transducer. It pertains to systems where an operator physically initiates or halts the movement of the recording medium.
[G11B 15/43] Control or regulation of mechanical tension of record carrier, e.g. tape tension -- The G11B 15/43 subclass covers devices and methods for controlling or regulating the mechanical tension of a moving record carrier such as magnetic tape, film, or other filamentary media. It includes systems that start, drive, stop or adjust tape tension to ensure proper recording and playback performance.
[G11B 15/44] Speed-changing arrangements -- G11B 15/44 covers mechanisms that vary the transport speed of filamentary or web-type record carriers in storage systems. It includes devices for accelerating, decelerating, or stopping the media during operation, such as speed-control sleeves, rollers, or electronic drive circuits used in magnetic tape, paper, or similar formats.
[G11B 15/442] Control thereof -- The entry covers mechanisms and methods for controlling the movement of filamentary or web-type recording media, including starting, stopping, speed regulation and synchronization of the carrier in devices such as printers, plotters or other information-storage equipment. It relates to the broader field of physics-based information storage where the record carrier is driven relative to a transducer.
[G11B 15/444] reversing arrangements  G11B15/442  takes precedence -- G11B 15/444 pertains to mechanisms for reversing the direction of filamentary or web form record carriers in information storage systems, with specific emphasis on the operational aspects that ensure proper and timely reversal. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with driving, starting, or stopping such media.
[G11B 15/446] by driving the reels only -- G11B 15/446 covers storage systems that move the recording medium solely by driving the supply or take-up reels. The classification applies to filamentary or web carriers such as magnetic tape where the reels, rather than the transducer, provide the motion needed for recording or playback.
[G11B 15/448] automatic reverse drive transfer therefor -- The code G11B 15/448 covers mechanisms that automatically reverse the drive of filamentary or web-type record carriers, enabling the system to start, stop or change direction without manual intervention, typically used for rewinding, positioning or bidirectional transport of tapes, films or similar media.
[G11B 15/46] Controlling, regulating, or indicating speed -- G11B 15/46 is a CPC subclass that deals with mechanisms which control, regulate or indicate the speed of moving record carriers such as filaments or webs. It includes drives, starters, stops and speed-monitoring devices that manage the relative motion between the carrier and the transducer in information-storage systems.
[G11B 15/463] by using pilot tracking tones embedded in binary coded signals, e.g. using DSV/CDS values of coded signals -- This subclass deals with methods that embed pilot tracking tones within binary-coded signals, such as DSV or CDS values, to drive, start or stop filamentary or web-form record carriers, providing precise control of their motion.
[G11B 15/467] in arrangements for recording or reproducing wherein both record carriers and heads are driven -- This subclass concerns recording or reproducing arrangements in which both the moving record carrier (such as a filamentary tape or web) and the transducer head are actively driven by separate mechanisms. It includes systems that synchronize the transport of the medium and the head for precise positioning.
[G11B 15/4671] by controlling simultaneously the speed of the tape and the speed of the rotating head -- This subclass covers techniques that regulate the tape transport speed together with the rotational speed of the scanning head, providing precise synchronization between the moving record carrier and the transducer during recording or playback. Coordinated control improves data density and reduces timing errors.
[G11B 15/4672] with provision for information tracking -- The classification covers devices that drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers while incorporating means to monitor and record the carrier's position or movement, enabling precise tracking of information along the medium.
[G11B 15/4673] by controlling the speed of the tape while the head is rotating -- G11B 15/4673 designates techniques that control the speed of a tape or other filamentary record carrier while the recording or playback head is rotating, enabling precise synchronization of tape movement with head motion. This falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 15/4675] with provision for information tracking -- The G11B 15/4675 subclass covers devices that drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers and incorporate a means for tracking stored information, such as counters or sensors that monitor the carrier's movement during recording or playback.
[G11B 15/4676] using signals recorded in tracks disposed in parallel with the scanning direction -- This subclass covers techniques that employ signals recorded in tracks that run parallel to the scanning direction, such as reading or processing data from parallel strips on a moving web or filamentary medium, including methods that control the driving, starting or stopping of the record carrier.
[G11B 15/4677] using auxiliary signals, i.e. pilot signals -- This subclass covers methods that employ auxiliary or pilot signals to control the motion of filamentary or web-type record carriers in data storage devices, such as starting, stopping or driving the medium. It falls under the broader field of physics-information storage where the carrier moves relative to a transducer.
[G11B 15/4678] superimposed on the main signal track -- The CPC code G11B 15/4678 refers to techniques that superimpose auxiliary signals onto the main signal track of a moving record carrier such as a filament or web. These added signals are used for functions like driving, starting or stopping the carrier and fall under information-storage methods based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 15/473] by controlling the speed of the heads -- The CPC subclass G11B 15/473 covers methods for controlling the speed of read/write heads in magnetic or optical recording systems where the recording medium moves relative to the transducer, typically involving filamentary or web-type carriers. It falls under the broader group dealing with driving, starting or stopping such record carriers.
[G11B 15/4731] control of headwheel rotation  disposition or construction of headwheel motor  G11B5/53 ,  G11B21/02 -- G11B 15/4731 relates to mechanisms that control the rotation of the headwheel in information-storage devices that move a filamentary or web record carrier, such as magnetic tape or film systems. It includes the disposition and construction of the headwheel motor and the associated drive, start-stop, and speed-control features.
[G11B 15/4733] with provision for information tracking, e.g. for time base correction -- This subclass covers systems that drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers while incorporating means to track their position for time-base correction or other timing adjustments, ensuring accurate synchronization of the recorded data during storage and playback.
[G11B 15/4735] using signals recorded in tracks disposed parallel with the scanning direction -- This subclass covers recording or reading techniques in which data signals are stored in tracks that run parallel to the direction in which the read/write head moves across the medium. It applies to filamentary or web-like carriers where the motion of the carrier relative to the transducer is controlled, such as in tape or web printers.
[G11B 15/4736] using auxiliary signals, i.e. pilot signals -- The CPC subclass G11B 15/4736 covers techniques for driving, starting or stopping filamentary or web-form record carriers by means of auxiliary or pilot signals, such as control tones or timing pulses. It falls within physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer, and addresses methods that use supplemental signals to regulate the motion of the recording medium.
[G11B 15/4738] superimposed on the main signal track -- G11B 15/4738 covers methods of adding auxiliary data onto the main signal track of a moving filamentary or web-type record carrier, such as magnetic tape, to control or indicate driving, start or stop functions. The superimposed information is recorded alongside the primary data without interfering with its retrieval.
[G11B 15/48] Starting -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for initiating motion of filamentary or web-type recording media, such as paper or magnetic tape, in storage devices. It includes mechanisms that start the carrier moving from rest, typically using rollers, drives, or motor controls.
[G11B 15/50] by mechanical linkage, e.g. clutch -- A sub-class of information-storage devices that move filamentary or web-type record carriers, focusing on driving, starting or stopping the carrier through a mechanical linkage such as a clutch, within the broader field of physics-based storage methods.
[G11B 15/52] by using signals recorded on, or derived from, record carrier -- G11B 15/52 covers techniques for initiating, controlling or halting the motion of filamentary or web-type record carriers by employing signals that are recorded on the carrier itself or extracted from it. This includes feedback or timing signals derived directly from the medium to synchronize drive mechanisms.
[G11B 15/54] by stroboscope -- G11B 15/54 covers methods of driving, starting or stopping filamentary or web-type record carriers using a stroboscopic light source. The stroboscope synchronises the motion of the carrier with timing pulses, enabling precise control during recording or playback.
[G11B 15/56] the record carrier having reserve loop, e.g. to minimise inertia during acceleration -- This subclass covers record carriers such as film or tape that incorporate a reserve loop or slack section, allowing the carrier's inertia to be reduced when the transport is accelerated, started or stopped. It falls under the broader category of information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 15/58] with vacuum column -- G11B 15/58 covers mechanisms that drive, start or stop filamentary or web-type record carriers by employing a vacuum column to control the movement of the media. The vacuum column provides a sealed zone that isolates the driven portion of the web from the remainder, allowing precise handling in printers, plotters and similar information-storage devices.
