[G11B 20/10157] PR3 or PR(2,1,-1), i.e. partial response class 3, polynomial (1+D)(2-D)=2+D-D2 -- This subclass covers signal-processing techniques for storage systems that employ partial-response class-3 equalization, defined by the polynomial (1+D)(2-D)=2+D-D², to shape the channel response and improve data detection. It applies to any recording or reproducing method where the relative motion between carrier and transducer is used.
[G11B 20/10166] partial response PR(3,4,4,3) -- The CPC entry G11B 20/10166 covers signal-processing techniques that use the partial-response method known as PR(3,4,4,3). This coding scheme shapes the transmitted waveform so that each symbol's response spans three preceding and three subsequent intervals, improving bandwidth efficiency and error resilience in magnetic or other relative-movement storage systems. It is applied independently of the specific recording or reproduction method.
[G11B 20/10175] PR4, PR(1,0,-1), i.e. partial response class 4, polynomial (1+D)(1-D)=(1-D2) -- G11B 20/10175 covers partial-response signaling of class 4 used in magnetic or optical information storage, where the recorded signal follows the polynomial (1+D)(1-D)=1-D². The method intentionally shapes intersymbol interference to a predictable pattern that can be equalized more simply, improving detection reliability without specifying particular recording or reproducing methods.
[G11B 20/10185] PR5 or PR(-1,0,2,0,-1), i.e. partial response class 5, polynomial -((1+D)2) *((1-D)2) =-1+2D2-D4 -- This subclass relates to signal-processing techniques for magnetic or optical recording where a partial-response class-5 filter is employed, characterised by the polynomial -(1+D)^2(1−D)^2 = -1+2D^2−D^4. It covers the design, analysis and implementation of such filters for decoding or equalising recorded signals, independent of the specific recording method.
[G11B 20/10194] using predistortion during writing  G11B20/10055  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Applying pre-distortion (e.g. by modifying the timing) during writing, e.g. by modifying the signal according to the known characteristics of the read/write channel
[G11B 20/10203] baseline correction  DC correction by choosing codewords of the modulation code  G11B20/1426 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Correcting the DC baseline of the read signal, slicing (adapting the threshold at which the signal will be recognised as a binary zero or one)
[G11B 20/10212] compensation for data shift, e.g. pulse-crowding effects -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compensating for data shift, e.g. addressing the fact that the timing of a peak value might be affected (advanced, delayed) by inter-symbol interference (ISI)
[G11B 20/10222] clock-related aspects, e.g. phase or frequency adjustment or bit synchronisation  dedicated sync patterns in the modulation code  G11B20/1403 -- The entry G11B 20/10222 covers clock-related functions in magnetic or optical storage systems where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer, including methods for phase or frequency adjustment and bit-synchronisation, as well as dedicated sync patterns embedded in the modulation code. It pertains to signal-processing techniques that are not tied to a specific recording or playback method.
[G11B 20/10231] wherein an asynchronous, free-running clock is used; Interpolation of sampled signals -- G11B 20/10231 covers signal-processing methods that use an asynchronous, free-running clock together with interpolation of sampled signals, applied to information-storage systems where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer, without being tied to a specific recording or playback technique.
[G11B 20/1024] wherein a phase-locked loop [PLL] is used -- The code G11B 20/1024 classifies signal-processing arrangements for moving-media storage devices that incorporate a phase-locked loop to lock onto the carrier frequency or timing of the recorded signal, improving read-back accuracy and synchronization between the transducer and the record carrier. It applies to systems where the PLL is used independently of the specific recording or reproduction method.
[G11B 20/1025] the PLL being discrete time or digital PLL -- G11B 20/1025 covers phase-locked loop circuits that operate in discrete time or are implemented digitally, rather than using continuous-time analog techniques. It pertains to signal-processing functions in information-storage devices where the PLL is part of the control or timing system for moving record carriers.
[G11B 20/10259] simultaneous timing recovery for multiple parallel tracks -- The entry covers signal-processing techniques for recovering timing information when several data tracks are read or written in parallel on a moving record carrier. It addresses methods that align the clock of each track without being tied to a specific recording or reproducing technology.
[G11B 20/10268] bit detection or demodulation methods -- The subclass G11B 20/10268 covers techniques for detecting or demodulating individual bits in a data stream, typically applied to magnetic or optical storage systems where a moving carrier passes a transducer. It addresses signal-processing algorithms that extract the binary information from the recorded waveform without referring to the specific recording or playback method.
[G11B 20/10277] the demodulation process being specifically adapted to partial response channels, e.g. PRML decoding -- The subclass G11B 20/10277 covers demodulation techniques that are specially adapted to partial-response channels, for example PRML decoding used in reading magnetic or optical storage signals. It is part of signal-processing methods applied to information-storage devices where the transducer moves relative to the record carrier.
[G11B 20/10287] using probabilistic methods, e.g. maximum likelihood detectors  G11B20/10277  takes precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 20/10287 pertains to signal processing techniques in information storage systems that employ probabilistic methods, such as maximum likelihood detectors, for enhancing data retrieval accuracy. This classification is specific to methods not tied to the recording or reproducing process and is part of a broader category dealing with relative movement between record carriers and transducers.
[G11B 20/10296] using the Viterbi algorithm -- This entry covers the use of the Viterbi algorithm in signal-processing functions for information-storage devices that employ relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer, such as magnetic or optical media. It includes decoding, error-correction and sequence-estimation techniques applied to the recorded signals.
[G11B 20/10305] signal quality assessment -- The G11B 20/10305 subclass covers techniques for evaluating the quality of read-out signals in information-storage systems where data are accessed by the relative motion of a carrier and transducer. It focuses on signal-processing methods that assess parameters such as signal-to-noise ratio, distortion, timing jitter, or error rates, without prescribing a specific recording or reproduction method.
[G11B 20/10314] amplitude of the recorded or reproduced signal -- Covers signal-processing aspects that deal with the amplitude of a signal during recording or playback, including measurement, adjustment or control of signal level, independent of the specific method used to record or reproduce the information. It applies to devices where a moving record carrier interacts with a transducer.
[G11B 20/10324] asymmetry of the recorded or reproduced waveform -- G11B 20/10324 covers signal-processing techniques that address asymmetry in the waveform of a stored or reproduced signal, such as correction or exploitation of unequal rise and fall times, within magnetic, optical or other media where the record carrier moves relative to a transducer. It is applied to processing that is independent of the specific recording or playback method.
[G11B 20/10333] wherein the asymmetry is linked to domain bloom -- This subclass addresses signal-processing methods that detect, compensate for or exploit asymmetrical magnetic-domain behaviour termed domain bloom, where the size or shape of magnetic domains expands under certain conditions on a moving record carrier, affecting the readout signal. It covers techniques for handling this asymmetry in magnetic storage systems without being tied to a specific recording or reproduction method.
[G11B 20/10342] sub-information or auxiliary signals different from the normal recording marks, e.g. signals reproduced from wobble tracks -- This subclass deals with auxiliary or sub-information signals that differ from the normal recording marks, such as data extracted from wobble tracks on magnetic or optical media. It covers the processing of these side-channel signals independent of the specific recording or reproduction method.
[G11B 20/10351] baseline shift, DC content, bias -- The code G11B 20/10351 addresses methods for detecting, compensating or otherwise handling baseline shifts, direct-current components and bias in electrical signals used in information-storage systems where data are read by relative motion of a record carrier and a transducer. It covers signal-processing techniques that correct DC offsets without being tied to a specific recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 20/10361] digital demodulation process -- The G11B 20/10361 subclass addresses digital demodulation processes used to recover information from a modulated carrier signal, emphasizing signal-processing techniques rather than the physical act of recording or reproducing data on a moving storage medium. It covers algorithms and methods that convert received digital waveforms into the original binary or multimedia content.
[G11B 20/1037] based on hard decisions, e.g. by evaluating bit error rates before or after ECC decoding -- G11B 20/1037 covers signal-processing methods that rely on hard bit decisions, such as measuring bit-error rates either before or after error-correcting code decoding, to assess or improve the reliability of information stored by relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer.
[G11B 20/10379] based on soft decisions, e.g. confidence values, probability estimates, likelihoods values or path metrics of a statistical decoding algorithm -- G11B 20/10379 classifies signal-processing techniques for storage systems that rely on relative motion between a medium and a transducer and that use soft-decision data from statistical decoders, such as confidence values, probability or likelihood estimates, or path metrics, to enhance recording or retrieval.
[G11B 20/10388] control of the read or write heads, e.g. tracking errors, defocus or tilt compensation -- This CPC subclass concerns control of read or write heads in magnetic or optical storage devices, addressing tracking errors, defocus and tilt compensation. It falls under physics, information storage, information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer, and covers signal processing not specific to recording or reproducing.
[G11B 20/10398] jitter, timing deviations or phase and frequency errors -- Covers techniques for handling or compensating irregularities in the timing of electronic signals such as jitter, phase noise, and frequency deviations in information-storage systems. The focus is on signal-processing methods that detect, measure, or correct these errors, independent of the recording or playback method.
[G11B 20/10407] by verifying the timing of signal transitions, e.g. rising or falling edges, or by analysing signal slopes -- The G11B 20/10407 subclass covers signal-processing techniques that determine information by checking the timing of signal transitions such as rising or falling edges, or by analysing the slopes of those transitions, applied to storage systems where data are read or written through relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer.
[G11B 20/10416] by verifying the timing of peak values -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 20/10416 pertains to signal processing techniques in information storage systems, specifically focusing on methods that verify the timing of peak values during read or write operations. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with signal processing independent of recording or reproducing methods.
[G11B 20/10425] by counting out-of-lock events of a PLL -- The code covers techniques that monitor a phase-locked loop and count each time it falls out of lock, using those events for signal-processing tasks such as timing extraction, error detection or data recovery in storage systems.
[G11B 20/10435] by verifying the timing of predetermined signal patterns, e.g. sync patterns -- This subclass covers techniques that verify the timing of predetermined signal sequences, such as synchronization patterns, to confirm proper alignment or detection in storage systems where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer.
[G11B 20/10444] by verifying the timing of zero crossings -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 20/10444 pertains to signal processing techniques in information storage systems, specifically focusing on methods that verify the timing of zero crossings for ensuring data integrity and synchronization during read or write operations.
[G11B 20/10453] physical shape of recording marks, e.g. their length, width, depth or contour -- The CPC entry G11B 20/10453 concerns the geometry of recording marks on a storage medium, such as their length, width, depth or overall contour. It addresses how the physical shape of these marks affects information storage that relies on relative movement between carrier and transducer, without focusing on the signal-processing aspects of recording or playback.
[G11B 20/10462] consistency with a reference waveform in a given time period, e.g. by calculating correlations or mean square errors -- This classification entry pertains to signal processing techniques that ensure data integrity during storage and retrieval by comparing signals to a reference waveform over a specific time frame, using methods such as correlation calculations or mean square error analysis.
[G11B 20/10472] derived from statistics of other quality measures, e.g. their mean, variance or skew -- The subclass covers signal-processing techniques that calculate statistical parameters-such as mean, variance or skewness-from other quality measures of stored data, applied to information-storage systems where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer.
[G11B 20/10481] optimisation methods -- G11B 20/10481 addresses optimisation methods used in signal processing for information-storage devices that rely on relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer, without being limited to any specific recording or playback technique.
[G11B 20/1049] using closed-form solutions -- G11B 20/1049 pertains to signal processing techniques applied to storage systems where information is recorded by relative motion of a medium and a transducer, where the processing is based on closed-form analytical solutions rather than iterative or numerical methods. It includes any mathematical formulations that provide explicit expressions for error correction, modulation, or data retrieval in such moving-medium devices.
[G11B 20/105] selecting parameter values from a plurality of predetermined settings -- G11B 20/105 covers signal-processing functions that select among a set of pre-defined parameter settings for devices that store information by relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer, such as hard-disk or optical-disk systems. The classification applies to control algorithms that choose optimal values for parameters like gain, timing or threshold without being tied to a specific recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 20/10509] iterative methods, e.g. trial-and-error, interval search, gradient descent or feedback loops  G11B20/10518  takes precedence -- A classification for signal-processing techniques applied to information-storage systems that rely on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer, covering iterative methods such as trial-and-error, interval search, gradient descent and feedback loops used to refine recording or retrieval processes.
[G11B 20/10518] using neural networks -- G11B 20/10518 covers the use of neural networks for signal processing in information storage devices where data is recorded or retrieved by relative movement between the carrier and the transducer, such as magnetic or optical media. The class lies in physics - information storage - information storage based on relative movement - signal processing not specific to recording or reproducing.
[G11B 20/10527] Audio or video recording; Data buffering arrangements  G11B20/12  -  G11B20/18  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Initially, G11B20/10527 was supposed contain all documents about how to record PCM audio data. Nowadays it also comprises many documents about how to use intermediate memories (buffers), e.g., playback buffers for ensuring a seamless playback of a recorded video stream while reading the data intermittently in high-speed bursts, or recording buffers for making sure that even in case discontinuous data reception the recording process will not be interrupted; G11B20/10527 will particularly be assigned if the aspect "memory" is important (e.g., addressing within the buffer, adjusting the read/write clock of the buffer, etc.). In the past (when people started recording digitised audio signals on record carriers), G11B20/10527 was also used for documents about A/D conversion, filtering, quantisation errors, dithering, oversampling, or sampling frequency conversion; these aspects are now classified in G11B20/10009 .
[G11B 20/12] Formatting, e.g. arrangement of data block or words on the record carriers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Formatting, e.g. arrangement of data block or words on the record carriers. General low-level structure of a record carrier (what to store where), e.g. the format of sector headers, the size of the lead-in area, etc.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Documents related to defect management -> G11B20/18 - File format conversion -> G06F16/1794 - File format or the syntax of recorded video streams -> H04N7/24
[G11B 20/1201] on tapes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Formatting aspects of tape storage devices; a distinction is made between tapes with longitudinal tracks, G11B20/1202 , transverse tracks, G11B20/1207 , and combinations of both, G11B20/1211 ; if applicable, a further distinction can be made between tapes which are specifically designed for storing A/V data ( G11B20/1204 ) and those designed for storing computer data ( G11B20/1205 ).
[G11B 20/1202] with longitudinal tracks only -- G11B 20/1202 concerns signal-processing functions applied to storage devices in which the record carrier moves relative to the transducer and the information is laid out in longitudinal (parallel) tracks only, without reference to a particular recording or reproduction technique.
[G11B 20/1204] for continuous data, e.g. digitised analog information signals, pulse code modulated [PCM] data -- This subclass covers signal processing methods for continuous data such as digitised analog information signals and pulse-code-modulated data, without regard to the specific recording or reproducing technique used.
[G11B 20/1205] for discontinuous data, e.g. digital information signals or computer program data -- G11B 20/1205 pertains to signal processing techniques in information storage systems where the data is discontinuous, such as digital information signals or computer program data, focusing on methods not tied to specific recording or reproducing processes.
[G11B 20/1207] with transverse tracks only -- G11B 20/1207 covers signal-processing features of information-storage devices that employ only transverse (cross-wise) tracks on a moving recording medium, such as helical-scan tape or disc systems. It relates to the handling of read-out or write-in signals where the carrier's motion is perpendicular to the track direction, without detailing the recording or reproducing method itself.
[G11B 20/1208] for continuous data, e.g. digitised analog information signals, pulse code modulated [PCM] data -- The CPC class G11B 20/1208 covers signal-processing techniques applied to continuous data streams such as digitised analog signals or pulse-code-modulated (PCM) data, without specifying the recording or reproduction method.
[G11B 20/1209] for discontinuous data, e.g. digital information signals or computer program data -- G11B 20/1209 covers signal processing techniques applied to discontinuous data such as digital information signals or computer program data, where the processing is not tied to a particular recording or playback method. The category falls under physics, information storage, and relative movement between record carrier and transducer, and addresses general processing functions for non-continuous data streams.
[G11B 20/1211] with different data track configurations  longitudinal control tracks with transverse user data tracks  G11B20/1207 -- G11B 20/1211 pertains to signal processing in information storage systems where the recording and reproducing methods are not specified. It specifically addresses configurations featuring longitudinal control tracks alongside transverse user data tracks, enhancing data organization and retrieval efficiency.
[G11B 20/1212] for continuous data, e.g. digitised analog information signals, pulse code modulated [PCM] data -- G11B 20/1212 relates to signal-processing functions applied to continuous data streams, for example digitised analog signals or pulse-code-modulated data, without reference to a particular recording or reproducing method. It is used in information-storage devices where the carrier moves relative to a transducer.
[G11B 20/1214] for discontinuous data, e.g. digital information signals or computer program data -- The CPC subclass G11B 20/1214 covers signal-processing techniques applied to discontinuous data such as digital information streams or computer program code, without limiting the description to the recording or playback method. It falls within the physics area of information storage that relies on relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer.
[G11B 20/1215] on cards  optical aspect of optical cards  G11B7/0033 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Formatting aspects record media if the form factor is a card.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Optical aspects of optical cards -> G11B7/0033
[G11B 20/1217] on discs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Formatting aspects of magnetic or optical disks; this is where most documents in G11B20/12 are currently being classified; a distinction can be made between recording A/V data, G11B20/1251 , recording computer or control/management data, G11B20/1252 , and recording mixtures of both, G11B20/1254 : of some relevance is G11B20/1258 , disks having a structure defined by multiple radial zones, e.g. zone constant angular velocity discs, ZCAV.
[G11B 20/1251] for continuous data, e.g. digitised analog information signals, pulse code modulated [PCM] data -- G11B 20/1251 covers signal-processing techniques applied to continuous data such as digitised analog signals or pulse-code-modulated (PCM) streams, without relating to the specific recording or playback method. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer in the physics field.
[G11B 20/1252] for discontinuous data, e.g. digital information signals or computer program data -- The subclass deals with signal-processing methods applied to discontinuous data such as digital information streams or computer program code, independent of the recording or playback technique. It covers handling of data bursts, packetized or non-continuous signals, focusing on processing rather than storage mechanics.
[G11B 20/1254] for mixed data, i.e. continuous and discontinuous data -- The G11B 20/1254 subclass covers signal-processing techniques that handle mixed data comprising both continuous (analog) and discontinuous (digital) components, without being tied to any particular recording or reproducing method. It addresses algorithms and processing steps for integrating, converting, or manipulating such hybrid data streams in information-storage systems.
[G11B 20/1258] where blocks are arranged within multiple radial zones, e.g. Zone Bit Recording or Constant Density Recording discs, MCAV discs, MCLV discs -- This subclass covers recording or reproducing methods where data blocks are organized into several concentric radial zones on a disc, enabling zone-bit recording, constant-density recording, or multi-carrier variable/low-velocity formats. It falls under signal-processing aspects that are not specific to the recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 20/1261] on films, e.g. for optical moving-picture soundtracks  optical aspect  G11B7/0032 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Formatting aspect of films, i.e. transparent record carriers which are primarily meant for recording photographic frames and accompanying audio or control data.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Formatting aspects of how to record movies on digital tapes or different kinds of disks -> G11B20/1201 , G11B20/1217
[G11B 20/1262] with more than one format/standard, e.g. conversion from CD-audio format to R-DAT format -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Record carriers involving more than one format/standard, e.g. conversion from CD-audio format to R-DAT format, disks having a CD and a DVD layer, discs storing normal PCM signal and additional MP3 tracks, etc.
[G11B 20/14] using self-clocking codes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In the strict sense, self-clocking codes for digital recording. Today virtually all codes are self-clocking, however, current record carriers do not have a separate track for bit clock synchronisation. G11B20/14 hence encompasses all kinds of modulation codes (e.g., the EFM code used on audio CDs).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Error correcting codes, error detecting codes in the context of recording and reproducing systems -> G11B20/1833 - Theory of error correcting codes, error correcting codes per se -> H03M13/00
[G11B 20/1403] characterised by the use of two levels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Although originally being meant to comprise binary modulation codes in general, this sub-group is now mainly used for documents about synchronisation patterns for bit clock recovery.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Synchronisation of separate data streams, e.g. audio and video channels -> G11B27/10 - Synchronisation patterns for stream synchronisation -> G11B27/3027 - Theory of binary codes in general, not in the specific context of record carriers -> H03M5/04
[G11B 20/1407] code representation depending on a single bit, i.e. where a one is always represented by a first code symbol while a zero is always represented by a second code symbol -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bit-by-bit coding, binary codes having one symbol representing a zero and another symbol representing a one, no interdependence between subsequent information bits.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Theory of bit-by-bit coding in general, not in the specific context of record carriers -> H03M5/06
[G11B 20/1411] conversion to or from pulse width coding -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pulse width modulation. A signal to be recorded is encoded by varying the pulse width of a square wave at a constant frequency. Examples: delta modulation, sigma-delta modulation.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sigma-delta encoded audio signals -> G11B20/10527 - Theory of pulse width modulation in general, not in the specific context of record carriers -> H03M5/08
[G11B 20/1415] conversion to or from pulse frequency coding -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pulse frequency modulation, information encoded by altering the repetition rate of the pulses, every pulse having the same fixed length. As pulse width modulation, this modulation scheme alters the duty cycle of the square wave.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Theory of pulse frequency modulation in general, not in the specific context of record carriers -> H03M5/10
[G11B 20/1419] to or from biphase level coding, i.e. to or from codes where a one is coded as a transition from a high to a low level during the middle of a bit cell and a zero is encoded as a transition from a low to a high level during the middle of a bit cell or  vice versa , e.g. split phase code, Manchester code conversion to or from biphase space or mark coding, i.e. to or from codes where there is a transition at the beginning of every bit cell and a one has no second transition and a zero has a second transition one half of a bit period later or  vice versa , e.g. double frequency code, FM code -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. binary phase modulation (Manchester codes); also phase or frequency modulation of wobbles. G11B20/1419 generally relates to codes where a one is coded as a transition from a high to a low level during the middle of a bit cell and a zero is encoded as a transition from a low to a high level during the middle of a bit cell or vice versa, e.g. split phase code, Manchester code conversion to or from biphase space or mark coding, i.e. to or from codes where there is a transition at the beginning of every bit cell and a one has no second transition and a zero has a second transition one half of a bit period later or vice versa, e.g. double frequency code, FM code. Biphase level codes in general: H03M5/12 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Theory of biphase level codes in general, not in the specific context of record carriers -> H03M5/12
[G11B 20/1423] Code representation depending on subsequent bits, e.g. delay modulation, double density code, Miller code -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Basic coding schemes wherein the input bits are not coded independently of each other, but their code representation depends on subsequent bits, e.g. delay modulation, double density code, Miller code.
[G11B 20/1426] conversion to or from block codes or representations thereof -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Binary block codes. This very prominent subgroup also includes run-length limited (RLL) codes and various kinds of DSV optimised codes, e.g. the Modified Frequency Modulation (MFM) used on floppy discs, the EFM and EFM+ codes used on CDs and DVDs, or the 17PP code used on Blu-Ray discs.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Theory of block codes in general, not in the specific context of record carriers -> H03M5/145
[G11B 20/1488] characterised by the use of three levels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ternary codes, i.e. modulation codes wherein the code may contain three different symbols which are commonly represented by three discrete signal levels.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Partial response signals exhibiting three possible signal levels -> G11B20/10009 - Theory of ternary codes in general, not in the specific context of record carriers -> H03M5/16
[G11B 20/1492] two levels are symmetric, in respect of the sign to the third level which is "zero" -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Termary codes wherein the possible signal levels are -a, 0, and a.
[G11B 20/1496] characterised by the use of more than three levels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  n-ary digital modulation codes with n=4 and above, e.g. quaternary modulation codes (4 possible signal levels, i.e. each symbol can per se convey two bits).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Partial response signals with n>3 signal values -> G11B20/10009 - Theory of n-ary codes, n>3, in general, not in the specific context of record carriers -> H03M5/20
[G11B 20/16] using non self-clocking codes, i.e. the clock signals are either recorded in a separate clocking track or in a combination of several information tracks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non self-clocking codes, i.e. the clock signals are not derivable from the modulated data sequence itself (which is the case for any modern RLL code) but instead they are either recorded in a separate clocking track or in a combination of several information tracks.
[G11B 20/18] Error detection or correction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Detecting and correcting errors, e.g. erroneous bits or sectors; testing the medium for defects. This sub-group covers, e.g., the detection of bad sectors, strategies for replacing these sectors by other sectors, the application of various kinds of error correction codes and error detection codes so as to reliably recover the recorded bit sequence, the usage of interleaving schemes for spreading the effect of local defects, the actual detection of such defects by verification and certification processes, the idea of mitigating the effects of a local defect by data interpolation, and the documentation of defects by maintaining different kinds of defect lists.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Defect management by using redundant hardware (e.g. RAID systems per se) -> G06F11/00
[G11B 20/1803] by redundancy in data representation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Obtaining additional robustness by simple redundancy, i.e. by recording the same data multiple times at different locations.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Redundancy generating ECC schemes that are more advanced than such a simple repetition code -> G11B20/1833
[G11B 20/1806] Pulse code modulation systems for audio signals  G11B20/1803  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Approaches particularly designed for audio signals ( G11B20/1809 : purely by interleaving, i.e. for mitigating the perceptual effect of a burst error; G11B20/1813 : by error correcting codes involving parity symbols).
[G11B 20/1809] by interleaving -- G11B 20/1809 pertains to signal-processing methods that employ interleaving of data in information-storage systems where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer. It covers techniques that rearrange the order of recorded bits or symbols to improve error resilience or access efficiency, without being tied to a specific recording or reproducing method. This subclass lies within the broader category of physics-related information storage based on relative movement.
[G11B 20/1813] by adding special bits or symbols to the coded information  G11B20/1809  takes precedence -- The subclass G11B 20/1813 deals with signal-processing techniques that alter coded data by inserting special bits or symbols-such as markers, flags, or control codes-into the information stream. This applies to information-storage devices where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer, without specifying a particular recording or playback method.
[G11B 20/1816] Testing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Testing the medium, recognising bad sectors, determining whether the medium is actually usable. If such tests take place during the recording/playback operation, see also G11B27/36 (monitoring). If the test involves recording a particular test pattern, the document will be classified in G11B20/182 .
[G11B 20/182] using test patterns -- G11B 20/182 covers the use of test patterns in signal-processing systems for information storage devices where data is read or written by relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer, such as magnetic or optical disks. It addresses the generation, insertion, detection or analysis of these patterns to assess performance, calibration or error detection without tying to any specific recording or playback method.
[G11B 20/1833] by adding special lists or symbols to the coded information  G11B20/1806 ,  G11B20/1866  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any error-correcting code (ECC) or Error-Detecting Code (EDC) used on record carriers.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - ECC in the specific context of dedicated computer hardware -> G06F11/00 - Theory of ECC, not in the specific context of record carriers -> H03M3/13
[G11B 20/1866] by interleaving  G11B20/1809  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any interleaving used for mitigating the effects of read/write errors, also if being combined with additional parity symbols.
[G11B 20/1876] Interpolating methods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Interpolation, missing or defective information is recovered by estimating the correct data values based on adjacent data items.
[G11B 20/1879] Direct read-after-write methods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Read-after-write methods. During a normal recording operation, a data item is read from the medium for immediate verification that it has been recorded correctly.
[G11B 20/1883] Methods for assignment of alternate areas for defective areas -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In case of defective areas (e.g., bad sectors), relocating the data that was supposed to be recorded to the defective area to another area. This other area can be part of a dedicated spare area (linear replacement), or it can be a sector following the defective sector (skip replacement). Subgroups for applying this principle to tapes ( G11B20/1886 ) and discs ( G11B20/1889 ).
[G11B 20/1886] with tapes -- This subclass covers signal-processing functions used with tape-based storage devices, such as equalisation, filtering, error-correction or data-format handling, where the processing is independent of the particular recording or playback method.
[G11B 20/1889] with discs -- G11B 20/1889 covers signal-processing techniques applied to disc-based information-storage systems where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer. It addresses the handling of read or write signals from rotating discs without being tied to any particular recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 2019/121] by photo-electric sensing -- G11B 2019/121 relates to devices that employ photo-electric sensors to detect or measure parameters of a recording medium or printed image. The classification covers systems that use light-based detection for registration, alignment, density control, or media presence in printing, copying or reading apparatus.
[G11B 20/20] for correction of skew for multitrack recording -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Correcting skew for multitrack recording, mainly in the context of magnetic tapes.
[G11B 2020/00014] the compressed signal being an audio signal -- G11B 2020/00014 covers technologies for recording, reproducing or processing an audio signal that has been compressed, including methods and devices for encoding, decoding, storing or transmitting digitally compressed sound such as MP3, AAC or similar codecs. It pertains to the handling of compressed audio data within storage media or playback systems.
[G11B 2020/00021] lossless audio compression -- G11B 2020/00021 classifies inventions that compress digital audio data without any loss of fidelity, using techniques such as predictive coding, transform coding, or integer-based encoding to enable exact reconstruction of the original signal. The entry covers algorithms, hardware, and software implementations for lossless audio compression in recording, storage, and transmission.
[G11B 2020/00028] Advanced audio coding [AAC] -- G11B 2020/00028 covers technologies for compressing digital audio using the Advanced Audio Coding (AAC) format, including methods of encoding, decoding and related signal processing to achieve efficient storage and transmission.
[G11B 2020/00036] AC-3, i.e. ATSC digital audio compression standard -- G11B 2020/00036 covers the AC-3 audio coding system, the digital audio compression standard used in the ATSC television broadcasting format. It includes inventions related to encoding, decoding, and processing of AC-3 bitstreams for high-quality, low-bit-rate sound transmission. This classification groups technologies that implement or improve the ATSC digital audio compression techniques.
[G11B 2020/00043] Adaptive transform acoustic coding [ATRAC] -- G11B covers recording, reproduction and processing of sound signals, and entry G11B 2020/00043 pertains to the Adaptive Transform Acoustic Coding (ATRAC) method. ATRAC is a digital audio compression technique that uses a combination of subband filtering and transform coding with adaptive quantisation to reduce data size while preserving fidelity, originally developed for Sony's MiniDisc and other consumer audio formats.
[G11B 2020/0005] DTS audio codecs -- The CPC code G11B 2020/0005 covers digital audio data compression and coding methods, specifically the DTS family of audio codecs that provide high-fidelity multichannel surround sound for cinema and home entertainment. It includes techniques for encoding, decoding and bit-stream formatting of DTS bit rates.
[G11B 2020/00057] MPEG-1 or MPEG-2 audio layer III [MP3] -- The CPC entry G11B 2020/00057 pertains to audio data compression methods and devices employing the MPEG-1 or MPEG-2 audio layer III standard, commonly known as MP3. It covers techniques for encoding, decoding, and processing digital audio streams using perceptual coding to reduce file size while preserving sound quality.
[G11B 2020/00065] Sigma-delta audio encoding -- G11B 2020/00065 belongs to CPC group G11B, covering information storage and retrieval, especially data conversion and coding methods. This entry concerns sigma-delta audio encoding, a technique that employs oversampling and noise shaping to convert analog audio signals into high-resolution digital form. The method is commonly used in high-fidelity audio converters and digital signal processing.
[G11B 2020/00072] the compressed signal including a video signal -- G11B 2020/00072 covers technologies related to compressed signals that contain video information. It includes methods, devices and formats for encoding, transmitting or storing video data using compression techniques such as intra-frame or inter-frame coding, and addresses the structure of the resulting compressed video signal.
[G11B 2020/00079] the compression ratio or quality level being adapted to circumstances, e.g. to the available recording space -- G11B 2020/00079 covers methods that adapt the compression ratio or quality level of a recording to the prevailing circumstances, such as the amount of storage space available, by dynamically adjusting encoding parameters to achieve the desired balance between file size and output quality.
[G11B 2020/10537] Audio or video recording -- G11B 2020/10537 pertains to devices and methods for capturing sound or moving images, such as microphones, cameras and associated recording equipment. It includes technologies that convert audio or visual information into stored analog or digital formats.
[G11B 2020/10546] specifically adapted for audio data -- G11B 2020/10546 designates inventions that are specially adapted for handling audio data, such as devices, media, or methods for recording, storing, processing, or reproducing sound signals. It falls under the CPC class G11B, which covers information storage and retrieval technologies, and identifies features uniquely tailored to audio applications.
[G11B 2020/10555] wherein the frequency, the amplitude, or other characteristics of the audio signal is taken into account -- This CPC subgroup covers inventions that process an audio signal while explicitly taking into account its frequency, amplitude or other signal characteristics, such as adaptive filtering, equalization, compression or signal-dependent control.
[G11B 2020/10564] frequency -- G11B in the CPC concerns reading, recording and reproducing information on various storage media. The 2020/10564 entry, titled "frequency," addresses the specification, measurement or control of signal or operating frequencies used in those recording or playback processes.
[G11B 2020/10574] volume or amplitude -- G11B 2020/10574 covers technologies for controlling the volume or amplitude of recorded or reproduced signals, such as gain adjustment, level control, or amplitude modulation in information storage devices.
[G11B 2020/10583] parameters controlling audio interpolation processes -- G11B 2020/10583 covers the control data used to manage audio interpolation techniques, such as sample-rate conversion, up-sampling or pitch-shifting. It includes parameters like interpolation factor, filter coefficients, window functions, and timing values that determine how original audio samples are mathematically reconstructed and blended into new sample points.
[G11B 2020/10592] specifically adapted for recording or reproducing multichannel signals -- G11B 2020/10592 covers devices and methods that are specially designed to record or reproduce signals consisting of multiple separate channels, such as stereo or surround-sound audio, multichannel video, or any system that processes several parallel signal streams simultaneously.
[G11B 2020/10601] surround sound signal -- G11B 2020/10601 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to technologies for surround-sound signal processing, including methods of encoding, decoding, distributing and reproducing multi-channel audio to create immersive sound fields.
[G11B 2020/10611] 3D video data -- G11B 2020/10611 classifies inventions dealing with three-dimensional video data, encompassing formats, encoding, storage and transmission methods for stereoscopic, multi-view or depth-map video content, as well as devices that capture, reproduce or process such data. It is used to index patents involving 3D video codecs, immersive video recording systems and playback equipment.
[G11B 2020/1062] Data buffering arrangements, e.g. recording or playback buffers -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/1062 pertains to inventions that provide data-buffering arrangements, such as temporary storage elements used during recording, playback or processing of digital information. It includes the design, control and management of buffers that smooth data flow, compensate for timing mismatches, or enable seamless media handling.
[G11B 2020/10629] the buffer having a specific structure -- G11B 2020/10629 is a CPC subgroup within data-processing apparatus that covers buffers defined by a particular internal structure, such as staged, segmented, or hierarchical memory organization used to store or transfer data between components. It classifies inventions that improve latency, throughput, or reliability through the arrangement of storage cells, queues, or registers.
[G11B 2020/10638] First-in-first-out memories [FIFO] buffers -- G11B refers to the class for storage, recording, and retrieval of data, including memory devices and data handling structures. The entry G11B 2020/10638 covers first-in-first-out (FIFO) memory buffers, which temporarily hold data and release it in the exact order it was received, a function commonly employed in digital circuits for timing and flow control.
[G11B 2020/10648] First-in-last-out memories [LIFO] buffers -- G11B 2020/10648 covers first-in-last-out (LIFO) memory structures, commonly called stacks, which store data such that the most recently written item is the first retrieved. The classification includes hardware and software implementations of LIFO buffers used in computer architectures, microcontrollers, and digital signal processing.
[G11B 2020/10657] Cache memories for random data access, e.g. buffers wherein the data output is controlled by a priority parameter other than retention time -- G11B 2020/10657 covers cache memory systems that provide random access to stored data, such as buffers whose read-out order is determined by a priority metric other than how long the data has been retained. These designs manage data flow based on factors like importance, frequency of use, or other criteria, improving performance in computing devices.
[G11B 2020/10666] Ring buffers, e.g. buffers wherein an iteratively progressing read or write pointer moves back to the beginning of the buffer when reaching the last storage cell -- The entry covers ring buffer designs, where a read or write pointer advances through storage cells and, upon reaching the final cell, returns to the first cell, forming a circular queue. Such structures provide continuous data flow and simplify buffer management in memory, communication and signal-processing systems.
[G11B 2020/10675] aspects of buffer control -- The CPC entry G11B 2020/10675 covers inventions that manage buffer control in information-handling or image-forming equipment, such as regulating paper or data flow to prevent overflow, underflow, or timing errors. It includes methods and devices that monitor buffer status and adjust feeding, processing or storage actions to maintain continuous operation. This classification is used for patents related to buffer-management techniques in printers, copiers and similar devices.
[G11B 2020/10685] input interface, i.e. the way data enter the buffer, e.g. by informing the sender that the buffer is busy -- The code covers mechanisms for managing how data are introduced into a buffer, including signalling to the sender when the buffer is full or busy so that transmission can be paused or adjusted. This includes input interfaces that provide flow-control feedback to prevent overflow.
[G11B 2020/10694] output interface, i.e. the way data leave the buffer, e.g. by adjusting the clock rate -- The G11B 2020/10694 subclass covers output interfaces for data buffers, describing how data are released from the buffer. It includes techniques that control the transfer rate, for example by adjusting the clock frequency that drives the output.
[G11B 2020/10703] processing rate of the buffer, e.g. by accelerating the data output -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/10703 addresses techniques for increasing the processing speed of a data buffer, such as methods that accelerate the output of buffered information. It covers control mechanisms, timing adjustments, or hardware changes that raise the rate at which stored data is read, transferred, or released. This includes any system designed to reduce latency or improve throughput of buffered data streams.
[G11B 2020/10712] buffer capacity, e.g. when the buffer capacity is exhausted, buffered data are overwritten with more recent data, accepting that the old data are lost -- The entry addresses methods for handling limited storage buffers in computing devices, specifically when a buffer reaches its capacity and newer data overwrite the older contents, resulting in loss of the original information. It relates to overflow management and data retention policies in digital systems.
[G11B 2020/10722] wherein the size of the buffer is variable, e.g. by adding additional memory cells for coping with input streams that have high bit rates -- The CPC class G11B 2020/10722 covers buffering techniques in digital data handling where the buffer capacity can be dynamically adjusted, for example by allocating extra memory cells to accommodate input streams with high data rates, ensuring smooth processing without overflow.
[G11B 2020/10731] wherein the buffer I/O can be temporarily suspended, e.g. by refusing to accept further data to be buffered -- The entry covers methods for temporarily halting the operation of a data buffer by refusing additional input, allowing the system to pause I/O without losing stored data, common in storage devices, communication interfaces, or memory management.
[G11B 2020/1074] involving a specific threshold value -- G11B 2020/1074 relates to data processing or printing apparatus that operates based on a defined threshold value. The classification is applied to systems that detect a parameter such as signal level, color density or exposure and trigger or modify a function when the measured value crosses the preset limit.
[G11B 2020/1075] the usage of the buffer being restricted to a specific kind of data -- G11B 2020/1075 concerns data processing or storage devices that limit the use of a buffer to a particular type of data, such as a defined file format, signal class, or protocol. The restriction may be enforced by hardware, firmware, or software to ensure proper handling or security of that data.
[G11B 2020/10759] content data -- The CPC entry G11B 2020/10759 covers inventions that relate to the handling, organization and processing of content data, including the structure, storage formats, metadata and methods for managing digital information. It falls within the G11B group on information storage, retrieval and input-output, focusing on techniques for representing and manipulating content in electronic form.
[G11B 2020/10768] by pre-caching the initial portion of songs or other recorded or downloaded data for starting playback instantly -- G11B 2020/10768 covers methods that pre-cache the initial segment of digital audio or other recorded data so playback can begin instantly, a technique used in streaming and download services to provide immediate start of songs or similar media.
[G11B 2020/10777] instructions or commands -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/10777 covers inventions that involve the generation, reception, interpretation or execution of instructions or commands, typically in data-processing or communication systems. It includes hardware or software means for handling control signals, command decoding, and command-driven operation of devices. This group is part of the broader G11B class for information storage and handling.
[G11B 2020/10787] parameters, e.g. for decoding or encoding -- G11B 2020/10787 covers the definition of parameters that influence decoding or encoding operations, such as control values, thresholds or settings used by algorithms to transform, store or retrieve data. It relates to the specification of these variable factors within information-processing and storage systems.
[G11B 2020/10796] address data -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/10796 covers systems and methods for handling address data, including the storage, retrieval, formatting, verification and updating of geographical or postal address information within databases or information-processing devices. It relates to addressing functions used in navigation, location-based services and data-exchange applications.
[G11B 2020/10805] involving specific measures to prevent a buffer overflow -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code G11B 2020/10805, used by the European and United States patent offices, pertains to methods that include particular steps to avoid buffer overflows in data storage systems. This classification is part of a detailed scheme for organizing patents related to information storage.
[G11B 2020/10814] involving specific measures to prevent a buffer underrun -- This subclass concerns techniques used in recording systems to avoid a buffer underrun, i.e. loss of data flow during write operations, by employing methods such as pre-buffering, flow-control, or speed adjustment to ensure continuous recording.
[G11B 2020/10824] the buffer being used to prevent vibrations or shocks from causing delays -- G11B 2020/10824 refers to buffer arrangements in data-recording or storage devices that absorb vibrations or shocks to prevent operational delays, covering mechanical or electronic shock-absorbing structures that keep the read/write process stable.
[G11B 2020/10833] Copying or moving data from one record carrier to another -- G11B 2020/10833 pertains to methods and devices for copying or moving data from one record carrier to another, including reading information from a source medium and writing it onto a target medium without altering the original content.
[G11B 2020/10842] wherein not all recorded data are copied or moved -- G11B 2020/10842 concerns data processing operations where only a portion of the information stored on a recording medium is transferred, leaving the remainder unchanged. It applies to devices or methods that copy or move selected data without affecting the rest of the recorded content. This subclass addresses partial data migration or duplication tasks.
[G11B 2020/10851] Erasing data on the record carrier -- The G11B 2020/10851 entry covers methods and apparatus for erasing data stored on a record carrier, including techniques such as overwriting, degaussing, demagnetising, and other processes that permanently remove or render the recorded information unreadable. It addresses both electronic and non-electronic storage media.
[G11B 2020/10861] Finalising a record carrier after a recording operation, e.g. to ensure compatibility with a ROM medium -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/10861 covers methods and devices that complete a record carrier after data has been written, such as finalising, locking or sealing the medium to make it behave like a ROM product and to guarantee that it can be read reliably by standard ROM readers.
[G11B 2020/1087] wherein a selection is made among at least two alternative ways of processing -- The code G11B 2020/1087 covers systems or methods that include a decision element selecting one of at least two alternative processing paths. It applies to data-handling, signal-processing or algorithmic functions where a selector, control logic or software determines which mode of operation is used.
[G11B 2020/10879] the kind of record carrier being the selection criterion -- G11B 2020/10879 refers to inventions in information-storage devices where the kind of record carrier-such as magnetic tape, optical disc, or paper-serves as the criterion for selecting or processing stored data, influencing the device's operation or data retrieval method.
[G11B 2020/10888] the kind of data being the selection criterion -- G11B 2020/10888 covers devices or methods that select information based on the type of data, such as distinguishing text, image, audio or other formats to trigger specific processing or routing actions. It applies to storage or retrieval systems where the selection criterion is the data kind rather than its content values.
[G11B 2020/10898] Overwriting or replacing recorded data -- G11B 2020/10898 covers technologies for overwriting or replacing data that has already been recorded on a storage medium, including methods for erasing, re-recording and updating information on magnetic, optical, semiconductor or similar media. It addresses both the physical processes and control mechanisms that enable data to be refreshed or rewritten without requiring a new medium.
[G11B 2020/10907] using pseudo-overwriting, i.e. virtually or logically overwriting data on WORM media by remapping recorded blocks to alternate areas -- The CPC entry G11B 2020/10907 covers techniques that simulate overwriting on write-once-read-many (WORM) storage by logically redirecting write operations to new physical locations while presenting the data as if it were overwritten, effectively remapping recorded blocks to alternate areas.
[G11B 2020/10916] Seeking data on the record carrier for preparing an access to a specific address -- This CPC entry concerns techniques for locating or extracting information stored on a record carrier in order to set up access to a particular address within the medium. It includes methods and devices that search the carrier's data structure, retrieve address pointers or location cues, and prepare the system for precise read or write operations at the identified address.
[G11B 2020/10925] involving an inter-layer jump, i.e. changing from one recording layer to another -- G11B 2020/10925 covers technologies that enable an inter-layer jump, meaning the recording or reading head moves from one data-storage layer to another within a multilayer medium. It applies to optical, magnetic or other multilayer recording systems where layer-to-layer transition is part of the operation.
[G11B 2020/10935] wherein a time constraint must be met -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code G11B 2020/10935, used by the EPO and USPTO, pertains to systems or methods where data processing or storage operations must adhere to specific time constraints. This classification is part of a detailed scheme for information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 2020/10944] Real-time recording or reproducing, e.g. for ensuring seamless playback of AV data -- This classification covers inventions that record or reproduce audio-visual signals in real time, aiming to eliminate gaps or buffering so playback is continuous. It includes methods, apparatuses and control techniques that synchronize capture and output, often used in streaming, broadcasting or live-event recording systems.
[G11B 2020/10953] Concurrent recording or playback of different streams or files -- The G11B 2020/10953 subclass covers devices or methods that can record or play back multiple separate data streams or files at the same time, allowing parallel handling of audio, video, or other media. It includes techniques for synchronizing, mixing, or switching between streams during concurrent operation. This is commonly applied in multimedia recording, broadcasting, and storage systems.
[G11B 2020/10962] wherein both recording and playback take place simultaneously -- G11B 2020/10962 covers technology in which a medium records a signal while the same signal is reproduced at the same time, such as duplex audio or video devices that allow real-time monitoring of the captured content.
[G11B 2020/10972] Management of interruptions, e.g. due to editing -- G11B 2020/10972 covers methods and devices for handling interruptions in data processing or communication operations, such as pausing, resuming, or temporarily halting a task when a user edits or modifies content. It includes techniques for preserving state, managing buffers and synchronising data before and after the interruption.
[G11B 2020/10981] Recording or reproducing data when the data rate or the relative speed between record carrier and transducer is variable -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/10981 pertains to methods and apparatus for recording or reproducing information in which the data transmission rate or the relative motion between the recording medium and the transducer is not fixed but varies during operation. It includes adaptive speed control, variable-bit-rate encoding, and related techniques for handling fluctuating recording conditions.
[G11B 2020/1099] wherein a disc is spun at a variable speed -- G11B 2020/1099 relates to information storage or reproduction devices where a disc, such as an optical or magnetic disc, is driven at a variable rotational speed, typically to match different data rates or playback requirements. The classification covers mechanisms and control systems that adjust the disc speed during operation.
[G11B 2020/1218] wherein the formatting concerns a specific area of the disc -- The entry covers techniques for formatting a particular region of an optical storage disc, including defining zones, arranging tracks or sectors, and preparing that area for data recording. It pertains to optical recording media where the formatting operation is limited to a specified disc area rather than the whole surface.
[G11B 2020/122] Burst cutting area [BCA] -- The burst cutting area (BCA) refers to the specific region on a recording or printing medium where a rapid, high-energy burst-often from a laser or thermal source-is used to cut, perforate, or separate the material, enabling precise sheet handling or page division in printing and copying processes.
[G11B 2020/1221] cluster, i.e. a data structure which consists of a fixed number of sectors or ECC blocks -- The CPC entry G11B 2020/1221 covers a cluster, defined as a data structure composed of a set number of sectors or error-correction code (ECC) blocks, used for organizing and managing storage or transmission units within memory devices and related technologies.
[G11B 2020/1222] ECC block, i.e. a block of error correction encoded symbols which includes all parity data needed for decoding -- G11B 2020/1222 refers to an error-correction code (ECC) block, a collection of encoded symbols that includes all parity data needed to reconstruct the original information during decoding. This block is employed in digital communication and storage systems to detect and correct errors, ensuring data integrity.
[G11B 2020/1224] extent, i.e. a set of sectors which numbers form a continuous ascending sequence -- The subclass defines "extent" as a set of storage sectors whose numbers form a continuous, ascending sequence without gaps, a concept used in data processing and memory-oriented devices to describe how blocks of information are organized and accessed.
[G11B 2020/1225] frame, i.e. a subunit of a sector containing user data, e.g. a sync frame -- G11B 2020/1225 covers technologies that define, generate or process a frame, which is a subunit of a sector that carries user data such as synchronization information. It includes framing structures in magnetic, optical or digital recording and transmission systems that organize data into discrete, identifiable blocks.
[G11B 2020/1227] one layer of multilayer disc -- G11B 2020/1227 relates to optical recording media that comprise a single data-storage layer within a multilayer disc structure. It covers the design, manufacture and use of one-layer sections of DVDs, Blu-ray or similar discs where only one layer is read or written, as distinct from double- or triple-layer configurations. This code is applied to patents dealing with the optical, material, or processing aspects of such single-layer segments.
[G11B 2020/1228] middle zone or outer guard area of a multilayer disc -- The classification covers the middle-zone or outer guard area of a multilayer optical disc, including protective or reference regions that separate recording layers, provide mechanical stability, and assist tracking or servo functions.
[G11B 2020/1229] lead-in area -- The CPC entry G11B 2020/1229 designates a lead-in area as a defined portion of a print sheet that serves as a reference zone for positioning subsequent printed elements, facilitating alignment in multi-part or sequential printing operations. It includes techniques and apparatus for creating or exploiting such areas to improve registration accuracy.
[G11B 2020/1231] lead-out area -- The CPC class G11B covers magnetic or optical data storage where a recording medium moves relative to a read/write head, and the "lead-out area" refers to the portion of the medium extending beyond the recorded tracks, used as a margin for tape or disc motion, head positioning and to prevent data loss at the end of a recording.
[G11B 2020/1232] sector, i.e. the minimal addressable physical data unit -- G11B 2020/1232 concerns the definition of a sector, the minimal addressable physical data unit on a recording medium, describing how the smallest individually accessible portion of storage is identified and managed. It includes details on block size, logical addressing and the interface between the physical medium and data processing.
[G11B 2020/1234] wherein the sector is a headerless sector, i.e. it does not comprise an ID field -- G11B 2020/1234 pertains to storage media in which a sector is defined as headerless, meaning the sector does not contain an identification field. It addresses formats or methods that access data blocks without a preceding ID field, and is used for patents describing such header-free sector arrangements.
[G11B 2020/1235] session, i.e. a contiguous area having its own lead-in area, program area and lead-out area -- G11B 2020/1235 defines a "session" as a continuous segment on an information-storage medium that includes its own lead-in area, program area and lead-out area, enabling the medium to organise multiple recordings or data blocks with independently controllable start, content and termination points. This concept is used in devices that manage separate playback or processing sections within a single stored medium.
[G11B 2020/1237] recording side of a single layer medium -- G11B 2020/1237 covers the side of a single-layer recording medium that carries the stored information, where the medium has only one functional layer such as a magnetic or optical surface. It concerns the design, treatment and use of that recording face for data writing, reading or erasing.
[G11B 2020/1238] track, i.e. the entire a spirally or concentrically arranged path on which the recording marks are located -- The entry defines a track as the continuous spirally or concentrically arranged path on a recording medium that carries the data marks. It covers the geometry and layout of such paths in magnetic, optical or other storage formats where the transducer follows the track to read or write information.
[G11B 2020/1239] the track being a pregroove, e.g. the wobbled track of a recordable optical disc -- The G11B 2020/1239 subclass covers optical disc data tracks that are formed as a pregroove, meaning the spiral track is pre-shaped with a wobbled or sinusoidal pattern before recording. This pregroove is used on recordable discs to encode information such as identification data or to assist in servo control during playback. It pertains to the physical structure of the track rather than the recorded content.
[G11B 2020/1241] user area, i.e. the area of a disc where user data are to be recorded -- The user area of a disc is the portion of the recording surface reserved for storing user data, separate from the lead-in, lead-out, and servo or control zones. It comprises the tracks and sectors where files and other information are written during normal operation.
[G11B 2020/1242] the area forming one or more zones, wherein each zone is shaped like an annulus or a circular sector -- The entry defines zones on a recording medium that are bounded by one or more annular or circular-sector shapes, specifying how data areas can be arranged in concentric rings or pie-slice sections. It pertains to the layout of storage regions in devices such as magnetic or optical discs.
[G11B 2020/1244] CAV zone, in which a constant angular velocity is used -- The CPC entry G11B 2020/1244 defines a CAV (constant angular velocity) zone on a rotating recording medium, where the spindle maintains a fixed angular speed across the track. This arrangement is used in storage devices such as hard-disk drives to standardize data transfer rates and simplify control of read/write operations.
[G11B 2020/1245] CLV zone, in which a constant linear velocity is used -- G11B 2020/1245 defines a CLV zone in a recording medium, where the medium moves at a constant-linear velocity so that the linear speed remains the same across the entire radius. This ensures uniform data density and consistent transfer rates, and is employed in magnetic disks, optical discs and related storage technologies.
[G11B 2020/1247] rewritable zone, e.g. a RAM zone of a hybrid disc having ROM and RAM areas -- A rewritable zone is a RAM-type area on a hybrid disc that can be altered after manufacture, coexisting with permanent ROM regions. It provides mutable storage on the same medium, enabling data to be updated while retaining fixed content elsewhere. This arrangement is used in optical-storage technologies where both read-only and read-write functions are required.
[G11B 2020/1248] ROM zone, i.e. an unrewritable zone -- A ROM zone is an area of a read-only memory that cannot be altered after it is manufactured, used to store permanent data such as firmware or system code. The classification covers devices and methods that define, implement or protect such unrewritable sections within semiconductor or other memory technologies.
[G11B 2020/1249] wherein the bits are arranged on a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice -- G11B 2020/1249 covers information-storage or recording media in which the individual data bits are positioned on a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice, meaning each bit has six nearest neighbours forming a honeycomb pattern, as opposed to the conventional rectangular grid. This arrangement can improve packing density and signal integrity in magnetic, optical or solid-state storage devices.
[G11B 2020/1255] Fixed Block Architecture [FBA] format -- G11B 2020/1255 classifies technologies that employ a Fixed Block Architecture (FBA) format for data storage, arranging data in uniform-size blocks rather than variable-length sectors. This format is used in disk drives and other storage subsystems to simplify address translation and improve access performance.
[G11B 2020/1257] Count Key Data [CKD] format -- G11B 2020/1257 covers the Count Key Data (CKD) format, a method of encoding and organizing key data items in a structured sequence for efficient processing, storage or transmission in digital systems. The classification pertains to techniques that count, label and retrieve such data elements in devices such as scanners, readers or data recorders.
[G11B 2020/1259] with ROM/RAM areas -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/1259 pertains to data-processing devices that incorporate distinct read-only memory (ROM) and random-access memory (RAM) regions, allowing permanent storage of program code together with volatile storage for data and instructions. It includes integrated circuits, microcontrollers and similar components where the two memory types are defined within the same architecture.
[G11B 2020/1264] wherein the formatting concerns a specific kind of data -- The CPC subgroup G11B 2020/1264 pertains to inventions in which the formatting of a particular type of data-such as structured, tabular, or coded information-is a focal technical aspect, covering methods, devices or software that organise, represent or prepare that data for storage, transmission or processing.
[G11B 2020/1265] Control data, system data or management information, i.e. data used to access or process user data -- G11B 2020/1265 covers non-user data that directs the functioning of an information storage system, such as control tables, configuration parameters, indexes, access-control lists and other management information. This data is used to locate, retrieve, organize or protect user-generated content within the device.
[G11B 2020/1267] Address data -- G11B 2020/1267 covers technologies for handling address data, including the collection, storage, formatting, validation and conversion of postal or geographic addresses. It also includes methods for parsing, standardising, geocoding and maintaining address databases.
[G11B 2020/1268] Address in pregroove [ADIP] information -- The G11B 2020/1268 entry pertains to information storage or retrieval techniques that embed address data within pre-grooved tracks, such as in optical or magnetic media, allowing precise location identification of recorded blocks. It covers systems and methods for encoding, reading, or processing these address-in-pregroove (ADIP) markers to facilitate data access and management.
[G11B 2020/1269] Absolute time in pregroove [ATIP] information -- The G11B 2020/1269 entry covers techniques for embedding and retrieving absolute time data in the pregroove of optical discs, commonly referred to as ATIP information. It includes methods for encoding timestamps in the spiral track of a disc to support precise addressing, synchronization and drive identification during playback or recording. This classification is applied to patents describing how absolute timing is recorded within the pregroove structure of media such as DVDs or Blu-ray discs.
[G11B 2020/1271] the address data being stored in a subcode, e.g. in the Q channel of a CD -- The entry covers methods for storing address or pointer information within a subcode channel of an optical disc, such as the Q channel of a compact disc, allowing location referencing or navigation separate from the main audio-video data. It pertains to data-storage formats that embed such address data in dedicated subcode fields.
[G11B 2020/1272] Burst indicator subcode [BIS] -- G11B 2020/1272 defines the burst indicator subcode, a signal element used in digital communication to mark the presence, start or end of a burst of data within a transmission frame. It is employed for synchronization, error detection and adaptive coding in data storage and transmission systems.
[G11B 2020/1274] stored in pre-pits, i.e. in embossed pits, ROM marks or prepits -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/1274 pertains to data storage techniques that use pre-formed pits on a carrier, such as embossed pits, ROM marks or other pre-etched indentations, to encode information. It covers methods and devices that read or write data directly from these pre-pits without requiring additional patterning during operation.
[G11B 2020/1275] Calibration data, e.g. specific training patterns for adjusting equalizer settings or other recording or playback parameters -- G11B 2020/1275 covers the provision of calibration data such as specific training patterns that are used to adjust equalizer settings or other parameters of recording or playback devices, enabling optimal signal processing performance.
[G11B 2020/1277] for managing gaps between two recordings, e.g. control data in linking areas, run-in or run-out fields, guard or buffer zones -- This subgroup pertains to techniques that handle spaces between successive recorded data segments on a medium, such as inserting control information, linking data, run-in or run-out fields, and guard or buffer zones to ensure proper separation and transition. It includes methods for detecting, generating, or processing these gap regions to improve data integrity and playback.
[G11B 2020/1278] Physical format specifications of the record carrier, e.g. compliance with a specific standard, recording density, number of layers, start of data zone or lead-out -- G11B 2020/1278 covers the definition of physical format parameters for a record carrier, including conformity to particular standards, recording density, number of layers, the location where the data zone begins, and the structure of the lead-out area.
[G11B 2020/1279] Permanent information and control data stored in the PIC zone of a Blu-Ray disc -- G11B 2020/1279 covers methods and apparatuses for storing permanent information and control data in the PIC (Program Information and Control) zone of a Blu-Ray disc, including the encoding, structuring and retrieval of navigation, authentication and disc-management data that remain unchanged throughout the disc's lifetime.
[G11B 2020/1281] Servo information -- The G11B subgroup deals with information storage devices that rely on relative movement of a recording medium and the read/write head. G11B 2020/1281 covers servo information, i.e., the tracks, patterns or signals embedded in magnetic or optical media that provide feedback for precise head positioning and tracking during read/write operations.
[G11B 2020/1282] in embedded servo fields -- G11B 2020/1282 covers recording media that contain servo information integrated within the data tracks, known as embedded servo fields. It relates to magnetic or optical discs where positioning signals are written directly in the recording layer to guide the read/write head, enabling continuous track tracking without separate servo bands.
[G11B 2020/1284] in servo fields which split data fields -- G11B 2020/1284 pertains to storage devices that use servo mechanisms to partition the recording surface into distinct fields, separating positioning information from user data.
