It covers methods and apparatus where the servo fields are interleaved with or adjacent to data fields to improve track tracking and read/write accuracy.
[G11B 2020/1285] Status of the record carrier, e.g. space bit maps, flags indicating a formatting status or a write permission -- G11B 2020/1285 covers methods for indicating the condition of a record carrier, such as using space-bit maps or flag bits to show formatting status, write permission, or other state information. It includes techniques for storing, reading and updating these status indicators on magnetic, optical or solid-state media. This enables devices to manage access control and media health without altering the user data.
[G11B 2020/1287] Synchronisation pattern, e.g. VCO fields -- G11B 2020/1287 covers synchronization patterns used in data recording or transmission systems, notably those generated by or applied to voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO) fields to align timing between signal sources or receivers. It includes designs for generating, detecting, or processing such patterns to maintain phase coherence in digital or analog communication links.
[G11B 2020/1288] Formatting by padding empty spaces with dummy data, e.g. writing zeroes or random data when de-icing optical discs -- G11B 2020/1288 concerns techniques for formatting optical discs by inserting dummy data into otherwise empty tracks or spaces, such as writing zeroes or random patterns, particularly in processes that remove ice or other contaminants from the disc surface. The classification covers methods that use padding to stabilize data handling and improve reading reliability after de-icing operations.
[G11B 2020/1289] Formatting of user data -- G11B 2020/1289 pertains to methods and devices for formatting user data, i.e., arranging, converting, or preparing data entered by a user so that it conforms to a specific structure or presentation before storage or transmission. It includes techniques for applying templates, enforcing syntactic rules, and adjusting layout to meet application requirements.
[G11B 2020/1291] wherein the formatting serves a specific purpose -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/1291 addresses inventions in which the visual or structural formatting of a character string-such as line breaks, indentation, special symbols, fonts or markup-carries a functional meaning or enables a specific operation, rather than serving only an aesthetic purpose. This includes formats designed for data parsing, control commands or user-guided actions.
[G11B 2020/1292] Enhancement of the total storage capacity -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/1292 covers inventions that increase the overall amount of data that can be stored on a medium or system, such as by using higher-density recording, multilayer structures, advanced encoding or compression techniques, or novel media architectures. It pertains to methods and devices that enhance total storage capacity beyond existing limits.
[G11B 2020/1294] Increase of the access speed -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/1294 covers inventions that improve the speed at which data can be accessed from storage media, including hardware, software and control techniques that reduce latency or increase throughput. It includes methods for faster retrieval, access-time optimization and acceleration of read/write operations in memory, disk, tape or other storage systems.
[G11B 2020/1295] wherein the focus is on the read access speed -- This CPC subclass deals with data storage devices or methods where the primary concern is the speed at which information can be read, covering technologies that improve retrieval times from magnetic, optical, semiconductor or other memory media. It is applied to patents focusing on performance optimisation of read-access operations.
[G11B 2020/1297] wherein the focus is on the write access speed -- G11B 2020/1297 is a CPC tag covering inventions in the information-storage field where the principal technical feature evaluated or enhanced is the speed of writing data to a medium. It belongs to class G11B, which deals with storage, retrieval and processing of recorded information, and specifically highlights write-access performance aspects.
[G11B 2020/1298] Enhancement of the signal quality -- G11B 2020/1298 refers to inventions that improve the quality of signals in data recording or playback systems, including error-correction methods, noise-reduction techniques and other signal-processing enhancements.
[G11B 2020/143] 4 to 6 modulation -- G11B covers data recording, processing and transmission, with sub-entries for specific signal-processing techniques. Code 2020/143 refers to modulation schemes that use four to six discrete signal states, such as 4-level pulse-amplitude modulation or similar multi-level techniques employed to increase data rate or spectral efficiency in digital communication systems.
[G11B 2020/1434] 8 to 9 modulation -- G11B 2020/1434 covers modulation techniques that use eight to nine distinct signal levels to encode information onto a carrier for recording or transmission. It includes designs for multi-level amplitude or phase encoding that increase data density or improve error resilience in storage media.
[G11B 2020/1438] 8 to 10 modulation -- The CPC entry G11B 2020/1438 covers methods and apparatus for converting digital information into analog signals using multi-level modulation with eight to ten distinct signal states, including the encoding, decoding and signal-processing techniques required for such schemes in data storage or transmission systems.
[G11B 2020/1442] 8 to 12 modulation -- The CPC entry G11B 2020/1442 pertains to methods that encode data by using eight to twelve discrete signal levels, i.e., multi-level pulse-amplitude or phase modulation, typically employed in magnetic or optical recording and related data-storage devices. It covers the generation, detection and processing of such multi-level modulated signals.
[G11B 2020/1446] 16 to 17 modulation -- This CPC entry covers techniques that use a modulation scheme in which data is represented by transitions between the 16th and 17th levels of a code, typically in magnetic or optical recording. It relates to methods for encoding, decoding or processing information on storage media that employ this 16 to 17 modulation.
[G11B 2020/1449] 24 to 25 modulation -- The CPC entry G11B 2020/1449 pertains to printing technologies that adjust a specific process parameter between the 24 and 25 levels, typically referring to fine modulation of tonal or density output in image printing.
[G11B 2020/1453] 17PP modulation, i.e. the parity preserving RLL(1,7) code with rate 2/3 used on Blu-Ray discs -- G11B 2020/1453 covers the 17PP modulation scheme, a parity-preserving run-length limited code with constraint (1,7) and a coding rate of 2/3 used in Blu-Ray optical disc recording to improve error resilience and data density.
[G11B 2020/1457] wherein DC control is performed by calculating a digital sum value [DSV] -- The CPC entry G11B 2020/1457 covers devices or methods for controlling direct-current (DC) operation in image-forming or printing apparatus by computing a digital sum value (DSV) and using that value to regulate the DC parameters, such as voltage or current, during the process.
[G11B 2020/1461] 8 to 14 modulation, e.g. the EFM code used on CDs or mini-discs -- This subclass covers the eight-to-fourteen modulation (EFM) scheme that converts 8-bit data symbols into 14-bit channel symbols for optical recording. It is the coding method used on compact discs, MiniDiscs and similar media to control run length and ensure reliable playback.
[G11B 2020/1465] 8 to 16 modulation, e.g. the EFM+ code used on DVDs -- G11B 2020/1465 deals with encoding techniques that employ 8 to 16 level modulation, such as the Eight-to-Fourteen Modulation Plus (EFM+) used for DVD data recording. It describes the conversion of user information into channel bits for optical disc storage, enhancing error resilience and allowing higher data density.
[G11B 2020/1469] modulation code with one or more merging bits between consecutive codewords -- A modulation code that inserts one or more merging bits between successive codewords to facilitate reliable data transmission or storage, often used to improve synchronization, reduce error propagation, or meet specific channel constraints. The merging bits serve as delimiters or control symbols that aid in distinguishing adjacent codewords.
[G11B 2020/1473] modulation code without any merging bits -- This CPC entry relates to coding techniques for data modulation in information-storage devices where the code words are generated without any merging bits, meaning each data symbol is transmitted independently and not combined with adjacent bits. It is applied to magnetic, optical or other recording media to improve signal integrity and simplify decoding.
[G11B 2020/1476] Synchronisation patterns; Coping with defects thereof -- The entry pertains to printing technology and covers methods and devices for generating synchronization patterns that align printed or imaged elements, as well as techniques for detecting, correcting or otherwise coping with defects or errors in those patterns to maintain registration accuracy.
[G11B 2020/148] using error detecting or error correcting codes -- G11B 2020/148 covers inventions that employ error-detecting or error-correcting codes in data processing, transmission, or storage systems, such as algorithms, hardware or software that identify and correct bit errors to improve reliability.
[G11B 2020/1484] Codewords used in servo patterns -- The entry covers the use of codewords incorporated in servo patterns that guide the positioning and tracking of read/write heads in storage media. It includes methods for embedding, detecting, and interpreting these control words within magnetic or optical servo tracks to ensure accurate data access.
[G11B 2020/1823] wherein a flag is set when errors are detected or qualified -- The code G11B 2020/1823 relates to error-handling features in data processing or storage systems, specifically to mechanisms that set a flag or indicator when an error is detected or qualified. It covers methods, devices or control logic that signal such conditions to trigger corrective actions, logging or user notification.
[G11B 2020/1826] wherein a defect list or error map is generated -- G11B 2020/1826 covers methods and apparatuses that generate a defect list or an error map for a data storage medium, typically during testing, formatting, or routine operation, to identify and record locations of physical or logical errors for later correction or avoidance. This classification applies to systems that automatically detect and report such defects in magnetic, optical, or solid-state storage devices.
[G11B 2020/183] wherein at least one additional attempt is made to read or write the data when a first attempt is unsuccessful -- The code covers devices or methods that, after a failed initial read or write operation, make at least one additional attempt to access the data. It applies to error-recovery strategies in storage or transmission systems, such as retry logic, automatic repeat request, or re-reading mechanisms.
[G11B 2020/1836] using a Reed Solomon [RS] code -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/1836 relates to inventions that employ Reed-Solomon error-correcting codes, which are used to detect and correct errors in digital data streams, storage media, or communication links. It resides in the G11B group covering error detection, correction and coding methods.
[G11B 2020/184] using a cross-interleaved Reed Solomon [CIRC] -- G11B 2020/184 covers the use of cross-interleaved Reed-Solomon coding (CIRC) for error detection and correction in digital data storage or transmission. It includes methods that interleave Reed-Solomon blocks to improve resistance to burst errors, as employed in compact disc audio and similar optical media.
[G11B 2020/1843] using a cyclic redundancy check [CRC] -- This group covers inventions that employ a cyclic redundancy check to detect or correct errors in digital data during transmission, storage or processing, such as in communication protocols, memory devices and data handling systems.
[G11B 2020/1846] using a picket code, i.e. a code in which a long distance code [LDC] is arranged as an array and columns containing burst indicator subcode [BIS] are multiplexed for erasure decoding -- G11B covers information storage and retrieval, and this entry relates to error-control coding techniques. It describes a picket code where a long-distance code is arranged as an array and columns that carry burst-indicator subcodes are multiplexed to enable erasure decoding, improving reliability of stored or transmitted data.
[G11B 2020/185] using an low density parity check [LDPC] code -- G11B 2020/185 relates to the use of low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes in information storage or transmission systems. It covers methods and devices that implement LDPC coding for error detection and correction, including encoding, decoding, and system integration. The class is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification for inventions that improve the reliability of digital data.
[G11B 2020/1853] using a product code which has inner and outer parity symbols -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/1853 covers methods and devices for constructing product codes that incorporate both inner and outer parity symbols to detect and correct errors, typically applied in data transmission and storage. Such codes improve reliability by allowing hierarchical error checking at multiple levels.
[G11B 2020/1856] using a turbo code -- G11B 2020/1856 covers methods, devices and systems that employ turbo codes for error correction in data transmission or storage. It includes the generation, encoding, decoding and performance optimization of turbo-coded signals, typically in communication, wireless and digital broadcasting applications.
[G11B 2020/1859] wherein a trellis is used for decoding the error correcting code -- This CPC entry relates to information processing where a trellis structure is employed to decode error-correcting codes, covering methods and apparatus that implement trellis-based algorithms such as Viterbi or BCJR for correcting transmission or storage errors.
[G11B 2020/1863] wherein the Viterbi algorithm is used for decoding the error correcting code -- The G11B 2020/1863 entry covers digital communication or data-processing devices in which the Viterbi algorithm is employed to decode error-correcting codes, typically convolutional or trellis-coded sequences. It relates to methods that find the most likely transmitted data path by evaluating possible state transitions and selecting the optimal sequence, thereby improving reliability in noisy transmission environments.
[G11B 2020/1869] Preventing ageing phenomena from causing data loss, e.g. by monitoring the age of record carriers or by recognising wear, and by copying information elsewhere when a record carrier becomes unreliable -- The G11B 2020/1869 subclass covers techniques for detecting ageing or wear of data-record carriers and preventing loss by monitoring their condition and automatically copying information to a more reliable medium when the original becomes unreliable.
[G11B 2020/1873] Temporary defect structures for write-once discs, e.g. TDDS, TDMA or TDFL -- The CPC subclass G11B 2020/1873 covers temporary defect structures employed in write-once optical discs to define and manage areas that are intentionally marked as defective for data encoding, such as temporary defect data structures (TDDS), temporary defect management areas (TDMA) or temporary defect flag lists (TDFL). It pertains to the creation, control and utilization of these provisional defect markers for organizing or protecting recorded information on write-once media.
[G11B 2020/1893] using linear replacement to relocate data from a defective block to a non-contiguous spare area, e.g. with a secondary defect list [SDL] -- A method for managing storage media errors that moves data from a defective block to a non-contiguous spare area using linear replacement, guided by a secondary defect list (SDL). The technique updates the defect record and redirects future accesses to the relocated data, improving reliability without requiring contiguous spare space. This approach is defined under the cooperative patent classification G11B 2020/1893.
[G11B 2020/1896] using skip or slip replacement to relocate data from a defective block to the next usable block, e.g. with a primary defect list [PDL] -- The entry G11B 2020/1896 relates to error-handling techniques in data storage where data stored in a defective block is moved to the next usable block by means of a skip or slip replacement method, often guided by a primary defect list that tracks faulty locations. This approach enables continued operation of memory or disk devices despite block failures.
[G11B 20/22] for reducing distortions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Strategies for reducing distortions, i.e. occasionally occurring degradations of the signal quality.
[G11B 20/225] for reducing wow or flutter  by controlling the speed of the record carrier  G11B15/46 ,  G11B19/28 -- G11B 20/225 pertains to techniques for minimizing wow and flutter in recording systems by regulating the speed of the record carrier, integrating aspects from both mechanical control (G11B15/46) and electronic servo mechanisms (G11B19/28).
[G11B 20/24] for reducing noise -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Strategies for reducing noise, i.e. systematically occuring degradations of the signal quality.  Obsolete technology
[G11B 21/00] Head arrangements not specific to the method of recording or reproducing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any details of head arrangements for any type of moving record carrier which are not already covered by subgroups specific to a particular method of recording.  G11B21/00 has two main areas: Driving and Moving ( G11B21/02 ) and Supporting ( G11B21/16 ).
[G11B 21/003] Disposition of fixed heads, e.g. for scanning, selecting or following of tracks -- G11B 21/003 covers the arrangement and positioning of fixed transducer heads used to scan, select or follow tracks on a recording medium, where the heads do not move relative to the medium. It falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer, specifically head configurations that are independent of the recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 21/006] for track following -- G11B 21/006 covers head arrangements that use a track-following mechanism to keep the transducer aligned with the recorded track as the carrier moves, ensuring proper positioning during read or write operations. It belongs to the physics branch of information storage that relies on relative motion between carrier and transducer, without being tied to any specific recording or reproducing technique.
[G11B 21/02] Driving or moving of heads -- G11B 21/02 covers the mechanisms that drive or move the read/write heads in storage devices where a record carrier and transducer move relative to each other. It includes actuation, positioning and control systems for head motion, independent of the specific recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 21/022] Programmed access in sequence to indexed parts of operating record carriers -- The code G11B 21/022 covers systems where an operating record carrier-such as a magnetic tape, drum or similar medium-is accessed in a programmed sequence to indexed sections, allowing predetermined portions to be read or written without continuous searching. This arrangement pertains to head configurations that are not tied to a specific recording or reproducing method but enable selective, indexed access to stored data.
[G11B 21/025] of rotating discs -- G11B 21/025 pertains to head arrangements for information storage systems that use rotating discs, focusing on aspects not specific to the method of recording or reproducing. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European and United States patent offices.
[G11B 21/027] of tapes -- G11B 21/027 pertains to head arrangements for tape storage systems, focusing on configurations and mechanisms that are not specific to a particular recording or reproducing method. This classification is part of the broader category of information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 21/03] for correcting time base error -- The G11B 21/03 subclass covers head arrangements used in information-storage devices that correct time-base errors, such as timing deviations between the moving record carrier and the transducer, without being limited to any particular recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 21/04] Automatic feed mechanism producing a transducing traverse of the head in a direction which cuts across the direction of travel of the recording medium, e.g. helical scan -- The subclass concerns automatic feeding systems that move the recording medium while the transducing head traverses a path crossing the medium's travel direction, exemplified by helical-scan arrangements in magnetic recording devices.
[G11B 21/043] for stationary discs -- G11B 21/043 covers head arrangements employed with stationary disc media, where the disc remains fixed and the read/write transducer moves relative to it. The classification includes mechanical or optical systems for accessing data on non-rotating discs such as optically readable plates or magnetic disks held still during operation.
[G11B 21/046] details of the feed mechanism -- G11B 21/046 covers the design and operation of feed mechanisms that move the recording medium relative to the read/write transducer in storage devices. It includes rollers, capstans, belts, or other arrangements that control speed, tension, and positioning of the carrier during recording or playback. The classification focuses on mechanical details rather than the recording method itself.
[G11B 21/06] the record carrier having means to ensure traverse movement of the head -- The subclass relates to record carriers that incorporate features forcing the read-write head to move transversely across the medium during operation, such as built-in guides, rails or actuators, to maintain correct alignment and tracking of data.
[G11B 21/08] Track changing or selecting -- G11B 21/08 covers mechanisms that move a transducer or head to different tracks, select a specific track, or track changes in position on a storage medium, without specifying the recording or reproducing technique. It applies to storage systems where the carrier and the head move relative to each other, such as magnetic or optical disks, and focuses on head-arrangement functions for track selection rather than the data encoding method.
[G11B 21/081] Access to indexed tracks or parts of continuous track -- The subclass covers apparatus or methods that retrieve data by moving to specific indexed sections of a continuously recorded medium, such as magnetic tape, where the access points are defined by track numbers or positions rather than by the recording technique itself.
[G11B 21/083] on discs -- The CPC subgroup G11B 21/083 covers head arrangements used with disc-type information carriers where the specific recording or reproducing method is not defined. It relates to the design of transducer heads that move relative to rotating discs, applicable to magnetic, optical or other disc-based storage formats.
[G11B 21/085] with track following of accessed part -- G11B 21/085 covers head configurations for magnetic or optical data carriers where the transducer follows the track of the portion being accessed, without being tied to a particular recording or playback technique. The arrangement ensures the head aligns with the desired track as the carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 21/086] on tapes -- The G11B 21/086 subclass covers tape-based recording and playback devices that use head arrangements without being tied to a particular recording or reproducing technique. It includes magnetic, optical or other heads positioned to interact with a moving tape carrier, focusing on the mechanical and geometric layout rather than the specific signal-processing method employed.
[G11B 21/088] with track following of accessed part -- G11B 21/088 covers head designs for storage devices where the transducer follows the track of the accessed part of the moving record carrier, maintaining alignment during reading or writing while the carrier moves relative to the head.
[G11B 21/10] Track finding or aligning by moving the head -- G11B 21/10 covers head arrangements that locate or align data tracks by moving the read/write head instead of relying on the recording or reproducing method. It applies to information-storage devices where the head is displaced to follow or locate tracks on a moving carrier, such as magnetic tapes or disks, enabling precise positioning for data access. This subclass falls under information storage based on relative motion between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 21/103] on tapes -- The code G11B 21/103 refers to head arrangements for magnetic or optical tape systems where the design of the recording-playback head is described without tying it to a particular recording or reproducing method. It falls under physics, information storage, storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 21/106] on disks -- G11B 21/106 pertains to head arrangements for information storage systems, specifically those used with disk media. This classification covers the design and operation of read-write heads in devices like hard drives or optical disc drives, focusing on their interaction with rotating disk surfaces.
[G11B 21/12] Raising and lowering -- The G11B 21/12 class covers mechanisms for raising and lowering recording or playback heads relative to the record carrier, independent of the specific recording or reproducing method. It includes head positioning systems that move the head up or down to engage or disengage contact with the medium.
[G11B 21/14] manually -- G11B 21/14 pertains to manually operated head arrangements in information storage systems, where data is recorded and retrieved based on the relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer. This classification covers devices that require human intervention for operation.
[G11B 21/16] Supporting the heads -- G11B 21/16 relates to the mechanical structures that support or position transducer heads in devices that store information by relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer. It covers brackets, frames, guides and other elements that maintain head alignment and spacing, without regard to the specific recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 21/18] while the head is moving -- G11B 21/18 covers head arrangements used in information-storage devices where the transducer head moves relative to the record carrier, without being tied to a particular recording or playback method. It falls under the physics subclass of information storage based on relative motion between carrier and head, describing configurations applicable while the head is in motion.
[G11B 21/20] while the head is in operative position but stationary or permitting minor movements to follow irregularities in surface of record carrier -- G11B 21/20 covers head arrangements where the transducer head stays in its operative position but is either fixed or allowed limited motion to follow surface irregularities of the record carrier. These configurations are not tied to a specific recording or reproducing method and belong to information-storage devices that rely on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 21/21] with provision for maintaining desired spacing of head from record carrier, e.g. fluid-dynamic spacing, slider -- The subclass G11B 21/21 covers head assemblies for magnetic recording that incorporate a system to keep a defined clearance between the head and the rotating disk, such as air-bearing sliders or other fluid-dynamic spacing methods. It applies to any head design where the spacing is maintained independently of the recording or playback technique.
[G11B 21/22] while the head is out of operative position -- G11B 21/22 pertains to the physical design of storage devices in which the read/write head is positioned out of its normal operative location, such as being parked, retracted or otherwise disengaged from the record carrier. The classification covers head arrangements that are not tied to a specific recording or reproducing method but address protection, standby or movement-related functions while the head is out of operative position.
[G11B 21/24] Head support adjustments -- G11B 21/24 concerns devices in which a transducer head is supported by a mechanism that allows its position, angle or pressure to be adjusted, applicable to systems where data are stored by relative movement between a record carrier and the head, and independent of the specific recording or reproducing technique.
[G11B 21/26] Means for interchange or replacement of head or head element -- The subclass G11B 21/26 covers devices and mechanisms that enable a read/write head or its constituent element to be detached, swapped or repositioned within a storage system that relies on relative movement between the record carrier and the transducer. It includes interchangeable modules, mounting frames and other fixtures that facilitate easy replacement or upgrading of the head assembly independent of any specific recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 2209/00] Recording or reproducing using a method not covered elsewhere in this subclass -- G11B 2209/00 covers recording or reproducing techniques that do not fall under any of the specific methods defined in earlier subclasses of the relative-movement information-storage group. It pertains to any unconventional or novel approach for writing or reading data where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer, but the method is not otherwise classified. This subclass serves as a catch-all for such unaddressed processes.
[G11B 2209/02] transducing on or investigating record carriers or information recording transducers or systems by using near-field interactions -- G11B 2209/02 pertains to the use of near-field interactions for transducing or investigating record carriers and information recording systems, focusing on methods not covered elsewhere in this subclass. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office.
[G11B 2220/00] Record carriers by type -- G11B 2220/00 covers classification of record carriers according to their physical type, grouping media such as magnetic, optical, semiconductor or paper-based storage elements used for information recording and retrieval. It is part of the broader group on information storage based on relative movement between the carrier and transducer.
[G11B 2220/17] Card-like record carriers -- The G11B 2220/17 subclass covers card-shaped record carriers, such as magnetic stripe cards, smart cards and similar flat media that store information on a surface read by a transducer moving relative to the card. It includes the physical form, materials and basic features of card-like carriers used for data storage.
[G11B 2220/20] Disc-shaped record carriers -- G11B 2220/20 covers disc-shaped record carriers, such as magnetic or optical discs, that store information by relative movement between the rotating disc and a read/write transducer. The class includes formats like floppy disks, CDs, DVDs and similar circular media used for data, audio or video recording.
[G11B 2220/21] characterised in that the disc is of read-only, rewritable, or recordable type -- The G11B 2220/21 subclass concerns disc-type record carriers that are characterised as read-only, rewritable or recordable. It classifies the functional type of the disc within information-storage systems where the carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 2220/211] Discs having both read-only and rewritable or recordable areas containing application data -- G11B 2220/211 pertains to discs that incorporate both read-only sections and rewritable or recordable sections, the latter capable of being altered after manufacture, with application data stored across these mixed-type areas.
[G11B 2220/213] Read-only discs -- G11B 2220/213 covers read-only optical discs such as CD-ROMs, DVD-ROMs and Blu-ray discs, where data are stored permanently on a rotating carrier and accessed by a stationary laser transducer. The class addresses the physical structure, material composition and manufacturing methods of these non-rewritable media.
[G11B 2220/215] Recordable discs -- G11B 2220/215 covers recordable discs where data are stored by changing the physical or magnetic properties of a disc-shaped carrier, such as CD-R, DVD-R, or Blu-ray recordable media. The classification pertains to discs that can be written by a transducer after manufacturing, enabling subsequent retrieval of the recorded information.
[G11B 2220/216] Rewritable discs -- G11B 2220/216 refers to patents concerning rewritable discs, a type of record carrier in which data is stored by moving the disc relative to a read/write transducer. The class covers optical media such as CD-RWs, DVD-RWs and Blu-ray RE that can be erased and re-recorded through laser-induced phase changes.
[G11B 2220/218] Write-once discs -- G11B 2220/218 covers write-once discs, such as CD-R and DVD-R, where data is recorded permanently in a single pass and cannot be erased or rewritten. These media are classified under record carriers by type within the information-storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer domain.
[G11B 2220/23] characterised in that the disc has a specific layer structure -- The CPC subclass G11B 2220/23 covers optical discs whose design specifies a particular multilayer structure, defining the arrangement and composition of layers such as data, reflective, and protective coatings. It focuses on how this layered architecture influences the disc's optical or mechanical performance during read-write operations.
[G11B 2220/232] Double-sided discs, i.e. two recording layers accessed from opposite sides -- This subclass covers double-sided discs that incorporate two distinct recording layers, each accessed from opposite faces of the carrier. It relates to information-storage devices where a transducer moves relative to the disc and the carrier type is a record carrier with bidirectional access.
[G11B 2220/235] Multilayer discs, i.e. multiple recording layers accessed from the same side -- The code covers discs that contain two or more recording layers stacked in a single substrate and accessed from the same side, such as multilayer optical or magnetic media where a single transducer moves over the surface to read or write on each layer.
[G11B 2220/237] having exactly two recording layers -- G11B 2220/237 covers information storage media where the record carrier comprises exactly two distinct recording layers, such as dual-layer optical discs or two-layer magnetic tapes, enabling higher capacity by stacking data-bearing layers while maintaining a single carrier structure.
[G11B 2220/25] characterised in that the disc is based on a specific recording technology -- This subclass covers disc-type storage media where the disc employs a particular recording technology, such as phase-change, magneto-optical, or holographic, rather than generic disc formats. It pertains to the relationship between the moving disc and the transducer that reads or writes data.
[G11B 2220/2504] Holographic discs -- Holographic discs are optical storage media that record data throughout the volume of the disc using holographic techniques, allowing high-density information retrieval. They rely on the relative movement between the rotating disc and a transducer that reads and writes holograms, enabling fast access and large capacities.
[G11B 2220/2508] Magnetic discs -- G11B 2220/2508 designates magnetic discs employed as record carriers in storage systems where the disc moves relative to the transducer, such as hard-disk platters and floppy-disk media, and includes the physical structure, materials and configurations of these magnetic media.
[G11B 2220/2512] Floppy disks -- The CPC code G11B 2220/2512 designates floppy disks as a type of record carrier within information storage based on relative movement between a carrier and a transducer. It is classified under physics, specifically the sub-field of magnetic media that move relative to the read-write head.
[G11B 2220/2516] Hard disks -- The G11B 2220/2516 entry categorises hard-disk technologies that use a rotating magnetic record carrier paired with a transducer head for data storage. It includes specifications of disk materials, geometry, spindle mechanisms and related design features of hard-disk drives.
[G11B 2220/252] Patterned or quantised magnetic media, i.e. bits are stored in predefined single domain elements -- This subclass covers magnetic storage media where the magnetic bits are confined to individually defined single-domain elements arranged in a regular pattern, allowing each bit to be quantized and isolated from neighboring areas. Such media use pre-fabricated magnetic domains rather than continuous films to store data.
[G11B 2220/2525] Magneto-optical [MO] discs -- Magneto-optical discs are data carriers that store information by altering the magnetic orientation of a thin film using a laser-heated spot, while reading is performed optically as the disc rotates past the transducer. The technology combines magnetic recording with optical reading, fitting the CPC class for information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 2220/2529] Mini-discs -- Mini-discs are small, magnetically recorded discs that store audio or data by moving a rotating record carrier relative to a magnetic transducer. They belong to the subclass of information storage devices that rely on relative motion between the carrier and read/write head, covering design, manufacturing and playback methods for these compact, removable media.
[G11B 2220/2533] MO disc using magnetic super resolution, i.e., the magnetic mark is smaller than the laser spot size -- The CPC subclass G11B 2220/2533 pertains to magneto-optical discs that employ magnetic super-resolution, producing magnetic marks smaller than the laser spot to increase data density. It is situated under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer → record carriers by type.
[G11B 2220/2537] Optical discs -- G11B 2220/2537 covers optical discs used as record carriers, where data are stored by reading and writing light on a rotating medium. The class includes CDs, DVDs, Blu-ray and similar discs that rely on a laser-based transducer interacting with a moving substrate.
[G11B 2220/2541] Blu-ray discs -- G11B 2220/2541 covers Blu-ray discs, a type of optical record carrier that stores data by moving a laser beam relative to the rotating disc surface. These discs use a 405 nm wavelength laser and high-density pits to achieve capacities from 25 GB to 100 GB or more.
[G11B 2220/2545] CDs -- G11B 2220/2545 covers compact discs, a type of optical information storage where a polymer or polycarbonate disc holds digital data that is read by a laser. The entry belongs to the physics hierarchy under information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 2220/255] CD-I, i.e. CD-interactive -- The code G11B 2220/255 classifies CD-interactive (CD-I) media, a compact disc format that stores audio or video together with data that enables interactive menus, games or navigation. It is placed under record carriers by type in the information-storage physics hierarchy.
[G11B 2220/2554] CD-V [CD-Video], CDV, or CD+V, as defined in IEC 61104 -- G11B 2220/2554 covers CD-Video (CD-V or CD+V) discs, an optical format that stores both video and digital audio/data on a single record carrier as defined in IEC 61104. The subclass falls under information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer, specifically the category of record carriers by type.
[G11B 2220/2558] CD-XA format, i.e. eXtended architecture extension of the CD-ROM standard -- The G11B 2220/2558 entry covers the CD-XA format, an extended architecture of the CD-ROM standard that stores both digital data and compressed audio on the same optical disc, allowing simultaneous playback and data access. It falls under the physics branch for information storage, specifically record carriers defined by the relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 2220/2562] DVDs [digital versatile discs] -- G11B 2220/2562 covers DVDs, a type of digital versatile disc that stores data optically on a rotating disc read by a laser transducer. The classification falls under physics, information storage based on relative motion between the disc and read head, specifically record carriers by type.
[G11B 2220/2566] DVDs belonging to the minus family, i.e. -R, -RW, -VR -- G11B 2220/2566 covers DVD discs of the minus family, namely recordable (-R), rewritable (-RW) and variant (-VR) formats, where data is recorded by creating pits or land marks on a dye-based record carrier. The classification falls under physics → information storage → information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer → record carriers by type.
[G11B 2220/257] DVDs belonging to the plus family, i.e. +R, +RW, +VR -- This subclass covers DVD optical discs that use the plus recording technology, including read-only (+R), read-write (+RW) and video-recordable (+VR) formats, defined by the way pits are formed on the record carrier and detected by the transducer.
[G11B 2220/2575] DVD-RAMs -- G11B 2220/2575 covers DVD-RAM discs, a type of record carrier in which data are stored on a rewritable optical disk that moves relative to a fixed read/write laser. These media use phase-change or magnetic alloy technology to allow multiple write-erase cycles while maintaining random access.
[G11B 2220/2579] HD-DVDs [high definition DVDs] -- G11B 2220/2579 designates HD-DVDs, a class of high definition optical discs that store video and data using a laser-read system where the disc moves relative to the transducer. They belong to the information-storage-by-relative-movement category of the CPC.
[G11B 2220/2583] wherein two standards are used on a single disc, e.g. one DVD section and one CD section -- The entry defines optical discs that incorporate two different recording standards on a single medium, for example a disc that contains a DVD-type region together with a CD-type region. It covers record carriers where multiple standards coexist on one disc.
[G11B 2220/2587] Laser Discs -- Laser discs are optical storage media that use a rotating reflective disc read by a laser beam to retrieve encoded information. The format stores video or data in concentric tracks, with the relative movement between the disc and the transducer enabling access. This classification covers the physical medium and its type as a record carrier.
[G11B 2220/2591] SFFO discs, i.e. small form factor optical discs -- SFFO (small form-factor optical) discs are compact, disc-shaped optical storage media that use a laser transducer to read and write data while the disc rotates relative to the sensor, enabling high-capacity storage in formats smaller than standard CDs or DVDs. This classification covers the design, dimensions, and functional aspects of such miniature optical record carriers.
[G11B 2220/2595] Super-resolution optical discs, i.e. optical discs wherein the size of marks is below the optical diffraction limit -- The entry covers optical discs that employ super-resolution techniques to record data marks smaller than the optical diffraction limit, using methods such as multilayer structures, near-field optics or advanced signal processing to increase storage density. It is situated under physics → information storage → relative movement between carrier and transducer → record carriers by type.
[G11B 2220/40] Combinations of multiple record carriers -- G11B 2220/40 designates inventions that employ two or more record carriers arranged together to store or transmit data, such as combined magnetic tape and optical disc modules or linked magnetic cards. The classification falls under information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer and addresses the mechanical and electronic integration of multiple media.
[G11B 2220/41] Flat as opposed to hierarchical combination, e.g. library of tapes or discs, CD changer, or groups of record carriers that together store one title -- The code G11B 2220/41 designates flat, non-hierarchical assemblies of record carriers such as tape libraries, disc stacks, CD changers or grouped media that together hold a single title, as opposed to hierarchical systems that organise carriers in multiple levels. It covers configurations where the carriers lie in a single plane or simple array rather than structured tiers.
[G11B 2220/412] Distributed storage methods, i.e. the system may autonomously determine for a storage device that provides enough storage capacity for recording -- G11B 2220/412 relates to distributed storage techniques in which a system automatically selects a storage device that has sufficient free capacity for recording, handling allocation across multiple media without user intervention.
[G11B 2220/415] Redundant array of inexpensive disks [RAID] systems -- This subclass covers RAID technology, where multiple low-cost disk drives are combined to provide data redundancy, performance improvement, or both. It includes methods for organizing, managing, and accessing data across the array to protect against drive failure.
[G11B 2220/417] Redundant array of inexpensive tapes [RAIT] systems -- Redundant array of inexpensive tapes (RAIT) systems employ multiple low-cost magnetic tape drives arranged to work together, supplying data redundancy and improved access speed much like RAID does for disk drives. The configuration distributes data across several tapes so that a failure of one carrier does not cause data loss, and parallel operation can raise throughput for large-scale information storage based on relative movement between the carrier and transducer.
[G11B 2220/45] Hierarchical combination of record carriers, e.g. HDD for fast access, optical discs for long term storage or tapes for backup -- This subclass covers systems that use multiple types of storage media arranged hierarchically, where faster but smaller-capacity media like hard-disk drives handle active data and slower, higher-capacity media such as optical disks or magnetic tapes provide long-term or backup storage. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 2220/455] said record carriers being in one device and being used as primary and secondary/backup media, e.g. HDD-DVD combo device, or as source and target media, e.g. PC and portable player -- The classification covers devices that combine two different record carriers in a single unit or use one carrier as both source and target, such as a hard-disk-DVD combo serving as primary and backup media, or a PC acting as source and a portable player as target.
[G11B 2220/60] Solid state media -- G11B 2220/60 designates solid-state media, data storage devices that use a fixed, non-moving record carrier such as flash memory, EEPROM or other semiconductor-based media. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer, specifically the record carriers by type sub-category.
[G11B 2220/61] wherein solid state memory is used for storing A/V content -- The subclass G11B 2220/61 covers information-storage systems that employ solid-state memory instead of moving media for storing audio-visual content, fitting within the broader category of information storage based on relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer.
[G11B 2220/63] wherein solid state memory is used as a supplementary storage medium to store auxiliary data for detecting or correcting errors on a main storage medium -- This subclass covers storage systems that employ solid-state memory as an auxiliary medium to hold data used for error detection or correction of a primary storage device. It is relevant when the main medium relies on relative motion between carrier and transducer, and the solid-state component provides supplemental error-control information.
[G11B 2220/65] wherein solid state memory is used for storing indexing information or metadata -- The G11B 2220/65 subclass covers technologies that employ solid-state memory to hold indexing data or metadata, rather than using the moving record carrier itself for such information. It applies to storage systems where the retrieval or organization of recorded data is facilitated by electronic tags stored in non-volatile memory, enhancing access speed and reliability. This falls under the broader category of information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer, specifically focusing on record carriers by type.
[G11B 2220/652] said memory being attached to the recording medium -- The code G11B 2220/652 covers devices in which a memory element is physically attached to the recording medium, such as a memory chip bonded to magnetic tape, optical disc or similar carriers, so that data can be stored directly on the carrier itself. This falls under physics → information storage → information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer → record carriers by type.
[G11B 2220/655] Memory in cassette [MIC] -- The CPC subclass G11B 2220/655 covers memory devices that use a cassette format, typically magnetic tape cassettes, where data is stored by relative movement between the tape and transducer. It addresses the design, operation and integration of cassette-based recording media in information storage systems.
[G11B 2220/657] Memory in disc [MID] -- G11B 2220/657 covers disc-type memory devices where the record carrier itself functions as the storage medium, such as magnetically or optically encoded discs that retain data without requiring a separate transducer for each bit. It includes designs that integrate the storage and retrieval mechanisms within the disc structure.
[G11B 2220/80] Indexing information stored in optical or magnetic or other strip attached to cassette or disc, e.g. barcodes attached to a recording medium -- The G11B 2220/80 subclass covers methods and apparatuses for indexing data that is recorded on an optical, magnetic or other strip physically attached to a cassette, disc or similar medium, such as barcodes or other markings that uniquely identify the stored information. This falls under record carriers by type within information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 2220/90] Tape-like record carriers -- The G11B 2220/90 subclass covers tape-like record carriers, which are flexible, elongated media on which data is stored by moving the tape relative to a transducer. It includes magnetic, optical, and other tape formats used for recording, playback, and archival purposes. This subclass groups all types of tape-shaped carriers within the broader category of information storage based on relative movement.
[G11B 2220/91] Helical scan format, wherein tracks are slightly tilted with respect to tape direction, e.g. VHS, DAT, DVC, AIT or exabyte -- G11B 2220/91 covers helical scan recording where the tracks are slightly tilted relative to the tape travel direction, a technique used in formats such as VHS, DAT, DVC, AIT and Exabyte. It is placed in the physics branch of information-storage technologies, specifically under record carriers by type.
[G11B 2220/913] Digital audio tape [DAT] format -- The code G11B 2220/913 covers the digital audio tape (DAT) format, a magnetic recording system that stores digitally encoded audio signals on a moving tape using a rotating head or similar transducer. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 2220/916] Digital data storage [DDS] format -- G11B 2220/916 is a CPC subclass that addresses digital data storage (DDS) formats used on record carriers whose motion relative to the transducer is the basis for information retrieval. It groups technologies that define how digital information is encoded, organized and accessed on moving media such as magnetic or optical disks.
[G11B 2220/93] Longitudinal format, wherein tracks are in the direction of the tape, read with a static head, e.g. DCC -- A longitudinal-format tape system in which the recorded tracks run parallel to the tape's length and are accessed by a stationary read head, such as the Digital Compact Cassette. This falls under information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer, classified as G11B 2220/93.
[G11B 2220/95] Serpentine format, wherein a single track or group of tracks traverses the tape plural times from one end to the other -- The classification covers tape recording formats where a single track or group of tracks is laid out in a serpentine pattern, so the recording head passes over the same physical track multiple times as the tape moves back and forth between its ends. This arrangement allows multiple data tracks on one linear tape while using the same path repeatedly.
[G11B 2220/953] Digital linear tape [DLT] format -- G11B 2220/953 covers the digital linear tape (DLT) format, a magnetic tape storage system in which data are recorded sequentially along a linear track on the tape. The classification addresses the physical tape medium, recording method, and format specifications used for high-capacity data backup and archival.
[G11B 2220/956] Linear tape open [LTO] format -- G11B 2220/956 covers the Linear Tape Open (LTO) format, a magnetic tape storage technology in which data is recorded linearly on an open-ended cartridge. The classification pertains to record carriers of this type, designed for high-capacity backup and archival applications.
[G11B 23/00] Record carriers not specific to the method of recording or reproducing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Materials for record carriers -> G11B5/62 , G11B7/241 - Manufacture of record carriers -> G11B5/84 , G11B7/26  **Glossary:** - Form factor: the size of a cartridge
[G11B 23/0007] Circuits or methods for reducing noise, for correction of distortion, or for changing density of recorded information,  volume compression or expansion circuits  per se   H03G7/00 -- G11B 23/0007 covers electronic circuits and techniques that directly reduce noise, correct distortion, or modify the recorded signal density in storage systems where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer. It includes volume compression and expansion circuits used to adjust playback levels without reference to a particular recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 23/0014] record carriers not specifically of filamentary or web form  G11B23/0057  takes precedence -- G11B 23/0014 covers record carriers that are not limited to filamentary or web forms, such as solid discs, plates, or other shapes, used in information storage systems where data is recorded by relative motion between the carrier and a transducer. It falls under physics - information storage - information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer. The more specific subclass G11B23/0057 takes precedence.
[G11B 23/0021] discs -- Discs are record carriers in which the storage medium moves relative to a transducer, covering any type of disc-magnetic, optical, or otherwise-without tying the classification to a specific recording or reproducing method. This subclass groups all such rotating-media formats used for information storage.
[G11B 23/0028] Details -- G11B 23/0028 pertains to detailed aspects of record carriers that are not specific to the method of recording or reproducing, focusing on elements such as structure, materials, and manufacturing processes. This classification is part of the broader category of information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 23/0035] means incorporated in the disc, e.g. hub, to enable its guiding, loading or driving  means for driving the head  G11B21/06 ; guiding the disc for centering or locking  G11B17/022 ; turntables or spindles for driving  G11B19/2009 -- G11B 23/0035 pertains to mechanisms within discs, such as hubs, that facilitate guiding, loading, or driving. This includes specific means for driving the head, centering or locking the disc, and turntables or spindles for driving, integrating various aspects of disc manipulation and control in storage devices.
[G11B 23/0042] with provision for auxiliary features  sensing such features  G11B17/00 ,  G11B19/02 -- This subclass addresses information-storage devices where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer and includes auxiliary features that are sensed-such as detecting tape speed, tension, head alignment or environmental conditions-using mechanisms defined in related subclasses G11B 17/00 and G11B 19/02, integrating these sensing functions within the storage system.
[G11B 23/005] flexible discs  G11B23/0035  takes precedence -- G11B 23/005 classifies flexible disc record carriers used for information storage where the disc moves relative to a transducer, without tying the carrier to any particular recording or reproducing method. It falls under physics → information storage → information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer, and is subordinate to G11B 23/0035.
[G11B 23/0057] Intermediate mediums, i.e. mediums provided with an information structure not specific to the method of reproducing or duplication such as matrixes for mechanical pressing of an information structure  for record carriers with directly readable mechanical information  G11B3/685 ; record carriers having a relief information structure provided with or included in layers not specific for a single reproducing method; apparatus or processes specially adapted for their manufacture -- The entry covers intermediate media that carry a physical information pattern independent of any particular reproduction technique, such as matrices used for mechanically pressing data or relief structures embedded in multilayer carriers, and includes specialized equipment and processes for making these media.
[G11B 23/0064] mediums or carriers characterised by the selection of the material -- G11B 23/0064 covers storage media whose defining feature is the selection of the material used for the record carrier, independent of the recording or playback method. It includes solid, liquid or composite substrates chosen for properties such as magnetic, optical, or electrical behavior, and applies to any carrier not otherwise limited by a specific recording technique.
[G11B 23/0071] additional layers for lubrication or wear protection  lubricating means not integrated in the record carrier structure  G11B23/50 -- G11B 23/0071 classifies the use of separate lubrication or wear-protective layers applied to a record carrier in data-storage devices that rely on relative motion between the carrier and a transducer. The layers are added as distinct coatings rather than being built into the carrier's structure, and the classification belongs to the broader group G11B 23/50.
[G11B 23/0078] information structure layers using metallic or dielectric coatings -- G11B 23/0078 covers record carriers that incorporate layered structures formed from metallic or dielectric coatings to store information. The layers serve as functional or protective films that enable or enhance data encoding, reading or rewriting on media where the carrier moves relative to a transducer. The classification applies regardless of the specific recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 23/0085] intermediate mediums using a photosensitive material, e.g. photo-resist -- The classification covers the use of intermediate recording media that incorporate a photosensitive layer such as photo-resist, which is altered by light exposure to store information before processing or transfer to a final carrier. These media act as temporary substrates in lithography, imaging, or data-imprint processes where the recorded pattern is later developed or moved to a permanent record carrier.
[G11B 23/0092] molding resin compositions -- It covers polymeric molding resin formulations used to produce record carriers such as disks or tapes where the carrier moves relative to a transducer, without tying the material to a specific recording or playback method. These compositions are chosen for stability, wear resistance, and suitable magnetic or optical properties.
[G11B 23/02] Containers -- G11B 23/02 covers containers used to hold or protect information storage media such as tapes, cards or discs, irrespective of the recording or playback method. The class addresses the design, material, sealing and handling features of such containers to ensure stability, safety and ease of transport.
[G11B 23/021] comprising means for reducing influence of physical parameters, e.g. temperature change, moisture  combined with means for reconditioning or cleaning  G11B23/507 -- G11B 23/021 pertains to information-storage devices whose record carriers incorporate elements that lessen the impact of physical conditions such as temperature changes or moisture, and that also provide means for cleaning, reconditioning or otherwise maintaining the carrier surface.
[G11B 23/023] Containers for magazines or cassettes -- G11B 23/023 covers containers specifically shaped to hold magazines or cassettes that carry information, providing protection and organization for the media without being tied to any particular recording or playback method.
[G11B 23/0233] Containers for a single cassette -- This subclass covers containers specifically designed to hold a single cassette used in systems where data are stored on a movable record carrier read by a transducer. The containers may provide protection, handling or transport of the cassette but are not tied to a particular recording method. They fall under information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 23/0236] Containers for several cassettes -- G11B 23/0236 covers containers designed to hold multiple cassettes, such as storage boxes or cases that organize and protect several magnetic or other media cassettes together. These containers may be made of plastic, metal or other materials and include features for easy insertion, removal and identification of the cassettes.
[G11B 23/027] Containers for single reels or spools -- G11B 23/027 covers containers designed to hold a single reel or spool used in information-storage media where the carrier moves relative to a transducer, such as magnetic tapes. The classification applies to any protective or transport housing whose purpose is to secure one reel or spool, regardless of the specific recording or playback method.
[G11B 23/03] Containers for flat record carriers -- G11B 23/03 pertains to containers designed specifically for flat record carriers such as paper sheets, magnetic or optical cards, and similar media. It covers protective or transport enclosures like boxes, envelopes, sleeves and other holders that secure the flat items without reference to how the information is recorded or reproduced.
[G11B 23/0301] Details -- G11B 23/0301 pertains to the detailed aspects of record carriers whose design is independent of the specific recording or reproducing technique, covering characteristics such as material composition, shape, surface treatment and mechanical properties. It applies to information-storage systems that rely on relative movement between the carrier and a transducer, where the carrier itself is a generic medium rather than a method-specific element.
[G11B 23/0302] Auxiliary features -- This subclass covers auxiliary components and functions that support information storage devices where data is recorded by relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer, such as buffers, synchronization aids, loading or unloading mechanisms, cleaning or protective elements, and other non-core features that are not specific to the recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 23/0303] Write protect features with a sliding part -- This subclass covers write-protect mechanisms that use a movable sliding component to physically block access to the recording surface, preventing accidental recording or erasure. It applies to storage devices where the record carrier and transducer move relative to each other, such as magnetic tapes, cards or disks, and the slide provides a distinct write-disable position.
[G11B 23/0305] Semiconductor memories -- G11B 23/0305 classifies semiconductor memory devices used for storing information, covering integrated circuits such as dynamic, static and flash memories where data are retained in semiconductor cells rather than on magnetic or optical media. The entry applies to record carriers that are not defined by a specific recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 23/0306] Means for locking the record carriers -- This subclass covers mechanisms that secure physical media such as disks, tapes or other record carriers within a storage or playback system. Locking devices keep the carrier correctly positioned relative to the transducer to ensure reliable data access, often by clamping, latching or magnetic retention.
[G11B 23/0307] Positioning or centering features -- The G11B 23/0307 subclass covers features that ensure a record carrier is accurately positioned or centered with respect to a transducer in storage systems where information is read or written by relative movement between the two. It applies to any carrier type that is not defined by a particular recording or playback method, focusing on alignment mechanisms that improve tracking and data integrity.
[G11B 23/0308] Shutters  G11B23/0317  takes precedence -- G11B 23/0308 covers shutters that are employed in information storage systems where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer, serving to block or reveal the carrier during operation. The class applies to any type of record carrier irrespective of the specific recording or reproducing technique. This subclass is subordinate to G11B23/0317, which has precedence.
[G11B 23/031] Indicating means, e.g. sticker, bar code -- G11B 23/031 covers indicating devices such as stickers, bar-codes or other visual marks applied to record carriers that are not defined by a particular recording or reproducing method. These markers serve to identify, classify or convey information about the carrier as it moves relative to a transducer.
[G11B 23/0311] Wrong insertion preventing means -- This subclass deals with devices or mechanisms that detect or block an incorrectly inserted record carrier-such as a tape, disk or cartridge-to prevent damage or malfunction, including sensors, interlocks, mechanical guides or other means that sense a wrong insertion and stop operation before the carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 23/0312] Driving features -- G11B 23/0312 covers the driving mechanisms that move a record carrier relative to a transducer in information-storage devices, where the carrier itself is not defined by the recording or playback method. It includes features such as motor types, control circuits, speed regulation, and start-stop arrangements used in tape, disc and similar storage media. This subclass falls under physics-based information storage based on relative movement.
[G11B 23/0313] Container cases -- Container cases are protective housings that enclose recording media such as magnetic tapes, optical discs or solid-state cards, shielding them from mechanical damage, contamination and environmental factors while allowing their transport, storage and handling. They are defined without reference to the specific recording or playback method of the carrier.
[G11B 23/0315] Materials -- G11B 23/0315 classifies the materials that form record carriers in information-storage devices where data is written or read by relative movement between the carrier and a transducer, independent of the specific recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 23/0316] Constructional details, e.g. shape -- The G11B 23/0316 subclass covers the physical construction of record carriers that are not defined by a particular recording or playback method, focusing on their shape, dimensions, material form and other structural features that affect how the carrier moves relative to a transducer.
[G11B 23/0317] Containers with interchangeable record carriers -- G11B 23/0317 concerns containers that hold interchangeable record carriers such as tapes, disks, cards or similar media, allowing the carriers to be inserted, removed or exchanged while the storage device remains stationary. The classification includes housings, cartridges, caddies and other enclosures that protect and align the media, independent of the specific recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 23/0318] Containers with incorporated transducing heads -- G11B 23/0318 covers containers such as cartridges or cassettes that have transducing heads permanently integrated into the package, enabling data to be recorded or read without separate head assemblies. The design typically combines the storage medium and the head in a single removable unit, simplifying handling and alignment.
[G11B 23/032] for rigid discs -- G11B 23/032 covers data-storage systems that employ rigid discs as the record carrier, where the disc moves relative to a transducer for reading or writing. The subclass is situated under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement, and it concerns disc media not tied to any particular recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 23/0321] rigid cartridges for single discs -- This class covers rigid cartridges designed to hold a single disc used in devices where the disc moves relative to a transducer, providing a fixed non-flexible housing that supports the disc but is not defined by any particular recording or playback method.
[G11B 23/0322] comprising latching or movable handling devices  G11B17/032  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass concerns information-storage devices whose record carrier is not tied to a particular recording or reproducing method and that incorporate latching or other movable handling mechanisms to control, load or secure the carrier.
[G11B 23/0323] for disc-packs -- This subclass covers arrangements of multiple disc carriers packaged together for information storage, regardless of the specific recording or reproducing technique used. It falls under the broader category of information storage based on relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer, and applies to disc-packs that are not tied to a particular medium or method.
[G11B 23/0325] comprising latching or movable handling devices  G11B17/038  takes precedence -- The subclass G11B 23/0325 covers information storage arrangements in which the record-carrier is manipulated by latching or other movable handling mechanisms, independent of the recording or reproducing technique. It falls under the broader category of storage based on relative motion between carrier and transducer. The more specific subclass G11B 17/038 has priority over this.
[G11B 23/0326] Assembling of containers -- The CPC subclass G11B 23/0326 covers techniques for putting together the housings or containers that hold record carriers such as tapes, disks or other media in systems where the carrier moves relative to a transducer. It addresses the mechanical design and assembly processes of these enclosures without regard to the specific recording or playback method employed.
[G11B 23/0327] for special applications not otherwise provided for -- G11B 23/0327 subclasses information-storage devices that employ relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer, where the carrier is not defined by a particular recording or playback technique, and the invention addresses special or unconventional applications not covered by other subclasses.
[G11B 23/0328] the disc having to be extracted from the cartridge for recording reproducing, e.g. cooperating with an extractable tray -- This subclass covers information-storage devices in which the disc must be taken out of its cartridge before recording or playback, for example systems that use an extractable tray to retrieve the disc. It sits under the broader group dealing with relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 23/033] for flexible discs -- G11B 23/033 refers to flexible disc record carriers where the storage method is not specified, describing media that operate by relative movement between the disc and a transducer. It falls under physics → information storage → information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer, focusing on flexible disc formats used for recording or reproducing data.
[G11B 23/0332] for single discs, e.g. envelopes -- The G11B 23/0332 class covers information-storage devices that use a single disc as the record carrier, such as envelope-type media, where data is written or read by relative motion between the disc and a transducer. It falls under the broader category of storage systems that rely on movement between a carrier and a read/write head.
[G11B 23/0335] for disc packs -- This subclass covers the design and features of packaging that holds one or more optical discs such as CD, DVD or Blu-ray, intended for storage, protection and distribution. It includes containers, carriers or kits that bundle disc packs together.
[G11B 23/0337] comprising latching or movable handling devices  G11B23/0325  and  G11B17/038  take precedence -- G11B 23/0337 pertains to information storage systems where the record carriers, not specific to recording or reproducing methods, feature latching or movable handling devices. This classification is part of a broader hierarchy under physics and information storage based on relative movement between the carrier and transducer.
[G11B 23/037] Single reels or spools -- The G11B 23/037 subclass covers information-storage media that are wound on a single reel or spool, such as magnetic tape or optical strip, without being part of a cassette or cartridge. It includes the design of the reel, the method of mounting the carrier, and related handling mechanisms.
[G11B 23/04] Magazines -- G11B 23/04 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to magazines used as record carriers in information storage systems, where the storage is based on relative movement between the carrier and a transducer. This classification covers the design and structure of such magazines that hold multiple recording media.
[G11B 23/041] Details -- The G11B 23/041 subclass covers record carriers whose description is independent of any particular recording or reproducing technique, focusing on their physical characteristics such as shape, size, material, surface texture, and other structural details. It addresses the specific features of the carrier itself rather than the method of encoding or retrieving data.
[G11B 23/042] Auxiliary features  sensing such features  G11B15/06 -- G11B 23/042 classifies auxiliary sensing features used in information-storage devices where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer, such as sensors for position, speed, alignment or surface condition of the carrier. The entry covers any ancillary components that detect or monitor these characteristics, irrespective of the specific recording or reproducing method employed.
[G11B 23/043] Brakes for tapes or tape reels -- G11B 23/043 covers braking mechanisms applied to magnetic or other tapes and their reels used in information storage devices where the carrier moves past a transducer. The code pertains to devices that control tape tension or stop motion, independent of the recording method.
[G11B 23/044] Reels or cores; positioning of the reels in the cassette -- The CPC subclass G11B 23/044 deals with the design and arrangement of reels or cores within a cassette, specifically how the reels are positioned relative to each other and to the cassette housing. It applies to storage media where the record carrier moves relative to a transducer and does not prescribe any particular recording or reproduction method.
[G11B 23/045] Covers -- G11B 23/045 pertains to covers for record carriers in information storage systems, where the storage involves relative movement between the carrier and a transducer. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[G11B 23/046] Indicating means, e.g. quantity of tape -- The G11B 23/046 subclass deals with devices that provide an indication of tape status, such as the amount of tape remaining, in information-storage systems where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer. It covers any kind of indicator that informs the user about tape quantity or position.
[G11B 23/047] Guiding means -- Guiding means refer to devices or structures that direct the relative motion of a record carrier with respect to a transducer in generic information-storage systems, such as rails, rollers, tracks or other mechanical guides that ensure precise positioning during recording or playback.
[G11B 23/048] Driving features -- The G11B 23/048 class defines the driving features of devices that move a record carrier relative to a transducer in information-storage systems. It covers the design, control and operation of motors, actuators, speed-regulation and positioning mechanisms that enable precise relative motion during recording, playback or data retrieval.
[G11B 23/049] Cassettes for special applications not otherwise provided for -- G11B 23/049 classifies cassettes designed for special or niche uses that are not covered by other specific cassette categories. It falls under information storage based on the relative movement between a record carrier and transducer, and applies to record carriers that are not defined by a particular recording or playback method.
[G11B 23/06] for housing endless webs or filaments -- The G11B 23/06 classification covers devices that provide a housing or enclosure for continuous, endless media such as webs or filaments used in information storage or retrieval. It includes structures that protect, guide or contain looped tape, film or belt systems that move relative to a transducer, enabling recording, playback or data transport in compact or integrated formats.
[G11B 23/07] using a single reel or core -- G11B 23/07 covers information storage devices where a single reel or core serves as the record carrier and moves relative to the transducer, without tying the classification to any particular recording or playback technique. This subclass falls under the broader categories of physics, information storage, and relative movement-based storage media.
[G11B 23/08] for housing webs or filaments having two distinct ends -- G11B 23/08 covers structures that provide a housing for continuous record carriers such as webs or filaments that possess two separate ends.
