The classification includes mechanisms that protect, guide, or contain these media during handling, storage or transport, without being tied to a particular recording or playback method.
[G11B 23/087] using two different reels or cores -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification concerns devices or methods for storing information where the medium moves relative to the read-write head and is handled using two distinct reels or cores, rather than a single continuous spool.
[G11B 23/08707] Details -- G11B 23/08707 designates technical details of record carriers in storage devices where the carrier's characteristics are defined independent of any particular recording or reproducing method, covering aspects such as material composition, geometry, surface treatment or configuration that influence the relative movement between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 23/08714] Auxiliary features  sensing such features  G11B15/06 -- The subclass covers detection of auxiliary features on record carriers whose design is not tied to a specific recording or reproduction method. It includes sensors that sense marks, holes, notches, barcodes or other physical cues on magnetic, optical or other media to aid positioning, registration or operation of the storage system.
[G11B 23/08721] Brakes for tapes or tape reels -- The code G11B 23/08721 covers braking devices applied to magnetic or other tapes and tape reels to control their translational or rotational movement. It includes mechanisms that slow, stop, or hold the carrier during recording, playback, or rewinding, ensuring precise positioning and preventing overspeed. This falls within the broader class for information storage systems that rely on relative motion between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 23/08728] Reels or cores; positioning of the reels in the cassette -- This subclass covers devices that employ reels or cores as the record carriers in a cassette format, focusing on the arrangement and positioning of the reels to ensure proper tension and alignment during playback or recording. It includes mechanisms for mounting, adjusting, or securing reels within the cassette housing.
[G11B 23/08735] Covers -- G11B 23/08735 pertains to covers for record carriers in information storage systems, where the storage involves relative movement between the carrier and a transducer. This classification is specific to protective or operational coverings designed to safeguard or enhance the functionality of the storage media.
[G11B 23/08742] in combination with brake means -- This subclass covers information storage devices that use a moving record carrier equipped with brake means to control or stop its motion, without specifying the recording or reproducing method. It applies to tape, disk or other carriers where precise positioning is required.
[G11B 23/0875] Indicating means, e.g. quantity of tape -- G11B 23/0875 covers devices that provide an indication of the amount or length of a flexible tape used as a record carrier, such as windows, counters, or sensors that display remaining tape quantity. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer, where the carrier type is not tied to a particular recording method.
[G11B 23/08757] Guiding means -- The CPC entry G11B 23/08757 "Guiding means" covers devices that direct or constrain the relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer in information-storage systems, such as rails, guide tracks, rollers or other mechanical elements that ensure precise positioning of the medium during recording or playback. It applies to storage methods where the carrier's movement is not defined by the recording or reproducing technique itself.

The sheets typically serve as protective or functional layers separating the medium from the transducer.
[G11B 23/08771] Pressure pads -- G11B 23/08771 pertains to pressure pads used as record carriers in information-storage devices where data is written or read by the relative movement of a carrier and a transducer. The classification covers pads that apply pressure to the transducer without being tied to a particular recording or reproduction technique.
[G11B 23/08778] Driving features, e.g. belt -- This subclass addresses the drive mechanisms that move a recording medium relative to the read-write transducer, such as belts, pulleys, rollers or other actuating elements that provide linear or rotary motion to the carrier in information-storage devices.
[G11B 23/08785] Envelopes -- The subclass G11B 23/08785 Envelopes covers the use of envelopes as record carriers in information-storage devices where data is written or read by relative movement between a carrier and a transducer, without limiting the method of recording or reproducing. It resides in the physics hierarchy under information storage and the sub-category of carriers not specific to a particular recording technique.
[G11B 23/08792] Shielding devices -- Shielding devices are components that isolate the transducer or recording head from external electromagnetic fields, stray magnetic flux, or mechanical contaminants, thereby enhancing signal fidelity and reliability in systems where a record carrier moves relative to the transducer, such as magnetic or optical storage media.
[G11B 23/093] the reels or cores being coaxial -- The classification covers storage devices where the recording media are wound on reels or cores that share the same central axis, meaning the reels or cores are coaxial. It applies to systems that rely on relative motion between the record carrier and the transducer, regardless of the specific recording or playback method.
[G11B 23/107] using one reel or core, one end of the record carrier coming out of the magazine or cassette -- G11B 23/107 covers information-storage devices in which a single reel or core holds the record carrier and only one end of the carrier protrudes from the magazine or cassette, allowing the medium to be fed and retrieved without a second supply reel.
[G11B 23/113] Apparatus or processes specially adapted for the manufacture of magazines or cassettes -- This class covers machines and methods designed specifically for producing magnetic or optical magazines and cassettes used in data storage systems where the record medium moves relative to a read/write head. It includes equipment for assembling, loading, or forming the carriers but not the recording or playback functions themselves.
[G11B 23/12] Bins for random storage of webs or filaments -- G11B 23/12 classifies bins designed for the random storage of continuous webs or filamentary media, where the record carriers are not tied to a specific recording or reproducing method. These bins receive, hold and dispense the material in a nondeterministic arrangement, supporting processes such as printing, textile handling or similar applications that require flexible, bulk storage of long, flexible media.
[G11B 23/14] providing ability to repeat location, e.g. using sprocket holes -- G11B 23/14 relates to methods and devices that enable a record carrier to be returned to a predetermined position repeatedly, typically by using physical markers such as sprocket holes or similar indexing features to ensure exact location control during reading or writing.
[G11B 23/16] Record carriers with single-track for recording at spaced intervals along the track thereof, e.g. for speech or language training -- The CPC subclass G11B 23/16 covers record carriers that provide a single continuous track divided into spaced recording intervals, such as those used for speech or language-training material. It falls within the physics domain of information storage based on relative movement between the carrier and the transducer, where the carrier itself is not defined by a particular recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 23/18] Record carriers with multiple tracks, e.g. with complementary and partial tracks such as paired "stereo" tracks -- The subclass G11B 23/18 covers record carriers that contain several parallel tracks on the same medium, such as paired or complementary tracks used for stereo or multi-channel recording. It includes carriers where the tracks are arranged side-by-side or partially overlapping, allowing separate or interrelated signals to be stored on the same physical sheet, strip, or disc.
[G11B 23/20] with provision for splicing to provide permanent or temporary connections -- G11B 23/20 classifies information-storage devices that incorporate a splicing feature, enabling permanent or temporary connections between sections of the record carrier to maintain a continuous path for data recording or playback. This includes tape, film or similar media where separate lengths can be joined to form a longer recording medium.
[G11B 23/22] of endless belts -- The CPC subclass G11B 23/22 covers information-storage devices that use endless belts as the moving record carrier, where the belt itself forms a continuous loop rather than a finite strip. This falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer, and the classification addresses the belt's structure, handling, and integration with read/write transducers.
[G11B 23/24] of tapes having multiple tracks parallel to edge of record carrier by offset splicing to form endless loop with one or more helical tracks -- G11B 23/24 pertains to the creation of endless loops from magnetic tape, where the tape has multiple parallel tracks. This process involves offset splicing to form one or more helical tracks, enhancing data storage and retrieval efficiency.
[G11B 23/26] of leaders for loading or threading, e.g. to form a temporary connection -- The subclass G11B 23/26 covers devices that employ leaders-short lengths of film, tape or similar material-to load or thread a record carrier, establishing a temporary mechanical or electrical connection during operation. It applies to information-storage media where the carrier itself is not defined by the recording or playback method.
[G11B 23/28] Indicating prior or unauthorised use, -- G11B 23/28 concerns devices or methods that signal a recording medium has already been used, accessed, or altered without permission. It includes physical or electronic indicators such as write-protect tabs, status lights, or tamper-evident marks that inform the user of prior or unauthorized use.
[G11B 23/281] by changing the physical properties of the record carrier -- G11B 23/281 covers techniques that store information by altering the physical properties of a record carrier, such as its magnetic, optical, or electrical characteristics, without prescribing a particular recording or reproducing method. It applies to any medium where data is encoded through changes in the carrier's material traits, enabling later retrieval by a transducer that detects those altered properties. This falls under information storage based on relative movement between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 23/282] Limited play -- G11B 23/282 covers recording media or systems that restrict the user to a predetermined portion of the stored information, allowing playback only of that limited segment rather than the whole record. Examples include looped audio cartridges, gated magnetic tapes, or any format where access is confined to a fixed play length.
[G11B 23/283] Security features, e.g. digital codes -- The classification G11B 23/283 covers security features applied to information storage devices where the record carrier moves relative to a transducer, such as digital codes or other authentication mechanisms. It includes methods and structures for protecting data integrity and preventing unauthorized access, irrespective of the specific recording or reproducing technique.
[G11B 23/284] on the record carrier -- G11B 23/284 concerns the record carrier itself, covering features such as its material, shape, surface structure, coding or markings, and other characteristics that are independent of any particular recording or reproducing method. It applies to carriers used where information storage relies on relative movement between the carrier and a transducer, without specifying the recording technology.
[G11B 23/285] on the container or cartridge -- The subclass concerns the container or cartridge that houses a record carrier in a system where data are stored by relative movement between the carrier and a transducer. It covers the design, materials, sealing, protective features and integration of the cartridge or container with the recording medium.
[G11B 23/286] Antitheft arrangements, e.g. Electronic Article Surveillance [EAS] tags -- The G11B 23/286 class covers antitheft systems that employ electronic article surveillance tags attached to items, using a record carrier that is not tied to any particular recording or playback method. It falls under physics-information storage based on relative motion between the carrier and a transducer, and addresses security-related tag technologies.
[G11B 23/287] by mechanical lock -- This subclass covers storage media that are held, positioned or secured by a mechanical lock, regardless of the recording or playback method. It includes devices such as clamped, latched or keyed holders for tapes, disks or other carriers that rely on a lock mechanism to maintain relative movement between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 23/288] Protecting disks from being written or overwritten -- G11B 23/288 covers methods and devices that prevent data on magnetic or optical disks from being altered, including write-protect switches, physical barriers, and electronic locking mechanisms that block recording or overwriting while allowing read access. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between the carrier and transducer.
[G11B 23/30] with provision for auxiliary signals -- A subclass for information-storage devices where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer and the system incorporates auxiliary signals-such as control, synchronization, or error-checking signals-to aid the recording or reproducing process. It covers mechanisms that use these additional signals alongside the primary data stream.
[G11B 23/32] Electrical or mechanical contacting means -- G11B 23/32 relates to devices that create electrical or mechanical contact between a moving record carrier and a transducer in data-storage systems, such as contact brushes, pins or other touching elements that enable signal exchange irrespective of the specific recording or reproducing method.
[G11B 23/34] Signal means additional to the main recording track, e.g. photoelectric sensing of sprocket holes for timing -- G11B 23/34 covers auxiliary signal methods that accompany the main recording track to aid synchronization, such as using a photoelectric sensor to detect sprocket holes and provide timing cues during playback or recording.
[G11B 23/36] Signals on record carriers or on containers and recorded by the same method as the main recording -- G11B 23/36 concerns the imprinting of auxiliary signals, markings or identification data directly onto the same record carrier or its container as the primary information, using the identical recording method. Examples include etched identifiers on discs, printed barcodes on magnetic tape reels, or embossed cues on optical media that accompany the main data.
[G11B 23/38] Visual features other than those contained in record tracks or represented by sprocket holes -- G11B 23/38 covers visual markings on a record carrier that are separate from the data tracks and not represented by sprocket holes, such as printed logos, color bands, labels or other indicia used for identification or aesthetics. The subclass applies to any media where the carrier moves relative to the transducer, regardless of the recording or playback method.
[G11B 23/40] Identifying or analogous means applied to or incorporated in the record carrier and not intended for visual display simultaneously with the playing-back of the record carrier, e.g. label, leader, photograph -- G11B 23/40 covers the use of identification or analogous features that are attached to or integrated into a record carrier but are not meant to be seen while the carrier is being played, such as printed labels, leader strips or photographs that identify the content.
[G11B 23/42] Marks for indexing, speed-controlling, synchronising, or timing -- G11B 23/42 covers marks placed on a recording medium that serve as reference points for indexing, speed control, synchronisation or timing during playback or processing. These marks are independent of the recording or reproduction method and are used to align or coordinate the relative movement of the carrier and transducer.
[G11B 23/44] Information for display simultaneously with playback of the record, e.g. photographic matter -- The class concerns systems that present visual material, such as photographs or other images, at the same time a stored record is being reproduced. The display is synchronized with playback, providing complementary information alongside the audio or video content.
[G11B 23/50] Reconditioning of record carriers -- The G11B 23/50 subclass covers methods and apparatuses for restoring or refurbishing record carriers, such as cleaning, repairing, re-magnetizing, or otherwise renewing media that store information, without addressing the specific recording or playback technique used. It includes processes that improve the carrier's condition for reuse.
[G11B 23/502] of tape carriers -- G11B 23/502 classifies tape-based record carriers used in information storage systems where the medium moves relative to a transducer, without reference to a specific recording or reproduction method. It covers the physical form of magnetic, photographic or other tapes that serve as the storage element, regardless of how data is written or read. This entry falls under the broader category of information storage based on relative motion between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 23/505] of disk carriers -- This subclass addresses disk-shaped carriers used for storing information where the carrier type is independent of any particular recording or playback technique. It falls under the broader group dealing with storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 23/507] combined with means for reducing influence of physical parameters, e.g. temperature change, moisture -- This subclass covers information storage devices that use relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer and include means to counteract the effects of physical parameters such as temperature changes or moisture.
[G11B 25/00] Apparatus characterised by the shape of record carrier employed but not specific to the method of recording or reproducing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanical structure of such apparatuses.  Documents which do not find a more appropriate classification in the depending subgroups.

These apparatuses rely on relative motion between the carrier and a transducer to store or retrieve data.

[G11B 25/10] Apparatus capable of using record carriers defined in more than one of the sub-groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  combi apparatus,  apparatus which combine a tape player(s) with a disc player(s),  apparatus which combine a tape or disc player with a hard disc drive (HDD).
[G11B 27/00] Editing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Editing;  editing operations performed on audio or video content recorded on the type of recording medium historically falling under the subclass G11B and extended to any type of recording medium storing physically audio and video content in a permanent manner, resulting in a modified or new recorded content. This covers as well the physical implementations of operations such as cut, paste, merge, adding sound track as well as the definition of the editing operations to be performed within an editor (non-destructive editing, playlist arrangements, editing operations in a video editor).  Indexing and addressing;  details concerning the type of information attached to a recording content which allows to access said content as well as information indicating reproduction of a sequence of addressed parts of recorded contents (play list typically). This can be with respect to the physical details of the recording medium (subcodes, lead-in, lead-out in case of a CD, AIT track for tape, prepits for DVD) carrying the information as long as the type of the recording medium falls under the subclass G11B . In addition, it covers the case of indexing or addressing information in a audio or video content which are not specific to the physical characteristics of the recording medium such as table of content, metadata and other information which allow navigation within a file containing audio video content (typically a specific file format with indexing and addressing information embedded) or other special modes of reproduction. Special modes of reproduction (trickplay, repeat) are also classified in G11B27/00 .  Timing or synchronizing;  Details relating to the synchronized reproduction of different components making up an audio video recording. By extension, synchronization of content between a main unit and an auxiliary video or audio player.  Monitoring;  Monitoring concerns the supervision of the progress of recording or reproducing, mainly monitoring power failure during recording or reproduction and logging the use of medium or apparatus for fault prevention It covers also the testing of the medium as a direct step in a recording and reproducing method and the use of information about the execution of the reproduction and/or recording (flags, power failure).  Measuring tape travel;  obsolete. Technical details concerning the measuring of tape travel are classified in G11B15/00 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Testing the correct function of read/write heads for magnetic disk drives -> G11B5/455 - Testing recording/reproducing heads -> G11B7/00 , G11B5/00 - Detecting defects on optical discs -> G11B7/00375 - Testing disk drives -> G11B19/048 - Synchronization linked to channel decoding -> G11B20/10009 , G11B20/1403 - Management of defective sectors, error correction -> G11B20/18 - Finding physical defects on optical discs by optical inspection -> G01N21/9506 - Peripheral management in general -> G06F3/00 - User interface in general -> G06F3/048 - Testing computer peripherals -> G06F11/2268 - Image processing -> G06T - Audio broadcast recording -> H04H60/27 - Television studio equipment -> H04N5/222 - Television broadcast recording -> H04N5/76 - Video Broadcasting -> H04N7/24 - A/V synchronization in transmission -> H04N7/52 - Video display of recorded content -> H04N9/00 - Video/audio coding aspects -> H04N19/00 , G11B20/00 - Network broadcasting -> H04N21/20 , H04N7/24  **Glossary:** - TOC: (Table of content) : collection of information allowing the definition and retrieval of individual pieces of audio and video content . - EDL: (Edit Decision List); collection of information (part of content used, editing commands to be executed and their chronological and spatial order, leading when executed to the creation of a piece of audio /video content - Playlist: collection of information in sequential order defining the reproduction order of recorded content, e.g. (user defined) program chain in dvd, mp3 playlist; merely a list of objects that are to be reproduced in sequence with no common timeline defined
[G11B 27/002] Programmed access in sequence to a plurality of record carriers or indexed parts, e.g. tracks, thereof, e.g. for editing;  transfer of record carriers from magazine  G11B15/68 ,  G11B17/10 ;  G11B17/22 -- The subclass covers methods and devices that automatically address a sequence of multiple storage media or indexed sections, such as tracks, to perform editing operations, and includes mechanisms for transferring those media from a magazine or similar supply system.
[G11B 27/005] Reproducing at a different information rate from the information rate of recording  for television signals  H04N5/783 -- The G11B 27/005 entry concerns techniques for reproducing television signals at an information rate that differs from the rate used during recording, enabling time-scale changes such as speed-up or slow-down of playback. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 27/007] reproducing continuously a part of the information, i.e. repeating -- G11B 27/007 covers methods or devices that continuously reproduce a portion of stored data by repeatedly reading or replaying it, such as looped playback or cyclic readout in media where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 27/02] Editing, e.g. varying the order of information signals recorded on, or reproduced from, record carriers -- This class covers methods for changing the order of information signals on a record carrier or during reproduction, including rearranging, inserting, deleting or otherwise editing stored data to alter its sequence.
[G11B 27/022] Electronic editing of analogue information signals, e.g. audio or video signals -- G11B 27/022 covers electronic techniques for editing analog signals such as audio or video, enabling operations like cutting, splicing, or mixing in real time without converting to digital form. It relates to storage systems where the recording medium moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 27/024] on tapes -- G11B 27/024 covers editing of tape media, describing techniques and apparatus for modifying recorded information on magnetic, optical or similar tapes by cutting, splicing, inserting, deleting or re-ordering sections.
[G11B 27/026] on discs -- G11B 27/026 pertains to editing information stored on discs within the field of information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[G11B 27/028] with computer assistance -- G11B 27/028 covers editing operations for information storage devices where data is recorded by the relative motion of a carrier and a transducer, and the editing is performed with the aid of a computer. This class includes computer-controlled processes that modify, add, delete or rearrange recorded information on such moving-medium media.
[G11B 27/029] Insert-editing -- G11B 27/029 covers methods and apparatus for inserting new data into information stored on media where the record carrier moves relative to a transducer, such as magnetic or optical discs. It details techniques for edit operations, including synchronization, gap creation, and control of write timing to seamlessly add or replace portions of the recorded signal.
[G11B 27/031] Electronic editing of digitised analogue information signals, e.g. audio or video signals -- G11B 27/031 covers electronic editing of digitised analogue signals such as audio or video, including methods for modifying, splicing, or processing the digital representation of those signals. It falls under the physics branch of information storage, specifically the sub-category dealing with relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer, and pertains to editing functions applied to the stored digital data.
[G11B 27/032] on tapes -- The subclass G11B 27/032 covers methods and apparatus for editing data stored on tapes, including operations such as inserting, deleting, rearranging, or overwriting recorded sections. It also addresses techniques for selecting tracks, erasing and re-recording, and splicing or otherwise modifying tape-based information streams.
[G11B 27/034] on discs -- The G11B 27/034 subclass covers methods and apparatus for editing information that is stored on disc media, where the recording surface moves relative to the transducer. It includes processes such as cutting, rearranging, or modifying recorded tracks or data blocks on optical, magnetic, or other types of discs.
[G11B 27/036] Insert-editing -- G11B 27/036 covers techniques for insert-editing on storage media where the record carrier moves relative to a transducer. It includes methods and apparatus that create space in existing recorded data and write new information into that gap, enabling insertion of additional content without overwriting surrounding data.
[G11B 27/038] Cross-faders therefor -- Cross-faders are editing devices that allow a user to smoothly blend two audio signals by moving a slider that varies the relative level of each source. They are used in systems where sound is recorded or reproduced by a moving record carrier and a transducer, enabling real-time mixing of tracks. This entry falls under the physics category of information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 27/04] using differential drive of record carrier and head -- The subclass covers techniques that control the record carrier and the transducer head with separate drive signals so that their relative motion is achieved by a differential drive. By varying the speeds of the carrier and head independently, precise positioning for recording or playback is obtained, especially in magnetic or optical storage devices.
[G11B 27/06] Cutting and rejoining -- G11B 27/06 covers techniques for cutting and rejoining a record carrier that moves relative to a transducer, such as splicing sections of magnetic tape, optical disc or other linear storage media to edit or repair recorded information. The methods enable precise segmentation and reconnection without disrupting the carrier's continuity or data integrity.
[G11B 27/10] Indexing -- G11B 27/10 covers methods and apparatus for creating and managing indexes that facilitate locating stored data, including algorithms and physical structures used to map keywords, identifiers or address information to recorded content on moving media. It relates to the editing stage of information storage where the index is generated or updated to improve retrieval efficiency.
[G11B 27/102] Programmed access in sequence to addressed parts of tracks of operating record carriers  access by moving the head  G11B3/08 ,  G11B5/54 ,  G11B7/085 ,  G11B21/022 ; by moving the record carrier  G11B15/005 ,  G11B17/005 , by driving of both record carrier and head  G11B15/1816 -- G11B 27/102 pertains to methods for programmed access in sequence to addressed parts of tracks on operating record carriers, involving the movement of heads or the record carrier itself, or a combination of both, to achieve precise data retrieval. This classification is part of the broader categories related to information storage and editing within the physics domain.
[G11B 27/105] of operating discs -- The subclass G11B 27/105 covers editing techniques applied to rotating record carriers such as magnetic or optical discs, including insertion, deletion, rearrangement or modification of recorded data while the disc is in motion. It pertains to apparatus and methods that manipulate the stored information by moving the disc relative to the transducer during the editing process.
[G11B 27/107] of operating tapes -- The G11B 27/107 entry covers the handling, loading, feeding, tensioning and control of tapes used in recording or playback devices where the tape moves relative to a transducer. It includes mechanisms and methods for operating magnetic, optical or other tape media within information-storage systems. This subclass falls under the broader category of information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 27/11] by using information not detectable on the record carrier -- G11B 27/11 relates to methods and apparatus for editing stored information where the editing decisions are based on data that cannot be sensed directly on the record medium, such as hidden codes, metadata, or other non-observable cues. This subclass falls under information storage based on relative movement between the carrier and a transducer.
[G11B 27/13] the information being derived from movement of the record carrier, e.g. using tachometer -- G11B 27/13 covers techniques that extract information from the movement of a record carrier, for example by using a tachometer to measure speed or rotation. This falls under physics-based information-storage methods where the carrier's relative motion is sensed and processed for editing or control purposes.
[G11B 27/15] using mechanical sensing means -- G11B 27/15 relates to information-storage devices that employ mechanical sensing means-such as pressure, displacement or vibration detectors-to read, write or edit data while the record carrier moves relative to the transducer. It covers mechanisms that convert physical movement into electrical signals for the storage process.
[G11B 27/17] using electrical sensing means -- The G11B 27/17 class covers editing techniques for information storage devices where the record medium moves relative to a transducer and the editing is performed by electrical sensing means, such as detecting magnetic or optical signals to modify or retrieve data. It falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer, focusing on electronic methods for reading, writing, or correcting stored information.
[G11B 27/19] by using information detectable on the record carrier -- G11B 27/19 concerns editing of stored data by using information that is already detectable on the record carrier to locate or define the edit position. It includes methods that rely on pre-encoded markers, patterns, or other detectable features on the medium to guide insertion, deletion or modification of data.
[G11B 27/22] Means responsive to presence or absence of recorded information signals -- G11B 27/22 covers devices that detect the presence or absence of recorded information signals on a moving medium and respond accordingly, such as sensors that sense magnetic or optical marks to control editing, playback or other processing functions.
[G11B 27/24] by sensing features on the record carrier other than the transducing track -- G11B 27/24 covers information-storage devices that read or verify data by detecting features on the recording medium that are not part of the primary transducing track, such as pits, lands, or surface irregularities, while the carrier moves relative to the sensor. This includes optical or magnetic methods that sense these ancillary markings to retrieve stored information.
[G11B 27/26] by photoelectric detection, e.g. of sprocket holes -- The CPC class G11B 27/26 covers devices that edit or control information storage media by detecting sprocket holes using photoelectric sensors. It pertains to systems where relative movement between the record carrier and transducer is monitored via light detection to synchronize processing.
[G11B 27/28] by using information signals recorded by the same method as the main recording -- The subclass deals with editing stored data by employing information signals that are recorded using the same method as the primary recording, such as modifying magnetic or optical tracks with identical write processes. It applies to storage systems where the carrier and transducer move relative to each other.
[G11B 27/30] on the same track as the main recording -- The G11B 27/30 subclass covers methods and apparatuses that write additional information on the same track as the primary recording, such as overdubbing, annotation, or error-correction data. It includes systems that synchronize the secondary write operation with the original track's motion and format.
[G11B 27/3009] used signal is a pilot signal inside the frequency band of the recorded main information signal -- The subclass G11B 27/3009 covers recording or reproducing methods that embed a pilot signal within the same frequency band as the main recorded information signal, using the pilot for functions such as synchronization, tracking or error correction during playback. It belongs to the physics field of information storage where the carrier moves relative to the transducer, specifically in the editing stage.
[G11B 27/3018] used signal is a pilot signal outside the frequency band of the recorded main information signal -- G11B 27/3018 covers information storage systems in which a pilot signal located outside the frequency band of the recorded main information signal is employed, typically to aid synchronization, tracking, or calibration during read/write operations. The pilot assists the transducer in distinguishing the intended data without interfering with the primary signal.
[G11B 27/3027] used signal is digitally coded -- This subclass covers editing techniques in which the signal used to write or modify data on a moving record carrier is digitally encoded, i.e., represented by discrete binary symbols rather than analog levels. It applies to storage devices that rely on relative motion between the carrier and a transducer, such as magnetic or optical systems, where the editing operations manipulate these digitally coded signals.
[G11B 27/3036] Time code signal -- G11B 27/3036 covers time code signals that embed precise timing information onto a recording medium to synchronize or locate positions in audio, video or data tracks. These signals are generated and read by transducers that move relative to the carrier, enabling editing, playback and post-production control.
[G11B 27/3045] superimposed on the recorded main signal, e.g. burn-in-time code -- A superimposed code is a secondary signal layered onto the main recorded data to embed identifying information, such as the time a storage medium was manufactured or programmed. This burn-in or time code is written simultaneously with the primary signal, allowing the identification to be read without disturbing the original data. It is used in magnetic or optical media to track provenance, usage, or batch details.
[G11B 27/3054] Vertical Interval Time code [VITC] -- The G11B 27/3054 classification covers vertical interval time code (VITC), a method that embeds a timecode signal in the vertical blanking interval of an analog video waveform. VITC provides frame-accurate timing information for editing, synchronization and metadata retrieval without requiring additional tracks, and is used in broadcast and post-production environments.
[G11B 27/3063] Subcodes -- This subclass deals with methods and apparatus for editing subcode data - such as control, timing, or indexing information - on record carriers that move relative to a transducer, for example magnetic tapes or optical discs. It includes techniques for inserting, deleting or modifying those subcodes during recording or playback.
[G11B 27/3072] Coded signal uses a correlation function for detection -- The class G11B 27/3072 covers methods in which a coded signal on a storage medium is identified by applying a correlation function to the received waveform. It includes techniques that compare the incoming signal to a known reference pattern to detect the presence and timing of the code, improving reliability of data retrieval in magnetic or optical recording systems.
[G11B 27/3081] used signal is a video-frame or a video-field (P.I.P) -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 27/3081 pertains to the editing of information storage, specifically addressing techniques where the used signal is a video frame or field, including picture-in-picture (P.I.P.) functionalities. This classification is part of the broader category dealing with information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 27/309] Table of contents -- G11B 27/309 covers arrangements for creating, displaying, updating or using a table of contents on a storage medium where the record carrier moves relative to a transducer, such as magnetic or optical disks. The classification includes devices and methods that generate a navigable list of stored items and provide means for accessing the selected item efficiently. It is applied to editing functions that manage the organization and retrieval of data within such media.
[G11B 27/32] on separate auxiliary tracks of the same or an auxiliary record carrier -- G11B 27/32 covers methods and apparatus for editing or manipulating data recorded on separate auxiliary tracks of a primary or auxiliary record carrier, such as using additional tracks for control, error correction, or supplementary information alongside the main data track.
[G11B 27/321] used signal consists of two 180-degr. phase shifted signals of the same frequency -- The classification covers recording or playback techniques that employ a signal formed from two identical-frequency components that are shifted by 180 degrees in phase. By combining these opposite-phase waves, the resulting waveform is used to write or read data on a moving or stationary storage carrier.
[G11B 27/322] used signal is digitally coded -- G11B 27/322 covers methods and devices in which the signal applied to a recording medium that moves relative to the read/write head is digitally encoded. It pertains to editing functions for magnetic or optical storage where data are represented by discrete code patterns rather than analog levels. This classification lies under physics-information storage-relative movement-editing.
[G11B 27/323] Time code signal, e.g. on a cue track as SMPTE- or EBU-time code -- G11B 27/323 covers the use of a time-code signal recorded on a cue track of a moving-record medium, such as the SMPTE or EBU time-code formats, to provide precise timing and synchronization information during playback or editing. The classification applies to methods that embed these codes alongside the primary data on tape, film or disc so that the recorder or editor can locate specific points in the recorded material.
[G11B 27/324] Duty cycle modulation of control pulses, e.g. VHS-CTL-coding systems, RAPID-time code, VASS- or VISS-cue signals -- G11B 27/324 covers methods that vary the duty cycle of control pulses to encode data, such as the control tracks on video tapes (VHS-CTL), rapid-time codes, and cue signals like VASS or VISS. These techniques are used for editing, synchronization and other functions in storage systems where a moving record carrier interacts with a transducer.
[G11B 27/325] Subcodes -- G11B 27/325 relates to the use of subcodes-auxiliary data embedded alongside the main recorded information-in storage systems where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer, such as magnetic or optical media, and is applied to editing, identification, or control functions.
[G11B 27/326] used signal is a video-frame or a video-field (P.I.P.) -- G11B 27/326 covers editing operations where the processed signal is a video frame or video field, such as picture-in-picture (PIP) functions. The subclass applies to information-storage systems that manipulate video data by using relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 27/327] Table of contents -- G11B 27/327 relates to the creation of a table of contents in information-storage systems that use relative movement between the record carrier and a transducer, covering devices and methods that edit and organize stored data to provide navigable headings or indexes.
[G11B 27/328] on a tape [TTOC] -- G11B 27/328 refers to techniques for editing data on a moving tape, where the record carrier (the tape) passes a transducer that reads or writes information. It covers methods and apparatus that modify, insert, delete, or rearrange recorded material during tape playback or recording, fitting within the broader category of information storage based on relative movement.
[G11B 27/329] on a disc [VTOC] -- G11B 27/329 covers techniques for creating, updating or managing a volume table of contents (VTOC) directly on a disc, where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer; it is an editing function that stores indexing information on the disc surface to enable navigation and access to recorded tracks.
[G11B 27/34] Indicating arrangements -- G11B 27/34 covers the design of devices that signal the position or status of a record carrier moving relative to a transducer, such as markers, sensors, or visual indicators that show where recording or reading occurs in magnetic, optical or similar storage media. It focuses on the arrangement of these indicating elements within the storage system.
[G11B 27/36] Monitoring, i.e. supervising the progress of recording or reproducing -- G11B 27/36 covers devices or methods that monitor the progress of recording or reproducing information on a storage medium, including error detection, speed tracking, and feedback mechanisms that supervise the operation.
[G11B 3/00] Recording by mechanical cutting, deforming or pressing, e.g. of grooves or pits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mainly vinyl disks and apparatuses for playing them  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Recording on or reproducing from the same record carrier wherein for these two operations the methods are covered by different main groups of groups G11B3/00 - G11B7/00 or by different subgroups of group G11B9/00 -> G11B11/00 - Recording simultaneously or selectively by methods covered by different main groups -> G11B13/00
[G11B 3/001] with vibrating mechanical coupling means between pick-up element and sound producing element -- The G11B 3/001 subclass covers recording methods that use a vibrating mechanical coupling between the pick-up element and the sound-producing element, such as a flexible arm or linkage that transmits acoustic vibrations to a recording stylus for cutting, deforming or pressing grooves or pits on a medium.
[G11B 3/002] element with stationary record carriers -- G11B 3/002 pertains to elements in which the record carrier remains stationary while a moving transducer effects the recording, such as a fixed disc or plate on which grooves or pits are mechanically cut, deformed or pressed.

[G11B 3/005] through hollow arms -- G11B 3/005 covers mechanical recording methods that employ hollow arms to transmit the motion of a stylus or cutting tool onto a record carrier. The hollow arms convey the relative movement needed to create grooves or pits by cutting, deforming or pressing, enabling the storage of information through direct physical inscription.
[G11B 3/006] using horns -- This subclass covers recording techniques that employ horns as acoustic transducers to imprint, cut or press grooves, pits or other physical modulations onto a record carrier, such as in phonograph or other mechanical storage devices, and is part of the broader group dealing with mechanical cutting, deforming or pressing of information-storage media.
[G11B 3/007] Devices for controlling sound, e.g. using acoustical impedances, using valves -- The G11B 3/007 class covers devices that regulate acoustic signals in mechanical recording systems, such as those that employ acoustic impedances or valves to shape or suppress sound during the cutting, deforming or pressing of grooves or pits on a record carrier. It falls under physics, information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/008] for digital information -- G11B 3/008 refers to methods for storing digital data by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing a record carrier as it moves relative to a transducer, creating grooves, pits or similar physical marks that represent the information. This sub-class falls under information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/02] Arrangements of heads -- G11B 3/02 covers the configuration and placement of recording or playback heads in devices that store information by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing grooves or pits into a moving carrier such as a disc or tape. It pertains to arrangements of the transducer heads that interact with the carrier as it moves relative to the head. This subclass falls under physics->information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.

[G11B 3/06] Determining or indicating positions of head -- G11B 3/06 covers methods and devices for determining or indicating the position of a recording or playback head relative to a moving record carrier, such as detecting groove or pit locations in mechanically cut media.
[G11B 3/08] Raising, lowering, traversing otherwise than for transducing, arresting, or holding-up heads against record carriers -- This subclass covers mechanisms that move a mechanical recording head - raising, lowering or traversing it - solely for alignment or access to the record-carrier, without performing the transducing, arresting or holding function. It is used in systems that cut or press grooves or pits into the medium.
[G11B 3/085] using automatic means -- G11B 3/085 refers to automatic mechanical recording techniques for data storage where a moving record carrier and transducer create grooves, pits or deformations. This subclass falls under physics-based information storage that relies on relative motion between the carrier and the recording head.
[G11B 3/08503] Control of drive of the head -- G11B 3/08503 addresses the control of the drive that moves the recording or playback head in devices where information is stored by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing a carrier, such as groove-based audio or data discs. It includes circuitry, feedback loops and methods for precise positioning, speed regulation and tracking of the head during operation.
[G11B 3/08506] for pivoting pick-up arms -- This subclass covers devices in which the transducer or stylus is mounted on a pivoting arm that swings over the recording medium, enabling the pick-up to follow grooves, pits or other track patterns during mechanical cutting, deforming or pressing of information onto the carrier.
[G11B 3/08509] using mechanical detecting means -- This subclass covers methods where the information on a medium is read by a mechanical sensing device that detects the physical shape of cut or pressed marks such as grooves or pits. The detecting element typically contacts or tracks the surface to convert the mechanical pattern into a signal.
[G11B 3/08512] using optical detecting means -- G11B 3/08512 covers technologies that record data by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing grooves or pits on a carrier and then detect the recorded information optically. It belongs to the physics subclass for information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 3/08516] using magnetic detecting means -- The G11B 3/08516 subclass covers information storage devices where data is mechanically inscribed as grooves or pits on a record carrier and the stored information is read by magnetic detecting means, using magnetic sensing of the carrier's motion to retrieve the encoded pattern.
[G11B 3/08519] for pick-up arms moving parallel to itself -- The G11B 3/08519 subclass pertains to mechanical information-storage devices in which the pickup arm that reads or writes the medium moves parallel to its own length, such as certain record-player or magnetic-tape heads that travel alongside the groove or track. This falls under physics-based storage where data are recorded by cutting or deforming grooves or pits on a carrier.
[G11B 3/08522] using mechanical detecting means -- G11B 3/08522 covers recording methods that employ mechanical detecting means, such as knives or styluses, to cut, deform or press grooves, pits or other physical markings on a moving record carrier for information storage. It falls under the physics field of information storage based on relative motion between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/08525] using optical detecting means -- The G11B 3/08525 subclass concerns recording methods that create grooves or pits on a record carrier by mechanical cutting, deformation or pressing, and that employ optical detectors to read the stored information, fitting within the physics-based information-storage hierarchy that relies on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/08529] using magnetic or electric detecting means -- This subclass pertains to mechanically recorded information-such as grooves, pits or deformations-where the stored data are read by magnetic or electric sensing devices rather than purely mechanical pickup. It covers systems that use a transducer moving relative to the record carrier and detect the pattern via magnetic or electric detecting means.
[G11B 3/08532] for fixed arms carrying a movable head -- G11B 3/08532 designates recording apparatus in which fixed arms hold a movable head that mechanically cuts, deforms or presses a record carrier-such as creating grooves or pits-to store information by relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/08535] Driving the head -- G11B 3/08535 addresses the means of moving or actuating the recording head in devices that store information by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing a record carrier, such as stylus-driven turntables. The classification covers mechanisms that control the head's position, speed or pressure relative to the medium during operation.
[G11B 3/08538] the head being driven by the same means as the record can -- This subclass covers devices where the recording or playback head is propelled by the same drive mechanism that moves the record medium, such as a cutting stylus that both creates grooves and is carried along by the same-motor or cam system. The arrangement ensures synchronized motion of head and record for precise mechanical inscription.
[G11B 3/08541] for pivoting pick-up arms -- The G11B 3/08541 subclass covers mechanical recording techniques that employ a pivoting pickup arm to read or write data, such as the movement of a stylus across grooves or pits on a rotating or sliding medium. It is part of the hierarchy dealing with information storage that relies on relative motion between the record carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 3/08545] driven by cams -- The G11B 3/08545 subclass covers recording devices where a cam mechanism drives the mechanical cutting, deformation or pressing of a record carrier such as grooves or pits to store information. It is part of the physics-based information-storage group that relies on relative movement between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 3/08548] using friction coupling -- G11B 3/08548 covers recording information by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing a medium where the drive is transmitted through a friction coupling between the transducer and the moving record carrier. It lies under physics, information storage based on relative movement, and mechanical recording methods.
[G11B 3/08551] for the horizontal movement only -- G11B 3/08551 covers recording techniques where the record carrier moves only horizontally relative to the transducer, such as cutting or pressing grooves without vertical displacement. It belongs to the mechanical-cutting subclass of information-storage methods that rely on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/08554] for pick-up arms moving parallel to itself -- G11B 3/08554 covers recording devices that use mechanical cutting, deforming or pressing to create grooves or pits, where the pickup arm moves parallel to its own longitudinal axis during playback or tracking. The subclass falls under information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/08558] driven by belt or analogous element -- G11B 3/08558 covers mechanical recording methods in which the motion that drives the cutting, deforming or pressing element is transmitted by a belt or a comparable component. It belongs to the information-storage group that records data by physically forming grooves or pits on a carrier.
[G11B 3/08561] driven by non-skip driving means, e.g. lead screw -- This subclass covers devices that record information by mechanically cutting or pressing a record medium using continuous drive mechanisms such as lead screws rather than intermittent or skip motions. It pertains to systems where the relative motion between the transducer and carrier is generated by non-skip drives.
[G11B 3/08564] the head being driven by means independent of the record carrier driving means -- G11B 3/08564 covers systems in which the recording head is moved by a drive that is independent of the mechanism that moves the record carrier, such as separate motors or actuators, rather than being mechanically linked to it. This applies to mechanical recording methods that cut, deform or press grooves or pits into the storage medium.
[G11B 3/08567] for pivoting pick-up arms -- The CPC subclass G11B 3/08567 covers mechanical recording techniques where a pickup arm pivots to follow the track of a rotating record or disc, such as in phonographs that use a stylus to cut, deform or press grooves while the carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 3/0857] driven by means which support the pick-up arm -- This subclass refers to mechanical recording systems where the movement that creates grooves is generated by a mechanism that holds and moves the pick-up arm, such as a rotating platter or carrier that directly drives the stylus. It pertains to information storage relying on relative motion between the medium and the transducer, specifically where the drive is provided by the arm-support structure.
[G11B 3/08574] the supporting element being different from the rotation-axes -- The entry covers recording systems in which the component that supports the record carrier-such as a spindle, cassette hub or bearing-is not the same as the axis around which the carrier rotates, separating the support function from the rotation axis. This distinction applies to mechanical cutting, deforming or pressing methods that create grooves or pits for data storage.
[G11B 3/08577] for the vertical movement only -- G11B 3/08577 designates recording processes that employ only vertical movement of the stylus or transducer relative to the record carrier. It covers cutting, deforming or pressing of grooves or pits, as used in hill-and-valley or other vertical-modulation storage formats.
[G11B 3/0858] using mechanical means -- The entry covers methods and apparatuses that record data by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing a record carrier, creating grooves, pits or other physical indentations through relative movement between the medium and a transducer. It includes any system that uses a stylus, engraving blade or similar mechanical means to produce a permanent pattern for information storage.
[G11B 3/08583] using electrical/magnetic means -- This subclass covers methods that use electrical or magnetic energy to assist the mechanical formation of grooves, pits or other record features on a storage medium, such as employing electromagnets, coils or electrically powered tools to cut, deform or press the carrier during recording.
[G11B 3/08587] for pick-up arm moving parallel to itself -- The G11B 3/08587 subclass covers mechanical recording methods in which the pickup arm that reads or writes data moves parallel to its own longitudinal axis, such as in devices that scan grooves or pits while the arm slides side-to-side. This falls under information storage based on relative motion between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/0859] driven by belt or analogous element -- The code refers to mechanical recording methods where the motion that creates grooves, pits or deformations on a storage medium is supplied by a belt, chain or similar drive element. It applies to devices in which a belt-or-analogous mechanism moves the recording stylus relative to the record carrier to inscribe the data.
[G11B 3/08593] driven by non-skip driving means, e.g. lead screw -- The subclass G11B 3/08593 covers recording apparatus in which the record carrier is moved continuously by a non-skip drive, such as a lead-screw mechanism, rather than by indexed steps. It applies to mechanical cutting, deforming or pressing of grooves or pits where the transducer follows the carrier's motion in a smooth, continuously driven fashion.
[G11B 3/08596] for fixed arms carrying a movable head -- G11B 3/08596 covers devices in which the recording carriage is mounted on fixed arms that support and guide a movable write head, used for mechanically cutting or deforming a record medium such as grooves or pits. The arrangement provides precise positioning of the head while allowing relative motion during recording.
[G11B 3/09] using manual means only -- G11B 3/09 concerns the manual creation of recorded data by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing grooves, pits or similar marks into a storage medium. The entry applies to methods that rely solely on hand-operated tools rather than powered or automated equipment, such as hand-cranked stylus or embossing devices used to inscribe information directly onto the record carrier.
[G11B 3/091] using magnetic means  G11B3/093  takes precedence -- The code covers methods that store data by mechanically shaping a medium - cutting, deforming or pressing grooves or pits - while using magnetic forces or fields to create the recorded pattern. It applies to technologies where magnetic means assist the mechanical recording process and is superseded by the more specific G11B3/093 subclass.
[G11B 3/092] using mechanical means  G11B3/093  takes precedence -- The subclass G11B 3/092 covers recording of information by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing a record carrier-such as forming grooves or pits-using mechanical means, where the data is stored through relative movement between the carrier and a transducer, and it takes precedence over the later subclass G11B 3/093.
[G11B 3/093] Means coupled to the cover -- G11B 3/093 concerns devices that attach a protective cover to a mechanical recording system, such as a turntable or disk unit, providing secure loading, alignment and shielding of the record carrier while allowing the transducer to operate.
[G11B 3/095] for repeating a part of the record -- This CPC subclass covers techniques for reproducing or repeating a segment of a mechanically recorded medium, such as copying grooves or pits on a disc or tape, using cutting, deforming, or pressing methods.
[G11B 3/0952] using automatic means -- This subclass concerns mechanical recording of information - such as cutting, deforming or pressing grooves or pits on a moving record carrier - where the process is performed by automatic means rather than manually. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/0955] using mechanical means for detecting the end of the recording -- G11B 3/0955 relates to mechanical detection of the end of a recording in devices that store information by cutting, deforming or pressing grooves or pits on a carrier. The entry covers systems that use a tactile or lever-based sensor to sense when the last recorded groove has been reached, enabling automatic stopping or rewind.
[G11B 3/0957] using optical means for detecting the end of the recording or the desired point thereof -- G11B 3/0957 covers devices that use an optical sensor to locate the end of a mechanically recorded track or a predefined position on the medium. The method applies to storage media where grooves or pits are created by cutting, deforming or pressing, and the optical means provide a non-contact way to detect the desired point during playback or further recording.
[G11B 3/10] Arranging, supporting, or driving of heads or of transducers relatively to record carriers -- G11B 3/10 covers mechanisms that position, hold or move recording or playback heads and other transducers in relation to a moving record carrier such as a magnetic tape, disc or drum. The class includes arrangements for aligning, supporting, or driving the heads to maintain proper contact or spacing during mechanical recording or reading. It falls under information-storage technologies that rely on relative motion between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 31/00] Arrangements for the associated working of recording or reproducing apparatus with related apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus where the recording and reproducing device is interfaced with the user.  Take-up mechanisms for earphones cable.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Constructional details or arrangements of data processing systems -> G06F1/16 - Output arrangements for transferring data from processing unit to output unit -> G06F3/00 - Accessing, addressing, or allocating within memories -> G06F12/00 - Protection against unauthorised use of memories -> G06F12/14 - Transfer of information between memories, I/O devices or central processing units -> G06F13/00 - Recording/reproducing of accompaniment for use with an external source, e.g. karaoke systems -> G10H1/361 - Transmission systems -> H04B - Transmission of digital information -> H04L - Data switching networks -> H04L12/00 - Loudspeakers, microphones -> H04R - Wireless communication network -> H04W
[G11B 31/003] with radio receiver -- This subdivision covers arrangements that integrate a recording or reproducing device with a radio receiver, enabling the stored information to be received, processed, or transmitted via radio signals. It pertains to information storage systems where the carrier moves relative to a transducer and is combined with radio reception functionality.
[G11B 31/006] with video camera or receiver -- G11B 31/006 covers arrangements that combine a video camera or video receiver with a recording or reproducing device that uses relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer, enabling synchronized capture and storage of visual information.
[G11B 31/02] with automatic musical instruments -- G11B 31/02 relates to the mechanical and electronic integration of recording or reproducing equipment with automatic musical instruments, covering how such devices are coordinated for synchronized playback or storage of sound. The classification lies within physics, specifically information storage based on relative movement between a record carrier and transducer, and addresses arrangements that enable the associated operation of the combined apparatus.
[G11B 3/12] Supporting in balanced, counterbalanced or loaded operative position , e.g. loading in direction of traverse -- G11B 3/12 covers arrangements that hold a recording element in a balanced, counter-balanced or loaded operating position while it moves across a medium, such as loading the head in the direction of traverse during mechanical cutting, deformation or pressing of grooves or pits.
[G11B 3/121] By using mechanical means not provided for in  G11B3/14 ,  G11B3/20 , e.g. using cams -- G11B 3/121 relates to recording techniques that use mechanical actions not covered by the preceding groups, for example employing cams to cut, deform or press grooves, pits or other indentations into a record carrier.
[G11B 3/122] Providing horizontal force, e.g. anti-skating  G11B3/124  takes precedence -- G11B 3/122 covers techniques for applying a horizontal force to a record carrier during playback, such as anti-skating mechanisms that counteract the outward pull on the tone-arm. These methods help keep the stylus centered in the groove, improving tracking stability and reducing distortion.
[G11B 3/124] Damping means therefor -- G11B 3/124 covers damping devices used in mechanical recording systems where information is stored by cutting, deforming or pressing grooves or pits onto a medium. The code addresses means for controlling vibrations or motion of the record-carrier or transducer to improve accuracy and signal quality.
[G11B 3/125] by using electric or magnetic means -- G11B 3/125 covers recording techniques that create grooves, pits or other indentations on a moving record carrier by applying electric or magnetic energy, rather than by direct mechanical pressure. It falls under information-storage methods that rely on relative movement between the carrier and the transducer, specifically those using electrically or magnetically generated forces to deform the medium.
[G11B 3/127] Providing horizontal force, e.g. anti-skating force  G11B3/128  takes precedence -- This classification covers mechanisms that apply a horizontal force to a record carrier during mechanical recording, such as anti-skating forces that keep a stylus centered in a groove. It falls under physics, information storage, storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer, specifically mechanical cutting or pressing.
[G11B 3/128] Damping means therefor -- G11B 3/128 covers damping means employed in mechanical recording systems that inscribe grooves or pits onto a record carrier, such as springs, dashpots or resilient layers that absorb vibrations during cutting or pressing. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/14] by using effects of gravity or inertia, e.g. counterweight -- The CPC subclass G11B 3/14 covers mechanical recording techniques that exploit gravity or inertia, such as using a counterweight to drive the cutting or embossing of grooves or pits on a record carrier. These methods fall under information storage based on relative movement between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 3/145] Providing horizontal force, e.g. anti-skating force  G11B3/18  takes precedence -- This subclass covers mechanisms that apply a horizontal force to a rotating record or disc, such as anti-skating devices that counteract the inward pull on a tonearm during playback. It includes designs for generating, adjusting or controlling such forces to improve tracking accuracy.
[G11B 3/16] adjustable -- G11B 3/16 covers adjustable mechanisms used in mechanical recording of information, where data is inscribed by cutting, deforming or pressing grooves or pits on a moving record carrier. It includes devices whose position, pressure or geometry can be varied to control the depth, width or spacing of the recorded marks.
[G11B 3/18] Damping by using viscosity effect -- The G11B 3/18 subclass covers techniques for reducing vibration in mechanical recording of information by employing viscous effects. It includes the use of fluids, greases or polymer layers that absorb energy and damp the relative motion between a record carrier and transducer during cutting, deforming or pressing.
[G11B 3/20] by elastic means, e.g. spring -- This subclass covers mechanical recording techniques in which elastic elements such as springs apply pressure or deformation to a record carrier, creating grooves, pits or other marks for information storage, and is part of the broader category of relative-movement based recording.
[G11B 3/22] adjustable -- G11B 3/22 relates to devices that record data by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing a record carrier-such as creating grooves or pits-and includes features that allow the recording parameters, like pressure, depth or spacing, to be adjusted during operation. This subclass covers adjustable mechanisms for controlling the relative movement between the record medium and the transducer.
[G11B 3/24] acting to decrease pressure on record -- The subclass pertains to methods that lower the force exerted by a recording stylus or other mechanical transducer on a record carrier during the creation of grooves or pits, employing springs, dampers, or variable-force mechanisms to improve recording fidelity and reduce wear.
[G11B 3/26] acting to increase pressure on record -- G11B 3/26 pertains to methods and apparatus for increasing the pressure applied during mechanical recording processes, such as cutting grooves or pressing pits into a record carrier. This classification is part of the broader category of information storage techniques involving relative movement between the storage medium and the transducer.
[G11B 3/28] providing transverse bias parallel to record -- G11B 3/28 relates to techniques for applying a transverse bias that runs parallel to the recording track on a medium where information is stored by mechanical cutting, deformation or pressing of grooves or pits, improving signal stability and recording quality.
[G11B 3/30] Supporting in an inoperative position -- G11B 3/30 covers devices that keep a recording medium-such as a disc, tape or cylinder-in a fixed, inoperative position to prevent movement during storage, transport or idle periods, typically using brackets, clamps or other holding structures. This falls under the physics subclass for information storage based on relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer.

[G11B 3/44] Styli, e.g. sapphire, diamond -- The G11B 3/44 class covers stylus elements-often made of hard materials such as sapphire or diamond-used to mechanically cut, deform or press grooves, pits or other marks into a recording medium. These stylus tips transfer the signal by direct physical contact with the carrier surface, enabling playback or storage of audio, video or data through relative movement between the carrier and the transducer.

[G11B 3/46] Constructions or forms , e.g. attachment of point to shank -- G11B 3/46 covers the structural design of mechanical recording media where a point or tip is attached to a supporting shank, detailing methods and forms for joining such recording elements that cut, deform or press grooves or pits into a carrier.
[G11B 3/48] Needles -- The G11B 3/48 entry covers needles that act as the transducing element in mechanical recording systems, where the needle's tip physically cuts, deforms or presses into a moving record carrier to create grooves or pits that store information. These devices are used in technologies such as phonographs and other systems that rely on relative motion between the needle and the recording medium.
[G11B 3/50] Anvils or other supports opposing stylus forces -- In mechanical recording systems that cut or press grooves into a medium, anvils or other supports are employed to oppose the forces exerted by the stylus. These backing elements stabilize the record carrier, ensuring consistent groove depth and shape while resisting deformation during the cutting process.
[G11B 3/52] Arrangements permitting styli to yield under excessive pressure -- The G11B 3/52 subclass covers mechanical recording systems where the stylus is allowed to yield or flex when subjected to excessive pressure, protecting the record medium and the recording head. It includes designs such as spring-loaded or compliant couplings that absorb overload in devices that cut, deform or press grooves or pits for data storage.
[G11B 3/54] Storing -- This subclass covers methods of storing data by physically forming grooves, pits or other deformations on a moving medium using mechanical cutting, embossing or pressing, where the information is read by relative motion between the carrier and a transducer. It includes technologies such as phonograph records, stamped optical-disc pits and other mechanically inscribed media.
[G11B 3/56] Sharpening -- Sharpening is the process of improving the edge or profile of a tool that mechanically cuts, deforms or presses grooves or pits into a recording medium, ensuring precise and consistent data inscription. It is a preparatory step that enhances the performance of recording devices used in mechanical information storage.
[G11B 3/58] Cleaning record carriers or styli, e.g. removing shavings or dust -- This CPC entry covers techniques and devices for cleaning record carriers or stylus elements, such as removing dust, shavings or other debris from mechanically recorded media like phonograph records, magnetic tapes or optical discs. It falls within the physics area of information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/5809] during transducing operation -- It refers to patents concerning the step of mechanical recording that occurs while the transducer is actively converting the signal, i.e., cutting or pressing the recording medium concurrently with signal transduction. The claim covers methods where the recording medium and transducer interact during the transducing phase.
[G11B 3/5818] for record carriers -- G11B 3/5818 relates to techniques for recording data on a record carrier by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing the surface to form grooves, pits or similar features, typically using a transducer that moves relative to the carrier. It falls under the hierarchy of physics, information storage, and information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/5827] using means contacting the record carrier -- G11B 3/5827 covers recording methods that employ a device physically touching the recording medium to create or modify grooves, pits or other mechanical deformations. This subclass falls under mechanical recording in information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/5836] means connected to the pick-up arm or head -- This subclass covers mechanical features that link the pickup arm or head to other parts of a recording device, such as levers, springs, or connectors that transmit motion or force.
