[G11B 3/5854] using means not contacting the record carrier -- G11B 3/5854 covers recording techniques that use non-contact means to inscribe information on a moving record carrier, such as lasers or electromagnetic fields, instead of physically cutting, deforming or pressing grooves or pits.
[G11B 3/5863] connected to the pick-up arm or head -- G11B 3/5863 covers mechanical recording methods such as cutting, deforming or pressing grooves or pits, specifically relating to elements that are connected to the pick-up arm or head of the device. This subclass falls under information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer within the broader physics field.
[G11B 3/5872] connected to a separate arm -- The G11B 3/5872 subclass covers mechanical recording techniques where a distinct arm is employed to cut, deform or press grooves or pits into a record carrier, forming the stored information. It falls within information storage based on relative movement between the carrier and the transducer, a subcategory of physics-related data storage methods.
[G11B 3/5881] for styli or needles only -- G11B 3/5881 covers recording methods in which the mechanical cutting or deformation of a record carrier is performed using only styli or needles. It applies to devices that create grooves, pits or other impressions by direct contact with a single-point tool, without additional shaping elements. The code is used for patents involving purely needle-based engraving of information-storage media.
[G11B 3/589] before or after transducing operation -- This CPC subclass relates to mechanical recording methods where the transducer operation occurs before or after the mechanical cutting or pressing of the record carrier. It covers processes in which encoding of information by cutting grooves, pits or similar deformations is performed either preceding or succeeding the transducing step.
[G11B 3/60] Turntables for record carriers -- G11B 3/60 refers to turntables designed to rotate record carriers for playback. The classification includes mechanisms that move the disc relative to a transducer, such as motor-driven platforms or manually cranked tables, and addresses the specific arrangement of bearings, drive systems and speed control needed for mechanical recording or reproducing of grooves or pits.
[G11B 3/61] Damping of vibrations of record carriers on turntables -- The G11B 3/61 class covers techniques that reduce or control vibrations of phonograph or other rotating record carriers while they are played on turntables. It includes damping mechanisms, materials, suspensions or structures designed to lessen resonances and improve sound fidelity during mechanical recording or playback.
[G11B 3/64] Re-recording, i.e. transcribing information from one grooved record carrier on to one or more similar or dissimilar record carriers -- G11B 3/64 covers re-recording, the process of transcribing data from one grooved record carrier onto one or more similar or dissimilar carriers. It includes mechanical copying methods that reproduce the original grooves, pits or depressions on a new medium, enabling duplication or transfer of stored information. This falls within the physics-based information-storage field dealing with relative movement between a record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/66] Erasing information, e.g. for reuse of record carrier -- G11B 3/66 covers techniques for erasing data from mechanically recorded storage media such as discs, tapes or cards, enabling the carrier to be reused. It includes methods that remove or obliterate grooves, pits or other physical imprints created by cutting, deforming or pressing.
[G11B 3/68] Record carriers -- It covers physical media that serve as record carriers for information stored by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing grooves or pits, such as disks, tapes or similar substrates. The class includes the composition, structure and preparation of these carriers used in mechanical recording systems.
[G11B 3/682] comprising protective coatings, e.g. anti static, anti-friction -- This subclass of G11B 3/682 covers recording media where the record carrier is provided with protective coatings such as anti-static or anti-friction layers to improve durability and performance. It falls under mechanical recording methods where grooves or pits are cut, deformed, or pressed.
[G11B 3/685] Intermediate mediums -- G11B 3/685 encompasses intermediate media employed in mechanical recording processes that cut, deform or press grooves or pits into a record carrier. It covers substrates such as magnetic tapes, optical discs or similar items that act as a bridge between the information source and the final storage surface.
[G11B 3/687] Testing thereof  investigating chemical or physical properties of materials  G01N
[G11B 3/70] characterised by the selection of material or structure -- G11B 3/70 covers recording methods that form grooves or pits by mechanical cutting, deforming or pressing, where the inventive feature is the selection of material or structural design of the record carrier or transducer, applied to physical information-storage devices that operate by relative movement between medium and head.
[G11B 3/702] for video discs with grooves  G11B3/705  takes precedence -- The G11B 3/702 subclass covers video discs that store information by cutting or pressing continuous grooves into the disc surface, which are read by a mechanical transducer following the relative movement of the carrier. This pertains to recording methods based on groove formation, and it is superseded by the more specific G11B 3/705.
[G11B 3/705] characterised by the selection of the material only -- G11B 3/705 covers recordings made by mechanical cutting, deforming or pressing where the only distinguishing feature is the material selected for the record carrier or transducer. It applies to storage media such as discs, drums or tapes that rely on relative movement between carrier and head, and the patent claims focus solely on material choice.
[G11B 3/707] for video discs with grooves -- G11B 3/707 classifies the recording of video discs by creating grooves or pits on the disc surface, a mechanical process that stores information through relative movement between the disc and a transducer. The code covers cutting, deforming or pressing of the grooves for playback.
[G11B 3/72] Groove formations, e.g. run-in groove, run-out groove -- G11B 3/72 covers the creation of grooves on a recording medium, such as run-in or run-out grooves, formed by mechanical cutting, deformation or pressing. These grooves are used to manage tracking or spacing in devices that store information by relative movement between the carrier and transducer.
[G11B 3/74] Multiple output tracks, e.g. binaural stereophonic -- G11B 3/74 relates to mechanically recorded media that carry more than one audio channel, such as binaural or stereophonic sound, by using separate output tracks cut or pressed into the carrier. It covers devices and methods for creating and reproducing these multiple tracks on records or similar storage formats.
[G11B 3/76] forming part of cinematograph films -- It covers methods for creating the physical portion of motion picture film that carries the recorded image, such as cutting, embossing or pressing grooves, pits or other patterns onto the film base. It falls under mechanical recording processes for information storage.
[G11B 3/78] Multiple-track arrangements -- G11B 3/78 covers devices that record data on a moving medium by mechanically cutting, deforming or pressing multiple parallel tracks, such as grooves or pits, onto the carrier. The arrangement enables simultaneous or sequential storage of separate data streams within the same medium.
[G11B 3/80] incorporating subsidiary guide means for heads, other than modulated grooves -- G11B 3/80 covers recording apparatus that employ auxiliary guide structures for the recording head, other than using modulated grooves, to control head motion during mechanical cutting, deforming or pressing of grooves or pits on a record carrier.
[G11B 3/90] with means indicating prior or unauthorised use -- The subclass G11B 3/90 concerns mechanically recorded media-such as grooves, pits or deformed surfaces-where the device incorporates features that reveal whether the record has already been used or has been accessed without authorization, typically by visual or tactile indicators attached to the carrier.

[G11B 5/00808] magnetic cards -- G11B 5/00808 relates to magnetic cards that store data by magnetising or demagnetising a moving record carrier as it passes a transducer. The class falls under physics, specifically information storage based on relative movement between the carrier and the read/write head.
[G11B 5/00813] magnetic tapes -- Magnetic tapes are a form of information storage in which data is recorded by magnetising or demagnetising a flexible polymer or metal film that moves relative to a read/write transducer. This technology falls under the CPC subclass G11B 5/00813, covering recording methods that rely on the relative motion between the record carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 5/00817] on longitudinal tracks only, e.g. for serpentine format recording -- This subclass covers magnetic recording methods that use only longitudinal tracks, such as serpentine-format recording where the transducer writes along the length of the medium and then reverses direction to write adjacent tracks.
[G11B 5/00821] using stationary heads -- G11B5/00821 covers magnetic recording techniques in which the transducer heads are fixed in place while the magnetic medium moves relative to them, enabling data to be written or read without head motion. This falls under physics-information storage where recording is done by magnetising or demagnetising the carrier.
[G11B 5/00826] comprising a plurality of single poles or gaps or groups thereof operative at the same time -- This CPC subclass covers magnetic recording or demagnetizing devices in which several single poles or gaps, or groups of them, function at the same time, such as multi-pole heads that write or read concurrently on a moving record carrier. It falls under physics → information storage → information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/0083] for parallel information processing, e.g. PCM recording -- G11B 5/0083 covers magnetic recording techniques that employ parallel information processing, such as pulse-code modulation, to write data onto a moving record carrier. [G11B 5/00834] using virtual scanning heads -- This subclass covers recording techniques that employ virtual scanning heads, where the magnetic reading or writing function is performed by electronically generated signals that mimic the motion of a physical head across the media, allowing headless or stationary transducer arrays to write or read data by varying magnetic fields.
[G11B 5/00839] using cyclically driven heads providing segmented tracks -- This CPC subclass pertains to magnetic recording techniques in which the recording heads are driven cyclically, producing a series of discrete, segmented tracks on the carrier rather than a continuous trace. It falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer, specifically recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/00847] on transverse tracks  G11B5/00878  takes precedence -- This classification code, G11B 5/00847, pertains to the method of recording information by magnetisation or demagnetisation on a record carrier, specifically when the recording is done on transverse tracks. However, it notes that the more specific code G11B 5/00878 takes precedence in cases where applicable.
[G11B 5/00852] using stationary heads -- The subclass covers magnetic recording or erasing where the transducer heads stay fixed while the record carrier moves past them, as in tape drives, hard-disk platters or other moving-media systems. It includes designs that rely on stationary heads for tracking, focusing and signal acquisition.
[G11B 5/00856] comprising a plurality of single poles or gaps or groups thereof operative in time sequence -- This subclass covers magnetic recording devices that employ multiple single poles, gaps, or groups of them which operate sequentially in time, encoding data by magnetising or demagnetising regions on a moving record carrier.
[G11B 5/0086] using cyclically driven heads providing segmented tracks -- The code G11B 5/0086 covers magnetic recording methods that employ heads driven in a cyclic motion to create segmented tracks on the medium. By moving the recording head back-and-forth or in another repetitive pattern, each track is divided into sections, improving data density or access reliability in magnetic storage devices.
[G11B 5/00865] for transducing on more than one segment simultaneously -- This subclass concerns magnetic recording where the transducer operates on several segments of the medium simultaneously, enabling parallel write or read actions across multiple tracks in a single operation.
[G11B 5/00869] the segments being disposed in different lateral zones of the tape -- The G11B 5/00869 subclass covers magnetic tape recording arrangements in which the tape is divided into separate lateral zones or segments, each positioned side-by-side across the width of the tape. It relates to methods that allocate different data streams, tracks or functions to these zones while the tape moves past the transducer.
[G11B 5/00873] the segments being disposed in different longitudinal zones of the tape -- G11B 5/00873 covers magnetic or demagnetized recording on a tape where the recording segments are positioned in separate longitudinal zones along the tape's length, allowing different sections to hold distinct data or functions. This zoning technique is used to manage track allocation, improve access or support multi-track formats.
[G11B 5/00878] transducing different track configurations or formats on the same tape -- G11B 5/00878 is a CPC subclass that covers techniques for using the same magnetic tape to record or read different track configurations or data formats, allowing multiple recording schemes on a single carrier.
[G11B 5/00882] configurations only, e.g. longitudinal and transverse -- G11B 5/00882 relates to magnetic recording configurations, specifically the orientation of recorded magnetic domains such as longitudinal or transverse. The classification covers devices and methods where the configuration of magnetic alignment is the defining feature.
[G11B 5/00886] simultaneously -- G11B 5/00886 pertains to the simultaneous recording and reading processes in magnetic storage systems, where data is written to and read from the medium concurrently. This classification covers techniques that enhance efficiency and speed in information storage devices.
[G11B 5/00891] formats only, e.g. analog and digital -- G11B 5/00891 categorises patents that concern only the data formats employed in magnetic recording, such as analog or digital encoding schemes, without addressing the recording mechanism itself. It is used for inventions that define or use specific magnetic data formats.

[G11B 5/02] Recording, reproducing, or erasing methods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Glossary:** - Shingled writing: Tracks are written in a sequential manner from an inner diameter (ID) to an outer diameter (OD), from OD to ID, or from OD and ID towards a middle diameter (MD) in a radial region of a disk in a hard disk drive (HDD). In other words, a first track is partially overwritten on one side when a second track adjacent to the first track is written, and subsequently a third track is written that partially overwrites the second track, and so forth
[G11B 5/022] H-Bridge head driver circuit, the "H" configuration allowing to inverse the current direction in the head -- The sub-class G11B 5/022 covers circuits that drive magnetic recording heads using an H-bridge configuration, which allows the current direction through the head to be reversed for bidirectional magnetisation of the record carrier. [G11B 5/024] Erasing -- G11B 5/024 covers techniques for erasing magnetic information stored on a record carrier, including demagnetising or remagnetising the medium to remove previously recorded data. It encompasses methods that prepare the carrier for reuse by eliminating residual magnetic signals.
[G11B 5/0245] Bulk erasing -- G11B 5/0245 concerns bulk erasing, the simultaneous demagnetisation of an entire magnetic record carrier rather than individual bits. It applies to magnetic media where the whole medium is cleared in a single operation, such as wiping tapes or disks.
[G11B 5/027] Analogue recording -- Analogue recording refers to the process of storing information on a magnetic record carrier by varying its magnetisation in proportion to the original signal, typically using a transducer that moves relative to the medium. This method captures continuous signal variations rather than discrete digital states.
[G11B 5/0275] Boundary displacement recording -- Boundary displacement recording is a magnetic recording method in which the positions of magnetised and demagnetised zones on a medium are shifted relative to a fixed reference, thereby encoding data by the displacement of the boundary between these zones. It is used in magnetic stripe cards and certain tape or disc technologies where data are written by moving the magnetic transition zone.
[G11B 5/03] Biasing -- G11B 5/03 - Biasing refers to the application of a static magnetic field to a recording medium to set its magnetic domains in a preferred direction before or during the magnetisation process, improving signal stability and reducing noise. This technique is used in magnetic recording systems where the carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 5/035] Equalising -- Equalising refers to techniques or devices that adjust the magnetic signal level during recording so that variations in carrier properties or recording conditions do not cause uneven amplitude. By compensating for these differences, the recorded magnetic bits maintain a consistent strength across the medium, improving reliability and read-back accuracy.
[G11B 5/09] Digital recording -- G11B 5/09 covers digital recording methods that store information by magnetising or demagnetising a moving magnetic medium such as tape or disk. It includes techniques for encoding binary data using variations in magnetic flux as the carrier passes a transducer, fitting within physics, information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.

[G11B 5/1871] Shaping or contouring of the transducing or guiding surface -- The subclass concerns techniques that shape or contour the surface of a magnetic transducer or the guiding surface that contacts the record carrier, such as modifying the geometry of read/write heads or guide rails, to improve magnetic recording performance, reduce wear, or enhance signal fidelity.
[G11B 5/1872] for improving the form of the electrical signal transduced, e.g. compensation of "contour effect" -- The subclass G11B 5/1872 covers methods and devices that improve the shape of the electrical signal generated during magnetic recording, such as compensating for the "contour effect" that distorts signal waveforms, thereby enhancing playback fidelity and reducing error rates.
[G11B 5/1874] specially adapted for composite pole pieces, e.g. for avoiding "pseudo-gap" -- G11B 5/1874 covers magnetic recording heads that employ composite pole pieces specially designed to avoid pseudo-gap effects, thereby enhancing magnetic flux continuity and recording performance.
[G11B 5/1875] "Composite" pole pieces, i.e. poles composed in some parts of magnetic particles and in some other parts of magnetic metal layers -- Composite pole pieces are magnetic pole structures that combine regions of magnetic particles with regions of magnetic metal layers, creating a tailored magnetic profile. This design is used in recording heads or similar devices to improve performance in magnetisation-based data storage.
[G11B 5/1877] including at least one magnetic thin film -- The G11B 5/1877 subclass covers recording methods that employ at least one magnetic thin film to store data by magnetising or demagnetising a record carrier. It includes devices, media or processes where a thin magnetic layer, such as a sputtered or deposited film, forms the functional element for magnetic information storage.
[G11B 5/1878] disposed immediately adjacent to the transducing gap, e.g. "Metal-In-Gap" structure -- G11B 5/1878 covers magnetic recording heads in which a conductive or magnetic element is placed directly within the transducing gap - a "metal-in-gap" configuration - to modify the field at the recording surface. The arrangement aims to improve field concentration, recording density or signal fidelity in devices that record by magnetising a moving carrier.
[G11B 5/193] the pole pieces being ferrite -- G11B 5/193 pertains to magnetic recording heads in which the pole pieces are formed from ferrite. Ferrite pole pieces provide high magnetic permeability and low eddy-current loss, improving the efficiency of magnetising or demagnetising a moving record carrier such as tape or disk.
[G11B 5/21] the pole pieces being of ferrous sheet metal -- A magnetic recording head whose pole pieces are formed from ferrous sheet metal, providing the magnetic flux paths that write or read data on a moving record carrier. This design is classified under G11B 5/21 for recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/23] Gap features -- Gap features refer to the design elements that create a magnetic flux gap in the recording head, shaping the magnetic field that writes or reads data on a magnetic medium. These features influence how the field interacts with the moving record carrier, affecting recording density, signal-to-noise ratio and the precision of magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/232] Manufacture of gap -- G11B 5/232 covers the production techniques for the magnetic gap in recording heads or similar components used in magnetic storage. The gap, which separates magnetic pole faces, is essential for writing and reading data on moving media. The classification addresses methods of forming, shaping and assembling this gap.
[G11B 5/235] Selection of material for gap filler -- G11B 5/235 concerns the selection of materials used as gap filler in magnetic recording heads, where a thin non-magnetic or low-permeability layer separates the pole piece from the magnetic medium to control flux distribution. It addresses properties such as permeability, mechanical strength, thermal stability and wear resistance that influence recording quality.
[G11B 5/245] comprising means for controlling the reluctance of the magnetic circuit  -- G11B 5/245 covers magnetic recording or reproducing apparatus that includes a mechanism to vary the magnetic-circuit reluctance, such as adjustable magnetic heads, flux-control yokes or auxiliary magnetic elements. By changing the reluctance, the device can optimize the magnetic field applied to the record carrier, improving write performance, recording density or signal quality. This subclass falls within information storage based on the relative motion between the carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/2452] where the dimensions of the effective gap are controlled -- G11B 5/2452 addresses magnetic recording techniques in which the dimensions of the effective gap between the transducer (such as a write-head) and the record carrier are deliberately controlled, allowing precise shaping of the magnetic field to enhance data density, fidelity or other performance characteristics.
[G11B 5/2455] the magnetic circuit including at least one magnetic thin film of controllable properties  for scanning  G11B5/4938 -- A magnetic circuit that incorporates one or more magnetic thin films whose properties can be adjusted for use in scanning heads of magnetic storage devices, enabling controlled magnetisation or demagnetisation of the record carrier during recording. This falls under physics-information storage where the carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 5/2457] disposed immediately adjacent to the gap  "composite" pole pieces  G11B5/1877 -- G11B5/2457 covers magnetic recording heads in which composite pole pieces are positioned directly next to the write gap. This arrangement seeks to improve magnetic flux concentration and reduce stray fields, enhancing recording density and signal quality. [G11B 5/255] comprising means for protection against wear -- G11B 5/255 pertains to magnetic or demagnetizing recording devices where the record carrier includes features that protect it from wear, such as hard coatings, lubricating layers, or wear-resistant structures, ensuring longevity of the magnetic medium.
[G11B 5/265] Structure or manufacture of a head with more than one gap for erasing, recording or reproducing on the same track -- This CPC subclass covers the structure and manufacture of magnetic recording heads that incorporate more than one gap, enabling erasing, recording or reproducing signals on the same track simultaneously. It belongs to the information storage field.
[G11B 5/2651] Manufacture -- The CPC class G11B 5/2651 covers manufacturing processes for devices that record data by magnetising or demagnetising a storage medium, such as hard-disk platters or magnetic tapes, focusing on how the record carrier and its transducer are produced and assembled.
[G11B 5/2652] with more than one gap simultaneously operative  with controlled single gap  G11B5/245 -- G11B 5/2652 pertains to magnetic recording devices in which the write head contains several gaps that function together but are managed as a single controlled gap, allowing multiple magnetic flux paths to be active simultaneously during recording. This subclass falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer → recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier.
[G11B 5/2654] for recording or erasing -- This CPC subclass G11B 5/2654 covers methods and devices that record or erase data on a magnetic medium by magnetising or demagnetising the carrier while it moves relative to the transducer. It applies to magnetic recording techniques where the write head interacts with the moving tape, disk, or similar media.
[G11B 5/2655] with all the gaps disposed within the track or "guard band" between tracks, e.g. with erase gaps operative on track edges, with wide erase gap followed by narrow write gap -- This subclass concerns magnetic recording in which the spacing between adjacent tracks-guard bands-is created wholly within the track area, employing erase gaps at the track edges and optionally a wide erase gap followed by a narrow write gap to separate recorded regions. It belongs to information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/2657] all the gaps having the same dimension in the direction transverse to the track direction -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 5/2657, under the PHYSICS and INFORMATION STORAGE categories, specifies a method for recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation where all gaps in the magnetic head have the same dimension in the direction transverse to the track direction.
[G11B 5/2658] for recording with premagnetization or biasing of record carrier or head -- G11B 5/2658 covers magnetic recording methods that employ a preliminary magnetisation or bias field applied to either the record carrier or the recording head before the actual recording process. It falls within the physics-based information-storage category dealing with relative movement between a record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/29] Structure or manufacture of unitary devices formed of plural heads for more than one track -- G11B 5/29 relates to the design and manufacturing of magnetic recording units that incorporate several heads to work on more than one track at a time. Such unitary multi-head devices enable parallel recording or playback, increasing data density and transfer speed.
[G11B 5/295] Manufacture -- G11B 5/295 covers manufacturing processes for magnetic recording media where information is stored by magnetising a record carrier. It includes methods for producing magnetic tapes, disks and other magnetic storage media and related steps such as coating, layering and forming the magnetic layer.
[G11B 5/31] using thin films -- G11B 5/31 covers methods of magnetic recording that employ thin-film magnetic layers on the record carrier. The classification applies to technologies where a moving transducer writes or erases data by magnetising or demagnetising these films, enabling high-density storage in devices such as tape, disk or stripe media.
[G11B 5/3103] Structure or manufacture of integrated heads or heads mechanically assembled and electrically connected to a support or housing -- Integrated magnetic read/write heads that are built as a single unit or assembled mechanically and wired to a support or housing are covered, including their internal geometry, layer arrangement, bonding techniques, and methods for attaching the heads to the drive structure.
[G11B 5/3106] where the integrated or assembled structure comprises means for conditioning against physical detrimental influence, e.g. wear, contamination  G11B5/3133  takes precedence -- This subclass concerns magnetic recording or demagnetisation devices whose integrated or assembled structure incorporates means to condition the system against physical detrimental influences such as wear or contamination, providing built-in protective measures. It is subordinate to G11B 5/3133.
[G11B 5/3109] Details  G11B5/3103  takes precedence -- G11B 5/3109 pertains to specific details in the process of recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation, where certain aspects are given precedence over others as defined under the broader category of information storage based on relative movement between a record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/3113] for improving the magnetic domain structure or avoiding the formation or displacement of undesirable magnetic domains -- G11B 5/3113 covers techniques for improving the magnetic domain structure of a record carrier or preventing the creation or movement of undesirable domains during magnetisation or demagnetisation, thereby enhancing recording reliability and data integrity in magnetic storage media.
[G11B 5/3116] Shaping of layers, poles or gaps for improving the form of the electrical signal transduced, e.g. for shielding, contour effect, equalizing, side flux fringing, cross talk reduction between heads or between heads and information tracks  G11B5/3113 ,  G11B5/245  take precedence -- G11B 5/3116 pertains to the design and shaping of magnetic recording head components, such as layers, poles, or gaps, aimed at enhancing signal quality through various means like shielding, contour effect, equalizing, side flux fringing, and reducing cross talk between heads or tracks. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with magnetic recording technology and is subject to precedence rules involving related codes.
[G11B 5/312] for reducing flux leakage between the electrical coil layers and the magnetic cores or poles or between the magnetic cores or poles -- G11B 5/312 concerns techniques for reducing magnetic flux leakage between the electrical coil layers and the magnetic cores or poles, or between the cores or poles themselves, in devices that record by magnetising or demagnetising a record carrier.
[G11B 5/3123] by using special coil configurations or conductors -- This CPC subclass covers magnetic recording methods that employ specially designed coil configurations or conductors to create or erase magnetisation on a moving record carrier, enabling data storage through relative motion between the carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/3126] using superconductors -- G11B 5/3126 covers magnetic recording techniques that use superconductors to enhance the magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier. Superconducting materials enable higher magnetic fields, lower energy loss and faster write speeds, improving overall performance of magnetic storage devices. This entry lies within physics, information storage and relative-movement based magnetic recording.
[G11B 5/313] Disposition of layers -- G11B 5/313 addresses how magnetic layers are arranged within a recording medium, covering the stacking order, spacing, orientation or other physical placement of magnetic films that store data by magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/3133] including layers not usually being a part of the electromagnetic transducer structure and providing additional features, e.g. for improving heat radiation, reduction of power dissipation, adaptations for measurement or indication of gap depth or other properties of the structure  G11B5/3106  takes precedence -- A subclass of magnetic recording heads that adds non-standard layers to the electromagnetic transducer, such as thermal-radiation coatings, low-loss films, or sensor layers for measuring gap depth or other structural properties, thereby improving heat dissipation and power consumption. These additional features are applied beyond the basic transducer structure and are governed by the broader G11B 5/3106 subclass.
[G11B 5/3136] for reducing the pole-tip-protrusion at the head transducing surface, e.g. caused by thermal expansion of dissimilar materials -- A method or structure for limiting the protrusion of the magnetic-head pole tip that occurs when different materials in the head expand at different rates due to heat, thereby improving recording stability and reducing wear.
[G11B 5/314] where the layers are extra layers normally not provided in the transducing structure, e.g. optical layers  G11B5/3196  takes precedence -- G11B 5/314 covers magnetic or demagnetic recording methods in which additional layers, not normally part of the transducing structure, are applied to the record carrier, such as optical coatings that modify the interaction between the carrier and the transducer. The broader subclass G11B 5/3196 has priority over this entry.
[G11B 5/3143] including additional layers for improving the electromagnetic transducing properties of the basic structure, e.g. for flux coupling, guiding or shielding  G11B5/3116 ,  G11B5/312  take precedence -- G11B 5/3143 is a sub-class of the CPC group for recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier, within the physics field of information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer. It covers inventions that add extra layers to the basic magnetic structure to enhance electromagnetic transducing performance, such as flux coupling, guiding or shielding, and it is subordinate to G11B5/3116 and G11B5/312.
[G11B 5/3146] magnetic layers -- G11B 5/3146 covers magnetic layers used in recording media where data is stored by magnetising or demagnetising a surface as the carrier moves relative to a transducer. It pertains to the composition, structure and properties of thin ferromagnetic films that form the data-bearing region of magnetic tapes, disks or cards.
[G11B 5/315] Shield layers on both sides of the main pole, e.g. in perpendicular magnetic heads -- The classification G11B 5/315 concerns magnetic recording heads that incorporate shielding layers on both sides of the main pole, a design commonly used in perpendicular magnetic heads to confine the magnetic flux, improve write efficiency and reduce cross-talk between adjacent tracks.
[G11B 5/3153] including at least one magnetic thin film coupled by interfacing to the basic magnetic thin film structure -- The code G11B 5/3153 covers magnetic recording media that incorporate at least one additional magnetic thin film which is directly interfaced to the primary magnetic thin-film layer. This arrangement is used to modify or improve the magnetic properties of the basic recording layer, such as coercivity, signal strength or stability, by coupling the films at their interface.
[G11B 5/3156] providing interaction by induced or exchange coupling -- This subclass covers magnetic recording methods that create or control the interaction between the transducer and the record carrier by means of induced magnetic fields or exchange coupling, facilitating the writing or reading of data as the carrier moves relative to the head.
[G11B 5/3159] superconductive layers -- G11B 5/3159 covers the use of superconductive layers in magnetic recording media, where thin films of superconducting material are incorporated into the record carrier to improve magnetic field generation or sensing during magnetisation and demagnetisation. This sub-class addresses the structure, composition and application of such layers to enhance write or read performance in information storage devices.
[G11B 5/3163] Fabrication methods or processes specially adapted for a particular head structure, e.g. using base layers for electroplating, using functional layers for masking, using energy or particle beams for shaping the structure or modifying the properties of the basic layers -- G11B 5/3163 covers specialized manufacturing techniques for magnetic recording heads, such as applying base layers for electroplating, employing functional layers as masks, or using energy and particle beams to shape structures or alter basic layer properties, all tailored to the specific head geometry.
[G11B 5/3166] Testing or indicating in relation thereto, e.g. before the fabrication is completed -- The G11B 5/3166 subclass covers methods for testing or indicating the performance of magnetic recording or erasure processes, such as magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier, during manufacturing or before the device is finished. It includes techniques that assess parameters like magnetic field strength, signal quality or material response to ensure proper operation prior to final assembly.
[G11B 5/3169] Working or finishing the interfacing surface of heads, e.g. lapping of heads -- The subclass G11B 5/3169 covers methods for working or finishing the interfacing surface of magnetic heads, for example lapping, to achieve the required flatness, smoothness and geometry that optimize recording and playback performance.
[G11B 5/3173] Batch fabrication, i.e. producing a plurality of head structures in one batch -- G11B 5/3173 covers methods for batch fabrication of magnetic transducer heads, where multiple head structures are produced together in a single manufacturing step. It addresses processes that enable simultaneous formation, shaping or assembling of several heads to increase production efficiency for devices that record data by magnetising a medium.
[G11B 5/3176] Structure of heads comprising at least in the transducing gap regions two magnetic thin films disposed respectively at both sides of the gaps  G11B5/2455 ,  G11B5/265  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - The magnetic circuit of the head in physical contact with, or immediately adjacent to the recording medium including at least one magnetic thin film of controllable properties -> G11B5/2455 - Structure or manufacture of a head with more than one gap for erasing, recording or reproducing on the same track -> G11B5/265
[G11B 5/3179] the films being mainly disposed in parallel planes -- This subclass deals with magnetic or demagnetic recording where the magnetic media consist of thin films arranged side by side in parallel planes, such as layered or stacked film structures used to store data. It covers the geometry of the media rather than the recording method itself.
[G11B 5/3183] intersecting the gap plane, e.g. "horizontal head structure" -- G11B 5/3183 pertains to magnetic recording heads where the gap plane intersects with other components, often referred to as a horizontal head structure, facilitating specific orientations and interactions in the recording process.
[G11B 5/3186] parallel to the gap plane, e.g. "vertical head structure" -- In magnetic recording the head is oriented so that its magnetic gap lies parallel to the gap plane, a configuration often called a vertical head structure. This arrangement affects the direction of the magnetic flux that writes or reads by magnetising or demagnetising the moving record carrier.
[G11B 5/3189] Testing -- G11B 5/3189 pertains to testing techniques for magnetic recording systems, specifically methods for evaluating the magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier. It includes procedures to assess the performance, quality and reliability of the storage medium.
[G11B 5/3193] of films or layers, e.g. continuity test -- This CPC subclass covers methods and apparatus for testing the continuity of magnetic films or layers on a record carrier, such as continuity tests used to detect gaps, breaks, or defects in thin magnetic coatings. It applies to manufacturing, quality control, and maintenance of magnetic recording media.
[G11B 5/3196] of thin magnetic films, e.g. functional testing of the transducing properties  G11B5/455  takes precedence -- G11B 5/3196 pertains to the testing of thin magnetic films used in information storage, focusing on evaluating the transducing properties of these materials. This classification is part of a broader category that deals with recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation and is specifically concerned with functional testing methods.
[G11B 5/325] Erasing heads using permanent magnets -- G11B 5/325 covers erasing heads that employ permanent magnets to demagnetize magnetic media. These heads are used in devices where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer, such as magnetic tape or disk systems, and they erase recorded data by generating a static magnetic field rather than by using an electric current.
[G11B 5/33] Structure or manufacture of flux-sensitive heads, -- G11B 5/33 covers the structural features and manufacturing processes of flux-sensitive heads, the magnetic transducers that detect changes in magnetic flux on a recording medium. It includes designs, materials, and fabrication techniques used to create heads for magnetic recording and playback.
[G11B 5/332] using thin films  G11B5/372 ,  G11B5/3903  take precedence -- G11B 5/332 pertains to the use of thin films in magnetic recording, with specific emphasis on technologies where other classifications like G11B 5/372 and G11B 5/3903 are more detailed and thus take precedence.
[G11B 5/335] with saturated jig, e.g. for detecting second harmonic -- G11B 5/335 covers magnetic recording or playback arrangements that use a saturated magnetic jig-or similar element-to generate or detect the second harmonic of the recorded signal, facilitating head alignment, bias control, or media diagnostics.
[G11B 5/35] having vibrating elements -- G11B 5/35 covers magnetic recording systems that employ vibrating elements to move the record carrier relative to the transducer, such as tape heads that oscillate to enhance magnetisation or demagnetisation. These devices use mechanical vibration to improve signal fidelity or track alignment.
[G11B 5/37] using galvano-magnetic devices, e.g. Hall-effect devices -- G11B 5/37 relates to the use of galvano-magnetic components, for example Hall-effect devices, to record information by magnetising or demagnetising a moving record carrier. The classification falls under physics-based information storage where the carrier moves relative to a transducer.
[G11B 5/372] in magnetic thin films -- The G11B 5/372 subclass pertains to magnetic thin-film materials and structures that serve as the recording layer in magnetic storage media, where data are written by magnetising or demagnetising the film. It is situated under physics → information storage → information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/374] Integrated structures -- G11B 5/374 covers integrated structures used in magnetic recording where the record carrier and the transducer are combined into a single unit, such as heads that incorporate magnetic layers, read/write gaps or shielding directly on the media substrate, enabling compact, efficient devices for recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/376] in semi-conductors  G11B5/372  takes precedence -- G11B 5/376 pertains to the recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier, specifically addressing techniques involving semi-conductors. However, it notes that G11B 5/372 takes precedence in certain contexts.
[G11B 5/378] Integrated structures -- The G11B 5/378 classification covers integrated structures used in magnetic recording, where functional elements such as the transducer, media support or magnetic layers are combined into a single unit. These structures enable the magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier without separate, discrete components, improving alignment, reliability and manufacturing efficiency.
[G11B 5/39] using magneto-resistive devices -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus that employ magneto-resistive elements to detect or write magnetic states on a moving medium, such as hard-disk read/write heads that use the change in resistance of a thin-film sensor to sense magnetic fields, allowing data recording via magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/3903] using magnetic thin film layers or their effects, the films being part of integrated structures -- This subclass covers recording methods that employ magnetic thin-film layers as part of integrated structures, using the films' magnetic properties or their effects to store data on a carrier. It includes techniques where the thin films serve as the recording medium within a device, enabling magnetisation or demagnetisation for data encoding.
[G11B 5/3906] Details related to the use of magnetic thin film layers or to their effects -- This subclass (G11B 5/3906) addresses technical details of magnetic thin-film layers employed in data recording, covering their composition, structure, manufacturing processes and the magnetic effects they produce that influence the performance of magnetisation-based storage devices.
[G11B 5/3909] Arrangements using a magnetic tunnel junction -- The code G11B 5/3909 covers arrangements that employ a magnetic tunnel junction as the active element for recording or sensing magnetic states in a storage device. Such junctions consist of two ferromagnetic layers separated by an ultrathin insulating barrier, enabling tunneling magnetoresistance used in MRAM, read heads and other magnetic recording applications.
[G11B 5/3912] Arrangements in which the active read-out elements are transducing in association with active magnetic shields, e.g. magnetically coupled shields  G11B5/3916  takes precedence -- G11B 5/3912 covers magnetic recording arrangements in which the active read-out elements operate together with active magnetic shields, such as magnetically coupled shield structures that enhance signal quality. The more specific subclass G11B 5/3916 is treated as preceding this entry.
[G11B 5/3916] Arrangements in which the active read-out elements are coupled to the magnetic flux of the track by at least one magnetic thin film flux guide -- The entry defines a magnetic-storage arrangement where the sensing elements are linked to the track's magnetic flux through at least one thin magnetic film that acts as a flux guide, directing the magnetic field from the recorded track to the read-out components.
[G11B 5/3919] the guide being interposed in the flux path -- This subclass covers magnetic recording arrangements that insert a guide or flux-concentrating element in the magnetic flux path to steer, shape or enhance the magnetic field between the transducer and the moving record carrier.
[G11B 5/3922] the read-out elements being disposed in magnetic shunt relative to at least two parts of the flux guide structure -- This CPC subclass (G11B 5/3922) covers magnetic reading arrangements in which the read-out elements are placed in a magnetic shunt that bridges at least two portions of the flux-guide structure, as used in recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier.
[G11B 5/3925] the two parts being thin films -- G11B 5/3925 covers magnetic recording arrangements in which both the record carrier and the transducer (or magnetic layer and substrate) are realised as thin-film structures, allowing close coupling and high-density data storage through magnetisation or demagnetisation of the thin film.
[G11B 5/3929] Disposition of magnetic thin films not used for directly coupling magnetic flux from the track to the MR film or for shielding -- The CPC subclass G11B 5/3929 covers the disposition of magnetic thin-film components that are not employed to directly couple magnetic flux from a recording track to a magnetoresistive sensor or to provide magnetic shielding, such as surplus, waste, recycling or disposal of these films in magnetic storage devices.
[G11B 5/3932] Magnetic biasing films -- Magnetic biasing films are thin magnetic layers applied to the surface of a recording medium to create a uniform bias field during magnetisation or demagnetisation. The bias improves linearity, reduces noise and stabilises domain formation, thereby enhancing the performance of magnetic tape, disk or similar carriers that move relative to a transducer.
[G11B 5/3935] Flux closure films not being part of the track flux guides -- The subclass G11B 5/3935 refers to magnetic recording devices that employ flux-closure films which are not incorporated into the track flux guides. These separate films provide a magnetic path to improve signal quality or reduce stray fields in media where the carrier moves relative to the read/write head.
[G11B 5/3938] the flux closure films being used for absorbing or reducing demagnetisating or saturating fields -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 5/3938 pertains to magnetic recording technology, specifically addressing the use of flux closure films designed to absorb or reduce demagnetizing or saturating fields in the recording process. This technique enhances data storage efficiency and reliability by mitigating unwanted magnetic effects.
[G11B 5/3941] the flux closure films being used for providing a closed magnetic circuit to the MR film -- G11B 5/3941 covers the use of flux-closure films that create a closed magnetic circuit around a magnetoresistive (MR) layer in magnetic recording media. The films channel magnetic flux to improve the MR film's response during recording or playback, reducing stray fields and enhancing signal fidelity.
[G11B 5/3945] Heads comprising more than one sensitive element -- The subclass G11B 5/3945 covers magnetic recording heads that incorporate two or more sensitive transducer elements within a single head assembly, enabling multiple read/write gaps or combined sensing functions for improved data handling or multi-track operation.
[G11B 5/3948] the sensitive elements being active read-out elements -- This CPC subclass covers magnetic recording systems in which the sensing elements that detect stored bits also serve as active read-out components, providing the signal directly rather than merely acting as passive detectors. It applies to devices where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer and data are written by magnetising or demagnetising the medium.
[G11B 5/3951] the active elements being arranged on several parallel planes -- A magnetic recording device in which the active elements, such as write or read heads, are positioned on several parallel planes rather than a single layer, allowing multiple data tracks to be written or read simultaneously and increasing recording density. This arrangement is used in systems that record by magnetising or demagnetising a moving carrier.
[G11B 5/3954] the active elements transducing on a single track -- This CPC subclass G11B 5/3954 covers magnetic recording where the transducing elements operate on a single track of the medium, the record carrier moving relative to the read/write head, and the information is stored through magnetisation or demagnetisation of the carrier.
[G11B 5/3958] the active elements being arranged in a single plane, e.g. "matrix" disposition -- The subclass G11B 5/3958 concerns magnetic recording systems where the active elements, such as heads or transducers, are arranged in a single-plane or matrix layout to interact uniformly with a moving record carrier. It falls under physics, information storage based on relative motion, and recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/3961] disposed at an angle to the direction of the track or relative movement -- A subclass for magnetic recording in which the recording head or transducer is positioned at an angle to the direction of the track or relative movement, influencing how the carrier is magnetised or demagnetised during operation.
[G11B 5/3964] for transducing on a single track -- This subclass covers magnetic recording methods in which a transducer reads or writes data on a single magnetic track while the record carrier moves relative to the transducer. It pertains to devices that use magnetisation or demagnetisation to store information on one track at a time.
[G11B 5/3967] Composite structural arrangements of transducers, e.g. inductive write and magnetoresistive read  G11B5/3906  takes precedence -- This subclass relates to composite transducer structures that integrate an inductive write element with a magnetoresistive read element for magnetic recording on moving media. It deals with the combined arrangement of these components to achieve recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of the record carrier.
[G11B 5/397] with a plurality of independent magnetoresistive active read-out elements for respectively transducing from selected components -- The G11B 5/397 subclass covers magnetic recording devices where data are read by several separate magnetoresistive sensors, each independently converting selected components of the recorded magnetic field into an electrical signal, typically in systems with relative motion between a record carrier and the read head.
[G11B 5/3974] from the same information track, e.g. frequency bands -- This subgroup concerns magnetic recording on a single track that is divided into separate frequency bands or zones, each carrying distinct information. It addresses methods of assigning different portions of the track to different signal frequencies to increase capacity or enable multiplexed data retrieval.
[G11B 5/3977] from different information tracks -- G11B 5/3977 covers magnetic recording methods that write or read data from separate information tracks on a storage medium, distinguishing each track's magnetic pattern from the others. It applies to techniques where the relative movement between the carrier and transducer is used to access distinct tracks for storing or retrieving information.
[G11B 5/398] Specially shaped layers -- G11B 5/398 concerns magnetic recording media that incorporate specially shaped layers, such as patterned, corrugated or non-planar magnetic films, designed to control magnetic domains and improve recording density or signal quality. The classification applies to any record carrier where the geometry of the magnetic layer itself is deliberately formed to achieve these effects.
[G11B 5/3983] with current confined paths in the spacer layer -- The entry G11B 5/3983 covers magnetic recording processes in which the electric current that writes or reads data is limited to defined, confined paths within the spacer layer separating the transducer from the magnetic medium. This approach improves precision and reduces stray fields in devices such as thin-film magnetic heads.
[G11B 5/3987] with provision for closing the magnetic flux during operation -- G11B 5/3987 classifies magnetic recording or erasing methods where the device includes a feature that closes the magnetic flux path during operation, such as a magnetic circuit with a movable yoke or a flux-closing sleeve. This arrangement reduces stray fields, improves write efficiency, and can protect the media.
[G11B 5/399] with intrinsic biasing, e.g. provided by equipotential strips -- G11B 5/399 covers magnetic recording techniques in which the record medium itself provides an intrinsic bias, for example through equipotential strips, allowing magnetisation or demagnetisation without an external bias field. This intrinsic bias simplifies head design and can improve signal stability.
[G11B 5/3993] in semi-conductors -- G11B 5/3993 covers magnetic recording techniques that use semiconductor substrates as the record carrier. It addresses the creation, alteration or removal of magnetic domains in semiconductor materials for storing information, typically by magnetisation or demagnetisation processes while the carrier moves relative to a transducer.
[G11B 5/40] Protective measures on heads, e.g. against excessive temperature -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Measures and methods (e.g. control of the operating functions) to protect the head against damages, e.g. against excessive temperature, head-record carrier collisions (means for their prediction, detection and avoidance), wear.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fluid-dynamic spacing of heads from record carriers per se -> G11B5/60
[G11B 5/41] Cleaning of heads -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cleaning of record carriers -> G11B23/50
[G11B 5/455] Arrangements for functional testing of heads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Functional testing of the heads when the manufacturing is completed and arrangements therefore, e.g. spin stands or test beds.
[G11B 5/4555] by using a spin-stand, i.e. a spinning disc or simulator -- This subclass covers magnetic recording methods that employ a spin-stand, where a rotating disc or its simulator moves relative to the transducer to write or erase data by magnetisation or demagnetisation. It is used for testing or developing magnetic recording heads and media under controlled relative motion.
[G11B 5/465] Arrangements for demagnetisation of heads -- The G11B 5/465 subclass covers designs and mechanisms used to demagnetise recording heads in magnetic storage devices, ensuring reliable operation by removing residual magnetic fields after use. It includes various arrangements such as coil-generated fields, permanent magnet configurations, or electronic pulse methods that reset head magnetisation. This helps prevent unwanted signal retention and improves data integrity.
[G11B 5/48] Disposition or mounting of heads relative to record carriers -- G11B 5/48 deals with the ways magnetic read/write heads are positioned and mounted relative to the moving record carrier, covering the geometry, support structures and alignment mechanisms that maintain proper head-carrier spacing and tracking during operation.
[G11B 5/4806] specially adapted for disk drive assemblies, e.g. assembly prior to operation, hard or flexible disk drives  G11B5/488  -  G11B5/54  take precedence -- This subclass covers components specially adapted for disk drive assemblies, such as pre-operation configurations of hard or flexible disk drives, and falls within the hierarchy of recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier. It is subject to the more general groups G11B5/488 through G11B5/54, which have precedence over it.
[G11B 5/4813] Mounting or aligning of arm assemblies, e.g. actuator arm supported by bearings, multiple arm assemblies, arm stacks or multiple heads on single arm  G11B5/484  takes precedence -- G11B 5/4813 pertains to the precise mounting and alignment of actuator arms in magnetic recording devices, addressing configurations such as multiple arm assemblies and head arrangements. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy focusing on information storage technologies involving relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/4826] Mounting, aligning or attachment of the transducer head relative to the arm assembly, e.g. slider holding members, gimbals, adhesive  G11B5/484  takes precedence; details of head housings or structures  G11B5/10 ,  G11B5/127 ; adjustment relative to the record carrier  G11B5/56 -- This CPC subclass deals with the methods and devices for mounting, aligning, or attaching the magnetic transducer head to the arm assembly of a recording/playback device, such as slider holders, gimbals, and adhesives. It falls under the physics field, information storage based on relative movement, specifically recording by magnetisation. The subclass supersedes G11B5/484 and is related to head housings (G11B5/10, G11B5/127) and adjustment relative to the record carrier (G11B5/56).
[G11B 5/483] Piezoelectric devices between head and arm, e.g. for fine adjustment -- G11B 5/483 classifies piezoelectric elements mounted between a magnetic recording head and its supporting arm that provide fine mechanical adjustment of the head-to-medium spacing during magnetisation-based recording, a subcategory of information-storage devices using relative movement.
[G11B 5/4833] Structure of the arm assembly, e.g. load beams, flexures, parts of the arm adapted for controlling vertical force on the head  G11B5/484  takes precedence -- The G11B 5/4833 classification covers the mechanical architecture of the arm assembly in magnetic recording heads, including load beams, flexures and other components designed to regulate the vertical force exerted on the head. This entry refines the broader category of recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier.
[G11B 5/484] Integrated arm assemblies, e.g. formed by material deposition or by etching from single piece of metal or by lamination of materials forming a single arm/suspension/head unit -- G11B 5/484 covers magnetic recording heads whose arm, suspension and read/write head are fabricated as a single integrated component. The arm may be produced by material deposition, etching of a solid metal piece, or lamination of multiple layers, yielding a monolithic or quasi-monolithic arm-suspension-head unit.
[G11B 5/4846] Constructional details of the electrical connection between arm and support -- This subclass concerns the mechanical and electrical design of the link that supplies power or signal between the moving arm of a magnetic recording head and its supporting structure. It includes features such as contacts, flex-circuits, connectors and mounting arrangements that ensure reliable electrical continuity during relative movement.
[G11B 5/4853] Constructional details of the electrical connection between head and arm -- G11B 5/4853 covers the design and construction of the electrical link that connects the magnetic read/write head to the arm in devices where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer. It includes features such as wiring, connectors, flex circuits and mounting arrangements that ensure reliable signal transmission during recording and playback.
[G11B 5/486] with provision for mounting or arranging electrical conducting means or circuits on or along the arm assembly -- The subclass G11B 5/486 covers magnetic recording devices where the arm assembly incorporates provisions for mounting or arranging electrical conductors or circuits on or along the arm. It is part of the hierarchy for physics-based information storage that uses relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer, specifically recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of the carrier.
[G11B 5/4866] the arm comprising an optical waveguide, e.g. for thermally-assisted recording -- G11B 5/4866 relates to magnetic recording heads whose arm incorporates an optical waveguide, a feature used in thermally-assisted recording. It is classified under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer → recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier.
[G11B 5/4873] the arm comprising piezoelectric or other actuators for adjustment of the arm -- A magnetic-recording head arm that includes piezoelectric or other actuators to fine-tune its position relative to the moving record carrier. The actuators provide precise angular or linear adjustments, improving tracking accuracy and signal quality during magnetisation or demagnetisation of the medium.
[G11B 5/488] Disposition of heads  G11B5/49 ,  G11B5/52  take precedence -- G11B5/488 relates to the mechanical arrangement and movement of magnetic recording heads in devices where the head and the magnetic medium move relative to each other. It covers head positioning, parking, loading, unloading, and alignment mechanisms that ensure proper contact or spacing during recording and playback. The code is applied when describing head disposition in magnetic tape or disk systems.
[G11B 5/4886] relative to rotating disc -- The code G11B 5/4886 covers magnetic recording techniques that use a rotating disc as the record carrier, defining how data is written or read by the relative motion between the disc and a magnetic transducer. It includes head positioning, disc rotation speed and related control methods.
[G11B 5/4893] relative to moving tape -- This subclass concerns magnetic recording on a moving tape, covering the transport, tensioning, guiding and positioning of the tape relative to the read/write head during recording or playback. It includes mechanisms that create relative motion between the tape and the transducer for magnetisation or demagnetisation of the record carrier.
[G11B 5/49] Fixed mounting -- G11B 5/49 covers magnetic recording devices in which the record carrier is held in a fixed position rather than being moved or rotated during operation. The fixed mounting ensures stable alignment between the magnetic medium and the transducer, improving reliability and signal fidelity.
[G11B 5/4907] Details for scanning  G11B5/4969  takes precedence -- G11B 5/4907 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to specific details for scanning magnetic recording carriers, with a note that G11B 5/4969 takes precedence. This classification is part of the broader category dealing with information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/4915] Structure of specially adapted heads  G11B5/3906  takes precedence -- G11B 5/4915 pertains to the architecture and design details of magnetic recording heads that are specially adapted for specific recording or playback tasks. It includes features such as shape, magnetic gap, pole material, and shielding tailored to enhance performance in systems where the record-carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 5/4923] in which zones of the transducing part are being physically controllable -- G11B 5/4923 refers to magnetic or demagnetic recording processes where the transducer is divided into distinct zones that can be individually controlled, allowing selective magnetisation of parts of the record carrier. This enables variable recording density, localized erasing, or region-specific data handling within the magnetic medium.
[G11B 5/493] Control of magnetic properties, e.g. saturation, anisotropy -- G11B 5/493 covers methods and devices for adjusting the magnetic characteristics of a recording medium, such as its saturation level, anisotropy, coercivity or permeability, to improve or modify how data are magnetised or demagnetised during storage. This includes techniques for material treatment, field application or temperature control that influence the medium's magnetic response.
[G11B 5/4938] of thin magnetic films -- The G11B 5/4938 subclass covers recording by magnetising or demagnetising a record carrier that employs thin magnetic films, where information is stored through the relative movement between the carrier and a transducer. It falls under physics, information storage, and specifically storage based on relative motion.
[G11B 5/4946] for formation or displacement of magnetic domains, e.g. walls, bubbles -- The subclass G11B 5/4946 addresses techniques for forming or shifting magnetic domains-including domain walls or magnetic bubbles-within a record carrier used for magnetic recording. It pertains to information-storage methods that rely on relative movement between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 5/4953] part of the structure being mechanically or magnetically coupled to or decoupled from, the transducing part -- This subclass covers devices in which a portion of the recording structure is mechanically or magnetically linked to, or separated from, the transducing element, thereby controlling the relative motion of the carrier during magnetic recording or playback.
[G11B 5/4961] Circuits -- G11B 5/4961 pertains to circuits employed in magnetic recording and demagnetisation processes, including those that drive read/write heads, control magnetic field strength, or manage signal timing for data storage on moving record carriers.
[G11B 5/4969] Details for track selection or addressing -- G11B 5/4969 pertains to the mechanisms and control techniques used to select or address individual tracks on a magnetic recording medium, including track identification, addressing formats, and the signals that direct the read/write head to the desired track.
[G11B 5/4976] Disposition of heads, e.g. matrix arrangement -- G11B 5/4976 covers the spatial arrangement of magnetic read/write heads in devices where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer, including matrix configurations and other head dispositions designed to optimise magnetisation-based recording and playback.
[G11B 5/4984] Structure of specially adapted switching heads  G11B5/3958  takes precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 5/4984 pertains to the structure of specially adapted switching heads used in magnetic recording technology, with a note that classification G11B 5/3958 should be considered first if applicable. This entry is part of the broader category dealing with information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/4992] Circuits -- G11B 5/4992 covers the electrical circuits used in magnetic recording and erasing where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer, including the drive and bias circuitry that control magnetisation and demagnetisation of the storage medium.
[G11B 5/50] Interchangeable mountings, e.g. for replacement of head without readjustment -- This subclass covers mounting systems for magnetic recording heads that can be swapped or replaced without the need to readjust the head-to-medium spacing. The design allows interchangeable head units, simplifying maintenance and ensuring consistent recording or playback performance.
[G11B 5/52] with simultaneous movement of head and record carrier, e.g. rotation of head -- G11B 5/52 covers magnetic recording methods where the transducer and the magnetic medium move relative to each other at the same time, such as when the recording head rotates while the medium also moves. This simultaneous motion can improve data density or writing performance.
[G11B 5/53] Disposition or mounting of heads on rotating support -- This class covers how magnetic read/write heads are positioned or attached to a rotating medium such as a disk, including their arrangement, mounting mechanisms and adjustment features that enable precise alignment with the record surface.
[G11B 5/531] Disposition of more than one recording or reproducing head on support rotating cyclically around an axis -- This subclass concerns magnetic recording or playback arrangements that employ two or more recording or reproducing heads mounted on a support which rotates cyclically around an axis, allowing the heads to sequentially or simultaneously contact the magnetic medium. It relates to devices where the rotating carrier carries multiple heads for creating or retrieving magnetised tracks.
[G11B 5/532] Parallel to the direction of movement of the tape, e.g. for transversal scanning -- This subclass concerns magnetic recording methods where the recorded tracks on a moving tape are oriented parallel to the tape's travel direction, such as in transversal scanning systems. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/534] inclined relative to the direction of movement of the tape, e.g. for helicoidal scanning -- The subclass covers magnetic recording or playback heads that are positioned at an angle to the direction the tape moves, as used in helical-scan systems. By scanning diagonally across the moving record carrier, the head writes or reads data in inclined tracks rather than straight across.
[G11B 5/535] perpendicular to the direction of movement of the tape, e.g. for longitudinal scanning -- The subclass G11B 5/535 deals with magnetic recording where the transducer or recording head is positioned perpendicular to the direction the tape moves, enabling longitudinal scanning of the media. It falls under information storage methods that rely on relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer.
[G11B 5/537] with all the heads disposed in a plane substantially parallel to the plane of the tape, e.g. for circular scanning -- The subclass refers to magnetic recording arrangements in which every read/write head is positioned in a single plane that is essentially parallel to the tape surface, allowing the heads to scan the tape in patterns such as circular scanning.
[G11B 5/538] Disposition or mounting of pole pieces on rotating support  magnetic switching of fixed head arrangements  G11B5/49 -- The subclass G11B 5/538 covers the arrangement and mounting of magnetic pole pieces on a rotating support as well as magnetic switching schemes for fixed-head recording heads. It relates to devices that use the relative motion between the record carrier and transducer to write or read data, focusing on how pole elements are positioned to generate the required magnetic fields.
[G11B 5/54] with provision for moving the head into or out of its operative position or across tracks -- This subclass covers magnetic recording arrangements in which the read-write head can be moved into or out of its operative position or shifted laterally across tracks. Such head movement is employed in tape drives, hard-disk units and similar devices to enable track selection, head parking or reduced wear.
[G11B 5/55] Track change, selection or acquisition by displacement of the head -- This subclass of G11B 5/55 addresses magnetic recording systems where the recording or playback track is changed, selected, or accessed by physically moving the transducer head relative to a stationary magnetic medium. It covers head-displacement mechanisms that adjust tracking, engage specific tracks, or acquire data without moving the record carrier.
[G11B 5/5504] across tape tracks -- G11B 5/5504 refers to magnetic recording methods where data are written across multiple parallel tracks of a magnetic tape, rather than along a single linear path. This technique involves magnetising or demagnetising sections of the tape carrier to store information, improving density and reliability in tape-based storage systems.
[G11B 5/5508] Control circuits therefor  G11B5/5513  takes precedence -- G11B 5/5508 pertains to control circuits for recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation, specifically addressing the operational management and regulation of magnetic recording processes. However, for more specific aspects, classification G11B 5/5513 should be consulted as it takes precedence.
[G11B 5/5513] Specially adapted for transducing in both travelling directions of tape -- G11B 5/5513 covers tape recording devices specially adapted to transduce signals while the tape moves in either direction, allowing recording and playback in both travelling directions of the tape.
[G11B 5/5517] Controlled by automatic tape drive reversing arrangement  reversing tape drive arrangements  G11B15/444 -- This CPC subclass covers magnetic recording devices where the tape motion is automatically reversed by a mechanism that drives the tape in opposite directions without manual intervention, used in tape drive systems for data storage. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/5521] across disk tracks  spiral track following  G11B5/596 -- Relates to magnetic recording on rotating disks where the transducer moves across adjacent tracks in a continuous spiral path, enabling data to be written or read along a single, unbroken track that follows the disk's rotation.
[G11B 5/5526] Control therefor; circuits, track configurations or relative disposition of servo-information transducers and servo-information tracks for control thereof  G11B5/556  takes precedence -- The G11B 5/5526 entry concerns the control systems for magnetic recording, including the electronic circuits and the layout or positioning of servo-information transducers and their corresponding servo tracks that manage magnetisation or demagnetisation of the medium. It specifies how these components are arranged to ensure precise tracking and head alignment during recording, and is subordinate to the broader G11B 5/556 category.
[G11B 5/553] Details -- G11B 5/553 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to specific details concerning the recording process by magnetisation or demagnetisation on a storage medium, under the broader category of information storage involving relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/5534] Initialisation, calibration, e.g. cylinder "set-up" -- G11B 5/5534 covers methods and apparatus for initialising or calibrating magnetic recording media, such as setting the magnetic properties of a cylinder or disc before data writing. It includes procedures to adjust bias, erase residual magnetisation, and verify the media's magnetic response to ensure reliable storage.
[G11B 5/5539] Skew adjustment, e.g. adjustment of the position of the first sector in each track with respect to the other tracks, for improving, e.g. access performance -- This CPC subclass covers methods for adjusting the angular offset, or skew, of the first sector on each track of a magnetic storage disk relative to the other tracks to improve access performance. It is situated under physics, information storage, and magnetic recording.
[G11B 5/5543] servo-format therefor -- G11B 5/5543 relates to servo-format techniques in magnetic recording, where servo tracks encoded on the record carrier guide the movement of the read/write head. The embedded servo information provides continuous feedback, enabling precise positioning of the transducer relative to the moving medium during magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/5547] "Seek" control and circuits therefor  G11B5/5556  takes precedence -- The CPC entry G11B 5/5547 covers the electronic control mechanisms and circuits that manage "seek" operations in magnetic recording devices, such as the positioning of read/write heads relative to a moving record carrier. It defines the functional elements that direct the carrier-to-head motion; the narrower subclass G11B 5/5556 supersedes it.
[G11B 5/5552] using fine positioning means for track acquisition separate from the coarse (e.g. track changing) positioning means -- The subclass G11B 5/5552 concerns magnetic recording devices that employ separate fine-positioning mechanisms to acquire a track, distinct from the coarse positioning used for larger track changes.
