It focuses on the precise alignment of the read/write head to a specific track while the overall head motion is controlled by a different system.
[G11B 5/5556] with track following after a "seek" -- G11B 5/5556 covers magnetic recording or reproducing devices that, after a seek operation, employ track-following to keep the transducer aligned with the recorded track. The classification includes servo-controlled mechanisms that adjust the head position during read/write to maintain proper track alignment.
[G11B 5/556] control circuits therefor -- G11B 5/556 covers control circuits for magnetic recording devices that store information by magnetising or demagnetising a moving record carrier, including the electronic hardware that drives the write head, regulates current and timing, and interfaces with signal processing.
[G11B 5/5565] system adaptation for compensation of variations of physical parameters, e.g. temperature -- G11B 5/5565 covers magnetic recording systems that include adaptive mechanisms to offset changes in physical conditions such as temperature, ensuring consistent magnetisation of the media. These adaptations may involve feedback loops, sensor-based adjustments, or control algorithms to maintain recording quality.
[G11B 5/5569] details of specially adapted mobile parts, e.g. electromechanical control devices  motors in general  H02K -- The subclass G11B 5/5569 concerns the design and engineering of specially adapted mobile components used in magnetic recording systems, such as electromechanical control devices and motors that drive the relative motion between a magnetic medium and its transducer. It addresses the configuration, actuation and control features of these moving parts to optimise magnetisation or demagnetisation of the record carrier.
[G11B 5/5573] Details of the magnetic circuit, e.g. of actuators -- G11B 5/5573 pertains to the design and specification of the magnetic circuit within actuators used for recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a storage medium. It covers features such as magnetic path geometry, core material, flux focusing and field-shaping elements that affect the motion and force of the transducer. This sub-class falls under information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/5578] Multiple actuators addressing the same disk, e.g. to improve data rate or access rate -- G11B 5/5578 covers magnetic disk storage devices that use two or more actuators to position read/write heads on the same platter, enabling simultaneous or faster head movement. The arrangement is intended to raise data-transfer rates or reduce access times by addressing the same disk with multiple actuators.
[G11B 5/5582] system adaptation for working during or after external perturbation, e.g. in the presence of a mechanical oscillation caused by a shock -- This subclass pertains to magnetic recording systems that incorporate means to continue operating or recover operation when exposed to external disturbances such as mechanical oscillations or shocks. It includes adaptive control, shock-absorbing structures or compensation techniques that keep the record carrier and transducer moving relative to each other and preserve data integrity during or after the perturbation.
[G11B 5/5586] Minimising seek noise, e.g. actuator noise -- The CPC entry G11B 5/5586 covers techniques for reducing or eliminating seek noise, such as actuator noise, in magnetic recording systems where a read/write head moves relative to the media. It includes methods that minimise vibration, acoustic emission or mechanical disturbances generated during head positioning, thereby improving signal quality and reliability of the recorded information.
[G11B 5/5591] across drum tracks -- G11B 5/5591 relates to magnetic recording on drums where the transducer operates across several parallel tracks on the rotating record carrier. It addresses methods for moving the head or the drum to write or read data laterally across the drum surface, enabling multi-track recording or retrieval.
[G11B 5/5595] Control circuits therefor -- The G11B 5/5595 subclass comprises control circuits for magnetic recording devices in which a record carrier moves relative to a transducer, covering electronic or electromechanical circuitry that regulates the magnetisation and demagnetisation processes during data storage.
[G11B 5/56] with provision for moving the head for the purpose of adjusting the position of the head relative to the record carrier, e.g. manual adjustment for azimuth correction or track centering -- G11B 5/56 covers magnetic recording or playback devices that allow the transducing head to be shifted, often manually, in order to fine-tune its position relative to the moving record carrier. This adjustment is used for azimuth correction, track centering or other alignment tasks that improve signal quality. The subclass falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative motion between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/58] with provision for moving the head for the purpose of maintaining alignment of the head relative to the record carrier during transducing operation, e.g. to compensate for surface irregularities of the latter or for track following -- G11B 5/58 covers magnetic recording devices where the transducing head is moved during operation to keep it aligned with the record carrier, allowing compensation for surface irregularities or enabling track-following while recording or playback. This sub-class falls under information storage based on relative movement between carrier and head.
[G11B 5/581] maintaining desired contact or spacing by direct interaction of forces generated between heads or supports thereof and record carriers or supports thereof, e.g. attraction-repulsion interactions -- This classification covers methods for controlling the gap or contact between magnetic heads and the storage medium by using forces that arise directly between them, such as magnetic attraction or repulsion, to maintain optimal spacing during recording or playback. It applies to devices where the head and carrier interact without separate control mechanisms.
[G11B 5/582] interactions in a magnetic field -- G11B 5/582 covers the effects and phenomena that arise when a magnetic field interacts with a magnetically recorded medium, including influences on magnetisation, demagnetisation and the behavior of the record carrier during recording or playback.
[G11B 5/583] using repulsion generated by superconductors in a magnetic field, e.g. by "Meissner effect" -- G11B 5/583 covers methods of recording magnetic information on a medium by using the repulsive force created when superconductors expel magnetic fields (Meissner effect). The technique leverages superconductors to manipulate magnetic fields for data writing, offering a novel approach within magnetic recording technologies.
[G11B 5/584] for track following on tapes -- G11B 5/584 relates to magnetic recording systems that incorporate mechanisms for maintaining the correct tape path, specifically track-following techniques used on magnetic tapes to keep the read/write head aligned with the recorded tracks during operation.
[G11B 5/588] by controlling the position of the rotating heads -- G11B 5/588 covers magnetic recording methods that control the position of rotating heads to write or erase data on a moving record carrier, such as in hard-disk drives where precise head placement determines the magnetised tracks. This subclass falls under information storage based on relative movement between the carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/592] using bimorph elements supporting the heads -- The G11B 5/592 subclass covers magnetic recording or erasing where the transducer head is mounted on a bimorph actuator that bends to move the head relative to the record carrier, providing precise control of head-media spacing and motion.
[G11B 5/5921] using auxiliary signals, e.g. pilot signals -- This CPC subclass covers methods of recording magnetic data on a medium where auxiliary signals such as pilot tones are employed to improve tracking, synchronization or error correction. It relates to systems that generate a supplementary signal alongside the main data to assist head positioning or signal processing during magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/5922] superimposed on the main signal -- G11B 5/5922 covers magnetic recording methods in which an additional signal is superimposed on the main recorded signal, such as a pilot tone, bias, or control track that is written together with the primary data on the same carrier. This technique is used to improve readout, synchronization or error detection while the carrier is magnetised or demagnetised.
[G11B 5/5923] recorded in horizontal suppression internal of video frame -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 5/5923, under the broader categories of Physics and Information Storage, specifically addresses techniques for recording information by magnetisation or demagnetisation within a video frame's horizontal suppression interval.
[G11B 5/5925] recorded in vertical suppression internal of video frame -- The subclass G11B 5/5925 covers magnetic recording methods in which a video signal is written on a carrier by magnetisation or demagnetisation and a vertical-suppression technique is applied within each video frame to reduce interference or improve picture quality, encompassing the devices and processes that implement this internal suppression during recording.
[G11B 5/5926] recorded in separate tracks, e.g. servo tracks -- The G11B 5/5926 class covers magnetic recording where the record carrier is divided into distinct tracks, such as separate servo or positioning tracks, alongside the data tracks. It addresses methods and devices that write or read information using these individual tracks to aid in precise head alignment and control.
[G11B 5/5927] Helicoidal tracks -- The code G11B 5/5927 refers to helicoidal recording tracks, where magnetic data is written along a helical path on a moving record carrier. This arrangement, used in magnetic tape formats such as compact cassettes, allows high-density information storage by magnetising or demagnetising the carrier in a spiral pattern.
[G11B 5/5928] Longitudinal tracks -- G11B 5/5928 covers magnetic recording media in which data are stored in longitudinal tracks that run parallel to the direction of motion of the record carrier relative to the transducer. It includes the geometry, layout and spacing of such tracks on tapes, disks or strips.
[G11B 5/596] for track following on disks -- The subclass addresses magnetic recording systems that maintain alignment of the read/write head with the data track on rotating disks, providing mechanisms for detecting and correcting lateral deviations during operation. It falls under information storage that relies on relative movement between the record carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 5/59605] Circuits  G11B5/59627  -  G11B5/59688  take precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 5/59605 pertains to circuits used in the process of recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation, specifically noting that more detailed classifications from G11B5/59627 to G11B5/59688 take precedence. This classification is part of a broader hierarchy dealing with information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/59611] Detection or processing of peak/envelop signals -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for detecting or processing peak or envelope signals in magnetic recording systems where the magnetic medium moves relative to the transducer. It includes signal conditioning, peak detection and envelope extraction to enhance read/write performance.
[G11B 5/59616] Synchronisation; Clocking  G11B5/59622  takes precedence -- G11B 5/59616 covers methods and apparatus that provide synchronization or clocking for magnetic recording, ensuring the timing of magnetisation and demagnetisation of the record carrier matches the transducer's operation. It deals with techniques that align the movement of the recording medium with the data signal to maintain proper data integrity. The more specific subclass G11B 5/59622 supersedes it.
[G11B 5/59622] Gain control; Filters -- The CPC entry G11B 5/59622 falls under physics, information storage based on relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer, specifically magnetic recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation. It covers circuitry and methods for controlling gain and implementing filters in magnetic recording heads or playback devices, ensuring proper signal amplification and noise reduction during the recording and retrieval processes.
[G11B 5/59627] Aligning for runout, eccentricity or offset compensation  G11B5/5534 ,  G11B5/59677 ,  G11B5/59688  take precedence -- The G11B 5/59627 entry covers techniques for correcting mechanical misalignments-such as runout, eccentricity or offset-during magnetic recording, ensuring the transducer and record carrier are properly aligned for accurate data imprinting. It supersedes related sub-entries G11B 5/5534, G11B 5/59677 and G11B 5/59688.
[G11B 5/59633] Servo formatting  G11B5/59627 ,  G11B5/59677 ,  G11B5/59683 ,  G11B5/59688  take precedence -- G11B5/59633 covers methods and apparatus for servo formatting of magnetic storage media, i.e., the creation of reference tracks that aid precise positioning of the read/write head relative to the moving record carrier.
[G11B 5/59638] Servo formatting apparatuses, e.g. servo-writers -- The G11B 5/59638 subclass covers apparatuses that format magnetic recording media by creating or writing servo tracks, such as servo-writers, which generate precise positioning signals to control head alignment during subsequent data recording. These devices ensure accurate track spacing and timing for reliable read/write operations.
[G11B 5/59644] Acquisition or selection of servo format from a system reference  after track seek  G11B5/5556 -- A method for acquiring or selecting the servo format of a magnetic recording system from a stored system reference after a track-seek operation, enabling precise positioning of the read/write head relative to the recorded tracks. This includes using the reference data to determine the proper servo pattern for subsequent data acquisition or tracking.
[G11B 5/5965] Embedded servo format  G11B5/59655  takes precedence -- G11B 5/5965 covers magnetic recording methods that embed servo information directly within the data tracks of a magnetised record carrier, enabling precise head positioning without separate servo zones. The entry defines the embedded servo format as a technique that integrates positioning signals into the recorded signal pattern, superseding earlier servo arrangements.
[G11B 5/59655] Sector, sample or burst servo format -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 5/59655 pertains to the specific formatting used in magnetic recording for sector, sample, or burst servo information, which aids in the precise tracking and positioning of read/write heads on a storage medium. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy under physics, specifically addressing information storage technologies that involve relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/59661] Spiral servo format -- G11B 5/59661 covers the use of a spiral-shaped servo track on a magnetic recording medium to supply positioning information for read/write heads, allowing precise tracking during data recording and retrieval. The format embeds the servo signal in a continuous spiral pattern rather than in separate concentric circles.
[G11B 5/59666] Self servo writing -- A self-servo writing system uses magnetic recording where the write head automatically controls its own position relative to the moving medium by extracting servo information from the recorded data itself, eliminating the need for separate guide tracks. This enables precise tracking during magnetisation or demagnetisation of the record carrier as it moves past the transducer.
[G11B 5/59672] Servo re-writing, e.g. for the correction of offsets or 'fading' of servo marks -- The entry covers techniques for re-writing servo information on magnetic media to correct positional offsets or fading of servo marks, allowing the recording system to maintain accurate tracking between the record carrier and transducer during operation. It falls under magnetic recording methods within information storage based on relative movement.
[G11B 5/59677] with optical servo tracking -- The G11B 5/59677 class covers magnetic recording systems that incorporate an optical servo mechanism to track the relative motion between the magnetic record carrier and the transducer, enhancing head positioning accuracy and data density.
[G11B 5/59683] for magnetoresistive heads -- This subgroup covers magnetic recording systems that employ magnetoresistive heads, which sense magnetic flux changes on a moving medium via the magnetoresistive effect. Such heads are used to detect or alter the magnetisation of the carrier in data storage devices.
[G11B 5/59688] Servo signal format patterns or signal processing thereof, e.g. dual, tri, quad, burst signal patterns -- G11B 5/59688 covers the definition and processing of servo-signal formats used in magnetic recording, including dual, triple, quad and burst-type patterns that guide the relative motion between the record carrier and the transducer. It falls under physics → information storage → magnetic recording.
[G11B 5/59694] System adaptation for working during or after external perturbation, e.g. in the presence of a mechanical oscillation caused by a shock -- G11B 5/59694 classifies magnetic recording devices that include adaptive features enabling them to continue operating or resume function during or after external disturbances such as mechanical oscillations caused by shock-induced vibration, ensuring reliable data storage despite relative movement between the medium and the transducer.
[G11B 5/60] Fluid-dynamic spacing of heads from record-carriers -- G11B 5/60 concerns the use of fluid-dynamic forces to keep a magnetic read/write head at a controlled distance from a moving record carrier such as magnetic tape or disk. It covers designs that generate air or other fluid flow to create a bearing that separates the head and medium, improving wear and recording stability.
[G11B 5/6005] Specially adapted for spacing from a rotating disc using a fluid cushion -- G11B 5/6005 covers magnetic recording systems in which a fluid film such as air or oil creates a fluid-cushion gap between a rotating disc and the transducer head. The cushion keeps the spacing precise, reducing wear and improving signal stability during magnetisation or demagnetisation of the carrier.
[G11B 5/6011] Control of flying height -- G11B 5/6011 covers methods and devices that control the flying height of a magnetic recording head relative to the media. It is part of the classification for magnetic recording where the head's distance is regulated to optimize magnetisation and demagnetisation. This helps maintain data integrity and recording performance.
[G11B 5/6017] using capacitive measurement -- G11B 5/6017 relates to magnetic recording methods that incorporate capacitive sensing to monitor or influence the magnetisation or demagnetisation of a moving record carrier, using changes in capacitance as the carrier passes the transducer. This approach combines magnetic storage principles with electrical capacitance measurement.
[G11B 5/6023] using inductive measurement -- G11B 5/6023 covers techniques for recording data on magnetic media that use inductive measurement, wherein magnetic fields generated by the record carrier are sensed by an inductive transducer to write or verify magnetised regions. The method applies to systems where the carrier moves relative to the sensor.
[G11B 5/6029] Measurement using values derived from the data signal read from the disk -- The class covers methods that determine operational or physical parameters by analysing the data signal read from a magnetic disk, such as measuring head position, rotational speed, timing errors or other characteristics derived from the read-back waveform.
[G11B 5/6035] using electrostatic forces -- This CPC subclass G11B 5/6035 within the physics information-storage hierarchy covers methods that record data by employing electrostatic forces to magnetise or demagnetise a storage medium. It relates to systems where relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer is used and electrostatic attraction or repulsion assists the recording process.
[G11B 5/6041] using magnetic forces -- G11B 5/6041 covers recording methods that employ magnetic forces to create relative movement between a magnetic record carrier and a transducer for magnetising or demagnetising the medium. It is a subcategory of information storage based on relative motion between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/6047] using magnetostrictive means -- G11B 5/6047 covers recording techniques that employ magnetostrictive effects, where a magnetic field is generated or altered by mechanical strain to magnetise or demagnetise a storage medium, enabling data to be written via the resulting magnetic changes. This subclass falls under the broader category of recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier.
[G11B 5/6052] using optical means -- G11B 5/6052 covers methods that use light-typically lasers or other optical sources-to write or erase magnetic information on a moving record carrier, employing optical energy to alter the carrier's magnetisation during relative motion between the carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/6058] using piezoelectric means -- G11B 5/6058 covers the use of piezoelectric devices to generate or sense magnetic fields for recording data on a moving magnetic medium. The classification applies to systems where mechanical stress on a piezoelectric element is converted into electrical signals that drive a write head, or where the element detects field changes during readout, integrating piezoelectric actuation with magnetisation or demagnetisation of the record carrier.
[G11B 5/6064] using air pressure -- G11B 5/6064 relates to magnetic or demagnetisation recording where air pressure is employed to move the record carrier relative to the transducer, a technique used in information storage systems that rely on relative motion between carrier and head.
[G11B 5/607] using thermal means -- G11B 5/607 covers magnetic recording processes that use heat to change the magnetisation of a medium. It includes thermomagnetic, heat-assisted or laser-induced methods where thermal energy writes data onto the carrier. The subclass is part of physics-based information storage that relies on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/6076] Detecting head-disk contact -- Detecting head-disk contact refers to methods and devices that monitor the magnetic recording interface to identify when the read/write head physically touches the disk surface, using sensors such as load-force, acoustic-emission or electrical-characteristic detectors to prevent damage and ensure reliable operation.
[G11B 5/6082] Design of the air bearing surface -- G11B 5/6082 concerns magnetic recording systems where a transducer flies above a rotating magnetic medium on an air film. It covers the design of the air-bearing surface that shapes the airflow, controls the flying height, and ensures stable, low-friction contact between the head and the record carrier.
[G11B 5/6088] Optical waveguide in or on flying head -- G11B 5/6088 covers magnetic recording heads that incorporate an optical waveguide either built into the head or applied on its surface. The waveguide channels light to or from the flying head, enabling optical alignment, heating, or signal processing while the head flies over the media. This technology combines magnetic recording with integrated photonic structures.
[G11B 5/6094] Preventing or discharging electrostatic charge build-up on the flying head -- G11B 5/6094 covers methods or devices for preventing or removing electrostatic charge that accumulates on the flying read/write head of a magnetic storage device. This includes techniques such as conductive coatings, grounding, or ionizing mechanisms to reduce head-disk interactions and improve reliability.
[G11B 5/62] Record carriers characterised by the selection of the material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Record carriers comprising a laminate of one or more layers deposited on a substrate. The record carrier consists of a layer of magnetisable material deposited on a substrate intended for information storage.  **Glossary:** - Antiferromagnetism: Antiferromagnetism occurs when the exchange interactions between neighboring atoms cancel each other, so the net magnetic moment is zero. Examples of antiferromagnetic materials are (Pt, Ir, Cr and Pd) Mn alloys, and select transition metal oxides. - Bonding agent: Secondary material that is usually an organic polymer holding a layer having magnetic particulate material together. - Continuous (magnetic) layer: Hard magnetic material formed as a grain (e.g. CoCr, L1 0 CoPt, or Co/Pt superlattices) wherein there is no distinct phase dielectric material separating the magnetic grains. Examples include CoCrPtB alloy layers and (Co/Pt) n multilayers. - Exchange Spring Medium: A type of recording medium utilizing a high coercive force magnetic layer exchange coupled to low coercive force magnetic layer; whereby the lower coercive force magnetic layer switched orientation prior to the high coercive force layer, thereby generating a 'torque' that assists in the switching of the bits in the high coercive force layer. - Ferrimagnetic material: Ferrimagnetic materials exhibit exchange interaction between neighboring atoms leading to adjacent moments; however, the magnetic moments are unequal and opposite in direction. The magnetic properties of ferrimagnetic materials are strongly temperature dependent and are characterised by their Curie temperature. Examples of ferrimagnetic materials are rare earth-transition metal amorphous alloys, such as GdFeCo, TbFeCo, and select granular transition-metal alloys. - Ferromagnetic material: Ferromagnetic materials exhibit exchange interaction between neighboring atoms leading to adjacent moments. Ferromagnetism is temperature dependent and field strength dependent. Typical ferromagnetic materials include transition metals such as Fe, Ni, and Co and their alloys. - Granular (magnetic) layer: Hard magnetic material formed as a grain (e.g. CoCr or FePt) with a dielectric material segregated to the grain boundaries and separating the grains from each other. Examples include CoCrPt-SiO 2 layers and FePt:C layers. - Hard magnetic material: Hard magnetic materials possess large coercive force, are difficult to demagnetize and retain their magnetization upon removal of an external applied magnetic field. Typical hard magnetic materials have coercive force values of several hundred Oe or higher (often reaching several kOe). - Longitudinal Anisotropy: Films possessing anisotropy or magnetization directed along/in the plane of the film (Figure 1). - Magnetic Recording Layer: Any magnetic layer that forms part of the lamina used in storing/recording a recorded bit. This does not include soft magnetic underlayer/keeper layers solely for assisting in the flux return from a magnetic head. - Paramagnetic material: Paramagnetic materials have magnetic moments not completely cancelled because of electronic configuration and exhibit a resultant moment. Paramagnetic susceptibility is strongly temperature dependent. Examples of paramagnetic materials are CoCr alloys at specific Cr concentrations and materials exhibiting specific size ranges of either the magnetic grains or particle dimensions. - Soft magnetic material: Soft magnetic materials possess low coercive force, are easy to demagnetize and lose substantially all their magnetization upon removal of any external applied magnetic field. Typical soft magnetic materials have coercive force values under 100 Oe (often under 10 Oe). - Vertical Anisotropy: Films possessing anisotropy or magnetization directed out of the plane of the film (Figure 2).
[G11B 5/627] of leaders for magnetic tapes, e.g. non-magnetic strips on the tapes or for connection -- G11B 5/627 covers the use of leaders-non-magnetic strips or similar elements-attached to magnetic tapes to aid handling, splicing, or connection of the tape. It relates to tape recording methods that use magnetisation or demagnetisation of the record carrier, focusing on the tape's start or end sections.
[G11B 5/633] of cinematographic films or slides with integral magnetic track -- G11B 5/633 relates to the magnetic recording of data directly onto cinematographic film or photographic slides by embedding a magnetic stripe or track within the medium. The code covers techniques and devices that create an integral magnetic track on the film or slide to store audio, subtitles or other information synchronized with the visual content.
[G11B 5/64] comprising only the magnetic material without bonding agent -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Media or magnetic material including a thin-film magnetic layer represented by a continuous layer free of polymeric binder having a thickness typically ranging from Angstrom level to several micrometres.  Media characterised by aspects of the magnetic layers other than the composition or the requirement that a plurality of magnetic layers exist in a specific interaction. For example, media where the orientation of a single magnetic layer is the inventive feature (tilted media), how the magnetic layer is utilized (servo tracking), etc.
[G11B 5/642] self supporting magnetic material, e.g. magnetisable wires -- G11B 5/642 concerns self-supporting magnetic materials, such as magnetisable wires, that serve as the record carrier itself without a separate substrate. It relates to magnetic recording by magnetising or demagnetising these materials, enabling information storage through the material's own structure rather than a backing layer.
[G11B 5/65] characterised by its composition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic medium having a single magnetic layer that is characterised by its composition.  Example: A Mn-Al recording layer.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Record carriers consisting of several layers -> G11B5/66
[G11B 5/653] containing Fe or Ni  containing Co  G11B5/656 ; containing inorganic, non-oxide compounds of Si, N, P, B, H or C  G11B5/657 ; containing oxygen  G11B5/658 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic medium in which the magnetic layer includes a majority component (by weight %, volume % or mole %) of iron or nickel, but does not also contain cobalt, oxygen or an inorganic, non-oxide compound of Si, N, P, B, H or C.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Containing cobalt -> G11B5/656 - Containing inorganic, non-oxide compounds of Si, N, P, B, H or C -> G11B5/657 - Containing oxygen -> G11B5/658
[G11B 5/656] containing Co  containing inorganic, non-oxide compounds of Si, N, P, B, H or C  G11B5/657 ; containing oxygen  G11B5/658 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic medium in which the magnetic layer includes a majority component (by weight %, volume % or mole %) of cobalt, but does not also contain oxygen or an inorganic, non-oxide compound of Si, N, P, B, H or C.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Containing inorganic, non-oxide compounds of Si, N, P, B, H or C -> G11B5/657 - Containing oxygen -> G11B5/658
[G11B 5/657] containing inorganic, non-oxide compound of Si, N, P, B, H or C, e.g. in metal alloy or compound  containing oxygen  G11B5/658 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic medium in which the magnetic layer includes an inorganic, non-oxide compound of Si, N, P, B, H, or C. This compound can be part of the alloy (e.g. CoCrPtB) or as a segregant compound separating the magnetic grains in the layer (e.g. FePt grains separated by a carbon or boron-nitride segregant material).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Containing oxygen -> G11B5/658
[G11B 5/658] containing oxygen, e.g. molecular oxygen or magnetic oxide -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic medium in which the magnetic layer includes magnetic metal oxide or a magnetic layer with uncombined oxygen present within the magnetic elemental metal or the alloy lattice structure, e.g. CoO x or CoCrPt-SiO 2 magnetic layers.
[G11B 5/66] the record carriers consisting of several layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic medium that contains more than one magnetic layer on the same side of the substrate. This includes soft, hard or paramagnetic layers, but excludes antiferromagnetic layers.  Multiple magnetic layers separated by non-magnetic or antiferromagnetic layers are classified in G11B5/676 .
[G11B 5/667] including a soft magnetic layer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic medium including two or more magnetic layers, in which at least one of the magnetic layers is a soft magnetic layer.
[G11B 5/672] having different compositions in a plurality of magnetic layers, e.g. layer compositions having differing elemental components or differing proportions of elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic medium including two or more magnetic layers, and in which each layer has a different composition.  Example:  Figure 1. A FePt:C / CoPt:TiO 2 layer structure or a laminate magnetic layer structure of FePt:C / FePt:SiO 2 / FePt:C.
[G11B 5/674] having differing macroscopic or microscopic structures, e.g. differing crystalline lattices, varying atomic structures or differing roughnesses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic medium including two or more magnetic layers, each having the same chemical constituents, but differing in crystal lattice or molecular arrangement.  Examples:  Figure 2:  Figure 3:  Figures 2 and 3. Unique magnetic layers with distinct oblique inclination angles (Figure 2) and unique magnetic layers with distinct grain size requirements (Figure 3).
[G11B 5/676] having magnetic layers separated by a nonmagnetic layer, e.g. antiferromagnetic layer, Cu layer or coupling layer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic medium including two or more magnetic layers, wherein at least one intervening nonmagnetic or antiferromagnetic layer is between the magnetic layers.
[G11B 5/678] having three or more magnetic layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic medium in which the medium has at least three magnetic layers on a single side of the substrate, with at least one intervening non-magnetic or antiferromagnetic layer.  Examples: (Co/Pt) n or (Co/Pd) n superlattice-type media layers.
[G11B 5/68] comprising one or more layers of magnetisable material homogeneously mixed with a bonding agent -- This subclass deals with magnetic recording media in which one or more layers of magnetisable material are uniformly mixed with a bonding agent, forming a homogeneous composite layer for recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/70] on a base layer -- This subclass concerns magnetic recording in which the data-bearing medium includes a distinct base layer on which the magnetisable coating is applied, such as a substrate or underlayer that supports the magnetic film. It covers methods of forming, treating or using that base layer for recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/7013] characterised by the dispersing agent -- G11B 5/7013 relates to magnetic recording processes where a dispersing agent is employed to separate magnetic particles within the recording medium, enhancing the uniformity, stability and performance of the magnetised layer on tapes, inks or other carriers.
[G11B 5/702] characterised by the bonding agent -- G11B 5/702 relates to magnetic recording or erasing of a medium where a bonding agent is used to attach the record carrier. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer and focuses on the characteristics of the adhesive or binder employed.
[G11B 5/7021] containing a polyurethane or a polyisocyanate -- G11B 5/7021 covers magnetic recording media or devices in which the moving record carrier or its coating contains a polyurethane or polyisocyanate, typically used as a binder, substrate or protective layer to improve mechanical strength, durability or chemical resistance of the magnetised surface. This falls under information-storage technologies that rely on relative motion between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 5/7022] containing mixtures of polyurethanes or polyisocyanates with other polymers -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 5/7022, under the broader categories of PHYSICS and INFORMATION STORAGE, specifically addresses recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation. It focuses on materials containing mixtures of polyurethanes or polyisocyanates with other polymers used in the manufacturing of magnetic recording media.
[G11B 5/7023] containing polyesters, polyethers, silicones, polyvinyl resins, polyacrylresins or epoxy resins  G11B5/7022  takes precedence -- G11B 5/7023 classifies magnetic recording media whose carrier includes polymeric materials such as polyesters, polyethers, silicones, polyvinyl, polyacryl or epoxy resins, used for magnetisation or demagnetisation of the record surface. This subclass follows G11B 5/7022 in the hierarchy of information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/7025] containing cellulosic derivates  G11B5/7022  takes precedence -- G11B 5/7025 is a CPC subclass for magnetic recording media whose record carrier contains cellulosic derivatives such as cellulose, paper or related materials. It falls under the broader group for recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation where the carrier moves relative to the transducer, and it is narrower than the parent subclass G11B 5/7022.
[G11B 5/7026] Radiation curable polymers -- G11B 5/7026 covers polymers that solidify when exposed to radiation such as UV light, electron beams or gamma rays and are used as coating or binder materials in magnetic recording media. The classification includes formulations that become permanently magnetisable after curing, enabling the creation of stable magnetic layers on flexible or rigid record carriers.
[G11B 5/7027] Graft polymers -- Graft polymers are polymeric molecules where side chains of a different polymer are chemically attached to a main backbone, providing tailored physical and chemical properties. In magnetic recording media they are used as binders or carriers to improve adhesion, durability, and magnetic particle distribution on the record carrier. This subclass covers inventions concerning such grafted polymer materials for magnetised or demagnetised recording surfaces.
[G11B 5/7028] Additives, e.g. crosslinking agents -- G11B 5/7028 covers chemical additives, particularly crosslinking agents, incorporated into magnetic recording media to alter the physical or magnetic characteristics of the carrier material. These substances are used to improve durability, stability, or overall recording performance.
[G11B 5/706] characterised by the composition of the magnetic material -- G11B 5/706 covers magnetic recording media where the defining feature is the specific composition of the magnetic material used. It includes alloys, oxides, or other magnetic compounds that impart particular recording or data-retention properties, and applies to devices that record by magnetising or demagnetising a moving carrier. This subclass falls under information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/70605] metals or alloys -- G11B 5/70605 pertains to the use of metals or alloys in the process of recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation, specifically within the context of information storage technologies where there is relative movement between the record carrier and transducer. This classification is part of a detailed system used by the European Patent Office and United States Patent and Trademark Office for patent documentation.
[G11B 5/7061] with a non-magnetic core -- The subclass G11B 5/7061 covers magnetic recording media that use a non-magnetic core-typically a plastic, ceramic or similar material-to provide a support structure while the magnetic layer stores data. It applies to systems where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 5/70615] containing Fe metal or alloys  G11B5/70621  takes precedence -- G11B 5/70615 classifies magnetic recording media whose magnetic layer contains iron metal or iron-based alloys. It falls under recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier and is superseded by the more specific subclass G11B 5/70621.
[G11B 5/70621] containing Co metal or alloys -- This subclass pertains to magnetic recording media that contain cobalt metal or cobalt-based alloys in the recording layer or substrate, affecting magnetic properties such as coercivity and stability. It is part of the CPC group for recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier.
[G11B 5/70626] containing non-metallic substances -- G11B 5/70626 relates to magnetic recording media whose magnetic layer incorporates non-metallic substances such as oxides, polymers, ceramics or other inorganic compounds that modify magnetic properties, protection or adhesion. It falls under physics-based information storage using relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer, specifically magnetisation or demagnetisation recording.
[G11B 5/70631] with a non-magnetic core -- Technology for magnetic recording where the record carrier does not contain a magnetic core, such as media where magnetic particles are deposited on a non-magnetic substrate or a thin magnetic layer is applied to a non-magnetic backing. The method involves magnetising or demagnetising the magnetic layer to store data while the supporting material remains non-magnetic.
[G11B 5/70636] CrO 2 -- Chromium dioxide (CrO₂) is a magnetic pigment employed in magnetic recording media such as tape and disk coatings, where its high coercivity and saturation magnetisation enhance signal strength and reduce noise. The material is applied to record carriers that move relative to the read/write transducer.
[G11B 5/70642] iron oxides -- The G11B 5/70642 entry covers the use of iron-oxide compounds as magnetic particles in recording media where data are stored by magnetising or demagnetising a carrier that moves relative to a transducer. It includes formulations, particle preparation and coating methods for tapes, disks and other movable record carriers that rely on the magnetic properties of iron oxides.
[G11B 5/70647] with a skin  G11B5/70657  takes precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 5/70647 pertains to the recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a record carrier, specifically addressing techniques involving a skin effect, though it is noted that classification G11B 5/70657 takes precedence in this context.
[G11B 5/70652] gamma - Fe 2  O 3 -- This classification code, G11B 5/70652, within the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the EPO and USPTO, pertains to the use of gamma-Fe2O3 (gamma iron oxide) in magnetic recording materials. It is part of a detailed hierarchy focusing on information storage technologies that involve relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/70657] with a skin -- G11B 5/70657 addresses magnetic recording arrangements in which the record carrier is provided with a skin-a thin protective or magnetic coating that contacts the recording head during magnetisation or demagnetisation. The skin can enhance wear resistance, signal fidelity, or enable specific recording techniques on the moving medium.
[G11B 5/70663] Preparation processes specially adapted therefor, e.g. using stabilising agents  G11B5/70668  and  G11B5/70673  take precedence -- This CPC subclass concerns processes for preparing magnetic recording media that are specially adapted, such as using stabilising agents, within the broader field of magnetic recording of information storage. It is designated G11B5/70663 and is superseded by the more specific subclasses G11B5/70668 and G11B5/70673.
[G11B 5/70668] containing a dopant -- G11B 5/70668 concerns magnetic recording media or devices that include a dopant to modify the magnetic properties of the record carrier. The dopant is added to the substrate or magnetic layer to enhance coercivity, signal stability or recording density. This subclass falls under information storage that relies on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/70673] containing Co -- G11B 5/70673 pertains to magnetic recording materials that contain cobalt, used in the process of magnetisation or demagnetisation for information storage. This classification is part of a detailed system covering various aspects of magnetic recording technology.
[G11B 5/70678] Ferrites -- G11B 5/70678 covers ferrite compounds employed as magnetic media or components in recording devices, where data are stored by magnetising or demagnetising a carrier. Ferrites provide high magnetic permeability and low loss, making them suitable for tape, disk and other magnetic storage formats that rely on relative motion between the carrier and the read/write transducer.
[G11B 5/70684] Ferro-ferrioxydes -- G11B 5/70684 covers the use of ferro-ferrioxides, mixed iron-oxide compounds such as magnetite, in magnetic recording media. It pertains to the magnetization or demagnetization of a record carrier where these compounds serve as the magnetic layer or additive. The subclass falls under information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/70689] Magnetite -- Magnetite refers to the use of the magnetic mineral as a recording medium in magnetic storage, covering methods and devices that employ its ferromagnetic properties to encode data through magnetisation or demagnetisation of a carrier moving relative to a transducer. This entry addresses the material's role in magnetic tapes, disks and similar record carriers.
[G11B 5/70694] Non-stoechiometric ferro-ferrioxydes, e.g. berthollide -- The G11B 5/70694 subclass concerns magnetic recording media that use non-stoichiometric ferro-ferrioxides, such as berthollide compounds, as the magnetisable layer. These mixed-oxide materials have variable composition, enabling tailored magnetisation and demagnetisation characteristics for information storage.
[G11B 5/708] characterised by addition of non-magnetic particles to the layer -- G11B 5/708 pertains to magnetic recording media where the magnetic layer is mixed with non-magnetic particles. Adding such particles adjusts the magnetic or mechanical characteristics of the layer, improving recording density, signal-to-noise ratio, or durability. This subclass falls under information storage based on relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer.
[G11B 5/7085] non-magnetic abrasive particles -- G11B 5/7085 covers the use of non-magnetic abrasive particles in the recording of information by magnetising a medium, such as cleaning, smoothing or texturing a magnetic record carrier before or during magnetisation. The particles modify the surface without adding magnetic properties, improving head-media contact and signal quality.
[G11B 5/71] characterised by the lubricant -- G11B 5/71 pertains to magnetic recording media that incorporate a lubricating layer on the record carrier. The lubricant reduces friction and wear during the relative movement between the carrier and the transducer, improving reliability and head-media contact. This subclass falls under magnetic recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation of a moving record carrier.
[G11B 5/712] characterised by the surface treatment or coating of magnetic particles -- This subgroup covers magnetic recording media where the magnetic particles are modified by applying a surface treatment or coating to improve properties such as corrosion resistance, tribology, or magnetic performance. The coating may be organic, inorganic, or composite layers applied during particle preparation or media manufacture.
[G11B 5/714] characterised by the dimension of the magnetic particles -- G11B 5/714 pertains to magnetic recording methods where the defining feature is the size of the magnetic particles on the record carrier. It applies to technologies that store information by magnetising or demagnetising a moving medium, with particle dimension used to characterize the recording medium.
[G11B 5/716] characterised by two or more magnetic layers -- G11B 5/716 refers to magnetic recording media that employ two or more separate magnetic layers on the record carrier, allowing data to be stored, read or written across multiple layers for higher capacity or specialized performance. It falls under physics → information storage → information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer, specifically recording by magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/718] at least one on each side of the base layer -- G11B 5/718 covers magnetic recording media that have magnetic material applied to both sides of a supporting substrate, enabling writing or erasing by magnetisation or demagnetisation while the carrier moves relative to the transducer. This double-sided design is used to increase data density or provide redundant storage on a single record carrier.
[G11B 5/72] Protective coatings, e.g. anti-static -- **Definition:** This place covers:  One or more coatings having specific utility for protecting the record carrier, e.g. from shock, static, head-medium crash, friction or corrosion.  The protective coatings on the outermost layer of the record carrier above any magnetic recording layer structure – the "outermost" being the layer furthest from the substrate and closest to the surface facing a recording or reproducing apparatus.
[G11B 5/722] containing an anticorrosive material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Protective coatings in which the material used has an express anti-corrosion activity or the protective coatings contain a material that is art-recognized as serving an anti-corrosive function, e.g. Cr.  Examples of corrosion resistance include tests in the description, e.g.  or corrosion resistance in the claims.
[G11B 5/725] containing a lubricant -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Protective coatings that include at least one lubricant material, i.e., a substance for reducing friction or wear.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Inorganic carbon protective coating -> G11B5/727
[G11B 5/7253] Fluorocarbon lubricant -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The lubricant is an organic compound of fluorine.
[G11B 5/7257] Perfluoropolyether lubricant -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The fluorine containing lubricant that includes a perfluoropolyether compound.

[G11B 5/7266] comprising a lubricant over the inorganic carbon coating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Protective coatings that include at least one non-organic carbon-based coating material and at least one lubricant coating; the lubricant can be physically or chemically bonded to the carbon-based coating; classification should also be done in G11B5/725 – G11B5/7257 , depending on the type of lubricant.

[G11B 5/727] Inorganic carbon protective coating, e.g. graphite, diamond like carbon or doped carbon -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Single protective coating, which is an inorganic carbon-based material, i.e. a compound that does not include organic carbon bonds.
[G11B 5/728] containing a bonding agent in the protective coating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Protective coating that is a bonding-agent type of coating, such as for use above binder-type media.
[G11B 5/73] Base layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic media in which each medium includes one or more non-magnetic layers under a lowermost magnetic recording layer.  Base layers are substrates or non-magnetic layers designated either by position (e.g. precoat layer, prelayer, base layer, underlayer, intermediate layer, lower layer, sublayer, ground layer, etc.) or function (e.g. nucleation layer, seed layer, barrier layer, corrosion prevention layer, diffusion prevention layer, texture layer, etc.).  **Glossary:** - Binder-type media: A recording medium where the recording layer includes (a usually polymeric) binder mixed with magnetisable particles. - Thin film-type media: A recording medium where the recording layer is substantially free of any polymeric material. - Non-magnetic: A material that has a zero magnetic moment. - Magnetic: A material that has a non-zero magnetic moment, including paramagnetic, ferromagnetic, and ferrimagnetic materials. - SUL: Soft Under Layer - a soft magnetic layer usually located between a substrate and a recording layer to direct the flux from the magnetic head through the media recording layer and back to a return head. - Soft Magnetic: A material exhibiting a (relatively) low coercivity, typically under 100 Oe. - Hard Magnetic: A material exhibiting a (relatively) high coercivity capable of storing data, typically over 1000 Oe.
[G11B 5/733] characterised by the addition of non-magnetic particles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic recording media having one or more base layers formed from a binder with included non-magnetic particles or filler.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Base layers having a non-magnetic layer under a soft magnetic layer -> G11B5/736 - Magnetic recording media substrates -> G11B5/739
[G11B 5/7334] Base layer characterised by composition or structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic recording media having one or more base layers formed from a binder with included non-magnetic particles or filler, where the particles are recited in name only and the inventive subject matter is in the binder composition (or structure) or a non-particulate additive composition (or structure).  **Glossary:** - Structure: The chemical structure of an organic compound, i.e. the arrangement of the atoms or molecules of one or more of the underlayers.
[G11B 5/735] characterised by the back layer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic recording media having one or more base layers formed on the opposite side of a support from where the recording layer is located (i.e. back layers).  Also included are back layers including a binder with non-magnetic particles or filler, where the particles or filler are nominal and recited in name only.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Magnetic recording media substrates -> G11B5/739  **Glossary:** - Back layer: A layer on the opposite side of a substrate from the recording layer structure; typically used for controlling the running and electrostatic properties of a tape-form medium.
[G11B 5/7353] for a thin film medium where the magnetic recording layer structure has no bonding agent -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic recording media, each having one or more back layers wherein the recording layer is a thin-film type structure, e.g. sputtered layer, CoCrPt alloy layer, Co/Pt multilayers.
[G11B 5/7356] comprising non-magnetic particles in the back layer, e.g. particles of TiO 2 , ZnO or SiO 2 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic recording media having one or more back layers characterized by inventive non-magnetic particles (e.g. oxides, carbon black, etc.).
[G11B 5/7358] specially adapted for achieving a specific property, e.g. average roughness [Ra] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Back layers including non-magnetic particles or filler recited as being added to achieve a specified inventive or non-conventional physical property.
[G11B 5/736] Non-magnetic layer under a soft magnetic layer, e.g. between a substrate and a soft magnetic underlayer [SUL] or a keeper layer  magnetic recording media substrates  G11B5/739 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers between a substrate and a soft magnetic underlayer.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Magnetic recording media substrates -> G11B5/739
[G11B 5/7361] Two or more non-magnetic layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including two or more inventive layers between a substrate and a soft magnetic underlayer wherein the inventive subject matter lies in the composition or structural arrangement of the layers.
[G11B 5/7362] Physical structure of underlayer, e.g. texture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including two or more inventive layers between a substrate and a soft magnetic underlayer wherein the physical macroscopic structure (e.g. texture, patterning, etc.) or microstructure (crystal plane, crystallographic texture, etc.) of at least one layer is also deemed inventive.
[G11B 5/7363] Non-magnetic single underlayer comprising nickel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including only a single inventive layer between a substrate and a soft magnetic underlayer wherein the layer is recited as including non-trace amounts of nickel.
[G11B 5/7364] Non-magnetic single underlayer comprising chromium -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including only a single inventive layer between a substrate and a soft magnetic underlayer wherein the layer is recited as including non-trace amounts of chromium.
[G11B 5/7365] Non-magnetic single underlayer comprising a polymeric structure, e.g. polymeric adhesion layer or plasma-polymerized carbon layer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including only a single inventive layer between a substrate and a soft magnetic underlayer wherein the layer is recited as being polymeric or a resin-based underlayer.  This includes polymeric or resin-based non-magnetic underlayers having particles, provided they are located under a soft-magnetic layer.
[G11B 5/7366] for heat-assisted or thermally-assisted magnetic recording [HAMR, TAMR] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers having specific utility for use in energy assisted (HAMR, TAMR, etc.) magnetic recording.  **Glossary:** - Energy Assisted: A recording process where, in addition to a magnetic field from a magnetic head, the reading and/or writing process is assisted by energy in the form of heat, microwaves, etc.
[G11B 5/7367] Physical structure of underlayer, e.g. texture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including one inventive layer between a substrate and a soft magnetic underlayer wherein the physical macroscopic structure (e.g. texture, patterning, etc.) or microstructure (crystal plane, crystallographic texture, etc.) of the layer is also deemed inventive.
[G11B 5/7368] Non-polymeric layer under the lowermost magnetic recording layer  base layers having a non-magnetic layer under a soft magnetic layer  G11B5/736 ; magnetic recording media substrates  G11B5/739 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-Polymeric base layers between a soft magnetic underlayer and the recording layer structure or, if no soft magnetic underlayer in the recording medium, any base layers under the recording layer structure.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Base layers having a non-magnetic layer under a soft magnetic layer -> G11B5/736 - Magnetic recording media substrates -> G11B5/739
[G11B 5/7369] Two or more non-magnetic underlayers, e.g. seed layers or barrier layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including two or more inventive layers in the required structural location within the media wherein the inventive subject matter lies in the composition or structural arrangement of the layers.
[G11B 5/737] Physical structure of underlayer, e.g. texture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including two or more inventive layers in the required structural location within the media wherein the physical macroscopic structure (e.g. texture, patterning, etc.) or microstructure (crystal plane, crystallographic texture, etc.) of at least one layer is also deemed inventive.
[G11B 5/7371] Non-magnetic single underlayer comprising nickel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including only a single inventive layer in the required structural location within the media wherein the layer is recited as including non-trace amounts of nickel.
[G11B 5/7373] Non-magnetic single underlayer comprising chromium -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including only a single inventive layer in the required structural location within the media wherein the layer is recited as including non-trace amounts of chromium.
[G11B 5/7375] for heat-assisted or thermally-assisted magnetic recording [HAMR, TAMR] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers in the required structural location within the media having specific utility for use in energy assisted (HAMR, TAMR, etc.) magnetic recording.  **Glossary:** - Energy Assisted: A recording process where, in addition to a magnetic field from a magnetic head, the reading and/or writing process is assisted by energy in the form of heat, microwaves, etc.
[G11B 5/7377] Physical structure of underlayer, e.g. texture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including two or more inventive layers in the required structural location within the media wherein the physical macroscopic structure (e.g. texture, patterning, etc.) or microstructure (crystal plane, crystallographic texture, etc.) of at least one layer is also deemed inventive.
[G11B 5/7379] Seed layer, e.g. at least one non-magnetic layer is specifically adapted as a seed or seeding layer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers in the required structural location within the media having specific utility for use as seed or seeding layers.  **Glossary:** - Seed or Seeding Layer: A non-magnetic layer explicitly recited as a 'seed' or 'seeding' layer or that is explicitly disclosed as only used for seeding the crystallographic growth of the immediately following layer in the deposition process.
[G11B 5/739] Magnetic recording media substrates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers in which a layer or laminate provides physical integrity to a magnetic recording media by acting as substrate or support for a magnetic recording layer.  This subgroup and its subgroups provide for substrates set forth with chemical or structural specificity.  Care must be taken to distinguish between (a) a composite or coated substrate and (b) a subsequently formed non-magnetic base layer when considering binder media type structures. A layer recited as an "underlayer", "undercoat", "lower layer" or "intermediate layer" is a layer distinct from a substrate. For a layer to be considered as a part of a substrate, it must be recited specifically in the disclosure of forming the substrate or as part of a substrate prior to any deposition of a recording layer structure.  Examples:
[G11B 5/73911] Inorganic substrates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including a substrate having at least one formed layer or portion comprising inorganic material.
[G11B 5/73913] Composites or coated substrates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inorganic substrates having two or more contiguous layers or portions of distinct components (e.g. glass containing metallic particles, etc.).  Included in this subgroup are an inorganic structural element and an organic compound; e.g. metallic particles and resin, provided that the substrate as a whole would be considered an inorganic substrate.  **Glossary:** - NiP: Nickel-Phosphorous (a conventional pre-coat deposited on substrates for smoothing and texturing purposes). - AlMg/NiP: An aluminium-magnesium alloy substrate coated with an NiP coating layer - Plating layer: A layer deposited by either an electrolytic or electroless plating method; typically an NiP layer.
[G11B 5/73915] Silicon compound based coating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including a substrate having at least one contiguous layer of a silicon compound.
[G11B 5/73917] Metallic substrates, i.e. elemental metal or metal alloy substrates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including a substrate that is an elemental metal or a metal alloy.
[G11B 5/73919] Aluminium or titanium elemental or alloy substrates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including a substrate that is elemental aluminium or titanium or an aluminium or titanium alloy (i.e., an alloy containing 40% or more aluminum and/or titanium).
[G11B 5/73921] Glass or ceramic substrates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including a substrate that is composed of glass or ceramic, including amorphous or crystalline glasses.  Included in this subgroup are glass or ceramic substrates including texturing.
[G11B 5/73923] Organic polymer substrates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Substrates composed of a solid polymer compound or polymeric composition (e.g. polyurethane, melamine resin, polyamide, etc.).
[G11B 5/73925] Composite or coated non-esterified substrates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Substrates that are materials other than an ester and are composed of a plurality of layers (e.g. a laminate or distinct particulate or non-particulate compounds containing in a single layer).  This subgroup includes coatings on an organic substrate directed to the improvement of the properties of the substrate and not affecting the crystalline anisotropy or magnetic orientations of a subsequently deposited layer (e.g. a coating solely for adhesive, texture, etc.).
[G11B 5/73927] Polyester substrates, e.g. polyethylene terephthalate -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including a substrate in which the polymer substrate includes an ester group thereon such as carboxylic acid ester.
[G11B 5/73929] comprising naphthalene ring compounds, e.g. polyethylene naphthalate substrates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including a substrate that comprises a polyester substrate including a naphthalene ring structure.
[G11B 5/73931] Two or more layers, at least one layer being polyester -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Substrates that are composed of a plurality of layers, wherein at least one inventive layer is a polyester.  This subgroup includes coatings on a polyester substrate directed to the improvement of the properties of the substrate and not affecting the crystalline anisotropy or magnetic orientations of a subsequently deposited layer (e.g. a coating solely for adhesive, texture, etc.).
[G11B 5/73933] Surface treated layers, e.g. treated by corona discharge -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including a polyester substrate that has been coated or surface treated.  Included in this subgroup are polyester substrate leader and trailer tapes.
[G11B 5/73935] characterised by roughness or surface features, e.g. by added particles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including a polyester substrate, typically containing particles, which has a defined and inventive roughness profile/property or surface feature, e.g. protrusion density.
[G11B 5/73937] Substrates having an organic polymer comprising a ring structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Base layers including a substrate that has a specific organic ring structure, e.g. benzyl groups or 1,4-dihydroxydimethylbenzene.
[G11B 5/74] Record carriers characterised by the form, e.g. sheet shaped to wrap around a drum -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Record carriers (tapes, cards, disks) specially shaped, e.g., bit patterned media, or discrete-track media  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Manufacturing of record carriers -> G11B5/84  **Glossary:** - Patterned Media and Bit Patterned Media: In Patterned Media (PM) and Bit-patterned-media (BPM), the magnetic recording layer on the media is patterned into small magnetic isolated data islands. In Bit-patterned media each island corresponds to a bit and is arranged e.g. in concentric data tracks in the case of disks media, while in patterned media the islands may correspond to discrete tracks or to servo patterns. Patterned-media may be longitudinal magnetic recording disks, wherein the magnetization directions are parallel to or in the plane of the recording layer, or perpendicular magnetic recording disks, wherein the magnetization directions are perpendicular to or out-of-the-plane of the recording layer. To produce magnetic isolation of the patterned data islands, the magnetization of the spaces between the islands is destroyed or substantially reduced to render these spaces essentially nonmagnetic. Alternatively, the media may be fabricated so that that there is no magnetic material in the spaces between the islands
[G11B 5/743] Patterned record carriers, wherein the magnetic recording layer is patterned into magnetic isolated data islands, e.g. discrete tracks -- G11B 5/743 pertains to magnetic record carriers whose recording layer is deliberately segmented into isolated magnetic islands or discrete tracks. This patterning isolates each data bit, allowing higher storage density and reduced interference between neighboring bits. The technology is applied in advanced magnetic media such as hard-disk and tape formats.
[G11B 5/746] Bit Patterned record carriers, wherein each magnetic isolated data island corresponds to a bit -- The G11B 5/746 subclass relates to magnetic record carriers that use a bit-patterned structure, where each isolated magnetic island on the medium represents a single data bit. It covers the manufacture, design and operation of such media that store information by magnetising or demagnetising these discrete islands, enabling very high-density recording.
[G11B 5/76] Drum carriers -- G11B 5/76 covers drum carriers used in magnetic recording systems, where a rotating cylindrical drum carries the magnetic record medium while a stationary head magnetises or demagnetises the surface to store data. The classification pertains to information storage devices that rely on relative movement between the record carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 5/78] Tape carriers -- G11B 5/78 classifies flexible magnetic tape carriers that serve as the record medium in systems where a moving transducer writes or reads data by magnetising or demagnetising the tape. The code covers the physical structure, material composition and dimensions of the tape itself, distinct from the magnetic heads or drive mechanisms that manipulate it.
[G11B 5/80] Card carriers -- G11B 5/80 designates card carriers used for magnetic or demagnetized recording, where data is stored by altering the magnetic state of a card-shaped record carrier in relation to a transducer. The class is nested under physics, information storage, and storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/82] Disk carriers -- G11B 5/82 covers disk-shaped record carriers that store data by magnetic polarisation, such as hard-disk platters and floppy disks. The class includes magnetic recording media where information is written and read through magnetisation or demagnetisation of the disk surface.
[G11B 5/825] flexible discs -- G11B 5/825 pertains to flexible magnetic discs, including floppy-disk-type media and other pliable magnetic carriers used for recording data by magnetising or demagnetising the surface while the disc moves relative to the read/write transducer. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/84] Processes or apparatus specially adapted for manufacturing record carriers -- The category G11B 5/84 includes processes and equipment specially designed for producing magnetic record carriers such as tapes, discs or other media that store data by magnetisation or demagnetisation. It covers manufacturing steps like forming, coating, surface treatment and apparatus that shape or condition the carrier for magnetic recording.
[G11B 5/8404] manufacturing base layers -- G11B 5/8404 covers processes for producing the foundational layers of magnetic recording media, such as substrates or undercoats that support the magnetic coating. It includes methods for applying, shaping or treating these base layers to achieve required mechanical or magnetic properties.
[G11B 5/8408] protecting the magnetic layer -- G11B 5/8408 covers techniques and materials that protect the magnetic layer of a recording medium from physical damage, corrosion, or magnetic degradation. It includes protective coatings, over-coats and barrier layers applied to magnetic tapes, disks or cards to preserve signal integrity and extend service life.
[G11B 5/8412] treatment by ultrasonics -- The G11B 5/8412 subclass covers the use of ultrasonic energy to treat magnetic recording media, such as applying high-frequency sound waves to a record carrier to influence its magnetisation, improve surface conditions, or aid demagnetisation processes during recording or erasing.
[G11B 5/8416] coating a support with a magnetic layer by precipitation -- A method for producing magnetic recording media in which a magnetic layer is formed on a support by precipitating magnetic particles onto the substrate, creating a continuous coating that can be subsequently magnetised for data storage. This technique falls under physics-based information storage that relies on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer for magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/842] Coating a support with a liquid magnetic dispersion -- G11B 5/842 covers methods for applying a liquid magnetic dispersion onto a support to form a magnetic coating that serves as a recording medium. The coated layer enables data storage by magnetising or demagnetising the carrier during operation.
[G11B 5/845] in a magnetic field -- The subclass G11B 5/845 covers recording techniques in which a magnetic field is applied to the record carrier during magnetisation or demagnetisation, influencing the storage of information as the carrier moves relative to the transducer. It pertains to magnetic-field-assisted processes for imprinting data onto magnetic media.
[G11B 5/848] Coating a support with a magnetic layer by extrusion -- G11B 5/848 covers a manufacturing process in which a magnetic layer is applied to a support by extrusion, using an extrudable composition that contains magnetic particles to form a recording coating. This method belongs to the subclass for recording by magnetisation of a record carrier.
[G11B 5/85] Coating a support with a magnetic layer by vapour deposition -- The subclass covers methods for forming a magnetic coating on a support by means of vapour deposition, creating a magnetic layer suitable for recording data through magnetisation or demagnetisation. It is applied to information-storage media where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 5/851] Coating a support with a magnetic layer by sputtering -- The CPC entry G11B 5/851 covers the process of applying a magnetic layer onto a substrate by sputtering, a physical-vapor-deposition technique used to create recording media that store data through magnetisation or demagnetisation. This method falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 5/852] Orientation in a magnetic field -- The subclass covers techniques for aligning magnetic domains or particles in a recording medium by applying a magnetic field, controlling the direction of magnetisation during or after recording. It includes methods and apparatus that use field orientation to set or improve the stored magnetic information on the carrier.
[G11B 5/855] Coating only part of a support with a magnetic layer -- The CPC subclass G11B 5/855 covers methods and apparatus for applying a magnetic coating to only a portion of a support, creating a partially magnetised recording medium. It relates to information storage where the record carrier and transducer move relative to each other and data is written by magnetising or demagnetising the carrier.
[G11B 5/858] Producing a magnetic layer by electro-plating or electroless plating -- This subclass covers methods for forming magnetic recording layers on a substrate by depositing magnetic material through electro-chemical means, either with an applied electric current (electro-plating) or by a chemical reduction process without current (electroless plating), enabling the production of magnetic media for storage based on magnetisation or demagnetisation.
[G11B 5/86] Re-recording, i.e. transcribing information from one magnetisable record carrier on to one or more similar or dissimilar record carriers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Master disks - i.e. original disks drawn preliminarily with magnetic information corresponding to a preformatted signal to be magnetically transferred (e.g. servo patterns or reference servo patterns for self-servo- writing) - used to duplicate information on lave disks  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Re-recording by varying the order of the information -> G11B27/029 , G11B27/036 - Transferring data from one type of record carrier to another type of record carrier -> G06K1/18
[G11B 5/865] by contact "printing" -- G11B 5/865 covers recording techniques that create magnetic marks by direct contact between a print element and the magnetic medium, often called contact printing, where the transducer physically imprints magnetisation or demagnetisation onto the record carrier.
[G11B 7/00] Recording or reproducing by optical means, e.g. recording using a thermal beam of optical radiation , reproducing using an optical beam at lower power -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In general terms, this group is subdivided into:  systems ( G11B7/002 - G11B7/003 ) e.g. tape, card, disc  methods of recording or reproduction (also erasing, overwriting), including holographic recording of coded data ( G11B7/004 - G11B7/0065 ); re-recording of data (transcription) ( G11B7/28 )  arrangement of information e.g. control area, land and groove structure, including details of discrete physical structures such as "pits" ( G11B7/007 - G11B7/013 )  access e.g. moving the optical pickup ( G11B7/085 )  servo e.g. moving the objective lens ( G11B7/09 )  heads e.g. types of heads ( G11B7/12 , G11B7/14 )  record carriers e.g. CD, DVD, BD ( G11B7/24 )  In principle, only aspects of the above subjects that are particularly adapted as a result of using light for recording/reproduction (e.g. track pitch, pit depth adapted to the wavelength of light used) should be classified in G11B7/00 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Recording on or reproducing from the same record carrier -> G11B11/00 - Recording simultaneously -> G11B13/00
[G11B 7/002] Recording, reproducing or erasing systems characterised by the shape of the carrier -- G11B 7/002 covers recording, reproducing or erasing apparatus whose operation depends on the geometric shape of the data carrier. The classification is applied to optical storage technologies where the carrier's form - such as disks, cylinders or patterned surfaces - determines the interaction with a moving read/write beam. It falls under physics-information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
