[G11B 7/0025] with cylinders or cylinder-like carriers , e.g. truncated cones -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Uncommon or outdated technology (in 2011)
[G11B 7/003] with webs , e.g. belts, spooled tapes or films of quasi-infinite extent -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Uncommon or outdated technology (in 2011)
[G11B 7/0031] using a rotating head, e.g. helicoidal recording -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Optical tape data storage systems that feed an optical tape helically around a drum while writing and/or reading digital data on the optical tape see e.g. US5524105  Uncommon or outdated technology (in 2011)
[G11B 7/0032] for moving-picture soundtracks, i.e. cinema  cameras or projectors with sound recording or reproducing means  G03B31/02 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Uncommon or outdated technology (in 2011)
[G11B 7/0033] with cards -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Optical storage of small amounts of data on cards (analogous to magnetic strip on bank cards) is normally classified in G06K19/06009 (media) or G06K7/10 (methods and apparatus)
[G11B 7/0037] with discs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This sub-group is a residual sub-group and should only be assigned if there is something about an optical disc system related to the optical nature of recording and reproduction that is not classifiable elsewhere in G11B7/00  this sub-group includes systems in which the label information is written optically on the non-data side of disc e.g. technologies such as Hewlett Packard LightScribe and Yamaha/FujiFilm LabelFlash  for labelling of optical data carriers that does not write the label data with the optical head used to write the main data, see G11B23/40
[G11B 7/00375] arrangements for detection of physical defects, e.g. of recording layer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This sub-group is a residual sub-group and should only be assigned if (part of) the subject-matter can not be classifed elsewhere, in particular in one of the following:  G11B7/0948 : servo control specially adapted for detection and avoidance or compensatin of imprefections on the carrier e.g. dust, scratches, dropouts  G11B20/1889 : defect management  G11B20/1816 testing e.g. of dropouts  G11B7/268 : checking for defects during/after manufacture  G01N21/9506 : Systems specially adapted for investigating the presence of flaws or contamination in optical discs
[G11B 7/004] Recording, reproducing or erasing methods -- The G11B 7/004 subclass covers methods for recording, reproducing or erasing information on media that rely on relative motion between the record carrier and an optical transducer. It includes techniques that use a thermal beam of optical radiation for writing and a lower-power optical beam for reading or erasing, applied to optical storage formats.
[G11B 7/0045] Recording -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Indexing Code G11B2007/00457 is assigned for two photon recording (including two photon recording in holographic data storage media
[G11B 7/00451] involving ablation of the recording layer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  For example, recording data as "pits" in a dye recording layer (e.g. CD-R, DVD-R, BD-R) not to be confused with spectral hole burning (see G11B7/00453 ) for materials used in recording layers see G11B7/242 and subgroups
[G11B 7/00452] involving bubble or bump forming -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Uncommon or outdated technology (in 2011)  Generally involves thermal expansion of a recording layer to form bumps which alter the amount of reflected light because ot the phase difference (interference effect) between light reflected by the protuberance and light reflected by the surface which is not raised.  Figure from EP 338776  For materials used in recording layers see G11B7/242 and subgroups.
[G11B 7/00453] involving spectral or photochemical hole burning -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Uncommon or outdated technology (in 2011)  Multiple bits can be stored in the same space using different frequencies atoms or molecules which are in different environments. The absorption line of a material is inhomogeneously broadened (comprised of many homogeneously broadened lines, due to the slightly different energies and therefore frequencies/wavelengths corresponding to the different environments.  Not to be confused with ablative recording (which is a thermal effect, not a spectral one).  For materials used in recording layers see G11B7/242 and subgroups.
[G11B 7/00454] involving phase-change effects -- **Definition:** This place covers:  For example, recording using chalcogenide materials e.g. GeSbTe.  This classification should only be assigned if:  Recording pulse sequences are classified in G11B7/0062 (for overwritable media) or in G11B7/00456 (for write-once media)  Phase change materials are classified in G11B7/243 and subgroups.
[G11B 7/00455] involving reflectivity, absorption or colour changes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  For example, photochromic recording in which the colour is changed; documents concerning recording in which the texture of the surface is changed to change the reflectivity are classifiable here.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Involving ablation of the recording layer -> G11B7/00451 - Involving bubble or bump forming -> G11B7/00452 - Involving spectral or photochemical hole burning -> G11B7/00453 - Involving phase-change effects -> G11B7/00454
[G11B 7/00456] Recording strategies, e.g. pulse sequences  G11B7/0062  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Overwriting strategies, e.g. recording pulse sequences with erasing level used for phase-change media -> G11B7/0062
[G11B 7/00458] Verification, i.e. checking data during or after recording -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This class should only be assigned for invention information (mostly older technology) in which the actual data is read during recording and compared with the data that should have been recorded, or verification using a separate read/verify beam.  Running optical power control (ROPC): G11B7/1263  Walking optical power control : G11B7/1263
[G11B 7/005] Reproducing -- The CPC class G11B 7/005 concerns reproducing information stored by optical means, such as playback of data recorded with a laser or thermal beam, and includes devices that read the recorded pattern using a lower-power optical beam.
[G11B 7/0051] involving phase depth effects -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Documents are only assigned this class (or code) if the particular problem or solution of the invention disclosed relates to the aspect of phase depth AND if there is no better classification (see below). (Phase depth effects are the most common basis for reproduction of information in G11B7/00 : the reproduction of the usual data pits in a CD, DVD, BD (i.e. pits in the plastic substrate, covered with a reflective layer) relies on this effect.)  Also reproduction of phase change media normally involves a phase depth effect, because the refractive indices of the various layers are adjusted to give a particular optical path length difference. (n.b. "phase" here has two different meaning - the physical state of the material ("phase change material") and the optical or physical difference in path length between two aread resulting in contructive or destruction optical interference "phase depth")  G11B7/24085 (Arrangement of the information on the record carrier) Details of discrete information structures, e.g. shape or dimensions of pits, prepits  (n.b. From 2012 revision of the IPC introduces new group G11B7/2407 for media characterised by the pits, and ECLA will be revised correspondingly)
[G11B 7/0052] involving reflectivity, absorption or colour changes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  For example, reproduction of data recorded in a photochromic material.
[G11B 7/0053] Reproducing non-user data, e.g. wobbled address, prepits, BCA -- This subclass covers techniques for optically reproducing data that is not directly written by the user, such as wobble addresses, pre-pits and burst cutting area marks on optical discs. It relates to the detection and decoding of these reference signals that guide positioning and synchronization during playback.
[G11B 7/0055] Erasing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mostly uncommon or outdated technology (in 2011) - nearly all modern commercial disc technology is of the write-once type (e.g. recording in dye layer) or of the overwritable type (e.g. recording in a layer of phase change material).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Overwriting -> G11B7/006 - Recording, reproducing or erasing by using optical interference patterns, e.g. holograms. -> G11B7/0065
[G11B 7/00552] involving colour change media -- The G11B 7/00552 subclass covers optical information storage techniques that use media whose colour changes when exposed to an optical beam. It includes recording or reproducing data by altering the colour of the recording layer, such as dye or phase-change materials, and reading the data with a lower-power optical beam. This falls under physics-based methods where the relative movement between carrier and transducer is involved.
[G11B 7/00555] involving liquid crystal media -- The subclass concerns optical recording or reproduction techniques that employ liquid-crystal media as the recording layer, where data are written or read by modulating light interacting with the liquid-crystal material. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer, specifically for optical methods such as thermal or low-power beam processes.
[G11B 7/00557] involving phase-change media -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mostly uncommon or outdated technology (in 2011) - most modern phase change materials are overwritable.
[G11B 7/006] Overwriting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rewritable is often synonymous with overwritable (but rewritable may mean merely erasable in old documents).
[G11B 7/0062] Overwriting strategies, e.g. recording pulse sequences with erasing level used for phase-change media -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Both G11B7/00456 and G11B7/0062 are assigned if the strategy or strategies disclosed is/are applicable to both write-once and rewritable media.  See Figure of a pulse strategy under G11B7/00456 .
[G11B 7/0065] Recording, reproducing or erasing by using optical interference patterns, e.g. holograms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Relationship between groups:  There are subgroups for certain aspects of holographic recording and where one (or more) or those subgroups is relevant they are assigned, and G11B7/0065 or Indexing Code G11B7/0065 are not assigned unless  there is "invention" information relevant to the system as a whole, or  if there is no better classification for the invention information.  Warning: These "holographic" subgroups were created in the second half of 2009, and the reclassification from G11B7/0065 has not been systematically done. For documents published before 2010, G11B7/0065 and Indexing Code G11B7/0065 should be searched.  If there is no subgroup specific to holography for the invention subject-matter (e.g. there are no specific subgroups under G11B7/242 for specific materials for holography), then the relevant general class is assigned and the Indexing Code G11B7/0065 . For example:  G11B7/08564 for galvanomirrors e.g. used in angular multiplexing G11B7/128 for SLM, acousto-optical, electro-optical, magneto optical modulators G11B7/128 and G11B7/1369 if modulator is liquid crystal device  G11B7/1392 for a diffuser (e.g. in speckle holography)  G11B7/1365 for polarization rotators G11B7/1372 , or subgroup, for lenses G11B7/1356 for double prism beam splitter G11B7/1395 for other beam splitters  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Where the recording mechanism of the holographic storage is of interest e.g. G11B2007/00457 is assigned for two-photon recording of holograms -> G11B7/0045 - Collinear holography: Where the object and reference beams are substantially parallel or coaxial before being focused (synonym: "coaxial", "common path", co-propagating) -> G11B2007/00653 - Counter propagating holography: Where the object and reference beams are directed to opposite sides of the medium (synonym: "standing wave" or "stationary wave") -> G11B2007/00656 - Holographic storage of images -> G03H
[G11B 7/007] Arrangement of the information on the record carrier, e.g. form of tracks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Aspects for data formats for standards such as CD, DVD, BD are not classified in G11B7/007 unless the technical problem underlying the invention arises because of the optical nature of the recording. In such cases the documents may be classifiable both in G11B7/007 and in G11B20/00 .  Standards for various aspects of the formats of optical discs are available from the Internet site of ECMA (www.ecma.org).  (e.g. CD-ROM, DVD-ROM, DVD-RAM, DVD-R, DVD-RW, CD-RW Ultra-speed)  White Papers for the Blu-ray Disc Format are available from the Internet site of the Blu-ray Disc Association (www.blu-raydisc.com)  e.g. the Physical Format Specifications for BD-RE and for BD-ROM  There are also ECMA standards for holographic discs (HVD-ROM, HVD)
[G11B 7/00718] Groove and land recording, i.e. user data recorded both in the grooves and on the lands -- A groove-and-land recording method stores user data in both the depressions (grooves) and the raised areas (lands) of an optical disc. This approach enables higher data density by utilizing the entire surface for recording, and it can be applied with optical beams of varying power for writing and reading. It falls under the CPC class G11B 7/00718, which covers recording or reproducing by optical means based on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/00736] Auxiliary data, e.g. lead-in, lead-out, Power Calibration Area [PCA], Burst Cutting Area [BCA], control information  sector headers or adresses in prepits  G11B7/00745 ; address data in track wobble  G11B7/24082 -- **Glossary:** - Burst cutting area: Auxiliary data recorded towards the inner periphery of the disc, as a type of bar code (stripes) . Because it is recorded as stripes, it can be read before the tracking servo is on. The data is phase-encoded (i.e. "0" is represented by 2 channel bits set to "1 0", and "1" by "0 1"), and the sequence of data bits is return-to-zero modulated.
[G11B 7/00745] Sectoring or header formats within a track  formats in general  G11B20/12 -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 7/00745 pertains to the specific formatting of sectors and headers within tracks on optical storage media, addressing the structure and organization of data for recording or reproducing processes. This classification is part of a broader category that includes various methods and systems for information storage based on the relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/00772] on record carriers storing information in the form of optical interference patterns, e.g. holograms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  When the invention information concerns multiplexing, the document should be classified in G11B7/00772 (since it has to do with the arrangement of the information) and assigned the relevant EC for the means (elements) by which the multiplexing is done. For example:  angular (azimuth) multiplexing:  G11B7/08564 for deformable or movable mirrors and G11B7/1362 when the movable mirror cooperates with stationary mirror(s):
[G11B 7/00781] Auxiliary information, e.g. index marks, address marks, pre-pits, gray codes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  For example, separate layers containing servo information for holographic discs, or marks around the edge for aligning page type holographic media.  Servo information for volume storage media that are not holographic: classify G11B7/0938 (or Indexing Code G11B7/0938 if the document discloses these details, but it is not particularly relevant to the invention information) in addition to the Indexing Code G11B7/00 : 00S4 to indicate the volumetric aspect of the storage medium itself.  Warning: This subgroup was created in the second half of 2009, and the reclassification from G11B7/0065 has not been systematically done. For documents published before 2010, G11B7/0065 and Indexing Code G11B7/0065 should be searched.
[G11B 7/0079] Zoned data area, e.g. having different data structures or formats for the user data within data layer, Zone Constant Linear Velocity [ZCLV], Zone Constant Angular Velocity [ZCAV], carriers with RAM and ROM areas -- G11B 7/0079 covers storage media that are divided into zones with distinct data structures or formats, such as zones operating at constant linear velocity or constant angular velocity, and may include separate RAM and ROM regions on the carrier. It relates to recording or reproducing by optical means where the relative movement between carrier and transducer varies across zones.
[G11B 7/013] for discrete information, i.e. where each information unit is stored in a distinct discrete location -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Only aspects of format that are adapted to solve a problem related to the optical recording. (In general, the data formats for optical recording media are not very closely related to the optical aspect and are classified in G11B20/12 )
[G11B 7/08] Disposition or mounting of heads or light sources relatively to record carriers -- The G11B 7/08 entry pertains to the arrangement and mounting of optical heads, light sources or sensors with respect to a record carrier. It includes mechanisms for positioning, aligning, focusing or otherwise fixing the head or light source relative to the medium in optical recording or playback systems.
[G11B 7/081] for time base error correction by moving the light beam -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Uncommon or outdated technology (in 2011)
[G11B 7/082] Aligning the head or the light source relative to the record carrier otherwise than during transducing, e.g. adjusting tilt set screw during assembly of head -- This CPC subclass covers methods for aligning the read/write head or optical source with the record carrier before or after transducing, such as adjusting tilt screws during assembly, and is distinct from alignment performed during actual signal conversion.
[G11B 7/083] relative to record carriers storing information in the form of optical interference patterns, e.g. holograms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus/methods aspects of access e.g. multiplexing are classified here, and if appropriate in the relevant optical element group.  If the optical elements used are not especially adapted for the type of access, but e.g. just used or controlled in a special way then the document should be classified in G11B7/083 and coded in the appropriate optical element group (e.g. galvanomirror G11B7/08564 or G11B7/00 : 0085B3 ).  If it is the arrangement of the information aspect of the multiplexing that is "invention information" it is classified in G11B7/00772  Warning: This subgroup was created in the second half of 2009, and the reclassification from G11B7/0065 has not been systematically done. For documents published before 2010, G11B7/0065 and G11B7/0065 should be searched.
[G11B 7/085] with provision for moving the light beam into, or out of, its operative position -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 7/085 pertains to methods and apparatuses for recording or reproducing information using optical means, specifically addressing mechanisms that move the light beam into or out of its operational position. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy under PHYSICS, focusing on INFORMATION STORAGE technologies.
[G11B 7/08505] Methods for track change, selection or preliminary positioning by moving the head -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangements for moving the whole head -> G11B7/0857
[G11B 7/08511] with focus pull-in only -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Changing layers in media with multiple data layers e.g. dual layer DVD.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Focus search for distinguishing between types of discs -> G11B19/127
[G11B 7/08517] with tracking pull-in only -- This subclass covers optical data-recording or playback devices in which the tracking function is realized solely by a pull-in mechanism, meaning the transducer is drawn toward the recorded track without an active push-out component. It applies to systems using thermal or low-power optical beams where relative motion between carrier and transducer is employed.
[G11B 7/08523] with both tracking and focusing pull-in -- This subclass addresses optical data-storage systems in which the recording or reproducing beam is kept aligned by mechanisms that provide both tracking and focusing pull-in as the record carrier moves relative to the transducer, typically using a thermal or low-power optical beam.
[G11B 7/08529] Methods and circuits to control the velocity of the head as it traverses the tracks -- The G11B 7/08529 subclass covers techniques and circuit designs that regulate the speed of a read/write head as it moves across tracks in optical information-storage media, ensuring precise positioning for reliable recording and playback. This includes dynamic control methods for head velocity during operation.
[G11B 7/08535] to maintain constant velocity during the traverse -- The class covers techniques for keeping the record carrier moving at a uniform speed while an optical beam-such as a thermal or low-power laser-is used to write or read data, ensuring consistent positioning and signal quality during the traverse.
[G11B 7/08541] involving track counting to determine position -- G11B 7/08541 covers optical information-storage devices that locate the read/write point by counting the number of tracks passed on a record carrier. It applies to systems where the position of the transducer is derived from a sequential track count rather than from absolute markers, enabling precise data access in optical media.
[G11B 7/08547] Arrangements for positioning the light beam only without moving the head, e.g. using static electro-optical elements -- This CPC subclass relates to optical data-storage devices in which the light beam is positioned or steered without moving the recording head, using fixed electro-optical components such as prisms, diffractive elements or liquid-crystal arrays. It covers static beam-deflection arrangements employed during recording or playback.
[G11B 7/08552] using electro-optical elements -- G11B 7/08552 covers methods and devices that employ electro-optical components for recording or reproducing information optically when the record medium moves relative to the transducer. It includes optical recording with a thermal beam and low-power optical playback using such elements.
[G11B 7/08558] using acousto-optical elements -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus that employ acousto-optical devices for recording or reproducing information on a storage medium where data are accessed by relative movement between the carrier and a transducer, typically using optical beams.
[G11B 7/08564] using galvanomirrors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  For example, multiplexing in holographic storage of data.
[G11B 7/0857] Arrangements for mechanically moving the whole head -- G11B 7/0857 covers devices that move the entire optical head as a unit to position it relative to the recording medium, rather than moving only individual components. The arrangement is used in optical disk systems where the whole head assembly is displaced by mechanical actuators to achieve tracking and focusing.
[G11B 7/08576] Swinging-arm positioners -- Swinging-arm positioners are mechanisms that mount a read/write transducer on a pivoted arm so the device can be swung into precise locations relative to the record carrier. This arrangement is used for optical recording or playback where the transducer must be positioned by controlled relative movement between the carrier and the optical beam.
[G11B 7/08582] Sled-type positioners -- A sled-type positioner is a linear mechanism that moves the recording medium relative to an optical transducer, allowing precise positioning for optical recording or low-power optical playback. It is used in systems where the carrier slides along a guide, enabling accurate relative motion between the record carrier and the beam source. This design supports optical storage methods based on relative movement.
[G11B 7/08588] with position sensing by means of an auxiliary system using an external scale -- This subclass covers optical data recording or playback systems that determine the location of the recording head or beam with an external scale or auxiliary device to sense position. It applies to devices using relative movement between the media and the optical transducer, such as thermal or low-power laser beams.
[G11B 7/08594] to access both sides of the disc with the same head assembly -- The code G11B 7/08594 covers devices that read or write optical discs from both sides using a single head assembly, eliminating the need for separate pickups for each side. It applies to systems where the disc and transducer move relative to each other, such as in dual-sided recording or playback with a shared optical head.
[G11B 7/09] with provision for moving the light beam or focus plane for the purpose of maintaining alignment of the light beam relative to the record carrier during transducing operation, e.g. to compensate for surface irregularities of the latter or for track following -- G11B 7/09 covers optical recording or reproducing systems that adjust the light beam or its focus plane during operation to keep alignment with the record carrier, for example by moving the beam to compensate for surface irregularities or to follow a track.
[G11B 7/0901] for track following only  G11B7/0925 ,  G11B7/094 ,  G11B7/0941 ,  G11B7/0943 ,  G11B7/0945 ,  G11B7/0946 ,  G11B7/0948  take precedence -- G11B 7/0901 covers optical recording or reproducing systems that provide only a track-following function for devices where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer. More specific subclasses such as G11B 7/0925, 7/094, 7/0941, 7/0943, 7/0945, 7/0946 and 7/0948 are treated as higher-ranking and therefore take precedence.
[G11B 7/0903] Multi-beam tracking systems -- Multi-beam tracking systems are arrangements that monitor and control several optical beams simultaneously as they follow data tracks on a moving recording medium, ensuring accurate positioning for recording or reading information. They are used in optical storage devices where multiple beams increase data rates or improve reliability.
[G11B 7/0904] Dithered tracking systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Uncommon or outdated technology in 2011.  Methods in which the beam is driven back and forth to generated the tracking error signal.
[G11B 7/0906] Differential phase difference systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[G11B 7/0908] for focusing only  G11B7/0925 ,  G11B7/094 ,  G11B7/0941 ,  G11B7/0943 ,  G11B7/0945 ,  G11B7/0946 ,  G11B7/0948  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electromechanical actuators for lens positioning ( G11B7/0857 takes precedence) -> G11B7/0925 - Methods and circuits for servo offset compensation -> G11B7/094 - Methods and circuits for servo gain or phase compensation during operation (for initialising servos G11B7/0945 ) -> G11B7/0941 - Methods and circuits for performing mathematical operations on individual detector segment outputs -> G11B7/0943 - Methods for initialising servos, start-up sequences -> G11B7/0945 - Specially adapted for operation during external perturbations not related to the carrier or servo beam, e.g. vibration -> G11B7/0946 - Specially adapted for detection and avoidance or compensation of imperfections on the carrier, e.g. dust, scratches, dropouts ( G11B7/095 takes precedence) -> G11B7/0948
[G11B 7/0909] by astigmatic methods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Figure from EP1220210
[G11B 7/0911] by far-field method -- The subclass G11B 7/0911 covers optical recording and reproducing techniques that employ a far-field method, where the light interacts with the recording medium at a distance greater than the near-field region. It includes processes such as writing with a thermal optical beam and reading with a lower-power optical beam, all performed without close-proximity coupling between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 7/0912] by push-pull method -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Push-pull tracking -> G11B7/0901
[G11B 7/0914] by non-optical methods, e.g. capacitive -- The G11B 7/0914 subclass covers recording or reproducing data on a moving medium by non-optical techniques, such as capacitive coupling, instead of using light. It falls within the broader category of information-storage methods that rely on relative movement between the record carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 7/0916] Foucault or knife-edge methods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Figure taken from JP60010424
[G11B 7/0917] Focus-error methods other than those covered by  G11B7/0909  -  G11B7/0916 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Uncommon or outdated technology (in 2011).  Further classification information:  The following Indexing Codes are assigned:  G11B2007/0919 critical angle methods  G11B2007/0919 dither methods  S11B/09B8F far-field methods  G11B2007/0924 skewed beams method
[G11B 7/0925] Electromechanical actuators for lens positioning  G11B7/0857  takes precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 7/0925 pertains to electromechanical actuators used for lens positioning in optical recording or reproducing devices, with a note that classification G11B 7/0857 should be considered first if applicable.
[G11B 7/0927] for focusing only  G11B7/0937  takes precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 7/0927 pertains to methods and apparatuses for focusing an optical beam in the process of recording or reproducing information on a storage medium, specifically when the focus control is used independently. This classification is part of a broader category that deals with information storage based on relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/0929] for tracking only  G11B7/0937  takes precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 7/0929 pertains to methods and systems for optical recording or reproducing that are specifically designed for tracking purposes, with a note indicating that the classification G11B 7/0937 should be used if applicable.
[G11B 7/093] for focusing and tracking  G11B7/0932  -  G11B7/0937  take precedence -- The G11B 7/093 subclass covers optical recording and playback systems that incorporate mechanisms for focusing the light beam on the record carrier and for tracking the beam as the carrier moves relative to the transducer, ensuring precise alignment during writing or reading of data.
[G11B 7/0932] Details of sprung supports -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sprung supports - e.g. lens holder support by wires or flat springs  also contains other support systems such as liquid, magnetic, combinations.
[G11B 7/0933] Details of stationary parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Stationary parts: e.g. the magnets on the sled, e.g. the yokes and magnets of a "normal" four-wire-sprung actuator.
[G11B 7/0935] Details of the moving parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Moving parts: lens holder and coils (or, occasionally, magnets) attached to it. Example:
[G11B 7/0937] Piezoelectric actuators -- The G11B 7/0937 entry covers the use of piezoelectric actuators to control the relative motion between a record carrier and an optical transducer in data-storage systems, enabling precise positioning for recording or reproducing information by optical means. It includes designs where the actuator drives the medium or the read/write head to achieve high-resolution movement during thermal-beam or low-power optical recording.
[G11B 7/0938] servo format, e.g. guide tracks, pilot signals -- G11B 7/0938 relates to servo formats in optical data storage, such as guide-tracks or pilot signals that are recorded with the data to enable precise positioning and tracking of the read/write transducer.
[G11B 7/094] Methods and circuits for servo offset compensation -- The classification covers techniques for adjusting the servo loop in optical storage devices to correct position errors caused by offsets between the read/write head and the recorded track. It includes circuit designs and control algorithms that compensate for bias in the tracking and focus servos during recording or playback.
[G11B 7/0941] Methods and circuits for servo gain or phase compensation during operation  for initialising servos  G11B7/0945 -- This subclass covers methods and circuitry that adjust servo gain or phase while an optical recording or playback system is operating, especially during the initialization of the tracking and focusing loops, ensuring stable performance as the carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 7/0943] Methods and circuits for performing mathematical operations on individual detector segment outputs -- The G11B 7/0943 class covers techniques and circuit designs that execute mathematical functions directly on the signals produced by each segment of an optical detector. It is used for processing data in optical information-storage systems where a moving record carrier is read by a transducer, such as thermal-beam recording or low-power optical playback.
[G11B 7/0945] Methods for initialising servos, start-up sequences -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Distinguishing between types of discs by using an initial focus search or scan -> G11B19/12
[G11B 7/0946] specially adapted for operation during external perturbations not related to the carrier or servo beam, e.g. vibration -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 7/0946 pertains to optical recording and reproducing systems designed to function effectively despite external disturbances unrelated to the carrier or servo beam, such as vibrations. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy under PHYSICS, specifically addressing information storage technologies.
[G11B 7/0948] specially adapted for detection and avoidance or compensation of imperfections on the carrier, e.g. dust, scratches, dropouts  G11B7/095  takes precedence -- G11B 7/0948 covers optical recording or reproducing systems that incorporate special means to detect, avoid or compensate for imperfections on the record carrier such as dust, scratches or drop-outs. The subclass focuses on adaptive methods for maintaining signal quality despite carrier defects, and it is subordinate to G11B 7/095.
[G11B 7/095] specially adapted for discs, e.g. for compensation of eccentricity or wobble -- G11B 7/095 covers optical recording or playback systems for disc media that incorporate special adaptations to compensate for disc eccentricity, wobble or other mechanical deviations. These measures improve precise relative motion between the disc and the read/write transducer, ensuring reliable data storage and retrieval.
[G11B 7/0953] to compensate for eccentricity of the disc or disc tracks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Acting on the tracking actuator.
[G11B 7/0956] to compensate for tilt, skew, warp or inclination of the disc, i.e. maintain the optical axis at right angles to the disc -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Acting on focusing or tilt actuator
[G11B 7/10] Interchangeable mountings, e.g. for replacement of head without readjustment -- G11B 7/10 covers interchangeable mounting arrangements for optical data storage devices that permit the replacement or repositioning of a read-write head without the need for readjusting the alignment between the carrier and the transducer. Such mountings enable quick head swaps while maintaining the precise relative motion required for recording or reproducing data.
[G11B 7/12] Heads, e.g. forming of the optical beam spot or modulation of the optical beam -- G11B 7/12 covers the heads used in optical information-storage systems, specifically the components that form or modulate the optical beam spot. It includes devices that shape the beam or vary its intensity to write or read data on a moving record carrier by optical means.
[G11B 7/121] Protecting the head, e.g. against dust or impact with the record carrier -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Brushes incorporated into CD form factor discs for cleaning e.g. EP1411505
[G11B 7/122] Flying-type heads, e.g. analogous to Winchester type in magnetic recording -- G11B 7/122 covers recording or reproducing heads that are supported above the record carrier by an air cushion, similar to the Winchester magnetic heads. These flying-type heads move relative to the medium, allowing non-contact operation and reduced wear. The classification applies to optical or magnetic systems where the head's position is maintained by relative motion.
[G11B 7/123] Integrated head arrangements, e.g. with source and detectors mounted on the same substrate -- Provides for optical recording or playback devices where the light source and photodetector are fabricated together on a single substrate, forming an integrated head. Such arrangements simplify alignment, reduce size and improve stability for systems that move a record carrier relative to the transducer.
[G11B 7/124] the integrated head arrangements including waveguides -- G11B 7/124 refers to integrated optical read-write head assemblies in which the light source, detector and any waveguides are combined into a single unit to direct, focus or collect light for recording or reproducing data on a moving storage medium. This integration simplifies alignment and can improve performance of optical storage devices.
[G11B 7/1245] the waveguides including means for electro-optical or acousto-optical deflection -- The entry G11B 7/1245 covers waveguide structures that incorporate elements for steering light beams by electro-optical or acousto-optical means, used in optical recording or playback where the carrier and transducer move relative to each other. It addresses designs that integrate such deflection mechanisms within the waveguide to control the path of the optical signal.
[G11B 7/125] Optical beam sources therefor, e.g. laser control circuitry specially adapted for optical storage devices -- G11B 7/125 covers optical beam sources and their control circuits specially adapted for optical-storage devices, such as lasers used to write or read data on a moving record carrier. It falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer, focusing on the light-generation hardware.
[G11B 7/126] Circuits, methods or arrangements for laser control or stabilisation -- G11B 7/126 covers circuits, methods and arrangements that control or stabilise laser operation in optical recording and playback systems. It includes feedback, power regulation, beam pointing and wavelength stabilisation to ensure accurate data inscription or retrieval on moving media.
[G11B 7/1263] Power control during transducing, e.g. by monitoring -- **Definition:** This place covers:  "Running optimum power control"  "walking optimum power control".  OPC carried out as a preparation when the medium is loaded or just before the transducing mode is started: G11B7/1267 Power calibration  **Glossary:** - Running OPC: Continuous adjustment of the writing power to the optimum power during recording. This compensates for changes in the optimum power during recording due changing conditions e.g. temperature change. (see for example the standard ECMA-394 "Recordable Compact Disc Systems CD-R - Multi-speed", Chapter 13 "Attachments", Annex 13 "Running OPC") - Walking OPC: According to wo 2006 018810 "Walking OPC calibration as disclosed in WO 03/065357 adapts the writing power at different instances during the writing process"
[G11B 7/1267] Power calibration -- This subclass covers techniques for calibrating the optical power used during recording or reproducing in optical storage systems that rely on relative movement between the medium and the transducer. It includes methods for measuring, adjusting, and controlling laser or thermal-beam intensity to ensure accurate data writing and reading.
[G11B 7/127] Lasers -- G11B 7/127 covers the use of lasers in optical data storage, where a laser beam writes or reads information by heating or illuminating a record carrier. It includes recording with a thermal laser and lower-power reproducing beams within the broader field of information storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/1275] Two or more lasers having different wavelengths -- G11B 7/1275 covers systems that use two or more lasers of different wavelengths for optical recording or playback where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer. Such arrangements exploit wavelength-dependent effects to enhance data density or enable multiplexed storage.
[G11B 7/128] Modulators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Speckle modulation in holographic storage, the following should be assigned as appropriate:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - The waveguides including means for electro-optical or acousto-optical deflection -> G11B7/1245
[G11B 7/13] Optical detectors therefor -- G11B 7/13 covers optical detectors employed in optical recording or playback of information where a moving record carrier is read or written with a light beam, such as thermal or low-power optical beams. These devices convert light signals into electrical signals to retrieve or verify stored data.
[G11B 7/131] Arrangement of detectors in a multiple array -- The subclass concerns how multiple light-sensing elements are positioned and interconnected in optical data-storage devices, detailing the geometric layout, spacing, and electrical wiring of detector arrays that read or reproduce information recorded by relative motion of a carrier and an optical beam.
[G11B 7/133] Shape of individual detector elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[G11B 7/135] Means for guiding the beam from the source to the record carrier or from the record carrier to the detector -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Documents in which the invention information concerns a common optical path  Documents in which the invention information concern the relative arrangement of different optical elements  Anti-reflection films on optical elements where the particular type of element is not important  Further classification information:  There is no specific classification in G11B7/00 for the manufacture of optical elements per se, therefore the manufacture of the optical elements is classified in the most relevant optical element group itself if this is closely related to the application of the element to optical recording/reproduction. (For mounting, aligning of elements in the head see G11B7/22 ).  Where subgroups of G11B7/135 are available for the means and for the function, both classification(s) for the elements and for the function are assigned.
[G11B 7/1353] Diffractive elements, e.g. holograms or gratings -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Irregular, non-repetitive phase steps on a relatively large scale -> G11B7/1367
[G11B 7/1356] Double or multiple prisms, i.e. having two or more prisms in cooperation -- The entry covers optical recording or playback systems that employ two or more prisms working together to manipulate light paths. By combining prisms, the device can split, steer, or focus beams more precisely, improving the reading or writing of data on moving optical media.
[G11B 7/1359] Single prisms -- G11B 7/1359 covers the use of a single prism in optical information storage systems, where a solitary refracting element directs or redirects a recording or reading laser beam onto the storage medium. This subclass falls under methods that record or reproduce data by optical means, specifically those relying on relative movement between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 7/1362] Mirrors -- The G11B 7/1362 subclass covers mirrors employed in optical information-storage systems that record or reproduce data by directing a light beam onto a moving record carrier. It includes mirrors that reflect, focus or steer the optical radiation used for writing or reading data in such devices.
[G11B 7/1365] Separate or integrated refractive elements, e.g. wave plates -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[G11B 7/1367] Stepped phase plates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  For example, plates used in apparatus compatible with multiple disc standards to control the aberration at one or more wavelengths  Any plate with a lateral spatially varying effect on the phase of the beam (i.e. in the plane of the plate) e.g. Figure 4 WO 2006/135053  This class is also assigned when the spatial variation is integrated into another element such as an objective lens (since this is essentially equivalent to a plate with the phase structure cooperating with the lens).
[G11B 7/1369] Active plates, e.g. liquid crystal panels or electrostrictive elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[G11B 7/1372] Lenses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Relative positioning of more than one type of lens (e.g. collimator and objective lens) e.g. for controlling magnification
[G11B 7/1374] Objective lenses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The SIL of compound objective lenses i.e. where SIL is between the objective lens and the optical data carrier  Further classification information.
[G11B 7/1376] Collimator lenses -- The CPC subclass G11B 7/1376 covers collimator lenses employed in optical recording and reproducing systems, where data storage relies on relative movement between a record carrier and an optical transducer. These lenses shape and direct light beams to improve focus and minimize divergence during recording or playback of information.
[G11B 7/1378] Separate aberration correction lenses -- G11B 7/1378 covers optical information-storage systems that employ separate lenses to correct aberrations in the recording or reproducing beam. It applies to devices where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer and the beam is focused or shaped by distinct corrective optics.
[G11B 7/1381] Non-lens elements for altering the properties of the beam, e.g. knife edges, slits, filters or stops -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Elements that:  Elements for apodisation (e.g. for "super-resolution" i.e. to reduce the beam width of a main lobe of the beam below the diffraction limit for that wavelength) but G11B7/1387 has precedence (i.e. if a lens for near-field apparatus includes a shielding element it is classified in G11B7/1387 , and not also G11B7/1381 ).  Note that after a recent reorganization (Q4/2011) the scope of this group has been broadened (it is no longer has the qualifier "as it falls on the detector")  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Diffractive elements, e.g. holograms or gratings -> G11B7/1353 - Double or multiple prisms, i.e. having two or more prisms in cooperation -> G11B7/1356 - Single prisms -> G11B7/1359 - Mirrors -> G11B7/1362 - Separate or integrated refractive elements, e.g. wave plates -> G11B7/1365 - Stepped phase plates -> G11B7/1367 - Active plates, e.g. liquid crystal panels or electrostrictive elements -> G11B7/1369
[G11B 7/1384] Fibre optics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Waveguide elements (mostly older technology), because they work using a similar principle.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Waveguide heads -> G11B7/1245
[G11B 7/1387] using the near-field effect -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In a hemispherical lens, the rays that come in at large angles (relative to optical axis) from the previous lens are totally internally reflected at the interface due to the refractive index difference BUT there is an evanescent wave which doesn't die to zero immediately. This can be used to read/write on a medium, as long as the medium is very close (e.g. if the hemispherical lens is on a flying head); Recording may also use the evanescent wave from a very fine tip held near a medium.  Warning: This subgroup was created in 2008 and the reclassification of documents published before 2009 has not been systematically done. For earlier documents G11B7/12 , G11B7/122 , G11B7/123 should be searched.  A sharply elongated optical fibre may act is a local emitter, similar to scanning near field optical microscopy (SNOM)
[G11B 7/139] Numerical aperture control means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means to control the angle of the outermost parts of the beam to the optical axis, therefore controlling the size of the spot at the focus.  For apparatus compatible with different standards this often involves some way to block the outer part of the beam for a particular wavelength (see e.g. US6396791 Figure 10(a)(b), paragraph 63, and the prior art shown in Figure 11, paragraph 14) using dichroic effects, diffraction grating or phase difference that affect one wavelength more than another, or polarisation (e.g. by using beams polarised in different directions for different wavelengths), but it may involve elements located elsewhere (e.g. US6160646 Figure 6-9, the asymmetrical grating in the central part of the lens is used for CD medium)  G11B7/139 is assigned for elements that allow a single lens to be used for different standards. Although switching between objective lenses in apparatus compatible with different standards e.g. CD, DVD, BD, changes the numerical aperture (as well as changing the aberration correction), such documents are not assigned G11B7/139 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Objective lenses with NA > 1 (i.e. for near field apparatus) -> G11B7/1387
[G11B 7/1392] Means for controlling the beam wavefront, e.g. for correction of aberration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spherical aberration, coma (also referred to as comatic aberration) and chromatic (i.e. varying with wavelength)  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Numerical aperture control means -> G11B7/139 - Means for shaping the cross-section of the beam, e.g. into circular or elliptical cross-section -> G11B7/1398
[G11B 7/13922] passive -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Numerical aperture control means -> G11B7/139
[G11B 7/13925] active, e.g. controlled by electrical or mechanical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The element specifically adapted for this purpose should also be classified.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Numerical aperture control means -> G11B7/139
[G11B 7/13927] during transducing, e.g. to correct for variation of the spherical aberration due to disc tilt or irregularities in the cover layer thickness -- The entry concerns optical recording or reproducing where, during the transducing step, the system adjusts the light path to compensate for spherical aberration caused by disc tilt or irregularities in the cover-layer thickness. This corrective action improves signal fidelity in devices such as optical discs that store data by relative movement between the disc and the transducer.
[G11B 7/1395] Beam splitters or combiners -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Diffractive elements, e.g. holograms or gratings -> G11B7/1353 - Double or multiple prisms, i.e. having two or more prisms in cooperation -> G11B7/1356
[G11B 7/1398] Means for shaping the cross-section of the beam, e.g. into circular or elliptical cross-section -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The shape of a contour of equal intensity
[G11B 7/14] specially adapted to record on, or to reproduce from, more than one track simultaneously -- This subclass covers optical recording or reproducing systems that can write to or read from several parallel tracks on a storage medium in a single operation, such as devices using multiple beams or an array of transducers to address multiple tracks simultaneously.
[G11B 7/22] Apparatus or processes for the manufacture of optical heads, e.g. assembly -- G11B 7/22 covers apparatus and processes for producing optical heads used in optical data storage, including assembly techniques for aligning the laser, detector and lens components. The classification falls under physics, information storage, storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer, specifically for devices that record or read by optical means.
[G11B 7/24] Record carriers characterised by shape, structure or physical properties, or by the selection of the material -- G11B 7/24 pertains to record carriers whose defining features are their shape, internal structure, or the physical properties of the material they are made from. The classification includes media such as optical discs, magnetic tapes or cards where the geometry or composition is deliberately selected to achieve specific storage, durability or read/write characteristics.
[G11B 7/24003] Shapes of record carriers other than disc shape -- G11B 7/24003 covers optical recording and reproducing systems that use non-disc shaped media such as tapes, sheets, cylinders or irregular forms, focusing on the geometry of the carrier. It falls under the sub-group that deals with relative movement between the record carrier and the transducer in information storage devices.
[G11B 7/24006] Cylindrical or shaft-shaped -- G11B 7/24006 covers optical recording or reproducing systems in which the record carrier is cylindrical or shaft-shaped, such as rotating rods or shafts that carry data written or read by a thermal or lower-power optical beam. The classification applies to devices where the relative motion between the carrier and the transducer is exploited for information storage based on optical means.
[G11B 7/24009] Tapes, long films or long sheets -- G11B 7/24009 covers tapes, long films or long sheets employed in optical recording or reproduction systems where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer. The entry pertains to information-storage technologies that use optical beams, such as thermal-beam recording or low-power optical playback, and falls under the physics-based information-storage hierarchy.
[G11B 7/24012] Optical cards -- This subclass covers optical cards, where data is stored on a card that is read and written by moving the card relative to an optical transducer, using a thermal optical beam for recording and a lower-power beam for reproduction. It is classified under information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/24015] Air-sandwiched discs -- This subclass concerns optical recording or playback devices that use a disc separated from the transducer by an air gap, such as air-sandwiched discs for thermal-beam recording or low-power optical readout, within the broader field of information storage based on relative motion between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/24018] Laminated discs -- G11B 7/24018 Laminated discs refers to disc-shaped storage media formed from layered materials that store data by optical recording or reproduction, typically using a moving thermal or low-power optical beam to write or read information. The classification applies to devices and methods employing such laminated optical discs within the broader context of information storage based on relative motion between the carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/24021] provided with a special shape or structure for centering or eccentricity prevention, e.g. alignment -- This subclass covers optical recording or playback devices that include a specially shaped or structured element to keep the beam centered on the record carrier and prevent eccentric motion, providing alignment for thermal-beam or lower-power optical recording.
[G11B 7/24024] Adhesion or bonding, e.g. specific adhesive layers -- It covers methods and materials for bonding components in optical data-storage devices, such as adhesive layers that attach the recording medium to a substrate or transducer. The classification applies to optical recording or reproducing where the record carrier moves relative to the light beam and specific adhesion techniques are used.
[G11B 7/24027] Layers -- This subclass covers optical recording or reproducing systems that employ multiple material layers to store information, such as multilayer discs or stacked media. It addresses the arrangement, composition and depth-selective access of these layers for data writing and reading.
[G11B 7/2403] Layers -- G11B 7/2403 concerns optical data-storage media that use multiple layers within the recording substrate. It covers the structure, materials and methods for recording or retrieving information from distinct layers with a moving optical beam, enabling higher capacity by stacking data in depth. The entry applies to multilayer configurations in optical recording or reproduction systems.
[G11B 7/24033] Electrode layers -- The G11B 7/24033 entry covers electrode layers applied to a record carrier that moves relative to an optical transducer, such as conductive coatings that supply electrical bias or heating during laser-based recording or low-power optical read-out. It pertains to the structure and materials of those electrode films.
[G11B 7/24035] Recording layers -- G11B 7/24035 covers the materials and structures that constitute the recording layer of an optical data-storage medium. It relates to the layer that stores information by undergoing physical or chemical changes when exposed to a focused optical beam during write or read operations. The classification includes various compositions, thicknesses and multilayer configurations designed for use where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 7/24038] Multiple laminated recording layers -- This subclass covers optical data storage media that contain several distinct recording layers laminated together, allowing data to be written and read by moving the medium relative to a light beam. The layers are individually accessed with different focal depths or wavelengths, increasing storage density while using thermal or low-power optical beams for recording and reproduction.
[G11B 7/24041] with different recording characteristics -- This subclass covers optical recording or reproducing methods in which the record carrier exhibits varying recording characteristics, such as differing sensitivities, thresholds, or material properties, that influence how an optical beam writes or reads data while the carrier moves relative to the transducer.
[G11B 7/24044] for storing optical interference patterns, e.g. holograms -- G11B 7/24044 covers the storage of data as optical interference patterns such as holograms, using optical recording or reproduction methods where an interference pattern is formed on a medium. This subclass falls under physics → information storage → information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/24047] Substrates -- G11B 7/24047 covers substrates used in optical information-storage systems, where data are recorded or reproduced by directing a light beam onto a movable carrier such as a disc or tape. The entry addresses the material, structure and preparation of the surfaces that support optical recording, including those designed for thermal-beam writing and low-power reading.
[G11B 7/2405] being also used as track layers of pre-formatted layers -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 7/2405 pertains to the use of layers in optical storage media that serve dual functions, such as providing both tracking and pre-formatting information for data recording and reproduction.
[G11B 7/24053] Protective topcoat layers lying opposite to the light entrance side, e.g. layers for preventing electrostatic charging -- It covers protective coating applied on the far side of an optical recording medium, the side opposite the incident light, intended to reduce or prevent electrostatic charge buildup during recording or playback. The classification belongs to optical recording/reproduction methods that use relative motion between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 7/24056] Light transmission layers lying on the light entrance side and being thinner than the substrate, e.g. specially adapted for Blu-ray® discs -- This CPC subclass G11B 7/24056 addresses light-transmission layers that are positioned on the light-entrance side of a recording medium, are thinner than the substrate, and are specially adapted for formats such as Blu-ray® discs.
[G11B 7/24059] specially adapted for near-field recording or reproduction -- G11B 7/24059 covers devices or methods for optical data recording or playback that are specifically designed to operate in the near-field region, where the distance between the light source and the medium is comparable to the wavelength, enabling high-resolution storage without physical contact.
[G11B 7/24062] Reflective layers -- G11B 7/24062 covers reflective layers incorporated in optical recording and playback media, such as those used in thermal-beam writing or low-power readout of data. The layers enhance signal return by reflecting the incident light toward the detector, enabling efficient storage and retrieval of information on moving record carriers.
[G11B 7/24065] Layers assisting in recording or reproduction below the optical diffraction limit, e.g. non-linear optical layers or structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Optical recording carriers adapted to be used in near-field or adapted to provide resolution below the diffraction limit e.g. provided with layers that act as masks. For example, "Super-RENS" (super resolution near field structure) media in which a low melting temperature layer such as Sb that acts as a controllable aperture.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - N: Conditioning of record carrier e.g. mechanised protection or means for reducing influence of physical parameters -> G11B7/24C
[G11B 7/24067] Combinations of two or more layers with specific interrelation -- The entry covers multilayer optical recording media in which two or more layers are combined with a defined relationship that affects how the carrier and transducer interact. It addresses designs where the relative positioning, optical properties, or functional coupling of the layers are specified to enable recording or reproducing data by optical means. This includes structures that use thermal or low-power optical beams for writing and reading across the layered system.
[G11B 7/2407] Tracks or pits -- G11B 7/2407 covers optical data storage methods that use physical tracks or pits on a recording medium to represent information, such as those created by a focused thermal or laser beam and later read back with a lower-power optical beam. The classification applies to systems where the relative movement between the carrier and the transducer creates these engraved features.
[G11B 7/24073] Tracks -- In optical data storage, tracks are the concentric or spiral pathways formed on the record carrier that guide the relative motion of the carrier and the optical transducer during recording or playback. These tracks define the precise location of information pits or lands and enable systematic access to stored data.
[G11B 7/24076] Cross sectional shape in the radial direction of a disc, e.g. asymmetrical cross sectional shape -- The CPC subclass G11B 7/24076 covers discs whose radial cross-sectional profile is non-circular or asymmetrical, defining the shape of the disc in the radial direction for optical recording or reproducing media. It falls under physics - information storage - information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/24079] Width or depth -- This subclass covers aspects of the physical dimensions of the recording track, specifically the width or depth of pits or marks created or read by optical or thermal beams in devices where the medium moves relative to the transducer. It relates to controlling or measuring these dimensions to affect storage density or signal quality.
[G11B 7/24082] Meandering -- G11B 7/24082 covers meandering - a technique where the recording medium follows a sinusoidal or serpentine path relative to an optical transducer during recording or playback, allowing the beam to trace multiple tracks or modulate signal density without moving the beam itself.
[G11B 7/24085] Pits -- Pits are microscopic depressions formed on an optical recording surface that represent binary data by their presence or absence. The pits are created by a focused thermal or laser beam that locally modifies the medium, and during playback a lower-power optical beam reads the variations in reflected light caused by the pit geometry. This method enables high-density information storage on discs such as CDs, DVDs and Blu-ray media.
[G11B 7/24088] for storing more than two values, i.e. multi-valued recording for data or prepits -- This CPC subclass addresses optical recording schemes where the relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer is used to store more than two discrete values per element, such as multi-level pits or pre-pits. It covers both high-power writing, e.g., thermal beams, and lower-power optical reading or writing techniques.
[G11B 7/24091] Combinations of pits and tracks with specific interrelation -- The code covers optical data storage media where pits and tracks are arranged in a defined pattern that controls their relative positions, enabling precise addressing or error correction. Such configurations exploit the interaction between pit geometry and track spacing to improve data density or read-write reliability.
[G11B 7/24094] Indication parts or information parts for identification -- The subclass G11B 7/24094 covers indication or information elements that identify data on an optical storage medium, such as markings, labels, codes or other visual cues used to recognize the recorded content or the medium itself. It includes identification tags, bar-codes, colored zones or printed symbols integrated with the record carrier.
[G11B 7/24097] Structures for detection, control, recording operation or replay operation -- The class covers structural components of optical information-storage devices in which the recording medium moves relative to the transducer, such as lenses, mirrors, thermal-beam emitters, and actuator mechanisms that detect, control, write or read data. It addresses the arrangements that enable precise beam interaction with the moving carrier during recording or playback.
[G11B 7/241] characterised by the selection of the material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Optical recording media such as CDs, DVDs, Blu-Ray discs and Holographic Versatile Discs (HVDs), Optical Cards etc. characterised by the materials.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Recording, reproducing or erasing methods -> G11B7/004 - Record carriers Indicating prior or unauthorized use by changing the physical properties of the record carrier - Limited play -> G11B23/282 - Sheet materials for thermography incl. laser writable labels (e.g. LightScribe® ) -> B41M5/26 - Sputtering targets for producing e.g. the reflective layer -> C23C14/3407 - Photosensitive materials for photography -> G03C1/00 - Materials for phase modulating patterns i.e. holographic images -> G03F7/001  **Glossary:** - 3D: three dimensional - Super-RENS: Super REsolution Near field Structure - Blue wavelength: 390 - 500 nm - "nanosize" or "nanoscale": related to a controlled geometrical size below 100 nanometres in one or more dimensions
[G11B 7/242] of recording layers -- The code G11B 7/242 covers features of recording layers used in optical information storage where data are written or read by moving a recording medium relative to a light beam. It includes the composition, structure and properties of the layers that receive or emit the optical signal.
[G11B 7/243] comprising inorganic materials only, e.g. ablative layers -- This CPC subclass deals with optical recording or reproducing devices that use only inorganic materials, such as ablative layers, in the recording medium. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer, covering methods where a thermal or low-power optical beam modifies an inorganic layer to store data.
[G11B 7/2433] Metals or elements of Groups 13, 14, 15 or 16 of the Periodic Table, e.g. B, Si, Ge, As, Sb, Bi, Se or Te -- The entry covers the use of metals or elements from groups 13-16 of the periodic table-such as boron, silicon, germanium, arsenic, antimony, bismuth, selenium or tellurium-in optical recording or reproducing media where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer, typically as recording layers or optical-transparent components.
[G11B 7/2437] Non-metallic elements -- G11B 7/2437 concerns information-storage techniques that use non-metallic elements such as organic dyes, semiconductor compounds or other non-metal materials as the active recording layer or transducer in optical recording or reproduction. It includes devices where these substances modulate light or heat to encode data on a moving medium.
[G11B 7/244] comprising organic materials only -- The subclass G11B 7/244 covers optical recording and playback systems that employ only organic materials as the recording medium, such as polymers, dyes or other carbon-based substances. Recording is performed with a high-power thermal optical beam that changes the material, while reproduction uses a lower-power beam to read the altered regions.
[G11B 7/245] containing a polymeric component -- The code G11B 7/245 covers optical recording or playback systems where a polymer material forms part of the medium or device, such as a polymeric layer that changes properties under a light beam to store data. Typically used for systems that employ thermal or lower-power optical beams to write or read information.
[G11B 7/246] containing dyes -- G11B 7/246 covers optical data-recording or playback systems that use a storage medium containing dyes which are chemically or physically altered by a light beam to represent information. The dyes may be thermally or photochemically changed to create readable marks.
[G11B 7/2463] azulene -- The G11B 7/2463 subclass covers optical recording and playback where a high-energy optical beam creates marks on a medium and a lower-power beam reads them, a technique often used in thermally-driven data storage. This entry specifies the use of the organic compound azulene as a recording layer material in such optical storage systems.
[G11B 7/2467] azo-dyes -- The entry G11B 7/2467 designates technologies that employ azo-dye compounds in optical recording or reproducing processes, where a light beam modifies the dye's absorption or fluorescence to store data on a moving carrier. It covers media, sensitizers and methods that use the reversible or irreversible photochemical change of azo dyes for information storage.
[G11B 7/247] methine or polymethine dyes -- This CPC subclass covers the use of methine and polymethine dyes in optical recording or playback processes where a record medium moves relative to a light source, such as thermal or low-power optical beams. It pertains to technologies that store information by altering dye-based layers during relative motion between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 7/2472] cyanine -- This subclass covers optical recording or reproducing processes that employ cyanine dyes as the photoreactive layer or sensitiser, typically in systems that use a thermal beam of light for writing and a low-power optical beam for reading. It falls under the CPC group for information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/2475] merocyanine -- Merocyanine refers to a family of photo-responsive organic dyes that undergo reversible structural changes when exposed to light, making them useful as recording media in optical data-storage systems that rely on a thermal or low-power optical beam to write and read information. In the CPC they are placed under G11B 7/2475, which covers optical recording/reproducing methods based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/2478] oxonol -- Oxonol denotes a class of organic dyes used as photochemically active compounds in optical recording media, where exposure to a recording beam creates or alters a visible pattern on the carrier. The classification covers the use of oxonol substances to form or modify a record that can later be reproduced optically.
[G11B 7/248] porphines -- G11B 7/248 covers porphine compounds used as optical recording materials, where data is written by a high-power thermal optical beam and read back with a lower-power beam. These substances form light-sensitive layers in storage media that rely on relative movement between the record carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 7/249] containing organometallic compounds -- G11B 7/249 covers optical data recording or playback media that incorporate organometallic compounds. These compounds are used in the recording layer or coating to modify the material's response to a focused optical beam, such as a thermal laser for writing and a lower-power beam for reading.
[G11B 7/2492] neutral compounds -- G11B 7/2492 covers neutral chemical compounds used in optical information-storage systems, such as those where a thermal beam records data or a low-power optical beam reproduces it. These substances act as non-ionic photosensitizers, dyes or media components that change optical properties under illumination to enable recording or playback.
[G11B 7/2495] as anions -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 7/2495 pertains to the recording or reproducing of information by optical means, specifically focusing on aspects related to anions in the process. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy under physics and information storage technologies.
[G11B 7/2498] as cations -- G11B 7/2498 covers optical recording or reproducing processes in which cations are employed as the active material on the recording medium. It falls under physics, information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer, specifically optical methods using a thermal or low-power light beam. The subclass addresses the use of cation-based layers to modify reflectivity or refractive index during data inscription.
[G11B 7/25] containing liquid crystals -- G11B 7/25 covers optical recording or playback media that incorporate liquid crystals as part of the record carrier. Such media use the light-responsive properties of liquid crystals to store or retrieve data, often by changing their optical characteristics under illumination.
[G11B 7/251] comprising inorganic materials dispersed in an organic matrix -- G11B 7/251 covers optical information storage media in which inorganic particles are embedded in an organic matrix, allowing recording or reproduction by an optical beam. The classification applies to devices that rely on relative motion between a record carrier and a transducer, such as thermal-beam recording or low-power readout. It falls under the physics hierarchy for information storage based on relative movement.
[G11B 7/252] of layers other than recording layers -- G11B 7/252 covers the use of layers that are not the active recording medium in optical information-storage devices. These may include protective, spacer, adhesive or other auxiliary layers that support or enhance the performance of the recorded or reproduced optical signal.
[G11B 7/253] of substrates -- G11B 7/253 pertains to the specifics of substrates used in optical recording or reproducing technologies, focusing on materials and structures that support the storage layer. This classification is part of a detailed system covering various aspects of information storage based on the relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/2531] comprising glass -- A glass substrate used as the recording medium in optical information-storage systems, where data are written or read by a moving optical beam such as a thermal beam or a lower-power reproducing beam, falls under CPC G11B 7/2531. This entry concerns glass-based carriers in optical recording and reproduction processes that rely on relative motion between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 7/2532] comprising metals -- G11B 7/2532 pertains to optical recording or playback techniques in which the recording medium contains metal elements, such as metallic layers, particles or coatings that alter reflectivity or absorption during the exposure process. The method relies on relative motion between the record carrier and the optical transducer.
[G11B 7/2533] comprising resins -- G11B 7/2533 covers optical recording or reproducing media that incorporate resin materials as part of the recording layer or protective coating. The classification applies to devices where a thermal or lower-power optical beam alters or reads the resin-based layer during the relative movement between the carrier and the transducer.
[G11B 7/2534] polycarbonates [PC] -- G11B 7/2534 designates polycarbonate materials employed in optical information storage devices where a record carrier moves relative to a light transducer. It covers the use of polycarbonate substrates, layers or discs for recording-reproducing data by a focused optical beam, such as in CDs, DVDs and similar media.
[G11B 7/2535] polyesters, e.g. PET, PETG or PEN -- G11B 7/2535 covers the use of polyester materials such as PET, PETG or PEN as the record carrier in optical information-storage devices, where data are written or read by an optical beam. It pertains to media that rely on the relative movement between the carrier and a transducer for recording or reproduction.
[G11B 7/2536] polystyrene [PS] -- G11B 7/2536 covers the use of polystyrene as a recording medium in optical data-storage systems where information is written with a thermal laser beam and read with a lower-power optical beam. It falls under physics-based information storage that relies on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/2537] epoxy resins -- G11B 7/2537 covers the use of epoxy resin compounds as components in optical recording or reproducing media, where information is stored by the relative motion of a record carrier and a transducer. Epoxy resins may be employed for forming the substrate, protective layers, or binding agents in devices that record or read data with optical beams.
[G11B 7/2538] polycycloolefins [PCO] -- G11B 7/2538 covers optical recording and playback techniques that employ polycycloolefin (PCO) polymers as the recording layer, used in media where a beam of optical radiation alters the material to store data and a lower-power beam reads it, typically with relative motion between the carrier and the transducer.
