[G11B 7/2539] biodegradable polymers, e.g. cellulose -- G11B 7/2539 covers the use of biodegradable polymers, such as cellulose, as the recording medium in optical information-storage devices where data are written or read by an optical beam moving relative to the carrier. This subclass addresses materials that decompose naturally while functioning in optical recording or reproducing processes.
[G11B 7/254] of protective topcoat layers -- This subclass concerns protective topcoat layers applied to optical recording media where data is written or read by a relative motion between the record carrier and a light source, such as a thermal beam for writing and a low-power beam for reading. It addresses the composition, structure and function of coatings that shield the recorded surface while permitting the necessary optical interaction for storage and retrieval.
[G11B 7/2542] consisting essentially of organic resins -- This subclass covers optical recording or playback media whose functional layer is made mainly of organic resin compounds, such as polymer films or coatings that store data by changes induced by a light beam. It applies to devices where the carrier moves relative to the transducer and the resin forms the essential recording medium.
[G11B 7/2545] containing inorganic fillers, e.g. particles or fibres -- The subclass covers optical information-storage media that employ inorganic fillers such as particles or fibres within the record carrier. These fillers are added to modify optical, thermal or mechanical properties of the medium used for recording with a thermal beam or reproducing with a lower-power optical beam.
[G11B 7/2548] consisting essentially of inorganic materials -- G11B 7/2548 denotes CPC subclass for optical recording or playback systems that rely on a relative motion between the storage medium and a transducer, using a high-power thermal optical beam for writing and a lower-power optical beam for reading, where the medium is composed essentially of inorganic materials.
[G11B 7/256] of layers improving adhesion between layers -- G11B 7/256 covers the use of intermediate layers that enhance adhesion between successive layers in optical recording or playback media, such as multilayer disks where adhesive films bond the substrate to functional recording layers, improving durability and performance of the optical storage device.
[G11B 7/257] of layers having properties involved in recording or reproduction, e.g. optical interference layers or sensitising layers or dielectric layers, which are protecting the recording layers -- G11B 7/257 relates to auxiliary layers that influence optical recording or reproduction, such as interference, sensitising or dielectric layers, which serve to protect the active recording layers in media where a transducer moves relative to the record carrier.
[G11B 7/2572] consisting essentially of organic materials -- The CPC subclass G11B 7/2572 concerns optical recording or reproducing systems in which the recording medium is essentially made of organic materials such as dyes, polymers or photolabile compounds, and which operate by a thermal or lower-power optical beam while the carrier and transducer move relative to each other. It resides under physics → information storage → information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/2575] resins -- This subclass deals with resin materials employed in optical recording or playback where data is written or read by a moving record carrier and a light beam, such as photosensitive layers on optical disks or other optically addressed media. It covers the composition, preparation, and application of resins that respond to thermal or low-power optical radiation for storing information.
[G11B 7/2578] consisting essentially of inorganic materials -- The subclass G11B 7/2578 covers optical information storage devices where the recording layer is made principally of inorganic substances. It pertains to systems that write or read data by directing a light beam onto a medium whose physical changes are driven by inorganic material properties. This includes thermal or low-power optical recording methods using such inorganic layers.
[G11B 7/258] of reflective layers -- G11B 7/258 covers optical recording and reproducing media that employ one or more reflective layers to bounce the light beam back toward the transducer, enabling data to be read or written. It includes devices such as optical discs where a metallic or dielectric coating serves as the reflective surface in the recording system.
[G11B 7/2585] based on aluminium -- The CPC entry G11B 7/2585 covers optical recording or reproducing processes that employ aluminium, such as using an aluminium layer as a reflective or recording surface in systems where a record carrier moves relative to an optical transducer. It pertains to methods that store data by altering or detecting aluminium-based media with a thermal or low-power optical beam.
[G11B 7/259] based on silver -- G11B 7/259 concerns optical recording and playback techniques that employ silver-based materials, such as silver halide or metallic silver layers, on a moving record carrier. The silver component interacts with the light beam to create or read data as the carrier and transducer move relative to each other.
[G11B 7/2595] based on gold -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11B 7/2595 pertains to the use of gold in recording or reproducing information by optical means, such as in the context of thermal beam or lower power optical beam technologies. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy under PHYSICS, specifically addressing INFORMATION STORAGE techniques involving relative movement between the record carrier and transducer.
[G11B 7/26] Apparatus or processes specially adapted for the manufacture of record carriers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Joining of disc substrates e.g. for DVDs.  G11B7/26 or a subclass is assigned when the process involves a single technical art for which provision exists elsewhere but where the adaptation is specific to the optical record carrier.  In this subgroup, special care should be taken to circulate the document to classifiers for the relevant "single technical art" - see the informative references.
[G11B 7/261] Preparing a master, e.g. exposing photoresist, electroforming -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electronic editing of signals on discs -> G11B27/034
[G11B 7/263] Preparing and using a stamper, e.g. pressing or injection molding substrates  production of optical record carriers, e.g. optical discs  B29D17/005 -- The G11B 7/263 subclass covers methods and tools for preparing and employing stampers used to press or inject-mold substrates that become optical record carriers such as CDs or DVDs, including the production of the disc-forming mold and its use in forming the final optical medium.
[G11B 7/265] Apparatus for the mass production of optical record carriers, e.g. complete production stations, transport systems -- The entry covers machinery used for large-scale manufacturing of optical record carriers, encompassing complete production stations and transport systems that move the media through processes such as recording, testing, and packaging.
[G11B 7/266] Sputtering or spin-coating layers  sputtering in general  C23C14/24 ; spin-coating in general  B05D1/005 -- This CPC subclass deals with optical recording and playback where the recording layer is formed by physical deposition methods. It includes the use of sputtering or spin-coating to apply thin films that serve as the data-bearing medium in devices that write or read by a moving record carrier and a light beam.
[G11B 7/268] Post-production operations, e.g. initialising phase-change recording layers, checking for defects  investigating the presence of flaws or contamination in optical discs  G01N21/9506 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This class is assigned for writing the BCA, which occurs during manufacture (not done by end user apparatus).  **Glossary:** - Burst code area: see Glossary of terms Figure in G11B7/00736
[G11B 7/28] Re-recording, i.e. transcribing information from one optical record carrier on to one or more similar or dissimilar record carriers -- G11B 7/28 covers the re-recording of data, meaning the transcription of information stored on one optical medium onto the same type of medium or onto a different optical carrier, using optical methods such as laser or thermal beams to write or copy the recorded pattern.
[G11B 9/00] Recording or reproducing using a method not covered by one of the main groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Recording on or reproducing from the same record carrier wherein for these two operations the methods are covered by different main groups of groups G11B3/00 - G11B7/00 or by different subgroups of group G11B9/00 ; Record carriers therefor driving or moving of heads G11B3/02 , G11B5/48 , G11B7/08 , G11B21/02 -> G11B11/00 - Marking using electrical current -> B41M5/20 - Measuring roughness or irregularity of surfaces -> G01B7/34  **Glossary:** - Near-field interaction: A very short distance interaction using scanning-probe techniques, e.g. quasi- contact or evanescent contact between head and record carrier
[G11B 9/02] using ferroelectric record carriers -- G11B 9/02 relates to information-storage devices that use ferroelectric materials as record carriers, where data is recorded or reproduced by relative movement between the carrier and a transducer using methods not covered by the main groups.
[G11B 9/04] using record carriers having variable electric resistance -- G11B 9/04 covers storage devices that record or reproduce data by altering the electric resistance of a moving record carrier, such as resistive tracks or variable-resistance media. It belongs to the physics field under information storage based on relative motion between carrier and transducer, and includes methods not assigned to the main groups.
[G11B 9/06] using record carriers having variable electrical capacitance -- The G11B 9/06 subclass covers devices that store or retrieve data by using record carriers whose electrical capacitance can be varied, such as capacitive cards or media where data are represented by changes in capacitance. It applies when the method does not fit any other main group in the relative-movement information-storage category.
[G11B 9/061] Record carriers characterised by their structure or form or by the selection of the material; Apparatus or processes specially adapted for the manufacture of record carriers  processes involving a single technical art and for which provision exists elsewhere,  see  the relevant class, e.g.  B05D ,  F16N ,  C08L -- G11B 9/061 pertains to the manufacturing processes of record carriers characterized by their structure, form, or material selection, specifically when these processes involve a single technical art and are covered in other classes such as B05D for coating methods, F16N for lubrication, and C08L for polymer compositions.
[G11B 9/062] characterised by the form, e.g. comprising mechanical protection elements -- G11B 9/062 covers recording or reproducing methods defined chiefly by the physical construction of the device, such as the inclusion of mechanical protection elements or specific housing forms, rather than by the functional principle of the method. It falls under information storage based on relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer and is used for techniques not classified in the main groups.
[G11B 9/063] characterised by the selection of the material -- G11B 9/063 classifies recording or reproducing techniques that rely on relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer, where the inventive aspect is the specific selection of the material used for the carrier, transducer or related components, rather than a distinct mechanical or electronic method.
[G11B 9/065] Additional layers for lubrication, wear protection or elimination of electrostatic charges of the interface between record carrier and head  G11B9/066 ,  G11B9/067  and  G11B9/068  take precedence -- G11B 9/065 relates to applying supplemental layers to the interface between a record carrier and its transducer head to provide lubrication, wear protection, or to neutralise electrostatic charge during recording or playback, for methods not covered by the main groups. The class falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between carrier and transducer.
[G11B 9/066] Electrically conductive layers  G11B9/068  takes precedence -- G11B 9/066 relates to information-storage systems that employ electrically conductive layers in devices where data is recorded or reproduced by relative movement between a record carrier and a transducer. The conductive layer may function as a write element, sensing electrode or protective coating, and it is superseded by the more specific subclass G11B 9/068.
[G11B 9/067] Dielectric layers; Processes for providing electrical conductivity to them  G11B9/068  takes precedence -- G11B 9/067 covers dielectric layers used in information-storage devices and the processes that impart electrical conductivity to those layers. It includes methods for doping, metallising or otherwise modifying the insulating material so it can function as a transducer element in recording or reproducing devices.
[G11B 9/068] Moulding resin compositions -- G11B 9/068 pertains to the moulding of resin compositions in the context of information storage, specifically for methods not covered by broader categories. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European and United States patent offices.
[G11B 9/07] Heads for reproducing capacitive information -- G11B 9/07 pertains to heads used for reproducing capacitive information, where a transducer reads charge variations from a moving record carrier. These heads are applied in storage systems that rely on relative motion between the carrier and the transducer and are not described in the main groups of the classification.
[G11B 9/075] using mechanical contact with record carrier, e.g. by stylus -- The subclass covers recording or reproducing information by direct mechanical contact between a stylus or similar element and the record carrier, such as a phonograph needle tracing grooves or a contact head engaging a magnetic or optical surface. It includes any method that relies on this physical interaction rather than optical, magnetic, or other non-contact techniques.
[G11B 9/08] using electrostatic charge injection -- G11B 9/08 covers techniques that record or reproduce data by injecting an electrostatic charge onto the recording surface, typically in systems where the record carrier moves relative to the transducer. This sub-group deals with methods that do not fall under the primary groups of the broader class for magnetic, optical or other conventional charge-based storage.
[G11B 9/10] using electron beam -- G11B 9/10 concerns the use of an electron beam to record or reproduce data on a medium in which information is stored by relative movement between the record carrier and a transducer, employing a method that is not covered by any of the main groups of the subclass.
[G11B 9/12] using near-field interactions -- G11B 9/12 covers recording or reproducing information by employing near-field interactions between the record carrier and the transducer, such as magnetic or capacitive coupling at very short distances, which are not classified in the main groups of the CPC.
[G11B 9/14] using microscopic probe means -- G11B 9/14 relates to recording or reproducing information by employing a microscopic probe that moves relative to the record carrier, a technique used in scanning-probe data-storage methods. It falls under physics → information storage → information storage based on relative movement between record carrier and transducer, and covers approaches not classified in the main groups.
[G11B 9/1409] Heads -- G11B 9/1409 defines the technical features of heads employed in recording or reproducing systems where a record carrier moves relative to a transducer. The subclass covers magnetic, optical or other transducer heads, their construction, positioning and operation in devices such as tape, disc or drum recorders that are not covered by principal groups. It pertains to the conversion of stored signals to or from a physical medium.
[G11B 9/1418] Disposition or mounting of heads or record carriers  G11B17/00  and  G11B19/00  take precedence -- G11B 9/1418 deals with how recording heads or media are positioned and mounted within information storage devices, specifying the spatial relationship between the transducer and the record carrier. More detailed aspects concerning magnetic heads (G11B17/00) or optical heads (G11B19/00) are covered by those subordinate groups.
[G11B 9/1427] with provision for moving the heads or record carriers relatively to each other or for access to indexed parts without effectively imparting a relative movement -- G11B 9/1427 covers data storage or playback systems in which the read/write heads or the media can be repositioned relative to each other, or where the device can access specific indexed sections without a continuous relative motion between head and carrier. This includes mechanisms such as movable head assemblies, rotating scanners, or selective addressing of tracks.
[G11B 9/1436] with provision for moving the heads or record carriers relatively to each other -- The subclass includes storage or reproduction devices that provide a mechanism for moving the recording heads and the record carriers relative to each other, such as systems that shift a read/write head across a tape, disk or other medium to access different tracks. It covers arrangements where the relative motion is part of the functional design rather than a fixed, static configuration.
[G11B 9/1445] switching at least one head in operating function; Controlling the relative spacing to keep the head operative, e.g. for allowing a tunnel current flow -- This subclass covers apparatus that actively switches at least one read/write head during operation and controls the head-to-medium spacing to keep the head functional, for example to permit a tunnelling current as part of the recording or reproducing process.
[G11B 9/1454] Positioning the head or record carrier into or out of operative position or across information tracks; Alignment of the head relative to the surface of the record carrier  G11B9/1445  takes precedence -- The code covers mechanisms that position a transducer head or the record carrier into or out of its working position and move it across data tracks, including loading, unloading, actuation and precise alignment of the head relative to the media surface. It applies to systems where these positioning functions are not covered by the main groups above.
[G11B 9/1463] Record carriers for recording or reproduction involving the use of microscopic probe means -- G11B 9/1463 covers record carriers specifically designed for recording or reproducing information by means of a microscopic probe, such as scanning probe or atomic-force techniques. The classification falls under physics → information storage → storage based on relative movement between the carrier and the transducer, and addresses media not covered by the main groups.
[G11B 9/1472] characterised by the form -- G11B 9/1472 classifies recording or reproducing processes that are defined by the specific form of the record carrier or transducer rather than by a particular motion type, covering methods not included in the main groups of relative-movement information storage.
[G11B 9/1481] Auxiliary features, e.g. reference or indexing surfaces -- The code G11B 9/1481 covers auxiliary elements added to information-storage media that move relative to a transducer, such as reference marks or indexing surfaces used to aid recording or playback, where the method does not fall within the primary groups of the subclass.
[G11B 9/149] characterised by the memorising material or structure -- This subclass covers storage and retrieval devices whose defining feature is the material or structure that retains the information, rather than conventional magnetic, optical or electronic processes. It applies to systems that record or reproduce data by exploiting the intrinsic properties of a specialized memorising medium such as phase-change films, polymer layers or nanostructured substrates.
[G11C] STATIC STORES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices or arrangements for storage of digital or analogue information in which no relative movement takes place between an information storage element and a transducer; which incorporate a selecting-device for writing-in or reading-out the information into or from the store.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Semiconductor memory devices -> H10B  **Glossary:** - 2D/3D: two/three dimensional - Storage element: an element which can hold at least one item of information and is provided with means for writing-in or reading-out this information - Memory: a device, including storage elements, which can hold information to be extracted when desired.
[G11C 11/00] Digital stores characterised by the use of particular electric or magnetic storage elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Digital stores characterised by arrangements of cells having volatile and non-volatile storage properties for back-up when the power is down -> G11C14/00 - Digital stores in which information comprising one or more characteristic parts is written into the store and in which information is read-out by searching for one or more of these characteristic parts, i.e. associative or content-addressed stores -> G11C15/00 - EPROM (Erasable PROM) EEPROM, E2PROM (Electrically-erasable PROM) EAROM (Electrically-alterable ROM) Flash memory -> G11C16/00 - G11C16/349 - Erasable programmable read-only memories -> G11C16/00 - Read-only memories programmable only once; Semi-permanent stores, e.g. manually-replaceable information cards -> G11C17/00 - ROM (Read-only Memory) -> G11C17/02 - G11C17/126 - PROM (Programmable ROM) -> G11C17/14 - G11C17/18 - Digital stores in which the information is moved stepwise, e.g. shift register -> G11C19/00 - Digital stores in which the information circulates -> G11C21/00  **Glossary:** - 2D: two dimensional
[G11C 11/005] comprising combined but independently operative RAM-ROM, RAM-PROM, RAM-EPROM cells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memories having two distinct arrays of memory elements, one with volatile memory elements and another one with non-volatile memory elements, the latter functioning as a backup memory for the volatile part of the memory.
[G11C 11/02] using magnetic elements -- The CPC subclass G11C 11/02 addresses digital storage devices that retain information by using magnetic elements, such as magnetic disks or tapes. It falls within the hierarchy of physics → information storage → static stores, describing static digital stores that rely on magnetic media for data retention.
[G11C 11/04] using storage elements having cylindrical form, e.g. rod, wire -- This class covers digital static storage devices that use cylindrical storage elements such as rods or wires, where information is held magnetically or electrically in the element's length or cross-section. It falls under Physics → Information Storage → Static Stores and addresses the specific geometry of the storage medium.
[G11C 11/06] using single-aperture storage elements, e.g. ring core -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Also the (now obsolete) magnetic-core memories.
[G11C 11/06007] using a single aperture or single magnetic closed circuit -- A subclass of digital static stores that use specific electric or magnetic storage elements, defining methods that employ a single aperture or a single magnetic closed circuit for data writing, reading or retention within the device.
[G11C 11/06014] using one such element per bit -- G11C 11/06014 refers to digital stores using one electric or magnetic storage element per bit. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system. It falls under static stores in the information storage hierarchy.
[G11C 11/06021] with destructive read-out -- G11C 11/06021 covers digital storage devices that use electric or magnetic elements and where the act of reading the data destroys or alters the stored information, known as destructive read-out. It applies to technologies such as certain core memories and read-only memories where retrieval consumes the stored charge.
[G11C 11/06028] Matrixes -- G11C 11/06028 - Matrixes refers to digital static storage structures that use arrays of particular electric or magnetic storage elements to organize data. These matrix configurations enable parallel access and high-density storage in memory devices.
[G11C 11/06035] Bit core selection for writing or reading, by at least two coincident partial currents, e.g. "bit"- organised, 2L/2D, or 3D -- A method for selecting the bit core during write or read operations that requires at least two simultaneous partial currents, applicable to bit-organized memory structures such as two-layer/ two-dimensional or three-dimensional static digital stores. This technique governs how electric or magnetic elements are addressed in static storage devices.
[G11C 11/06042] "word"-organised, e.g. 2D organisation or linear selection, i.e. full current selection through all the bit-cores of a word during reading -- This CPC subclass describes digital static stores where data are organised and accessed by whole words, using two-dimensional or linear layouts that select all bit-cores of a word at once during reading, and applies to electric or magnetic storage elements.
[G11C 11/0605] with non-destructive read-out -- The code G11C 11/0605 refers to digital static storage devices that employ specific electric or magnetic storage elements and enable read-out of the stored information without altering or destroying it. It falls under the physics hierarchy for information storage, covering non-destructive read-out methods for such electronic or magnetic memories.
[G11C 11/06057] Matrixes -- The G11C 11/06057 entry relates to matrix-type digital storage structures where data bits are organized in a regular two-dimensional array of electric or magnetic cells. These matrices enable random-access addressing by selecting specific rows and columns, and are employed in memory chips, cross-point memories and similar static storage devices.
[G11C 11/06064] "bit"-organised (2 1/2D, 3D or similar organisation) -- This CPC subclass pertains to digital memory structures where bits are arranged in non-planar geometries such as 2½-dimensional, three-dimensional or comparable layouts. It covers static electric or magnetic storage elements organized beyond traditional planar arrays to achieve higher density or novel access patterns.
[G11C 11/06071] "word"-organised (2D organisation or linear selection) -- The entry pertains to digital static stores that use electric or magnetic elements arranged in a two-dimensional pattern or accessed linearly as distinct word units, such as word-organized memory cells. It is classified under physics, information storage, static stores.
[G11C 11/06078] using two or more such elements per bit -- Covers digital static storage devices in which each bit is recorded using two or more distinct electric or magnetic storage elements, a technique that provides redundancy, error-correction or multi-level encoding within the memory cell.
[G11C 11/06085] Multi-aperture structures or multi-magnetic closed circuits, each aperture storing a "bit", realised by rods, plates, grids, waffle-irons,(i.e. grooved plates) or similar devices -- This CPC subclass deals with static digital storage devices where each aperture of a multi-aperture or multi-magnetic closed circuit stores a single bit, implemented with rods, plates, grids, waffle-iron-type grooved plates, or similar structures. It falls under physics → information storage → static stores.
[G11C 11/06092] Multi-aperture structures or multi-magnetic closed circuits using two or more apertures per bit -- This subclass covers digital static stores in which each bit is defined by two or more apertures or by multi-magnetic closed circuits, using multiple apertures per bit to create magnetic states. The technique aims to increase data density or improve reliability of magnetic or electric storage elements.
[G11C 11/061] using elements with single aperture or magnetic loop for storage, one element per bit, and for destructive read-out -- This subclass covers digital static memory devices that store each bit in a single aperture or magnetic-loop element, employing one element per bit and using a destructive read-out method that erases the stored information during retrieval.
[G11C 11/063] bit-organised, such as 2L/2D-organisation or three-dimensional [3D]-organisation, i.e. for selection of an element by means of at least two coincident partial currents both for reading and for writing -- This subclass covers digital static memory cells where data bits are arranged in two-layer/two-dimensional or three-dimensional patterns, and where selecting a cell for reading or writing is achieved by applying at least two intersecting partial currents. The classification applies to technologies that use specific electric or magnetic storage elements organized in such multi-dimensional bit structures.
[G11C 11/065] word-organised, such as two-dimensional [2D]-organisation, or linear selection, i.e. for selection of all the elements of a word by means of a single full current for reading -- The G11C 11/065 subclass covers digital storage devices that read an entire word of data, such as a two-dimensional array or a linear block, by applying a single full current to all elements of the word simultaneously, enabling rapid retrieval of stored bits.
[G11C 11/067] using elements with single aperture or magnetic loop for storage, one element per bit, and for non-destructive read-out -- G11C 11/067 covers static digital storage devices that use a separate electric or magnetic element-such as a single aperture or magnetic loop-for each stored bit, enabling non-destructive read-out of the information. This subclass falls under the physics domain of information storage, specifically static stores that employ individual storage elements.
[G11C 11/08] using multi-aperture storage elements, e.g. using transfluxors -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Using multi-axial storage elements -> G11C11/10
[G11C 11/10] using multi-axial storage elements -- The G11C 11/10 subclass concerns digital static stores that use multi-axial storage elements, meaning data is recorded on media that can be accessed along several axes to increase density or speed of retrieval. It lies within the hierarchy of physics → information storage → static stores.
[G11C 11/12] using tensors -- G11C 11/12 covers digital storage systems that employ tensor structures or operations for organizing, encoding or retrieving data. It includes devices or methods that use multi-dimensional arrays of bits or magnetic/electric elements to implement tensor-based storage formats, error-correction or compression within static stores.
[G11C 11/14] using thin-film elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memories using a single magnetic layer.  Memories using domain wall displacement in a shift register like manner, see also G11C19/0833 .
[G11C 11/15] using multiple magnetic layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memories using multiple magnetic layers not using any spin effect.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With cylindrical configuration -> G11C11/155
[G11C 11/155] with cylindrical configuration -- G11C 11/155 covers static digital storage devices that use electric or magnetic elements arranged in a cylindrical shape. It includes memory modules such as cylindrical magnetic tape reels or rod-type solid-state arrays where data is stored along the length of the cylinder.
[G11C 11/16] using elements in which the storage effect is based on magnetic spin effect -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memories using magnetic spin effect, i.e. where the memory elements have magnetic tunnel junctions.
[G11C 11/161] details concerning the memory cell structure, e.g. the layers of the ferromagnetic memory cell -- G11C 11/161 covers technical details of memory cell architecture, specifically the layering and composition of ferromagnetic memory cells used in static digital storage devices. It focuses on the physical construction of the cells rather than their operation or system integration.
[G11C 11/165] Auxiliary circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Auxiliary circuitry for MRAM elements.
[G11C 11/1653] Address circuits or decoders -- G11C 11/1653 classifies address circuits or decoders employed in digital static stores that use electric or magnetic storage elements, such as RAM or magnetic core memory. It covers the hardware that generates or interprets memory addresses for locating data within these static information-storage devices.
[G11C 11/1655] Bit-line or column circuits -- Bit-line or column circuits are circuit arrangements used in static digital memory arrays to address, read, write or amplify signals along the vertical or horizontal lines that connect individual memory cells, enabling selective access to stored bits.
[G11C 11/1657] Word-line or row circuits -- Word-line or row circuits are circuit arrangements used in static digital memory devices to select and address individual rows of storage cells, typically employing electric or magnetic elements for storage. They control the activation of word lines that connect memory cells to read/write circuitry, enabling precise data access in static stores.
[G11C 11/1659] Cell access -- **Definition:** This place covers:  MRAM specific details of selecting a memory element, e.g. select transistors, diodes or mere word line or bit line selection voltages.
[G11C 11/1673] Reading or sensing circuits or methods -- G11C 11/1673 covers reading and sensing circuits and methods used to retrieve data from static digital storage devices that employ electric or magnetic storage elements, such as solid-state memory or magnetic drums. The classification addresses the electronic architectures and techniques that detect stored bits without altering them, enabling reliable data access in non-volatile memory systems.
[G11C 11/1675] Writing or programming circuits or methods -- The subclass deals with the design, implementation and programming of circuits and methods used to write data to static digital storage devices such as EEPROM, flash or magnetic memory cells. It includes techniques for controlling write operations, programming algorithms and circuit architectures that enable data storage.
[G11C 11/1677] Verifying circuits or methods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of checking written data in MRAM elements.
[G11C 11/1693] Timing circuits or methods -- G11C 11/1693 covers timing circuits or methods used in static digital storage systems that rely on particular electric or magnetic storage elements, detailing how clock signals, synchronization and timing control are implemented to manage data read-write operations within such stores. It falls under physics → information storage → static stores.
[G11C 11/1695] Protection circuits or methods -- Protection circuits or methods are specialized designs that safeguard static digital storage devices, such as magnetic or electric memory elements, from damage caused by electrical disturbances, over-voltage, or electrostatic discharge. These circuits can include clamping, filtering, or shutdown mechanisms that preserve data integrity and device reliability during abnormal operating conditions.
[G11C 11/1697] Power supply circuits -- G11C 11/1697 covers the design and implementation of power-supply circuits for static digital storage devices that rely on electric or magnetic storage elements, such as memory registers, solid-state drives and magnetic core stores, focusing on how voltage and current are managed to maintain data integrity.
[G11C 11/18] using Hall-effect devices -- G11C 11/18 concerns digital storage technologies that read or write data by employing Hall-effect devices, which sense magnetic fields to detect stored bits. The classification applies to static magnetic memories where Hall sensors replace conventional read heads.
[G11C 11/19] using non-linear reactive devices in resonant circuits -- A resonant circuit that incorporates non-linear reactive components-such as varactors, saturable reactors, or nonlinear inductors-to achieve frequency-dependent behavior or tuning is classified under G11C 11/19. This code groups digital storage technologies that employ such nonlinear elements to control or modify signal resonance within static electric or magnetic storage structures.
[G11C 11/20] using parametrons -- The subclass G11C 11/20 covers static digital memory devices that employ parametrons-oscillating circuit elements used for storing or processing data-in their operation, typically involving electric or magnetic storage elements. It addresses designs, configurations and methods that apply parametron technology to achieve information storage.
[G11C 11/21] using electric elements -- G11C 11/21 covers digital static stores that retain data using electric storage elements such as capacitors, charge-based cells or voltage levels. It includes the structures, materials and operating principles of devices where information is represented by electric charge rather than magnetic or optical means.
[G11C 11/22] using ferroelectric elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memories using ferroelectric elements. This covers memories with capacitive elements where the insulating dielectric material between the capacitor plates is a ferroelectric material.
[G11C 11/221] using ferroelectric capacitors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of FRAM memory elements comprising a ferroelectric capacitor.
[G11C 11/223] using MOS with ferroelectric gate insulating film -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of FRAM memory elements comprising a transistor with ferroelectric material, e.g. a FEFET.
[G11C 11/225] Auxiliary circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Auxiliary circuitry for FRAM elements.
[G11C 11/2253] Address circuits or decoders -- The entry G11C 11/2253 covers address circuits and decoders used in static digital storage devices that employ electric or magnetic storage elements. It describes circuitry that generates or translates binary address signals into selection lines for accessing specific memory cells, enabling read and write operations in static stores.
[G11C 11/2255] Bit-line or column circuits -- G11C 11/2255 addresses bit-line or column circuits that control the selection, reading and writing of data along the rows and columns of static digital memory arrays such as DRAM or SRAM, linking the storage cells to external interfaces.
[G11C 11/2257] Word-line or row circuits -- Word-line or row circuits are the control electronics that select a specific row of storage cells in a static digital memory array. By applying voltage to designated word lines, they enable reading or writing of the bits stored in that row, providing address decoding and timing for the memory operation.
[G11C 11/2259] Cell access -- **Definition:** This place covers:  FRAM specific details of selecting a memory element, e.g. select transistors, diodes or mere word line or bit line selection voltages.
[G11C 11/2273] Reading or sensing circuits or methods -- Describes circuits or methods that read or sense data stored in digital static stores that use electric or magnetic storage elements, such as sense amplifiers, read heads, and detection circuits for retrieving information from memory cells.
[G11C 11/2275] Writing or programming circuits or methods -- The code covers technologies and processes for encoding data onto static digital storage media, including the circuitry and methods used to write or program magnetic or electronic patterns in memory cells. It addresses the design of circuits and techniques that create or modify stored information on such static stores.
[G11C 11/2277] Verifying circuits or methods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of checking written data in MRAM elements.
[G11C 11/2293] Timing circuits or methods -- G11C 11/2293 covers timing circuits and methods applied to digital static stores that use particular electric or magnetic storage elements, enabling synchronization, clock generation or timing control within such memory devices. It resides under Physics → Information Storage → Static Stores.
[G11C 11/2295] Protection circuits or methods -- This CPC subclass concerns circuits and methods that protect digital static storage devices-memory elements that store data using electric or magnetic storage elements-from over-voltage, over-current, or other damaging conditions. It includes protective architectures, control logic and techniques designed to prevent data loss or device failure.
[G11C 11/2297] Power supply circuits -- Power supply circuits provide the necessary electrical energy to static digital storage devices that rely on electric or magnetic elements, ensuring stable operation and data retention. In the CPC hierarchy they fall under the physics of information storage, specifically static stores, and are classified under G11C 11/2297.
[G11C 11/23] using electrostatic storage on a common layer, e.g. Forrester-Haeff tubes -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Using ferroelectric elements -> G11C11/22
[G11C 11/24] using capacitors -- G11C 11/24 covers digital storage devices that use capacitors as the primary storage element. It includes static random-access memory and other charge-based memory technologies that retain data without power. The classification is nested under physics - information storage - static stores.
[G11C 11/26] using discharge tubes -- This subclass covers digital static stores that use discharge tubes-such as cathode-ray or plasma tubes-to retain information. The stored data is represented by charge patterns or ionisation states within the tube, providing a form of electric or magnetic storage element for binary data.
[G11C 11/265] counting tubes, e.g. decatrons or trochotrons -- This subclass addresses counting tubes such as decatrons and trochotrons, vacuum-filled devices that store numeric values by accumulating charge or pulses in discrete steps, providing static electronic memory for counting applications.
[G11C 11/28] using gas-filled tubes -- This subclass covers digital storage devices that use gas-filled tubes as the active element for storing data. It falls under static stores in information storage, encompassing technologies that rely on particular electric or magnetic storage elements, such as CRT-based memory systems.
[G11C 11/30] using vacuum tubes -- G11C 11/30 pertains to digital static storage devices that employ vacuum-tube as the active storage element. It falls under the physics branch, information storage, static stores, and covers technologies where electric or magnetic storage is implemented with vacuum-tube circuitry.
[G11C 11/34] using semiconductor devices -- G11C 11/34 covers digital storage systems that employ semiconductor devices such as transistors, diodes or integrated circuits to store data. It includes technologies like flash memory, DRAM, SRAM and other solid-state approaches where information is retained by electronic charge or magnetic states within semiconductor structures.
[G11C 11/35] with charge storage in a depletion layer, e.g. charge coupled devices -- G11C 11/35 covers static digital storage devices that retain information by storing charge in a depletion layer, exemplified by charge-coupled devices where charge packets are shifted across semiconductor wells. These devices rely on electric fields rather than magnetic elements to maintain data.
[G11C 11/36] using diodes, e.g. as threshold elements -- G11C 11/36 covers digital static storage devices that employ diodes, for example as threshold elements, to control or detect data states. It is a subclass of static stores within information-storage physics, focusing on devices that use specific electric or magnetic storage components.
[G11C 11/38] using tunnel diodes -- G11C 11/38 concerns the application of tunnel diodes in static digital storage devices that rely on electric or magnetic elements to retain data. It addresses circuit designs and techniques that employ the negative resistance of tunnel diodes to improve read/write operations or speed in such memories.
[G11C 11/39] using thyristors -- The G11C 11/39 subclass deals with digital static storage devices that use thyristors as the active electric or magnetic elements for recording, retaining, or retrieving data, such as memory cells employing semiconductor switching devices. It falls under the broader categories of physics, information storage, and static stores, and specifies implementations where thyristor technology is integral to the storage operation.
[G11C 11/40] using transistors -- The CPC subclass G11C 11/40 covers digital static storage devices that employ transistors as the active elements for storing data, typically using electric or magnetic storage mechanisms. It falls under the hierarchy of Physics → Information storage → Static stores, describing how transistor-based circuits enable the retention of bits without the need for moving parts.
[G11C 11/401] forming cells needing refreshing or charge regeneration, i.e. dynamic cells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Basically this group covers DRAMs - and some further types of cells needing to be periodically and frequently refreshed such gain cells.  This group and its sub groups concerns mainly DRAM cells of any type from classic one transistor-one capacitor cell types to more exotic types such single transistor cells or gain cells. All cells have in common that they require to be updated or rewritten or refreshed frequently in order to retain their data.
[G11C 11/402] with charge regeneration individual to each memory cell, i.e. internal refresh -- G11C 11/402 covers static digital memory stores in which each memory cell incorporates its own charge-regeneration circuit, providing internal refresh of the stored data without external refreshing circuitry. This subclass relates to static storage devices that use individual cell-level refresh mechanisms to maintain stored information.
[G11C 11/4023] using field effect transistors -- This subgroup covers static digital memory devices that store data by means of field-effect transistors as the storage element. It falls under physics → information storage → static stores and describes electronic memory that uses transistor-based cells for electric data retention.
[G11C 11/4026] using bipolar transistors -- G11C 11/4026 covers digital static storage devices-such as SRAM or similar memory cells-where the storage elements are implemented with bipolar transistors, detailing how these transistors are used to retain binary information without periodic refresh.
[G11C 11/403] with charge regeneration common to a multiplicity of memory cells, i.e. external refresh -- G11C 11/403 covers digital static storage devices that employ an external refresh scheme, using a common charge-regeneration circuit to periodically restore the stored charge of a plurality of memory cells and preserve data integrity.
[G11C 11/404] with one charge-transfer gate, e.g. MOS transistor, per cell -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mostly 1T1C cells but single transistor cells are also found here (see also G11C2211/4016 for SOI and isolated well single transistor cells).
[G11C 11/4045] using a plurality of serially connected access transistors, each having a storage capacitor -- G11C 11/4045 relates to static digital memory structures that use a series of access transistors, each transistor being coupled with its own storage capacitor, allowing sequential cell access while preserving the stored charge in each capacitor.
[G11C 11/405] with three charge-transfer gates, e.g. MOS transistors, per cell -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cells with three charge-transfer gates, but it also contains gain cells and other cells with access transistors combined with another charge storage transistor. Finally, it covers also partially capacitor based cells with two access transistors and those with more than 3 access transistors (multiport DRAM cells see also G11C8/16 ).
[G11C 11/406] Management or control of the refreshing or charge-regeneration cycles -- This classification covers techniques for governing the periodic refresh or charge-regeneration processes required by static digital memory devices, such as DRAM and similar electric or magnetic storage elements, to maintain data integrity during operation.
[G11C 11/40603] Arbitration, priority and concurrent access to memory cells for read/write or refresh operations -- The CPC entry G11C 11/40603 covers techniques for arbitrating priority and managing concurrent access to memory cells during read, write or refresh operations in static digital storage devices that use electric or magnetic elements. It addresses how multiple accesses are coordinated to prevent conflicts and ensure data integrity. This subclass falls under physics → information storage → static stores.
[G11C 11/40607] Refresh operations in memory devices with an internal cache or data buffer -- The G11C 11/40607 class covers techniques for carrying out refresh operations in memory devices that include an internal cache or data buffer, keeping stored information valid over time. It relates to static digital storage technologies that use electric or magnetic storage elements.
[G11C 11/40611] External triggering or timing of internal or partially internal refresh operations, e.g. auto-refresh or CAS-before-RAS triggered refresh -- The code concerns methods that externally initiate or schedule refresh cycles within digital static storage devices, such as auto-refresh commands or CAS-before-RAS sequences that trigger internal or partially internal refresh of electric or magnetic memory cells. It focuses on timing control of refresh operations.
[G11C 11/40615] Internal triggering or timing of refresh, e.g. hidden refresh, self refresh, pseudo-SRAMs -- The code G11C 11/40615 covers methods for internally initiating or controlling the refresh cycle of static memory cells, such as hidden refresh, self-refresh, and pseudo-SRAM techniques that embed timing within the device. This classification falls under physics → information storage → static stores and relates to the use of specific electric or magnetic elements for digital storage.
[G11C 11/40618] Refresh operations over multiple banks or interleaving -- Refresh operations that are performed across several memory banks or using interleaved architectures to maintain data integrity in static digital stores. The entry covers methods for coordinating refresh cycles among multiple banks to improve reliability, speed and power efficiency.
[G11C 11/40622] Partial refresh of memory arrays -- The entry concerns techniques for partially refreshing memory arrays in digital storage devices, where only selected portions of the array are refreshed to preserve data integrity while reducing power consumption and latency. It applies to static stores that use electric or magnetic elements, enabling more efficient maintenance of stored information.
[G11C 11/40626] Temperature related aspects of refresh operations -- The G11C 11/40626 category addresses how temperature influences the refresh operations of static digital storage devices that rely on electric or magnetic elements, such as DRAM. It covers thermal effects on refresh timing, energy use and reliability.
[G11C 11/4063] Auxiliary circuits, e.g. for addressing, decoding, driving, writing, sensing or timing -- G11C 11/4063 pertains to auxiliary circuits used with static digital storage devices, such as those that provide addressing, decoding, driving, writing, sensing or timing functions to support electric or magnetic memory elements.
[G11C 11/4067] for memory cells of the bipolar type -- G11C 11/4067 classifies patents that describe bipolar-type memory cells employed in static digital storage devices, where data is retained by the charge states of bipolar transistors. It falls under the physics domain, specifically information storage and static stores.
[G11C 11/407] for memory cells of the field-effect type -- The subclass G11C 11/407 concerns static digital storage devices that use field-effect transistors as memory cells. It covers designs in which the storage element is a field-effect type, such as MOS or similar transistors, employed to retain data without power.
[G11C 11/4072] Circuits for initialization, powering up or down, clearing memory or presetting -- G11C 11/4072 refers to circuits that initialize, power up or down, clear, or preset digital static stores that rely on electric or magnetic storage elements, ensuring the memory is correctly set or reset before or after use.
[G11C 11/4074] Power supply or voltage generation circuits, e.g. bias voltage generators, substrate voltage generators, back-up power, power control circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All the power supply or voltage generation circuits, e.g. bias voltage generators, substrate voltage generators, back-up power, power control circuits specific to DRAM - see also G11C5/14 - G11C5/147 for memory power circuits in general; see also G11C11/4076 and G11C7/22 for some power management aspects (changing memory state or mode and DPD e.g. deep power down wake-ups ) and G11C11/406 for the refresh aspects in low power mode (self-refresh).  The group contains also most of the documents concerning cells with a controlled back plate and back plate voltage circuits.
[G11C 11/4076] Timing circuits -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Regeneration management -> G11C11/406
[G11C 11/4078] Safety or protection circuits, e.g. for preventing inadvertent or unauthorised reading or writing -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Protection of memory contents during checking or testing -> G11C29/52
[G11C 11/408] Address circuits -- Address circuits are electronic components in static digital storage devices that generate and decode location signals to select specific memory cells for reading or writing. They typically convert binary address inputs into the voltages or magnetic fields needed to access the targeted storage element, enabling organized data retrieval and storage.
[G11C 11/4082] Address Buffers; level conversion circuits -- The code G11C 11/4082 pertains to circuits that temporarily hold or convert address signals for static digital memory devices, such as address buffers and level-shifting interfaces that match voltage levels between a processor and memory cells.
[G11C 11/4085] Word line control circuits, e.g. word line drivers, - boosters, - pull-up, - pull-down, - precharge -- The G11C 11/4085 classification covers circuits that manage the activation of word lines in static and dynamic memory arrays. It includes word-line drivers, boosters, pull-up and pull-down devices, and pre-charge circuits used to select rows and prepare storage cells for reading or writing.
[G11C 11/4087] Address decoders, e.g. bit - or word line decoders; Multiple line decoders -- G11C 11/4087 covers circuits that translate address inputs into signals that select individual or groups of storage lines in static digital memory arrays, such as bit- or word-line decoders and multiple-line decoder arrangements used with electric or magnetic storage elements.
[G11C 11/409] Read-write [R-W] circuits -- Read-write (R-W) circuits are electronic or magnetic components that can both retrieve and store data in static digital storage devices. They provide the logic and timing necessary to detect stored bits and to modify them, enabling reversible information handling in devices such as DRAM or magnetic core memory.
[G11C 11/4091] Sense or sense/refresh amplifiers, or associated sense circuitry, e.g. for coupled bit-line precharging, equalising or isolating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Precharging, equalising or isolating circuits for DRAMs are in G11C11/4094 and in general also in G11C7/12 .
[G11C 11/4093] Input/output [I/O] data interface arrangements, e.g. data buffers -- The G11C 11/4093 entry covers arrangements for input-output data interfaces in digital static storage devices, such as data-buffer structures that manage the transfer of information between storage elements and external circuitry.
[G11C 11/4094] Bit-line management or control circuits -- The entry covers circuits that manage bit-lines in digital memory arrays, providing functions such as pre-charging, equalizing, sensing and timing control to enable reliable read and write operations. These circuits belong to static stores that use electric or magnetic storage elements.
[G11C 11/4096] Input/output [I/O] data management or control circuits, e.g. reading or writing circuits, I/O drivers or bit-line switches -- The entry G11C 11/4096 covers circuits that control the flow of data to and from digital static storage devices, such as reading and writing circuitry, input/output drivers, and bit-line switching elements that manage how bits are accessed and transferred.
[G11C 11/4097] Bit-line organisation, e.g. bit-line layout, folded bit lines -- A bit-line organisation refers to the way individual data lines are arranged within static digital memory cells, covering layouts such as straight, folded or multiplexed configurations to optimise read/write speed, signal integrity and chip area. This entry classifies innovations that modify the physical geometry or routing of those lines in static electric or magnetic stores.
[G11C 11/4099] Dummy cell treatment -- **Glossary:** - 1T-1C: One transistor one capacitor cells - Self-refresh: A power saving memory operation mode in which the refreshes its cells autonomously - Auto-refresh: An external signal (from a memory controller usually) triggers the refresh, but the rest of the refresh operation is done by circuitry internal to the memory (refresh address counters etc ...) - Twin cell: Arrangement in which a single datum is stored in two (or more) cells - Gain cell: Cells (usually made of three transistors) in which the charge is stored in a gate electrode of a gain transistor (thereby controlling the conductivity of that transistor), - SOI: Silicon on Insulator - often used to make single transistor DRAM cells (or ZRAM)
[G11C 11/41] forming cells with positive feedback, i.e. cells not needing refreshing or charge regeneration, e.g. bistable multivibrator or Schmitt trigger -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memories having memory cells with positive feedback or a latch, i.e. a Static RAM or SRAM. This group only covers the aspects of the memory device itself. Manufacturing is covered by H10B99/00 and array structures by H10B10/00 .
[G11C 11/411] using bipolar transistors only -- This CPC subclass defines digital static stores that employ only bipolar transistors in their circuitry. It covers memory devices where the storage elements are accessed or controlled solely by bipolar transistor technology.
[G11C 11/4113] with at least one cell access to base or collector of at least one of said transistors, e.g. via access diodes, access transistors -- G11C 11/4113 classifies static digital storage cells in which at least one cell is accessed through the base or collector of a transistor, typically by means of an access diode or an access transistor, allowing selective read-write operations within the memory array.
[G11C 11/4116] with at least one cell access via separately connected emittors of said transistors or via multiple emittors, e.g. T2L, ECL -- A static digital memory cell accessed through individually connected transistor emitters, allowing each cell to be reached via separate emitter lines or multiple emitters such as in T2L or ECL configurations. This design is used in static stores where the storage element is electric or magnetic and the cell access architecture relies on distinct emitter connections.
[G11C 11/412] using field-effect transistors only -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group contains SRAM memory cells per se.
[G11C 11/4125] Cells incorporating circuit means for protecting against loss of information -- The CPC subclass G11C 11/4125 defines storage cells that embed circuit elements specifically for safeguarding stored data, such as error-detection, correction, write-protection or power-loss recovery functions. It applies to static digital memory cells that employ electric or magnetic storage elements and include built-in protection against information loss.
[G11C 11/413] Auxiliary circuits, e.g. for addressing, decoding, driving, writing, sensing, timing or power reduction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuitry providing for power, address decoding, signal control etc. required for the functioning of the SRAM. This group and its dependents cover SRAMs having bipolar as well as FET transistor memory cells.
[G11C 11/414] for memory cells of the bipolar type -- G11C 11/414 covers digital static storage devices that employ bipolar memory cells, where data is stored using semiconductor structures that switch between two stable charge states. These cells are a type of electric storage element used in non-volatile memory technologies.
[G11C 11/415] Address circuits -- Address circuits are electronic components that generate and control the address signals used to select specific locations in digital static storage devices, enabling the read or write of data. They typically include decoders, multiplexers and timing logic that translate input address codes into the appropriate control signals for the storage elements.
[G11C 11/416] Read-write [R-W] circuits -- G11C 11/416 covers the design of read-write circuits used with static digital storage devices that employ electric or magnetic storage elements such as SRAM, DRAM or magnetic core arrays. It includes circuit architectures that enable both retrieval and modification of stored bits, addressing timing, voltage levels and control logic for reliable operation.
[G11C 11/417] for memory cells of the field-effect type -- G11C 11/417 categorises patents concerning memory cells that use field-effect devices, such as MOSFETs, as the storage element in static digital memories. These cells store bits by modulating the conductivity of a transistor channel, allowing non-volatile or volatile storage in integrated circuits.
[G11C 11/418] Address circuits -- Address circuits are electronic components that generate, decode and route address signals to select specific locations within a digital storage element such as a static or dynamic memory cell. They translate binary address inputs into physical lines that activate the desired storage node, enabling read or write operations in electric or magnetic memory arrays.
[G11C 11/419] Read-write [R-W] circuits -- G11C 11/419 covers read-write circuits used in static digital storage devices that employ specific electric or magnetic storage elements. These circuits enable both retrieval and modification of stored data within such non-volatile memory systems.
[G11C 11/42] using opto-electronic devices, i.e. light-emitting and photoelectric devices electrically- or optically- coupled -- G11C 11/42 covers digital static storage devices that use opto-electronic components such as light-emitting diodes, lasers or photodiodes, which are coupled to the memory circuit either electrically or optically. The classification applies to arrangements where light is employed for reading, writing or signalling in the storage system.
[G11C 11/44] using super-conductive elements, e.g. cryotron -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memories using superconductive elements like squids
[G11C 11/46] using thermoplastic elements -- The G11C 11/46 subclass covers digital static storage devices that use thermoplastic materials as the storage medium. It includes technologies where data is recorded by heating and reshaping the polymer or similar material to create stable, electrically readable states, such as phase-change or rewritable polymer memory. This falls under physics → information storage → static stores, describing digital stores characterized by specific electric or magnetic storage elements.
[G11C 11/48] using displaceable coupling elements, e.g. ferromagnetic cores, to produce change between different states of mutual or self-inductance -- The CPC subclass G11C 11/48 falls under physics → information storage → static stores and covers digital memory devices that employ movable coupling elements such as ferromagnetic cores to alter mutual or self-inductance between states, thereby representing data through changes in inductive characteristics.
[G11C 11/50] using actuation of electric contacts to store the information -- G11C 11/50 refers to techniques that store digital data by moving electric contacts to make or break connections, using the physical position of the contacts to represent bits. It is a sub-category of static digital stores within the broader fields of information storage and physics.
[G11C 11/52] using electromagnetic relays -- This subclass covers digital storage devices that use electromagnetic relays as the storage element, where the relays retain information by means of their magnetic or electric states. It pertains to static, non-volatile memory that relies on relay-based electric or magnetic storage mechanisms.
[G11C 11/54] using elements simulating biological cells, e.g. neuron -- **Definition:** This place covers:  As used in neural networks.
[G11C 11/56] using storage elements with more than two stable states represented by steps, e.g. of voltage, current, phase, frequency -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Additional aspects relating to the cell type rather than multi-state storage per se should be classified under the relevant cell technology. For counting arrangements comprising multi-stable elements of this type see H03K25/00 , H03K29/00 .
[G11C 11/5607] using magnetic storage elements -- This CPC subclass covers technologies that employ magnetic storage elements in digital static stores. It includes methods and devices where data is recorded, retained, or retrieved using magnetic media such as hard-disk drives, magnetic tape, or ferromagnetic memory cells. The classification falls under physics, information storage, and static stores.
[G11C 11/5614] using conductive bridging RAM [CBRAM] or programming metallization cells [PMC] -- This subclass covers digital static storage devices that employ conductive bridging RAM or programming metallization cells. These memory technologies store data by forming and dissolving conductive filaments within a solid electrolyte, providing non-volatile operation. The classification is used for inventions related to the materials, structures, and methods for programming and reading such cells.
[G11C 11/5621] using charge storage in a floating gate -- This subclass covers memory devices that store information by trapping electric charge in a floating-gate structure, a technique used in non-volatile memories such as flash and EEPROM. The charge remains on the isolated gate without power, allowing data retention in static electronic stores.
[G11C 11/5628] Programming or writing circuits; Data input circuits -- The subclass deals with circuits that program or write data to static digital storage devices, including mechanisms for loading information into magnetic or electronic memory cells. It also covers data-input circuitry that converts external signals into a form suitable for storage.
[G11C 11/5635] Erasing circuits -- Erasing circuits are circuit arrangements that delete or reset data stored in static digital memory devices that use electric or magnetic storage elements, allowing selective or complete clearing of stored information. They encompass methods for removing bits from registers, flip-flops, magnetic cores or similar elements in order to prepare the memory for new data.
[G11C 11/5642] Sensing or reading circuits; Data output circuits -- The G11C 11/5642 subclass deals with electronic circuits that detect or read information stored in static digital memory elements and generate the corresponding data signals. It includes sense amplifiers, read channels, data line drivers and other output circuitry that convert stored magnetic or electric states into usable electronic outputs.
[G11C 11/565] using capacitive charge storage elements -- A static digital store that records data by storing electric charge on a capacitor is covered by this subclass. The method relies on capacitive charge storage elements to retain bits without needing magnetic media, enabling non-volatile memory functions in devices such as DRAM or capacitive touch sensors.
[G11C 11/5657] using ferroelectric storage elements -- G11C 11/5657 refers to the use of ferroelectric storage elements in digital static memory devices. It falls within the physics → information storage → static stores hierarchy and addresses systems that store data by exploiting the polarization states of ferroelectric materials.
[G11C 11/5664] using organic memory material storage elements -- G11C 11/5664 covers digital static storage devices that employ organic-memory materials as the storage element. It pertains to systems where data are retained by organic compounds acting as electric or magnetic storage media, distinguishing them from inorganic or semiconductor-based memories.
[G11C 11/5671] using charge trapping in an insulator -- G11C 11/5671 covers the implementation of digital storage devices that store information by trapping electrical charge within an insulating material. The method is used in static-memory technologies such as flash memory, where the trapped charge represents binary data.
[G11C 11/5678] using amorphous/crystalline phase transition storage elements -- G11C 11/5678 covers digital static storage devices that encode data by inducing an amorphous-to-crystalline phase change in a material, typically using electrical pulses to switch between high- and low-resistivity states. The classification includes phase-change memory cells and similar devices that rely on the reversible structural transformation of the storage element.
[G11C 11/5685] using storage elements comprising metal oxide memory material, e.g. perovskites -- This subclass covers digital memory devices that store data in static cells built from metal-oxide materials, such as perovskite-based resistive or capacitive elements. The storage mechanism relies on the electrical properties of the metal-oxide layer, providing a non-volatile means of retaining information.
[G11C 11/5692] read-only digital stores using storage elements with more than two stable states -- G11C 11/5692 covers read-only digital storage media that store data by using storage elements capable of more than two stable states, such as multi-level cells in flash memory or phase-change materials. These technologies allow each cell to represent multiple bits, increasing density while the data cannot be rewritten after fabrication.
[G11C 13/00] Digital stores characterised by the use of storage elements not covered by groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Digital stores or memories:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Digital stores in which the storage effect is based exclusively on magnetism e.g. Magnetic RAM (MRAM) -> G11C11/00 , G11C23/00 , G11C25/00
[G11C 13/0002] using resistive RAM [RRAM] elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  RRAM storage elements; for MRAM storage elements see G11C11/15 and G11C11/16 .
[G11C 13/0004] comprising amorphous/crystalline phase transition cells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  RRAM elements in which the electrical resistance change is based on an amorphous to crystalline or crystalline to amorphous transition in a phase change material.
[G11C 13/0007] comprising metal oxide memory material, e.g. perovskites -- **Definition:** This place covers:  RRAM elements in which the electrical resistance change is based on a switching mechanism in metal oxides e.g. TiO, NiO, HfO2, CuO.
[G11C 13/0009] RRAM elements whose operation depends upon chemical change -- **Definition:** This place covers:  RRAM elements in which the electrical resistance change is based on the formation and breaking of chemical bonds.
[G11C 13/0011] comprising conductive bridging RAM [CBRAM] or programming metallization cells [PMCs] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  RRAM elements in which the electrical resistance change is based on ion movement in a solid electrolyte between metal electrodes.
[G11C 13/0014] comprising cells based on organic memory material -- This subclass covers digital static storage devices that use cells built from organic memory material, distinguishing them from other static stores that rely on conventional semiconductor elements. It falls under physics > information storage > static stores.
[G11C 13/0016] comprising polymers -- G11C 13/0016 denotes digital static storage devices that employ polymer-based storage elements not covered by other CPC groups. It lies within Physics → Information Storage → Static Stores → Digital stores characterised by the use of storage elements not covered by groups, specifying that the storage medium includes polymers.
[G11C 13/0019] comprising bio-molecules -- G11C 13/0019 designates static digital storage devices that employ storage elements outside the standard groups, specifically those that incorporate bio-molecules as the information-bearing medium. This subclass falls under PHYSICS → INFORMATION STORAGE → STATIC STORES, covering non-conventional, molecule-based data retention technologies.
[G11C 13/0021] Auxiliary circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Auxiliary circuitry for RRAM elements.
[G11C 13/0023] Address circuits or decoders -- This CPC subgroup defines circuitry that generates or decodes address signals to select specific storage locations in static digital memory devices whose storage elements are not covered by other groups. It includes address generators, row-column decoders and related logic used in ROM, PROM, EPROM and similar static stores.
[G11C 13/0026] Bit-line or column circuits -- G11C 13/0026 relates to the circuitry that links memory cells to sense amplifiers or control logic in static or dynamic memory arrays, including bit-line drivers, sense amplifiers and column-selection networks. These bit-line or column circuits handle the read and write operations for data stored along the columns of semiconductor memory devices.
[G11C 13/0028] Word-line or row circuits -- G11C 13/0028 covers circuit arrangements that select or address rows of storage cells, commonly called word-line or row circuits, in static digital memory arrays where the storage elements are not defined by other CPC groups. These circuits provide the electrical paths for writing to or reading from each row of the store.
[G11C 13/003] Cell access -- G11C 13/003 covers techniques for accessing individual cells in static digital storage devices, including addressing schemes, selection circuits, and control methods that enable reading or writing to specific memory locations in non-volatile or volatile cell arrays. It is situated under information storage, static stores in the physics section.
[G11C 13/0033] Disturbance prevention or evaluation; Refreshing of disturbed memory data -- The CPC subclass G11C 13/0033 relates to methods and devices that prevent, detect, or correct disturbances in static digital storage elements such as charge leakage or dielectric breakdown, and to techniques for refreshing or restoring corrupted memory data. It includes error-checking, self-recovery, and refresh cycles applied to non-volatile memories that are not covered by other groups.
[G11C 13/0035] Evaluating degradation, retention or wearout, e.g. by counting writing cycles -- The classification G11C 13/0035 pertains to techniques for assessing the degradation, data-retention capability or wear-out of static digital storage devices, typically by monitoring the number of write or read cycles they have experienced. It is used for evaluating the reliability and lifespan of such storage elements within the broader field of information storage physics.
[G11C 13/0038] Power supply circuits -- G11C 13/0038 covers power supply circuits used in digital static storage devices whose storage elements are not defined in other groups. It includes circuits that generate, regulate, or convert electrical power to operate such memory units. The code is classified under physics - information storage - static stores.
[G11C 13/004] Reading or sensing circuits or methods -- The G11C 13/004 class covers circuits and methods used to read or sense data stored in static digital memory devices whose storage elements are not covered elsewhere. It includes techniques for detecting stored electrical, magnetic, or optical states and converting them into usable signals, typically applied to non-volatile memory such as EEPROM, flash, or other solid-state storage.
[G11C 13/0059] Security or protection circuits or methods -- G11C 13/0059 refers to security or protection circuits or methods used in digital static storage devices, covering techniques that prevent unauthorized access, tampering, or data loss in memory elements not classified elsewhere. It falls under the CPC hierarchy Physics > Information storage > Static stores.
[G11C 13/0061] Timing circuits or methods -- The G11C 13/0061 subgroup covers timing circuits or methods used with static digital storage devices, such as clock generators, delay lines, and synchronisation circuits that control the timing of read, write or refresh operations in non-volatile or volatile memory elements. It focuses on the generation and management of timing signals rather than the storage media themselves.
[G11C 13/0064] Verifying circuits or methods -- G11C 13/0064 covers circuits and methods used to verify the correct operation of static digital storage devices, including testing of memory cells, address decoders and read-write logic. It encompasses diagnostic and self-checking techniques that ensure data integrity and proper functionality of non-volatile and volatile stores.
[G11C 13/0069] Writing or programming circuits or methods -- The subclass G11C 13/0069 pertains to the design, programming and operation of circuits and methods that write data into static digital storage elements not covered by other groups, such as non-volatile memory cells, EEPROM or flash structures, focusing on the mechanisms that encode information onto these static stores.
[G11C 13/0097] Erasing, e.g. resetting, circuits or methods -- G11C 13/0097 relates to techniques for erasing or resetting the state of digital static storage elements that are not defined in other groups, covering circuit designs and methods that clear stored data to a known condition. It applies to non-volatile memory technologies lacking conventional classifications.
