[G11C 13/02] using elements whose operation depends upon chemical change -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Storage elements in which the electrical resistance change is based on the formation and breaking of chemical bonds (for RRAM G11C13/0009 takes precedence).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - RRAM elements whose operation depends upon chemical change -> G11C13/0009
[G11C 13/025] using fullerenes, e.g. C60, or nanotubes, e.g. carbon or silicon nanotubes -- This subclass defines digital static storage devices that record data by employing fullerene molecules such as C60 or tubular nanostructures like carbon or silicon nanotubes as the storage elements. It falls under physics, information storage, and static stores not covered by other groups.
[G11C 13/04] using optical elements -- G11C 13/04 refers to digital static storage systems that employ optical elements for recording, reading or processing data, such as optical memories, holographic or photonic storage devices. It covers implementations where the storage medium is not otherwise classified in existing CPC groups, focusing on the role of lenses, mirrors, waveguides or other optical components in the storage architecture.
[G11C 13/041] using photochromic storage elements  G11C13/042  takes precedence -- G11C 13/041 groups digital static storage devices that use photochromic materials as the storage element, whereby data is recorded by changing the material's optical properties with light exposure. It falls under static digital stores not covered by other groups and is superseded by the more specific G11C 13/042.
[G11C 13/042] using information stored in the form of interference pattern -- G11C 13/042 covers methods and devices that retrieve or process data that has been recorded as an optical interference pattern, such as holographic or interferometric storage. The classification applies to digital static storage techniques where the stored information is represented by the spatial distribution of light-wave interference rather than conventional magnetic, electronic or polymeric media.
[G11C 13/043] using magnetic-optical storage elements -- The subclass G11C 13/043 covers technologies that use magnetic-optical media for data storage, where magnetic and optical effects are combined to record and retrieve information. It falls under static digital stores not covered by other groups and includes devices such as magnetic-optical disks and drives.
[G11C 13/044] using electro-optical elements -- The CPC subclass G11C 13/044 covers digital data-storage techniques that employ electro-optical components, such as electro-optic crystals, modulators or photodetectors, where the stored information is represented by electrically induced changes in optical properties. It applies to static digital stores that do not fall under other specific storage-element groups.
[G11C 13/045] using photochromic storage elements -- This subclass covers data recording and retrieval methods that employ photochromic storage elements, where information is written, retained, and read by inducing reversible colour changes in the material. It includes devices that use light-induced molecular transformations to represent binary states in static, non-magnetic digital stores.
[G11C 13/046] using other storage elements storing information in the form of an interference pattern -- The code refers to digital storage media that employ non-traditional storage elements in which data are recorded as interference patterns, such as holographic or photorefractive systems. It covers methods and devices that create, read and process these patterns to store and retrieve information, distinguishing them from conventional magnetic or optical storage formats.
[G11C 13/047] using electro-optical elements  G11C13/042  takes precedence -- G11C 13/047 pertains to digital storage methods that utilize electro-optical elements, specifically when these are not covered by the more specific classification G11C 13/042. This code is part of a broader hierarchy dealing with static information storage within physics.
[G11C 13/048] using other optical storage elements -- G11C 13/048 covers digital static storage devices that use optical media other than conventional laser-read disc formats, such as holographic, polymer or multi-layer optical elements. It addresses the design, recording and retrieval methods for these alternative optical storage components within the broader field of information storage physics.
[G11C 13/06] using magneto-optical elements -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Using information stored in the form of an interference pattern -> G11C13/042
[G11C 14/00] Digital stores characterised by arrangements of cells having volatile and non-volatile storage properties for back-up when the power is down -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memories with arrangements to save information from a volatile to a nonvolatile memory when power supply is lost and to restore the information from the nonvolatile to the volatile memory when power is restored.  Sub-groups cover details of cells adapted for this purpose, classified first by volatile and then by nonvolatile storage type.
[G11C 14/0009] in which the volatile element is a DRAM cell -- A digital static storage device in which the volatile portion consists of DRAM cells, providing temporary data retention that can be backed up by non-volatile cells when power is lost. This subcategory falls under static stores of information storage, covering arrangements of cells with both volatile and non-volatile properties.
[G11C 14/0018] whereby the nonvolatile element is an EEPROM element, e.g. a floating gate or metal-nitride-oxide-silicon [MNOS] transistor -- A digital static memory where non-volatile storage is provided by an EEPROM element, for example a floating-gate or metal-nitride-oxide-silicon transistor, enabling data retention during power loss.
[G11C 14/0027] and the nonvolatile element is a ferroelectric element -- The subclass G11C 14/0027 covers digital memory arrays that combine volatile cells with a ferroelectric element as the non-volatile component to retain data when power is removed. It belongs to the CPC hierarchy PHYSICS - INFORMATION STORAGE - STATIC STORES - digital stores featuring both volatile and non-volatile backup.
[G11C 14/0036] and the nonvolatile element is a magnetic RAM [MRAM] element or ferromagnetic cell -- This CPC subclass G11C 14/0036 concerns static digital storage devices that use arrangements of cells with both volatile and non-volatile properties, where the non-volatile element is implemented as magnetic RAM (MRAM) or a ferromagnetic cell, enabling data retention when power is removed.
[G11C 14/0045] and the nonvolatile element is a resistive RAM element, i.e. programmable resistors, e.g. formed of phase change or chalcogenide material -- G11C 14/0045 pertains to digital storage cells that combine volatile and non-volatile properties for data retention during power loss, specifically focusing on configurations where the nonvolatile component is a resistive RAM element, such as those utilizing phase change or chalcogenide materials.
[G11C 14/0054] in which the volatile element is a SRAM cell -- This subclass covers digital static storage devices whose volatile memory element is a SRAM cell, often paired with non-volatile cells to retain data when power is removed. It is classified under CPC G11C 14/0054 within physics → information storage → static stores.
[G11C 14/0063] and the nonvolatile element is an EEPROM element, e.g. a floating gate or MNOS transistor -- This subclass covers digital static memory arrays that combine volatile and non-volatile cells, where the non-volatile element is an EEPROM device such as a floating-gate or MNOS transistor, enabling data backup after power loss.
[G11C 14/0072] and the nonvolatile element is a ferroelectric element -- This subclass covers digital memory devices that use a ferroelectric element as the non-volatile storage component, providing data retention when power is removed. It falls under static stores in information storage physics and addresses backup functionality through volatile and non-volatile cell arrangements.
[G11C 14/0081] and the nonvolatile element is a magnetic RAM [MRAM] element or ferromagnetic cell -- G11C 14/0081 covers static digital storage devices that combine volatile memory with a non-volatile element implemented as magnetic RAM (MRAM) or a ferromagnetic cell, providing data retention even when power is removed. It falls under physics → information storage → static stores.
[G11C 14/009] and the nonvolatile element is a resistive RAM element, i.e. programmable resistors, e.g. formed of phase change or chalcogenide material -- G11C 14/009 pertains to digital storage cells that combine volatile and non-volatile properties for data retention during power loss, specifically where the nonvolatile component is a resistive RAM element, such as those made from phase change or chalcogenide materials.
[G11C 15/00] Digital stores in which information comprising one or more characteristic parts is written into the store and in which information is read-out by searching for one or more of these characteristic parts, i.e. associative or content-addressed stores -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memories wherein a sought data word for a given field (characteristic part) is supplied as input to the memory, which is able to search its stored data contents to determine if the supplied data word is present among said data contents. If a match or 'hit' is established, the address(es) in the memory where the supplied data word was found is/are returned. Optionally the contents of all fields of the matching word are returned.
[G11C 15/02] using magnetic elements -- This subclass covers digital static storage devices that write information as distinct characteristic parts and retrieve it by searching for those parts, i.e., associative or content-addressed memories, and specifically relates to implementations that use magnetic elements for storing and accessing the data.
[G11C 15/04] using semiconductor elements -- G11C 15/04 covers the use of semiconductor devices in static digital memory systems where data is stored as characteristic parts and retrieved by searching for those parts, i.e., associative or content-addressable storage.
[G11C 15/043] using capacitive charge storage elements -- G11C 15/043 addresses the use of capacitive charge storage elements in digital associative memories. It concerns static stores where information is written as charge on capacitors and read out by searching for characteristic parts, enabling content-addressed data retrieval.
[G11C 15/046] using non-volatile storage elements -- G11C 15/046 covers methods and systems that employ non-volatile storage elements in static digital stores where data is written as characteristic parts and later retrieved by searching for those parts, i.e. associative or content-addressed memory. It falls under the physics class of information storage and addresses the use of persistent media for such retrieval.
[G11C 15/06] using cryogenic elements -- G11C 15/06 concerns static digital storage devices that hold information as characteristic parts and retrieve it by searching for those parts, i.e., associative or content-addressable stores, and that make use of cryogenic elements such as low-temperature memory cells or superconducting components.
[G11C 16/00] Erasable programmable read-only memories -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memories of the type in which charge is stored in a non-volatile manner, either in a "floating gate" capacitor (see G11C16/0408 ) or trapped in the gate insulator of a transistor (see G11C16/0466 ).  In both cases, the effect of charge storage is to modify the threshold voltage of the transistor, e.g. from depletion to enhancement mode (stored state discriminated by conduction or not at zero gate voltage) or from 'normal' to 'deep' enhancement (stored state discriminated by conduction or not at small positive gate voltage).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Digital stores characterised by arrangements of cells having volatile and non-volatile storage properties for back-up when the power is down -> G11C14/00
[G11C 16/02] electrically programmable -- G11C 16/02 designates electrically programmable static memory devices, such as EPROMs, where data is written by applying electrical signals to alter the charge state of memory cells. The classification covers the structures, programming circuits and methods used to set or change stored information without external light sources, including related control and interface mechanisms.
[G11C 16/04] using variable threshold transistors, e.g. FAMOS -- This subclass covers EPROM type static memories that use transistors whose threshold voltage can be varied, such as floating-gate MOS (FAMOS) devices. By adjusting the threshold, stored charge can be erased and rewritten, providing electrically erasable, non-volatile data storage.
[G11C 16/0408] comprising cells containing floating gate transistors  G11C16/0483 ,  G11C16/0491  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Also cells in which the floating gate is composed of "nanocrystals".  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Comprising cells having several storage transistors connected in series -> G11C16/0483 - Virtual ground arrays -> G11C16/0491
[G11C 16/0416] comprising cells containing a single floating gate transistor and no select transistor, e.g. UV EPROM -- **Definition:** This place covers:  May also comprise cells with additional control gates, e.g. an erase gate.
[G11C 16/0425] comprising cells containing a merged floating gate and select transistor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The also known as "split-gate" or "1½ transistor" cells.
[G11C 16/0433] comprising cells containing a single floating gate transistor and one or more separate select transistors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The classical EEPROM cells.
[G11C 16/0441] comprising cells containing multiple floating gate devices, e.g. separate read-and-write FAMOS transistors with connected floating gates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Aso complementary-pair type cells, in which two floating gate transistors store opposite states. This type is often used to store redundancy information, see also G11C29/789 .
[G11C 16/045] Floating gate memory cells with both P and N channel memory transistors, usually sharing a common floating gate -- G11C 16/045 covers floating-gate memory cells that incorporate both p-type and n-type channel transistors, typically sharing a single floating gate to store charge. The design enables complementary operation, improving read/write characteristics and reducing cell size in erasable programmable read-only memory arrays.
[G11C 16/0458] comprising two or more independent floating gates which store independent data -- G11C 16/0458 classifies static, non-volatile memory devices that contain two or more independent floating-gate structures, each capable of storing its own data. The entry applies to erasable programmable read-only memories where separate gates provide distinct storage cells within a single chip.
[G11C 16/0466] comprising cells with charge storage in an insulating layer, e.g. metal-nitride-oxide-silicon [MNOS], silicon-oxide-nitride-oxide-silicon [SONOS]  G11C16/0483 ,  G11C16/0491  take precedence -- G11C 16/0466 covers erasable programmable read-only memory cells that store charge in an insulating layer, such as metal-nitride-oxide-silicon (MNOS) and silicon-oxide-nitride-oxide-silicon (SONOS) structures. It falls under physics → information storage → static stores → erasable programmable read-only memories.
[G11C 16/0475] comprising two or more independent storage sites which store independent data -- G11C 16/0475 covers erasable programmable read-only memory devices that contain two or more separate storage locations, each capable of holding distinct data independent of the others, enabling multiple independent data areas within a single chip.
[G11C 16/0483] comprising cells having several storage transistors connected in series -- **Definition:** This place covers:  i.e. NAND type cells.
[G11C 16/0491] Virtual ground arrays -- **Definition:** This place covers:  i.e. arrays in which the individual cell transistors are formed between parallel bitlines, one of which is selected by decoder circuitry to be ground and the other as a normal bitline.
[G11C 16/06] Auxiliary circuits, e.g. for writing into memory -- The CPC entry G11C 16/06 covers auxiliary circuitry used to write data into erasing-programmable read-only memory devices, such as PROMs and EPROMs. It pertains to supporting components that generate, control or boost the write voltage and timing required for programming the static storage cells.
[G11C 16/08] Address circuits -- Address circuits are the electronic structures that generate the binary locations used to select and access individual cells within erasable programmable read-only memories. They decode input addresses into signals that enable reading or writing of the specified memory site.
[G11C 16/10] Programming or data input circuits -- G11C 16/10 covers the circuitry that writes or loads data into static, non-volatile memory cells such as erasable programmable read-only memories. It includes programming interfaces, voltage drivers, address decoders and other components that supply the signals required to store or modify user data in EPROM, EEPROM or similar devices.
[G11C 16/102] External programming circuits, e.g. EPROM programmers; In-circuit programming or reprogramming; EPROM emulators -- G11C 16/102 covers external circuits used to program or reprogram EPROM devices, including dedicated EPROM programmers, in-circuit programming or reprogramming methods, and hardware that emulates EPROM behavior for testing or development.
[G11C 16/105] Circuits or methods for updating contents of nonvolatile memory, especially with 'security' features to ensure reliable replacement, i.e. preventing that old data is lost before new data is reliably written -- This subclass covers circuit designs and updating methods for non-volatile memories such as EPROM, EEPROM, or flash where security measures guarantee that the previous data is retained until the new data is successfully programmed, preventing loss during rewrite operations.
[G11C 16/107] Programming all cells in an array, sector or block to the same state prior to flash erasing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Erase preprogramming, also called preconditioning.
[G11C 16/12] Programming voltage switching circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Especially high-voltage switches (see e.g. EP862183, figures 1-3), ramp generators (see e.g. EP903750, figure 4).
[G11C 16/14] Circuits for erasing electrically, e.g. erase voltage switching circuits -- G11C 16/14 covers electrical erasing circuits used in erasable programmable read-only memory devices, such as circuits that apply an erase voltage or switch the erase voltage on and off. These circuits enable selective removal or resetting of stored data in static, non-volatile memory arrays.
[G11C 16/16] for erasing blocks, e.g. arrays, words, groups -- This classification concerns techniques for erasing groups of memory cells in erasable programmable read-only memories, such as blocks, arrays, words or other defined sets. It includes circuit designs, control methods, or programming commands that enable selective removal of stored charge from multiple locations in a single operation.
[G11C 16/18] Circuits for erasing optically -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. ultraviolet erase.
[G11C 16/20] Initialising -- G11C 16/20 - Initialising refers to the procedures and circuitry used to set the initial state of erasable programmable read-only memories, usually by applying a voltage or charge to prepare the cells for data storage. This includes bulk or selective initialization of EPROM, EEPROM and similar static storage devices.
[G11C 16/22] Safety or protection circuits preventing unauthorised or accidental access to memory cells -- G11C 16/22 covers circuits designed to safeguard the contents of erasable programmable read-only memory cells against unauthorized reading, modification, or inadvertent disturbance. These protection mechanisms may include lock-bits, access control logic, or voltage-monitoring arrangements that block or disable access when tampering is detected.
[G11C 16/225] Preventing erasure, programming or reading when power supply voltages are outside the required ranges -- The G11C 16/225 subclass concerns safety or control mechanisms for EPROM-type static memory devices that block erasure, programming or data readout when the supply voltage lies outside the specified operating range. It includes voltage-monitoring circuits, lock-out circuits and related methods that protect the memory from inadvertent alteration or corruption.
[G11C 16/24] Bit-line control circuits -- Bit-line control circuits are electronic components that manage the voltage and timing of the conductors used to read from or write to individual memory cells in erasable programmable read-only memories. They ensure proper selection, isolation, and data transfer on the bit lines during programming and retrieval operations, improving reliability and performance of static storage devices.
[G11C 16/26] Sensing or reading circuits -- G11C 16/26 covers circuits that sense or read data from erasable programmable read-only memory (EPROM) devices, such as address decoders, sense amplifiers and data output stages. It belongs to the static stores sub-category of information storage in physics, describing reading functions that do not modify the stored content.
[G11C 16/28] using differential sensing or reference cells, e.g. dummy cells -- G11C 16/28 relates to erasable programmable read-only memories that improve data detection by using differential sensing or reference cells, such as dummy cells, to compare a target cell against a known reference and thus enhance reliability and reduce noise.
[G11C 16/30] Power supply circuits -- G11C 16/30 covers power supply circuits used with erasable programmable read-only memory devices, such as voltage regulators, charge pumps and other circuitry that provide the bias and programming energy needed to write or erase stored data.
[G11C 16/32] Timing circuits -- G11C 16/32 covers timing circuits that generate or control clock signals for static storage devices such as erasable programmable read-only memories. These circuits manage the timing of read, write, and erase operations to ensure reliable data handling within EPROM technology. It falls under physics, information storage, static stores, and EPROM subclasses.
[G11C 16/34] Determination of programming status, e.g. threshold voltage, overprogramming or underprogramming, retention -- The code G11C 16/34 covers techniques for assessing the programming condition of erasable programmable read-only memories, such as measuring a cell's threshold voltage to identify over-programmed, under-programmed, or properly programmed states and evaluating retention characteristics. It focuses on diagnostic methods that detect and quantify programming errors in static storage devices.
[G11C 16/3404] Convergence or correction of memory cell threshold voltages; Repair or recovery of overerased or overprogrammed cells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  What is also known as "overerase/overprogram correction" or "threshold convergence".
[G11C 16/3409] Circuits or methods to recover overerased nonvolatile memory cells detected during erase verification, usually by means of a "soft" programming step -- This subclass covers circuits and methods that restore non-volatile memory cells that have been over-erased and identified during erase verification, typically by applying a soft programming pulse to bring the cell back within its normal operating window. The techniques are used in EEPROM and flash memory to improve yield and reliability.
[G11C 16/3413] Circuits or methods to recover overprogrammed nonvolatile memory cells detected during program verification, usually by means of a "soft" erasing step -- G11C 16/3413 covers circuits or methods that restore non-volatile memory cells that have been overprogrammed and identified during program verification, typically by applying a soft-erase operation to reduce the stored charge without fully erasing the cell. These techniques enable correction of write errors while preserving surrounding data.
[G11C 16/3418] Disturbance prevention or evaluation; Refreshing of disturbed memory data -- A classification for techniques that detect, prevent, or assess errors caused by disturbances in non-volatile static memory cells, and methods for refreshing or restoring the original data in erasable programmable read-only memories to maintain data integrity.
[G11C 16/3422] Circuits or methods to evaluate read or write disturbance in nonvolatile memory, without steps to mitigate the problem -- G11C 16/3422 covers circuits or methods that detect or assess disturbances occurring during read or write operations in erasable programmable read-only memories, focusing solely on measuring or evaluating the problem without providing steps to mitigate it.
[G11C 16/3427] Circuits or methods to prevent or reduce disturbance of the state of a memory cell when neighbouring cells are read or written -- G11C 16/3427 covers circuit designs and operational methods that protect the stored data of a memory cell from being altered when adjacent cells are accessed, such as reading or writing. It includes techniques that limit electrical coupling, charge leakage, or disturbance currents to preserve cell integrity during neighboring operations.
[G11C 16/3431] Circuits or methods to detect disturbed nonvolatile memory cells, e.g. which still read as programmed but with threshold less than the program verify threshold or read as erased but with threshold greater than the erase verify threshold, and to reverse the disturbance via a refreshing programming or erasing step -- Methods and circuits for identifying nonvolatile memory cells whose threshold voltages have drifted outside verification limits-still reading as programmed but below the program-verify level, or reading as erased but above the erase-verify level-and subsequently correcting the disturbance by applying a refresh programming or erasing operation. This technology is used in erasable programmable read-only memories to maintain data integrity.
[G11C 16/3436] Arrangements for verifying correct programming or erasure -- G11C 16/3436 refers to arrangements that test whether an erasable programmable read-only memory has been correctly programmed or erased, using circuits or procedures that detect errors in the stored data after the operation. This sub-class falls under physics → information storage → static stores → erasable programmable read-only memories.
[G11C 16/344] Arrangements for verifying correct erasure or for detecting overerased cells -- The code G11C 16/344 covers devices and methods that check whether an erasable programmable read-only memory cell has been properly erased, and that identify cells that have been over-erased and become non-functional, typically by measuring residual charge or resistance to ensure data integrity in static storage technologies.
[G11C 16/3445] Circuits or methods to verify correct erasure of nonvolatile memory cells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  May lead to repeated erase, verify steps until correctly erased or retry limit reached (= failure).
[G11C 16/345] Circuits or methods to detect overerased nonvolatile memory cells, usually during erasure verification -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuits that make no attempt at recovery; the device is therefore regarded as faulty.
[G11C 16/3454] Arrangements for verifying correct programming or for detecting overprogrammed cells -- The subclass G11C 16/3454 covers circuitry and methods that verify that an erasable programmable read-only memory has been correctly programmed and detect cells that have been over-programmed, such as by using verification read cycles, sense-amplifier checks or error-detection logic.
[G11C 16/3459] Circuits or methods to verify correct programming of nonvolatile memory cells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuits that may lead to repeated program, verify steps until correctly programmed or retry limit reached (= failure).
[G11C 16/3463] Circuits or methods to detect overprogrammed nonvolatile memory cells, usually during program verification -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuits that make no attempt at recovery; the device is therefore regarded as faulty.
[G11C 16/3468] Prevention of overerasure or overprogramming, e.g. by verifying whilst erasing or writing -- G11C 16/3468 covers techniques for preventing over-erasure or over-programming of erasable programmable read-only memories, such as monitoring the cell state while erasing or writing and halting the operation when the desired level is reached. The methods aim to improve data integrity and device reliability in static storage technologies.
[G11C 16/3472] Circuits or methods to verify correct erasure of nonvolatile memory cells whilst erasing is in progress, e.g. by detecting onset or cessation of current flow in cells and using the detector output to terminate erasure -- The entry covers circuits or methods that verify correct erasure of non-volatile memory cells while the erasure is in progress, for example by detecting the onset or cessation of current flow in the cells and using the detector output to terminate the erasure operation.
[G11C 16/3477] Circuits or methods to prevent overerasing of nonvolatile memory cells, e.g. by detecting onset or cessation of current flow in cells and using the detector output to terminate erasing -- G11C 16/3477 covers circuits or techniques that stop an erase operation in non-volatile memory cells once the erase current begins to drop or stops, preventing over-erasing. The approach monitors the onset or cessation of current flow in the cells and uses that signal to terminate the erase pulse.
[G11C 16/3481] Circuits or methods to verify correct programming of nonvolatile memory cells whilst programming is in progress, e.g. by detecting onset or cessation of current flow in cells and using the detector output to terminate programming -- The G11C 16/3481 subclass addresses circuits or methods that monitor non-volatile memory cells during programming, detecting the onset or cessation of current flow to verify correct data writing and to stop the programming process when appropriate.
[G11C 16/3486] Circuits or methods to prevent overprogramming of nonvolatile memory cells, e.g. by detecting onset or cessation of current flow in cells and using the detector output to terminate programming -- G11C 16/3486 covers circuits or methods that safeguard non-volatile memory cells from being over-programmed by monitoring the onset or cessation of current flow in the cells and using the detector's signal to halt the programming operation. This technology is applied to erasable programmable read-only memories, a subclass of static information-storage devices.
[G11C 16/349] Arrangements for evaluating degradation, retention or wearout, e.g. by counting erase cycles -- This subclass covers circuitry or methods that monitor the condition of erasable programmable read-only memories such as EEPROM or flash by measuring erase-cycle count or other wear indicators to assess degradation, data retention or impending failure. It is used for reliability diagnostics, wear-leveling and lifetime management in memory devices.
[G11C 16/3495] Circuits or methods to detect or delay wearout of nonvolatile EPROM or EEPROM memory devices, e.g. by counting numbers of erase or reprogram cycles, by using multiple memory areas serially or cyclically -- This CPC subclass deals with circuits or methods that monitor or extend the lifespan of nonvolatile EPROM or EEPROM devices, for example by counting erase or reprogram cycles or by employing multiple memory areas in series or cyclically. It falls under physics → information storage → static stores → erasable programmable read-only memories.
[G11C 17/00] Read-only memories programmable only once -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memories in which the stored data are permanently defined at the time of manufacturing (mask ROM) or which are adapted to be programmed with data one time only after manufacture (PROM).
[G11C 17/005] with a storage element common to a large number of data, e.g. perforated card  G11C17/02 ,  G11C17/04  take precedence -- The CPC subclass G11C 17/005 covers read-only memory devices that store many data items on a single shared storage medium, such as perforated cards or similar media where the information is fixed after manufacturing and cannot be rewritten. It falls under static stores and one-time programmable ROM technologies.
[G11C 17/02] using magnetic or inductive elements -- This subclass covers read-only memory devices that are programmed a single time using magnetic or inductive storage elements, such as core-memory or magnetic stripe technologies. It pertains to static storage methods where data is retained without power and cannot be altered after initial programming.
[G11C 17/04] using capacitive elements -- G11C 17/04 refers to read-only memory devices that are programmed only once by employing capacitive elements. It is classified under physics, information storage, static stores, and specifically addresses ROM technologies that use capacitance to retain data after a single programming operation.
[G11C 17/06] using diode elements -- The G11C 17/06 subclass covers one-time programmable read-only memories that are realized with diode elements, such as diode-matrix ROMs in which each memory cell is formed by a diode to store a fixed binary state. It relates to static information-storage devices in the physics field that can be programmed only once.
[G11C 17/08] using semiconductor devices, e.g. bipolar elements -- This subclass covers the use of semiconductor components, particularly bipolar devices, to implement read-only memories that can be programmed only once. It includes circuit designs where the memory state is set by permanent changes in the semiconductor structure.
[G11C 17/10] in which contents are determined during manufacturing by a predetermined arrangement of coupling elements, e.g. mask-programmable ROM -- The code G11C 17/10 designates read-only memory cells whose stored data are fixed during fabrication by a predetermined pattern of coupling elements, for example mask-programmed ROM. These devices are programmed only once and cannot be altered after manufacture.
[G11C 17/12] using field-effect devices -- This subclass covers one-time programmable read-only memories that are programmed by means of field-effect devices such as MOSFETs, CMOS transistors or other FET structures. It includes techniques where a memory cell is permanently altered by applying an electric field to create a conductive path or to charge a floating gate, thereby storing a fixed binary value.
[G11C 17/123] comprising cells having several storage transistors connected in series -- **Definition:** This place covers:  i.e. NAND-type cells.
[G11C 17/126] Virtual ground arrays -- **Definition:** This place covers:  i.e. arrays in which the individual cell transistors are formed between parallel bitlines, one of which is selected by decoder circuitry to be ground and the other as a normal bitline.
[G11C 17/14] in which contents are determined by selectively establishing, breaking or modifying connecting links by permanently altering the state of coupling elements, e.g. PROM -- G11C 17/14 covers read-only memory devices that are programmed once by selectively establishing, breaking, or modifying connections among coupling elements, typically using permanent alterations such as those found in PROMs.
[G11C 17/143] using laser-fusible links -- G11C 17/143 covers methods for programming read-only memories that can be written only once by using laser-fusible links. The technique employs a focused laser beam to permanently fuse selected interconnects, thereby defining the stored data without any electrical programming step.
[G11C 17/146] Write once memory, i.e. allowing changing of memory content by writing additional bits -- Write-once memory refers to a static, read-only storage device whose contents can be altered only by adding new bits, not by erasing or overwriting existing ones. It is used for one-time-programmable ROMs and similar devices where data is permanently recorded after the initial write.
[G11C 17/16] using electrically-fusible links -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. fuses, antifuses.
[G11C 17/165] Memory cells which are electrically programmed to cause a change in resistance, e.g. to permit multiple resistance steps to be programmed rather than conduct to or from non-conduct change of fuses and antifuses  digital stores using resistance random access memory elements  G11C13/0002 -- G11C 17/165 covers memory cells that are programmed electrically to alter their resistance, allowing several distinct resistance levels rather than a simple on/off state. These cells are used in digital storage such as resistance-random-access memory (RRAM) and include technologies like programmable fuses and antifuses with multiple resistance steps.
[G11C 17/18] Auxiliary circuits, e.g. for writing into memory -- G11C 17/18 pertains to auxiliary circuitry that facilitates the programming of one-time programmable read-only memories, such as charge-pump generators, voltage-level shifters, or write drivers that create the high-voltage or precise timing pulses needed to write data into the memory cells.
[G11C 19/00] Digital stores in which the information is moved stepwise, e.g. shift registers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Digital stores or memories in which information is cascaded between neighbouring data storage locations in a chain under the control of at least one common clock signal.
[G11C 19/005] with ferro-electric elements (condensers) -- The subclass G11C 19/005 relates to static digital storage devices, such as shift registers, that move information stepwise using ferroelectric elements, typically ferroelectric capacitors, to store each bit. It focuses on memory designs where the ferroelectric material provides the storage medium within the sequential logic.
[G11C 19/02] using magnetic elements -- G11C 19/02 covers digital static storage devices that move information stepwise, such as shift registers, which employ magnetic elements to represent and transfer binary data. The classification applies to magnetic-based shift registers and similar devices that store bits magnetically while shifting them through the circuit.
[G11C 19/04] using cores with one aperture or magnetic loop -- G11C 19/04 relates to digital static storage devices, such as shift registers, in which information moves stepwise and the storage element is a magnetic core having a single aperture or a magnetic loop configuration. This classification captures designs that use that one-aperture core or loop to represent binary states.
[G11C 19/06] using structures with a number of apertures or magnetic loops, e.g. transfluxors -- This subclass covers digital static storage devices that shift data stepwise by using arrays of apertures or magnetic loops, such as transfluxor structures, to move bits within the register.
[G11C 19/08] using thin films in plane structure -- The G11C 19/08 subclass covers digital static storage devices such as shift registers where data is moved stepwise, and where the storage elements are implemented as thin-film structures laid out in a planar configuration. It falls under physics → information storage → static stores → digital stores with stepwise information movement.
[G11C 19/0808] using magnetic domain propagation -- G11C 19/0808 relates to methods that shift digital data in static storage devices by propagating magnetic domains, such as in shift-registers, instead of using conventional electronic gating. The approach leverages the movement of magnetic domain walls to move bits stepwise along a storage medium.
[G11C 19/0816] using a rotating or alternating coplanar magnetic field -- This subclass covers digital static storage devices, such as shift registers, where data is transferred stepwise by applying a rotating or alternating magnetic field that lies in a single plane. The technique uses a coplanar magnetic field to move bits within the store without physical displacement of the medium.
[G11C 19/0825] using a variable perpendicular magnetic field -- G11C 19/0825 relates to static digital storage devices, such as shift registers, where information is moved stepwise, and discloses the use of a magnetic field that varies in strength or direction perpendicular to the storage medium to write, shift or modify stored data.
[G11C 19/0833] using magnetic domain interaction -- This CPC entry covers techniques that use magnetic domain interaction to move bits within static magnetic digital stores such as shift registers. It relates to physics-based information storage where data is transferred stepwise without moving the storage medium.
[G11C 19/0841] using electric current -- G11C 19/0841 concerns digital static storage devices such as shift registers where stored bits are moved stepwise through the circuit by means of an applied electric current. The classification focuses on the use of current to transfer or advance data within these registers, covering techniques for controlling the stepwise motion of information.
[G11C 19/085] Generating magnetic fields therefor, e.g. uniform magnetic field for magnetic domain stabilisation -- G11C 19/085 covers methods and apparatus for generating magnetic fields that stabilise magnetic domains in static digital storage devices such as shift registers. It includes the production of uniform magnetic fields and related techniques used to maintain domain integrity during stepwise data movement.
[G11C 19/0858] Generating, replicating or annihilating magnetic domains (also comprising different types of magnetic domains, e.g. "Hard Bubbles")  G11C19/0866  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass G11C 19/0858 covers techniques for generating, replicating or destroying magnetic domains used in static digital storage devices such as shift registers and includes various domain types like hard bubbles. It resides in the physics, information storage, static stores hierarchy and is superseded by the more specific subclass G11C 19/0866.
[G11C 19/0866] Detecting magnetic domains -- G11C 19/0866 concerns techniques for detecting magnetic domains in static digital storage media such as shift registers and other stepwise information carriers. It includes magnetic read heads, sensor arrays and methods that identify the polarity or orientation of magnetic regions used to store bits. The class falls under physics, information storage, static stores.
[G11C 19/0875] Organisation of a plurality of magnetic shift registers -- The entry G11C 19/0875 classifies the structural arrangement and interconnection of several magnetic shift registers within a digital storage system, detailing how multiple registers are organized to perform stepwise data movement. It addresses designs that coordinate the registers to achieve larger or more complex storage functions.
[G11C 19/0883] Means for switching magnetic domains from one path into another path, i.e. transfer switches, swap gates or decoders -- G11C 19/0883 covers devices that redirect magnetic domains from one data path to another, such as transfer switches, swap gates or decoders, used in static digital storage systems where information is moved stepwise, for example in shift-register architectures.
[G11C 19/0891] using hybrid structure, e.g. ion doped layers -- The G11C 19/0891 entry covers static digital storage elements such as shift registers that move information stepwise, employing hybrid structures like ion-doped layers to achieve the data propagation.
[G11C 19/10] using thin films on rods -- The entry covers static digital storage devices where data is transferred stepwise, such as shift-register-type stores, that employ thin-film coatings applied to cylindrical rods to record and move information along the rod's length. It relates to the physical implementation of thin-film technologies for this class of shift-register storage.
[G11C 19/12] using non-linear reactive devices in resonant circuits -- The entry covers the incorporation of nonlinear reactive components such as varactors, Schottky diodes or nonlinear inductors into resonant circuits to modify resonance characteristics, enable frequency tuning or signal shaping, and improve performance of static digital storage elements such as shift registers.
[G11C 19/14] using magnetic elements in combination with active elements, e.g. discharge tubes, semiconductor elements -- The G11C 19/14 subclass covers digital static storage devices that combine magnetic components with active elements such as discharge tubes or semiconductor devices, enabling the stepwise movement of stored data. It describes technologies where magnetic elements are integrated with active circuitry to manipulate binary information within shift-register-type architectures.
[G11C 19/18] using capacitors as main elements of the stages -- G11C 19/18 covers shift-register type digital stores where the individual storage stages are implemented primarily with capacitors. The capacitors serve as the main charge-holding elements that represent binary states and are sequentially shifted through the register. This approach is used in static memory designs that move data stepwise.
[G11C 19/182] in combination with semiconductor elements, e.g. bipolar transistors, diodes -- The class covers digital static storage devices that move information stepwise, such as shift registers, when implemented with semiconductor components like bipolar transistors or diodes. It pertains to register circuits that transfer data serially using these semiconductor elements.
[G11C 19/184] with field-effect transistors, e.g. MOS-FET -- The G11C 19/184 subclass covers static digital storage devices that move data stepwise using field-effect transistors such as MOS-FETs, including shift-register architectures and similar circuits that shift bits within a fixed array. It falls under physics → information storage → static stores → digital stores with stepwise data movement.
[G11C 19/186] using only one transistor per capacitor, e.g. bucket brigade shift register -- This CPC class addresses digital static stores where information moves stepwise, such as shift registers, and specifically covers implementations that use a single transistor per capacitor, for example bucket-brigade shift registers.
[G11C 19/188] Organisation of a multiplicity of shift registers, e.g. regeneration, timing or input-output circuits -- The code pertains to digital static memory where data is shifted sequentially, focusing on how several shift registers are organized together. It includes structures for regenerating signals, coordinating timing among registers and linking input-output circuits to manage the flow of information.
[G11C 19/20] using discharge tubes -- This subclass concerns the use of discharge tubes-such as neon, phosphor-coated or other gas-filled tubes-to realise stepwise data movement in static digital stores like shift registers. It includes methods and apparatus where information bits are stored, shifted or retrieved by controlling electron discharge processes within the tube.
[G11C 19/202] with vacuum tubes  G11C19/207  takes precedence -- G11C 19/202 pertains to digital storage systems where information is transferred stepwise, specifically those utilizing vacuum tubes. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European and United States patent offices, under the broader category of static information storage devices in physics.
[G11C 19/205] with gas-filled tubes  G11C19/207  takes precedence -- G11C 19/205 pertains to digital storage devices that use stepwise information movement, specifically those employing gas-filled tubes. However, for more precise classification, G11C 19/207 should be used if applicable.
[G11C 19/207] with counting tubes -- G11C 19/207 covers digital shift-register type storage that uses counting tubes, such as vacuum-tube based flip-flops or electron-tube counters, to move binary information step by step through the register. These devices implement static stores where data is transferred sequentially by tube-based counting stages.
[G11C 19/28] using semiconductor elements -- G11C 19/28 classifies digital static storage devices that move data stepwise, such as shift registers, when they are built with semiconductor elements. It falls under physics → information storage → static stores and covers the implementation of these stepwise-moving stores using semiconductor technology.
[G11C 19/282] with charge storage in a depletion layer, i.e. charge coupled devices [CCD] -- G11C 19/282 covers digital static storage devices where information is moved stepwise by shifting charge packets in a depletion layer, commonly known as charge-coupled devices (CCDs). These devices store charge in potential wells and transfer it sequentially, enabling image sensors and memory registers.
[G11C 19/285] Peripheral circuits, e.g. for writing into the first stage; for reading-out of the last stage -- The CPC subclass G11C 19/285 covers peripheral circuits for digital static stores where data is moved stepwise, such as shift-registers, including the circuitry used to write into the first stage and to read out from the last stage. It is classified under physics, information storage, static stores.
[G11C 19/287] Organisation of a multiplicity of shift registers -- G11C 19/287 covers the architecture and interconnection of multiple shift registers used in digital static storage. It includes techniques for arranging registers in series, parallel, or cascaded configurations and the control circuitry that coordinates data movement across them. The classification addresses the organization of these stepwise data-transfer devices within larger systems.
[G11C 19/30] using opto-electronic devices, i.e. light-emitting and photoelectric devices electrically- or optically-coupled -- G11C 19/30 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers digital storage systems that use opto-electronic devices for stepwise information movement, such as shift registers, where these devices are either electrically or optically coupled. This classification is specific to the integration of light-emitting and photoelectric components in memory circuits.
[G11C 19/32] using super-conductive elements -- This CPC subclass relates to digital static storage devices, such as shift registers, that employ superconductive elements to move data stepwise, improving speed, power efficiency and signal integrity. The use of superconducting materials reduces resistance and enables faster data propagation within the register.
[G11C 19/34] using storage elements with more than two stable states represented by steps, e.g. of voltage, current, phase, frequency -- G11C 19/34 covers digital static storage devices where data is transferred in discrete steps, such as shift registers, that employ storage elements capable of more than two stable states, for example distinct voltage, current, phase or frequency levels.
[G11C 19/36] using semiconductor elements -- The G11C 19/36 subclass covers digital static storage devices that shift data stepwise, such as shift registers, when implemented with semiconductor components. It pertains to the use of semiconductor elements to realise these information-storage functions.
[G11C 19/38] two-dimensional [2D], e.g. horizontal and vertical shift registers -- This code covers static digital storage devices that shift data stepwise in two dimensions, such as horizontal and vertical shift registers. The registers move bits across a matrix of storage cells, enabling parallel or raster-like data transfer.
[G11C 2013/0042] Read using differential sensing, e.g. bit line [BL] and bit line bar [BLB] -- The entry covers memory circuits that sense data by comparing a signal on a bit line with the opposite signal on a complementary bit line, using differential sensing to improve noise immunity and speed. It applies to read operations in semiconductor memories where both lines are monitored simultaneously to determine the stored binary value.
[G11C 2013/0045] Read using current through the cell -- The CPC subclass G11C 2013/0045 concerns methods for reading information stored in an electrically readable cell by applying a current through the cell and detecting the resulting electrical response. It covers read-out techniques that sense voltage, resistance, or charge variations caused by the current to determine the stored data. This includes read operations in memory devices such as DRAM, SRAM, and other semiconductor storage cells.
[G11C 2013/0047] Read destroying or disturbing the data -- The CPC subclass G11C 2013/0047 covers methods, devices or media that read stored information while simultaneously erasing, altering or otherwise disturbing the data, typically for security, tamper-evidence or self-destruction purposes. It includes read-while-destroy mechanisms, destructive read-out techniques, and related control or monitoring features that prevent subsequent reuse of the data after access.
[G11C 2013/005] Read using potential difference applied between cell electrodes -- The G11C 2013/005 entry covers methods of retrieving stored data by applying a voltage across the electrodes of a memory cell and measuring the resulting potential difference. This read-out technique is used for electrochemical or similar devices where the cell's charge state is inferred from the voltage observed between its terminals.
[G11C 2013/0052] Read process characterized by the shape, e.g. form, length, amplitude of the read pulse -- This CPC entry concerns optical reading methods in which the shape of the read pulse - its waveform, duration or amplitude - is specifically defined and used to influence the detection of recorded information. It includes techniques for generating or adjusting the pulse shape to improve read accuracy, signal quality or data retrieval speed.
[G11C 2013/0054] Read is performed on a reference element, e.g. cell, and the reference sensed value is used to compare the sensed value of the selected cell -- The code covers methods where a reference element such as a cell is read and its sensed value serves as a baseline against which the sensed value of another selected cell is compared, enabling calibration or differential measurement. This is used in devices like memory arrays, sensor matrices, or imaging sensors.
[G11C 2013/0057] Read done in two steps, e.g. wherein the cell is read twice and one of the two read values serving as a reference value -- The entry covers methods for reading a memory cell in a two-step process, where the first read provides a reference value and the second read is compared to it to determine the stored data. This approach enhances accuracy or error detection in electronic storage devices.
[G11C 2013/0066] Verify correct writing whilst writing is in progress, e.g. by detecting onset or cessation of current flow in cell and using the detector output to terminate writing -- This entry covers methods and apparatus that monitor a write operation in progress by sensing the onset or cessation of current flow in a storage cell, using the detected signal to confirm correct inscription and to terminate the write process.
[G11C 2013/0071] Write using write potential applied to access device gate -- G11C 2013/0071 covers methods of writing data to a storage element by applying a write-potential to the gate of an access device, such as a transistor or selector, used to program, erase, or modify the recorded information. The entry pertains to techniques that manipulate the electrical state of memory cells through gate voltage control, falling within the CPC subclass for recording media and information-storage processes.
[G11C 2013/0073] Write using bi-directional cell biasing -- The G11C 2013/0073 entry covers methods for recording information by applying electrical bias to a memory cell in both directions, allowing data to be written by switching the cell's state forward or reverse. It includes devices and control schemes that use bi-directional biasing to set or erase bits in storage media such as ferroelectric, electro-chromic or other bistable cells.
[G11C 2013/0076] Write operation performed depending on read result -- This CPC subclass covers methods or devices where a write to a storage medium is executed only after evaluating data that has been read, such as conditional write, write-after-read verification, or write triggered by specific read results. It includes control logic that decides whether to perform the write based on the content, status, or outcome of a preceding read operation.
[G11C 2013/0078] Write using current through the cell -- G11C 2013/0078 covers technologies that produce characters or images by passing an electric current through a cell containing a writable medium, such as electro-chemical or electrophoretic displays and related printing methods. The classification includes devices and processes where the current modifies the state of the medium to create visible marks.
[G11C 2013/008] Write by generating heat in the surroundings of the memory material, e.g. thermowrite -- This CPC subgroup covers methods for recording data in memory devices by locally heating the material surrounding the storage element, causing a change in its physical or magnetic state that represents information. The technique, often called thermowriting, uses heat sources such as laser pulses or resistive elements to modify the memory without direct electrical contact.
[G11C 2013/0083] Write to perform initialising, forming process, electro forming or conditioning -- The code G11C 2013/0083 pertains to writing operations that are employed to initialise, form, electro-form or otherwise condition a data-storage medium, for example creating initial magnetic or ferroelectric domains or establishing conductive structures before normal recording. It covers methods where the write step prepares the medium rather than storing user data.
[G11C 2013/0085] Write a page or sector of information simultaneously, e.g. a complete row or word line -- The G11C 2013/0085 subclass covers systems and methods that enable a full page, line or defined sector of characters to be entered or recorded in a single operation, such as printing an entire row or word line at once. It includes devices with parallel recording heads, multi-key matrices or other mechanisms that write multiple symbols simultaneously.
[G11C 2013/0088] Write with the simultaneous writing of a plurality of cells -- The CPC entry G11C 2013/0088 covers techniques and apparatuses that write data by simultaneously forming a plurality of individual cells, such as multi-dot printing heads, matrix or array-based recording elements, and related control methods that enable concurrent marking of multiple pixels or characters.
[G11C 2013/009] Write using potential difference applied between cell electrodes -- G11C 2013/009 concerns writing or marking methods that create visible characters or images by applying a potential difference between cell electrodes, typically using electrochemical, electrophoretic or similar processes to alter the surface.
[G11C 2013/0092] Write characterized by the shape, e.g. form, length, amplitude of the write pulse -- G11C 2013/0092 covers recording methods in which the write operation is defined by the waveform of the write pulse, specifying characteristics such as shape, duration, and amplitude to control the recording process.
[G11C 2013/0095] Write using strain induced by, e.g. piezoelectric, thermal effects -- This CPC entry covers methods and apparatus that create marks on a recording medium by inducing strain in a writing element, usually through piezoelectric, thermal or similar effects that cause deformation and contact with the surface. It includes printers, plotters and inkless writing devices that employ such strain-induced mechanisms.
[G11C 2029/0401] in embedded memories -- G11C 2029/0401 refers to technologies that embed memory components within electronic devices or systems. It includes designs, architectures and methods for integrating RAM, ROM, flash or other memory types directly on a chip or circuit board, and for accessing or managing those built-in memories.
[G11C 2029/0403] during or with feedback to manufacture -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for manufacturing information-storage media in which the production process is monitored and adjusted in real time using feedback signals, such as sensor data on layer thickness, composition, or magnetic/optical properties. It includes systems that employ closed-loop control during the fabrication of disks, tapes, or similar storage devices to ensure precise quality and performance.
[G11C 2029/0405] comprising complete test loop -- Equipment or devices that incorporate a complete test loop, meaning a closed-loop arrangement that generates test signals, processes them and evaluates the results within the same system for self-testing, calibration or verification purposes.
[G11C 2029/0407] on power on -- G11C 2029/0407 covers technological aspects of turning a device on, including power-on detection, initiation of start-up sequences, and management of the power supply in data processing or display equipment.
[G11C 2029/0409] Online test -- The CPC entry G11C 2029/0409 covers computer-implemented methods and systems for conducting assessments over a network, including the creation, delivery, response capture, scoring and feedback of tests administered online. It pertains to the technological processes that enable electronic testing environments, remote test administration and real-time evaluation of examinee answers.
[G11C 2029/0411] Online error correction -- The G11C 2029/0411 entry covers methods that detect and correct data errors while a system is operating, allowing real-time recovery of corrupted information during transmission, storage or processing. It includes online error-correction codes, automatic retransmission schemes and adaptive correction mechanisms that function without halting normal operation.
[G11C 2029/1202] Word line control -- G11C 2029/1202 covers mechanisms and software that manage the positioning of words and lines on a printed page, including word spacing, line justification, hyphenation and line-break control in printers, typewriters and display devices.
[G11C 2029/1204] Bit line control -- The G11C 2029/1204 entry covers technologies for controlling the bit lines in semiconductor memory arrays, including circuits that select, sense, and drive individual lines during read and write operations. It includes methods for timing, amplification and isolation of bit lines to improve speed, power efficiency and signal integrity in devices such as DRAM and SRAM.
[G11C 2029/1206] Location of test circuitry on chip or wafer -- This subclass covers inventions that specify where test structures or circuitry are placed on an integrated circuit die or wafer, including regions such as periphery, core, edge, or dedicated test pads, to facilitate testing or measurement. It includes methods for arranging test components relative to functional circuitry to improve test access, yield, or reliability.
[G11C 2029/1208] Error catch memory -- G11C 2029/1208 refers to error catch memory, a type of static storage device that includes mechanisms for detecting and correcting memory errors during operation. It covers technologies such as parity checking, error-correcting codes, and fault-tolerant architectures used to improve data integrity in computers and other electronic systems.
[G11C 2029/1802] Address decoder -- G11C 2029/1802 covers address decoders, which are circuit components that translate binary or encoded input signals into specific memory or device locations, enabling selection of storage cells or functional blocks in digital systems. Used in memory addressing, microcontrollers and data routing, these decoders often employ combinational logic to generate one-hot outputs based on an input address.
[G11C 2029/1804] Manipulation of word size -- G11C 2029/1804 relates to techniques for changing the size of data words within a computing system, such as expanding, shrinking, or reformatting word lengths to match processor architectures, memory banks, or communication protocols. It includes hardware and software methods for word-size conversion, packing, unpacking, and alignment.
[G11C 2029/1806] Address conversion or mapping, i.e. logical to physical address -- G11C 2029/1806 covers methods and apparatus for translating logical addresses used by software into the actual physical locations where data are stored. It includes hardware or software mechanisms such as page tables, address translation units and mapping algorithms employed in memory and storage systems.
[G11C 2029/2602] Concurrent test -- G11C 2029/2602 covers techniques for carrying out concurrent tests, where multiple elements or functions are examined at the same time rather than sequentially. The classification is applied to devices or methods that perform simultaneous diagnostic or performance testing on recording or data-processing equipment.
[G11C 2029/3202] Scan chain -- G11C 2029/3202 covers scan-chain arrangements in image-scanning devices, where a series of sensing elements are linked sequentially to convert lines or areas of a document into electrical signals. The classification includes designs that use a chain of detectors or CCD/CMOS elements to improve resolution, speed, or integration in scanners, copiers and related optical-reading equipment.
[G11C 2029/3602] Pattern generator -- G11C 2029/3602 covers devices, systems or methods that create visual or tactile patterns for use in printing, display, textile or similar applications. It includes electronic or mechanical pattern generators that produce repeating or random designs, often controlled by software or programmable logic.
[G11C 2029/4002] Comparison of products, i.e. test results of chips or with golden chip -- G11C 2029/4002 covers techniques for comparing semiconductor products, including the measurement and analysis of test results from a chip against a reference or golden chip to assess performance, functionality or quality.
[G11C 2029/4402] Internal storage of test result, quality data, chip identification, repair information -- G11C 2029/4402 covers technologies that store test results, quality metrics, chip identification data or repair information within the device itself, typically using on-chip memory or embedded non-volatile storage. It includes methods for recording, retrieving and managing such internal data to support diagnostics, quality assurance and maintenance.
[G11C 2029/5002] Characteristic -- G11C 2029/5002 covers methods and apparatus for detecting, recording or using distinguishing characteristics of documents or media, such as physical, chemical or optical traits, to identify, classify or process the item.
[G11C 2029/5004] Voltage -- G11C 2029/5004 pertains to inventions that involve voltage, encompassing the generation, measurement, regulation and conversion of electrical potential in apparatuses used for recording, transmitting or processing information. It includes technologies such as voltage sources, detectors, stabilisers and controllers that affect the electrical characteristics of signal-handling devices.
[G11C 2029/5006] Current -- The CPC subclass G11C-2029/5006 covers technologies that employ electric current for the operation of recording media, such as the generation, control, or measurement of current used to write, read, erase, or modify stored information on magnetic, optical, or other types of storage surfaces. This includes circuitry and methods that harness current pulses or continuous currents to affect the physical state of the recording layer.
[G11C 2029/5602] Interface to device under test -- This class covers hardware or software arrangements that provide an interface between test-equipment and a device under test, enabling monitoring, control or data exchange for verification, debugging or measurement. It includes connectors, adapters, communication protocols and signal-conditioning circuitry that link testing systems with the target device.
[G11C 2029/5604] Display of error information -- G11C 2029/5604 covers the presentation of error information in electronic devices, including the generation and display of error messages, codes, and diagnostic data on screens, LEDs or other visual indicators. It relates to how systems inform users of faults or operational problems.
[G11C 2029/5606] Error catch memory -- The CPC entry G11C 2029/5606 covers memory systems that detect, capture and store information about errors occurring during operation, including error-catch buffers, logging mechanisms and associated control circuitry. It pertains to devices and methods that record fault data to enable diagnosis, correction or recovery of corrupted stored data.
[G11C 21/00] Digital stores in which the information circulates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Digital stores or memories in which information bits circulate stepwise in a closed loop or ring arrangement.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - In which information circulates stepwise -> G11C19/00
[G11C 21/005] using electrical delay lines -- The entry G11C 21/005 covers methods that employ electrical delay lines as a means of static digital storage, where information is retained by the time it takes a signal to travel through a transmission line. It addresses circuits and techniques that use the propagation delay of electrical pulses to write, read, or shift stored bits within the line.
[G11C 21/02] using electromechanical delay lines, e.g. using a mercury tank -- G11C 21/02 refers to static digital information storage devices that employ electromechanical delay lines, such as mercury-filled tanks, to temporarily hold data as it circulates within the store. It falls under physics → information storage → static stores.
[G11C 21/023] using piezoelectric transducers, e.g. mercury tank -- This subclass covers techniques that employ piezoelectric transducers-such as a mercury-filled tank-to generate or detect acoustic signals for reading, writing or processing data in digital static storage devices where information circulates.
[G11C 21/026] using magnetostriction transducers, e.g. nickel delay line -- G11C 21/026 covers digital static storage systems that employ magnetostriction transducers, such as nickel delay lines, to read, write, or delay data signals within the store. These devices use the mechanical deformation of magnetic materials to convert electrical pulses into acoustic waves for storage or processing.
[G11C 2207/00] Indexing scheme relating to arrangements for writing information into, or reading information out from, a digital store -- The classification G11C 2207/00 covers indexing schemes that organize how data is written to or read from digital storage media, enabling efficient access and management of information in static stores.
[G11C 2207/002] Isolation gates, i.e. gates coupling bit lines to the sense amplifier -- G11C 2207/002 covers isolation gates that connect bit lines to sense amplifiers in static digital memory cells. These gates selectively enable or block signal transfer during read or write operations, helping control access to stored data. The classification falls under physics, information storage, static stores and relates to arrangements for writing or reading information from a digital store.
[G11C 2207/005] Transfer gates, i.e. gates coupling the sense amplifier output to data lines, I/O lines or global bit lines -- The G11C 2207/005 entry covers transfer gates that connect the output of a sense amplifier to data, I/O or global bit lines in static digital storage devices. These gates manage the flow of information between the read circuitry and the memory array during write and read operations.
[G11C 2207/007] Register arrays -- G11C 2207/007 covers register arrays, which are groups of storage elements organized to hold binary data that can be accessed simultaneously or sequentially for writing or reading operations. The classification applies to static digital stores where the arrangement of these registers enables fast, predictable data handling in electronic devices.
[G11C 2207/06] Sense amplifier related aspects -- G11C 2207/06 covers technical features of sense amplifiers used in static digital storage devices, including their design, operation, and integration for reading stored bits. It encompasses circuit configurations, biasing, and performance optimisation of amplifiers that detect small voltage differences during data retrieval.
[G11C 2207/061] Sense amplifier enabled by a address transition detection related control signal -- A sense amplifier that is activated by a control signal generated when an address line changes, used to detect and amplify the small voltage differences of a stored bit in a static digital memory cell. The address-transition detection provides timing for the amplifier, improving read speed and reliability. This concept falls under physics-information storage-static stores.
[G11C 2207/063] Current sense amplifiers -- G11C 2207/063 refers to current sense amplifiers, which are circuits that detect and amplify the small currents flowing from memory cells during read operations in static digital storage devices. These amplifiers enable reliable retrieval of stored bits by converting the sensed current into a voltage level suitable for further processing.
[G11C 2207/065] Sense amplifier drivers -- G11C 2207/065 refers to the circuitry that drives sense amplifiers in static digital memory arrays, providing the bias and control signals needed to read stored bits and assist write operations. It falls under the static stores branch of information storage in the physics section of the CPC.
[G11C 2207/066] Frequency reading type sense amplifier -- Frequency reading type sense amplifier is a circuit used in static digital storage systems that senses stored data by converting the output into a frequency-modulated signal for readout, improving the detection of small charge or voltage differences and enabling faster, more accurate data retrieval.
[G11C 2207/068] Integrator type sense amplifier -- An integrator type sense amplifier is a circuit used in static digital storage devices to detect and amplify the small voltage differences representing stored data during read operations. It integrates the sensed signal over time to improve signal-to-noise ratio, enabling reliable retrieval of information from the memory cell. This technology falls under static stores within information storage physics.
[G11C 2207/10] Aspects relating to interfaces of memory device to external buses -- The entry covers the technical features of how a memory device connects to and communicates with external bus systems, including the electrical and logical interface specifications, signaling methods, timing relationships and protocol adaptations required for reliable data transfer between the memory and surrounding circuitry.
[G11C 2207/101] Analog or multilevel bus -- G11C 2207/101, titled Analog or multilevel bus, pertains to bus architectures that convey analog or multilevel signals for writing data into or reading data from static digital storage devices, describing how information is transferred between the storage medium and associated circuitry.
[G11C 2207/102] Compression or decompression of data before storage -- G11C 2207/102 relates to methods and devices that compress or decompress data before it is written to a digital memory, aiming to reduce the amount of storage required or to speed up later retrieval.
[G11C 2207/104] Embedded memory devices, e.g. memories with a processing device on the same die or ASIC memory designs -- This subclass covers integrated memory structures where the storage component and a processing device share the same semiconductor die, including on-chip SRAM, DRAM, non-volatile memories and ASIC memory designs. It pertains to static storage arrangements for writing to or reading from digital stores within the broader physics and information-storage hierarchy.
[G11C 2207/105] Aspects related to pads, pins or terminals -- The code G11C 2207/105 classifies technical features concerning pads, pins or terminals that form the physical contact points in static digital storage devices. It addresses how these elements are arranged to write information to, or read information from, a solid-state memory cell.
[G11C 2207/107] Serial-parallel conversion of data or prefetch -- G11C 2207/107 covers techniques that convert a serial data stream into parallel form or that prefetch data for faster access in static digital storage devices. It includes circuitry and algorithms that reorder bits or bytes to match parallel input/output interfaces, improving read/write efficiency.
[G11C 2207/108] Wide data ports -- G11C 2207/108 - Wide data ports covers designs for high-bandwidth interfaces that enable rapid or simultaneous transfer of data to and from static digital storage devices, typically by increasing the number of parallel lines or the channel width to improve write and read speeds.
[G11C 2207/12] Equalization of bit lines -- Equalization of bit lines refers to techniques that balance the voltage levels on the parallel conductors connecting memory cells to ensure reliable read and write operations in static digital storage devices. This improves signal integrity and reduces timing errors.
[G11C 2207/16] Solid state audio -- G11C 2207/16 covers solid-state audio technologies that store and retrieve sound data on static digital media. It includes methods for encoding, writing, reading, and playback of audio signals using non-volatile memory such as flash or other solid-state components.
[G11C 2207/22] Control and timing of internal memory operations -- This subclass covers methods and circuitry that manage when and how data is written to or read from internal memory, including sequencing, gating and synchronization of memory access within digital devices. It includes control logic, timing generators and related techniques that ensure correct operation of static storage elements.
[G11C 2207/2209] Concurrent read and write -- G11C 2207/2209 covers technologies that allow a digital storage medium to be accessed for reading and writing at the same time, such as multi-port memory or concurrent access protocols. The classification is used for inventions that enable simultaneous data retrieval and recording without interfering with each other.
[G11C 2207/2218] Late write -- Late write refers to a method of recording data into a static digital memory after a planned delay or trigger rather than at the moment of generation, enabling deferred updating of stored information for purposes such as error correction, logging or batch processing. It is classified under CPC G11C 2207/2218 for arrangements that write or read information in static stores.
