[G11C 2207/2227] Standby or low power modes -- G11C 2207/2227 covers arrangements that place a static digital storage device into a standby or low-power condition when it is not actively being read or written. The classification includes mechanisms for reducing energy use, controlling power-down sequences, and preserving stored data while the device remains powered in a reduced state.
[G11C 2207/2236] Copy -- The entry G11C 2207/2236 "Copy" covers methods, apparatuses and processes for duplicating data or information stored in a static digital medium, including operations that reproduce existing content onto the same or another storage device. It includes techniques for creating exact replicas of recordings for backup, distribution or transfer purposes.
[G11C 2207/2245] Memory devices with an internal cache buffer -- G11C 2207/2245 relates to memory devices that incorporate an internal cache buffer to speed up data transfer between the main storage and the processor. It falls under physics → information storage → static stores and covers arrangements for writing to or reading from a digital store.
[G11C 2207/2254] Calibration -- G11C 2207/2254 covers calibration procedures for static information-storage devices, focusing on methods and apparatus that adjust or verify the performance of writing and reading operations in digital stores. It includes techniques for correcting signal levels, timing, and error rates to ensure reliable data recording and retrieval.
[G11C 2207/2263] Write conditionally, e.g. only if new data and old data differ -- G11C 2207/2263 covers methods for writing data to a static digital store only when a condition is met, typically when the new information differs from the data already stored. It defines conditional write operations that prevent unnecessary overwriting, improving efficiency and data integrity in information-storage devices.
[G11C 2207/2272] Latency related aspects -- The CPC entry G11C 2207/2272 covers latency-related aspects of static digital storage devices, focusing on the time delays involved in writing data to, or reading data from, such stores. It addresses performance factors that affect how quickly information can be stored or retrieved.
[G11C 2207/2281] Timing of a read operation -- G11C 2207/2281 addresses the timing of a read operation in static digital information stores. It concerns how and when data is retrieved from a storage medium, including synchronization, latency and other temporal aspects of reading stored bits.
[G11C 2207/229] Timing of a write operation -- The entry G11C 2207/229 concerns the timing of a write operation in static digital storage, defining how the moment data is recorded is controlled and synchronized with other system processes. It relates to arrangements for writing information into, or reading it out from, a digital store.
[G11C 2211/00] Indexing scheme relating to digital stores characterized by the use of particular electric or magnetic storage elements -- G11C 2211/00 covers indexing methods for digital storage devices that rely on particular electric or magnetic memory elements, such as solid-state cells, magnetic disks or tapes. It addresses schemes that organize, locate and retrieve data within these static, non-volatile stores.
[G11C 2211/401] Indexing scheme relating to cells needing refreshing or charge regeneration, i.e. dynamic cells -- The CPC class G11C 2211/401 concerns indexing schemes for digital storage cells that require periodic refreshing or charge regeneration, such as dynamic random-access memory. It falls under PHYSICS → INFORMATION STORAGE → STATIC STORES and deals with electronic or magnetic storage elements.
[G11C 2211/4013] Memory devices with multiple cells per bit, e.g. twin-cells -- G11C 2211/4013 covers memory devices that store a single bit using multiple physical cells, such as twin-cell architectures. These designs improve reliability or performance by distributing each bit across more than one electric or magnetic storage element.
[G11C 2211/4016] Memory devices with silicon-on-insulator cells -- This CPC subclass concerns memory devices that use silicon-on-insulator (SOI) cells to store data, falling under static digital stores in the physics-information storage hierarchy and focusing on electric or magnetic storage elements built with SOI technology.
[G11C 2211/406] Refreshing of dynamic cells -- Refreshing of dynamic cells refers to the periodic regeneration of charge in memory elements whose stored data decays over time, such as the capacitors in dynamic random-access memory. The process restores the original signal level to prevent data loss, ensuring reliable operation of static-store digital devices that rely on electric or magnetic storage elements.
[G11C 2211/4061] Calibration or ate or cycle tuning -- This entry covers methods for calibrating or tuning the operating cycles of static electric or magnetic storage elements, such as adjusting timing, voltage or current parameters to maintain data integrity and optimize performance. It applies to digital stores that retain information without moving parts, like various types of RAM or magnetic memory cells.
[G11C 2211/4062] Parity or ECC in refresh operations -- The entry covers techniques that apply parity bits or error-correcting codes during the refresh of static digital memory cells, such as electric or magnetic storage elements, to detect and correct data errors and maintain integrity.
[G11C 2211/4063] Interleaved refresh operations -- G11C 2211/4063 refers to interleaved refresh operations in static digital storage devices that employ electric or magnetic storage elements. It describes methods that stagger the periodic refreshing of stored data across different groups of cells, improving reliability and reducing latency in the overall refresh process.
[G11C 2211/4065] Low level details of refresh operations -- The G11C 2211/4065 subclass addresses the technical specifics of refresh operations in static digital storage devices, detailing how electric or magnetic storage elements are periodically recharged or re-magnetized to preserve data. It includes circuit designs, timing methods and algorithms that manage the refresh cycle to ensure reliable information retention.
[G11C 2211/4066] Pseudo-SRAMs -- Pseudo-SRAMs are memory structures that mimic the speed and random-access behavior of static RAM while being built from alternative storage elements such as DRAM cells or non-volatile magnetic components. They are classified under static digital stores that rely on particular electric or magnetic storage technologies.
[G11C 2211/4067] Refresh in standby or low power modes -- G11C 2211/4067 covers methods for refreshing data in static storage devices when they are in standby or low-power conditions. It applies to digital memory technologies that require periodic refresh to maintain data integrity during power-saving modes.
[G11C 2211/4068] Voltage or leakage in refresh operations -- G11C 2211/4068 covers static digital storage devices that employ electric or magnetic elements and concerns the voltage levels and leakage currents that occur during refresh operations. It includes methods and circuits for controlling or compensating these effects to improve power efficiency and data integrity in memories such as DRAM.
[G11C 2211/56] Indexing scheme relating to -- G11C 2211/56 covers indexing schemes for static digital storage devices that employ particular electric or magnetic storage elements, detailing methods for organizing, locating, or retrieving information within such non-volatile memories. It falls under Physics → Information Storage → Static Stores in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[G11C 2211/561] Multilevel memory cell aspects -- The G11C 2211/561 entry classifies technologies for multilevel memory cells, where a single static storage element holds more than one bit by using several distinct electric or magnetic states. It covers design, operation and control aspects of such cells in digital storage devices, including level-spacing, programming, read-back and error-correction techniques. This falls under the broader hierarchy of physics-based information storage and static stores.
[G11C 2211/5611] Multilevel memory cell with more than one control gate -- The CPC entry G11C 2211/5611 describes multilevel memory cells that employ more than one control gate to store several bits within a single cell, a design used in static digital stores that rely on electric or magnetic storage elements. It falls under the hierarchy PHYSICS → INFORMATION STORAGE → STATIC STORES.
[G11C 2211/5612] Multilevel memory cell with more than one floating gate -- This classification covers multilevel memory cells that incorporate more than one floating gate to store multiple bits per cell, enabling higher density in static digital storage devices that use electric or magnetic elements. It falls under physics - information storage - static stores.
[G11C 2211/5613] Multilevel memory cell with additional gates, not being floating or control gates -- The code G11C 2211/5613 classifies multilevel memory cells that include extra gates beyond the primary storage node, where those additional gates are neither floating nor control gates, enabling the storage of multiple bits per cell in static electric or magnetic digital storage devices.
[G11C 2211/5614] Multilevel memory cell comprising negative resistance, quantum tunneling or resonance tunneling elements -- A multilevel memory cell that stores several bits per element by employing devices with negative resistance, quantum-tunneling or resonance-tunneling behavior, enabling digital static storage with multiple discrete charge or conductance states in a single physical location.
[G11C 2211/5615] Multilevel magnetic memory cell using non-magnetic non-conducting interlayer, e.g. MTJ -- It covers memory cells that store multiple bits per cell by employing a magnetic tunnel junction with a non-magnetic insulating layer, enabling multilevel storage. This technology falls under static digital storage using magnetic elements.
[G11C 2211/5616] Multilevel magnetic memory cell using non-magnetic conducting interlayer, e.g. GMR, SV, PSV -- This CPC class covers static digital storage devices that store data magnetically using multilayer structures where a non-magnetic conductive interlayer, such as giant magnetoresistance (GMR), spin valve (SV) or pseudo-spin valve (PSV), enables multiple discrete magnetic states per cell. It pertains to physics-based information-storage technologies that implement multilevel magnetic memory cells.
[G11C 2211/5617] Multilevel ROM cell programmed by source, drain or gate contacting -- A multilevel ROM cell that stores several bits per element is programmed by electrically contacting the source, drain, or gate terminals. This method allows each cell to be set to distinct threshold levels, enabling higher data density in static, non-volatile memory arrays. It falls under the digital static-store subclass of information storage technologies.
[G11C 2211/562] Multilevel memory programming aspects -- The CPC subclass G11C 2211/562 falls under Physics → Information storage → Static stores and addresses multilevel memory programming aspects. It covers methods for writing and controlling multiple discrete charge or magnetic states within a single static storage element, enabling more than one bit per cell in digital electric or magnetic memory devices.
[G11C 2211/5621] Multilevel programming verification -- The G11C 2211/5621 entry, titled Multilevel programming verification, falls under the CPC group for static digital stores that use electric or magnetic storage elements. It covers techniques for checking and validating the programming of multilevel cells, such as flash memory, to ensure that each charge level correctly represents the intended data. The classification is used for patents concerning verification methods in multilevel storage technologies.
[G11C 2211/5622] Concurrent multilevel programming of more than one cell -- G11C 2211/5622 classifies techniques for simultaneously programming several memory cells at multiple levels within static electric or magnetic storage devices, allowing concurrent multilevel writes to improve write speed and storage density. It pertains to digital stores that use particular electric or magnetic storage elements and covers methods that address more than one cell in a single operation.
[G11C 2211/5623] Concurrent multilevel programming and reading -- The code G11C 2211/5623 classifies technologies that allow a storage medium to be programmed and read at several hierarchical levels simultaneously. It covers concurrent operations on multi-level cells or bits in static electric or magnetic memories, enabling faster data access and higher density by using distinct charge or magnetic states for each level.
[G11C 2211/5624] Concurrent multilevel programming and programming verification -- G11C 2211/5624 classifies technologies that address concurrent multilevel programming techniques and verification methods for static digital storage devices built from specific electric or magnetic storage elements, covering how software can be developed and checked across multiple execution levels within such fixed-memory systems.
[G11C 2211/5625] Self-converging multilevel programming -- Self-converging multilevel programming is a technique for writing data to static digital stores that uses multiple voltage or magnetic levels which automatically adjust toward target values during recording. The method improves storage density and error tolerance by allowing each cell to converge on its intended level without precise external control.
[G11C 2211/563] Multilevel memory reading aspects -- G11C 2211/563 covers technical features for reading data from multilevel memory cells in static digital storage devices that use distinct electric or magnetic storage elements. It addresses methods and apparatuses that detect multiple charge or magnetic states per cell to retrieve stored information with improved precision or speed.
[G11C 2211/5631] Concurrent multilevel reading of more than one cell -- G11C 2211/5631 concerns technologies that enable simultaneous reading of multiple memory cells at different hierarchical levels within static digital storage devices using electric or magnetic elements. It covers concurrent multilevel access methods that retrieve more than one cell during a single operation, improving read speed and data throughput in solid-state or magnetic memory.
[G11C 2211/5632] Multilevel reading using successive approximation -- G11C 2211/5632 covers techniques for extracting several bits from a single memory cell by applying successive approximation to the stored charge or magnetic state. The method enables multilevel reading in static digital storage devices that use distinct electric or magnetic storage elements.
[G11C 2211/5633] Mixed concurrent serial multilevel reading -- Mixed concurrent serial multilevel reading refers to a method for retrieving data from static digital storage devices that use electric or magnetic elements capable of storing several bits per cell. It enables simultaneous reading of multiple data streams in a serial fashion across different storage levels, improving access speed and data density.
[G11C 2211/5634] Reference cells -- Reference cells are standardized storage elements used as benchmarks in static digital storage technologies that employ specific electric or magnetic storage components. They serve to characterize performance, reliability and electrical parameters of memory devices, providing a common basis for comparison across different implementations.
[G11C 2211/564] Miscellaneous aspects -- The class G11C 2211/564 covers various non-specific technical features, methods or devices related to static digital storage that uses electric or magnetic elements, where the particular aspects do not fall under more narrowly defined subclasses. It serves as a catch-all for miscellaneous innovations in static electronic or magnetic memory technologies.
[G11C 2211/5641] Multilevel memory having cells with different number of storage levels -- The CPC entry G11C 2211/5641 covers multilevel memory devices where individual cells store more than one bit by using different numbers of distinct electric or magnetic states. It falls under physics - information storage - static stores and pertains to digital storage elements employing electric or magnetic technologies.
[G11C 2211/5642] Multilevel memory with buffers, latches, registers at input or output -- This subclass covers static digital memory devices that store data in multiple voltage or charge levels and incorporate buffers, latches or registers at their input or output terminals. The added circuitry serves to isolate the memory cells, speed data transfer, and maintain data integrity during read and write operations. It applies to electric or magnetic storage elements such as DRAM, SRAM, flash, or emerging multilevel technologies.
[G11C 2211/5643] Multilevel memory comprising cache storage devices -- G11C 2211/5643 covers multilevel memory architectures that incorporate one or more cache storage devices to improve data access speed and efficiency. It classifies digital static stores that use distinct electric or magnetic elements arranged in hierarchical levels, typically found in computers and other electronic systems.
[G11C 2211/5644] Multilevel memory comprising counting devices -- The code refers to multilevel memory structures that incorporate counting devices to control multiple storage levels within static electric or magnetic memory arrays. It sits under physics → information storage → static stores in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[G11C 2211/5645] Multilevel memory with current-mirror arrangements -- G11C 2211/5645 classifies multilevel memory cells that employ current-mirror arrangements to create several distinct charge or voltage states per storage element, a static electric storage technique within the broader field of information-storage physics.
[G11C 2211/5646] Multilevel memory with flag bits, e.g. for showing that a "first page" of a word line is programmed but not a "second page" -- G11C 2211/5646 pertains to multilevel memory systems where flag bits are used to indicate the programming status of different pages within a word line, such as marking if the first page is programmed while the second is not. This classification is part of an indexing scheme for digital storage elements characterized by specific electric or magnetic properties.
[G11C 2211/5647] Multilevel memory with bit inversion arrangement -- This CPC subclass covers multilevel memory devices that employ a bit-inversion arrangement to store multiple bits per cell using electric or magnetic storage elements. It falls under physics, information storage, static stores and pertains to digital static memory architectures.
[G11C 2211/5648] Multilevel memory programming, reading or erasing operations wherein the order or sequence of the operations is relevant -- The entry G11C 2211/5648 covers methods for programming, reading or erasing multilevel memory cells in which the order or sequence of those operations is significant, such as techniques that manage multiple charge or magnetic states within a single storage element to store more than one bit per cell.
[G11C 2211/5649] Multilevel memory with plate line or layer, e.g. in order to lower programming voltages -- This CPC entry covers static digital storage devices that use a multilayer or plate-line architecture to implement multilevel memory cells, allowing several distinct charge or magnetic states per cell. The design aims to reduce the programming voltage required to set or read each level, improving power efficiency in solid-state memories.
[G11C 2211/565] Multilevel memory comprising elements in triple well structure -- G11C 2211/565 covers multilevel memory devices that employ a triple-well structure, a form of static digital storage where data are held in several charge levels within electric or magnetic elements. The code is placed under physics - information storage - static stores and addresses designs that use three wells to boost storage density.
[G11C 2213/00] Indexing scheme relating to -- G11C 2213/00 covers indexing schemes applied to static data stores such as magnetic, optical or solid-state memory, including methods for creating, updating and searching index structures like tables, trees, hash tables or other hierarchical arrangements that facilitate rapid retrieval of stored information. It addresses the organization of data pointers rather than the physical storage medium itself.
[G11C 2213/10] Resistive cells -- Resistive cells are non-volatile memory elements that store data by changing the electrical resistance of a material, typically using metal-oxide or phase-change layers. Classified under G11C 2213/10, they fall within static storage technologies in the physics domain, covering devices such as resistive-random-access memory (RRAM) and similar resistive-switching structures used for information retention without power.
[G11C 2213/11] Metal ion trapping, i.e. using memory material including cavities, pores or spaces in form of tunnels or channels wherein metal ions can be trapped but do not react and form an electro-deposit creating filaments or dendrites -- The subclass covers techniques that use a memory material containing cavities, pores, tunnels or channels to capture metal ions without allowing them to react, thereby preventing electro-deposits such as filaments or dendrites. It pertains to static information-storage devices where ion trapping improves reliability and safety.
[G11C 2213/12] Non-metal ion trapping, i.e. using memory material trapping non-metal ions given by the electrode or another layer during a write operation, e.g. trapping, doping -- This CPC subclass covers technologies where a memory material captures non-metal ions supplied by an electrode or adjacent layer during a write operation, thereby altering the material's electrical or optical state through ion trapping or doping.
[G11C 2213/13] Dissociation, i.e. using memory material including molecules which, during a write operation, are dissociated in ions which migrate further in the memory material -- G11C 2213/13 covers memory devices that store data by dissociating molecules into ions during a write operation, allowing the ions to migrate within the storage medium to represent information. It is applied to static storage technologies where ion movement is the primary recording mechanism.
[G11C 2213/14] Use of different molecule structures as storage states, e.g. part of molecule being rotated -- The code G11C 2213/14 covers data-storage techniques that encode bits by switching a molecule between distinct structural configurations, such as rotating a segment of the molecule to represent different states. This approach treats each molecular conformation as a static storage element within a broader class of information-storage technologies.
[G11C 2213/15] Current-voltage curve -- G11C 2213/15 covers the measurement or representation of the current-voltage characteristic of static storage devices such as memory cells. It relates to techniques for plotting or analyzing the I-V curve to assess performance, reliability, or operating conditions.
[G11C 2213/16] Memory cell being a nanotube, e.g. suspended nanotube -- This CPC subclass G11C 2213/16 falls under physics → information storage → static stores and relates to memory cells that employ a nanotube, such as a suspended nanotube, as the active storage element. The classification covers designs, fabrication methods and operation of such nanotube-based memory cells for static storage applications.
[G11C 2213/17] Memory cell being a nanowire transistor -- The G11C2213/17 subclass pertains to memory cells that use nanowire transistors as their storage element, describing semiconductor structures where a nanowire channel forms the active part of a static memory device. It falls under the physics-information storage-static stores hierarchy and addresses designs for both volatile and non-volatile memory arrays.
[G11C 2213/18] Memory cell being a nanowire having RADIAL composition -- The CPC subclass G11C 2213/18 covers static-store memory cells built as nanowires whose material composition varies radially, meaning the core and outer layers are made from different substances to achieve specific electrical or magnetic properties for data retention. It belongs to the physics-information-storage hierarchy under static stores.
[G11C 2213/19] Memory cell comprising at least a nanowire and only two terminals -- G11C 2213/19 designates a memory cell that incorporates at least one nanowire and is equipped with only two electrical terminals. It is classified under static stores within information storage in the physics domain, covering nanowire-based two-terminal memory technologies.
[G11C 2213/30] Resistive cell, memory material aspects -- The CPC subclass G11C 2213/30 pertains to resistive cells employed in memory devices, emphasizing the material aspects that enable resistive switching for data storage. It is situated within the hierarchy physics → information storage → static stores and addresses the composition, structure and operation of such memory materials.
[G11C 2213/31] Material having complex metal oxide, e.g. perovskite structure -- Materials comprising complex metal oxides, notably perovskite-type crystals, are covered as static storage media for information storage. Their high dielectric constant, ferroelectric or multiferroic behavior enables non-volatile memory, capacitive storage and related devices. The classification addresses the composition, synthesis and use of such oxides in static data-holding applications.
[G11C 2213/32] Material having simple binary metal oxide structure -- The entry G11C 2213/32 defines materials composed of a simple binary metal oxide lattice that are employed in static information-storage devices. Such compounds, for example zinc oxide or titanium dioxide, serve as dielectric or charge-storage layers in non-volatile memory cells, offering particular electrical and thermal properties suited to static storage applications. This classification falls under Physics → Information Storage → Static Stores.
[G11C 2213/33] Material including silicon -- The classification G11C 2213/33 covers materials that contain silicon and are used in static information-storage devices. It pertains to the physical aspects of silicon-based media such as semiconductor memory cells or other non-volatile storage components. This entry falls under the hierarchy Physics → Information storage → Static stores.
[G11C 2213/34] Material includes an oxide or a nitride -- G11C 2213/34 covers static information storage devices that employ an oxide or nitride material as the storage medium, such as non-volatile semiconductor memories like EEPROM and flash. The classification falls under physics, information storage, static stores.
[G11C 2213/35] Material including carbon, e.g. graphite, grapheme -- G11C 2213/35 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system refers to materials used in static information storage devices that include carbon, such as graphite or graphene, highlighting their specific application and properties in memory technology.
[G11C 2213/50] Resistive cell structure aspects -- The G11C 2213/50 subclass deals with the physical structure of resistive cells used in static information-storage devices. It covers design features, materials and geometry of cells whose data state is represented by differing resistance levels, such as those employed in resistive RAM or similar non-volatile memory technologies.
[G11C 2213/51] Structure including a barrier layer preventing or limiting migration, diffusion of ions or charges or formation of electrolytes near an electrode -- G11C 2213/51 covers static information-storage devices that incorporate a barrier layer to impede or limit the migration of ions or charges and to prevent electrolyte formation at or near an electrode, thereby enhancing device stability and longevity.
[G11C 2213/52] Structure characterized by the electrode material, shape, etc. -- G11C 2213/52 is a CPC subclass that deals with the structural design of static electrochemical storage devices, such as batteries or capacitors, where the key features are defined by the electrode material, its shape, geometry and arrangement. It covers inventions that specify how these characteristics influence the overall device performance and integration.
[G11C 2213/53] Structure wherein the resistive material being in a transistor, e.g. gate -- G11C 2213/53 pertains to memory devices with a specific focus on structures where the resistive material is integrated into a transistor, such as the gate, enhancing functionality and performance.
[G11C 2213/54] Structure including a tunneling barrier layer, the memory effect implying the modification of tunnel barrier conductivity -- This subgroup covers memory devices where a tunneling barrier layer is part of the structure and the stored information is represented by a change in the barrier's conductivity. It includes designs such as resistive-switching or tunnel-junction cells where the barrier conductivity is altered by voltage or current pulses.
[G11C 2213/55] Structure including two electrodes, a memory active layer and at least two other layers which can be a passive or source or reservoir layer or a less doped memory active layer -- This classification concerns a layered memory device comprising two electrodes surrounding a memory-active layer together with at least two additional layers that may serve as a passive barrier, a source, a reservoir, or a less-doped version of the active material. The structure is used for static information storage in electro-optical or electronic systems.
[G11C 2213/56] Structure including two electrodes, a memory active layer and a so called passive or source or reservoir layer which is NOT an electrode, wherein the passive or source or reservoir layer is a source of ions which migrate afterwards in the memory active layer to be only trapped there, to form conductive filaments there or to react with the material of the memory active layer in redox way -- The entry covers a memory device comprising two electrodes, an active layer that stores information, and a separate passive or reservoir layer that is not an electrode but supplies ions. After programming, these ions migrate into the active layer where they become trapped to form conductive filaments or participate in redox reactions, enabling the switching of resistance states.
[G11C 2213/70] Resistive array aspects -- G11C2213/70 covers the design and operation of resistive memory arrays used for static data storage. It includes the structure, addressing, read/write methods and performance aspects of resistive cross-point or memristor arrays in solid-state storage devices.
[G11C 2213/71] Three dimensional array -- G11C 2213/71 covers three-dimensional array structures used for static information storage, where data is organized in a volumetric grid of cells rather than a planar layout. The code applies to storage media that exploit depth to increase capacity, such as multi-layered or bulk-addressable memory devices.
[G11C 2213/72] Array wherein the access device being a diode -- G11C 2213/72 covers static information-storage arrays in which each memory cell is accessed through a diode, typical of diode-selected read-only or programmable read-only memory structures. It is classified under physics → information storage → static stores.
[G11C 2213/73] Array where access device function, e.g. diode function, being merged with memorizing function of memory element -- The code covers arrays in which the device that provides access, such as a diode or similar selector, is combined with the element that stores data, creating a single component that both selects and remembers bits. This integration is used to improve density and simplify circuitry in static storage technologies.
[G11C 2213/74] Array wherein each memory cell has more than one access device -- G11C 2213/74 covers memory arrays in which each storage cell is equipped with multiple access devices such as read/write transistors or selectors. This architecture allows the cell to be accessed through more than one pathway, which can improve redundancy, speed, or enable multi-port operation. The classification falls under static stores in information storage physics.
[G11C 2213/75] Array having a NAND structure comprising, for example, memory cells in series or memory elements in series, a memory element being a memory cell in parallel with an access transistor -- G11C 2213/75 classifies solid-state memory arrays built with a NAND architecture, where memory cells or memory elements are connected in series and each cell is paired in parallel with an access transistor. This subclass falls under physics, information storage, static stores and covers designs of NAND flash or similar non-volatile memory structures.
[G11C 2213/76] Array using an access device for each cell which being not a transistor and not a diode -- The CPC code G11C 2213/76 covers arrays where each memory cell is accessed by a device that is neither a transistor nor a diode, such as optical, magnetic, or other non-semiconductor selectors. This classification falls under static storage technologies within information storage physics.
[G11C 2213/77] Array wherein the memory element being directly connected to the bit lines and word lines without any access device being used -- This CPC subclass covers memory arrays in which each storage element is directly attached to the bit and word lines, eliminating any separate access transistor or selector. Such designs are typical of static storage structures where the cell itself forms the connection to the addressing circuitry.
[G11C 2213/78] Array wherein the memory cells of a group share an access device, all the memory cells of the group having a common electrode and the access device being not part of a word line or a bit line driver -- This subclass covers memory arrays in which a group of cells is accessed through a shared device that is connected to a common electrode for all cells, while the shared device is not implemented as a conventional word-line or bit-line driver. The arrangement allows collective control of the group without separate line drivers for each cell, improving density and simplifying circuitry.
[G11C 2213/79] Array wherein the access device being a transistor -- This CPC subclass covers static memory arrays in which each storage cell is accessed through a transistor, typical of static random-access memory structures. It includes designs where the access device is a field-effect transistor that selects individual cells for reading or writing.
[G11C 2213/80] Array wherein the substrate, the cell, the conductors and the access device are all made up of organic materials -- G11C 2213/80 covers arrays for static data storage in which the substrate, the memory cells, the interconnecting conductors and the access devices are all formed from organic materials, enabling fully organic electronic storage structures within the physics-information-storage hierarchy.
[G11C 2213/81] Array wherein the array conductors, e.g. word lines, bit lines, are made of nanowires -- G11C 2213/81 relates to static information-storage arrays in which the conductive elements, such as word lines or bit lines, are fabricated from nanowire structures. Employing nanowires enables reduced feature dimensions and potentially higher speed or lower power consumption in memory devices.
[G11C 2213/82] Array having, for accessing a cell, a word line, a bit line and a plate or source line receiving different potentials -- This classification covers semiconductor memory arrays where each memory cell is accessed via a word line, a bit line and an additional plate or source line that receives a different potential, enabling multi-terminal read/write operations. It pertains to static storage devices that use the separate potentials to improve cell isolation, speed or power consumption.
[G11C 2216/00] Indexing scheme relating to -- The G11C 2216/00 class covers indexing schemes used to organize information stored in static media, such as magnetic, optical or other non-volatile devices. It includes methods for creating, managing and applying tables of contents, catalogs, metadata structures or other systematic descriptors that enable efficient retrieval of stored data.
[G11C 2216/02] Structural aspects of erasable programmable read-only memories -- G11C 2216/02 relates to the structural aspects of erasable programmable read-only memories, covering the physical architecture, cell design, material choices and layout techniques used to implement EEPROM and similar non-volatile storage devices. This subclass focuses on how the memory cells are organized and fabricated to allow repeated erasing and programming. It is situated under physics, information storage and static stores in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[G11C 2216/04] Nonvolatile memory cell provided with a separate control gate for erasing the cells, i.e. erase gate, independent of the normal read control gate -- This CPC subclass G11C 2216/04 falls under physics, information storage, static stores and covers nonvolatile memory cells that incorporate a dedicated control gate used solely for erasing, separate from the gate used for reading.
[G11C 2216/06] Floating gate cells in which the floating gate consists of multiple isolated silicon islands, e.g. nanocrystals -- G11C 2216/06 covers floating-gate memory cells whose charge-storing gate is formed from a plurality of isolated silicon islands, for example nanocrystals, rather than a single continuous layer. This structure is used in static information-storage devices such as flash memories to improve scaling, retention and endurance.
[G11C 2216/08] Nonvolatile memory wherein data storage is accomplished by storing relatively few electrons in the storage layer, i.e. single electron memory -- G11C 2216/08 relates to nonvolatile memory that stores data by trapping only a few electrons, typically a single electron, in the storage layer. This single-electron approach enables ultra-low power operation and very high density for static storage applications within the physics domain of information storage.
[G11C 2216/10] Floating gate memory cells with a single polysilicon layer -- The CPC entry G11C 2216/10 defines floating-gate memory cells that employ a single polysilicon layer as the charge-storage structure. It pertains to static information-storage devices and covers the design, fabrication and operation of such cells where the floating gate and control gate are formed from one polysilicon sheet, enabling compact, low-cost non-volatile memory.
[G11C 2216/12] Reading and writing aspects of erasable programmable read-only memories -- The CPC entry G11C 2216/12 covers the reading and writing processes for erasable programmable read-only memories, detailing how data are accessed, programmed and erased in non-volatile storage cells. It includes the electrical, timing and circuit techniques required to modify or retrieve information stored without power.
[G11C 2216/14] Circuits or methods to write a page or sector of information simultaneously into a nonvolatile memory, typically a complete row or word line in flash memory -- This classification covers circuit designs or writing methods that program an entire page or sector of a non-volatile memory device in a single operation, such as writing a whole row or word line of flash memory at once. It applies to static storage technologies within the physics field, focusing on simultaneous data programming techniques.
[G11C 2216/16] Flash programming of all the cells in an array, sector or block simultaneously -- The classification covers methods for programming flash memory where every cell in a selected array, sector or block is written at the same time. It addresses bulk write operations, timing control and data management for static storage devices in physics-based information storage.
[G11C 2216/18] Flash erasure of all the cells in an array, sector or block simultaneously -- G11C2216/18 covers methods for erasing entire flash memory structures in a single operation, such as clearing all cells of an array, sector, or block simultaneously. This enables rapid data deletion and simplifies memory management in static storage devices.
[G11C 2216/20] Suspension of programming or erasing cells in an array in order to read other cells in it -- G11C 2216/20 pertains to methods that temporarily suspend the programming or erasing of specific cells within a static storage array so that other cells can be read without interference. It includes techniques for pausing write or erase operations to enable reliable read-out of neighboring memory locations.
[G11C 2216/22] Nonvolatile memory in which reading can be carried out from one memory bank or array whilst a word or sector in another bank or array is being erased or programmed simultaneously -- The classification covers nonvolatile memory devices that allow a read operation from one memory bank or array while a different bank or array is being erased or programmed at the same time. This capability enables concurrent access and improves performance in static storage technologies.
[G11C 2216/24] Nonvolatile memory in which programming can be carried out in one memory bank or array whilst a word or sector in another bank or array is being erased simultaneously -- This subclass covers non-volatile memory devices that enable concurrent operations, allowing data to be programmed in one memory bank or array while a different bank or array is erased at the same time. The arrangement improves throughput and reduces latency in static storage systems.
[G11C 2216/26] Floating gate memory which is adapted to be one-time programmable [OTP], e.g. containing multiple OTP blocks permitting limited update ability -- A floating-gate memory device designed for one-time programming (OTP) that can contain several OTP blocks, allowing a limited number of post-program updates. It belongs to static storage technologies within information storage and physics, and is classified under CPC entry G11C 2216/26.
[G11C 2216/28] Floating gate memory programmed by reverse programming, e.g. programmed with negative gate voltage and erased with positive gate voltage or programmed with high source or drain voltage and erased with high gate voltage -- This subclass covers floating-gate memory cells that are programmed by applying a voltage of opposite polarity to that used for erasing, such as writing with a negative gate voltage and erasing with a positive one, or using a high source/drain voltage for programming and a high gate voltage for erasing. The reverse-programming approach is intended to improve write speed, endurance, or device architecture in non-volatile storage technologies.
[G11C 2216/30] Reduction of number of input/output pins by using a serial interface to transmit or receive addresses or data, i.e. serial access memory -- G11C 2216/30 covers methods that lower the count of input/output pins on static memory devices by using a serial interface to convey addresses or data, enabling serial access memory. This subclass falls under physics - information storage - static stores.
[G11C 2229/00] Indexing scheme relating to checking stores for correct operation, subsequent repair or testing stores during standby or offline operation -- G11C 2229/00 relates to indexing methods that enable the monitoring, verification and maintenance of static storage devices, allowing checks of correct operation and facilitating repair or testing while the stores are in standby or offline mode. It defines how data can be organized to support integrity checks, fault detection and maintenance without requiring the device to be actively used.
[G11C 2229/70] Indexing scheme relating to -- In the Cooperative Patent Classification (EPO/USPTO) this entry, G11C 2229/70, falls under PHYSICS - INFORMATION STORAGE - STATIC STORES and concerns indexing schemes used to verify, diagnose, or test static data stores while they are in standby or offline operation.
[G11C 2229/72] Location of redundancy information -- The class G11C 2229/72 covers techniques for determining where redundant data is placed within static information-storage devices so that the stored data can be verified, repaired or tested while the device is in standby or offline. It pertains to checking the correct operation of static stores and locating redundancy information for maintenance or diagnostic purposes.
[G11C 2229/723] Redundancy information stored in a part of the memory core to be repaired -- **Definition:** This place covers:  See US2006190762 (figure 1 - shown), US2009067276.
[G11C 2229/726] Redundancy information loaded from the outside into the memory -- **Definition:** This place covers:  See US2008307251 (fig.1), US2009072886 (fig.1 - shown).
[G11C 2229/74] Time at which the repair is done -- G11C 2229/74 concerns the timing of repairs for static information-storage units, specifying when maintenance or testing is performed during standby or offline periods to verify correct operation.
[G11C 2229/743] After packaging -- G11C 2229/743 - After packaging concerns procedures for verifying the correct operation of static information storage devices once they have been packaged, including testing, maintenance and repair during standby or offline periods. The classification falls under physics, information storage, static stores and addresses post-packaging checks to ensure reliability.
[G11C 2229/746] Before packaging -- **Definition:** This place covers:  See US2006221729, figure 2.
[G11C 2229/76] Storage technology used for the repair -- G11C 2229/76 relates to technologies for monitoring and maintaining static information storage devices, focusing on methods that check correct operation, perform repairs, or test the stores while they are in standby or offline mode. It addresses procedures and equipment used to ensure reliable performance of such storage systems.
[G11C 2229/763] E-fuses, e.g. electric fuses or antifuses, floating gate transistors -- The entry covers electronic fuses and antifuses such as floating-gate transistors used to protect or program static memory devices, and includes methods for checking, testing, or repairing these stores during standby or offline operation.
[G11C 2229/766] Laser fuses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  See US2008180983 (figure 2), US2006221729 (figure 3).
[G11C 23/00] Digital stores characterised by movement of mechanical parts to effect storage, e.g. using balls -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Micromechanical and nanomechanical systems for data storage.  **Glossary:** - MEMS: Microelectromechanical systems - NEMS: Nanoelectromechanical systems
[G11C 25/00] Digital stores characterised by the use of flowing media -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Digital stores or memories whose operation is based on fluid or liquid media.
[G11C 27/00] Electric analogue stores, e.g. for storing instantaneous values -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Static stores or memories comprising:
[G11C 27/005] with non-volatile charge storage, e.g. on floating gate or MNOS -- G11C 27/005 covers static electric analogue storage devices that retain charge without power, i.e., non-volatile charge storage. It includes technologies such as floating-gate transistors and metal-nitride-oxide-semiconductor (MNOS) structures used for preserving instantaneous values in memory applications.
[G11C 27/02] Sample-and-hold arrangements -- G11C 27/02 encompasses sample-and-hold circuits that temporarily store an analog voltage representing an instantaneous signal value for later use. These electric analogue stores are employed in data converters and signal-conditioning stages to capture a snapshot of a waveform before further processing.
[G11C 27/022] using a magnetic memory element -- G11C 27/022 classifies technologies that use magnetic memory elements to store instantaneous analog electrical quantities, such as voltage or current, in static, non-volatile storage devices. It covers implementations where the magnetic state represents the analog value for later retrieval, forming part of the broader group of electric analogue stores within information-storage physics.
[G11C 27/024] using a capacitive memory element  G11C27/04  takes precedence -- This subclass covers technologies that employ capacitors as memory elements to retain data, such as charge-based storage, within the broader category of static electric analogue stores. It is a subcategory of G11C27/04, which addresses capacitive storage concepts.
[G11C 27/026] associated with an amplifier  G11C27/028  takes precedence -- G11C 27/026 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to electric analogue stores, specifically those associated with an amplifier. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with static information storage within physics and is used by both the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[G11C 27/028] Current mode circuits, e.g. switched current memories -- The CPC subclass G11C 27/028 covers circuit designs that store information by keeping currents flowing instead of voltages, such as switched-current memory cells. It includes current-mode architectures used for static analogue storage of instantaneous values in electric analogue stores.
[G11C 27/04] Shift registers -- Shift registers are sequential digital circuits that move bits through a chain of storage elements, acting as static electric analogue stores that hold instantaneous values as the data is shifted. They are used for timing, data serialization, and temporary buffering in electronic systems.
[G11C 29/00] Checking stores for correct operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The two main fields Test and Repair of semiconductor memories.  Concerning Test: This group covers test in particular  Repair of memories is found below at G11C29/70  **Glossary:** - Scrubbing: Background test - Automatic test machine: External tester - Monitoring: Observation of normal operation - Scrambling, mapping: Translation of a logic address in a physical address or vice versa
[G11C 29/003] in serial memories -- G11C 29/003 relates to techniques for testing serial memory devices to confirm they operate correctly, encompassing error detection and verification procedures applied to static storage systems that retain data in a sequential manner.
[G11C 29/006] at wafer scale level, i.e. wafer scale integration [WSI] -- The code G11C 29/006 covers methods and apparatus for checking the correct operation of static memory stores that are implemented at the wafer-scale level, known as wafer-scale integration. It pertains to testing and verification techniques applied to large-area memory arrays fabricated on a single semiconductor wafer.
[G11C 29/02] Detection or location of defective auxiliary circuits, e.g. defective refresh counters -- G11C 29/02 deals with methods and apparatus for detecting or locating faulty auxiliary circuits such as defective refresh counters in static data-storage devices, ensuring the correct operation of magnetic or semiconductor storage elements. It falls under the hierarchy PHYSICS → INFORMATION STORAGE → STATIC STORES → Checking stores for correct operation.
[G11C 29/021] in voltage or current generators -- The G11C 29/021 subclass covers techniques for verifying the proper operation of static information-storage cells by using voltage or current generators to test the stored data. It includes methods that apply controlled electrical signals to a memory element and detect its response to confirm correct storage. This is used in checking stores for correct operation within the broader physics and information-storage categories.
[G11C 29/022] in I/O circuitry -- G11C 29/022 pertains to the testing and verification of static information storage devices, specifically focusing on input/output (I/O) circuitry. This classification is used for methods and apparatuses designed to ensure the correct operation of I/O circuits in memory systems.
[G11C 29/023] in clock generator or timing circuitry -- G11C 29/023 covers techniques for monitoring static memory elements in clock generators or timing circuits to ensure they function correctly, including error detection, self-test and status-checking of registers, counters or ROMs used for timing control.
[G11C 29/024] in decoders -- G11C 29/024 covers techniques for verifying that static storage elements within decoders operate correctly, such as checking read/write cycles and data retrieval functions to ensure proper performance.
[G11C 29/025] in signal lines -- This group covers methods and apparatus for checking the proper operation of static storage cells via the signal lines that address them, including techniques for reading, testing, or verifying stored data or cell condition through those lines.
[G11C 29/026] in sense amplifiers -- G11C 29/026 relates to sense amplifiers, the circuits that read the small voltage or current differences from static-memory cells and amplify them to logical levels for processing. These devices are used in static storage technologies such as DRAM and SRAM to verify correct operation of stored bits.
[G11C 29/027] in fuses -- G11C 29/027 addresses methods and apparatus for testing the condition of fuses employed in static data-storage devices, ensuring the fuses function correctly and the stored information remains reliable. It pertains to checking stores for correct operation by examining fuse integrity.
[G11C 29/028] with adaption or trimming of parameters -- G11C 29/028 covers techniques for verifying the proper functioning of static information-storage media in which the checking process incorporates adaptation or trimming of operating parameters. It falls under physics → information storage → static stores → checking stores for correct operation.
[G11C 29/04] Detection or location of defective memory elements -- It covers techniques for testing static memory devices by detecting or pinpointing faulty cells, using electrical or logical methods to verify correct operation. This includes built-in self-test, error-checking circuits and diagnostic algorithms for identifying defective storage elements.
[G11C 29/06] Acceleration testing -- G11C 29/06 covers methods for checking the proper operation of static information-storage devices by subjecting them to acceleration forces. It includes drop, vibration, shock and other mechanical-stress tests that assess a medium's robustness and data integrity under dynamic loading conditions.
[G11C 29/08] Functional testing, e.g. testing during refresh, power-on self testing [POST] or distributed testing -- G11C 29/08 covers functional testing of static information storage devices, such as verifying correct operation during refresh cycles, power-on self-tests (POST), or through distributed testing methods. It focuses on checking that the stored data and device behavior meet required performance and reliability standards.
[G11C 29/10] Test algorithms, e.g. memory scan [MScan] algorithms -- G11C 29/10 covers algorithms that test static memory devices for correct operation, including memory-scan routines that search for and identify errors or faulty cells. These methods are used to verify data integrity and performance of storage stores.
[G11C 29/12] Built-in arrangements for testing, e.g. built-in self testing [BIST] -- This CPC subclass covers built-in mechanisms for verifying the correct operation of static information storage devices, such as self-test circuits integrated into memory arrays. It includes designs that automatically check data integrity and functionality without the need for external testing equipment.
[G11C 29/12005] comprising voltage or current generators -- This CPC subgroup covers static information storage devices that are checked for correct operation by applying voltage or current generators. It includes testing and diagnostic arrangements that use electrical sources to verify the state of memory cells or other non-volatile stores.
[G11C 29/1201] comprising I/O circuitry -- G11C 29/1201 concerns static information storage devices that incorporate input-output circuitry for verifying that stored data are correctly read or written. The classification covers mechanisms that monitor and test the operation of memory cells during access.
[G11C 29/12015] comprising clock generation or timing circuitry -- A subclass of static-store checking methods that verifies correct operation of information-storage devices, specifically using clock-generation or timing circuitry to monitor and control the stored data. This code applies to patents describing timing-based diagnostics for static memory systems.
[G11C 29/14] Implementation of control logic, e.g. test mode decoders -- G11C 29/14 covers the implementation of control logic used for testing static information-storage devices, such as circuits that decode test-mode signals, generate test patterns, or monitor read/write operations to verify correct functioning of the storage elements. It is applied in methods that check stores for proper operation.
[G11C 29/16] using microprogrammed units, e.g. state machines -- The G11C 29/16 class covers techniques that employ microprogrammed units, such as state machines, to monitor and verify the proper functioning of static information-storage devices. It is used for checking stored data or system states to ensure correct operation.
[G11C 29/18] Address generation devices -- Address generation devices are circuits or logic units that produce memory addresses for accessing data stored in static storage elements such as ROM or RAM. They translate program instructions or control signals into specific location identifiers, enabling reliable reading, writing, or verification of stored information.
[G11C 29/20] using counters or linear-feedback shift registers [LFSR] -- G11C 29/20 covers methods that use digital counters or linear-feedback shift registers to test and monitor static information storage devices, ensuring correct operation by generating predictable patterns, detecting errors or verifying read/write integrity.
[G11C 29/22] Accessing serial memories -- G11C 29/22 covers methods and apparatus for accessing serial memories, a subcategory of static storage devices used to retrieve data stored in a sequential format. It includes techniques for reading, writing, and managing address sequencing in these memory arrays, often to verify correct operation or to perform data retrieval in information-storage systems.
[G11C 29/24] Accessing extra cells, e.g. dummy cells or redundant cells -- G11C 29/24 covers methods and apparatus for accessing additional memory cells such as dummy or redundant cells in static storage devices, enabling verification, replacement or error-checking of primary storage locations. The techniques are used to test or recover data by reading or writing to these extra cells.
[G11C 29/26] Accessing multiple arrays -- G11C 29/26 concerns techniques for reading and writing data in static memory structures where several arrays are involved, focusing on methods that verify correct operation of the storage while accessing multiple arrays simultaneously. It falls under physics-based information storage and the checking of store functionality.
[G11C 29/28] Dependent multiple arrays, e.g. multi-bit arrays -- G11C 29/28 classifies static memory systems that use several dependent storage cells arranged as multi-bit arrays to verify correct operation of stored data. The code applies to technologies that check the integrity of static stores by reading multiple linked bits simultaneously.
[G11C 29/30] Accessing single arrays -- G11C 29/30 covers techniques for accessing individual cells or locations in a single, static storage array, emphasizing the read-out or verification of stored data. It pertains to methods that check a memory store's correct operation by retrieving specific entries. This falls under physics → information storage → static stores → checking stores for correct operation.
[G11C 29/32] Serial access -- G11C 29/32 defines serial access methods for static storage media, where data are read or written in a sequential order to verify correct operation of the store. The class falls under checking stores for correct operation within physics-information storage.
[G11C 29/34] Accessing multiple bits simultaneously -- G11C 29/34 covers techniques for reading several stored bits at the same time in static memory devices. It includes parallel access methods, multi-bit sensing circuits and error-checking procedures that verify correct operation of the store.
[G11C 29/36] Data generation devices, e.g. data inverters -- The entry covers devices that generate or convert data signals for use with static information stores, such as data inverters that regenerate or adapt stored bits in order to verify that the storage element is operating correctly.
[G11C 29/38] Response verification devices -- G11C 29/38 covers devices that verify the correct operation of static data stores by checking the stored information and confirming that the response matches the expected result. These response verification devices monitor readout signals, compare them to reference values and signal any errors, ensuring reliable performance of memory or other static storage systems.
[G11C 29/40] using compression techniques -- G11C 29/40 covers the use of data compression techniques in the verification of static information storage devices, including methods that compress data to test integrity, detect errors, or reduce the size of test patterns during operation checks.
[G11C 29/42] using error correcting codes [ECC] or parity check -- The code G11C 29/42 addresses methods that employ error-correcting codes or parity-check techniques to verify and maintain the correct operation of static information storage devices, encompassing algorithms and hardware that detect and correct data errors to ensure integrity.
[G11C 29/44] Indication or identification of errors, e.g. for repair -- G11C 29/44 covers methods and devices that detect and signal errors in static information-storage media during checking operations, allowing repair or corrective action. It is positioned under physics, information storage, static stores, and checking stores for correct operation.
[G11C 29/4401] for self repair -- G11C 29/4401 covers self-repair methods applied to static information storage devices, enabling the system to detect faults and automatically restore proper operation. It includes mechanisms that monitor storage integrity and initiate corrective actions without external intervention, improving reliability for memory and other passive data stores.
[G11C 29/46] Test trigger logic -- The G11C 29/46 subclass covers methods and circuitry for testing the trigger logic that monitors static storage devices to ensure they operate correctly, typically by detecting fault conditions and initiating diagnostic actions. It falls under the physics of information storage, specifically the verification of static stores.
[G11C 29/48] Arrangements in static stores specially adapted for testing by means external to the store, e.g. using direct memory access [DMA] or using auxiliary access paths -- G11C 29/48 relates to arrangements in static memory stores that are specially adapted for external testing, such as using direct memory access or auxiliary access paths to verify correct operation of the stored data.
[G11C 29/50] Marginal testing, e.g. race, voltage or current testing -- The G11C 29/50 code covers methods for checking static data stores to ensure they operate correctly, focusing on marginal-condition testing such as race-condition detection, voltage level verification, or current monitoring. It belongs to the physics-based hierarchy of information-storage → static stores.
[G11C 29/50004] of threshold voltage -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11C 29/50004 pertains to the checking of static information storage devices for correct operation, specifically focusing on methods or apparatus related to the threshold voltage. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with the physical aspects of information storage and retrieval systems.
[G11C 29/50008] of impedance -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry G11C 29/50008 pertains to the checking of static information storage devices for correct operation, specifically focusing on the measurement or testing of impedance within these stores. This classification is part of a broader category that deals with various aspects of information storage physics and technology.
[G11C 29/50012] of timing -- G11C 29/50012 pertains to the verification of timing in static information storage devices, ensuring that data operations occur at the correct intervals. This classification is part of a broader category focused on checking the operational integrity of memory stores.
[G11C 29/50016] of retention -- G11C 29/50016 covers techniques for verifying that a static storage element retains its data correctly over time. It relates to methods that check the retention characteristics of memory cells or other non-volatile storage devices to ensure reliable operation.
[G11C 29/52] Protection of memory contents -- The G11C 29/52 class comprises methods and devices that ensure stored digital information remains unaltered and confidential, including encryption, error-detecting codes, and tamper-proof mechanisms. It falls under static memory stores and focuses on verifying the correct operation of the storage.
[G11C 29/54] Arrangements for designing test circuits, e.g. design for test [DFT] tools -- G11C 29/54 covers arrangements that facilitate the design of test circuits for static storage devices, including design-for-test tools that generate built-in test structures and control logic to verify correct operation of memory cells.
[G11C 29/56] External testing equipment for static stores, e.g. automatic test equipment [ATE] -- External testing equipment for static stores, such as automatic test equipment, comprises devices that verify the correct operation of non-volatile memory or other static storage elements by applying stimuli, measuring responses, and diagnosing faults. This category covers test systems used during manufacture, maintenance or quality control to ensure data integrity and performance of static storage devices.
[G11C 29/56004] Pattern generation -- The CPC subclass G11C 29/56004 covers methods for generating test patterns used to verify the correct operation of static information storage devices such as ROMs and other non-volatile memories. It focuses on creating data patterns that can be applied to check stored bits for faults during manufacturing or maintenance.
[G11C 29/56008] Error analysis, representation of errors -- The code covers techniques for analyzing and modelling errors in static information-storage devices, focusing on methods that detect, characterize and represent faults to ensure correct operation. It includes error-pattern identification, statistical representation of error distributions and strategies for corrective handling.
[G11C 29/56012] Timing aspects, clock generation, synchronisation -- G11C 29/56012 covers the timing aspects of static data-store checking, including the generation of clocks and synchronization techniques that ensure correct operation of storage devices. It pertains to methods and circuitry that create precise timing signals and coordinate them during verification of stored information.
[G11C 29/56016] Apparatus features -- G11C 29/56016 refers to apparatus features used to examine static information storage devices and confirm they operate correctly. It encompasses hardware elements that perform checks on memory cells, registers or other non-volatile stores to detect errors or verify data integrity.
[G11C 29/70] Masking faults in memories by using spares or by reconfiguring -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Repairing defective memory devices by using redundant (spare) elements; repairing defective memory devices by reconfiguring the address space (this implies a reduced memory capacity compared with a non-defective device); algorithms for effecting such repairs.
[G11C 29/702] by replacing auxiliary circuits, e.g. spare voltage generators, decoders or sense amplifiers, to be used instead of defective ones -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: EP1141834, figure 1.
[G11C 29/72] with optimized replacement algorithms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Replacement algorithms (failure map to redundancy implementation ).
[G11C 29/74] using duplex memories, i.e. using dual copies -- The G11C 29/74 subclass covers techniques that employ duplex, or dual-copy, memories in static information-storage devices to verify correct operation. It includes methods that write identical data to two separate locations and compare them during readout or testing to detect errors and ensure data integrity.
[G11C 29/76] using address translation or modifications -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Both with and without dedicated spares - typically address translations via pointers.  Techniques used for redundancy in this kind of applications are always very similar and almost all of them involve sector mapping, counting of read/erase operations, wear-out detection, etc. Many of these documents include lower level features related to flash memories (flash).
[G11C 29/765] in solid state disks -- G11C 29/765 relates to techniques for verifying the correct operation of solid-state disks, a type of static information-storage device. It falls under physics, specifically the sub-field of checking stores for proper functioning.
[G11C 29/78] using programmable devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Could include disconnecting faulty elements.
[G11C 29/781] combined in a redundant decoder -- The G11C 29/781 subclass covers systems that use a redundant decoder to verify the correct operation of static information stores, comparing multiple decoder outputs or employing duplicate decoding logic to detect and correct errors in stored data. This approach enhances reliability of memory checking processes.
[G11C 29/783] with refresh of replacement cells, e.g. in DRAMs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In DRAMS, how to refresh the redundant lines which are substituting faulty lines.
[G11C 29/785] with redundancy programming schemes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuse related issues; most of these documents have descriptions at transistor level, explaining how the fuse circuits are built or how they are included in the redundancy decoding elements.
[G11C 29/787] using a fuse hierarchy -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In this case, a master fuse can be used to decide whether a redundant line or decoder is in use. This allows to save time when programming the fuses. See EP646866, fig.3 (reference 50).
[G11C 29/789] using non-volatile cells or latches -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In these documents, the "fuse" is not a fuse as such, but rather a non-volatile memory cell or a combination of a fuse with a latch to retain its status (i.e. the fuse is only read at power up to reduce stress thereon). See US5619469, fig2.
[G11C 29/80] with improved layout -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Under this group, techniques which tend to reduce real estate by several means. When the document is a pure layout description - i.e. without a clear functionality other than a "new layout" - it is included in this 'head group'. It should be clear that there is a clear "layout" approach in the description and figures.
[G11C 29/802] by encoding redundancy signals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transmission of faulty/redundant addresses between the fuse boxes and the final decoders is done by encoding: For 2n redundant lines only n lines have to be routed. See EP496282, fig.2.
[G11C 29/804] to prevent clustered faults -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Faults which involve several (adjacent) lines can be solved in a simplified way, 'simplified' implying using a reduced amount of coding or storage for the defect. These kind of faults are very frequent due to impurities of a big size. See US5281868, fig. 3,4.
[G11C 29/806] by reducing size of decoders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solutions that might have a lower redundancy efficiency (e.g. lines can only be replaced in sets of four, regardless of the fault) but allows to have fewer and/or smaller comparators. See EP239196, fig 3.
[G11C 29/808] using a flexible replacement scheme -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All kinds of solutions in which different combinations of faulty/redundant replacement are possible to allow for a higher level of repair with a lower number of spares. For instance, when redundant lines in a block can be used to substitute faulty lines in any other block, or when the same redundant line can be used for row or column repair, etc. See US5469388 fig 4A.
[G11C 29/81] using a hierarchical redundancy scheme -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Faults are solved in a hierarchical way. For instance: lines are replaced with redundant lines in a given block or, if not possible, then the complete block is repaired with a redundant block as a whole unit, etc. See US5295101, fig. 7.
[G11C 29/812] using a reduced amount of fuses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The amount of real estate occupied by fuses is lower.
[G11C 29/814] for optimized yield -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The layout is done in such a way that a certain cost function associated with the yield is optimized. A lot of NPL and thesis-like stuff. See XP237814.
[G11C 29/816] for an application-specific layout -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Certain applications (ROMs, synchronous memories, cache memories) demand redundancy solutions which are very related to the nature of the application itself and therefore more likely to be relevant in an application-oriented context.
[G11C 29/818] for dual-port memories -- **Definition:** This place covers:  For the case of Dual port RAMs, video memories and the like.
[G11C 29/82] for EEPROMs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  These redundancy solutions are usually taking into account the block erase operations as well as the aging of EEPROM cells due to program/erase. Many of these documents are also classified with the solid state disks solutions ( G11C29/765 ). Normally documents put in this group work at a lower level (i.e. row/columns in a block, rather than sector mapping level.
[G11C 29/822] for read only memories -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Redundancy solutions for Mask ROMs which have specific layouts.
[G11C 29/824] for synchronous memories -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Same as above for the case of synchronous memories. Timing (synchronicity) requirements as well as the specific (i.e. sequential) nature of read/write operations are linked to some redundancy requirements.
[G11C 29/83] with reduced power consumption -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Most of the solutions for this problem are related to the avoidance of DC faults.
[G11C 29/832] with disconnection of faulty elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A common way to reduce power consumption in these cases.
[G11C 29/835] with roll call arrangements for redundant substitutions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Roll call circuits to identify redundancy substitutions.
[G11C 29/838] with substitution of defective spares -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solving the problem of a faulty spare element which must be disabled for redundant substitution.
[G11C 29/84] with improved access time or stability -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In this group, solutions which propose to eliminate or reduce the difference in speed between a non-faulty and a faulty line selection. Many of these documents are actually addressing stability problems (e.g. obtain a reliable voltage level before sampling fuse values) employing similar solutions such as ATDs.
[G11C 29/842] by introducing a delay in a signal path -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The trick here usually is to insert a delay element in the faster path to make sure both paths (normal and redundant) are equally slow. This delay element usually consists of slower transistors in the path or a chain of inverters. See US5777931, fig.5.
[G11C 29/844] by splitting the decoders in stages -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A normal/redundant selection signal is already obtained at a predecoding stage which allows to start some kind of preselection. See US5550776, fig.4.
[G11C 29/846] by choosing redundant lines at an output stage -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Usually the case with column redundancy: Addresses are input simultaneously to faulty and normal column decoders and only at the output (usually by means of a multiplexer) it is decided whether a redundant replacement is pertinent. See US4473895, fig.1.
[G11C 29/848] by adjacent switching -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Columns are shifted one or more position skipping the faulty one(s). There are redundant columns at the end of the chain. See EP434200, fig. 4.
[G11C 29/86] in serial access memories, e.g. shift registers, CCDs, bubble memories -- This subclass addresses methods for checking the correct operation of serial access memory devices, such as shift registers, charge-coupled devices and bubble memories, by monitoring data integrity, timing and control signals during serial read/write cycles.
[G11C 29/88] with partially good memories -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Partially good memories, degraded memories.
[G11C 29/883] using a single defective memory device with reduced capacity, e.g. half capacity -- The classification covers techniques that employ a single memory module that is partially defective, operating it at its reduced, for example half, capacity while still performing functional checks of the storage device. Such methods allow continued use of flawed hardware for testing or limited applications without requiring full replacement.
[G11C 29/886] combining plural defective memory devices to provide a contiguous address range, e.g. one device supplies working blocks to replace defective blocks in another device -- The classification G11C 29/886 addresses techniques that join several memory devices, including ones with defects, to form a single contiguous address range, allowing functional blocks in one device to replace faulty blocks in another. This falls under physics-information storage-static stores-checking stores for correct operation, focusing on integrating defective memories to maintain reliable operation.
