[G11C 5/00] Details of stores covered by group -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of arrangements providing supporting functions for semiconductor memory devices, concerning protection against loss of information, memory layout and stacking, signal line and power line interconnection, memory modules and their electrical interconnections, and power supplies including backup supplies, as well as charge pumps, voltage and current reference generators as well as circuits for stabilization of voltages and currents, which are common to all semiconductor memories types covered by subclass G11C , as detailed in main groups G11C13/00 , G11C14/00 , G11C16/00 , G11C17/00 , G11C19/00 , G11C21/00 , G11C23/00 , G11C25/00 , G11C27/00 as well as G11C11/00 .
[G11C 5/005] Circuit means for protection against loss of information of semiconductor storage devices -- G11C 5/005 covers circuits designed to protect semiconductor memory cells from data loss, including error-detecting and correcting logic, redundancy schemes, backup registers and refresh mechanisms that detect faults or power-fail events and preserve or restore stored information.
[G11C 5/02] Disposition of storage elements, e.g. in the form of a matrix array -- G11C 5/02 pertains to the arrangement of storage elements within static information storage devices, such as the layout of cells in a matrix array. The code covers the physical positioning and organization of bits or cells to optimize access or density in non-volatile memory structures.
[G11C 5/025] Geometric lay-out considerations of storage- and peripheral-blocks in a semiconductor storage device  geometrical lay-out of the components in integrated circuits, geometrical lay-out of the components in integrated circuits  H10D89/10 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Geometrical layout considerations of the internal components of a memory device.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Geometrical lay-out of the components in integrated circuits -> H10D89/10
[G11C 5/04] Supports for storage elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electrical aspects of memory modules such as e.g. SIMM, DIMM or flash memories.
[G11C 5/05] Supporting of cores in matrix -- G11C 5/05 classifies storage media in which data-holding cores are embedded in and supported by a surrounding matrix, a design used to provide structural stability and protect the active elements in static information-storage devices. This includes magnetic or optical media where the cores are fixed within a polymer, glass or other solid material to maintain alignment and reliability.
[G11C 5/06] Arrangements for interconnecting storage elements electrically, e.g. by wiring -- G11C 5/06 covers the electrical connection schemes used to link static storage elements such as capacitors, batteries or similar devices, including wiring layouts and interconnect architectures that enable charge transfer and control within a storage system.
[G11C 5/063] Voltage and signal distribution in integrated semi-conductor memory access lines, e.g. word-line, bit-line, cross-over resistance, propagation delay -- This CPC subclass deals with the design and analysis of voltage and signal distribution networks in integrated circuit memory, focusing on word-line and bit-line routing, their resistive coupling, cross-over effects and timing delays. It covers methods to mitigate propagation delay and ensure reliable access.
[G11C 5/066] Means for reducing external access-lines for a semiconductor memory clip, e.g. by multiplexing at least address and data signals -- In static semiconductor memory devices, G11C 5/066 covers techniques that lessen the number of external access lines by combining address and data signals onto shared conductors, typically through multiplexing, thereby simplifying chip packaging and reducing pin count for memory clips.
[G11C 5/08] for interconnecting magnetic elements, e.g. toroidal cores -- G11C 5/08 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers arrangements for interconnecting magnetic elements, such as toroidal cores, within static magnetic storage devices. The code applies to the physical construction of magnetic circuits that link these elements to form a functional storage medium.
[G11C 5/10] for interconnecting capacitors -- A G11C 5/10 invention relates to arrangements or circuits that connect two or more capacitors within a static storage device, such as a charge-based memory or energy-storage cell. The focus is on the interconnection techniques that enable the capacitors to function together for storing or manipulating information.
[G11C 5/12] Apparatus or processes for interconnecting storage elements, e.g. for threading magnetic cores -- G11C 5/12 covers devices and methods that link individual storage elements together, for example the threading of magnetic cores in static magnetic memory arrays. The classification falls under physics - information storage - static stores and addresses the physical interconnect architecture that enables coherent data retention across multiple elements.
[G11C 5/14] Power supply arrangements -- G11C 5/14 covers the design and configuration of power supply systems for static information storage devices, such as solid-state, magnetic, or optical media that retain data without motion. The code includes provisions for voltage regulation, backup sources, and power management circuitry that ensure reliable operation of non-volatile storage units.
[G11C 5/141] Battery and back-up supplies -- The G11C 5/141 subgroup deals with batteries and other backup power supplies that provide energy to static information-storage devices, ensuring continued operation or data preservation during main-power interruptions. It includes the design and integration of such supplies with storage media and their control circuitry.
[G11C 5/142] Contactless power supplies, e.g. RF, induction, or IR -- G11C 5/142 covers devices that deliver electrical energy without a physical connection, using methods such as radio-frequency fields, inductive coupling or infrared radiation. It includes the design, control and application of these contactless power supplies for charging or powering equipment.
[G11C 5/143] Detection of memory cassette insertion or removal; Continuity checks of supply or ground lines; Detection of supply variations, interruptions or levels  G11C5/148  takes precedence ; Switching between alternative supplies  G11C5/141  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Covering the detection of change in supply voltage, on the voltage or the ground side, in general.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Battery and back-up supplies -> G11C5/141 - Details of power up or power down circuits, standby circuits or recovery circuits -> G11C5/148
[G11C 5/144] Detection of predetermined disconnection or reduction of power supply, e.g. power down or power standby -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Voluntary power down or standby arrangements.
[G11C 5/145] Applications of charge pumps; Boosted voltage circuits; Clamp circuits therefor  G11C5/141  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Battery and back-up supplies -> G11C5/141
[G11C 5/146] Substrate bias generators  G11C5/141  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Battery and back-up supplies -> G11C5/141
[G11C 5/147] Voltage reference generators, voltage or current regulators; Internally lowered supply levels; Compensation for voltage drops  G11C5/141  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Battery and back-up supplies -> G11C5/141
[G11C 5/148] Details of power up or power down circuits, standby circuits or recovery circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Covering the characteristics of the power up and power down circuits, the standby circuits and recovery circuits.
[G11C 7/00] Arrangements for writing information into, or reading information out from, a digital store -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All aspects of reading and writing of data to an address memory cell in general except the addressing of the cell. The addressing and row circuitry is covered in G11C8/00 . G11C7/00 is more about column and input/output circuitry "In general" means that technology or cell specific documents are not covered here but in their respective groups.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Details of stores -> G11C5/00 - Auxiliary circuits for stores using semiconductor devices -> G11C11/4063 , G11C11/413
[G11C 7/005] with combined beam-and individual cell access -- The G11C 7/005 subclass covers arrangements that access a static digital storage medium using both a combined beam, which can address multiple storage cells at once, and an individual-cell beam that targets single cells for precise reading or writing. This hybrid approach is used to improve speed or efficiency in optical or other non-volatile memory systems.
[G11C 7/02] with means for avoiding parasitic signals -- G11C 7/02 covers arrangements in static digital storage devices that incorporate measures to suppress unwanted parasitic signals during write or read operations, such as shielding, grounding, or signal-conditioning techniques that prevent interference from affecting data integrity.
[G11C 7/04] with means for avoiding disturbances due to temperature effects -- G11C 7/04 covers arrangements for writing information to or reading it from digital static storage devices that incorporate features to avoid temperature-induced disturbances, such as thermal compensation, temperature-controlled environments or low-thermal-sensitivity materials.
[G11C 7/06] Sense amplifiers -- G11C 7/06 covers sense amplifiers used in static digital memory devices to detect and amplify the small electrical signals that represent stored bits during read operations. These components are part of the arrangements for writing information into, or reading information out from, a digital store.
[G11C 7/062] Differential amplifiers of non-latching type, e.g. comparators, long-tailed pairs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Most of the current sense amplifiers (current mirrors based) and most of the multi stage sense amplifiers.
[G11C 7/065] Differential amplifiers of latching type -- The code G11C 7/065 relates to differential amplifiers employed in latching circuits for static digital memory. Such amplifiers boost differential signals to set or release the latch that stores a bit, thereby controlling the write and read actions of the memory cell.
[G11C 7/067] Single-ended amplifiers -- Single-ended amplifiers are circuits having one active input and one grounded reference, employed in the read/write paths of static digital storage devices such as magnetic or optical memories. The classification covers their design, biasing, and integration for reliable signal amplification in data-transfer operations.
[G11C 7/08] Control thereof -- G11C 7/08 concerns the control mechanisms that manage the writing of data to, or reading of data from, a static digital storage medium, including timing, addressing and signal handling to ensure correct data transfer. It is situated in the hierarchy physics-information storage-static stores.
[G11C 7/10] Input/output [I/O] data interface arrangements, e.g. I/O data control circuits, I/O data buffers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Input/output (I/0) data interface arrangements.  Covers all the synchronous memory data interfaces (Synclink, SDR, DDR, Rambus).
[G11C 7/1003] Interface circuits for daisy chain or ring bus memory arrangements -- Interface circuits that manage data transfer in daisy-chain or ring-bus memory systems link individual memory modules together, allowing sequential or circular access to stored bits. They provide the control and timing signals needed to write information into, or read it from, each module while maintaining the overall bus integrity.
[G11C 7/1006] Data managing, e.g. manipulating data before writing or reading out, data bus switches or control circuits therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In particular:  It also contains sometimes memories with ECC circuits. However these should be searched and classified in G06F11/1044 .
[G11C 7/1009] Data masking during input/output -- G11C 7/1009 covers methods that alter or hide data as it is written to or read from a digital storage device, such as applying masks, substituting values, or scrambling bits for security, privacy, or testing purposes. The techniques operate during the input or output phase without changing the underlying storage architecture.
[G11C 7/1012] Data reordering during input/output, e.g. crossbars, layers of multiplexers, shifting or rotating -- G11C 7/1012 covers techniques for reorganising data as it is written to or read from a digital storage device, such as using crossbar switches, layered multiplexers, or shift- and rotate-based circuits. These mechanisms rearrange the order of bits or words during input or output operations to improve access patterns or interface compatibility.
[G11C 7/1015] Read-write modes for single port memories, i.e. having either a random port or a serial port -- This CPC entry covers read-write operation modes for memory devices that have a single access port, whether that port is organized for random access or serial access. It pertains to arrangements for writing to or reading from static digital storage.
[G11C 7/1018] Serial bit line access mode, e.g. using bit line address shift registers, bit line address counters, bit line burst counters -- The G11C 7/1018 entry covers serial access techniques for bit lines in static digital storage devices, where the bits are addressed sequentially rather than in parallel. It includes the use of bit-line address shift registers, address counters or burst counters to generate the successive addresses needed for writing or reading data.
[G11C 7/1021] Page serial bit line access mode, i.e. using an enabled row address stroke pulse with its associated word line address and a sequence of enabled column address stroke pulses each with its associated bit line address -- The code covers a method of accessing bits in a static memory by issuing a row address pulse that activates the corresponding word line, followed by a series of column address pulses that each select a specific bit line for serial read or write operations.
[G11C 7/1024] Extended data output [EDO] mode, i.e. keeping output buffer enabled during an extended period of time -- G11C 7/1024 covers devices or methods that keep the output buffer of a digital storage system active for a prolonged period, allowing an extended data output (EDO) mode. This enables continued data transmission after a write or read command without requiring immediate buffer reset. The entry falls within the static stores sub-section of information storage physics.
[G11C 7/1027] Static column decode serial bit line access mode, i.e. using an enabled row address stroke pulse with its associated word line address and a sequence of enabled bit line addresses -- G11C 7/1027 covers a static column decoding technique for accessing bits in a digital memory array, where a row address pulse activates a specific word line and a series of bit line addresses are enabled sequentially. The method provides serial column access using a single enabled row address stroke.
[G11C 7/103] using serially addressed read-write data registers  G11C7/1036  takes precedence -- This subclass covers techniques whereby data is stored in a digital memory and accessed through registers that are addressed one after another in a serial fashion, such as shift-register based read/write operations. It applies to static storage devices and is superseded by the more specific G11C7/1036.
[G11C 7/1033] using data registers of which only one stage is addressed for sequentially outputting data from a predetermined number of stages, e.g. nibble read-write mode -- The entry covers methods that address a single stage of a multi-stage data register to output data sequentially, such as reading or writing groups of bits (nibbles) from a predetermined number of stages. It pertains to static digital storage arrangements where only one register stage is selected at a time for sequential data transfer.
[G11C 7/1036] using data shift registers -- The G11C 7/1036 subclass covers inventions that employ data shift registers to move or reposition digital bits during read-or write-operations in static storage devices. It addresses methods and apparatuses that use serial shifting of data to access, write, or retrieve information stored in non-volatile memory.
[G11C 7/1039] using pipelining techniques, i.e. using latches between functional memory parts, e.g. row/column decoders, I/O buffers, sense amplifiers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The pipelining in clock synchronous and wave pipelining in clockless (asynchronous) memories.
[G11C 7/1042] using interleaving techniques, i.e. read-write of one part of the memory while preparing another part -- This subclass deals with interleaving techniques for static digital storage, where one portion of memory is read while another portion is being prepared for writing. The approach enables overlapping read-write operations to increase data transfer speed and efficiency.
[G11C 7/1045] Read-write mode select circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mode setting registers and bonding pads.
[G11C 7/1048] Data bus control circuits, e.g. precharging, presetting, equalising -- G11C 7/1048 covers circuits that control a digital data bus by precharging, presetting or equalising its lines to ensure reliable writing or reading of information. Such circuits manage voltage levels and timing so that bits can be transferred without errors.
[G11C 7/1051] Data output circuits, e.g. read-out amplifiers, data output buffers, data output registers, data output level conversion circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Together with G11C7/1078 , most of the synchronous interfaces documents.
[G11C 7/1054] Optical output buffers -- Optical output buffers are devices that temporarily store and regulate optical data streams as they leave a digital storage medium, smoothing variations in data rate between the source and downstream optical transmission or processing components. They help maintain signal integrity and prevent data loss in high-speed optical communication and storage systems.
[G11C 7/1057] Data output buffers, e.g. comprising level conversion circuits, circuits for adapting load -- G11C 7/1057 covers circuits that buffer data as it leaves a digital storage device, including level-conversion circuits and other circuitry that adapts the output to the electrical characteristics of the attached load. These output buffers ensure reliable signal transmission between the storage medium and downstream electronics.
[G11C 7/106] Data output latches -- The G11C 7/106 subclass refers to data-output latches, which are circuit elements that temporarily hold or latch digital information as it is retrieved from a static storage device for transmission to downstream circuitry. These latches ensure stable, synchronized output during read-out operations in memory and register systems.
[G11C 7/1063] Control signal output circuits, e.g. status or busy flags, feedback command signals -- This subclass deals with circuits that generate control-signal outputs such as status indicators, busy flags, or feedback command signals for digital storage devices, enabling monitoring or regulation of the store's operation. It covers the design and functionality of these output mechanisms.
[G11C 7/1066] Output synchronization -- The G11C 7/1066 entry covers techniques for coordinating the timing of data read-out from a digital storage medium so that the output stream matches a required rate or phase, ensuring that successive bits, bytes or larger blocks are delivered in synchrony with downstream processing or transmission systems. It includes methods for aligning clock signals, buffering, and control of read heads or electronic interfaces to achieve precise output timing.
[G11C 7/1069] I/O lines read out arrangements -- The G11C 7/1069 subclass covers arrangements that read out data from static digital storage by using input-output line configurations, detailing how signals are extracted from memory cells or other fixed-state stores. It falls under Physics → Information storage → Static stores → Arrangements for writing information into, or reading information out from, a digital store.
[G11C 7/1072] for memories with random access ports synchronised on clock signal pulse trains, e.g. synchronous memories, self timed memories -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Many early SDRAMs but became rapidly obsolete as most dynamic memories are now synchronous.
[G11C 7/1075] for multiport memories each having random access ports and serial ports, e.g. video RAM -- G11C 7/1075 covers designs for multiport memory devices that provide both random-access and serial interfaces, allowing simultaneous reading and writing operations. These memories are typically used in video RAM applications where multiple data streams must be handled concurrently. The classification addresses the circuitry and control methods that integrate the different ports within a single static storage unit.
[G11C 7/1078] Data input circuits, e.g. write amplifiers, data input buffers, data input registers, data input level conversion circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Together with G11C7/1051 , most of the synchronous interfaces documents.
[G11C 7/1081] Optical input buffers -- G11C 7/1081 covers optical input buffers, devices that temporarily hold optical data before it is written to or read from a digital storage medium. These buffers manage the timing and flow of light-based signals to ensure reliable data transfer in static storage systems.
[G11C 7/1084] Data input buffers, e.g. comprising level conversion circuits, circuits for adapting load -- G11C 7/1084 concerns data-input buffers for static digital storage, encompassing circuits that convert voltage or signal levels and adapt the electrical load to suit the storage medium, thereby facilitating reliable writing of information into the store.
[G11C 7/1087] Data input latches -- Data input latches are circuit elements that capture and temporarily hold digital signals during a write operation, ensuring that the correct bits are transferred into a static storage cell. They synchronize incoming data with the store's timing and maintain the data until it can be permanently recorded.
[G11C 7/109] Control signal input circuits -- This CPC subcategory deals with circuits that receive control signals for managing digital storage devices, covering input circuitry that interprets command signals to direct read/write operations in static storage systems.
[G11C 7/1093] Input synchronization -- G11C 7/1093 covers arrangements that coordinate the timing of data entering a digital storage medium, ensuring that input signals are synchronized with the write process of the static store. It includes circuits, control methods, and timing mechanisms that align input streams with storage operations.
[G11C 7/1096] Write circuits, e.g. I/O line write drivers -- The G11C 7/1096 subclass covers electronic circuits that generate write signals for static digital storage devices, such as the drivers that control I/O lines during data recording. These circuits manage voltage or current levels to reliably store bits in memory cells.
[G11C 7/12] Bit line control circuits, e.g. drivers, boosters, pull-up circuits, pull-down circuits, precharging circuits, equalising circuits, for bit lines -- G11C 7/12 relates to circuits that control bit lines in static digital memory stores, such as drivers, boosters, pull-up and pull-down circuits, as well as precharging and equalising circuits. These components regulate voltage and timing on bit lines during writing and reading of data.
[G11C 7/14] Dummy cell management -- G11C 7/14 in the Cooperative Patent Classification addresses techniques and circuits used for managing dummy cells in memory devices to improve read/write operations or to maintain data integrity. This includes methods for handling dummy cells that are not part of the main memory array but are essential for the proper functioning of the storage system.
[G11C 7/16] Storage of analogue signals in digital stores using an arrangement comprising analogue/digital [A/D] converters, digital memories and digital/analogue [D/A] converters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Also many early solid state music players and early solid state memory cards (e.g. Compact Flash, SDcard).These should not be classified here but in G11B , G06F and G06K .
[G11C 7/18] Bit line organisation -- Bit line organisation refers to the way conductive lines that carry binary data are arranged within a memory array, influencing access speed, density and signal integrity. The entry covers layout, segmentation and addressing of bit lines in static digital stores such as DRAM and SRAM.
[G11C 7/20] Memory cell initialisation circuits, e.g. when powering up or down, memory clear, latent image memory -- The subclass G11C 7/20 covers circuits that initialise memory cells, such as power-on or power-down reset, memory-clear functions, and restoration of latent-image data. These circuits are used in static digital stores where information is written to or read from memory cells.
[G11C 7/22] Read-write [R-W] timing or clocking circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Glossary:** - Dummy cell: cell that is not used for storage but to provide a reference voltage or current during sensing - Embedded: memories which are designed to be incorporated on the same die as a microcontroller/processor usually in ASICs or System on a Chip circuits - not cache memories - Burst: Read or Write cycle during which a series of 2, 4, 8 or more external data are sequentially input to or output from the memory device - Precharge: to prepare the memory for a subsequent read operation (or a write operation) by charging bit lines and/or word lines to a certain voltage
[G11C 7/222] Clock generating, synchronizing or distributing circuits within memory device -- G11C 7/222 pertains to clock-generating, synchronizing or distribution circuits incorporated in memory devices. It covers timing-signal generators and distribution networks that coordinate read/write operations within static digital stores, ensuring proper synchronization of internal memory functions.
[G11C 7/225] Clock input buffers -- Clock input buffers are circuit elements that condition and distribute timing signals to the write or read heads of a digital storage medium, ensuring proper synchronization of data transfer. They typically amplify, shape, and align the clock pulses to meet the timing requirements of static storage devices.
[G11C 7/227] Timing of memory operations based on dummy memory elements or replica circuits -- G11C 7/227 covers techniques for controlling the timing of read or write cycles in static digital memory devices by using dummy cells or replica circuits that mimic the behavior of real memory elements. These dummy structures are employed to generate appropriate delays, improve reliability, or synchronize operations without affecting stored data.
[G11C 7/24] Memory cell safety or protection circuits, e.g. arrangements for preventing inadvertent reading or writing -- This subclass pertains to circuits that safeguard memory cells by preventing unintended read or write operations, such as latch-up inhibition, write-disable or read-protect mechanisms, thereby ensuring data integrity in digital storage devices.
[G11C 8/00] Arrangements for selecting an address in a digital store -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuitry used for decoding a memory address selecting a row line, a bank , a block or a range of memory cells in a semiconductor memory device.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - For memory cells of the field-effect type -> G11C11/407 - Auxiliary circuits, e.g. for addressing, decoding, driving, writing, sensing, timing, power reduction -> G11C11/413
[G11C 8/005] with travelling wave access -- The subclass covers static memory devices in which a traveling wave propagates across the storage medium to select the address of the data to be read or written, eliminating mechanical movement of the medium or transducer. It is used in acoustic-wave or surface-wave addressing schemes for digital stores.
[G11C 8/04] using a sequential addressing device, e.g. shift register, counter -- G11C 8/04 relates to arrangements that select an address in a static digital store by using a sequential addressing device such as a shift register or counter. It covers methods of address selection in memory arrays that rely on sequential counting or shifting operations.
[G11C 8/06] Address interface arrangements, e.g. address buffers -- The G11C 8/06 subclass covers arrangements that present or manage the address of a location within a static digital store. It includes address-selection interfaces such as address buffers, registers or other circuitry that retrieve or set a memory address for data access.
[G11C 8/08] Word line control circuits, e.g. drivers, boosters, pull-up circuits, pull-down circuits, precharging circuits, for word lines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuitries that have an electrical effect on the rows or word lines. e.g. applying a voltage or a potential to the word line or row.
[G11C 8/10] Decoders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Decoders, circuitry which processes the address information to make a single or plural selection of word line or row line possible. However, these decoder circuits are usually not used for the electrical activation of the row or word line. This is the task of the word line control circuits.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Address mapping per se -> G06F12/02
[G11C 8/12] Group selection circuits, e.g. for memory block selection, chip selection, array selection -- Group selection circuits are electronic arrangements that enable the simultaneous activation of multiple memory elements, such as whole memory blocks, chips, or array sections, based on a chosen address. They manage address decoding to quickly select and connect the desired storage region within static digital stores.
[G11C 8/14] Word line organisation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In particular, global and local word line structure.
[G11C 8/16] Multiple access memory array, e.g. addressing one storage element via at least two independent addressing line groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Only documents describing memories where each storage cell on its own has two or more ports.
[G11C 8/18] Address timing or clocking circuits -- G11C 8/18 designates circuits that generate timing or clock signals used to select memory addresses in static digital storage devices, such as RAM or other addressable registers. It includes address-generation logic, timing control units and related synchronization elements that ensure correct sequencing of read and write operations.
[G11C 8/20] Address safety or protection circuits, i.e. arrangements for preventing unauthorized or accidental access -- G11C 8/20 covers circuits that safeguard the address-selection function of a static digital memory, preventing unauthorized or accidental access. It includes lockout logic, password or key verification, error-detection and other protective measures that block illegal or erroneous address operations.
[G11C 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- G11C 99/00 is a residual group in the physics-based information storage subclass that covers static storage technologies whose subject matter does not fit any of the defined groups. It includes atypical or emerging storage media, unconventional implementation methods, and other unclassified static storage concepts.
[G12] INSTRUMENT DETAILS -- G12 pertains to the structural and functional characteristics of scientific and technical instruments. It includes specifications of design-construction, materials, and auxiliary parts that define an instrument's performance. The subclass is used to classify patents describing detailed aspects of instrument architecture and components.
[G12B] CONSTRUCTIONAL DETAILS OF INSTRUMENTS, OR COMPARABLE DETAILS OF OTHER APPARATUS, NOT OTHERWISE PROVIDED FOR -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Only details which are restricted to mechanical measuring instrument.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Details restricted to the measuring instruments covered by the relevant subclasses of class G01 , e.g. G01D -> G01D - Constructional details restricted to electric apparatus, e.g. casings, screenings -> H02B - Constructional details restricted to electronic apparatus, e.g. casings, screenings -> H05K  **Glossary:** - Apparatus:: a category of subject matter which is a machine or device, described in terms of its functional capabilities or structural features, that is used to make a product, or to carry out a non-manufacturing process or activity.
[G12B 1/00] Sensitive elements capable of producing movement or displacement for purposes not limited to measurement -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Actuating mechanisms of small size, e.g. Micromotion actuators for microdisplacement.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Thermometers using bimetallic elements -> G01K5/62 - Thermally-actuating switches -> H01H37/00
[G12B 1/02] Compound strips or plates, e.g. bimetallic -- G12B 1/02 covers compound strips or plates, such as bimetallic elements, that bend or change shape with temperature or other stimuli to produce motion for use in instruments and devices. These components act as sensitive actuators or displacement elements in measurement or control systems.
[G12B 1/04] Hollow bodies having parts which are deformable or displaceable under pressure, e.g. Bourdon tube, bellows -- G12B 1/04 covers hollow bodies whose walls contain sections that flex or shift when pressurised, such as Bourdon tubes or bellows. These components generate movement or displacement in response to fluid pressure and are used in devices that convert pressure changes into mechanical motion.
[G12B 11/00] Indicating elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[G12B 11/02] Scales -- Scales in G12B 11/02 refer to weighing devices whose construction or indication elements are part of instruments, especially those combined with data processing or control functions. The subclass covers design, calibration, and display features of such scales within larger measurement systems.
[G12B 11/04] Pointers -- G12B 11/04 covers pointers, the indicating elements that move or remain fixed to display a measurement or setting on an instrument. These components are part of the constructional details of devices used in physics, serving to point to values or positions.
[G12B 13/00] Calibrating of instruments and apparatus -- G12B13/00 covers methods and devices for calibrating instruments and apparatus, encompassing techniques to adjust, verify, or maintain measurement accuracy. It includes systems that provide reference standards or automated calibration routines for various physical devices.
[G12B 15/00] Cooling -- This class covers cooling arrangements for data processing or information handling machines, such as computers, including methods and apparatus for removing heat from electronic components. It includes air, liquid, cryogenic and other cooling techniques integrated into instrument construction.
[G12B 15/02] by closed-cycle fluid-circulating systems -- The code G12B 15/02 designates cooling arrangements that employ a closed-cycle fluid-circulating system, wherein a coolant is repeatedly pumped through a loop to remove heat from an instrument or apparatus without exchange with the external environment. This subclass covers the constructional details of such closed-cycle cooling mechanisms.
[G12B 15/04] by currents of fluid, e.g. air, in open cycle -- G12B 15/04 covers cooling methods that employ moving fluids, such as air, in an open-cycle system. It applies to instruments and apparatus where heat is removed by directing a fluid stream across components, rather than using sealed or closed-loop cooling media. This falls under constructional details of instruments in the physics domain.
[G12B 15/06] by contact with heat-absorbing or radiating masses, e.g. heat-sink -- This subclass covers cooling arrangements that remove heat from a device by direct contact with a heat-absorbing or radiating mass, such as attaching a heat-sink to dissipate thermal energy from electronic or mechanical instruments. It pertains to the constructional details of instruments where thermal management is achieved through conductive coupling to a mass that absorbs or radiates the heat.
[G12B 17/00] Screening -- The G12B 17/00 subclass "Screening" covers the structural features of instruments that employ screens, filters, gratings, meshes or similar devices to selectively transmit or block particular signals, particles, radiation or data. It includes designs of apertures, shielding or separator elements that are integral to the instrument's operation, enabling tasks such as measurement, protection, or signal conditioning. This entry pertains to constructional details not captured elsewhere in the instrument-related hierarchy.
[G12B 17/02] from electric or magnetic fields, e.g. radio waves -- The G12B 17/02 subclass concerns the design of screening measures that protect instruments from external electric or magnetic fields, such as radio-wave interference. It includes shielding enclosures, conductive coatings, layout techniques and other constructional features intended to reduce electromagnetic coupling with the surrounding environment.
[G12B 17/04] from visible, ultraviolet, or infrared light -- G12B 17/04 designates the structural features of devices that employ screening using visible, ultraviolet or infrared light, such as optical sensors or filters in data processing and measurement equipment. It covers the arrangement of light-sensitive elements, masks, and associated optics used to select or block specific wavelengths.
[G12B 17/06] from heat -- G12B 17/06 relates to the formation of a screened image on a recording medium by the application of heat, encompassing thermal printing and heat-developed screening techniques. It falls under instrument construction details for devices that create or modify data patterns using heat-based processes.
[G12B 17/08] from influences producing mechanical damage, e.g. caused by blast, by external objects, by person -- The G12B 17/08 subclass addresses screening techniques for magnetic-recording systems that protect against mechanical damage such as blasts, impact by external objects, or intentional sabotage. It falls under physics instrument details concerning constructional aspects of apparatus not otherwise classified.
[G12B 3/00] Details of movements not otherwise provided for -- G12B 3/00 classifies the structural features of mechanical movements in data-processing or information-handling devices that are not covered by more specific subclasses. It addresses the design of moving parts such as drives, positioning systems, or feed mechanisms where the particular motion is otherwise uncoded. This subgroup captures generic movement details of instruments and comparable apparatus.
[G12B 3/02] Caging of movements, i.e. locking of movements when not in use -- G12B 3/02 pertains to mechanisms that lock or restrain moving parts of an instrument when it is not in use, preventing unintended motion. It covers caging devices and other means of immobilising movements in physics or measurement apparatus, where such locking features are not classified elsewhere.
[G12B 3/04] Suspensions -- G12B 3/04 covers suspension mechanisms used in instruments and apparatus, detailing how components are supported or isolated to control motion. It includes springs, dampers, and other devices that reduce vibrations or permit controlled movement where the movement is not otherwise specified. The code is applied to the constructional aspects of instrument design.
[G12B 3/06] Reducing effects of friction, e.g. by vibration -- The subclass G12B 3/06 deals with methods for lessening friction in moving parts of instruments, often by applying vibration or similar actions to reduce wear and resistance. It falls under physics → instrument details → constructional details of instruments → details of movements not otherwise provided for, and covers any arrangement that mitigates friction-related effects in mechanical or electromechanical devices.
[G12B 3/08] Damping of movements, e.g. to promote rapid non-oscillatory movement to a final reading -- G12B 3/08 pertains to the damping of moving parts in devices so that motion quickly settles without oscillation, ensuring a rapid, stable final reading. It covers mechanisms such as hydraulic, magnetic, pneumatic or friction-based dampers that control inertia in instruments and similar apparatus. This subclass is used for designs where swift, non-oscillatory positioning is essential for accurate measurement or operation.
[G12B 3/10] using eddy currents -- The G12B 3/10 subclass covers inventions that employ eddy currents to generate, control or damp mechanical movement in instruments or comparable apparatus. It includes designs where induced currents in conductive parts produce magnetic fields that interact with motion, providing braking, positioning, sensing or actuation functions not otherwise specified in more generic movement categories. This classification is used for detailed constructions of devices whose operation relies on eddy-current effects.
[G12B 5/00] Adjusting position or attitude, e.g. level, of instruments or other apparatus, or of parts thereof -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Adjusting mechanisms of small size, e.g. sensor positioning devices.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Level per se -> G01C9/00 - Adjusting means of optical elements -> G02B7/00
[G12B 7/00] Compensating for the effects of temperature -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Compensating by cooling -> G12B15/00
[G12B 9/00] Housing or supporting of instruments or other apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanical details of casings, housings, cabinets etc. for accommodating a mechanical measuring instrument, or supports and devices for carrying or holding such instruments.
[G12B 9/02] Casings -- G12B 9/02 relates to the design and construction of housings or supporting structures for instruments and other apparatus, specifically the casings that protect, enclose and mount functional components. It addresses the physical arrangement and protective enclosures used in devices such as measurement tools, computers and comparable equipment.
[G12B 9/04] Details, e.g. cover -- G12B 9/04 pertains to the design and construction of outer coverings, casings or protective shells that house or support instruments or apparatus, such as covers, lids, and enclosures that shield internal components.
[G12B 9/06] Metal casings -- The G12B 9/06 subclass pertains to metal casings that house or support instruments and other apparatus, emphasizing their constructional features. It falls within physics instrument details, covering structural designs for metal enclosures used in devices such as data-processing or communication equipment.
[G12B 9/08] Supports -- The G12B 9/08 class addresses structural elements that hold, mount or otherwise support instruments or apparatus, such as frames, brackets, housings or other supporting devices. It covers designs for attaching, securing or positioning equipment within a broader system, focusing on the constructional details of the supporting structures.
[G12B 9/10] Instruments boards -- G12B 9/10 covers the design and construction of boards that serve as the housing or support structure for instruments and comparable apparatus. It includes mounting frames, circuit boards and other substrate configurations that provide mechanical stability and integration for electronic or measuring devices.
[G16] INFORMATION AND COMMUNICATION TECHNOLOGY [ICT] SPECIALLY ADAPTED FOR SPECIFIC APPLICATION FIELDS -- This class does not cover : pattern recognition, which is covered by group G06F18/00 ; digital computing or data processing systems or methods specially adapted for administrative, commercial, financial, managerial or supervisory purposes, which are covered by subclass G06Q ; image data processing or generation, which is covered by subclass G06T . In this class, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: "ICT [information and communication technology]" also covers "IT [information technology]"; "ICT specially adapted for" also covers the expression "digital computing or data processing systems or methods specially adapted for", which is used in group G06F17/00 .
[G16B] BIOINFORMATICS, i.e. INFORMATION AND COMMUNICATION TECHNOLOGY [ICT] SPECIALLY ADAPTED FOR GENETIC OR PROTEIN-RELATED DATA PROCESSING IN COMPUTATIONAL MOLECULAR BIOLOGY -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or systems for genetic or protein-related data processing in computational molecular biology.  Computational processing of data related to nucleic acids, proteins, peptides, or amino acids.  Bioinformatics methods or systems where the digital data processing is inherent or implicit, although not explicitly mentioned.  **Glossary:** - systems biology: Simulation and mathematical modelling of relationships and interactions between molecular entities in sub-cellular systems integrating genetic and/or protein-related data to describe the dynamic behaviour of, for example, protein-protein/protein-ligand interactions, regulatory networks and metabolic networks - phylogeny: Reconstruction of an evolutionary development and history of a species or higher taxonomic grouping of organisms; typically represented as a phylogenetic tree; methods for creating phylogenetic trees - phylogenetic tree: Tree-like graphical representation of phylogenetic relationships - molecular structure: 2-dimensional or 3-dimensional arrangement of atoms, groups of atoms or domains in nucleic acids, proteins, peptides and amino acids - structure alignment: Form of alignment to establish structural and functional equivalences between two or more proteins based on their secondary or tertiary structures - protein folding: Process by which a polypeptide chain folds into a specific 3-dimensional structure - domain: Domain of a protein is an element of the overall molecular structure that is self-stabilising and often folds independently of the rest of a polypeptide chain - drug targeting: Drug design strategy aiming at optimising the properties of a medicinal compound, based on the 3-dimensional structure of a target, for delivery to a particular tissue or organ in the body - functional genomics: Experimental analyses aiming at assessing the function of genes in determining traits, physiology and/or development of an organism, making use of computational and high-throughput technologies - proteomics: Large-scale study of the functions of proteins and their interactions with other molecular entities in a biological system - genotype: Genetic makeup or profile of an organism with respect to a trait - ploidy: Number of sets of chromosomes in a cell/cells of an organism - allele: Alternative form of a gene (one member of a pair) that is located at a specific position (locus) on a specific chromosome - snp: Single nucleotide polymorphism: a DNA sequence variation that involves a change in a single nucleotide and is commonly present in a part of a population - motif: Specific nucleotide or amino acid sequence pattern - population genetics: Study of genetic variation and genetic evolution of populations - linkage disequilibrium: Tendency of alleles located close to each other on the same chromosome to be inherited together - mutagenesis: Process by which the genetic information of an organism is changed, resulting in a mutation - gene expression: Process by which proteins are made or transcribed from the instructions encoded in DNA - gene expression profiling: Determination of the pattern of genes expressed, i.e. transcribed, under specific circumstances or in a specific cell line - probe design and optimisation for microarrays: Designing and selecting (i) optimal, highly specific probes, e.g. oligonucleotides, cDNA, fragments for hybridisation experiments with microarrays and (ii) optimal sets of probes, e.g. oligonucleotides, cDNA, to be chemically attached to a solid support to form an array - microarray: Plurality of nucleic acid probes attached to a substrate, which form an ordered pattern - sequence alignment: Process of comparing nucleic or amino acid sequences, generally by a linear alignment in such a way that equivalent positions in adjacent sequences are brought into the correct alignment with each other by introducing insertions in suitable positions, in order to identify similarities and/or differences amongst the compared sequences - sequence assembly: Method by which linear portions of sequence information are assembled to obtain full length gene sequence data - in silico: Performed on a computer or via computer simulations - ontology: Classification methodology for formalising a subject's knowledge in a structured and controlled vocabulary
[G16B 10/00] ICT specially adapted for evolutionary bioinformatics, e.g. phylogenetic tree construction or analysis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Analysis of orthologous, paralogous, syntenic, or taxonomic relationships.  Generation of pedigrees and phylogenetic trees.  Mere mention of evolutionary data is not sufficient to justify classification in this place.
[G16B 15/00] ICT specially adapted for analysing two-dimensional [2D] or three-dimensional [3D] molecular structures, e.g. structural or functional relations or structure alignment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structural architecture of proteins, peptides, amino acids, and nucleic acids and the prediction thereof.  Processes including structural alignment, protein folding, domain topology, molecular modelling, receptor-ligand modelling, docking methods, structural-functional relationships and drug targeting using structure data, as well as two- and three-dimensional structure prediction and/or analysis.  The structure types include secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structures.  Mere mention of structural data is not sufficient to justify classification in this place.
[G16B 15/10] Nucleic acid folding -- G16B 15/10 covers computer-implemented methods and systems for predicting or modeling the three-dimensional folding of nucleic acids such as DNA or RNA. It includes algorithms for secondary structure prediction, energy minimisation and structure alignment to assess functional relationships. The subclass is used for software tools that analyse or simulate nucleic acid conformations in computational molecular biology.
[G16B 15/20] Protein or domain folding -- G16B 15/20 covers computer-based techniques for modelling, simulating or predicting the folding of proteins or protein domains, including algorithms that infer three-dimensional structures from amino-acid sequences, evaluate folding pathways, and assess structural stability. It also includes software tools for visualising and analyzing folded conformations.
[G16B 15/30] Drug targeting using structural data -- G16B 15/30 covers methods and computer systems that use three-dimensional or two-dimensional molecular structure information to identify or design drug targets. It includes techniques such as structure-based virtual screening, docking and alignment that exploit structural relationships to guide drug discovery. The class falls under ICT for bioinformatics within the physics hierarchy.
[G16B 20/00] ICT specially adapted for functional genomics or proteomics, e.g. genotype-phenotype associations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Assessment of the function of genes and proteins in determining traits, physiology and/or development of an organism, making use of computational and large scale, high-throughput technologies.  Genotypic-phenotypic associations, including genotyping and genome annotation, linkage disequilibrium analysis and association studies, population genetics, alternative splicing, and small Interfering RNA design (siRNA, RNAi).  Binding site identification, mutagenesis analysis, protein-protein or protein-nucleic acid interactions.  Mere mention of gene or protein function is not sufficient to justify classification in this place.
[G16B 20/10] Ploidy or copy number detection -- G16B 20/10 pertains to methods and systems in bioinformatics for detecting ploidy or copy number variations, which are critical in understanding genetic disorders and cancer. This classification covers computational tools specifically designed to analyze and interpret complex genomic data related to the number of chromosome sets or specific gene copies within a cell.
[G16B 20/20] Allele or variant detection, e.g. single nucleotide polymorphism [SNP] detection -- The G16B 20/20 subclass covers computer-based methods and systems for detecting genetic alleles or variants, such as single-nucleotide polymorphisms, typically used in functional genomics and proteomics. It includes algorithms, software tools, and data-processing techniques that identify genotype-phenotype associations or other molecular-biology variations.
[G16B 20/30] Detection of binding sites or motifs -- G16B 20/30 covers methods and systems for identifying specific binding sites or sequence motifs in biological data such as DNA, RNA or protein sequences. These techniques are applied in functional genomics and proteomics to associate genotypic patterns with phenotypic traits.
[G16B 20/40] Population genetics -- The entry G16B 20/40 deals with computational techniques used in population genetics, including methods for analysing genetic variation, allele-frequency estimation and genotype-phenotype association studies within functional genomics and proteomics contexts. It is part of ICT specially adapted for bioinformatics.
[G16B 20/50] Mutagenesis -- The G16B 20/50 entry covers information-technology systems and methods specifically adapted for functional genomics or proteomics that deal with mutagenesis. It includes computational tools for generating, storing, analysing and interpreting mutation data, such as genotype-phenotype association studies, and software that supports the design, detection and evaluation of genetic or protein-altering experiments.
[G16B 25/00] ICT specially adapted for hybridisation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Analysis of hybridization or gene/protein expression information. This includes microarray analysis, gel electrophoresis analysis and sequencing by hybridisation (SBH). Further covered technologies include modelling polymerase chain reaction (PCR) data, primer or probe design and probe optimisation, microarray design, normalisation, expression profiling, noise correction models, and expression-ratio estimation.  Mere mention of hybridisation or gene/protein expression is not sufficient to justify classification in this place.
[G16B 25/10] Gene or protein expression profiling -- G16B 25/10 covers computer-based methods and systems for profiling gene or protein expression, such as microarray or RNA-seq data analysis, enabling classification, visualization and interpretation of biological activity. It falls under ICT specialized for bioinformatics within the physics and information-technology hierarchy.
[G16B 25/20] Polymerase chain reaction [PCR] -- G16B 25/20 covers the polymerase chain reaction, a technique that amplifies DNA sequences, classified under ICT specially adapted for hybridisation in bioinformatics. It pertains to software and hardware facilitating PCR processes within molecular biology.
[G16B 25/30] Microarray design -- G16B 25/30 concerns the design of microarrays, the patterned arrangement of nucleic-acid probes on a substrate for simultaneous analysis of many DNA or RNA sequences. It addresses the layout, synthesis and spatial organization of probe features to enable high-throughput hybridisation assays used in bioinformatics and molecular-biology applications.
[G16B 30/00] ICT specially adapted for sequence analysis involving nucleotides or amino acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Comparison of sequence information, wherein the sequences are nucleic acids, proteins, or peptides. The comparisons include methods of alignment, homology identification, motif identification, single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) discovery, haplotype identification, fragment assembly, and gene finding.  Mere mention of sequence data is not sufficient to justify classification in this place.
[G16B 30/10] Sequence alignment -- G16B 30/10 covers computer-based methods and systems for aligning biological sequences, where nucleotide or amino-acid strings are compared to reveal similarity, differences or evolutionary relationships. It includes algorithms such as dynamic-programming and heuristic approaches, scoring schemes, gap handling and tools for multiple-sequence alignment.
[G16B 30/20] Sequence assembly -- G16B 30/20 relates to computer-implemented techniques for assembling biological sequences from fragments, such as algorithms that reconstruct DNA or protein sequences using overlapping reads. It is part of ICT specially adapted for sequence analysis in computational molecular biology, nested under broader physics and ICT categories.
[G16B 35/00] ICT specially adapted for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In silico (i.e. computer based) designing and screening of combinatorial nucleic acids, protein, or peptide libraries.  Mere mention of nucleic acid, protein, or peptide combinatorial libraries is not sufficient to justify classification in this place.
[G16B 35/10] Design of libraries -- G16B 35/10 covers methods and systems for creating and organising genetic or protein libraries used in computational molecular biology. It includes algorithmic or software approaches for selecting library composition, encoding and sampling strategies to support bio-informatic analyses.
[G16B 35/20] Screening of libraries -- G16B 35/20 covers methods and systems for screening large collections of biological or chemical entities to identify candidates with desired properties. It includes high-throughput assays, automated handling, and data analysis techniques used in drug discovery, diagnostics, and functional genomics.
[G16B 40/00] ICT specially adapted for biostatistics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Discovery and/or analysis of patterns within a vast amount of genetic or protein-related data, wherein the emphasis is placed on the method of analysis and is largely independent of the particular type of bioinformatic data.  Covered methods based on machine learning and statistical models; supervised and unsupervised learning techniques include bioinformatic pattern finding, knowledge discovery, rule extraction, correlation, clustering, and classification.  Multivariate analysis of protein or gene-related data, e.g. analysis of variances (ANOVA), principal component analysis (PCA), and support vector machines (SVM).
[G16B 40/10] Signal processing, e.g. from mass spectrometry [MS] or from PCR -- G16B 40/10 covers signal processing techniques applied to data produced by mass-spectrometry or polymerase-chain-reaction instruments, facilitating extraction, transformation and interpretation of molecular information in bioinformatics and biostatistical analysis.
[G16B 40/20] Supervised data analysis -- G16B 40/20 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the EPO and USPTO, pertains to supervised data analysis within the field of biostatistics. This classification covers methods and systems that use labeled data for training algorithms to predict outcomes or classify biological data accurately.
[G16B 40/30] Unsupervised data analysis -- G16B 40/30 covers techniques for analysing data without predefined categories, such as clustering, principal component analysis and other pattern-discovery methods. It is applied to computational molecular biology, genetics and biostatistical tasks where the data are examined to reveal inherent structure or relationships.
[G16B 45/00] ICT specially adapted for bioinformatics-related data visualisation, e.g. displaying of maps or networks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Visual representations specifically adapted to bioinformatic data, wherein the emphasis is placed on the method of visualisation and is largely independent of the particular type of bioinformatic data.  For example: graphics generation, map display (e.g. haplotype maps, linkage maps), and network display (e.g. genetic networks, protein-protein interaction networks, metabolic networks).
[G16B 5/00] ICT specially adapted for modelling or simulations in systems biology, e.g. gene-regulatory networks, protein interaction networks or metabolic networks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Simulation or mathematical modelling of relationships and interactions between molecular entities on a subcellular level, integrating data related to genetic and/or proteins to describe the dynamic behaviour of protein-protein/protein-ligand interactions, regulatory or metabolic networks.  Mere mention of modelling or simulation is not sufficient to justify classification in this place.
[G16B 50/00] ICT programming tools or database systems specially adapted for bioinformatics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Software specially adapted to assist in programming procedures within bioinformatics.  Database systems specially adapted for managing bioinformatic data. For example: ontologies, heterogeneous data integration, data warehousing, and computing architectures.  Encryption and compression algorithms for genetic data.
[G16B 50/10] Ontologies -- G16B 50/10 covers ontologies used in ICT programming tools and database systems tailored for bioinformatics, defining controlled vocabularies and hierarchical relationships to represent biological entities and their properties. It enables consistent data integration, annotation and reasoning across genetic and protein datasets.
[G16B 50/20] Heterogeneous data integration -- G16B 50/20 pertains to the integration of diverse biological and molecular data types, focusing on methods and systems that enable interoperability and comprehensive analysis across different data sources in bioinformatics.
[G16B 50/30] Data warehousing -- G16B 50/30 covers data-warehousing solutions designed for bioinformatics, enabling the storage, integration and fast retrieval of large genetic or protein-related data sets. These systems provide scalable architectures and query tools that support computational molecular-biology applications, fitting within the ICT-focused branch of the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[G16B 50/40] Encryption of genetic data -- G16B 50/40 covers ICT programming tools and database systems designed to encrypt and protect genetic or protein-related data. It includes methods, algorithms and software for secure storage, transmission and processing of bioinformatics information, ensuring confidentiality and integrity of genetic sequences and related datasets.
[G16B 50/50] Compression of genetic data -- G16B 50/50 covers methods, systems and software that compress genetic data such as DNA, RNA or protein sequences, employing specialised encoding, algorithms or data-structures to reduce file size while preserving biological information. It is a bioinformatics subclass within ICT programming tools for computational molecular biology.
[G16B 5/10] Boolean models -- G16B 5/10 covers the use of Boolean models to represent and analyse gene-regulatory, protein-interaction or metabolic networks in systems biology. It includes methods for constructing, simulating and interpreting discrete logical models of biological pathways and network behavior.
[G16B 5/20] Probabilistic models -- G16B 5/20 concerns the use of probabilistic models for representing and analysing biological networks such as gene-regulatory, protein-interaction or metabolic systems. It covers statistical approaches that predict relationships, dynamics or uncertainties in these networks, facilitating computational molecular-biology applications within the ICT domain tailored to genetics and proteomics.
[G16B 5/30] Dynamic-time models -- G16B 5/30 covers computer-implemented methods that represent biological processes as dynamic-time models, allowing simulation of temporal changes in gene regulation, protein interactions or metabolic pathways. It includes algorithms that compute state evolution over continuous or discrete time steps to predict system behavior.
[G16B 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- G16B 99/00 is a residual group for computer-implemented inventions in bioinformatics that do not fit any other specific subgroup of the subclass. It covers generic, emerging or miscellaneous ICT methods and systems for processing genetic or protein data in computational molecular biology.
[G16C] COMPUTATIONAL CHEMISTRY -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Data processing methods or systems for the storage, retrieval, analysis, distribution or visualisation of physicochemical or structural data of chemical particles, elements, compounds, or mixtures.  Computational theoretical chemistry.  Computational materials science, i.e. data processing methods or systems for investigating physics or chemistry of new or existing materials or phenomena associated with their design, synthesis, processing, characterization, or utilisation.  This subclass also covers computational chemistry and computational materials science methods or systems where the digital data processing is inherent or implicit, although not explicitly mentioned.  **Glossary:** - design: Software-based creation or planning - handling: Covers retrieval, analysis, visualisation or storage - quantum chemistry: Branch of chemistry, in which quantum mechanics is applied to theoretical studies of chemical systems - molecular mechanics: Branch of chemistry, in which classical mechanics is applied to model molecular systems - molecular dynamics: Computer-based simulation method for studying movements of atoms and molecules in time by applying Newton's equations of motion and molecular mechanics force fields - in silico: Performed on a computer or via computer simulations
[G16C 10/00] Computational theoretical chemistry, i.e. ICT specially adapted for theoretical aspects of quantum chemistry, molecular mechanics, molecular dynamics or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Computer-based calculations and theoretical analysis of:  Mere mention of modelling or simulation using QM, MM, MD, etc. is not sufficient to justify classification in this group.
[G16C 20/00] Chemoinformatics, i.e. ICT specially adapted for the handling of physicochemical or structural data of chemical particles, elements, compounds or mixtures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Subject matter related to cheminformatics as specified in the subgroups.
[G16C 20/10] Analysis or design of chemical reactions, syntheses or processes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Computer-assisted analysis and design of chemical reactions, processes and syntheses. The following are examples of the subjects covered:
[G16C 20/20] Identification of molecular entities, parts thereof or of chemical compositions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Computer-assisted, measurement-based identification of molecules, parts of molecules or their molecules structures, and of compositions of multi-component mixtures, e.g. computer-assisted structure elucidation (CASE).  Computer-assisted, measurement-based, qualitative and quantitative analysis of samples.  Measurement may be based on mass spectrometry (MS), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), spectroscopy, chromatography, electrophoresis or the like.
[G16C 20/30] Prediction of properties of chemical compounds, compositions or mixtures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Computer-assisted prediction of physical, physicochemical and biological properties of chemical compounds, compositions or mixtures. The following are examples of the subjects covered:  Computer-assisted prediction of a drug dosage or drug regimen.  Computer-assisted prediction of the concentration of a pharmaceutical active agent/drug based on molecular data, wherein the drug/active agent can be any molecule other than in the group of nucleic acids, proteins, peptides and their conjugates.
[G16C 20/40] Searching chemical structures or physicochemical data -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Database search of chemical structures or physicochemical data. The following are examples of the subjects covered:
[G16C 20/50] Molecular design, e.g. of drugs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Computer-assisted design and modelling of molecules to be used for any purpose. The following are examples of the subjects covered:  Computer-assisted drug formulation.
[G16C 20/60] In silico  combinatorial chemistry -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In silico designing and screening of combinatorial chemical libraries of compounds other than nucleic acids, proteins, peptides, or amino acids.  Mere mention of combinatorial libraries of particular types of compounds is not sufficient to classify in this place.
[G16C 20/62] Design of libraries -- G16C 20/62 concerns the design of chemical libraries in chemoinformatics, encompassing computational methods for selecting, generating and organizing collections of molecules or fragments for virtual or experimental screening. It includes algorithms for diversity analysis, property-based sampling and combinatorial enumeration to support drug discovery and materials research.
[G16C 20/64] Screening of libraries -- G16C 20/64 covers computational methods for screening chemical libraries, including algorithms and software that evaluate large sets of compounds to identify candidates with desired physicochemical or biological properties, often used in drug discovery and materials design.
[G16C 20/70] Machine learning, data mining or chemometrics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Computer-assisted discovery and/or analysis of patterns within a vast amount of physicochemical data, wherein the emphasis is placed on the methods of analysis and is largely independent of the particular type of physicochemical data.  Analysis methods are based on any of:
[G16C 20/80] Data visualisation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Visual representations specifically adapted to structural and/or physicochemical data, wherein the emphasis is placed on the method of visualisation and is largely independent of the particular type of structural and/or physicochemical data.  The following are examples of the subjects covered:
[G16C 20/90] Programming languages -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Software specially adapted to assist in programming procedures within computational chemistry.  Encryption and compression algorithms specially adapted for chemical data, e.g. chemical fingerprints.  Database systems specially adapted for managing chemical data.  The following are examples of the subjects covered:
[G16C 60/00] Computational materials science, i.e. ICT specially adapted for investigating the physical or chemical properties of materials or phenomena associated with their design, synthesis, processing, characterisation or utilisation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Computer-assisted mathematical modelling of the structures (e.g. metals, polymers, ceramics, composites, biomaterials, nanomaterials) by applying the knowledge of physics, physical chemistry and chemistry.  In particular modelling of:  Computer-assisted investigation of existing materials and design of new ones.  Computational investigation of existing materials and design of new ones.
[G16C 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- G16C 99/00 is a catch-all group in the CPC subclass for computational chemistry, covering inventions that involve computer-based chemical modeling, simulation or data processing but whose subject matter does not fall within any of the defined specific groups of the subclass. It is used when the technical features cannot be assigned to a more detailed classification elsewhere in G16C.
[G16H] HEALTHCARE INFORMATICS, i.e. INFORMATION AND COMMUNICATION TECHNOLOGY [ICT] SPECIALLY ADAPTED FOR THE HANDLING OR PROCESSING OF MEDICAL OR HEALTHCARE DATA -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cross-sectional aspects of computer, information or communication science with medical or healthcare science, where the focus is clearly placed on digital computing or data processing or handling system or methods, which are specially adapted for medical or healthcare science.  It deals with the ICT resources, devices, systems and methods for the handling and processing of information and data in healthcare, medicine and well-being.  Healthcare informatics tools include not only computers, systems and devices but also clinical guidelines, formal medical terminologies, and information and communication systems. It is applied to the areas of medical care, nursing, diagnosis, therapy, dentistry, surgery, pharmacy, public health, occupational health, wellbeing, medical research and medical cooperation.  **Glossary:** - Handling: of medical or healthcare data covers the acquisition, storage, retrieval, analysis, management, transmission or usage of such data
[G16H 10/00] ICT specially adapted for the handling or processing of patient-related medical or healthcare data -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the handling or processing of patient-related medical or healthcare data, e.g. for electronic clinical trials or questionnaires, for data related to laboratory analysis or for patient-specific data.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - ICT specially adapted for medical reports -> G16H15/00 - ICT specially adapted for therapies or health-improving plans -> G16H20/00 - ICT specially adapted for the handling or processing of medical images -> G16H30/00
[G16H 10/20] for electronic clinical trials or questionnaires -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the handling and processing of patient-related medical or healthcare data for electronic clinical trials or questionnaires, surveys, polls, studies, forms, enquiries, statistics, with or without manually input data.  Example 1: Receiving first information about a patient via a first user interface that is communicatively coupled to a communication network, receiving second information about the patient via a second user interface that is communicatively coupled to the communication network, where the first information and the second information, together, comprise answered questions, evaluating the answered questions to yield follow-up questions and a suggested diagnosis, and transmitting the suggested diagnosis and the follow-up question to the second user interface via the communication network.  Example 2: A method for managing a clinical trial, comprising: enabling information exchanges over an Internet-based network with users participating in a clinical trial; deploying over the Internet-based network for use by the users clinical trial setup information including tools to automate creation of a data repository; providing to the users over the Internet-based network, collaborative clinical trial setup and administration functions which allow the users to use the clinical trial setup information to collaborate with each other and administer the clinical trial; performing clinical trial management functions.
[G16H 10/40] for data related to laboratory analysis, e.g. patient specimen analysis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the handling or processing of patient-related medical or healthcare data related to laboratory analysis.  For example:  Example 1: Confidentially providing laboratory results to a consumer of a sample retrieval kit comprising: labelling the sample with a unique identification number; correlating the identification number with the consumer's identification number; testing the sample to obtain a clinical value; correlating the unique identification number with the clinical value; and electronically providing the clinical value to the consumer in a secure electronic manner.  Example 2: Sample processing system with plurality of tracking devices coupled to a respective plurality of sample processing containers and storing sample identification information for a patient sample for use with sample processing devices configured to process the patient sample according to workflow steps of a processing protocol. A plurality of readers is configured to receive signals from the plurality of tracking devices. A controller may be coupled to the plurality of readers and configured to determine if the patient sample is processed according to the processing protocol based at least in part on the signals from the plurality of tracking devices.
[G16H 10/60] for patient-specific data, e.g. for electronic patient records -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the handling or processing of patient-specific data, e.g. for electronic patient records.  It covers patient records (in any form of: local, remote, confidential, public, centralized, distributed, condensed) or reference data thereto; their conversion and merging and handling, and corresponding privacy, security or confidentiality aspects.  It covers also databases, other data storage means and methods for handling patient-specific data, or reference data thereto.  Example: A central computer has access to consolidated medical and biographical records databases and provides controlled access for local or remotely situated patient computers and healthcare computers. Patients can control access to their own records and/or specific parts of their records. Patients, health care providers and/or interested individuals may also inquire about patient records. Patient records can also be created based on sorting and merging data from different sources.
[G16H 10/65] stored on portable record carriers, e.g. on smartcards, RFID tags or CD -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the handling or processing of patient-related medical or healthcare data for patient-specific data, e.g. for electronic patient records, which are stored on portable record carriers, e.g. on smartcards, RFID tags or CD.  At least part of the patient-specific data or reference data thereto, is stored in such a manner that it is wearable or portable for the patient. This is often used for assisting patient identification or fast access to patient information, e.g. for accidents, emergencies, patient visits to doctor's offices or doctors' visits. It covers also patients' RFID tags.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  Healthcare Informatics (30a), i.e. Information and Communication Technology or ICT  1a.  1b.  1c.  **Glossary:** - RFID: radio-frequency identification
[G16H 15/00] ICT specially adapted for medical reports, e.g. generation or transmission thereof -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for medical reports, e.g. generation or transmission or handling or processing thereof.
