A medical report is an account of a person's health or wellbeing, or a person's medical, clinical or health history. It can be on paper, screen or merely stored.  It covers report generation and distribution, as well as codes, standards and software algorithms for generating reports, which are applicable to any kind of medical or healthcare data: patient-related data or non-patient-related data. Report analysis is however classified in the fitting diagnosis or treatment groups.  Example: Reporting healthcare data by creating displays or reports based on aggregating data from healthcare results data and history data. Optionally, patient progress indicators may be determined, narrative reports may be generated which integrate fixed textual matter with patient data, in various formats.
[G16H 20/00] ICT specially adapted for therapies or health-improving plans, e.g. for handling prescriptions, for steering therapy or for monitoring patient compliance -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for therapies or health-improving plans, e.g. for handling prescriptions, monitoring patients' compliance, handling therapy or health-improving plans.  It relates to any kind of therapies, healthcare, regimen, strategies, trainings, plans, schedules, prescriptions or recommendations, relating to improving, restoring or maintaining health, well-being, or medical, clinical or bodily conditions. Well-being also includes lifestyle.  **Glossary:** - Prescription: Healthcare plan for therapy
[G16H 20/10] relating to drugs or medications, e.g. for ensuring correct administration to patients -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for therapy or health-improving plans which are based on drugs or medication, e.g. for ensuring correct administration to patients. It covers:  With this, the aim is to make sure that the right patient receives the right medication at the right times and in the right dosages, form, procedure and route.
[G16H 20/13] delivered from dispensers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for therapy or health-improving plans which are based on drugs or medication delivered to patients from dispensers. Dispensers are meant as automatic machines or containers designed to release a specific amount of drugs or medications. It also covers tags and labels for drugs.  Example 1: Medication dispensing system comprising medicine cabinet with compartments containing medications for access by healthcare attendant for preparing medication dosages for patients; delivery device, e.g. mobile cart, with sections receiving dosages prescribed for patients. Medicine cabinet includes a processor and memory for storing the names of patients and their prescribed medication dosages, and a display screen for displaying the patient names and their respective prescribed medication dosages. The delivery device, e.g. tray, includes a display screen for displaying the patient names and their respective medication dosages, and a communication link with the medicine cabinet through which the cabinet processor communicates to the delivery device the patient names and their respective medication dosages.  Example 2: Drug dispensing control system has a drug-dispensing unit with canister receiving a prescribed amount of medication from a prescription filling system. The canister includes a single dose dispensing mechanism. A circuitry, provided in the drug-dispensing unit, allows access to a dose of the medication at a predetermined interval.  Example 3: Medication packets are automatically dispensed in the correct dosage amount and provided with identifiers and instructions, e.g. labels, tags, bar codes and/or human readable information.
[G16H 20/17] delivered via infusion or injection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for therapy or health-improving plans which are based on drugs or medication delivered to patients via infusion or injection.  Example: Communication system from medication infusion pump to hospital information management system, including a pump with pump operation circuitry for monitoring characteristics of pump operation indicative of administration of medication to a patient. A communication device is connected to the pump operation circuitry for communicating a pump signal representing the pump operation characteristics. The pump communication device communicates with a hospital information management system which receives the pump signal and which also includes a computer capable of storing, displaying and adjusting the pump operation characteristics.
[G16H 20/30] relating to physical therapies or activities, e.g. physiotherapy, acupressure or exercising -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for therapy or health-improving plans which are based on physical therapies or physical activities.  The following is a non-exhaustive list of therapies related to this group:
[G16H 20/40] relating to mechanical, radiation or invasive therapies, e.g. surgery, laser therapy, dialysis or acupuncture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for therapy or health-improving plans which are based on mechanical, radiation or invasive therapies.  The following is a non-exhaustive list of therapies related to this group:
[G16H 20/60] relating to nutrition control, e.g. diets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for therapy or health-improving plans which are based on nutrition control, e.g. diets.  It also covers the use of nutraceuticals, food supplements or food dispensers.  Example: Weight control programs, weight and diet goals, caloric intake and expenditure determination, planning and monitoring, determining nutritional or caloric contents.
[G16H 20/70] relating to mental therapies, e.g. psychological therapy or autogenous training -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for therapy or health-improving plans which are based on mental therapies, e.g. psychotherapy, autogenous training, lifestyle management.
[G16H 20/90] relating to alternative medicines, e.g. homeopathy or oriental medicines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for therapy or health-improving plans which are based on alternative medicines, e.g. homeopathy or oriental medicines, in particular Chinese medicine.
[G16H 30/00] ICT specially adapted for the handling or processing of medical images -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the handling or processing of medical, clinical or healthcare images, pictures, photographs, scans, or videos.  "Handling or processing" covers anything from generating, acquiring, transmitting, communicating, sharing, converting, formatting, viewing, using, editing, annotating, marking, tagging, storing, up to archiving and deleting.
[G16H 30/20] for handling medical images, e.g. DICOM, HL7 or PACS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the handling of medical images.  For example:  **Glossary:** - DICOM: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine - HL7: Health Level Seven International - PACS: Picture Archiving and Communication Systems

[G16H 40/00] ICT specially adapted for the management or administration of healthcare resources or facilities -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the management, administration, handling, controlling, monitoring, treating, maintaining, configuring, servicing, networking, scheduling, using or operation of healthcare resources or facilities or of medical equipment or devices. It covers any kind of ICT which relates to these aspects.
[G16H 40/20] for the management or administration of healthcare resources or facilities, e.g. managing hospital staff or surgery rooms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the management or administration of healthcare resources or facilities, e.g. managing hospital or office staff, patients, surgery rooms, inventory management.  It covers also hospital networks, scheduling staff and equipment, measuring quality and efficiency, managing workflows, hygiene monitoring, also hygiene dispensers.  Hospital information systems.  Healthcare administrative and organisational software.  Example:
[G16H 40/40] for the management of medical equipment or devices, e.g. scheduling maintenance or upgrades -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the management of medical equipment or devices, e.g. scheduling maintenance or upgrades, calibrating, servicing, updating, mending, or repairing. It is independent from whether it happens locally or remotely.
[G16H 40/60] for the operation of medical equipment or devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the operation of medical equipment or devices, or of their interfaces.  **Glossary:** - Operation: covers also monitoring or control - Local: relates to operation by which (i) a patient, (ii) a medical equipment or device, and (iii) a medical practitioner or operator are in a single geographically enclosed area - Remote: relates to operation by which (i) a patient, (ii) a medical equipment or device, or (iii) a medical practitioner or operator are geographically separated - Transformation: of medical or healthcare data, means any contextual, content-related or structural transformation of said data
[G16H 40/63] for local operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the local operation, monitoring or control of medical equipment or devices.  Example: A doctor controlling a surgery over Ethernet connection, but located in the same intensive care unit hosting the surgery room.
[G16H 40/67] for remote operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the remote operation of medical equipment or devices, or of their interfaces.  The following is a non-exhaustive list of subjects related to this group:
[G16H 50/00] ICT specially adapted for medical diagnosis, medical simulation or medical data mining -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for medical diagnosis, medical simulation or medical data mining.  ICT specially adapted for monitoring, detecting or modelling of epidemics or pandemics.It also covers any subject-matter relating to medical, clinical, health-related or wellbeing-related diagnosis or diagnostic decisions, or merely diagnostic data processing, or data processing for supporting diagnosis; and also any algorithms or methods for medical, clinical, health-related or wellbeing-related decisions or for supporting such decisions. Also any kind of scoring, calculating indices, risks, or health-related values, bodily, physiologic or psychologic parameters.  It covers ICT adapted for medical simulation and modelling only insofar as it relates to medical disorders. Note that it is about ICT and data processing for medical simulation and modelling, not medical simulation and modelling as such.  Epidemics or pandemics can relate to any kind of diseases, illnesses, infections, bodily parameters, health issues, healthiness, bioterrorism, i.e. anything which causes medical, clinical, health-related or well-being symptoms to people.
[G16H 50/20] for computer-aided diagnosis, e.g. based on medical expert systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for medical diagnosis or computer-aided diagnosis, e.g. based on medical expert systems.  It covers any artificial intelligence or any heuristics used for supporting, assisting or automating diagnosis, or data analysis for diagnostic purposes.  It covers also rule-based systems, fuzzy logic, and also any trivial, i.e. non-complex computer-aided diagnosis and computer-aided medical decision making.
[G16H 50/30] for calculating health indices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for calculating health indices, for individual health risk assessment.  The following is a non-exhaustive list of subjects related to this group:
[G16H 50/50] for simulation or modelling of medical disorders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for medical simulation or modelling of medical disorders.  It covers the simulation or modelling of the state, evolution or transformation of medical disorders.
[G16H 50/70] for mining of medical data, e.g. analysing previous cases of other patients -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for mining of medical data, e.g. analysing previous cases of other patients.  The following is a non-exhaustive list of subjects related to this group:  Example: Data mining framework for mining structured clinical information includes a data miner that mines information from a clinical patient record (CPR) using domain-specific knowledge contained in a knowledge base. The data miner includes components for extracting information from the CPR, combining all available evidence over time, and drawing inferences from this combination process. The mined information may be stored in a structured CPR.
[G16H 50/80] for detecting, monitoring or modelling epidemics or pandemics, e.g. flu -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for monitoring, detecting or modelling of epidemics or pandemics, e.g. for flu, Ebola, HIV or bioterrorism alerts.  It covers also medical data mining for detecting unusual medical patterns in geographic regions or population groups.
[G16H 70/00] ICT specially adapted for the handling or processing of medical references -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the handling and processing of medical references, i.e. medical data not linked to a specific patient.  The following is a non-exhaustive list of subjects related to this group:  Example: Retrieve, consolidate, verify, annotate, store, organize, update and share medical reference data.
[G16H 70/20] relating to practices or guidelines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the handling and processing of medical references relating to:
[G16H 70/40] relating to drugs, e.g. their side effects or intended usage -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the handling and processing of medical references relating to:
[G16H 70/60] relating to pathologies -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for the handling and processing of medical references relating to pathologies. Also covers any kind of information related to pathophysiological processes, or to origins, nature, conditions and course of diseases, bodily malfunctions and other deviations from healthy conditions.
[G16H 80/00] ICT specially adapted for facilitating communication between medical practitioners or patients, e.g. for collaborative diagnosis, therapy or health monitoring -- **Definition:** This place covers:  ICT specially adapted for facilitating communication between medical practitioners or patients, e.g. for collaborative diagnosis, therapy or health monitoring.  ICT specially adapted for consultations between medical practitioners or patients, their cooperation, teleconferencing, white-boarding, sharing findings or interworking.  The expression "between practitioners and/or patients" encompasses any of the following combinations:
[G16Y] INFORMATION AND COMMUNICATION TECHNOLOGY SPECIALLY ADAPTED FOR THE INTERNET OF THINGS [IoT] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  IoT related aspects.  In this subclass, IoT is defined as a system that executes the following four processes.  In other words, IoT is defined as a system consisted of three main components, namely: (a) physical objects ("things"), (b) network and (c) function, as defined in the Glossary.  See illustrative examples below.  Illustrative image of IoT with server  Illustrative image of IoT without server  l Illustrative image of IoT with HUB  **Glossary:** - Object: Objects (the "things") are embedded with technology enabling these objects to sense and collect information and connecting to network. Examples of such objects are: Objects, which perform specific functions with little or no electronic components, e.g. windows, doors or clothes; Objects, which include electronic components that perform specific functions independently from their functions for connecting to network, e.g. home electronic appliance and vehicles. On the other hand, generic computing and communicating devices, e.g. computer or laptop, on their own are not regarded as objects. However, in case that these devices are embedded in physical objects and enable the objects to sense and collect information and connect to network, then the devices can be regarded as a part of objects (the "things"). For example, smartphones, which are attached to vehicles to sense and deliver position data to a server, can be regarded as part of the objects. - Thing: see "object" - Function: Means processing information and outputting the results obtained by information processing. Processing information, here, includes:Complex information processing such as analyzing input information, e.g. data analysis.Complex information processing such as creating control information from input information.Processing information, here, doesn't include;Simple or less complex information processing such as only monitoring input information without data analysis, e.g. security camera system.Simple or less complex information processing such as only conveying control information to operate device, e.g. remote control system. - Network: Means the communication system of interconnected objects which have their own IP addresses. A typical example of such network is the Internet.
[G16Y 10/00] Economic sectors -- G16Y 10/00 covers ICT and IoT solutions that are tailored to particular economic sectors, such as manufacturing, agriculture, transport, health care, energy or finance. It includes devices, networks, data processing and services designed to meet the specific requirements, standards or business models of those industries, enabling sector-focused automation, monitoring and efficiency improvements.
[G16Y 10/05] Agriculture -- G16Y 10/05 pertains to information and communication technologies specifically adapted for the Internet of Things that are applied in the agricultural sector, covering smart farming devices, sensor networks, data analytics and automated control systems that enhance crop management, livestock monitoring and resource efficiency. This classification falls under the physics-based ICT sub-field for IoT applications in agriculture.
[G16Y 10/10] Forestry -- G16Y 10/10 relates to ICT and IoT solutions designed for forestry, covering sensor networks, data acquisition, and management systems that monitor tree health, growth and forest conditions. It includes devices and methods for remote-sensing, automated harvesting and environmental impact assessment within the forestry sector.
[G16Y 10/15] Fishing -- G16Y 10/15 refers to ICT solutions for the Internet of Things that are adapted to the fishing sector, including sensor networks, data acquisition and processing, remote monitoring of vessels, gear and catches, and automated control systems that support management, safety and sustainability.
[G16Y 10/20] Mining -- G16Y 10/20 covers ICT solutions for mining that employ Internet-of-Things devices such as sensors, actuators and communication networks to monitor equipment, track environmental conditions, optimise resource extraction and improve safety. The classification groups hardware and software for remote data acquisition, real-time analytics and autonomous control specific to mining operations.
[G16Y 10/25] Manufacturing -- G16Y 10/25 classifies ICT solutions specifically tailored for the Internet of Things that support manufacturing activities. It covers hardware, software and communication methods that enable smart factories, sensor-based monitoring, data-driven process control and integration of production equipment into IoT networks. This entry falls under the physics-based ICT branch for IoT applications.
[G16Y 10/30] Construction -- The G16Y 10/30 subclass covers information and communication technology specially adapted for the Internet of Things when applied to construction activities, such as building monitoring, smart infrastructure management and automated construction processes. It includes devices, sensors, networking and data handling methods that enable real-time control, safety monitoring and efficiency optimisation on construction sites. This entry falls under the broader ICT for IoT branch of the CPC system.
[G16Y 10/35] Utilities, e.g. electricity, gas or water -- The CPC subclass G16Y 10/35 covers technologies that apply information and communication technology to the management and operation of utility services such as electricity, gas, and water, typically within the context of the Internet of Things. It includes systems for monitoring, data acquisition, control and optimization of utility infrastructure, enabling remote sensing, automated decision-making and improved resource efficiency.
[G16Y 10/40] Transportation -- G16Y 10/40 covers technologies that apply Internet-of-Things concepts to transportation, such as connected vehicles, smart fleet monitoring, and data-driven traffic management. It includes hardware and software that enable real-time communication between transport assets and external networks, aiming to improve safety, efficiency and service quality.
[G16Y 10/45] Commerce -- G16Y 10/45 covers ICT solutions specifically adapted for the Internet of Things that support commercial activities such as electronic trading, payment processing, order handling, inventory management and supply-chain coordination, enabling automated and networked commerce between devices and users.
[G16Y 10/50] Finance -- G16Y 10/50 covers financial services and systems that use ICT, including IoT devices, for banking, payments, investment, and risk management. It includes methods, apparatus, and computer programs that enable electronic transactions, data analysis, and electronic money handling, often integrated with sensors and networked objects.
[G16Y 10/55] Education -- The G16Y 10/55 classification covers information and communication technologies adapted for the Internet of Things that are used in educational contexts, such as smart-classroom devices, IoT-enabled learning platforms, and sensor-based tools for teaching, training and assessment. It includes hardware, software and services that facilitate interactive, data-driven learning environments.
[G16Y 10/60] Healthcare -- The G16Y 10/60 classifies ICT and IoT solutions that support health-care activities, such as remote patient monitoring, tele-medicine platforms, wearable diagnostic devices and data-driven health management systems. It covers the integration of sensors, communication networks and software that enable collection, transmission and analysis of medical information for improved diagnosis, treatment and wellness monitoring.
[G16Y 10/65] Entertainment or amusement -- This CPC subclass covers ICT systems and devices designed for the Internet of Things that provide entertainment or amusement functions, such as games, interactive media, or leisure-oriented services integrated into connected objects. It includes hardware, software and communication methods that enable playful experiences in IoT environments.
[G16Y 10/70] Broadcasting -- G16Y 10/70 covers broadcasting within the Internet of Things, including systems and methods for disseminating audio, video or data streams to large numbers of interconnected devices. It addresses transmission techniques, network architectures and protocols that enable one-to-many delivery of content or control information to IoT nodes.
[G16Y 10/75] Information technology -- G16Y 10/75 denotes technologies and methods that constitute general information technology, encompassing the design, implementation and management of hardware, software and data-processing systems. It includes networked devices, data storage, processing and communication functions, and serves as a foundational classification for ICT solutions applicable to fields such as the Internet of Things.
[G16Y 10/80] Homes -- G16Y 10/80 covers ICT solutions specifically designed for residential environments, including smart-home devices, automation of lighting, heating, security and energy management, as well as communication interfaces that connect household appliances within the Internet of Things.
[G16Y 10/90] Chemistry -- G16Y 10/90 covers ICT solutions for the Internet of Things that incorporate chemical functions, such as sensors, analyzers, or processors for detecting, measuring, or controlling chemical substances. It includes hardware and software enabling chemical data acquisition, interpretation, and transmission within IoT networks, often for environmental monitoring, health diagnostics, or industrial process control.
[G16Y 20/00] Information sensed or collected by the things -- This subclass relates to methods and systems for acquiring data from physical objects, often called things, in Internet-of-Things environments. It includes sensors that detect environmental, positional or operational parameters and produce digital information for transmission, storage or further processing.
[G16Y 20/10] relating to the environment, e.g. temperature -- G16Y 20/10 concerns IoT devices that collect environmental data, for example temperature measurements, and make that information available to other systems. It is situated in the ICT branch specially adapted for the Internet of Things, focusing on sensed or gathered information.
[G16Y 20/20] relating to the thing itself -- G16Y 20/20 concerns patents that address the physical thing itself in Internet-of-Things systems, focusing on the device or object that generates or senses data. It is part of the ICT category specially adapted for the IoT and deals with the thing rather than the surrounding network or data processing.
[G16Y 20/30] relating to resources, e.g. consumed power -- G16Y 20/30 covers IoT devices that manage and monitor resource-related data such as power consumption, battery status, or energy usage, enabling efficient allocation and control of the devices' energy resources. This subclass focuses on methods and systems that collect, process, or transmit information about the resources consumed by the connected "things."
[G16Y 20/40] relating to personal data, e.g. biometric data, records or preferences -- G16Y 20/40 covers techniques for collecting, storing, processing or using personal data such as biometric identifiers, user records and preference profiles within Internet of Things devices or services. It addresses the handling and protection of such data in IoT applications.
[G16Y 30/00] IoT infrastructure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The construction, setting, control, maintenance or management of IoT systems per se.
[G16Y 30/10] Security thereof -- G16Y 30/10 covers protective measures for Internet-of-Things infrastructure, including methods and devices that ensure confidentiality, integrity, authentication and access control for IoT networks, sensors, actuators and related data exchange. It addresses encryption, secure key management, intrusion detection and other security protocols designed to safeguard connected objects and services.
[G16Y 40/00] IoT characterised by the purpose of the information processing -- **Glossary:** - Maintenance: The use of IoT for keeping an object condition to a certain level. - Management: The use of IoT for controlling an object under some policies or in order to achieve objective.
[G16Y 40/10] Detection -- G16Y 40/10 - Detection deals with techniques and apparatus for recognizing physical or logical states in Internet-of-Things systems, including sensors, signal-processing algorithms and pattern-recognition methods that identify events, abnormalities or environmental changes to initiate further processing or actions.
[G16Y 40/20] Analytics -- G16Y 40/20 covers analytical techniques and systems that process data generated by Internet-of-Things devices, focusing on extracting, interpreting or visualising information to support decision-making in IoT applications. It includes methods for real-time monitoring, pattern detection, statistical analysis and predictive modelling applied to sensor streams and connected devices.
[G16Y 40/30] Control -- The G16Y 40/30 subclass covers control mechanisms within Internet-of-Things systems, including monitoring, directing, and actuating networked devices based on processed data. It encompasses feedback loops, remote management, and automated decision-making that adjust device behavior to achieve desired outcomes.
[G16Y 40/35] Management of things, i.e. controlling in accordance with a policy or in order to achieve specified objectives -- G16Y 40/35 pertains to methods and systems for managing IoT devices by applying policies or objectives, covering control mechanisms that ensure devices operate according to predefined rules such as resource allocation, performance tuning, or security enforcement.
[G16Y 40/40] Maintenance of things -- G16Y 40/40 covers methods, systems and devices for the upkeep and servicing of physical objects within the Internet of Things, including remote monitoring, diagnostic analysis and predictive maintenance that use information processing to keep things functional. It enables automated or semi-automated interventions based on sensor data to extend lifespan and reduce downtime.
[G16Y 40/50] Safety -- The CPC subclass G16Y 40/50 covers safety aspects of Internet-of-Things systems, defining measures that protect users, data and hardware from hazards, failures or attacks. It includes methods for fault detection, safe operation, risk assessment and mitigation tailored to IoT-enabled information processing.
[G16Y 40/60] Positioning -- The G16Y 40/60 subclass covers techniques for determining or improving the geographical location of devices within the Internet of Things, including algorithms, sensors and communication methods that enable positioning of IoT objects. It addresses both indoor and outdoor positioning, often using signals such as Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, RFID, GPS or hybrid approaches to support location-aware IoT applications.
[G16Z] INFORMATION AND COMMUNICATION TECHNOLOGY [ICT] SPECIALLY ADAPTED FOR SPECIFIC APPLICATION FIELDS, NOT OTHERWISE PROVIDED FOR -- G16Z concerns information and communication technology that is specially adapted for particular application fields when no more specific CPC subclass applies, covering customized user interfaces, domain-specific communication protocols, and tailored data-processing methods designed for unique operational contexts.
[G16Z 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other main groups of this subclass -- G16Z 99/00 designates ICT inventions that do not fit any other main group within the subclass for specially adapted applications, acting as a residual category for subject matter not otherwise provided for. It captures miscellaneous technologies and methods in the information and communication field that are outside the scope of existing groups.
